title: Migration to Abyssinia: Fleeing Persecution for Faith
summary: Early Muslims fled persecution in Mecca by migrating to Abyssinia, seeking refuge to preserve their faith and safety.
story: Concerning the migration of those Companions of the Prophet (peace be upon him) who escaped from persecution with their faith from Mecca to Abyssinia.
lessons: True faith sometimes requires sacrifice and leaving behind what is familiar and comfortable.
Seeking safety and refuge when facing oppression is not weakness but wisdom and trust in God's plan.
The early Muslims' willingness to endure displacement rather than abandon their beliefs shows that spiritual integrity is worth any worldly hardship.
practical_advice: When facing unbearable pressure to compromise your values, consider what changes in your environment or circumstances might give you space to heal and grow.
Reach out to communities or individuals who share your values when you feel isolated in your struggles.
Remember that difficult seasons of life are often temporary — the migrants to Abyssinia eventually returned, and their patient endurance became a source of honor.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab Accepts Islam
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, once a fierce opponent of the early Muslims, accepted Islam, bringing great strength and relief to the persecuted community.
story: 'Umar b. al-Khattab accepts Islam.
lessons: Even the most hardened hearts can change — never lose hope in people's capacity for transformation.
A single turning point can shift the entire trajectory of a person's life and impact countless others.
God's plan often works through the most unexpected means and people.
practical_advice: If you are struggling with someone who opposes or dismisses you, hold onto hope rather than despair — people change in ways we cannot predict.
Reflect on moments in your own life when your perspective shifted dramatically, and use that as evidence that growth is always possible.
When relief finally comes after a long period of hardship, take time to express genuine gratitude rather than moving on too quickly.

title: The Confinement in the Quarter of Abu Talib: The Boycott
summary: The Quraysh tribes of Banu Hashim and Banu Abd al-Muttalib were confined to a quarter and subjected to a harsh economic and social boycott documented in a written agreement, causing severe hardship to the early Muslim community.
story: An account of the disagreement of the Quraysh tribes of the Banu Hashim and the Banu Abd al-Muttalib in helping the Messenger of God (peace be upon him). Also concerning their confinement of them in the quarter of Abu Talib for a lengthy period and their writing an evil, brazen document to that effect. And the signs of the prophethood and proofs of the truth that became evident regarding all this.
lessons: Collective suffering endured for a righteous cause can forge an unbreakable sense of community and shared purpose.
Oppressive systems, no matter how formally documented or socially enforced, do not last forever — truth and justice eventually prevail.
Signs of divine support often appear in the middle of hardship, reminding believers that they are not abandoned.
practical_advice: When you feel socially or economically cut off, focus your energy on strengthening the bonds within your immediate circle rather than seeking validation from those who have rejected you.
Document your experiences during difficult periods — just as this painful chapter was recorded, writing or journaling can help you process hardship and find meaning in it later.
Look for small signs of hope and support even in the darkest circumstances, as they are reminders that your situation is not permanent.

title: Abu Bakr's Determination to Emigrate to Abyssinia
summary: Abu Bakr, known as 'the Trusting,' resolved to emigrate to Abyssinia to escape persecution, demonstrating his steadfast commitment to faith over worldly security.
story: An account of the determination of Abu Bakr, 'the Trusting', to emigrate to Abyssinia.
lessons: Decisive action in the face of fear, taken for a principled reason, is one of the highest expressions of courage.
Trusting in God does not mean waiting passively — it often means taking difficult steps forward with faith.
Those who are known for their trustworthiness and good character carry that strength with them even into exile and hardship.
practical_advice: When you are faced with a frightening but necessary decision, break it down into one concrete step you can take today rather than being paralyzed by the full weight of the change.
Surround yourself with people of good character during difficult transitions — their steadiness will support your own.
Remind yourself of past moments when you trusted and things worked out, using those as evidence to counter fear about current challenges.

title: The Night Journey and Ascension: Isra and Mi'raj
summary: The Prophet Muhammad was taken on a miraculous night journey from Mecca to Jerusalem and then ascended through the heavens, where he witnessed extraordinary signs — a moment of profound divine reassurance during one of the most difficult periods of his mission.
story: Concerning the Isra', the Night Journey, of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) from Mecca to Jerusalem, his ascent from there to heaven, and the signs he saw there.
lessons: When a believer is at their lowest, God's care and closeness can manifest in the most unexpected and profound ways.
Experiences that seem impossible to others can be a source of deep personal certainty and spiritual grounding for the one who lives them.
The divine is not distant or indifferent — even in the midst of loss and rejection, the Prophet was honored and lifted up.
practical_advice: In your darkest moments, remain open to unexpected sources of comfort and reassurance — they often arrive when you have stopped looking for them.
When you have an experience of clarity, peace, or spiritual connection, hold onto the memory of it and return to it mentally during difficult times.
Do not let others' disbelief in your experiences or struggles invalidate what you know to be true about your own inner life and journey.

title: The Splitting of the Moon
summary: During the time of the Prophet, the moon was split as a visible sign of divine power, which was witnessed but rejected by those who refused to believe.
story: Concerning the splitting of the moon in the time of the Prophet (peace be upon him).
lessons: Some people will reject even the clearest evidence when their hearts are closed — you cannot force acceptance or belief in others.
Miraculous signs are not primarily for convincing skeptics but for strengthening the faith of those already on the path.
God's power operates beyond the limits of what human beings consider normal or possible.
practical_advice: Let go of the exhausting effort to prove yourself to people who have already decided not to believe you — focus instead on those who are genuinely open.
Reflect on the 'signs' in your own life — moments of unexpected help, recovery, or clarity — as reminders of something greater at work.
When others dismiss what you know to be true, find grounding in your own direct experience rather than in their validation.

title: The Death of Abu Talib: Losing a Protector
summary: The Prophet lost his uncle Abu Talib, who had been his primary protector for decades, leaving him more vulnerable to the hostility of the Quraysh at one of the most difficult moments of his mission.
story: The death of Abu Talib, uncle of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him), and thereafter that of Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, and the wife, may God be pleased with her, of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him).
lessons: Grief over those who protected and supported us is a deeply human response, and even the Prophet experienced profound loss.
The loss of a protector does not mean the loss of divine protection — earthly supports are temporary, but God's care is not.
Loss often arrives in clusters, testing our resilience in ways that singular trials do not.
practical_advice: When you lose someone who was a source of safety or support in your life, give yourself permission to grieve fully rather than pushing through too quickly.
Identify other sources of support in your life — community, faith, routine — that can hold you when a key relationship is no longer there.
After significant loss, take small and manageable steps forward rather than expecting yourself to function at full capacity.

title: The Death of Khadija: Losing a Beloved Companion
summary: The Prophet lost his beloved wife Khadija, his first supporter and most intimate companion, in the same period he lost his uncle Abu Talib — a year known as the Year of Grief.
story: The death of Abu Talib, uncle of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him), and thereafter that of Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, and the wife, may God be pleased with her, of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him).
lessons: The grief of losing a life partner who believed in you before anyone else is one of the deepest forms of loss a person can experience.
Gratitude for what was shared does not diminish grief — both can coexist honestly.
God does not require that we be free of sorrow to continue our purpose; grief and mission can walk together.
practical_advice: Honor the memory of those you have lost by continuing to embody the values they believed in you to carry.
When grief feels unbearable, connect with others who loved the same person — shared memory can be a form of healing.
Be gentle with yourself in the period immediately following loss; reduced capacity is not failure, it is a natural response to profound pain.

title: The Prophet's Journey to Ta'if: Rejection and Resilience
summary: After the deaths of his wife and uncle, the Prophet traveled to Ta'if seeking support for his mission, but was met with mockery and physical harm, and returned to Mecca without the help he had sought.
story: On his departure to the people of Ta'if to call them to Almighty God, and to come to the aid of his faith; how they refused him this and he returned to Mecca.
lessons: Rejection by those you hoped would help you is one of the most painful human experiences, and the Prophet himself was not spared from it.
The fact that someone refuses your message or your need does not mean the message or the need is wrong.
Returning from failure without giving up altogether is itself a profound act of courage and faith.
practical_advice: When you reach out for help and are turned away, resist the temptation to conclude that you are not worthy of support — the problem is rarely yours alone.
Allow yourself to feel the sting of rejection honestly, but set a boundary on how long you will dwell in it before taking your next step.
After a significant setback, return to your foundation — your core values, your closest relationships, your daily practices — before attempting the next step forward.

title: Calling the Arab Tribes During Pilgrimage Seasons
summary: The Prophet repeatedly called on various Arab tribes during pilgrimage seasons to support him, and was refused by all of them — until the Ansar of Medina were chosen by God to fulfill that role.
story: On the call of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) to the Arab tribes during the pilgrimage seasons to help and support him against those persecuting and denying him. None of them agreed, since God Almighty had reserved that great honour to the Ansar (the 'Helpers' or 'Partisans') of Medina, may God be pleased with them.
lessons: Sometimes the help you need is being withheld from one source because it is being prepared from another — trust in the timing of things.
Persistence in seeking support, even after repeated refusals, is a form of faith in action.
The ones who ultimately help you may not be who you expected — God's provision often comes from unexpected directions.
practical_advice: If you have been turned down repeatedly in seeking help, try widening your circle rather than deepening your frustration with those who said no.
Reflect on times in your life when what you needed came from an unexpected source, and let that pattern build your trust during current seasons of waiting.
Continue showing up and making your needs known even when the response has repeatedly been no — the right 'yes' may be just around the corner.

title: The First and Second Pledges of Al-Aqaba: The Ansar Embrace Islam
summary: Groups of people from Medina met the Prophet during the pilgrimage seasons and pledged their support and allegiance, marking the beginning of the migration to Medina and a turning point in Islamic history.
story: The commencement of the acceptance of Islam by the Ansar, the 'Helpers' of Medina, God bless them. An account of the second meeting at al-Aqaba. That which gives the names of those who attended the second meeting at al-Aqaba, their number, according to Ibn Ishaq, totalling seventy-three men and two women.
lessons: After long periods of difficulty and rejection, the arrival of genuine support can feel overwhelming — receiving help graciously is its own form of wisdom.
A small group of sincere and committed people can change the course of history.
When people choose to stand with you at personal cost to themselves, their loyalty is among the greatest gifts life can offer.
practical_advice: When support finally arrives after a long drought, receive it with gratitude rather than suspicion — not everyone who offers help has a hidden agenda.
Build your relationships during good times so that a network of genuine care exists before you desperately need it.
Acknowledge and express appreciation to those who stood by you during your most difficult chapters — they deserve to know what their loyalty meant.

title: The Emigration from Mecca to Medina: The Hijra
summary: The Prophet and his companion Abu Bakr emigrated from Mecca to Medina, a dangerous and historic journey that marked a new beginning for the Muslim community.
story: The beginning of emigration from Mecca to Medina. On the cause of the emigration of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him). The emigration of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) from Mecca to Medina accompanied by Abu Bakr 'the Trusting', God be pleased with him.
lessons: Leaving what is familiar for the sake of a higher purpose is one of the most defining acts a person can undertake.
True companionship means walking beside someone through danger, not just sharing comfort in ease.
Every migration — physical, emotional, or spiritual — carries both loss and the seed of renewal.
practical_advice: If you are facing a major life transition, focus on what you are moving toward rather than only what you are leaving behind.
Choose carefully who accompanies you through life's most significant changes — their character will shape the journey.
Give yourself time to grieve what you leave behind, even when the leaving is the right decision.

title: The Story of Umm Ma'bad: Hospitality and Witnessing the Prophet
summary: During the migration to Medina, the Prophet and Abu Bakr stopped at the tent of Umm Ma'bad, a woman who witnessed the Prophet's presence and described him with remarkable detail, becoming an unexpected witness to his character and blessing.
story: The story of Umm Ma'bad al-Khuza'iyya.
lessons: Sometimes the most profound spiritual encounters happen in ordinary settings and with people outside the inner circle of a movement.
Generosity and hospitality, even when resources are scarce, open doors to unexpected blessing.
The character of a truly great person is visible even to strangers who encounter them briefly.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of genuine hospitality — welcoming others with warmth, even in simple ways, creates connections that can be transformative.
Pay attention to the character of the people who pass through your life, even briefly — some encounters carry more significance than they first appear.
When you recognize something truly good and noble in a person or moment, do not hesitate to speak of it — bearing witness to goodness is itself an act of generosity.

title: The Prophet's Arrival in Medina: A New Beginning
summary: The Prophet's arrival in Medina was met with tremendous joy and welcome from its people, marking the transition from years of persecution in Mecca to a community where he could build and lead freely.
story: On the entry of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) into Medina, and where he established therein his dwelling, comprising matters related thereto.
lessons: After long seasons of hardship, moments of true arrival and welcome deserve to be received with full presence and gratitude.
New beginnings carry both joy and the weight of enormous responsibility — holding both with wisdom is a sign of maturity.
A community that genuinely welcomes the vulnerable and the displaced enacts one of the highest forms of human goodness.
practical_advice: When a difficult chapter finally ends and a new one begins, mark the transition consciously — do not let relief pass without acknowledgment and gratitude.
If you are in a position to welcome others who are starting over, do so generously and without making them feel the weight of their dependence.
Allow yourself to feel joy when good things finally come, without guilt and without immediately bracing for the next difficulty.

title: Abd Allah ibn Salam Accepts Islam in Medina
summary: A prominent Jewish scholar in Medina, Abd Allah ibn Salam, accepted Islam after recognizing the Prophet, demonstrating that sincere seekers of truth can find it regardless of their background.
story: On the acceptance of Islam by Abd Allah b. Salam, may God be pleased with him.
lessons: Genuine truth-seeking without attachment to prior identity or social standing is one of the rarest and most admirable human qualities.
A person's background or previous beliefs do not determine their capacity for growth and transformation.
Courageous intellectual honesty — being willing to follow truth wherever it leads — is spiritually and personally liberating.
practical_advice: Be willing to question long-held assumptions about yourself and the world, especially when evidence suggests that growth requires it.
When you recognize truth, do not delay acting on it out of fear of social cost — the longer you wait, the harder it becomes.
Honor people in your life who have made difficult transitions toward truth and integrity — their example is one of the most powerful teachers available to you.

title: Building the Mosque in Medina: Community and Sacred Space
summary: The Prophet established a mosque in Medina, building a center of worship and community life that became the heart of the new Muslim society.
story: On the building of the holy mosque during the period of the residence of the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) at the home of Abu Ayyub, may God be pleased with him. Indications of the excellence of this noble mosque and this exalted place.
lessons: Creating spaces dedicated to reflection, community, and worship is one of the most lasting contributions a person can make.
Sacred spaces are built not just from materials but from the intention and sacrifice of those who construct them.
A community that invests in shared spaces of meaning and gathering invests in its own long-term cohesion and resilience.
practical_advice: Invest in creating spaces in your own life — physical or relational — that are set apart for reflection, connection, and renewal.
When you contribute to something larger than yourself, even in small ways, you become part of a legacy that extends far beyond your individual life.
Treat the places where your community gathers with care and respect — they hold more significance than their physical dimensions suggest.

title: The Brotherhood Between the Emigrants and the Helpers
summary: The Prophet established a formal bond of brotherhood between the Emigrants from Mecca and the Helpers of Medina, creating one of the most remarkable examples of solidarity and mutual support in history.
story: On the establishment of friendship between the Emigrants and the Helpers through the document he had drawn up between them. On the nature of that brotherly relationship he made between them, and on the friendly relations he established with the Jews who were in Medina. On the establishment by the Messenger of God (peace be upon him) of a pact of brotherhood between the Emigrants and the Helpers so that they would be friends.
lessons: Intentional acts of solidarity — formally naming and committing to bonds of care — create community structures that can survive enormous pressure.
Generosity toward those who are starting over, as the Helpers showed to the Emigrants, is one of the most powerful expressions of human goodness.
Building bridges across difference, as the Prophet did in establishing relations with the Jewish community of Medina, reflects wisdom as well as principle.
practical_advice: Actively invest in your relationships rather than assuming they will sustain themselves — intentional acts of care and commitment strengthen bonds.
If someone in your life is going through a major transition or loss, consider what concrete support you can offer, not just emotional presence.
Look for opportunities to build relationships across lines of difference in your own community — the richness this creates outlasts any short-term discomfort.

title: The Institution of the Call to Prayer in Medina
summary: Upon arriving in Medina, the Prophet established the adhan, the call to prayer, as a regular practice summoning the community to worship — creating a rhythm of remembrance that would anchor Muslim life across centuries.
story: On the call to prayer and its legitimacy upon the arrival of the Prophet (peace be upon him) in Medina.
lessons: Establishing regular rhythms of remembrance and connection is one of the most powerful tools for sustaining faith and community through difficulty.
Simple practices, repeated consistently, can become the architecture of a life well-lived.
A call that brings people together across difference toward a shared purpose is among the most unifying sounds a community can know.
practical_advice: Build regular anchoring practices into your daily life — moments of pause, reflection, or gratitude — that interrupt the rush and reconnect you to what matters most.
Consider what practices you can establish now that will sustain you through future difficulty, rather than only building habits in response to crisis.
Share practices that ground you with those you care about — communal rhythms of meaning are more sustaining than solitary ones.

title: The First Migration to Abyssinia
summary: The first group of Muslims, facing severe persecution in Mecca, emigrated to Abyssinia at the Prophet's direction, becoming the first emigrants in Islam. The Prophet told them of a just king there where truth prevailed and no one was harmed.
story: It has been recounted above how the polytheists abused those believers who lacked power or protection and treated them with severe violence and great humiliation. God, Almighty and Glorious is He, had kept them away from His Messenger (SAAS), and had given him the protection of his uncle, Abu Talib, as was mentioned above. And all praise and recognition is due to God. Al-Waqidi has related that their passage to Abyssinia occurred in the month of Rajab in the fifth year of the Mission, and that the first group to migrate consisted of eleven men and four women. He told how they made their way to the sea, either on foot or riding, and there they hired a boat to Abyssinia at a cost of one-half of one dinar. These persons were: Uthman b. Affan and his wife Ruqayya, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Hudhayfa b. Utba and his wife Sahla, daughter of Suhayl, al-Zubayr b. al-Awwam, Mus'ab b. Umayr, Abd al-Rahman b. Awf, Abu Salama b. Abd al-Asad and his wife Umm Salama, daughter of Abu Umayya, Uthman b. Maz'un, Amir b. Rabi'a al-Anzi, his wife Layla, daughter of Abu Hathma, Abu Sabra b. Abu Ruhm, Hatib b. Amr, Suhayl b. Bayda' and Abd Allah b. Mas'ud. God bless them all! Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS), witnessed the trials descending upon his Companions, he compared this with his own good state that derived from his own status with God and from his uncle Abu Talib, and, recognizing that he was unable to prevent the evil befalling them, he told them, "I wish you would go forth into the land of Abyssinia, for there is a king in whose realm no one is harmed, where truth prevails. Stay there until God gives you relief from your plight." The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS), thereupon left for Abyssinia, fearing the unrest and fleeing with their faith unto God. This was the first emigration that occurred in Islam. The first Muslim to depart was Uthman b. Affan along with his wife Ruqayya, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS).'
lessons: When facing persecution and hardship, seeking a place of safety is a legitimate and wise response, not a sign of weakness.
True leadership involves recognizing the limits of one's ability to protect others and guiding them toward safety rather than forcing them to endure avoidable harm.
God provides relief from trials, and emigrating for one's faith is an act of trust that relief will come in due time.
practical_advice: When your current environment is causing you serious harm and you have the means to leave, consider whether relocating — even temporarily — might be the healthiest choice for your wellbeing.
If you feel trapped in a difficult situation, look for a trusted guide or mentor who can help you identify a safer path forward, just as the Prophet directed his companions.
Remind yourself that enduring hardship for a higher purpose, while also seeking practical solutions, is not a contradiction — it is wisdom.

title: Uthman and Ruqayya: The First Family in Exile
summary: Uthman b. Affan and his wife Ruqayya, the Prophet's daughter, were the first to depart for Abyssinia. The Prophet received news of them only when a Quraysh woman described seeing them traveling humbly — Uthman leading an old donkey carrying Ruqayya.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related, similarly, from a hadith of Ya'qub b. Sufyan, from Abbas al-Anbari, from Bishr b. Musa, from al-Hasan b. Ziyad al-Burjumi, who said, 'Qatada stated to us, "The first man to emigrate to God Almighty along with his family was Uthman b. Affan, God be pleased with him." I heard al-Nadr b. Anas state, "I heard Abu Hamza (by whom he meant Anas b. Malik) say, 'Uthman b. Affan along with his wife Ruqayya, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), left for Abyssinia. A long time elapsed without the Messenger of God (SAAS), having news of them, until a Quraysh woman came to him and said, "O Muhammad, I saw your son-in-law with his wife in his company." "In what state did you see them?" he asked. She replied, "I saw him leading a decrepit old donkey on which his wife was mounted." The Messenger of God (SAAS), exclaimed, "May God be with them both! Uthman is the first man since Lot, upon whom be peace, to take himself and his family into exile."'"'
lessons: Sacrifice and hardship in the path of faith can strip away worldly comfort, yet such simplicity becomes a mark of honor rather than shame.
A long silence or lack of news from loved ones who are in difficulty can cause great anxiety, but trust in God's protection sustains the heart during such waiting.
The Prophet's compassionate response — praying for Uthman and honoring his sacrifice — shows that recognizing others' hardship and affirming them is itself an act of leadership.
practical_advice: When separated from loved ones and worried about their wellbeing, channel that anxiety into sincere prayer and trust rather than rumination.
If you are going through a period of difficulty and feel unrecognized or forgotten, remember that your sacrifice is seen by God even when others are unaware.
Practice honoring and verbally affirming the sacrifices of those around you, as the Prophet's words of recognition for Uthman offered a form of emotional validation that endures through history.

title: Ja'far b. Abi Talib and the Second Group of Emigrants to Abyssinia
summary: After the initial migration, more Muslims followed to Abyssinia in a second wave. Ja'far b. Abi Talib, who would later serve as their spokesman before the Negus, was among this group, accompanied by his wife Asma bint Umays.
story: Then Ja'far b. Abu Talib left, accompanied by his wife Asma', daughter of Umays. Abd Allah b. Ja'far was born to him in Abyssinia. The Muslims followed on after one another, eventually being reunited in Abyssinia. Musa b. Uqba has claimed that the first migration to Abyssinia occurred when Abu Talib and those who had allied with him in support of the Messenger of God (SAAS), went into the shi'b. There are differing views on this. But God knows best. He also claimed that the departure of Ja'far b. Abu Talib came only in the second migration to Abyssinia, after the return of some of those who had gone there initially. (They returned) when they heard that the polytheists had accepted Islam and were praying. When they arrived in Mecca, Uthman b. Maz'un among them, they did not find it true that the polytheists had accepted Islam, as they had been told. Some of them then returned to Abyssinia, while others remained in Mecca. Other Muslims also went to Abyssinia at the time of the second migration. Musa b. Uqba stated, 'Ja'far b. Abu Talib was among those who left the second time.' But Ibn Ishaq's referring to his having gone on the first migration is very plain, as will be shown. But God knows best. However, he clearly was in a second group of the emigrants who went on the first migration; he acted as their spokesman and translator before the Negus and others.
lessons: False rumors and misinformation can cause people to make hasty decisions, such as the Muslims who returned prematurely believing the persecutors had converted; verifying news before acting is essential.
When one path closes or proves mistaken, returning to a place of safety rather than persisting in a dangerous mistake demonstrates wisdom and humility.
God places the right people in the right roles at the right time — Ja'far's presence among the emigrants was providential, as his eloquence before the Negus would prove decisive for the community.
practical_advice: Before acting on news that seems too good to be true — especially news that would lead you to abandon a safe situation — take time to verify it through reliable sources.
If you have made a decision based on false information and find yourself in a difficult position, do not let pride prevent you from correcting course and returning to safety.
Recognize that your particular gifts and abilities may be exactly what your community needs in a moment of crisis — stay present and prepared so you can serve when called upon.

title: The Migration to Abyssinia: The Full List of Emigrants
summary: Ibn Ishaq records the names of the approximately 83 Muslims who migrated from Mecca to Abyssinia to escape persecution, listing the families, couples, children, and allies who made the journey.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The number of those Muslims who migrated and went to Abyssinia, not counting the little children and those born there, totalled 83. This assumes that 'Ammar b. Yasir was among them, but there is some doubt about him."
lessons: When persecution becomes unbearable, seeking refuge is a dignified and sanctioned response — leaving a place of harm is not defeat but wisdom.
Community is preserved through collective action; the emigrants did not flee alone but supported one another, bringing families and allies together.
The willingness to leave behind everything familiar — home, property, social standing — for the sake of faith reflects a profound level of conviction and trust in God.
practical_advice: If you are in an environment that is actively harming your wellbeing, recognize that removing yourself from that environment is a valid and courageous choice, not a failure.
When facing a major life upheaval, lean on your community — identify the people around you who share your values and make plans together rather than in isolation.
Make a list of what truly matters to you at your core. When you feel overwhelmed by what you are losing, returning to that list can reground you in what you are moving toward.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib Speaks Before the Negus of Abyssinia
summary: When Quraysh sent emissaries to the Negus of Abyssinia demanding the return of the Muslim refugees, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib courageously spoke on behalf of the Muslims, explaining Islam's teachings on monotheism, prayer, almsgiving, and the status of Jesus, upon which the Negus declared his sympathy with the Muslims and refused to hand them over.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, "Hasan b. Masa related to us that he heard Hudayj, brother of Zuhayr b. Muswiya say, on the authority of Abu Ishaq, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Utba, from Ibn Mas'ud, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us, a body of some 80 men, to the Negus. These included 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, Ja'far, 'Abd Allah b. Arfata, 'Uthman b. Maz'an, and Abu Musa. These men went to the Negus. "'Then Quraysh dispatched to him 'Amr b. al-'As and 'Umara b. al-Walid with a gift. "'When they went in to the Negus, they bowed down before him. Then they took up positions to the left and right before him and said, "A group of our people have taken up residence in your land, after having turned away from us and from our religion." "'"Where are they?" he asked. "'"In your land. Send for them," they suggested. "'He did so and Ja'far said, "I will be your spokesman today." And they followed him. "'He made salutation, but did not bow down. They asked him, "What is wrong with you, not bowing down before the king?" "'He replied, "We bow down only before God, the Almighty and Glorious." "'"What is this all about?" he was asked. "'He answered, "God has sent a messenger to us and told us to bow down to no one but God, the Almighty and Glorious. He also ordered us to pray, and to give alms." "'"Amr commented, "They differ with you about Jesus, son of Mary." "'He (the Negus) asked, "What do you say about Jesus son of Mary and His mother?" "'He (Ja'far) replied, "We say as God did: He is His word and His spirit which He cast into the Virgin whom no man had touched and no child had been inside." "'The king then picked up a stick from the ground and said, "O people of Abyssinia, and you, priests and monks; by God, they are not so much as the size of this stick away from what we say! Welcome to you and greetings to him from whom you come! I bear witness that he is the Messenger of God, and that it is him we find in the Bible. He is the Messenger of whom Jesus, son of Mary, made prophecy. You may reside wherever you wish; and, by God, were it not for my role as king, I would go to him so that I could be the one who carries his shoes for him!" "'He then commanded that the present the two messengers had brought be returned to them."'
lessons: Speaking truth with clarity and composure in a hostile setting — as Ja'far did before the Negus — can change the outcome entirely; preparation and conviction matter more than political maneuvering.
When you represent others who are vulnerable, taking responsibility as a spokesperson is an act of profound service and courage.
Justice can be found in unexpected places; the Negus, a non-Muslim king, recognized the truth and protected the believers, reminding us that God can open doors of mercy through any means He chooses.
practical_advice: When you must advocate for yourself or others in a high-stakes situation, prepare by knowing your core message clearly — Ja'far distilled the essence of his faith into a few calm, precise sentences.
Do not assume that those in positions of power will automatically be adversarial; present your case honestly and let the truth speak for itself.
In moments of great anxiety before a confrontation, remind yourself that your role is to convey the truth faithfully — the outcome is in God's hands, not yours.

title: The Negus Returns the Qurayshi Gift and Grants the Muslims Refuge
summary: After hearing Ja'far's explanation of Islam and the Islamic position on Jesus, the Negus of Abyssinia sided with the Muslim refugees, returned the gifts brought by Quraysh's envoys, and granted the Muslims full permission to stay in his land in safety.
story: "He then commanded that the present the two messengers had brought be returned to them. "'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud hurried forth thereafter, and lived to take part in the battle of Badr. "'He claimed that the Prophet (SAAS) prayed for forgiveness for him (the king) when news of his death reached him.'" The chain of authorities for this is excellent, and the narrative is good. It gives evidence that Abu Musa was among those who migrated from Mecca to Abyssinia, even though this hadith is not recorded by some authorities.
lessons: An act of justice from a person of authority — like the Negus returning the gift and granting safety — can be a turning point that sustains an entire community; never underestimate the power of one fair decision.
The Prophet's prayer for the Negus after his death illustrates that gratitude should be expressed even when the person is no longer present to receive it, and that goodness is recognized and honored.
God's protection of the believers came through the goodwill of a just ruler, reminding us that divine care often operates through ordinary human channels.
practical_advice: When someone extends unexpected kindness or protection to you during a difficult time, acknowledge it deeply — gratitude reinforces the bonds that sustain us through hardship.
If you are in a position of leadership or influence, even in a small context, recognize that a single fair and courageous decision on your part can change the trajectory of people's lives.
When you have done what you can and placed your trust in God, practice releasing the outcome — the refugees had no control over the Negus's decision, but they had done everything right on their end.

title: The Second Account of Ja'far Before the Negus: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari's Narration
summary: In a second, more detailed narration transmitted through Abu Musa al-Ash'ari, the encounter between the Muslim refugees and the Negus is recounted, including Ja'far's declaration that Muhammad is the messenger prophesied by Jesus, the Negus's powerful affirmation, and the humiliation of the Qurayshi envoys.
story: Abu Nu'aym stated in Dala'il (The Signs): "Also, Abu Ahmad related to us, from 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Shirawayh, from Ishaq b. Ibrahim, he being Ibn Rahawayh, from 'Ubayd Allah b. Musa, from Isra'il, from Abu Ishaq, from Abu Burda, from Abu Musa, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered us to depart with Ja'far b. Abi Talib, to the land of the Negus.' "Quraysh heard of this and so they sent 'Amr b. al-'As and 'Umara b. al-Walid, having obtained a gift for the Negus. "They went in to the Negus with the present, which he accepted, and they made obeisance before him. "Then 'Amr b. al-'As said, 'Certain people from our land have abandoned our religion. They are here in your country.' "'In my country?' the Negus asked. "'Yes,' they replied. "So he sent for us and Ja'far told us, 'Let none of you speak. I will be your spokesman today.' "And so we went to the Negus when he was seated on his throne, with 'Amr b. al-'As on his right, 'Umara on his left, and the priests seated in two ranks. 'Amr and 'Umara had told the king, 'They will not make obeisance to you.' "When we arrived, some of his priests and monks there with him hurried to us and told us to bow down before the king. But Ja'far replied, 'We bow down only before God, the Almighty and Glorious.' "When we reached the Negus, he asked, 'What prevents you from bowing down?' "Ja'far replied, 'We bow down only before God.' "The Negus then asked him, 'Well, what is this all about?' "Ja'far explained, 'God sent a messenger among us; he is that same Messenger Jesus, son of Mary, God's prayers and peace be upon Him, said would come after himself, his name being "Ahmad". He ordered us to worship God and to associate no god with Him, to perform the prayers, to pay alms, to be good, and to abstain from what is forbidden.' "His speech astonished the Negus. "When 'Amr b. al-'As saw this, he exclaimed, 'May God save the king! They disagree with you concerning Jesus, son of Mary.' "The Negus then asked Ja'far, 'What does your master say about the son of Mary?' "Ja'far replied, 'He says of Him what God stated — that he is God's spirit and His word, and that He brought him forth from the Virgin whom no man had approached and in whom there had been no child.' "The Negus then picked up a stick from the ground and raised it up, saying, 'Ye priests and monks, these people do not add so much as the weight of this to what we say about the son of Mary.' "'Welcome to you,' he continued, 'and greetings to him from whom you have come. I bear witness that he is the Messenger of God and that it is he of whom Jesus made prophecy. Were it not for the duties of my kingdom, I would go to him to kiss his shoes. Stay in my land as long as you wish.' "He then ordered that food and fine garments be brought for us. And he said, 'Give back to those other two their gift.'"
lessons: Coordinated, unified representation under a trusted and prepared spokesperson — as the Muslims did with Ja'far — is far more effective than speaking individually from anxiety or impulse.
Truth articulated with clarity has a power that political gifts and manipulation cannot overcome; the Negus was moved not by the Qurayshi presents but by the honesty of Ja'far's words.
Maintaining your principles publicly, even under social pressure such as the expectation to bow, is a form of quiet courage that commands more respect than compliance born of fear.
practical_advice: Before entering a difficult conversation or confrontation, agree with your support network on who will speak and what the core message is — clarity of purpose reduces anxiety for everyone involved.
When others attempt to undermine you through misdirection or by highlighting your differences, stay grounded in what you actually believe and say it plainly; do not get drawn into their framing.
Recognize that standing firm on a principle — even a small, symbolic one like refusing to bow — can itself be a powerful testimony that opens hearts and changes minds.

title: 'Amr ibn al-'As Betrays 'Umara to the Negus Out of Personal Grudge
summary: After the Qurayshi envoys failed in their mission before the Negus, 'Amr ibn al-'As, nursing a personal grudge against his companion 'Umara from an incident at sea, whispered a slanderous accusation to the Negus that resulted in 'Umara's bizarre punishment.
story: 'Amr b. al-'As was short, while 'Umara was a handsome man. While at sea, they had been drinking. 'Amr had his wife with him and, after they had been imbibing, 'Umara said to 'Amr, 'Tell your wife to kiss me!' 'Amr replied, 'You should be ashamed!' Then 'Umara picked up 'Amr and flung him into the sea. 'Amr kept calling out to 'Umara until he brought him back on to the boat. "'Amr hated him for this, and told the Negus, 'If you were to leave, 'Umara would take your place with your wife.' At that the Negus summoned 'Umara and had air blown into his urethra so that he flew away with the wild birds."
lessons: Harboring resentment and seeking revenge — as 'Amr did against 'Umara — ultimately destroys the one who carries the grudge as much as it harms others; bitterness is a poison that corrodes judgment.
The consequences of indulging in forbidden behavior, as illustrated by the drunken scene at sea, ripple outward in unpredictable and damaging ways long after the moment has passed.
When people are united in a wrong cause, as the Qurayshi envoys were, internal rivalry and moral failure will eventually undermine them from within.
practical_advice: When someone has wronged or humiliated you, pause before acting on the impulse for revenge — ask yourself whether the action you are considering reflects your values or merely your wounded pride.

Examine whether unresolved grievances from the past are currently shaping decisions you are making, and seek to process those feelings in a constructive way rather than letting them drive harmful behavior.
Avoid placing yourself in compromising situations with people whose moral compass differs significantly from yours, as such environments can drag you into choices you will deeply regret.

title: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari's Journey to Abyssinia and the Two Migrations
summary: Abu Musa al-Ash'ari and some fifty companions from Yemen learned of the Prophet's migration and sailed to Abyssinia, where they found Ja'far ibn Abi Talib already residing. They remained with him until joining the Prophet at the time of Khaybar, earning the distinction of two migrations.
story: This chain of authorities is good; what it apparently shows is that Abū Mūsā was at Mecca, and that he did go with Ja'far, son of Abū Tālib, to Abyssinia. What is true according to Yazid b. 'Abd Allāh b. Abū Burda, on the authority of his grandfather Abū Burda, from Abū Mūsā, is that the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS) became known to them while they were in Yemen. They therefore went out into exile along with some 50 persons in a boat that took them to the Negus in Abyssinia. They found that Ja'far b. Abū Tālib and his companions were already living there. Ja'far told them to stay, and they did stay with him until they joined the Messenger of God (SAAS), at the time of Khaybar. He stated, 'Abū Mūsā witnessed what took place between Ja'far and the Negus and reported this.' This is how al-Bukhārī related this in his chapter on the migration to Abyssinia: 'Muhammad b. al-'Alā' related to us, quoting Abū Usāma, quoting Burayd b. 'Abd Allāh from Abū Burda, from Abū Mūsā, who said, 'The departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS) became known to us while we were in Yemen. So we embarked on a boat which took us to the Negus, in Abyssinia. There we found Ja'far b. Abū Tālib, God bless him, and we stayed there with him until we left and joined the Prophet (SAAS), when Khaybar was conquered. And so the Prophet said, 'For you people of the boat there are two migrations.'''
lessons: Sincere commitment to faith may require leaving behind familiar comforts, yet God honors every sacrifice made for His sake.
Community and brotherhood in exile can sustain the believer; finding fellow Muslims in a foreign land is itself a mercy from God.
Every hardship endured in the path of truth carries its own unique reward, as the Prophet's words about 'two migrations' remind us that God does not overlook any difficulty borne for His sake.
practical_advice: When you feel isolated or far from your community, actively seek out others who share your values — their presence can become a source of immense strength.
Keep a record or memory of the sacrifices you have made for your faith; revisiting them during difficult times can renew your resolve and sense of purpose.
Trust that periods of waiting and uncertainty, like the companions' stay in Abyssinia, are not wasted — they are often seasons of preparation for a greater role ahead.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib's First Audience with the Negus and the Quraysh Envoys
summary: Quraysh sent Amr ibn al-As and Umarah ibn al-Walid to the Negus demanding the return of the Muslim refugees. Ja'far ibn Abi Talib spoke on behalf of the Muslims, explaining their faith, and the Negus refused to hand them over without hearing their side.
story: Quraysh sent 'Amr b. al-'Ās and 'Umāra b. al-Walīd with a gift from Abū Sufyān to the Negus. They told him — and we were there with him — 'Some low-class and silly people of ours have come to you. So hand them over to us!' He replied, 'No, not until I hear what they have to say.' So he sent to us and asked, 'What's all this they say?' We replied, 'These are people who worship idols. God sent a messenger to us in whom we believed and trusted.' The Negus then asked them, 'Are these people slaves of yours?' 'No,' they replied. 'Do they have debts owing to you?' he then asked. 'No,' they answered. 'Then leave them alone,' he said. At this we left his presence, but 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ told him, 'These people speak different things of Jesus from what you do.' He replied, 'If they do not say about Jesus as I do, I will not allow them to stay in my land a single hour of the day!' And so he sent for us, this second invitation being harder upon us than the first. The Negus asked, 'What does your master say about Jesus, son of Mary?' We replied, 'He says that He is the spirit of God and His word that He cast to a chaste virgin.' He then dispatched a messenger, saying, 'Summon to me such-and-such a priest, such-and-such a monk.' Some men of theirs came and he asked, 'What do you say concerning Jesus, son of Mary?' They replied, 'You are the most knowledgeable of us; what do you say?' The Negus replied, having picked something up from the ground, 'Jesus is no more different from what these people say than this much.' He then asked, 'Is anyone harming you people?' They replied, 'Yes.' So then a crier announced, 'Whoever harms any one of them is to be fined four dirhams.' Then he asked, 'Is this enough for you?' We replied, 'No.' So the king doubled the fine.
lessons: Speaking the truth with clarity and composure, even before powerful authorities, is a form of courage that God can turn into protection.
A just ruler who insists on hearing all sides before making a judgment is a mercy to the oppressed; we should always seek fair arbiters in our disputes.
The Muslims' honest description of their faith — without exaggeration or defensiveness — won them protection; integrity is its own shield.
practical_advice: When facing accusations or pressure from others, resist the urge to panic. Prepare your words carefully and speak the truth of your situation simply and clearly.
If you are in a difficult situation where others are trying to turn people against you, focus on what you can control: your honesty and your composure.
Seek out just and fair-minded people to help mediate conflicts in your life rather than trying to fight every battle alone.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib's Return from Abyssinia and Reunion with the Prophet at Khaybar
summary: After years in Abyssinia, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib and the Muslim refugees returned to the Prophet, arriving at the same time as the victory at Khaybar. The Prophet embraced Ja'far and expressed that he did not know which of the two joys — the victory or Ja'far's arrival — pleased him more.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) emigrated to Medina and proclaimed himself there, we told him (the Negus) 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has proclaimed himself and gone to Medina; he has killed those we told you about. We wish to travel to him, so send us back.' 'Yes, I will,' he replied. He gave us mounts and provisioned us, then said, 'Inform your master how I have treated you. This man, my friend, will go with you. I bear witness that there is no god but God and that he is the Messenger of God. Ask him to pray for forgiveness for me.' So we left and went to Medina where the Messenger of God (SAAS) met us. He embraced me, then said, 'I don't know what pleases me more — the victory at Khaybar, or the arrival of Ja'far!' This coincided with the victory at Khaybar. He then sat down. The envoy of the Negus then said, 'This man, Ja'far, ask him how our master treated him.' So he, Ja'far, said, 'Yes, he acted in such-and-such a way, then gave us mounts and provisions and bore witness that there is no god but God and that you are the Messenger of God. He also told me, 'Ask him to pray for forgiveness for me.'' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose, performed the prayer ablutions and prayed three times: 'O God, forgive the Negus!' And the Muslims present said, 'Amen'. Then Ja'far said, 'I then told the envoy of the Negus, 'Leave now, and tell your master what you saw of the Messenger of God (SAAS).''
lessons: Long separations from those we love for the sake of a higher purpose make the reunion all the sweeter; God does not forget the sacrifices of those who strive in His path.
The Prophet's love for his companions was deep and personal; nurturing genuine bonds of brotherhood and care is a prophetic value.
Interceding in prayer for those who have shown us kindness — as the Prophet prayed for the Negus — is a noble and lasting form of gratitude.
practical_advice: When reuniting with someone after a long and difficult separation, allow yourself to fully feel and express that joy — it is a human and spiritually healthy response.
Make a habit of praying for those who have helped you or shown you kindness, even if they are not of your faith or community; this is a form of gratitude that transcends barriers.

Remind yourself during long periods of hardship and waiting that the reunion — with loved ones, with ease, with God's mercy — is coming, and its sweetness will be proportional to the patience you showed.

title: Umm Salama's Account: The Prophet's Counsel to Migrate to Abyssinia
summary: As persecution of Muslims in Mecca intensified, the Prophet, unable to protect his followers, counseled them to migrate to Abyssinia where a just king would offer them safety and freedom to practice their faith.
story: When events turned threatening and even violent in Mecca for the supporters of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and actual harm was done to them because of their faith, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was unable to defend them, being himself under the protection of his family and his uncle, so that he experienced none of their sufferings, he told them, 'In Abyssinia there is a king who allows no one to be persecuted in his domain. So go to his country until such time as God provides for you some relief and solution to your troubles.' And so we did set out in groups and rejoined one another there. We found hospitality, friendship and security to engage in our religion in Abyssinia, and had no fear of harm.
lessons: The Prophet's wisdom in directing his companions toward safety rather than needless confrontation teaches us that protecting life and faith sometimes requires strategic retreat.
God's relief does not always come immediately or in the form we expect; sometimes it comes in the form of a distant land, a stranger's hospitality, and a season of quiet.
A leader who cannot personally shield those he loves still serves them faithfully by guiding them toward the best available path — this is a form of love and responsibility.
practical_advice: When you are overwhelmed and cannot see a way forward in your current situation, actively look for environments — communities, spaces, relationships — where you can practice your values without fear.
Do not wait for a perfect solution before seeking relief; sometimes moving toward a good enough refuge is the wisest and most faithful step.
If you are in a leadership or caregiving role and feel helpless to protect someone, focus on what guidance and resources you can offer rather than dwelling on what is beyond your control.

title: The Quraysh Envoys' Plot to Have the Muslims Expelled from Abyssinia
summary: Enraged that the Muslims had found safety in Abyssinia, Quraysh sent Amr ibn al-As and Abdullah ibn Abi Rabi'a with gifts for the Negus and his generals, intending to bribe their way to having the refugees expelled without the king even speaking to them.
story: When Quraysh saw that we had gained accommodation and security there, they were angered and agreed to make representations to the Negus to remove us from his country and send us back to them. They therefore dispatched 'Amr b. al-'Āṣ and 'Abd Allāh b. Abū Rabī'a. They gathered gifts for the king and for all his generals, without exception. They told their two envoys, 'Give a present to each general before you speak among them, and then give the king his gifts. And if you are able to have him hand over the exiles to you without him speaking to them, then do so.' The envoys did go to the king, not having omitted giving the presents to each of his generals. They then addressed them, saying, 'We have only come to see the king about some fools of ours who have abandoned their people and their religion, and who have not joined your faith. Their people have sent us to have the king return them to them. When we speak with him, then indicate that he should do so.' They agreed to this. The envoys then gave their gifts to the Negus. The most favoured of their gifts from Mecca to him were leather products. Mūsā b. 'Uqba related that they presented to him a mare, a gown and a silk garment. When they took their gifts in to him, they told him, 'O king, some of our foolish young have abandoned their people's religion and have not entered your faith. They have come here with a fabricated religion we do not know and have taken refuge in your land. Their tribes, their own fathers and uncles, have sent us to you so that you will return these people to them. They know them best. They will not adopt your faith, and you should prevent them from doing so.'
lessons: Those who oppose truth often resort to manipulation, bribery, and deception; recognizing these tactics protects us from being deceived or discouraged by them.
The believer's best response to political scheming against them is not counter-scheming but integrity and trust in God, who can turn the hearts of even kings.
Oppressors often frame their victims as the problem — as 'fools' or deviants — but a just authority, like the Negus, looks past the framing to the facts.
practical_advice: When others are working behind the scenes to undermine you, resist the urge to retaliate with the same tactics; focus instead on presenting your truth with clarity and dignity.
Be aware that people who wish you harm may try to shape how others perceive you before you even get a chance to speak; prepare yourself to address those narratives calmly when the opportunity comes.
Cultivate relationships with fair-minded people and communities so that when you are misrepresented, there are those willing to hear your side before passing judgment.

title: The Negus Refuses to Surrender the Muslims Without Hearing Them
summary: Despite pressure from his own generals and the Quraysh envoys, the Negus insisted on hearing the Muslim refugees speak for themselves before making any decision about their fate, demonstrating his commitment to justice.
story: The king was angered at this and replied, 'No! By God's life, I will not return them to them before summoning these people and talking to them to discover their views. They are a group who have taken refuge in my land and have chosen my company above that of all others. If they are indeed as your people say, I will return them to them. But if they are otherwise, I will protect them, give their people no access to them and no satisfaction.' Mūsā b. 'Uqba related that his generals indicated to him that he should return them to their people, but the king refused, saying, 'No! By God, I will not, not until I hear what they have to say and what their position is.'
lessons: Justice requires hearing all parties before rendering a judgment; this principle, honored by the Negus, is a universal human value and a divine command.
God can place the hearts of the righteous — even among non-Muslims — in a position of protection over the believers when they are most vulnerable.
Standing firm against peer pressure, even from one's own advisors, in defense of what is right is a mark of true leadership and nobility of character.
practical_advice: Before forming an opinion about someone based on what others have told you, make a genuine effort to hear their perspective directly.
When you feel that others are deciding your fate without your input, remember the Negus's example and advocate respectfully but firmly for your right to be heard.
If you are in a position of authority or influence, practice the discipline of suspending judgment until you have gathered full information, especially when the vulnerable are involved.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib Presents Islam to the Negus: Faith, Jesus, and the Muslim Greeting
summary: Brought before the Negus for a second, more fraught audience, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib eloquently described the essence of Islam, the Muslim understanding of Jesus as the spirit and word of God, and the reason Muslims greet with 'peace' rather than bowing — winning the king's protection.
story: When the exiles came before him, they greeted him by saying, 'Peace be upon you', but they did not bow down before him. He addressed them, 'Will you not tell me why you do not greet me in the same manner as others of your people who have come to me? And tell me what it is you say about Jesus and what your religion is. Are you Christians?' 'No,' they replied. 'Are you Jews then?' 'No.' 'Do you worship in your people's religion?' 'No.' 'What is your religion?' 'It is Islam.' 'What is Islam?' 'We worship God,' they replied, 'and we associate no other god with Him.' 'Who was it brought you this faith?' 'A man from among ourselves, whose face and genealogy we know. God sent him to us as He sent messengers to those who came before us. He ordered us to behave with kindness, honesty, trustworthiness and good faith. He forbad us to worship idols and ordered us to worship God alone who has no associate. We believed him and recognised God's words. We realized that what he presented came from God. But when we did all this our people behaved with enmity towards us and towards the truthful Prophet; they disbelieved in him and wanted to kill him. They wanted us to worship idols, and so we fled to you, bringing our faith and our blood to you away from our own people.' 'By God,' he exclaimed, 'this is the very niche from which Moses' affair emerged!' 'And regarding our greeting,' Ja'far explained, 'the Messenger of God (SAAS) told us'
lessons: Clarity, honesty, and simplicity in explaining one's faith is more powerful than elaborate rhetoric; Ja'far's straightforward account of Islam moved a king.
Being willing to stand apart from social customs — as the Muslims did by not bowing — and explain why with calm confidence is a mark of principled identity.
The core of Islam — worshipping God alone, living with honesty and trustworthiness — is a message that resonates across cultures and faiths when presented with sincerity.
practical_advice: When asked to explain your beliefs or values to someone unfamiliar with them, focus on the essentials: what you believe, how it shapes how you live, and why it matters to you personally.
Do not be ashamed of what makes you different from those around you; be prepared to explain your practices with warmth and without defensiveness.
When you are in a vulnerable or unfamiliar situation, ground yourself in the clarity of what you believe and who you are — that inner clarity will come through in how you speak and carry yourself.

title: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib's Defense Before the Negus: The Speech on Islam and Jesus
summary: Ja'far ibn Abi Talib eloquently defended Islam before the Negus (King of Abyssinia), describing how the Prophet reformed the Muslims and explaining the Islamic view of Jesus. The Negus was moved to tears and refused to hand the refugees over to the Qurayshi envoys.
story: who has no associate. We believed him and recognised God's words. We realized that what he presented came from God. But when we did all this our people behaved with enmity towards us and towards the truthful Prophet; they disbelieved in him and wanted to kill him. They wanted us to worship idols, and so we fled to you, bringing our faith and our blood to you away from our own people.' "'By God,' he exclaimed, 'this is the very niche from which Moses' affair emerged! ' "'And regarding our greeting,' Jacfar explained, 'the Messenger of God (SAAS) told us that the people of paradise greet one another with a wish for peace and he ordered us to do the same. We greeted you the same way we greet one another. "'And regarding Jesus, son of Mary, he was the servant and messenger of God, His word that He cast to Mary and also His spirit. He was the son of the chaste Virgin.' "The king then picked up a stick and said, 'By God, Mary's son did not add to this the weight of this stick more!' "The Abyssinian leaders then said, 'By God, if the Abyssinians heard this they would depose you!' "'By God,' he responded, 'I will never say anything about Jesus different from this; God did not acquiesce to the people about me when He restored my kingdom to me. Shall I then obey the people concerning God's religion? God forbid such a thing! '" Yiinus stated, from Thn Iqhaq, "And so the Negus sent for them and assembled them, nothing being more distasteful to 'Amr b. al-'A$ and 'Abd Allah b. Abn Rabi'a than that he should hear their words. "When the Negus's messenger came to them, the exiles assembled and asked one another what to say. "'What can we say?' they asked, and decided, 'By God, we will tell what we know and all about the religion we follow, what it was our Prophet (SAAS) brought to us, come what may! ' "When they went in to him their spokesman was Ja'far b. Aba Tzlib, God be pleased with him. The Negus asked him, 'What is this religion you follow? Have you left the faith of your people without entering either Judaism or Christianity?' "Ja'far replied, '0king, we were a people of polytheism. We worshipped idols, ate the meat of animals that had died, offended rules of hospitality and permitted things forbidden, as in the shedding of one another's blood and so on. We completely ignored matters of right and wrong. And so God sent to us a prophet, from among ourselves, whose honesty and trustworthiness we knew well. He summoned us to pray to God alone and without associate, told us to respect rights of kinship, to honour rights of hospitality, to pray to God the Almighty and Glorious, to fast for Him and to worship none other than Him.'" Ziyad stated, from Ibn Ishaq, "And so he called us to God, to affirm His oneness, to worship Him, and to tear down all the other stones and idols we and our forefathers had worshipped apart from Him. He ordered us to be truthful in our speech, to keep to our trust, to respect kinship ties and hospitality rights and to abandon things forbidden and the shedding of blood. He forbad us to do anything immoral, to tell lies, to misuse the funds of orphans, or to make false accusations against women of virtue. He ordered us to worship God and to associate no other god with Him. He told us to pray, to give alms and to fast." He stated, "And he ennumerated for him all aspects of Islam. "And so we believed in him and trusted him, following him in the instructions he brought from God. We worshipped God alone, without partner and associating no one with Him. We forbad what he had forbidden and considered permissible what he allowed us. "But our people aggressed against us and harmed us, seeking to draw us out of our faith, to return us to the worship of idols instead of God, and to have us again consider permissible the abominations we had previously allowed. "When they treated us with violence and persecution, besieged us and prevented us from performing our religion, we left for your country and chose you above all others. We desired your hospitality and hoped we would not be harmed in your domain, 0 king." She (Umm Salama) went on to state, "The Negus replied, 'Did you bring anything with you from what he brought?' "Jacfar then recited to him a passage from KHY'S (swat Muyam: XIX) and, I swear, the Negus wept so hard his beard was soaked! And all his bishops cried so hard they wet their bibles! "He then said, 'These words certainly come from the niche that Moses brought with him! Pass on, rightly guided! No, by God, I will not return them to you and I will not give you satisfaction.' "So we did leave his presence. The more sensible of the two envoys, in our view, was 'Abd Allah b. Rabi'a, for 'Amr b. al-'AS said, 'By God, I will go to him tomorrow and tell him something that will turn them all upside down: I will tell him that they claim that the god he worships, Jesus, son of Mary, is a servant (of God)!' But 'Abd Allah b. Rabi'a told him, 'No, don't do that; even if they have opposed us, they are related to us and have a certain right.' "But he insisted, 'By God, I will do it!' "The next day he went in to the king and said, '0 king, they say terrible things about Jesus. Send for them and question them about him.' "He did indeed do so, and nothing like this had happened to us before. We asked one another what we should say about Jesus if he were to ask about him. All agreed that we should definitely say about him what God had said and our Prophet had told us to say. "The exiles then went in to the king who had his generals with him. He asked, 'What is it you say about Jesus, son of Mary?' "Ja'far replied, 'We say he is God's servant, His messenger, His spirit and His word that He cast into Mary, the chaste Virgin.' "The Negus stretched his hand down to the ground and picked up a stick between two fingers, saying, 'Jesus, son of Mary is not more than the size of this little stick different from what you say.' "His generals grumbled at this and he responded, 'Grumble away, by God! You people may leave now and you are shuyGm in my land! (The word shuyam means in their language: those who are secure, i.e. those whose life is safe.) Whoever reviles you will be fined! (He repeated this phrase three times.) I'd rather not have dabr than that I harmed one man among you! (Dubr in their language means: gold.)'" Ziyad stated, on the authority of Ibn Ishaq, that what he said was, "I'd rather not have a dabr of gold." Ibn Hishzm stated that others say the word zabr was used, meaning a mountain in their language. The Negus then said, "I swear, God took no bribe from me when He returned my kingdom to me. And He did not acquiesce to the people about me; so am I to acquiesce to them about him? Return their presents to the two envoys. I don't need them. And, you two, get out of my country!" The two men did depart in disgrace, with what they had brought having been returned to them. Umm Salama continued, "And so we stayed on there in comfort and security."
lessons: Speaking the truth with courage, even under immense pressure, can move the hearts of those who sincerely seek it — as Ja'far's honest words moved the Negus to tears.
When facing an unjust accusation or a hostile audience, trust in God and commit to saying only what is true and divinely guided, rather than what is politically convenient.
Divine protection can come from unexpected sources; God can soften the heart of a just ruler to shelter those who have no worldly power of their own.
practical_advice: When you are pressured to compromise your values or identity in order to gain approval, pause and ask yourself what a person of integrity would say — then say that, regardless of consequence.
If someone attempts to provoke conflict between you and others through distortion or manipulation, respond with clarity and calm truth rather than defensiveness or retaliation.

When you feel surrounded by hostility with no way out, remember that God can open doors of safety through people and places you never anticipated — this can be a source of hope when your situation feels impossible.

title: 'Amr ibn al-'As Attempts a Second Scheme Against the Muslim Refugees
summary: After failing to convince the Negus to return the Muslim refugees, 'Amr ibn al-'As devised a second plot to turn the king against the Muslims by raising the question of their belief about Jesus. The Muslims feared the worst but committed to speaking only the truth, and the Negus again vindicated them.
story: "So we did leave his presence. The more sensible of the two envoys, in our view, was 'Abd Allah b. Rabi'a, for 'Amr b. al-'AS said, 'By God, I will go to him tomorrow and tell him something that will turn them all upside down: I will tell him that they claim that the god he worships, Jesus, son of Mary, is a servant (of God)!' But 'Abd Allah b. Rabi'a told him, 'No, don't do that; even if they have opposed us, they are related to us and have a certain right.' "But he insisted, 'By God, I will do it!' "The next day he went in to the king and said, '0 king, they say terrible things about Jesus. Send for them and question them about him.' "He did indeed do so, and nothing like this had happened to us before. We asked one another what we should say about Jesus if he were to ask about him. All agreed that we should definitely say about him what God had said and our Prophet had told us to say. "The exiles then went in to the king who had his generals with him. He asked, 'What is it you say about Jesus, son of Mary?' "Ja'far replied, 'We say he is God's servant, His messenger, His spirit and His word that He cast into Mary, the chaste Virgin.' "The Negus stretched his hand down to the ground and picked up a stick between two fingers, saying, 'Jesus, son of Mary is not more than the size of this little stick different from what you say.' "His generals grumbled at this and he responded, 'Grumble away, by God! You people may leave now and you are shuyGm in my land! (The word shuyam means in their language: those who are secure, i.e. those whose life is safe.) Whoever reviles you will be fined! (He repeated this phrase three times.) I'd rather not have dabr than that I harmed one man among you! (Dubr in their language means: gold.)'"
lessons: When others attempt to manipulate a situation to harm you, remaining steadfast in truth is both a moral duty and often the most effective form of self-protection.
Not all opposition comes from obvious enemies — sometimes those within our own community or background act against our interests, as 'Amr did against fellow Arabs.
A community facing a shared threat grows stronger when its members consult one another, align on principle, and face the challenge together rather than each person acting alone.
practical_advice: When you anticipate being put on the spot about your beliefs or identity, take time beforehand to consult with trusted people and clarify what you truly believe and how you want to express it.
If someone is trying to sabotage your reputation or standing with others, do not respond with counter-attacks; let your honest, composed words speak for themselves.
When a scheme against you fails because you held to the truth, use that experience as a reference point for the next time you feel tempted to compromise — remember that truth was your protection.

title: The Muslim Refugees' Joy at the Negus's Victory Over the Rebel
summary: An Abyssinian rebel rose against the Negus, terrifying the Muslim refugees who feared losing their protector. Al-Zubayr swam the Nile to witness the battle, and when the Negus was victorious, the community erupted in joy and relief.
story: Umm Salama continued, "And so we stayed on there in comfort and security. "But soon a certain Abyssinian rose in revolt and challenged the rule of the Negus. And, I swear, nothing had ever caused us so much grief as this, since we feared that that rebel would defeat him and then there would be a king who might not recognise our rights as the Negus had done. "And so we began saying prayers to God, supplicating Him to give victory to the Negus who went out to do battle. "The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked one another which one of them should go and witness the engagement to see which side would be victorious. "Al-Zubayr, one of the youngest of them, volunteered. "They then blew air into a skin which they attached to his chest and he set off on it, swimming across the Nile. He came out on the other bank, went to the site where the two sides were meeting and witnessed the battle. "God defeated and killed that rebel leader, giving the Negus victory over him. "Al-Zubayr then returned to us, waving his shirt and yelling, 'Rejoice! God has given victory to the Negus!' "" Umm Salama concluded, "I swear by God we were never happier than at the Negus's victory. Thereafter we remained there with him, until some of us returned to Mecca while others stayed there."
lessons: Even in safety, a believer's security is never guaranteed by worldly circumstances alone; anxiety can return at any moment, reminding us to keep our trust anchored in God rather than in people or situations.
When a community faces an uncertain threat, shared prayer and collective action — even small acts like sending one person to gather news — reflect a healthy and faithful response.
Gratitude after relief is one of the deepest spiritual emotions; the companions' joy at the Negus's victory teaches us to fully acknowledge and celebrate God's mercy when it arrives.
practical_advice: When you feel your sense of security suddenly threatened after a period of peace, recognize that fear is natural — but channel it into prayer and constructive action rather than panic or despair.
In times of collective uncertainty, identify what small, concrete step your community can take to gather information or prepare, and volunteer if you are able, as al-Zubayr did.
After a period of anxiety resolves in your favor, take a moment to consciously acknowledge relief and gratitude — this practice of emotional recognition strengthens resilience for future hardships.

title: The Story of the Negus: His Father's Murder, His Sale into Slavery, and His Rise to Kingship
summary: The Negus's remarkable life story is recounted: his father was killed by jealous Abyssinians, he was sold into slavery as a child, but God eventually restored him to the throne — which is why he declared he would never oppress others as he had been oppressed.
story: Al-Zuhri stated, "I related this hadith on the authority of Umm Salama to Wrwa b. al-Zuhayr and he asked, 'Do you know what was meant by the Negus's words? "I swear, God took no bribe from me when he returned my kingdom to me; so am I to accept a bribe about Him? And He did not acquiesce to the people about me; so am I to obey them about him?"' "I replied, 'No; Aba Bakr b. 'Abd al-Ra?unm b. al-Haith b. Hisham, relating on the authority of Umm Salama, did not tell me that.' "Wrwa explained, 'Well, 'A'isha related to me that his father had been the king of his people and had a brother who had fathered twelve sons. Whereas his own father had only one son, the Negus himself. The Abyssinians discussed this among themselves and decided to kill the father of the Negus and to appoint his uncle over them in his place. For since the uncle had twelve sons of his own who would inherit the kingship, then Abyssinia would remain for a long time free from unrest. They therefore attacked and killed the father of the Negus and made his brother king. "'The Negus then went to live with his uncle; eventually he had such influence over him that no one but he could direct his affairs. He was intelligent and judicious. When the Abyssinians saw his status with his uncle, they told one another, "We have seen how this young man ranks with his uncle, and we could never be safe if he ruled over us, because he knows we killed his father. If he became king, he would not fail to kill every nobleman among us. Let us therefore speak to the king about him so that he will kill or exile him from our land." "'So they went to his uncle and told him, "We have seen how this young man ranks high with you, but you know that we killed his father and put you in his place.
