We could never be safe if he ruled over us, for he would kill us. You should either kill him or exile him from our land." "'"What?" he responded, "You killed his father yesterday and today I am to kill him! No, but I will exile him." "'So they took him out and stood him in the market where they sold him to a certain merchant for 600 or 700 dirhams who put him aboard a ship and sailed away with him. "'When evening came, autumn storm clouds arose and his uncle went outside beneath them to'"
lessons: God's plan for a person's life can involve tremendous suffering and injustice before restoration arrives — the Negus's story is a reminder that worldly loss is not the final word.
A person who has experienced profound injustice and been restored by God often becomes someone who refuses to inflict injustice on others, as the Negus's refusal to sell the Muslims demonstrates.
Intelligence, integrity, and genuine capability — qualities the Negus showed even in his uncle's household — can serve a person even when circumstances strip away everything else.
practical_advice: If you are going through a season of loss, exile, or displacement that feels unjust and permanent, reflect on the stories of those who were restored after seemingly irreversible hardship — these accounts are meant to carry you through.

When you have been wronged by people motivated by fear or self-interest, try to understand their psychology without excusing their actions; this understanding can prevent bitterness from consuming you.

If God has brought you through a hard season and restored something you lost, let that experience make you more just and compassionate toward others who are vulnerable — use your story as a source of empathy, not entitlement.

title: The Rivalry and Betrayal Between 'Amr ibn al-'As and 'Umara on the Voyage to Abyssinia
summary: During the Quraysh delegation's voyage to the Negus, 'Umara ibn al-Walid attempted to murder 'Amr ibn al-'As by throwing him overboard, creating a lasting enmity between the two men that would have lasting consequences.
story: What is implied is that when they left Mecca, 'Amr's wife was with him and also that Wm%a was a handsome young man. They were together in the boat and Wmaa was attracted to the wife of 'Amr b. al-'A$. So he threw 'Amr into the sea to kill him, hut he swam back to the boat. TJmzra then told him, "If I'd known you could swim so well, I wouldn't have thrown you in!" 'Amr consequently hated him. Having failed to achieve their objective with the Negus regarding the Emigrants, Wmaa ingratiated himself with some of the Negus's people. But 'Amr warned the Negus of this and the latter had 'Umara bewitched so that he lost his mind and wandered off into the dcsert with the wild beasts. Al-Umawi related his story at very great length, to the effect that he lived on into the time of the reign of 'Umar b. d-Khattab, when some of the Companions hunted him down and seized hi. He then began saying, "Dispatch me! Dispatch me! Or I will die!" Since they did not dispatch him, he died right then and there.
lessons: Betrayal by those who should be allies is a painful but ancient human experience; how one responds to such treachery reveals more about one's character than the treachery itself.
Wrongdoing sets in motion consequences that the wrongdoer cannot foresee or control — 'Umara's actions ultimately led to his own ruin.
Anger and hatred born from genuine injustice are understandable human emotions, but allowing them to define one's path can become all-consuming.
practical_advice: If you have been betrayed by someone you trusted or worked alongside, allow yourself to acknowledge the anger and hurt without letting it become the defining force of your decisions going forward.
When someone wrongs you, resist the urge to retaliate in ways that compromise your own integrity; focus instead on protecting yourself and those in your care through honest means.
Recognize that those who harm others often carry the seeds of their own undoing — this understanding can help release the grip of bitterness and allow you to move forward.

title: Abu Talib's Poem Urging the Negus to Protect the Muslim Emigrants
summary: Abu Talib, unable to protect the Muslim emigrants in person, composed verses addressed to the Negus urging him to continue his just and kind treatment of Ja'far and his companions who had taken refuge in Abyssinia.
story: Ziyad related, from Ibn Ishaq, that when Abii Talih saw Quraysh engaged in this he wrote to the Negus some verses urging him to act with justice and kindness towards those of his people who were living with him: "Would that I knew how it is so far off for Ja'far and 'Amr, and it is relatives who can he the most bitter enemies. And whether the actions of the Negus towards Ja'far and his companions are still kind, or whether some mischief-maker has hindered that. You should know, may you avoid disaster, that you are noble, and that no alien would have complaint to you. We know that God has given you much abundance, and that the means of all goodness is attached to you."
lessons: When those we love are far away and we cannot protect them directly, creative and sincere means of advocacy — like Abu Talib's poetry — express a love that transcends physical distance.
Family bonds and concern for community do not require agreement in belief; Abu Talib's care for the Muslim emigrants shows that protective love can exist across differences of faith.
Acknowledging the goodness in others and appealing to their noble qualities is often more effective than demands — Abu Talib's approach was one of dignity and respect.
practical_advice: When someone you care about is going through hardship and you feel helpless to intervene directly, find a meaningful way to advocate for them — a heartfelt letter, a sincere dua, or a word to those who can help.
If you are separated from loved ones and anxious about their wellbeing, channel that anxiety into positive action rather than letting it become paralysing worry.
Practice recognizing and naming the goodness in people when you ask something of them — it builds connection and makes your appeal more likely to be received well.

title: The Negus's Hidden Testimony and His Defense of Jesus Before His People
summary: When the Abyssinians revolted against the Negus for abandoning their faith, he cleverly wrote his true belief about Jesus on a document hidden in his garment, and when challenged publicly gave a response that satisfied his people without compromising his sincere conviction.
story: Ziyad related, from Muhammad b. Ishxq, "Jacfar b. Muhammad related to me that his father said, 'The Abyssinians gathered together and told the Negus, "You have abandoned our faith." Then they revolted against him.' "He sent to Jacfar and his companions and prepared boats for them, telling them, 'Embark on these, and remain as you are. If I am defeated then leave for wherever you want. If I am victorious, then stay.' "He then made his way to a document and wrote down the words, 'He testifies that there is no god but God and that Jesus is His slave, His messenger, His spirit and His word that he cast unto Mary.' He then placed the document inside his gown at his right shoulder. "He then went outside to the Abyssinians who were lined up before him and said, '0 Abyssinians, am I not your rightful leader?' 'Yes,' they replied. "'And how,' he asked, 'do you rate my conduct towards you?' "'As fine conduct.' "'So why are you discontented?' "'You have abandoned our faith and claimed that Jesus is His slave and His messenger.' "'And what is it you say of Jesus?' he asked them. "'We say that he is the son of God.' "Placing his hand across his chest over his gown, he then gave his testimony that, 'Jesus, son of Mary, was no more than this.' But he meant what he had written. "His people were satisfied and left. "This reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and when the Negus died he prayed for him and sought God's forgiveness for him."
lessons: Maintaining faith under extreme social and political pressure requires both courage and wisdom — the Negus found a way to hold his convictions without needlessly provoking a crisis that would harm others.
God sees the intentions of the heart; the Negus's sincerity was recognized by the Prophet himself, who prayed for him and sought forgiveness for him after his death.
Protecting those in your care — as the Negus did for the Muslim emigrants by preparing boats for them — is itself an expression of righteous faith.
practical_advice: When your beliefs put you at odds with those around you and open confrontation would cause harm, reflect on how to be honest in your heart before God while being wise in your outward conduct.
Remember that God's judgment is based on what is in the heart — when you feel pressure to conform outwardly, hold fast to your sincere internal conviction and seek God's guidance.
If you are in a position of responsibility and those under your care face danger, prioritize their safety proactively before the crisis fully arrives, as the Negus did by preparing the boats.

title: The Prophet's Funeral Prayer for the Negus Across the Distance
summary: On the day the Negus died in Abyssinia, the Prophet Muhammad performed the funeral prayer for him in Madinah, announcing his death to his companions and calling him 'a good man' and 'your brother Ashama.'
story: It is established in both jahih collections, on the authority of Abii Hurayra, God be pleased with him, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) lamented the death of the Negus on the day he died and went with others to the place of prayer. There he lined up with them and four times recited the litany, Allahu Akbar, God is most great. Al-Bukhari stated: "The Death of the Negus. Abti al-Rabic related to us, as did Ibn Wyayna, from Ibn Jurayj, from <At%', from Jiibir, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated on the day when the Negus died, "Today a good man died; arise and pray for your brother Ashama."'" This is also related from a hadith of Anas b. Malik, Ibn Masciid, and others. In some accounts he is named Ashama, though in one it is Mqhama. His name was Ashama b. B&. He was an honest, devout and intelligent man, just and honourable. May God be pleased with him. Yiinus stated, from Ibn Ishaq, "The name of the Negus was Mashama." In one manuscript d-Bayhaqi has corrected this to Asham. 'The name means in Arabic 'atryya; gift. The word 'Negus' is the title for a king, equivalent to using the word 'Chosroe' or 'Heraclius'." Ziyad related, from Muhammad b. Ishiiq, "When the Negus, God bless him, died, we used to say that a light could still be seen at his grave."
lessons: The Prophet's prayer for the Negus across the distance teaches that sincere faith and righteous character transcend borders, titles, and ethnicity — goodness is recognized and honoured by God regardless of where one lives.
Grief for a righteous person who has passed is a noble emotion — the Prophet openly lamented the Negus, modelling that mourning the good is itself an act of love.
Calling the Negus 'your brother' established a bond of brotherhood grounded in shared faith and values, reminding us that our truest community is built on character rather than kinship alone.
practical_advice: When you lose someone whose goodness touched your life, allow yourself to grieve openly and honour their memory — suppressing that grief is not strength, and acknowledging it is not weakness.
Reflect on the people in your life who have shown you kindness, justice, or protection, and consider how you can honour their legacy through your own conduct.
Expand your understanding of who belongs to your community — look for goodness and sincerity in people regardless of their background, as the Prophet recognised these qualities in the Negus.

title: The Joy of the Prophet at the Conquest of Khaybar and the Return of Ja'far
summary: On the day of the conquest of Khaybar, the remaining Muslim emigrants to Abyssinia returned to Madinah under Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and the Prophet expressed that he could not decide which event brought him greater joy.
story: I add the comment that the fact that Abii Hurayra, may God be pleased with him, witnessed the prayers for the Negus points to his having died after the conquest of Khaybar, the year when the remainder of the Emigrants to Abyssinia arrived along with Jacfar b. Abii Talib, God bless hi, namely on the day of the conquest of Khaybar. It is therefore related that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "By God, I don't know which makes me happier - the conquest of Khaybar or the arrival ofJacfar b. Abii Txlib!" They brought with them presents and treasures from the Negus, God he pleased with him; and accompanying them as passengers on their Yemeni boat were the followers of Abii Miisa al-Ash'ari and his people the Ash'aris, may God be pleased with them. And with Ja'far and the presents of the Negus.
lessons: Joy and gratitude can be felt simultaneously for multiple blessings — the Prophet's inability to choose between two sources of happiness is a beautiful model of a heart full of thankfulness.
Years of patient separation and hardship — as endured by the emigrants in Abyssinia — eventually give way to reunion; faithfulness through the long wait is itself a form of worship.
Victory and reunion together represent a fullness of blessing that neither alone could provide, reminding us that God often gives multiple gifts at once as a sign of His generosity.
practical_advice: When good things happen after a long period of difficulty, take time to fully receive and acknowledge the joy — do not rush past it or diminish it by immediately moving to the next concern.
If you have been separated from loved ones or from a community you belong to, hold onto hope that reunion is possible, and let that hope sustain you through the waiting.
Practice naming your blessings specifically and out loud, as the Prophet did — this deepens gratitude and helps anchor positive emotions in memory during harder times.

title: The Prophet Honors the Delegation of the Negus
summary: When a delegation from the Negus (King of Abyssinia) visited the Prophet, he rose to serve them himself, saying he wished to repay their kindness to his Companions.
story: The delegation from the Negus went in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he arose to serve them. His Companions objected, saying, 'Let us do that for you, O Messenger of God.' He replied, 'They honoured my Companions, and I wish to repay them.'
lessons: True leadership includes humility and personal service to others, not just commanding others to act.
Gratitude should be expressed in action, not merely in words — repaying kindness with kindness is a prophetic virtue.
Recognizing the good done by others, even non-Muslims, and publicly honoring it reflects noble character.
practical_advice: When someone helps a person you love, find a concrete way to thank them directly rather than delegating that gratitude.
Practice humility by performing acts of service yourself, even when you have others who could do it for you.
Keep a mental note of kindnesses extended to you or your loved ones and look for opportunities to genuinely reciprocate.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab's Sister and the Turning Point of His Life
summary: Umar set out with his sword to kill the Prophet, only to discover his own sister had secretly embraced Islam; striking her caused him deep remorse and opened his heart to the Quran.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The acceptance of Islam by 'Umar, so I have been told, came about as a result of the conversion of his sister Fatima, daughter of al-Khattab, who was married to Sa'id b. Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl. Her husband had also converted, and they were concealing their conversion from 'Umar.

'Nu'aym b. 'Abd Allah al-Nahham, a member of the Banu 'Adi tribe, had also converted but concealed this fact from his people.

'Khabbab b. al-Aratt was attending the home of Fatima, daughter of al-Khattab, to read the Qur'an to her. One day 'Umar went out, his sword at his waist, looking for the Messenger of God (SAAS) who, along with a group of his followers, he had been told, were in a house at al-Safa. The group with him, including men and women, totalled some 40 persons. Among these, along with the Messenger of God (SAAS), were his uncle Hamza, Abu Bakr b. Abi Quhafa, al-Siddiq, "the Trusting", and 'Ali b. Abi Talib, God be pleased with them, along with other Muslim men who had stayed behind in Mecca and not departed for Abyssinia.

'Nu'aym b. 'Abd Allah met him and enquired, "Where are you heading, 'Umar?"

'He replied, "I'm looking for Muhammad, that Sabian who has disunited Quraysh, ridiculed their dreams, criticized their religion and slandered their gods. I'm going to kill him!"

'Nu'aym told him, "By God, you've lost your mind, 'Umar! Do you think that 'Abd Manaf would allow you to walk on the face of the earth once you killed Muhammad? Why don't you go home and sort out your household!"

'"What about my household?"

'"Your brother-in-law and nephew Sa'id b. Zayd and your sister Fatima. They have accepted Islam and become followers of Muhammad (SAAS) in his religion. Look to them!"

'Umar then returned to his sister Fatima where Khabbab b. al-Aratt was; he had with him a manuscript of surat Taha that he was reading to her.

'When they heard 'Umar's approach, Khabbab hid inside a bedroom, or somewhere else in the house, and Fatima took the manuscript and hid it under her thigh. But 'Umar had heard Khabbab reciting it as he approached the door.

'As he came in, he demanded, "What was that murmuring sound I heard?"

'"I heard nothing," they both replied.

'"Well I did," he insisted. "And I've been told you are following Muhammad in his religion." He then struck out at his brother-in-law Sa'id b. Zayd, and when his sister Fatima rose to defend her husband he hit her and wounded her.

'When he did this, his sister and brother-in-law admitted, "Yes, we have accepted Islam and do believe in God and in His Messenger. Do whatever you want!"

'When 'Umar saw the blood on his sister he was sorry for what he had done and repentant and he told her, "Give me that document you were just reading so that I can see what it is Muhammad brings." 'Umar was literate.

'When he said that, she replied, "We don't trust you with it."

'"Don't be afraid," he told her, and swore by his gods to return it to her when he had read it.

'When he said this she had hopes of his accepting Islam and so she said, "But being a polytheist you are unclean, and only the purified may touch it."

'So 'Umar arose and washed himself, and she gave him the sheet on which surat Taha was written. When he had read a passage of it, he said, "How fine, how wonderful these words are!"

'When Khabbab b. al-Aratt heard this he came out and said, "By God, 'Umar, I do so hope that God has selected you because of the prayer of His Prophet (SAAS), for yesterday I heard him say, 'O God, give help to Islam through Abu al-Hakam b. Hisham, or through 'Umar b. al-Khattab!' So come now to God, 'Umar!"

'At that 'Umar said, "Khabbab, lead me to Muhammad so that I can accept Islam."'
lessons: Anger and rage can cause us to harm those we love most; the moment of seeing that harm can be a turning point toward genuine self-reflection.
The courage of Fatima — standing firm in her faith even while bleeding — shows that quiet, steadfast conviction can reach hearts that argument alone cannot.
God's guidance can come through unexpected doors; 'Umar's entire trajectory changed through an encounter he intended to use for violence.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelming anger, pause before acting — ask yourself what harm your reaction might cause to people you actually care about.
If you are in a difficult family situation where your beliefs differ from those around you, remain calm and dignified; your character is often more persuasive than your words.
If you carry guilt over harm you have caused someone, let that remorse be a genuine turning point rather than just an emotion — take a concrete step toward making things right.

title: Umar's Arrival at the House of al-Safa and His Acceptance of Islam
summary: Armed with his sword, Umar arrived at the house where the Prophet and his Companions had gathered; the Prophet welcomed him, and Umar declared his faith, transforming the Muslim community's strength overnight.
story:
'Umar picked up his sword and put it on then left for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions. He knocked on the door and when they heard his voice, one of the Companions got up and looked outside through a chink in the door. He saw 'Umar, wearing his sword, then went back in to the Messenger of God (SAAS) in a fright and exclaiming, "O Messenger of God, it's 'Umar b. al-Khattab, armed with his sword!"

'Hamza said, "Well, let him in. If he has come with good, we'll reciprocate, but if he's come in evil, we'll kill him with his sword." The Messenger of God (SAAS) also said, "Let him in!"

'So the Companion did so, and the Messenger of God (SAAS), got up and went across the room to meet him, taking him by his waist or by the place where his cloak joined, and giving him a strong pull, saying, "What brings you, Ibn al-Khattab? By God, I don't see you stopping until God sends down a thunderbolt upon you!"

''Umar replied, "O Messenger of God, I come to you to believe in God, in His messenger, and in what has come down from God!"

'At this the Messenger of God (SAAS) let out a great cry of Allahu Akbar, and the people in the house all knew that 'Umar had accepted Islam.

'The Companions of the Messenger of God left their places elated that now 'Umar had accepted Islam along with Hamza, knowing that these two men would protect him and help them obtain justice from their enemies.'
lessons: Fear is natural even for the most devoted believers; the Companions were frightened by 'Umar's arrival, yet the Prophet met uncertainty with openness and trust in God.
A single transformation in one person can change the fortunes of an entire community — never underestimate the significance of one heart turning toward good.
The Prophet's courage in personally going to meet 'Umar — someone who had come to harm him — demonstrates that leadership sometimes requires meeting hostility with dignity rather than defensiveness.
practical_advice: When you are afraid of someone or something, try to discern whether the fear is calling you to caution or whether it is preventing you from an act of courage that could bring great good.
If someone who once opposed or hurt you shows signs of change, be willing to extend a genuine welcome rather than holding only to their past.
Celebrate the positive changes of others wholeheartedly — the Companions' joy at 'Umar's conversion reminds us that communal encouragement strengthens everyone's resolve.

title: Ibn Mas'ud on the Power of Umar's Conversion
summary: Ibn Mas'ud testified that the Muslims could not pray openly at the Ka'ba until Umar accepted Islam, describing his conversion as a victory, his migration as a help, and his leadership as a mercy.
story:
'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud used to say, 'We were unable to pray at the ka'ba until 'Umar accepted Islam; having done so, 'Umar battled Quraysh until he was able to pray at the ka'ba, and we prayed with him.'

I comment that it is well established in the Sahih collection of al-Bukhari, that Ibn Mas'ud said, 'We have been powerful ever since 'Umar b. al-Khattab accepted Islam.'

Ziyad al-Bakka'i stated, 'Mis'ar b. Kidam related to me, from Sa'd b. Ibrahim, who said, "Ibn Mas'ud stated, 'The acceptance of Islam by 'Umar was a victory, his migration was a help, and his reign was a mercy. We could not pray at the ka'ba until he accepted Islam; when he did so 'Umar battled Quraysh until he was able to pray at the ka'ba, and we prayed with him."'
lessons: Sustained oppression can make even the most basic acts of worship feel like a distant dream; perseverance through that period is itself a form of faith.
The arrival of support — whether a person, a resource, or a change in circumstances — can be a mercy from God that transforms a community's ability to practice openly.
What looks like a simple act (one person converting) can be a watershed moment for an entire movement; individual choices carry communal weight.
practical_advice: When you feel unable to practice your values or faith openly due to social pressure, document and hold onto the small ways you maintain your identity — those acts matter deeply.
Recognize the people in your life whose presence genuinely strengthened you during a difficult time and express gratitude to them.
If you are in a position of strength, use it to create space for others to do what is good, just as 'Umar used his standing to allow communal prayer.

title: Umm Abd Allah Witnesses 'Umar's Unexpected Gentleness Before His Conversion
summary: As the Muslim emigrants prepared to flee to Abyssinia due to persecution, Umar — still a fierce opponent of Islam — paused to wish them well with an unexpected gentleness that moved Umm Abd Allah deeply.
story:
'Abd al-Rahman b. al-Harith b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a related to me, from 'Abd al-'Aziz b. 'Amir b. Rabi'a, from his mother Umm 'Abd Allah, daughter of Abi Hathma, who said, "We were preparing to depart for Abyssinia — 'Amir having gone off to get something we needed — when 'Umar came along and stopped, he being a polytheist at the time. We had suffered violence and harm from him.

'He said, 'So you're leaving then, Umm 'Abd Allah?'

'"Yes," I replied. "We're leaving for one of God's countries, since you have so maltreated and harmed us, so that God may provide us some way out."

'He commented, "Well, God be with you!"

'I saw in him a gentleness I never had before. He then left, our departure, so it seemed to me, having saddened him.

''Amir then arrived with our things and I commented, "O father of 'Abd Allah, I wish you could have seen 'Umar just now, his gentleness and concern for us."

'He asked, "Do you look to his accepting Islam?"

'"Yes, I do," I replied.

'He commented, "That man you saw won't accept Islam before al-Khattab's donkey does!"

'He said this in despair at him, for all his harsh opposition to Islam that had been seen."'
lessons: Even in those who oppose us there can be glimpses of goodness — remaining open to seeing humanity in adversaries is a sign of spiritual maturity.
Forced migration due to persecution is one of the most painful human experiences, yet leaving with dignity and trust in God is itself an act of faith.
Human beings are capable of more transformation than those closest to them can imagine; despair about another person's change is understandable but need not be the final word.
practical_advice: When you are forced to leave a place or situation due to mistreatment, hold onto the framing that you are moving toward God's provision rather than only away from harm.
Notice unexpected kindness even from difficult people — it does not require you to forget past harm, but it can soften bitterness and keep your heart from hardening.
Avoid making absolute predictions about other people's capacity for change; history repeatedly shows that the most unlikely individuals can be transformed.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab's Conversion to Islam
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab, a fierce opponent of Islam, unexpectedly hears the Quran recited by the Prophet during a night visit to the Ka'ba and experiences a profound transformation, leading him to embrace Islam.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abi Najih al-Makki related to me, from his Companions 'Ata, Mujahid and others, that regarding 'Umar's acceptance of Islam, he himself would say, 'I used to be very remote from Islam. Before Islam I used to be a great man for wine; I loved drinking it. We used to have a place at al-Hazwara where we would meet.
"'One night I went out looking for my drinking partners, but found not one of them there. So I told myself I should go off to a wine dealer named so-and-so to get some for myself to drink.
"'But I could not find him. So I told myself I might as well go to the ka'ba and circumambulate it seven or seventy times.
"'So I went to the mosque and there, standing praying, I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS). When he prayed he would face towards al-Sham, placing the ka'ba between himself and it. The spot he prayed at was between the two corners, al-asmad (the one with the 'black stone') and al-yamani (the one towards Yemen).
"'When I saw him, I told myself I might just as well spend the evening listening to Muhammad. If I came too close to him to listen I might scare him, so I approached from the hijr side, passing beneath its covering and walking slowly while the Messenger of God (SAAS) continued standing there reciting the Qur'an. Eventually I stood facing towards him with nothing there between us but the covering over the ka'ba.
"'When I heard the Qur'an my heart was touched by it, I wept and Islam entered within me.
"'I stayed where I was until the Messenger of God (SAAS) had finished his prayer and departed. When he left he would pass by the home of Ibn Abi Husayn, whose dwelling happened to be at Dar al-Raqa'a, which was owned by Mu'awiya. I followed him until, as he went between the house of 'Abbas and that of Ibn Azhar, I caught up with him. When he heard my voice he recognized me and thought I had followed him only to harm him. So he told me to keep away, then said, "What brings you here at this hour, Ibn al-Khattab?"
"'I replied, "I have come to believe in God and in His messenger, and in what has come down from God."
"'The Messenger of God (SAAS) expressed thanks to God at this and then he said, "God has guided you, Umar!" He then touched my chest and prayed for me to be constant.
"'After that I left and the Messenger of God (SAAS) went inside his house.'"
lessons: Guidance can come in unexpected moments — when Umar set out looking for wine and companions, God led him instead to the Quran and transformation. No one is too far gone to change.

The Quran has the power to pierce even the hardest heart. Umar wept and Islam entered him not through argument but through simply listening. Openness, even accidental openness, can be the door to profound change.
True change requires courage to act on what you feel. Umar followed the Prophet, declared his faith, and faced hostility publicly rather than hiding his transformation.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually empty or are searching without direction, try sitting quietly with Quran recitation — allow yourself to simply listen without analysis or judgment.
When you experience a moment of spiritual clarity or emotional breakthrough, do not dismiss it. Take one concrete step to act on it, as Umar did by approaching the Prophet immediately.
If you have felt far from faith or feel your past defines you, remember Umar's story: no distance from God is permanent. Begin wherever you are tonight.

title: Umar Publicly Announces His Islam and Faces the Quraysh
summary: After accepting Islam, Umar boldly announces his conversion publicly, confronts the enraged Quraysh in physical battle, and is ultimately protected by a respected elder, demonstrating both courage and divine protection.
story:
Ibn Ishaq said, "Nafi', the freed-man of Ibn Umar, related to me that Umar's son said, 'When Umar accepted Islam, he asked, "Which man of Quraysh is best at making announcements?"
""'Jamil b. Ma'mar al-Jumahi," he was told.
"'Next morning Umar went to the man.' Ibn Umar continued, 'So I followed him to see what he would do; I was a lad at the time, but understood all I saw.
"'When Umar reached him, he asked, "Jamil, do you know that I have accepted Islam and entered into the faith of Muhammad (SAAS)?"'
"Ibn Umar went on, 'Well, I swear, he had no sooner told him this than off he went, his gown trailing. Umar followed him and I did so too until he stopped at the mosque gate, where he shouted at the top of his voice, "O Quraysh!" (They were there at that time in their chambers around the ka'ba.) "Ibn al-Khattab has become a Sabian!"
"'Umar, standing right behind him, yelled, "He lies! But I have become a Muslim. I do testify that there is no god but God and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God!"
"'People were most enraged against him and soon he was battling hard against them, they against him. They kept up this fighting until the sun was high over their heads.
"'At last he was exhausted and sat down, while they stood all around him. He told them, "Do what you want! But I swear by God that if we were 300 men strong, either we'd have left the battlefield to you or you would have to us!"
"'While they were so engaged, a Quraysh sheikh finely dressed in a robe from Yemen and an embroidered shirt came up and stood there. He asked, "What's going on with you?"
""'Umar has become a Sabian," they replied.
"'"So what? If a man chooses to do something, what's it got to do with you? Do you think his tribe, the Banu 'Adi, will give him over to you like this? Let the man go!"
"Ibn Umar went on, 'And I swear, they drew back like a garment being stripped from him.'
"'And when my father Umar went off into exile to Medina, I asked him, "Father, who was that man who drove the people away from you at Mecca who was fighting you the day you accepted Islam?"
"'"That, my son," he replied, "was al-'As b. Wa'il al-sahmi."'"
lessons: Standing by your convictions publicly may bring immediate hardship, but doing so with clarity and courage commands a kind of respect even from opponents.
God can bring protection from unexpected sources — the man who protected Umar was not a Muslim, yet he was the instrument of Umar's safety. Divine care does not always come in expected forms.

True conviction does not wait for a safe moment. Umar chose the most public possible declaration rather than a quiet, private faith, understanding that real change sometimes demands public accountability.
practical_advice: When you feel pressure to hide or downplay what you believe or value, consider what one honest, clear statement of your position might accomplish — even if the response is hostile.
In moments when you feel outnumbered or exhausted from standing your ground, remember that relief and protection can arrive from directions you never anticipated. Do not give up before help comes.
If you are facing a difficult transition — changing a habit, leaving a harmful environment, or making a hard choice — make it concrete and tell someone. Accountability to others strengthens resolve.

title: Twenty Christians from Abyssinia Hear the Quran and Accept Islam
summary: A delegation of twenty Christians who had heard about the Prophet traveled to Mecca, listened to the Quran, were moved to tears, and embraced Islam, despite being immediately confronted and ridiculed by Abu Jahl and the Quraysh.
story:
Al-Bayhaqi said, 'Al-Hakim related to us, al-Asamm informed us, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, and Yunus related to us, all from Ibn Ishaq, who stated, "Then 20 men came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) while he was in Mecca, or nearby; these were Christians, news having spread about him to them from Abyssinia. They found him at the meeting place and spoke with him and asked him questions, while the men of Quraysh were in their chambers there around the ka'ba.
"'When they had finished putting the questions they wanted to ask the Messenger of God, he invited them to come to God, the Almighty and Glorious, and he recited the Qur'an to them. When they heard it, their eyes overflowed with tears. They then responded positively to him, believed in him and accepted and trusted him, recognising what had been described to them about him in their scripture.
"'When they arose to leave him, Abu Jahl and a group of Quraysh blocked their path and told them, "What a hopeless party you are! You were sent here by your co-religionaries back home to undertake an investigation for them and to return with information about this fellow, yet no sooner did you sit down in comfort with him than you abandoned your faith and believed all he told you. We've never known a group more foolish than you." Or words to that effect.
"'They replied, "We do not denigrate you; peace be upon you! We act as we see fit, as do you as you see fit. We spare no pains to attain what is good."'" It is said that the group of Christians were from Najran; but God alone knows whether that was so. It is also said, though God alone knows, that the following verses were revealed about them, "And those to whom we gave the book before it do believe in it. When it is recited to them, they say, 'We do believe in it; it is the truth from our Lord. We were accepting (its message) even before it came.' Those shall receive their reward twice, for they have been steadfast and repay evil with good and expend from what We have given them. And when they hear idle gossip they turn away from it and say, 'We act as we see fit, as do you as you see fit. Peace be upon you; we do not desire the ignorant and foolish'" (surat al-Qasas; XXVIII, v.52-5).
lessons: Sincere truth-seeking, even across religious traditions, can lead a person to recognition of what is right. These Christians came to investigate and left transformed because they came with open hearts.
When faced with mockery after making a sincere and principled decision, a composed and dignified response — as these Christians gave — is more powerful than anger or defensiveness.
The Quran's impact is not limited to one people or background. Its recitation moves hearts across every kind of divide when those hearts are genuinely open.
practical_advice: If you are searching for meaning or truth, pursue that search seriously and follow it wherever it leads — do not let social pressure or fear of judgment stop you from investigating what calls to your heart.
When others ridicule a sincere choice you have made, practice the response of these Christians: acknowledge others calmly, state your own path clearly, and disengage without hostility.
Reflect on moments when something — a verse, a piece of writing, a story — moved you to tears unexpectedly. Those moments of being moved are worth paying attention to and sitting with.

title: The Prophet's Letter to the Negus of Abyssinia
summary: The Prophet Muhammad sent a formal letter to the Negus of Abyssinia inviting him to Islam, calling him to worship God alone and bearing witness to the core principles of monotheism, as part of a broader campaign of letters sent to world rulers.
story: Al-Bayhaqi in his book Dala'il (The Signs) entitles a chapter "On the contents of the document sent by the Prophet (SAAS) to the Negus." He then relates, from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yunus, from Ibn Ishaq, as follows, "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Compassionate: this is a document from Muhammad, the Messenger of God (SAAS), to the Negus, al-Asham, the great leader of Abyssinia: Peace be upon those who follow guidance, believe in God and His messenger, and testify that there is no god but God alone, Who has no partner, Who took no female partner for Himself, and Who has no child, and they testify that Muhammad is His servant and His messenger. I call out to you in God's name, for I am His messenger. So submit (to Him) and be at peace! O People of the Book, come to an agreement that is equal between us both; that we will worship none but God and associate no other god with Him; that we will not take one another as masters to the exclusion of God. But if they do turn aside, then say, 'Bear witness that we are Muslims' (surat Al-'Imran; III, v.63). But if you refuse, then upon you is the sin of those of your people who are Christians." Al-Bayhaqi related it thus after the narrative of the migration to Abyssinia. Al-Zuhri stated, 'The letters to them from the Prophet (SAAS) were all the same, i.e. copies, and all quoted this same verse which comes from surat Al-'Imran, a chapter that is incontrovertibly from the Medina period.'
lessons: The Prophet's outreach to rulers of other nations shows that the call to truth is universal and does not stop at borders, cultures, or the boundaries of political power.

Inviting others to what you believe is right can be done with dignity, clarity, and respect — the Prophet's letter opened with peace and made its case through shared principles rather than demands or threats.
A message rooted in sincerity and clear principle stands on its own, regardless of whether it is accepted. The responsibility is to convey, not to compel.
practical_advice: If there is someone in your life you have been wanting to share something important with — a value, a concern, a truth — consider writing it down clearly and calmly rather than waiting for the perfect spoken moment.
When extending an olive branch or seeking common ground with someone who sees the world differently, begin from shared values rather than points of difference, as the Prophet's letter did.
Reflect on the courage it takes to send a sincere message knowing it may be rejected. Do not let fear of rejection prevent you from saying what matters.

title: The Prophet's Letter to the Negus and His Reply
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) sent a letter to the Negus of Abyssinia calling him to Islam and requesting he receive the Muslim emigrants hospitably. The Negus replied affirming his belief in the Prophet's message and his acceptance of Islam.
story: Negus al-Asham ... perhaps the word "al-Asham" is an interpolation by the narrator, based on his understanding. But God knows best. More relevant here than this is what al-Bayhaqi also narrated, from al-Haim, from Aha al-Hasan b. 'Abd Allah al-Faqih, of Merve, who stated, "Hammad h. Ahmad related to us, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq and Salama h. al-Fadl, all from Muhammad h. Ishaq, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent 'Amr b. Umayya al-Damri with a letter he wrote to the Negus concerning Ja'far b. Aha T 3 i b and his companions: "In the name of God the most Merciful and compassionate: from Muhammad, Messenger of God, to the Negus al-Asham, king of Abyssinia: Peace be upon you. I express to you my praises for God the King, the All-Holy, the Faithful, and the Protector; I testify that Jesus is the spirit of God and His word that He cast unto Mary, the good, chaste, unviolated Virgin. And so she bore Jesus, whom He created from His spirit and His breath, just as He created Adam by His hand and breath. I call upon you to believe the One God Who has no associate. To Him all obedience is due. (I call upon you, tr.) to follow me, to believe in me as well as in that which has come down to me. For I am the Messenger of God. And I have sent to you my nephew Ja'far along with a group of Muslims. When they come to you receive them hospitably. Do not he haughty, for I invite you and your forces to God the Almighty and Glorious. I have conveyed my message and given advice. So accept my advice. And peace he upon those who follow the true path." "'The Negus wrote back to the Messenger of God (SAAS) as follows: "In the name of God, the most Merciful and Compassionate: To Muhammad the Messenger of God from the Negus al-Asham b. Abjar: Peace be upon you, 0 Prophet of God, from God, along with the mercy and blessings of God. There is no god hut Him who guided me to Islam. Your letter, 0 Messenger of God, in which you made reference to Jesus, has reached me. And, by the God of the heavens and the earth, Jesus is no more than what you stated. We recognize what you have been sent with to us. We have received your nephew and his friends with hospitality. And I do testify that you are the Messenger of God, that you speak the truth and are to be believed. I pledge my allegiance to you and to your nephew, accepting Islam at his hands for God, the Lord of the universe. I have sent to you, 0 Prophet of God, Ariha h. al-Asham b. Abjar, for I possess nothing hut myself. If you wish for me to come to you, I will do so, 0 Messenger of God, for I testify that what you say is truth."'"
lessons: Truth and sincerity in communication can open hearts across vast distances and cultural divides.
A leader who is guided by genuine faith rather than political self-interest will respond to truth with humility and openness.
God's guidance is not limited by geography, race, or social status — it reaches whoever sincerely seeks it.
practical_advice: When reaching out to someone you hope to influence for good, lead with sincerity, clarity, and respect rather than pressure or force.
If you are in a position of authority or influence, practice the Negus's example by setting aside pride and responding to truth with an open heart.
Trust that honest, heartfelt communication — even across great differences — can build bridges where none seemed possible.

title: The Boycott of Banu Hashim and the Confinement in the Enclave of Abu Talib
summary: Quraysh united against the Muslims and the Banu Hashim, imposing a severe social and economic boycott that confined them to Abu Talib's quarter for three years, cutting off food, trade, and all social relations in an attempt to force the surrender of the Prophet.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba stated, from al-Zuhri: "The polytheists thereafter became so violent in their actions against the Muslims that the plight of the latter became extreme. And Quraysh so united in their evil intent as to agree to kill the Messenger of God (SAAS) in full view. "When Aha Talib realized their purposes, he gathered the Bana 'Abd al-Murplib and told them to include the Messenger of God (SAAS) in their enclave and to protect him against those wishing to murder him. "The Muslims and non-believers of the tribe did agree to this, some out of a desire to uphold traditions of protection, others out of their faith and conviction. "When Quraysh realized that these were in agreement to protect the Messenger of God (SAAS), the polytheists among them made a pact not to sit down with, nor to do business with them, nor to enter the homes of these people until they surrendered the Messenger of God (SAAS) to execution. They cunningly drew up agreements and pacts to the effect that they would never accept peace or reconciliation with the Bana Hashim until they delivered him up for death. "The Bann Hashim therefore remained for three years confined to their enclave, suffering great deprivation. Their markets had been cut off; no food was allowed into Mecca for them, and all their business dealings were interrupted. "Their purpose in all this was to spill the blood of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "When it was bed time, Abn T&h would tell the Messenger of God (SAAS) to sleep on the former's bed, and he would do the same, the intent being that anyone wishing to harm or assassinate him would he unaware of this. And while people slept, Abn Talib made sure that one of his sons, brothers or nephews would lie on the bed of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he would tell the latter to take their places instead."
lessons: Community solidarity in the face of oppression — even from non-believers motivated by tribal loyalty — demonstrates that protection of the vulnerable is a deeply human value.
Enduring prolonged hardship with patience, rather than surrendering one's principles, builds a spiritual strength that cannot be broken by external pressure.
Those who sacrifice their own comfort and safety to protect others, as Abu Talib did each night, embody the highest expressions of love and loyalty.
practical_advice: When facing sustained hardship or social exclusion, lean into your community and identify those who, regardless of their own differences with you, will stand by you out of principle.
In times of prolonged stress and deprivation, focus on what you can control — your integrity, your relationships, your daily acts of care — rather than the vastness of what is being taken from you.
If you are caring for someone in danger, small daily acts of protection and sacrifice, like Abu Talib's nightly precautions, are expressions of love that sustain both the protector and the protected.

title: The Miracle of the Woodworm and the Nullification of the Boycott Document
summary: After three years of boycott, God sent woodworm to devour the unjust pact document hung in the Ka'ba, consuming every mention of God's name and leaving only the words of oppression. The Prophet informed Abu Talib, who confronted Quraysh, and the document was found destroyed exactly as the Prophet had foretold.
story: "At the start of the third year certain men of the tribes of 'Abd Manaf and Qusayy, along with others whose mothers were from the Bann Hzshim, became critical of this and recognized that they were breaking the bonds of kimship and disregarding what was right. They therefore decided one night to revoke what they had previously agreed to, and to free themselves thereof. "They discovered, however, that God had dispatched woodworm to attack their documents; they devoured everything making reference to their pact. "It is said that these documents were hung from the ceiling of the temple. Every reference to God's name was devoured; all that remained was material relating to polytheism, injustice and destruction of ties of kindred. "God, the Almighty and Glorious, made His messenger aware of what He had done to their documents, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) told this to Abii Tdib. "But Aba Tnlib responded, 'No! May the stars never mislead me!' "He then hurried off with a band of the Banii 'Abd al-Munalib and went to the mosque which was full of Quraysh. When the latter saw them heading for them they were surprised, believing them to he doing so because of the extent of their suffering and that they were coming to deliver the Messenger of God (SAAS) to them. "Ahn Talih spoke to them as follows: 'Various things we don't need to tell you of have occurred between you. Bring now your deeds referring to the pacts you have made; perhaps now there can be peace between us all.' "The reason he said this was because he was afraid they might look at the deeds before bringing them. "They did now bring them down, proud of them and not doubting that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was to be delivered to them. They placed them down, saying, 'Well, the time has come for you to proceed to a reconciliation with your people. Only one man has caused this rupture between us. You have placed him in a position where he has brought the danger of destruction and corruption to your people.' "Abii T d i b responded, 'I have only come to you now to offer you justice. My nephew has informed me - and he never lied to me - that God will have no part of that document there before you and that He has erased all of His names from it, leaving only references to your treachery, your breaking of the bonds of kinship and your unjust public demonstrations against us. "'If what my nephew has said is so, then you should recognize that and, by God, we will never surrender him to you before the last of us is dead! "'But if what he said is false, then we will deliver him to you for you to kill or to spare his life.' "'We'll agree to that', they replied. "They then opened up the deed and found that he (Muhammad), the truthful and the trustworthy (SAAS) had proved correct. When Quraysh saw it to be as Aba T d i b had said, they said, 'By God, this has to be some magic done by your man!' "They then reneged and reverted to even worse impiety and violence against the Messenger of God (SAAS) than before, doing their best to destroy him as they had formerly compacted. "That group of men of the Banii 'Abd d-Munalib responded by saying, 'It is others, not us, who are more blameworthy in telling ties and using magic. We very well know that the boycott of us you agreed on was closer to sorcery and magic than what we did. If you had not agreed to use magic the deeds before you would not have been spoiled and God's name would not have been erased while reference to your evil was retained. Is it we who rely on magic, or you?'" "Thereafter some men of 'Abd Manaf and Qusayy, along with those of Quraysh born to women of the Banii Hashim, including Abn al-Bakhturi, al-M~$~ib. 'Adi, Zuhayr b. Aba Umayya b. al-Mughira, Zam'a b. al-Aswad and Hish- b. 'Amr, of the Bana 'Amir b. Lu'ayy (the last-mentioned having had charge of the deed), along with other prominent members of their community, stated that they disavowed the action. "To this Abii Jahl, God damn him, responded, 'But this matter is not revocable!' "Abii T a b spoke verses regarding their deed and praised the men who had disavowed their former agreement, and he also commended the Negus."
lessons: Injustice carries within it the seeds of its own destruction — no document, pact, or scheme built on oppression can stand indefinitely against divine justice.

The Prophet's calm certainty in conveying God's message, even about something as seemingly small as a document, demonstrates that trust in God's knowledge produces a tranquility that no crisis can shake.
Even among those who oppose truth, there are always individuals whose conscience eventually moves them to stand against injustice — we should never assume all hearts are closed.
practical_advice: When you have been wronged and feel powerless against those with more influence, remember that injustice is inherently unstable and that patience in the face of oppression is not weakness but a form of deep strength.

In a confrontation with those who have acted unjustly, speak calmly and offer them a fair test of truth rather than resorting to anger or ultimatums — Abu Talib's composed strategy proved more effective than any outburst could have been.

When facing those who refuse to accept clear evidence and instead double down on hostility, recognise that your responsibility is to convey truth clearly and leave the outcome to God, not to force acceptance.

title: Quraysh's Written Pact to Boycott Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib
summary: After the Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib refused to surrender the Prophet, Quraysh formally agreed to a written pact banning all marriage, trade, and social dealings with these clans, hanging the document in the Ka'ba and intensifying persecution of the Muslims.
story: Thereafter al-Bayhaqi related, through Yiinus, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, as having said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) set out to fulfil the mission entrusted to him, the Banii Hashim and the Bana al-Muttalib undertook to protect him and refused to deliver him. Even though they were opposed to him and were engaged in the same religion as their people at large, they were none the less reluctant to humiliate themselves and deliver him over, conscious of the loathing Quraysh had for him. "The Bann Hashim and the Bana al-Mugdib having taken their stand and Quraysh having recognized that they had no access to Muhammad, they met and agreed to write a document defining their relationship to the Banii Hashim and the Banii 'Abd al-Munalib. They agreed that they would not marry them nor allow their women to marry them, nor engage in buying or selling with them. They wrote a document to that effect which they hung up in the ka'ba. "They then attacked the Muslims, restricting their movements and subjecting them to violence. Their plight and insecurity became" [extreme].
lessons: The dignity of a community under siege lies not in their material comfort but in their refusal to betray those entrusted to their care, even at great personal cost.
Institutional oppression — when prejudice is codified into law or formal agreement — is among the most dangerous forms of injustice, yet it is also a sign of the oppressor's desperation.
The loyalty shown by the Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib, including non-believers who refused to abandon their kinsman, teaches that moral courage is not the exclusive property of the faithful.
practical_advice: If you are experiencing systemic exclusion or social ostracism for standing by your values, find strength in the knowledge that you are in noble company — the earliest Muslims endured the same.
In moments when your entire social and economic world seems to be closing in, focus on the one thing no boycott can reach: your inner relationship with God and your own integrity.
Identify the people in your life who, like the Banu Hashim, would stand by you not for religious reasons but simply because it is right — and cherish and reciprocate that loyalty.

title: The Boycott Document Against Banu Hashim and Banu Abd al-Muttalib
summary: Quraysh wrote a document of total social and economic boycott against Banu Hashim and Banu Abd al-Muttalib, hung it in the Kaaba, and forced the Muslims into a state of extreme hardship and isolation.
story: Quraysh had for him. "The Bann Hashim and the Bana al-Mugdib having taken their stand and Quraysh having recognized that they had no access to Muhammad, they met and agreed to write a document defining their relationship to the Banii Hashim and the Banii 'Abd al-Munalib. They agreed that they would not marry them nor allow their women to marry them, nor engage in buying or selling with them. They wrote a document to that effect which they hung up in the ka'ba. "They then attacked the Muslims, restricting their movements and subjecting them to violence. Their plight and insecurity became extreme." Thereafter Ibn Ishaq related the story in full, telling of their entry into the enclave of Ahn Tdib and of the severe trouble they suffered there, to such a degree that their children would be overheard inside writhing with the pangs of hunger. Eventually most Quraysh regretted their plight and expressed their hatred for their accursed document. They then told how God, in His mercy, sent down woodworm against the document and these demolished every word relating to God, leaving therein only what referred to evil, to the boycott and to slanderous talk. Also related was how God Almighty informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) of this and how he told this to his uncle Aha Talih.
lessons: Oppression and injustice, no matter how powerful they appear, carry within them the seeds of their own undoing — Allah's mercy intervenes in ways beyond human expectation.
Steadfastness in the face of social exclusion and economic hardship is a mark of deep faith; the early Muslims endured extreme suffering without abandoning their convictions.
When a community unites around truth, even the most coordinated efforts to crush them cannot permanently succeed.
practical_advice: When you face social rejection or isolation for your values, remind yourself that such tests are temporary and that patience through them builds spiritual strength.
If you are experiencing economic hardship or social boycott, focus on what remains within your control — your character, your relationships with trusted loved ones, and your connection to Allah.
Document injustices you face and seek support from those around you who share your values, just as the Banu Hashim rallied together in the enclave of Abu Talib.

title: The Miracle of the Woodworm and the Destruction of the Boycott Document
summary: Allah sent woodworm to destroy the Quraysh boycott document hung in the Kaaba, erasing every word of oppression while leaving only the words of evil — a divine sign that was revealed to the Prophet who then informed his uncle Abu Talib.
story: Eventually most Quraysh regretted their plight and expressed their hatred for their accursed document. They then told how God, in His mercy, sent down woodworm against the document and these demolished every word relating to God, leaving therein only what referred to evil, to the boycott and to slanderous talk. Also related was how God Almighty informed the Messenger of God (SAAS) of this and how he told this to his uncle Aha Talih.
lessons: Allah can dismantle the instruments of oppression through means no one anticipates — trust that divine justice operates even when it is invisible to human eyes.
The Prophet's confidence in sharing divine news with his uncle, even in the darkest of times, teaches us to remain transparent and trusting with those who support us.
Moments of miraculous relief are reminders that hopelessness is never the appropriate response to difficulty — a turn can come from the most unexpected direction.
practical_advice: When a situation feels utterly beyond resolution, recall that divine help often arrives through means completely outside human calculation — hold onto hope rather than surrendering to despair.
Share good news and signs of relief with those who are suffering alongside you; it renews collective strength and gratitude.
Reflect on past moments in your life when relief came unexpectedly — use those memories as anchors of faith during current hardships.

title: Abu Lahab Abandons His Clan and Sides with Quraysh
summary: Abu Lahab, despite being from Banu Hashim, abandoned his own clan and allied with Quraysh against the Prophet, even boasting of his betrayal to Hind bint Utba — an act that earned him the condemnation of a Quranic chapter.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When Quraysh had done this the Bann Hashim and the Bana al-Mu~alih allied with Aba T d i b and entered his enclave and joined forces with him. "But Aha Lahab h. 'Abd al-Wzza b. 'Abd al-Mutralib left the Bauo Hashim and gave his support to Quraysh. "Husayn h. 'Abd Allah related to me that Aba Lahab met Hind, daughter of Wtba b. Rabi'a when he parted with his own people and gave his support t o Quraysh against them. H e asked her, '0 daughter of Wtba, have I not given my support to al-Lat and al-'Uzza and broken with those who have abandoned them and given their support against them?' "'Yes, indeed,' she replied. 'And may God reward you well, 0 Aba 'Utba!'" Ibn Ishaq continued, "I was told that one of the things he would say was, 'Muhammad promises me things I do not see that he claims to exist after death. What apart from that had he put in my hands?' "He then blew into his hands and addressed them thus: 'May you both perish! I see nothing in you of what Muhammad talks!' "And so God Almighty sent down His words: 'May the hands of Abii Lahab perish, and may he perish too!'" (sarat al-Lahab; CVI, v.1).
lessons: The betrayal of a family member or close ally is one of the most painful tests a person can face, yet the Prophet's mission continued undeterred — personal loyalty cannot be the foundation of one's purpose.
Mocking the promise of the afterlife and choosing worldly alliances over truth is a path that leads to lasting spiritual ruin, as Abu Lahab's fate illustrates.
Divine justice is comprehensive — those who actively work to harm truth and its bearers do not escape accountability, even if they are powerful or well-connected.
practical_advice: If someone close to you — a family member or trusted person — betrays or undermines you, resist the urge to retaliate with bitterness; focus instead on those who remain steadfast in their support.
When people mock your beliefs or the things you hold sacred, respond with dignity rather than anger — the truth does not need to be defended through argument alone.
Reflect honestly on whether your current alliances and loyalties are aligned with your values, or whether social pressure is pulling you away from what you truly believe is right.

title: Abu Talib's Poem Defending the Prophet During the Boycott
summary: Abu Talib composed a powerful poem declaring his clan's unwavering commitment to protect the Prophet, warning Quraysh of the bitterness of prolonged warfare and the injustice of their document.
story: "And so God Almighty sent down His words: 'May the hands of Abii Lahab perish, and may he perish too!'" (sarat al-Lahab; CVI, v.1). Ibn Ishaq went on, "When Quraysh had agreed in this way and acted towards him as they did, Abo Talib spoke the following verses: '0 do announce our disagreement to the Lu'ayy, to those Lu'ayy of the Bana Ka'b: Do you not know that we have found Muhammad to be a prophet like Moses of whom it is written in the very first hooks? And that among men there is love for him and that none is better than those endowed by God with love, And that the document you have put up is an evil thing, like the raging young camel? Wake up, wake up before the moist earth is dug (for your graves) and those without sin will be just like sinners. Do not follow the orders of those who lie, and so cut your ties with us after friendship and kinship, Drawing on an endless warfare; and the milk of war can turn very hitter to those who taste it. We will not, by the Lord of the temple, deliver up Ahmad because of the tribulations of time or the agony, And when our necks and hands and yours will otherwise be cut by glinting blades of Qussi steel! In a narrow battlefield where broken spears will be seen, over which vultures hover, like drinkers at a party, And the space where the horsemen gallop and the heroes cry out will make it like a battle in a war. Was our forefather Hshim not courageous, and did he not advise his sons to thrust and to strike? We do not tire of warfare before it tires of us, nor do we complain at whatever misfortunes may strike. We are people who stay firm and prudent at times when the spirits of brave men fly off in terror."'
lessons: Loyal supporters who stand by the truth-bearer — even without fully embracing the faith themselves — play a vital role in the survival and flourishing of a righteous cause.
Expressing pain and resolve through words, poetry, and honest communication is a healthy and dignified response to injustice.
Ancestral courage and noble lineage carry moral weight — drawing on the legacy of those who came before us can be a source of strength during trials.
practical_advice: When you are under pressure and facing collective hardship, articulate your position clearly and courageously — silence can be misread as acceptance of injustice.
Draw on the example of resilient people in your family history or community as a source of motivation when you feel like giving up.
If you are supporting someone who is being persecuted or marginalized, consider how you can make your support visible and vocal, as Abu Talib did for the Prophet.

title: Two to Three Years of Hardship in the Enclave of Abu Talib
summary: The Muslims endured two to three years of extreme hardship in the enclave of Abu Talib, surviving in near-starvation conditions while Quraysh enforced their total boycott.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "And they remained under those conditions for two or three years in the utmost distress, with nothing getting into them unless secretly, from those members of Qwaysh who wanted to keep ties with them."
lessons: Prolonged suffering tests the depth of one's faith and commitment in ways that brief trials cannot — endurance over years, not just days, is the mark of true steadfastness.
Even in the most totalizing systems of oppression, there are always individuals who quietly resist and extend help — never assume you are entirely without allies.
The community's survival depended on unity and mutual support within the enclave — collective resilience is more powerful than individual endurance alone.
practical_advice: When you are going through an extended period of hardship with no clear end in sight, break time into smaller, manageable segments rather than looking at the full weight of the trial ahead.
Identify even small acts of kindness or support from others around you — recognizing these prevents the distortion that comes from feeling completely abandoned.
Build and invest in your close community now, before times of crisis, so that when hardship comes there is an existing network of trust and solidarity to rely on.

title: Abu Jahl Confronts Hakim ibn Hizam Over Food for Khadijah
summary: Abu Jahl attempted to stop Hakim ibn Hizam from delivering food to his aunt Khadijah inside the besieged enclave, but was confronted and physically overpowered by Abu al-Bakhtari who defended the right to deliver what rightfully belonged to her.
story: "Aba Jahl h. Hisham, so they say, met Hakim b. H i z m b. Khuwaylid h. Asad with whom was a slave hearing wheat for Hizam's aunt Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid, who was in the enclave along with the Messenger of God (SAAS). Aba Jahl took hold of him and said, 'Are you taking food to the Banii Hashim? I swear, you'll not go, you and your food, without me making a scandal about you in Mecca!' "Aba al-Bakhtari h. H i s h m h. al-Harith b. Asad then came up to him and asked, 'What's going on between you and him?' "He replied, 'He's carrying food into the Bana Hashim.' "Aba al-Bakhtari commented, 'This is food belonging to his aunt that she had sent h i and he had at home. Would you prevent him from taking to her her own food? Get out of the man's way!' "But Abii Jahl, God damn him, refused and they began to fight. Aha ai-Bakhtari picked up a camel's jawbone and struck him with it, giving him a head wound. He then stomped on him. "Hamza b. 'Ahd al-Mu@lih was nearby and saw ail this. But they did not like to tell the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions of this incident lest they gloat over them."
lessons: Standing up against a bully on behalf of someone who has been wronged — even at personal risk — is a form of moral courage that Islam honors.
Those enforcing unjust systems often overreach into areas that even their own peers find indefensible, revealing the moral bankruptcy of oppression.
Protecting the dignity of others, particularly in their access to basic necessities, is an act of justice that transcends tribal or ideological lines.
practical_advice: If you witness someone being treated unjustly, ask yourself what Abu al-Bakhtari asked: 'What is actually going on here?' — gathering facts before acting prevents reactive escalation while still enabling principled intervention.
When enforcing rules or boundaries in your own life, examine whether you are applying them fairly or whether ego and power have begun to distort your judgment, as they did with Abu Jahl.
If you are in a leadership or gatekeeping role, be careful not to weaponize your position against people's basic needs — such actions erode moral authority and invite legitimate resistance.

title: The Prophet Continues Calling to Islam Fearlessly Despite the Boycott
summary: Despite Quraysh's persecution and boycott, the Prophet continued calling people to Islam by day and night, openly and secretly, fearing no one — protected by Allah through his uncle Abu Talib and the Banu Hashim.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) continued as before, calling upon his people by day and night, secretly and openly, calling out the commands of God Almighty, fearing no man. God having provided the Messenger of God (SAAS) with protection against Quraysh through the aid of his uncle and his relatives of the Banii Hashim and the Bans 'Abd al-Muywlib, Quraysh were unable to treat him with the violence they wished. Consequently they set about mocking, ridiculing him and disputing with him.
lessons: Unrelenting commitment to one's mission, regardless of the social and physical cost, is a defining quality of prophetic character and one we can aspire to in our own spheres of responsibility.
When direct violence is blocked, opponents often resort to ridicule and mockery — recognizing this pattern helps us not to be destabilized by it.

Divine protection often comes through human means — the loyalty of family and clan was Allah's instrument for shielding the Prophet, reminding us that human solidarity and divine help are not separate things.
practical_advice: If you are being ridiculed or mocked for your values or beliefs, recognize this as a sign that your message has reached those who feel threatened by it — it is a form of acknowledgment, not just rejection.
Continue your work and your calling consistently, even during periods of opposition — consistency itself is a powerful statement of conviction.
Be grateful for and actively strengthen the relationships with people who protect and support you — they are among Allah's greatest gifts to you.

title: Quraysh Mock the Prophet and Quranic Revelation Responds — Abu Lahab, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, al-As ibn Wa'il
summary: Quraysh leaders including Abu Lahab, Umayyah ibn Khalaf, and al-As ibn Wa'il mocked and slandered the Prophet, and Allah responded with specific Quranic verses addressing each of them by name or implication.
story: Quraysh were unable to treat him with the violence they wished. Consequently they set about mocking, ridiculing him and disputing with him. The Qur'an then began coming down concerning the evil deeds of Quraysh and those who had declared themselves to be his enemies. Some of these were mentioned by name to us, while others were inferred among those polytheists to whom general reference was made. Ibn Ishaq mentions Abii Lahab and the revelation of a chapter concerning him; also, he referred to Umayya b. Khalaf and the revelation of the words of the Almighty, "Woe to every defaming slanderer!" (sarat al-Humaza; CIV, v.1). The whole sayat refers to the latter man. Also there was reference to al-cA~b. Wa'il in the revelation, "And have you then seen him who disbelieved in our signs and who said, 'Certainly I will he awarded wealth and children'" (snrat Maryam; XIX, v.77).
lessons: Those who use their tongues to slander, defame, and mock truth-bearers will face accountability — the Quran's direct responses to these individuals is a reminder that no act of oppression is overlooked.
The Quran's responsiveness to real-time events in the Prophet's life shows that divine guidance is intimately connected to lived human experience, not detached from it.
Wealth and social status do not protect a person from divine accountability — al-As ibn Wa'il's boast about wealth and children was met with a direct Quranic rebuke.
practical_advice: When you are being slandered or defamed, resist the urge to descend into a public war of words — trust that truth carries its own weight and that accountability comes in forms beyond your control.
Be mindful of how you speak about others, especially those whose beliefs differ from yours — mockery and defamation corrode the mocker's character more than the target's.
If you find yourself using your wealth or status to feel superior or immune to consequences, examine that feeling carefully — it is one of the most spiritually dangerous forms of arrogance.

title: Abu Jahl Threatens to Insult Allah If the Prophet Does Not Stop Criticizing Their Gods
summary: Abu Jahl threatened to insult Allah if the Prophet continued criticizing Quraysh's idols, prompting the Quranic command not to insult what others worship, to prevent greater harm.
story: And then there was Aba Jahl h. Hisham and his saying to the Prophet of God (SAAS), "You had better stop insulting our gods, or we will insult your God whom you worship." Regardmg this, God's revelation came: "Do not insult those whom they worship besides God, for they, in enmity and without knowledge, will insult God" (sorat al-Ancam; VI, v.108).
lessons: Wisdom in calling to truth sometimes means restraining a permissible act to prevent a greater harm — not every battle needs to be fought on every front simultaneously.
The Quran models a sophisticated ethical framework: even when you are in the right, your actions must be evaluated in terms of their consequences for those you are trying to reach.
Abu Jahl's threat reveals how oppressors often escalate when they feel cornered — understanding this pattern helps in crafting more effective and less reactive responses.
practical_advice: Before speaking or acting on a matter of principle, consider whether your action might provoke a response that causes greater harm than the problem you are addressing.
When in conflict with someone who holds different beliefs, find language and approaches that do not invite unnecessary escalation — de-escalation is not weakness, it is strategic wisdom.
Reflect on areas of your life where holding your tongue or moderating your expression might actually serve your deeper goals better than full directness.

title: Al-Nadr ibn al-Harith Competes with the Prophet's Storytelling by Narrating Persian Tales
summary: Al-Nadr ibn al-Harith would follow the Prophet's teaching sessions by narrating Persian legends, dismissing the Quran as mere ancient stories — and Allah revealed verses directly addressing this mockery.
story: Also, there was al-Nadr b. al-Hiwith b. Kalada b. 'Alqarna, or 'Alqarna b. Kalada according to al-Suhayli and others, who used to sit down after the Prophet of God (SAAS) had finished one of his sessions in which he would recite the Qur'an and pray to God. AI-Nadr would then narrate tales of Rustum and Isfandiyzr and the wars between them in ancient Persia. Then he would comment, "I swear by God, Muhammad is no better a story-teller than me. His talk is only of ancient legends from which he borrows, just as I do." And so God Almighty sent down: "And they say: 'These are only legends of the Ancients that he has borrowed; they are dictated to him morning and evening'" (swat al-Furqan; XXV, v.5). And also: "Woe to every sinful liar" (sarat al-JZthlyya; XLV, v.7).
lessons: Reducing divine revelation to mere human storytelling is a form of intellectual arrogance that blinds a person to the truth — the dismissal of the Quran as 'old stories' was one of the earliest and most persistent forms of rejection.
Those who compete with truth by offering entertainment or distraction are not engaging with the actual content of the message — evasion masquerading as intellectual critique is not genuine inquiry.
Allah's direct response to al-Nadr's mockery shows that every attempt to undermine revelation is noted and answered — truth does not need to prove itself through argument alone.
practical_advice: When someone dismisses your values or beliefs as outdated or borrowed rather than engaging with their substance, do not feel obligated to defend yourself through their frame — the quality of what you stand for speaks for itself.
Be honest with yourself about whether your objections to something are genuine intellectual concerns or whether they are motivated by a desire to not change or be accountable.

If you find yourself using distraction, humor, or entertainment to avoid confronting serious spiritual questions, create space to sit with those questions honestly — avoidance only delays the reckoning.

title: Al-Nadr ibn al-Harith Is Silenced by the Prophet in the Mosque — The Debate About Idols as Firewood for Hell
summary: Al-Nadr ibn al-Harith argued with the Prophet in the mosque but was ultimately silenced when the Prophet recited Quranic verses declaring that idols and their worshippers would be fuel for hellfire — leading Abd Allah ibn al-Ziba'ra to attempt a counter-argument.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "One day, so we have been told, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat down in the mosque with al-Walid b. d-Mughira. Al-Nadr b. al-Hzith came over and sat with them; also present were several men of Quraysh. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke, but al-Nadr argued with him. T h e Messenger of God (SAAS) then addressed al-Nadr and ultimately silenced him. Having done so, he then recited to them all, 'You, along with that which you worship besides God, are the firewood for hell; you are on your way there. Were these gods they would not be ending up there; and all there will remain therein forever. Therein wailing is their lot, and therein they hear not' (snrat al-Anhiyri'; XXI, v.98-100). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose just as 'Abd A l l ~ hb. al-Ziba'ra al-Sahml approached and sat down. "Al-Walid b. al-Mughira spoke to him, 'I swear, al-Nadr b. al-H~ith's foregoing argument with Ibn 'Abd al-Murcalib (i.e. Muhammad) was indeed short lived!
