Muhammad (silenced him by) claiming that we and those gods of ours we worship are to be firewood for hell-fire!' "'Abd All& b. al-ZibaCra replied, 'If I'd found h i saying that, I'd have disputed with him. Ask Muhammad: "Is everything besides God that is worshipped, along with those who worship them, to be'"
lessons: The power of divine speech is not in rhetorical cleverness but in the clarity and weight of truth — the Prophet silenced his opponents not through debate tactics but through reciting revelation.
Those who are defeated in one argument quickly look for new angles of attack — persistence in opposition to truth is a form of stubbornness that ultimately harms only the one who persists.
Public forums where truth is proclaimed are important — the mosque setting reminds us that sacred spaces should be places of honest and courageous discourse.
practical_advice: When you are in a difficult discussion or debate about your values, you do not always need to have the perfect argument ready — sometimes the clearest and most direct statement of what you believe is more powerful than any rhetorical maneuver.
Notice when someone who has lost one argument immediately pivots to find a new one — this pattern often signals that the issue is not intellectual curiosity but resistance to change.
After a difficult confrontation or debate, give yourself space to process what happened before reacting — the Prophet's act of simply rising and leaving after making his point is itself instructive.

title: The Debate Over Idol Worship and the Argument of Ibn al-Ziba'ra
summary: Ibn al-Ziba'ra challenged the Prophet's statement that idolaters and their gods would be fuel for hellfire, arguing that angels, Jesus, and Uzayr are also worshipped yet are righteous. The Prophet refuted this, and God revealed verses clarifying the distinction.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) then arose just as 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ziba'ra al-Sahmi approached and sat down. Al-Walid b. al-Mughira spoke to him, 'I swear, al-Nadr b. al-Hārith's foregoing argument with Ibn 'Abd al-Muttalib (i.e. Muhammad) was indeed short lived! Muhammad (silenced him by) claiming that we and those gods of ours we worship are to be firewood for hell-fire!' 'Abd Allāh b. al-Ziba'ra replied, 'If I'd found him saying that, I'd have disputed with him. Ask Muhammad: "Is everything besides God that is worshipped, along with those who worship them, to be firewood for hell? We worship angels, the Jews worship Uzayr, and the Christians worship Jesus."' Al-Walid and those with him at the meeting were impressed by what Ibn al-Ziba'ra had said, and thought he had made a cogent and winning argument. This was reported to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who replied, 'All those wanting to be worshipped aside from God will be there in hell with those who worship them. Those people merely worship devils, and those they (the devils) have ordered them to worship.' God Almighty then revealed, 'Those to whom good has already gone from us will be kept far away from it (hell). They will not hear its slightest sound, but will remain forever in what it is they have so desired' (surat al-Anbiya'; XXI; v.101-2). This verse is referring to Jesus, 'Uzayr and those rabbis and monks who lived in obedience to God Almighty. And regarding their assertion that they were worshipping angels and that these were the daughters of God, He sent down: 'And they say that God the Beneficent has taken a son for himself. All glory be to Him! No, (not that) but they are honoured servants' (surat al-Anbiya'; XXI, v.26). And then there were the verses following that. Concerning the good impression made upon the polytheists by what al-Ziba'ra said, God sent down: 'And when the son of Mary is mentioned as an example, your people make a great outcry. They ask, "Are our gods better, or is he?" They only raise this objection for argument's sake. They really are contentious people!' (surat al-Zukhruf; XLIII, v.57-8). This argumentation they pursued was futile. And they were aware of that, because they were an Arab people. It is part of their language that ma, i.e. "that which," in the above quotation implies in Arabic something that does not think. The statement: 'You, along with that which you worship besides God are the firewood for hell; you are on your way there' (surat al-Anbiya'; XXI, v.98) refers to the rocks fashioned as idols. It does not include the angels that they claimed to be worshipping inside these images. Nor does it include Jesus, Uzayr or any of the holy persons because that item of language, ma, cannot include them, directly or by implication. They well knew that the comparison they were making to Jesus, son of Mary, was futile. As the Almighty stated, 'They only raise this objection for argument's sake. They really are contentious people' (surat al-Zukhruf; XLIII, v.57-8). God also stated, 'He', (meaning Jesus) 'was merely a servant whom We favoured' (that is, by prophethood) 'and We made him an example for the people of Israel' (surat al-Zukhruf; XLIII, v.59). That is, as an indication of the totality of God's power to achieve His wishes, having created him from a female without a male. Similarly, God created Eve from a male without a female, and Adam without either, while He created all the rest of mankind from both male and female.
lessons: Truth does not need clever rhetoric to prevail; when arguments are built on distortion, God Himself clarifies the reality.
Sophistry and emotional manipulation can temporarily impress an audience, but sincere engagement with truth ultimately exposes hollow reasoning.
God distinguishes between those who sincerely seek truth and those who argue merely to win; our intentions matter profoundly.
practical_advice: When someone twists your words or misrepresents your position, take a breath before responding and focus on clarifying the truth calmly rather than matching their combativeness.
If you feel confused by seemingly clever arguments against your beliefs, return to foundational principles and seek scholarly guidance rather than letting doubt fester in isolation.
Practice distinguishing between genuine questions and argumentative provocation; respond to the former with patience and the latter with measured, clear boundaries.

title: Al-Walid b. al-Mughira Questions Why Revelation Came to Muhammad
summary: Al-Walid b. al-Mughira complained that revelation was given to Muhammad rather than to himself or Abu Mas'ud al-Thaqafi, whom he considered greater leaders. God revealed verses responding to this arrogance.
story: Ibn Ishaq also referred to al-Walid b. al-Mughira as having said, 'Does God make revelation to Muhammad and not me, even though I am chief and leader of Quraysh and also ignores Abu Mas'ud 'Amr b. 'Amr al-Thaqafi, lord of Thaqif, even though we are the greatest men of the two towns?' Regarding this statement, God sent down: 'And they say, "Why was this Qur'an not revealed to some great men of the two towns?"' (surat al-Zukhruf; XLIII, v.31), along with other verses thereafter.
lessons: Worldly status and social rank have no bearing on divine selection; God chooses whom He wills based on qualities beyond human measure.
Pride in one's position can become a veil that prevents recognizing truth even when it stands directly before us.
The expectation that God must conform to human hierarchies reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of the nature of divine will.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or passed by for an opportunity you believed you deserved, examine whether entitlement may be clouding your perspective.
Reflect regularly on the difference between external status and inner spiritual readiness; invest in the latter rather than assuming the former guarantees recognition.
If resentment toward someone else's success arises within you, use it as a prompt for honest self-examination rather than directing that energy outward.

title: Ubayy b. Khalaf Pressures 'Uqba b. Abi Mu'ayt to Spit in the Prophet's Face
summary: Ubayy b. Khalaf threatened to end his friendship with 'Uqba unless he publicly dishonored the Prophet, which 'Uqba did. God later revealed verses about the regret of those who followed wrongdoers over the Messenger.
story: Ibn Ishaq also related, 'Ubayy b. Khalaf said to 'Uqba b. Abi Mu'ayt, "Have I not heard that you sat down with Muhammad and listened to him? I'll never set eyes on you again unless you spit in his face!" And 'Uqba, that enemy of God, God damn him, did that! And so God sent down, "And a day (will come) when the evil-doer will bite his hands and say, 'Would that I had taken my path with the Messenger; woe is to me! How I wish I had not taken so-and-so as my friend!'" (surat al-Furqan; XXV, v.27,28), along with following verses.'
lessons: Peer pressure can lead even someone with initial openness to truth into acts of cowardice and cruelty; choosing one's companions wisely is a matter of moral survival.
The regret of following a wrongdoing companion is one of the most profound forms of loss described in scripture; we are shaped by those we keep close.
Standing firm under social pressure to harm or mock the righteous is a test of character that defines a person's spiritual trajectory.
practical_advice: Identify relationships in your life where you feel pressured to act against your conscience, and begin setting boundaries around those interactions.
When a friend's expectation of loyalty conflicts with your ethical values, remind yourself that true friendship never demands you compromise your integrity.
If you have acted wrongly because of someone else's pressure, acknowledge your own responsibility in the choice rather than only blaming the instigator, and seek to make amends.

title: Ubayy b. Khalaf Challenges the Prophet with a Crumbled Bone
summary: Ubayy b. Khalaf crumbled an old bone in his hand and blew it toward the Prophet, mockingly asking if God could restore it. The Prophet affirmed God's power to resurrect, and God revealed verses on resurrection from Surah Ya-Sin.
story: Ibn Ishaq also related, 'And Ubayy b. Khalaf took an old, disintegrating bone to him and said, "O Muhammad, do you claim that God can restore this after it has disintegrated?" He then crumbled it in his hand and blew it into the air towards the Messenger of God (SAAS). He replied, "Yes, I do say that. God will restore it and you too, even after you are like that. And then He will place you in the fire!" And God revealed, "He coins a metaphor for us, forgetting his own creation, saying, 'Who will give life to bones that are rotten?' Say, 'He will give life to them who first created them! He has knowledge of all creation'"' (surat Ya-Sin; XXXVI, v.78-9) to the end of that surat.'
lessons: Mockery and ridicule directed at faith often reveals the mocker's own unacknowledged fear of the truths being proclaimed.
The argument for resurrection is embedded in the miracle of original creation itself; the One who created from nothing can certainly restore what existed.
The Prophet's calm and direct response to contempt demonstrates that dignity is maintained not by retaliation but by confident truth-telling.
practical_advice: When your beliefs or values are ridiculed, resist the urge to match the other person's energy; instead, offer a measured, grounded response and let the truth speak for itself.
In moments of doubt about divine power or providence, reflect on the original creation of the universe and of life itself as evidence of what is possible.
Practice responding to provocative people with clarity and composure; prepare calm, principled answers to common challenges so you are not caught off guard.

title: The Polytheists of Quraysh Propose Mutual Worship with the Prophet
summary: Four leading Qurayshi polytheists blocked the Prophet during his circumambulation of the Ka'ba and proposed that he worship their gods if they worship his. God revealed Surah Al-Kafirun in response, making the boundaries of monotheism absolute.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'I have been told that once, when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was making a circumambulation of the ka'ba, near its doorway, he was obstructed by al-Aswad b. al-Muttalib, al-Walid b. al-Mughira, Umayya b. Khalaf and al-'As b. Wa'il. They said to him, "O Muhammad, come on! We'll worship what you do if you'll worship what we do. Let's just co-operate, you and us, in the whole thing!" Regarding them God revealed, "Say: O you disbelievers! I will not worship what you worship"' (surat al-Kafirun; CIX; v.1-2).'
lessons: Compromise on core principles of belief may be presented as cooperation or pragmatism, but accepting it leads to the dissolution of one's foundational identity.
The clarity of the Quranic response — an unambiguous, dignified refusal — models how to decline manipulation without hostility or lengthy debate.
Standing firm in one's convictions even when pressured by powerful opponents is itself a form of worship and an act of courage.
practical_advice: When someone proposes a compromise that would require you to betray your core values, practice giving a clear and respectful refusal without over-explaining or apologizing.
Recognize that offers of mutual accommodation can sometimes be strategies to gradually erode firm commitments; scrutinize what is truly being asked of you.
Strengthen your sense of identity and values in quiet moments so that when challenges arise in public or under pressure, your response comes from a place of inner clarity rather than panic.

title: Abu Jahl Mocks the Zaqqum Tree
summary: When the Prophet recited verses about the zaqqum tree of hell, Abu Jahl mockingly called it dates and butter and invited people to enjoy it. God revealed verses affirming the zaqqum as the food of sinners.
story: 'When Abu Jahl heard reference to the al-zaqqum tree, he said, "Do you know what this al-zaqqum is? It is dates mashed in butter!" He added, "Bring it along; we'll enjoy it!" And so God revealed, "The zaqqum tree is the food of sinners"' (surat al-Dukhan; XLIV, v.43-4).
lessons: Ridiculing divine warnings with humor is a psychological defense mechanism against confronting uncomfortable truths; it does not make those truths disappear.

The leaders of disbelief often used mockery as a tool to discourage those around them from taking the message seriously; we must be careful not to let others' cynicism shape our relationship with truth.
Warnings about consequences — whether spiritual or worldly — deserve serious reflection rather than dismissal, no matter how uncomfortable they make us feel.
practical_advice: When you encounter important warnings or serious truths, resist the social instinct to deflect with humor; sit with the discomfort and examine what the message is asking of you.
If someone around you dismisses spiritual or moral concerns with mockery, do not let their reaction become your benchmark for what deserves serious consideration.
Cultivate the habit of reflecting on the long-term consequences of your choices rather than focusing only on immediate comfort and enjoyment.

title: The Prophet Frowns at the Blind Man Ibn Umm Maktum
summary: While the Prophet was engaged in conversation with a prominent Qurayshi leader hoping to guide him to Islam, the blind companion Ibn Umm Maktum interrupted repeatedly seeking Quranic recitation. The Prophet frowned and turned away, and God revealed verses of correction.
story: Ibn Ishaq also related, 'Al-Walid b. al-Mughira was standing talking with the Messenger of God (SAAS) who was addressing him, wishing to convert him to Islam, when Ibn Umm Maktam, that is, the son of 'Atika, daughter of 'Abd Allah b. 'Ankatha, passed by. Ibn Umm Maktum, who was blind, addressed the Messenger of God (SAAS) and began asking him to recite the Quran. This so upset the Messenger of God (SAAS) as to anger him, and this was because he was interfering with his efforts to convert al-Walid to Islam as he was hoping. When the blind man persisted, he turned away from him frowning and ignored him. And so God Almighty sent down, "... and he frowned and turned away when the blind man came to him ..." up to the words, "... exalted, purified"' (surat 'Abasa; LXXX, v.1-14). It is also said that the person who was talking to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when Ibn Umm Maktum came along was Umayya b. Khalaf. But God knows best.
lessons: Divine correction came swiftly and directly to the Prophet himself, demonstrating that no one is above accountability and that God honors the sincere seeker over worldly status.
The eager and sincere believer — even one who is physically disadvantaged — holds greater worth in God's sight than the powerful person who remains distant from faith.
Our strategic calculations about who is most worth our time can blind us to the person standing right in front of us who has genuine need and sincere desire.
practical_advice: Notice when you are prioritizing impressive or influential people over those who are more vulnerable or less socially prominent; make a conscious effort to correct this imbalance.
When you make a mistake in how you treat someone — even with good intentions behind it — accept the correction with humility rather than defensiveness and make repair.
Set aside time to give undivided attention to people in your life who sincerely seek your guidance or support, rather than always saving your best energy for those you hope to impress.

title: The Prostration During Surah Al-Najm and the Return of Emigrants from Abyssinia
summary: When the Prophet recited Surah Al-Najm, Muslims, polytheists, and spirits all prostrated together, which spread news suggesting Mecca had accepted Islam. A group of emigrants returned from Abyssinia on the basis of this misunderstood report.
story: What is well established in the sahih collections of traditions and elsewhere is that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat down one day with the polytheists and God revealed to him, 'By the star when it sets, your companion has not gone astray' (surat al-Najm; LIII, v.1-2). When he had recited this surat completely, he prostrated in prayer as did the Muslims, polytheists and spirits all. But that (the prostrations of the polytheists along with the Muslims) had a reason which is mentioned by exegetes in their commentary on the words of Almighty God, 'And we did not send before you any messenger or any prophet without him wishing something and the devil then influencing his wish; and so God annuls whatever the devil has inspired, and affirms His own signs. And God is knowledgeable, wise' (surat al-Hajj; XXII, v.52). Al-Bukhari stated, 'Abu Ma'mar related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Warith, quoting Ayyub, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) prostrated himself in prayer having recited surat al-Najm, and all the Muslims, the polytheists and men and spirits alike prostrated too."' Al-Bukhari also stated, 'Muhammad b. Bashshar related to us, as did Ghundar and Shu'ba, from Abu Ishaq, who said, "I heard al-Aswad say, quoting 'Abd Allah, 'The Prophet (SAAS) recited surat al-Najm in Mecca and prostrated in prayer. And those there with him also prostrated except for one old man who picked up a handful of dust or dirt which he raised to his forehead, saying, "This suffices for me." And later I saw him killed as an unbeliever.'"' Imam Ahmad (b. Hanbal) stated that Ibrahim recounted to him, saying, 'Rabbah related to us, from Ma'mar, from Ibn Tawus, from 'Ikrima b. Khalid, from Ja'far b. al-Muttalib b. Abi Wada'a, who heard his father say, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) recited surat al-Najm in Mecca and then prostrated, and all those there with him did the same. But I lifted up my head, declining to prostrate." Al-Muttalib had not then accepted Islam. After that he never heard anyone recite surat al-Najm without him prostrating along with the reciter.' What is implied here is that when the narrator saw that the polytheists had prostrated themselves in imitation of the Messenger of God (SAAS) he believed that they had accepted Islam, made peace with him and that there was no longer any dispute between them. The news of that quickly spread and reached the Emigrants in Abyssinia, and they believed it to be true. A group of them, hoping that was so, came back, while another group remained there. Each of them was well intentioned and correct in acting as they did.
lessons: Powerful divine words can move even hardened hearts momentarily; the recitation of Quran carries a force that transcends intellectual resistance.
Acting on incomplete information — even with the best of intentions — can lead to unintended consequences; seeking verification before major decisions is essential.
The power of truth is such that even those opposed to it can be momentarily overwhelmed by its presence, though the heart's ultimate response depends on the will of the individual.
practical_advice: Before making major life decisions based on news or information, invest the effort to verify from multiple reliable sources, especially when the stakes are high.
When you hear something that seems too good to be true — that a long-standing problem has suddenly resolved — allow yourself cautious hope while still seeking confirmation.
Spend time in regular recitation or listening to Quran, and approach it with an open heart; allow its words to reach deeper than intellectual engagement alone.

title: Return of Emigrants from Abyssinia After False News of Peace
summary: A group of Muslim emigrants in Abyssinia returned to Mecca after hearing false news that the Quraysh had accepted Islam, only to find the persecution ongoing. Ibn Ishaq names all 33 men who returned.
story: polytheists had prostrated themselves in imitation of the Messenger of God (SAAS) he believed that they had accepted Islam, made peace with him and that there was no longer any dispute between them. The news of that quickly spread and reached the Emigrants in Abyssinia, and they believed it to be true. A group of them, hoping that was so, came back, while another group remained there. Each of them was well intentioned and correct in acting as they did. Ibn Ishaq gave the names of all of them that returned. These were: 'Uthman b. 'Affan and his wife Ruqayya, daughter of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abu Hudhayfa b. 'Utba b. Rabi'a and his wife Sahla, daughter of Suhayl, 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh b. Ri'ab, 'Utba b. Ghazwan, al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwa, Mus'ab b. 'Umayr, Suwaybir b. Sa'd, Tulayb b. 'Umayr, 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf, al-Miqdad b. 'Amr, 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud, Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad and his wife Umm Salama, daughter of Abu Umayya b. al-Mughira, and Shammas b. 'Uthman. Also there were Salama b. Hisham and 'Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a who were imprisoned in Mecca until after the battles of Badr, Uhud and al-Khandaq. There is some dispute over 'Ammar b. Yasir, as to whether or not he ever went to Abyssinia. There also returned Mu'attib b. 'Awf, 'Uthman b. Maz'un, his son al-Sa'ib, along with his two brothers Qudama and 'Abd Allah, both sons of Maz'un. Then there were Khunays b. Hudhafa and Hisham b. al-'As b. Wa'il, the latter being imprisoned in Mecca until after the battle of al-Khandaq. 'Amir b. Rabi'a and his wife Layla, daughter of Abi Hathma, also returned, as did 'Abd Allah b. Makhrama. 'Abd Allah b. Suhayl b. 'Amr also returned, but he was imprisoned until the battle of Badr, when he joined the Muslims and fought on their side. Also Abu Sabra b. Abi Ruhm returned, along with his wife Umm Kulthum, daughter of Suhayl. As did al-Sakran b. 'Amr b. 'Abd Shams and his wife Sawda, daughter of Zam'a. He died in Mecca before the migration to Medina and the Messenger of God (SAAS), thereafter married his widow. Sa'd b. Khawla, Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah, 'Amr b. al-Harith b. Zuhayr, Suhayl b. Bayda' and 'Amr b. Abi Sarh also returned. They totalled 33 men; may God be pleased with them! Al-Bukhari stated, ''A'isha reported that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I was given a vision of your abode in exile, a place of date-palms between two tracts of black stones."' Some (of those who had returned from Abyssinia) then migrated to Medina, while the rest of those who had remained in Abyssinia did so as well.
lessons: Acting on hope and good faith, even when the outcome is not what we expected, is itself a form of courage and righteousness.
Uncertainty and false news are tests of character; those who acted with good intentions were still honored by God regardless of the outcome.
Community bonds are powerful — the emigrants returned out of longing for their homeland and their Prophet, reminding us that love and belonging are deeply human needs.
practical_advice: When you receive hopeful news, it is natural to act on it; if things don't turn out as expected, remind yourself that your intentions matter deeply to God.
If you feel disappointed after acting on hope that didn't materialize, reflect on the example of the emigrants: they were praised for their good intentions regardless of the outcome.
When facing uncertainty about a major life decision, consult trustworthy people and proceed with sincere intention, trusting that God honors honest effort.

title: The Prophet's Vision of Medina Given to the Emigrants
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) told 'A'isha that he had been shown a vision of the future home of the emigrants — a land of date-palms between two tracts of black stones, which was Medina.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, ''A'isha reported that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "I was given a vision of your abode in exile, a place of date-palms between two tracts of black stones."' Some (of those who had returned from Abyssinia) then migrated to Medina, while the rest of those who had remained in Abyssinia did so as well.
lessons: Divine reassurance often comes before the trial is over, as a mercy to sustain the believer's heart through hardship.
God's plans for the believer are greater than what the believer can currently see — exile and suffering can be the path to a promised home.
Prophetic visions and glad tidings remind us that hardship is not permanent and that God does not abandon those who sacrifice for His sake.
practical_advice: When you feel displaced, far from home, or uncertain about the future, remember that God may already be preparing a place of belonging for you that you cannot yet see.
Hold onto signs of hope, however small — just as the emigrants were given a vision of Medina before they ever arrived there.
Journal or reflect on moments where things worked out better than you feared; these become your personal evidence that hardship often precedes relief.

title: 'Abd Allah b. Mas'ud and the Change in Prayer Rules Upon Return from Abyssinia
summary: When the emigrants returned from Abyssinia, they greeted the Prophet during prayer as they had before, but he no longer replied. When they asked him why, he explained that prayer demands complete focus and silence.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Yahya b. Hammad related to us, quoting Abu 'Awana, from Sulayman b. Ibrahim, from 'Alqama, from 'Abd Allah, who said, 'We used to greet the Prophet (SAAS), while he prayed, and he would reply to us. But when we came back from the land of the Negus we would greet him the same way, but he would not reply. So we said to him, "O Messenger of God, when we used to greet you, you would reply to us. But since our return from the land of the Negus, you don't reply."' He responded, 'Prayer keeps one engrossed.'" Al-Bukhari also related this, as did Muslim, Abu Da'ud and al-Nasa'i, by different chains of authority, from Sulayman b. Mahran, from al-A'mash. This substantiates the interpretation of those who explain the authentic account of Zayd b. Arqam that is in both Sahih collections: "We used to talk during the prayer, until God's revealed verse was sent down, 'Stand up before God in humility' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.238). And so we were ordered to remain silent and were forbidden to talk."
lessons: Religious practice develops and deepens over time; what was once acceptable may be refined as the community grows in its understanding.
Asking sincere questions when confused is a virtue, not a weakness — the companions asked directly and received a clear, gentle answer.
Worship requires presence of heart and mind; distraction, even by good intentions like greeting a beloved person, can diminish the quality of our connection with God.
practical_advice: When a spiritual practice or rule changes and you don't understand why, approach the matter with curiosity rather than frustration, and seek knowledge from reliable sources.
Practice mindful presence during your prayers or moments of worship — set aside greetings, phone checks, and wandering thoughts as an act of respect for that sacred time.
If you feel confused by changing expectations in a community or relationship, ask directly and calmly rather than assuming the worst.

title: 'Uthman b. Maz'un Gives Up the Protection of al-Walid b. al-Mughira
summary: 'Uthman b. Maz'un, seeing his fellow Muslims suffer persecution while he was protected by a powerful polytheist, voluntarily relinquished that protection, declaring he would accept only the protection of God.
story: Regarding 'Uthman b. Maz'un, Salih b. Ibrahim b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Awf related to me from someone who was told the following about 'Uthman, 'When 'Uthman b. Maz'un saw the plight of the followers of the Messenger of God (SAAS), while he himself was able to come and go freely under the protection of al-Walid b. al-Mughira, he said, "By God, here I am moving about freely under the protection of a polytheist while my friends and followers in religion suffer harm and insult for God that do not afflict me, and this constitutes a great deficiency in me!" 'So off he went to al-Walid b. al-Mughira and told him, "O Abu 'Abd al-Shams, you have fulfilled your pledge, but now I'm giving back to you your protection." '"Why is that, cousin?" he asked. "Has any one of my people harmed you?" '"No," he replied, "it's just that what I want is the protection of God, the Almighty and Glorious. I don't want the protection of anyone else." '"Well then," al-Walid said, "you should go to the mosque and give me my protection back in public, just as I declared my protection over you." 'And so they both did go to the mosque, where al-Walid b. al-Mughira announced, "This is 'Uthman, and he has come to relieve me of my protection of him." ''Uthman said, "He speaks the truth. I have found him to be trustworthy and honourable in his protection, but I prefer to be protected only by God. I have given him back his pledge of protection."'
lessons: True solidarity means refusing to accept comfort that is denied to your brothers and sisters; 'Uthman's conscience would not let him enjoy safety while others suffered.
Reliance on God alone — even when it means giving up worldly security — is a mark of deep faith and spiritual maturity.
Acknowledging a 'deficiency' in oneself honestly, as 'Uthman did, is the first step toward moral and spiritual growth.
practical_advice: If you find yourself in a position of comfort or privilege while others around you suffer unjustly, ask yourself what, if anything, you are called to give up or share in solidarity.
Practice tawakkul (trust in God) by gradually reducing your dependence on human approval, social status, or worldly protections as your source of security.
When you feel a pang of guilt or discomfort about an inequity you benefit from, take that feeling seriously — it may be your conscience pointing you toward a more principled path.

title: 'Uthman b. Maz'un Struck in the Eye After Defending His Faith
summary: After relinquishing al-Walid's protection, 'Uthman b. Maz'un was struck in the eye by a man of Quraysh during a gathering. When al-Walid offered to restore his protection, 'Uthman refused, saying his other eye longed for the same honor.
story: 'Uthman, God bless him, then left and joined an assembly of Quraysh who were being addressed by Labid b. Rabi'a b. Malik b. Ja'far. 'Uthman sat down with them. Labid spoke a verse: 'Is not all but God vanity?' ''Uthman commented, "You speak the truth!" 'Labid then said, "And all pleasures must fade." 'At this 'Uthman protested, "You lie! The pleasures of paradise will last forever!" 'Labid commented, "O men of Quraysh, those who sat with you used not to be so insulted; when did this come about so?" 'One man replied, "This is just one of those fools allied with him; they have abandoned our faith. Don't take to heart what he says." ''Uthman spoke back to this man and they became angry with one another. The other man then got up and hit 'Uthman in the eye, making it black. Al-Walid, who was nearby and saw what had happened to 'Uthman, commented, "Well, nephew, your eye didn't need to suffer so; earlier you were fully protected!" ''Uthman replied, "Not so; I swear the only problem is that my good eye is in need of what the other one suffered for God's sake! Actually I am under the protection of One stronger and more capable than yourself, O Abu 'Abd al-Shams!" 'To this al-Walid replied, "Come on now, nephew, just come back under my protection." '"No," 'Uthman told him."'
lessons: Suffering for the sake of truth and principle can become a source of pride and spiritual honor rather than shame or victimhood.
Standing firm in one's convictions, even when physically attacked and offered an easy way out, is one of the highest forms of moral courage.
The believer's ultimate protection and dignity comes from God, not from the powerful figures of this world — 'Uthman's response to al-Walid embodies this conviction completely.
practical_advice: When you are mocked, belittled, or even harmed for standing by your principles, remind yourself that the discomfort is temporary but the integrity you preserve is lasting.
Rather than seeking validation or protection from worldly sources of power, invest in your relationship with God, which is the most durable source of inner security.

If someone offers to restore your comfort or status in exchange for compromising your values, pause and reflect on what 'Uthman said: refuse to return to a protection that costs you your spiritual integrity.

title: Abu Salama Seeks and Receives the Protection of Abu Talib
summary: When Abu Salama sought protection from Abu Talib, the Banu Makhzum objected that Abu Talib was already protecting his nephew Muhammad. Abu Talib defended his right to protect his sister's son, and Abu Lahab unexpectedly silenced the objectors.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Regarding Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad: my father, Ishaq b. Yasar related to me that Salama b. 'Abd Allah b. Abi Salama told him that when Abu Salama sought the protection of Abu Talib some men of the Banu Makhzum went to the latter and asked him, 'O Abu Talib, you've already given protection from us to your nephew Muhammad; how is it you are now doing the same for one of our own men?' "Abu Talib replied, 'He sought out my protection, and he is my sister's son. And if I am not to protect my sister's son, how could I protect my brother's son?' "Abu Lahab then arose and said, 'O men of Quraysh, you've treated this sheikh harshly, and you're constantly attacking him for giving protection to his own people. Either put an end to this, or let's all take his side in this so he attains his goal.' "They replied, 'All right, Abu Lahab, we'll stop behaving contrary to your wishes.' He had been their ally and assistant against the Messenger of God (SAAS), and they wanted to maintain that. "Abu Talib had high hopes of him when he heard him speak thus and anticipated his aid regarding the Messenger of God (SAAS). And so he spoke the following verses urging Abu Lahab to support himself and the Messenger of God (SAAS): 'A man with Abu 'Utayba as his uncle is secure from harm as in a pasture; I tell him - though how could my advice benefit him - O Abu Mu'tib, hold your ground firmly, And do not as long as you live accept a course you'll be blamed for, whenever you attend a gathering, Leave the path of the weak to others than yourself, for you were not created to stay weak, And fight, for fighting is just and you will never see a real warrior humbled until he surrenders. And why is it, when they have done you no great harm, nor abandoned you when having victory or defeat? May God punish 'Abd Shams, Nawfal, Taym and Makhzum for us with disaster and death, For their deserting our alliance after friendship and affection in order to attain things forbidden. You lie, by God's temple! Us dispossess Muhammad? Not even at the enclave did you see (us) turn against (him)."'
lessons: Those in positions of tribal or social authority have a profound responsibility to use that position to protect the vulnerable, as Abu Talib consistently did.
Help can sometimes come from unexpected quarters — even those who oppose you in other matters may act justly in a specific situation, as Abu Lahab did here.
Family loyalty and honor, when rightly ordered, can be a vehicle for protecting the oppressed and the righteous.
practical_advice: Do not assume that because someone opposes you in one area, they will never act in your favor — approach people as complex individuals capable of both hostility and justice.
If you are in a position of influence or authority, use it to speak up for those who are being treated harshly, even when it is socially costly.
When others question your loyalty to vulnerable people in your care, respond as Abu Talib did: with principled clarity about your obligations, not defensiveness.

title: Abu Bakr's Determination to Emigrate and His Encounter with Ibn al-Daghina
summary: After enduring ongoing suffering in Mecca, Abu Bakr sought the Prophet's permission to emigrate. He was granted it and set out, but was met by the tribal leader Ibn al-Daghina before reaching his destination.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "I was told by Muhammad b. Muslim al-Zuhri, from 'Urwa, from 'A'isha, that when Abu Bakr, 'the Trusting', God be pleased with him, experienced suffering in Mecca and witnessed the antagonism of Quraysh for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his own supporters, he asked permission from the Messenger of God (SAAS) to move away and he granted him this request. "And so Abu Bakr, God bless him, did leave and, having travelled a day or two away from Mecca, was met by Ibn al-Daghina, brother of the Banu al-Harith b. Bakr b. 'Abd Manat b. Kinana, who was at that time the leader of the Ahabish."
lessons: Even the closest and most devoted companions of the Prophet (SAAS) reached a limit of what they could endure — seeking relief from oppression is not weakness but a legitimate human response.
Every departure and every journey holds within it the possibility of an unexpected encounter that changes everything — Ibn al-Daghina's appearance would prove to be providential.
Asking permission and proceeding with the blessing of those we trust before making major life changes reflects wisdom and proper conduct in community.
practical_advice: When you are exhausted by a difficult environment or relationship, give yourself permission to seek relief — doing so is not a failure of faith but an acknowledgment of your human limits.
Before making a major decision to leave a difficult situation, consult with a trusted mentor or guide, as Abu Bakr consulted the Prophet (SAAS).
When setting out on a difficult journey, remain open to unexpected help or encounters along the way — sometimes God places assistance in the most unlooked-for places.

title: Abu Bakr Seeks Protection of Ibn al-Daghina
summary: Facing severe persecution from Quraysh, Abu Bakr left Mecca heading toward Abyssinia. He was met by Ibn al-Daghina, chief of the Ahabish, who offered him protection and escorted him back to Mecca.
story: witnessed the antagonism of Quraysh for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his own supporters, he asked permission from the Messenger of God (SAAS) to move away and he granted him this request. "And so Abii Bakr, God bless him, did leave and, having travelled a day or two away from Mecca, was met by Ibn al-Daghina, brother of the Bann al-Harith b. Bakr h. 'Abd ManXt b. K i n a a , who was at that time the leader of the Ahabish." Al-Waqidi gave this man's name as al-Harith b. Yazid, be being of the tribe of Bakr b. 'Ahd Manat h. Kinas. Al-Suhayli, however, gave his name as Malik. ('A'isha continued) "'What are you heading for, Abii Bakr?' the man asked. Aha Bakr replied, 'My people have offended me, treated me badly and forced me to leave.' '"Why is that? By God, you're a credit to the tribe, aiding those in distress, and you are kindly towards the needy. Go hack; you are under my protection!' "He did return with him and, back in Mecca, Ibn al-Daghina stood up with him and stated, '0 Quraysh, I have placed the son of Aha Quhafa under my protection. Let no man do anything but good to him.' "And they did leave him alone."
lessons: One's good character and reputation can become a source of protection even among those who are not of your faith — Abu Bakr's integrity preceded him.
There is honor in accepting help from unexpected sources; relying solely on human protection, however, is temporary and only God's protection is lasting.
Persecution and forced departure can feel like failure, but Allah may redirect your path in ways that preserve both your faith and your dignity.
practical_advice: When you feel expelled or rejected by your community, reflect on the positive character you have built — it speaks for you even when you cannot speak for yourself.
Do not be too proud to accept support from others during hardship; accepting help graciously is a sign of wisdom, not weakness.
If you must leave a toxic environment, do so with the permission and counsel of those wiser than you, as Abu Bakr sought the Prophet's permission before departing.

title: Abu Bakr's Mosque and the Quraysh's Fear of His Recitation
summary: After returning under Ibn al-Daghina's protection, Abu Bakr built a small mosque at his home and wept while reciting the Quran, drawing crowds and alarming Quraysh leaders who pressured Ibn al-Daghina to restrict him.
story: 'A'isha went on, "Aha Bakr used to have a mosque at the gate of his home among the Bana Jum& and there he would pray. He was a very sensitive man and would shed tears when reciting the Qur'm. Young men, slaves and women would stand nearby astonished at his hehaviour. "Some Quraysh men went to Ibn Daghina and told him, LOIbn Daghina, surely you didn't protect this man to harm us? When he prays and recites what Muhammad brought he is moved (to tears) and he then attracts attention; we fear that he may influence our young men, women and the weak. Go and tell him to go inside his house, where he can do as he wishes."' She went on, "So Ibn d-Daghina did go to Aba Bakr and told him, 'I didn't give you protection so that you could do your people harm. They take offence at the place you attend and feel that you are harming them. Go inside your house and do there whatever it is you want.' "(Aha Bakr asked) 'So shall I give you back your protection and rely on God?' "'Do return my protection.' "'Consider it returned.' "Ihn al-Daghina then stood and stated, '0 Quraysh, the son of Aba Quhafa has given me hack my protection over him. D o with him whatever you wish.'"
lessons: Sincere worship and emotional connection to the Quran can move hearts in ways no argument can — even enemies recognized the power of Abu Bakr's recitation.
Human protection has limits; when it becomes a constraint on worship, releasing it in favor of reliance on God is the more dignified choice.
Those who fear truth often do so not because it is harmful, but because they sense its power to transform those who hear it.
practical_advice: When external support comes with conditions that compromise your spiritual practice, calmly assess whether that support is truly serving you.
Let your worship be an expression of genuine emotion and connection rather than performance; authentic devotion resonates with people far more than formality.
If your spiritual practice is restricted or criticized, remember that even the closest allies of the Prophet faced such pressures — you are not alone in this struggle.

title: Abu Bakr Returns the Protection of Ibn al-Daghina and Relies on God Alone
summary: When Ibn al-Daghina ultimatum required Abu Bakr to either curtail his open worship or surrender his protection, Abu Bakr chose to return the protection and rely solely on Allah, demonstrating complete trust in God over human security.
story: Al-Bukhai then recounted the rest of the tradition concerning the emigration of Abii Bakr, God bless h i , with the Messenger of God (SAAS), as will be described hereafter. Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd d - W m a n b. al-@sim related to me, from his father al-@sim b. Muhammad, the son of b. Abii Bakr, 'the Trusting', as follows: 'He was confronted - that is Abii Bakr was confronted-after he had left the protection of Ibn al-Daghina, on his way to the ka'ba by some fool from Quraysh who tossed dirt up over his head. Then al-Walid b. al-Mnghira - or perhaps it was al-'AS b. Wa'il- passed by Abii Bakr, who spoke as follows: "Don't yon see how this idiot is behaving?" He was told, "You brought this on yourself." Meanwhile Aba Bakr kept repeating, "0 God, how forbearing You are!"'"
lessons: True tawakkul (reliance on God) means voluntarily surrendering human securities when they come at the cost of one's integrity and freedom of worship.
Responding to humiliation and injustice by turning to God rather than retaliating reveals a spiritual strength that cannot be threatened.
The response 'You brought this on yourself' reflects how oppressors often blame the oppressed — recognizing this pattern helps one not internalize false guilt.
practical_advice: When you are mistreated after standing for your values, resist the urge to retaliate; instead, use the moment to express gratitude to God for His forbearance as Abu Bakr did.
Release relationships or agreements that require you to diminish your worship or spiritual authenticity — God's protection is more enduring than any human covenant.
If others tell you that your suffering is your own fault for living by your principles, do not accept that narrative; holding firm to your values is never the cause of another's cruelty.

title: Hisham b. Amr Secretly Supplies the Besieged Banu Hashim
summary: During the social and economic boycott of Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib, Hisham b. Amr secretly brought camels loaded with food and wheat to them at night, demonstrating courageous compassion in defiance of Quraysh's oppressive decree.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "While the Bann H a s h i i and the Banii al-Muytalih were living in their house assigned to them by Quraysh in the document they had written, a certain group of Quraysh set about abrogating that document. "No one took more pains to do this than Hisham b.'Amr b. al-Hzrith b. Habib b. Nasr b. M&k b. His1 b. 'Amir b. Lu'ayy; this was because he was the nephew of Nadla b. Hishim b. 'Abd Manzf by his mother, and H i s h h had close ties to the Bann H s h i m . He was, moreover, deemed highly by his people. "When, at night, the Bann Hashim and the Bann al-Muttalib were restricted to their building in the enclave, he would, as I have been told, bring up a camel loaded down with food. When he reached the entrance to the enclave he would release the halter from the camel's head and strike its side to send it on down to them. Next time he would bring it loaded with wheat and do the same."
lessons: One person acting with sincere compassion and courage, even secretly, can make an immense difference to those who are suffering.
Kinship and human bonds have the power to pierce through systems of oppression; Hisham's family ties to Banu Hashim motivated him to act when others turned away.
Good deeds done in secret, without recognition, are among the most sincere expressions of character.
practical_advice: When you see people suffering from unjust systems, look for small, consistent acts of relief you can offer — even anonymous help carries immense moral weight.
Do not wait for others to take the first step in opposing injustice; Hisham acted before any coalition was formed.
Let family and community ties remind you of your responsibilities to others — compassion rooted in relationship is a powerful motivator for justice.

title: Hisham b. Amr Builds a Coalition to Abrogate the Boycott Document
summary: Hisham b. Amr approached four Quraysh leaders one by one — Zuhayr b. Abi Umayya, Mut'im b. Adi, Abu al-Bakhtari, and Zam'a b. al-Aswad — persuading each to join a secret coalition committed to revoking the unjust boycott document against Banu Hashim.
story: "He went to Zuhayr b. Abii Umayya h. al-Mughira h. 'Ahd All& b. 'Amr b. Makhznm, whose mother was 'Atika, daughter of 'Abd al-Mutralib, and said, 'Zuhayr, does it please you to eat and dress well and to marry women while your uncles are where you know them to be, engaging in no buying nor selling, arranging no marriages, nor having any arranged with them? For my part, I swear that if they were the uncles of Aha al-Hakam b. Hisham and you were to ask him to treat them as you have been asked to do, he would never have agreed to your request.' "'Shame on you, H i s h h ! What can I do? I'm just one man. If someone else would join me, I'd set about abrogating it.' "Hisham replied, 'You have found one such man.' 'Who is that?' he asked. 'Myself,' came the answer. 'Well,' Zuhayr went on, 'find us a third then.' "And so Hisham went to al-Mut'i h. 'Adi and told him, L M u t c i , are you pleased to have two clans of the Bann 'Abd Manaf suffer while you look on, in agreement with Quraysh about that? By God, if you enable them to do this they will soon be treating you the same way.' "'Damn it! What am I to do? I'm just one man,' Mutcim commented. "Hisham replied, 'I have found you a second.' 'Who?' 'Myself.' "'Then find a third,' Muc'im asked. 'I already have.' "'Who is that?' "'Zuhayr h. Ahn Umayya.' "'Then find us a fourth,' asked Mutcim. "So H i s h h went to Abn al-Bakhtari h. Hisham and told him what he had said to M u t ' i h. 'Adi. "Ahn al-Bakhtari asked, 'Can you find anyone else to help in this?' "'Yes, I can,' Hisham replied. "'Who?' he asked. "'Zuhayr b. Aba 'Umayya, Mut'im h. 'Adi and myself are with you.' "'Find us a fifth,' he asked. "And so Hisham went to Zamca b. al-Aswad b. al-Mut@lih b. Asad, spoke to him and reminded him of their kinship and their rights. "'Is there anyone else helping in what yon are inviting me to do?' asked Zam'a. "'Indeed yes,' Hishxm replied, naming the others. "And so they agreed to meet at night on the promontory of Mt. al-Hajiin on the upper outskirts of Mecca. There they met and agreed to defy the document until they were able to revoke it. Znhayr told them, 'I will begin and be the first to speak out.'"
lessons: Systemic injustice can be dismantled when people of conscience build alliances deliberately and patiently, one relationship at a time.
The feeling of 'I am only one person' is a common obstacle to justice, but it dissolves the moment you find even one ally — start there.
Appealing to people's self-interest and moral conscience together, as Hisham did, is a wise and effective strategy for building coalitions against oppression.
practical_advice: When facing an unjust situation you cannot change alone, identify one like-minded person and begin building from there rather than being paralyzed by the scale of the problem.
Use honest moral appeals when trying to rally others to a just cause — remind them of shared values and the consequences of silence, as Hisham reminded each leader of their ties to the oppressed.
Meet and coordinate in private before acting publicly; planning with care, as the five men did at Mt. al-Hajun, increases the effectiveness of collective action.

title: Zuhayr Publicly Denounces the Boycott Document at the Kaaba
summary: The morning after the secret coalition meeting, Zuhayr b. Abi Umayya performed tawaf around the Kaaba and then publicly challenged the Quraysh assembly, declaring he would not sit until the unjust boycott document against Banu Hashim was torn up.
story: "Next morning they went off to their assembly meeting and Zuhayr attended wearing a ceremonial robe. He made seven circumambnlations of the kacba then went before the gathering and said, '0 people of Mecca, shall we eat all kinds of food and dress at will while the Bann H s h i m are in distress, unable to engage in any trade? By God, I won't take my seat until this damnable boycott document is tom up! ' "Abn Jahl, who was over to one side of the
lessons: Public moral courage, when backed by preparation and community, has the power to shift the tide of oppression; Zuhayr's bold declaration was not reckless but carefully coordinated.
Performing acts of worship before speaking truth to power — as Zuhayr made tawaf before addressing the assembly — grounds one's actions in devotion rather than ego.
Even in societies where injustice is normalized, there are always those whose conscience can be awakened; do not assume that the powerful are uniformly complicit.
practical_advice: Before taking a difficult public stand, engage in spiritual preparation — prayer, reflection, or worship — to center your intention and calm your anxiety.
When injustice has gone unchallenged for too long, sometimes a single clear, unequivocal statement of conscience is what breaks the silence and emboldens others.
Draw on the support of your coalition before acting publicly so that you are not standing alone — Zuhayr knew four allies were present before he spoke.

title: The Revocation of the Boycott Document Against Banu Hashim
summary: A group of noble Qurayshi men, led by Zuhayr, publicly challenged the unjust boycott document against Banu Hashim, ultimately discovering it had been consumed by worms except for God's name, leading to its revocation.
story: told them, 'I will begin and be the first to speak out.' "Next morning they went off to their assembly meeting and Zuhayr attended wearing a ceremonial robe. He made seven circumambulations of the ka'ba then went before the gathering and said, 'O people of Mecca, shall we eat all kinds of food and dress at will while the Banu Hashim are in distress, unable to engage in any trade? By God, I won't take my seat until this damnable boycott document is torn up!' "Abu Jahl, who was over to one side of the mosque, retorted, 'By God, it will not be torn up!' "Zam'a b. al-Asad then said, 'By God, you are the worst liar! We weren't pleased with the document when it was written.' "'Zam'a speaks the truth,' Abu al-Bakhtari joined in. 'We do not like or agree to what is written there.' "Al-Mu'tim b. 'Adi then spoke up, 'You are both right, and anyone denying that is a liar. We are innocent before God of the document and what is written on it.' "Hisham b. 'Amr then stated something similar. "Abu Jahl observed, 'This is something decided at night, something discussed elsewhere.' "Meanwhile, Abu Talib was seated to one side of the mosque. "Al-Mu'tim b. 'Adi then arose to tear up the document but discovered that worms had already consumed it, all except for the words, 'In your name, O God!' "It was Mansur b. 'Ikrima who had written it and, so they say, his hand had become paralysed." Ibn Hisham stated, "Some scholars relate that the Messenger of God (SAAS) told Abu Talib, 'Uncle, God has sent worms to work upon the document of Quraysh and they have left alone only names relating to God and have removed the evil, boycott and lies from it.' "Abu Talib asked, 'Is it your Lord who told you this?' "'Yes,' he replied. "'Well, I swear by God,' Abu Talib commented, 'no one has come in to you.' "Abu Talib then went out to Quraysh and asked, 'O Quraysh, my nephew has informed me of such-and-such things. Bring your document, and if it is as he says, then withdraw and remove the boycott. But if he is lying, I will hand my nephew over to you.' "'We'll agree to that,' they said, and so compacted. "They then looked and found it as the Messenger of God (SAAS) had said. But this only increased their malice. "Thereupon the group of Quraysh men took their action to revoke the document." Ibn Ishaq stated that when it had been torn up and its contents revoked, Abu Talib spoke the following verses in praise of those who had taken action to annul the document: "Has our Lord's action reached our men at sea, despite their distance, for God is most kind to people, And will tell them that the document has been torn up, and that all things that displease God come to nought. Falsehood and deceit alternated and conjoined in it, but ultimately deceit is not bound to prevail. Those uninvolved in it gathered together on a level plateau, while its fate remained undetermined. It was an incident so worthy of being considered evil as to have arms and necks severed for it. And for the people of all Mecca to leave in flight, their hearts quaking in fear of its evil, The ploughman was left to ponder his options, whether because of it to head for low ground or for high, So let him of Mecca's people whose prestige intoxicates him know that our glory in Mecca's heart is longer lived. We grew up there when those there were few and we have always had our prestige and praise for us increase. We fed our guests until they were forced to leave leftovers, and till the hands of those inundated with food began to give out. God reward that group who united at al-Hajun behind a chief who guides rightly and decisively, Who sat on the heights at al-Hajun as though they were kings, though even more noble and glorious than that. Each hero helping in that, though walking with difficulty and impeded by his chainmail, Courageously moving to great deeds, like a flame flaring in a torchbearer's hands Men of the most noble of Lu'ayy b. Ghalib whose faces burn in anger if they are insulted: Men tall in stature, their swords reaching to the middle of their shanks, men for whom the clouds receive rain and bring joy. Generous lords and sons of lords, urging their guests to eat, piling the food, Constructing safety and paving the way for their tribesmen when we travel the country. Every blame-free man strives to keep this peace; (every) leader of great renown for that reason. They spent part of the night asleep then awoke quietly, while the rest still slept. They sent back Sahl b. Bayda' well pleased, and Abu Bakr and Muhammad were delighted by it. When was it that other clans helped out in our affairs, though of old, before that, we have been well liked? In former times we never acquiesced in injustice, and achieved our aims without having to use violence. O Tribe of Qusayy, will you not consider, and do you want what tomorrow will bring? The relationship between you and me is like the saying, 'O Mt. Aswad, you could explain it, if only you spoke.'"
lessons: Injustice, no matter how firmly established by powerful people, cannot endure forever — those who stand on truth must speak out even when they are in the minority.
Divine intervention often works through natural means and human conscience simultaneously; the worms consuming the document and the men rising to revoke it were two dimensions of the same mercy.
Oppression has a natural limit. When hardship seems permanent, remaining steadfast and patient allows the turning point that God has already decreed to arrive in its appointed time.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped by an unjust situation that seems beyond your control, look for allies who share your values — collective moral courage, like that of Zuhayr and the group, can dismantle what feels immovable.

If you have been treated unjustly and feel powerless, reflect on this account as a reminder that divine support can come through unexpected channels, and focus your energy on what you can do rather than despairing over what you cannot.
When you witness injustice, consider whether speaking up — calmly and publicly as Zuhayr did — might encourage others around you who privately agree but have not yet found their voice.

title: Al-Tufayl b. 'Amr al-Dawsi Accepts Islam Despite the Quraysh's Warnings
summary: Al-Tufayl, an honoured poet of the Daws tribe, came to Mecca where the Quraysh chieftains pressured him not to listen to the Prophet. Despite stuffing his ears with cotton, he was moved to hear the Prophet's words, accepted Islam, and returned home to invite his family.
story: Al-Tufayl was an honoured and respected man of Daws. He came to Mecca where he was met by the Quraysh chieftains, who warned him about the Messenger of God (SAAS), and forbad him to meet with him or to listen to him talk. Al-Tufayl related, "By God, they kept on at me until I agreed not to hear him nor to speak to him. I even stuffed my ears with cotton when I went to the mosque to prevent myself hearing anything he said; I didn't want to listen to him. "I went to the mosque, and there was the Messenger of God (SAAS) standing in prayer at the ka'ba. I went over near to him, and God required me to hear some of what he said. "And I heard wonderful speech. So I told myself, 'May my mother not be bereft! I swear, here am I, a man who is intelligent and a poet, and someone who can well distinguish good from bad! What prevents me from hearing what this man is saying? If what he says is good, I will accept it; if bad, I will reject it.'" He went on, "So I stayed there until the Messenger of God (SAAS) went off home and I followed him till he went inside his house. Then I went in to him and said, 'O Muhammad, your people have told me such-and-such.' He continued, "And I swear, they so scared me that I stuffed my ears with cotton so as not to hear what you say. But then God insisted on making me hear your words. And I heard fine speech. So explain your situation to me.' Al-Tufayl continued, "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) explained Islam to me and recited the Qur'an to me and, I swear, I never heard words more sweet than those, nor any matter more just. "And so I accepted Islam and bore true witness, saying, 'O Prophet of God, I am a man who is obeyed by his people. I am now returning to them and will invite them to Islam. Pray to God to give me a sign that will help me with them when I do so.' "And so he said, 'O God, give him a sign.' "I then left for home and travelled till I came to a pass that enabled me to look down on to my village. But then there appeared between my eyes a light like that of a lamp. I said to myself, 'O God, not right in my face, for I fear that they will think it some exemplary punishment that has struck my face for my abandoning my faith.' "Then the light moved and alighted on the head of my whip. And the villagers watched that light atop my whip that resembled a suspended candle, and they continued to do so as I was descending to them from the pass, right until I was there among them. "When I dismounted, my father, an aged sheikh, came over to me, but I told him, 'Off with you, father; you and I must have nothing to do with one another.' "'Why is that, my son?' he asked. "'I have accepted Islam, and adopted the religion of Muhammad (SAAS).' "'Well, son, your religion shall be mine.' "'Then go and wash yourself, clean your clothing and come to me so that I can instruct you in what I have been taught.' "He did go and wash himself and cleaned his clothing. When he returned, I explained Islam to him and he accepted it. "Then my wife came to me and I told her, 'Go away; you and I can have nothing to do with one another.' "'Why is that, my father and my mother be your ransom?' "'Islam has divided me from you; I have adopted the religion of Muhammad (SAAS).' "'Well, your religion is mine,' she said. "I told her, 'Go to the hima, the sacred shrine, of Dhu al-Shara and purify yourself of it.' Dhu al-Shara was an idol of Daws and the hima was a shrine around it that they kept sacrosanct; it had a stream whose water descended from a mountain. "She replied, 'May my father and mother ransom you, don't you fear something will happen to the children because of
lessons: Peer pressure and social conformity can temporarily block us from truth, but sincere reasoning and an open heart will ultimately overcome external manipulation.

Seeking a sign or a practical means of support when embarking on a difficult mission is not weakness — even the Prophet (SAAS) prayed for Al-Tufayl to have a sign, showing that God supports those who use wisdom alongside faith.
True conviction transforms not just the individual but ripples outward to family and community; Al-Tufayl's courage in accepting truth led directly to his father and wife also embracing Islam.
practical_advice: When people around you are pressuring you away from something that feels true and good, pause and ask yourself — as Al-Tufayl did — whether your resistance is based on your own reasoning or simply on fear of others' opinions.

If you are about to make a significant life change and feel anxious about how those close to you will respond, take courage from Al-Tufayl's example: speak your truth clearly and with love, and trust that sincere conviction is often contagious.

When you feel confused and pulled in different directions by conflicting voices, seek out the primary source for yourself rather than relying solely on what others say about it — Al-Tufayl's transformation began the moment he chose to listen with his own ears.

title: Al-Tufayl ibn Amr and the Islamization of the Daws Tribe
summary: Al-Tufayl ibn Amr al-Dawsi embraces Islam in Mecca, convinces his wife and father, then spends years patiently preaching to his resistant tribe before eventually bringing 70-80 families to the Prophet at Khaybar.
story: "Islam has divided me from you; I have adopted the religion of Muhammad (SAAS)." 'Well, your religion is mine,' she said. 'I told her, Go to the hrma, the sacred shrine, of Dhii d-SharZ and purify yourself of it.' Dhii al-Shara was an idol of Daws and the hsma was a shrine around it that they kept sacrosanct; it had a stream whose water descended from a mountain. 'She replied, May my father and mother ransom you, don't you fear something will happen to the children because of Dhii &Sham?' 'Not at all; I guarantee that,' I replied. 'She did go and wash herself and, when she returned I explained Islam to her and she accepted it. 'I then called upon the Daws tribe to accept Islam, but they resisted. I went to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Mecca. I told him, O Messenger of God, fornication has defeated me with Daws; say a prayer to God for them. 'He said, O God, lead Daws aright. Then he said to me, Return to your people, preach to them and be kind to them. 'And so I remained in Daws territory asking them to embrace Islam until the Messenger of God (SAAS), emigrated to Medina. Then the battles of Badr, Uhud and the khandaq, the trench, took place. Thereafter I went to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), taking those of my people who had become Muslims, while he was at Khaybar. I set up camp, with some 70 or 80 tents of Daws families at Medina, and then we joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Khaybar; there he gave us an equal share of the spoils, along with the other Muslims.'
lessons: Patience in calling others to truth may take years before results appear — al-Tufayl preached for years through multiple major battles before his tribe responded.
The Prophet's guidance to 'be kind to them' reminds us that gentleness, not force or frustration, is the means of changing hearts.
When those we love resist our values, we must trust God and continue with wisdom, as al-Tufayl did with his own family and tribe.
practical_advice: When you feel your efforts are having no impact on those around you, remind yourself that change in others follows God's timing, not ours — keep showing up with kindness.
If you are trying to guide a loved one and feeling defeated, bring your frustration to God in prayer as al-Tufayl brought his complaint to the Prophet.
Rather than abandoning relationships because of differences in values, look for ways to remain present and gentle, modelling what you believe through your character.

title: Al-Tufayl Burns the Idol Dhu al-Kaffayn
summary: After the conquest of Mecca, al-Tufayl ibn Amr requests permission to destroy the idol Dhu al-Kaffayn of his tribe, travels to burn it, and recites verses declaring his liberation from idol worship.
story: I remained with the Messenger of God (SAAS) until God conquered Mecca through him. Then I asked him, 'O Messenger of God, dispatch me to burn Dhii al-Kaffayn, the idol of 'Amr b. Humitma.' Ibn Ishaq stated, 'And he did leave to do so. As al-Tufayl set fire to the idol he spoke the verse, O Dha al-Kaffayn, I am not one of your worshippers; our history is older than yours; I have stuffed your heart with fire.' He then returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and remained at Medina with him until the Messenger of God (SAAS) died.
lessons: True liberation comes from breaking free of whatever holds power over us — al-Tufayl's act of burning the idol was both a physical and spiritual declaration of freedom.
The journey from fear and false attachment to confident faith can take years, but the arrival is worth every step.
Gratitude for guidance is best expressed through action, as al-Tufayl showed by completing the work of his conversion even after years had passed.
practical_advice: Identify the 'idols' in your own life — habits, fears, or attachments that hold unhealthy power over you — and take one concrete step toward freeing yourself from them.
Celebrate milestones in your spiritual journey; al-Tufayl marked his tribe's full liberation with a symbolic act. Find your own meaningful way to mark moments of growth.
Write or say aloud a declaration of what you no longer belong to — this can be a powerful act of closure and forward movement.

title: Al-Tufayl's Vision and His Martyrdom at Yamama
summary: On his way to the battle of Yamama, al-Tufayl recounts a dream to his companions and interprets it himself as a foretelling of his own martyrdom, which indeed comes to pass.
story: On his way to Yamama he had a vision and he asked his companions to interpret it to him. He recounted, 'I saw that my head had been shaved, a bird came forth from my mouth, and a woman met me and placed me in her vagina. Then I saw my son frantically searching for me, but he was withheld from me.' 'Let's hope it's a good omen,' they told him. He said, 'I'm going to interpret it myself.' 'What, then?' they asked. He explained, 'The shaving of my head means its being laid down. The bird exiting it is my soul. The woman who placed me in her vagina is the earth that will be dug out for me, and into which I will disappear. As for my son's searching for me, and then his being kept from me, I see him striving to have happen to him what has happened to me.' He was killed, God bless him, as a martyr in al-Yamiima, and his son was severely wounded but later recovered. Eventually he was martyred in the year of the battle of al-Yarmiik, during the caliphate of Wmar. God bless him.
lessons: Those with deep faith can face the prospect of death with calm clarity rather than panic — al-Tufayl interpreted his own dream of death without despair.
The legacy of a person of faith continues through those they inspire; al-Tufayl's son followed his father's path to martyrdom, showing how character is transmitted across generations.
Accepting what we cannot control, including our mortality, is not resignation but a form of profound trust in God.
practical_advice: When facing something frightening or uncertain, practice naming what you fear clearly rather than avoiding it — honest acknowledgment, as al-Tufayl modelled, can bring unexpected calm.
Reflect on the legacy you wish to leave for those who come after you, and let that shape how you live today.
If you are grieving the loss of someone who died with faith and purpose, draw comfort from the Islamic understanding that such a death is honoured and that reunion is possible.

title: The Companion of Al-Tufayl Who Took His Own Life
summary: A companion of al-Tufayl who emigrated to Medina, becoming ill and depressed, cut his own hand and bled to death; al-Tufayl later sees him in a dream in good condition but with his hands covered, and the Prophet prays for him.
story: When the Prophet (SAAS) emigrated to al-Medina, al-Tufayl b. 'Amr joined him, along with another of his people. They hated the town and (the second man, tr.) fell ill and, depressed, took an arrow head and used it to cut between his fingers. Blood streamed from his hands and did not stop until he was dead. Al-Tufayl saw the man in a dream in pleasant circumstances and observed that he had his hands covered. So he asked him, 'What did your Lord do?' He replied, 'He forgave me because I had gone to join His Prophet (SAAS).' Al-Tufayl asked, 'Why do I see you covering your hands?' He answered, 'I was told that what I had spoiled would never be restored!' The account continued, 'And al-Tufayl recounted this to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who spoke the words, O God, do forgive him for his hands.' Muslim related this from Abo Bakr b. Abo Shayba and Ishaq b. Ibrzhim, both of them giving as their source an account of Ibn Harb.
lessons: Even in a moment of profound failure, God's mercy can encompass a soul — the man was forgiven because of his sincere intention to be close to the Prophet, despite his terrible act.
Depression and physical illness can cloud a person's judgment severely; this story is a reminder to treat suffering people with compassion rather than judgment.
The consequences of harmful choices can linger even after forgiveness — the covered hands — which is a gentle reminder that our actions matter while also affirming that mercy is real.
practical_advice: If you are struggling with hopelessness or a dark period, please reach out to someone you trust or a mental health professional — your suffering is real and help is available.
When you feel trapped by guilt over past mistakes, hold onto the truth that sincere turning toward God and goodness carries immense weight, even when our circumstances are broken.
If someone around you is experiencing depression, take it seriously — check in on them, sit with them, and encourage them gently toward support rather than leaving them alone with their pain.

title: A'sha ibn Qays Travels to the Prophet with Verses of Praise
summary: The renowned poet A'sha ibn Qays composes a celebrated ode in praise of the Prophet and sets out toward him intending to accept Islam, recounting in verse his journey and the teachings he has heard.
story: Ibn Hisham stated, 'Khallzd b. Qurra b. Khalid al-Sudnsi, and other sheikhs of Bakr b. W2il told me, from certain scholars, that AcshZ b. Qays b. Tha'laba b. Wkxba h. Sa'b b. 'Ali b. Bakr b. Ws'il went to see the Messenger of God (SAAS) intending to accept Islam. He spoke the following verses in praise of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Did your eyes not close that night, O bleary-eyed one? You lay awake all night through restlessness. And the sleeplessness was not for love of a woman, for before that you had forgotten your lover of Mahdad, But I see that fate, that traitor, spoils what my hands restore. I have thus lost age, youth and wealth, for by God this fate, how it changes! I have constantly pursued wealth throughout my progression from childhood to youth to maturity to old age. Daily I raced fast red-grey camels across the distance between at-Nujayr and Sarkhada. O you enquiring where she has gone, she has an appointment with the people of Yathrib. And if you enquire about me, then (know that) there are many kind-hearted ones who ask about A'sha, enquiring where he went. My mount stretches her swift legs and draws them back, carefully turning out her hooves, not splaying them. Sprightly she is, even in noonday heat, when you would imagine even the afternoon chameleon bending its neck. I swore I'd not assuage her fatigue nor her sore feet until she reached Muhammad. When she should kneel at Ibn Hashim's door, only then would it rest and receive, through his munificence, some generosity. (He is) a prophet who sees what you do not, whose fame, I swear, has spread high and low in the land. He has gifts and favours that do not end, and his giving today does not prevent him giving tomorrow. Did you not, I repeat, hear the advice of Muhammad, the Prophet of God, when he preached and testified? If you did not travel with cargo of piety and after death met with others who have done so, You will surely regret not being like them; so, therefore, prepare for a fate that is definitely arranged. Beware of carrion; do not approach it, and do not take the share of that which some weapon has already allotted (to others) And do not venerate raised monuments, nor worship idols, but pray to God alone. Do not go near a free woman; her privacy is forbidden you; marry or be celibate. Do not cut off ties with your relatives for some motive, nor take them as captives under restraint. And glorify God at evening and at morning; do not praise the devil; praise only God. Do not ridicule those suffering poverty, and do not consider wealth man's way to eternity.'
lessons: A'sha's poem shows that even a person still on the journey toward faith can articulate profound spiritual truths — we do not need to have arrived to begin speaking of what matters.
The poet's reflections on lost youth and wealth are a reminder that worldly pursuits alone leave the soul unsatisfied, and that recognition of this is itself the beginning of wisdom.
The values embedded in A'sha's verses — care for the poor, family ties, worship of God alone, personal integrity — are timeless ethical anchors that apply across every era.
practical_advice: If you feel you have wasted years chasing things that did not fulfil you, know that the recognition of this is not failure — it is the beginning of a wiser path forward.
Consider expressing your spiritual longings creatively, as A'sha did through poetry — writing, art, or music can help process and clarify what the heart is seeking.
Use A'sha's list of ethical reminders as a personal checklist: are there relationships you have neglected, people you have looked down upon, or habits that pull you away from your values?

title: Al-A'sha's Encounter with Quraysh and His Delayed Acceptance of Islam
summary: The poet al-A'sha was on his way to accept Islam but was intercepted by Quraysh polytheists who warned him about the prohibition of wine, causing him to delay his conversion. He died that same year without ever returning to the Prophet.
story: Ibn Hisham stated, "And when al-A'sha was in Mecca or near thereto, some Quraysh polytheists stopped him and asked what business he had there. He told them that he had come seeking the Messenger of God (SAAS). "One of the Quraysh commented, 'But Abū Basīr, he is forbidding sex!' Al-A'sha responded, 'I swear, that's no desire of mine.' "'But he's also forbidding wine,' the man objected. "'Well, that's something I do hold dear; I'll go off and have my fill of it for a year, then I'll come back and accept Islam!' "And leave he did. But he died that same year without returning to the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Thus does Ibn Hisham tell this story here. He extracts a great deal from Muhammad Ibn Ishaq, God bless him, and this is one thing added to his account by Ibn Hisham, God bless him! Wine was not in fact forbidden in Medina until after the fighting with the Banū al-Nadir, as will be explained hereafter. It is apparent that the decision of al-A'sha to accept Islam was taken only after the migration. There is evidence for this in his poetry. One verse reads, "O you enquiring where she has gone, she has an appointment with the people of Yathrih."
