It would have been more appropriate for Ibn Hisham to have recounted this anecdote within material relating to the post-migration period, rather than here. But God knows best. Al-Suhayli stated, "This is an error on the part of Ibn Hisham and those who follow him, for everyone agrees that use of wine was only prohibited in Medina after the battle of Uhud." He has also stated, "It is said that the person who spoke to al-A'sha was Abū Jahl b. Hisham in the house of 'Utba b. Rabi'a." Abū 'Ubayda, however, related that the one who spoke to him thus was 'Amir b. al-Tufayl in Qays territory while he was on his way to the Messenger of God (SAAS). He stated, "And his saying '... then I will come to him and accept Islam' definitely does not bring him out of a state of disbelief. But God knows best."
lessons: Procrastination in responding to truth can have permanent consequences — al-A'sha's choice to delay cost him the greatest opportunity of his life.
Peer pressure and worldly attachments can cloud our judgment and divert us from what we know to be right.
The enemies of truth often use our weaknesses strategically to keep us from guidance; recognizing these tactics is part of spiritual vigilance.
practical_advice: When you feel drawn toward something good, act on it promptly rather than postponing — the right moment is often now.
Identify the specific worldly attachments or habits that others use to hold you back from positive change, and address them honestly.
Surround yourself with people who encourage your growth rather than exploit your weaknesses to keep you stagnant.

title: The Wrestling Contest of Rukana and the Miracle of the Walking Tree
summary: Rukana, the strongest man of Quraysh, challenged the Prophet to a wrestling match as a test of his truthfulness. The Prophet threw him multiple times and then called a tree to walk toward him as a miracle, yet Rukana still returned to his people calling it magic.
story: Ibn Ishaq quoted his father, Ishaq b. Yasar, as having related to him as follows: "Rukana b. 'Abd Yazid b. Hashim b. al-Muttalib b. 'Abd Manaf was the strongest man of Quraysh. "Once he was alone in one of the defiles of Mecca with the Messenger of God (SAAS), who asked him, 'Rukana, won't you fear God and accept my invitation to you?' "He replied, 'If I knew that what you say is true, I would follow you.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked him, 'If I were to wrestle you down would you know that what I say is true?' "'Yes, I would,' Rukana replied. "'Come on then, let's wrestle,' the Messenger of God (SAAS) said. "And so Rukana did wrestle with him and when the Messenger of God (SAAS) went after him, he threw him down, powerless. "Rukana then said, 'Another round, Muhammad!' "Once again he threw him. Rukana then said, 'Muhammad, I swear, Muhammad, this is amazing! Can you really throw me?' "He replied, 'I'll show you something even more amazing, if you like, if you will fear God and follow me.' "'What is it?' "'I'll call out to that tree you see and it will come to me.' "'Call for it then.' "He did so and it came and stood there right before the Messenger of God (SAAS). Then he told it, 'Go back to your place!' And it did so. "And so Rukana went to his people and told them, 'O Banū 'Abd Manaf, you could have your man compete in magic with the whole world. I swear I've never seen a better magician than him.' He then recounted to them what he had seen and what he had done." Ibn Ishaq related this story thus, with an incomplete line of transmission. Abū Dā'ud al-Tirmidhi related, from an account of Abū al-Hasan al-'Askalai, from Abū Ja'far b. Muhammad b. Rukana, from his father, Rukana, who said he had wrestled the Prophet (SAAS) who had defeated him. Then al-Tirmidhi commented, "Strange! We know nothing of Abū al-Hasan, nor of Rukana's son." My comment is that Abū Bakr al-Shafi'i related, with an excellent chain of authorities, from Ibn 'Abbas, God bless them both, that Yazid b. Rukana wrestled the Messenger of God (SAAS) who threw him three times, each time for a wager of 100 sheep. The third time Rukana said, "Muhammad, no one has ever put my back on the ground before you, and no one was more hateful in my sight before this than you! I testify that there is no god but God, and that you are the Messenger of God." At that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stood up from him and gave him back his sheep.
lessons: Even the most powerful people have moments when they are confronted by a truth greater than themselves; humility before evidence is a mark of wisdom.
Miracles and signs are shown to those who sincerely seek the truth, yet the heart must be willing to receive them — seeing is not always believing.
The Prophet's approach of meeting people where they are — engaging Rukana through wrestling rather than lecturing him — teaches us to communicate truth in ways the other person can understand.
practical_advice: When trying to reach someone who is resistant, consider their values and language rather than insisting on your own approach.
Reflect on moments when you have witnessed something remarkable and ask yourself honestly whether pride or social pressure held you back from acknowledging the truth.
Practice returning things freely and generously, as the Prophet returned the sheep to Rukana, to demonstrate that truth is its own reward and not a means of gain.

title: Quraysh's Mockery of the Poor and Weak Companions in the Mosque
summary: Quraysh mocked the Prophet's early followers — men like Khabbab, Ammar, and Suhayb — saying that if Islam were true, God would not have guided the poor and weak before the nobles. God responded by revealing verses affirming the worth of those who sincerely seek His face.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to have some of his most vulnerable Companions sit close by him in the mosque, such Muslims as Khabbab, 'Ammar, Abū Fukayha Yasar, the freed-man of Safwan b. Umayya and Suhayb. Quraysh would ridicule these people, telling one another, 'These are his companions, as you see, but has God really chosen them from among us to receive guidance and the true religion? If what Muhammad brought were any good, they would not have joined him first; God would certainly not have put them before us.'" And so God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed, "Do not drive away those who pray to their Lord morning and night seeking His face. You are not accountable for them in the least, nor are they for you. If you repulse them you will be acting unjustly. Thus have we put some to the test through others, so that they would say, 'Are they those of us whom God has favoured?' 'Does God not know best who gives thanks?' And when those who believe in our revelation come to you then say, 'Peace be upon you; your Lord has prescribed for Himself mercy so that if one of you does wrong in ignorance, then later repents and reforms — He is indeed merciful, forgiving'" (Surat al-An'am; VI, v.52-4).
lessons: True worth in God's sight is not measured by social status, wealth, or lineage but by sincerity, devotion, and the seeking of His face.
Being mocked or looked down upon for your faith or your humble circumstances is a trial shared by the best of people throughout history.
God's mercy encompasses those who sincerely turn to Him regardless of their past wrongs, offering hope and dignity to every human soul.
practical_advice: When you feel dismissed or undervalued because of your status or background, remember that sincerity of heart is the measure that truly matters before God.
Resist the temptation to judge a community or movement by the worldly status of its members — look instead at the quality of character and devotion you find there.
If you have wronged others by looking down on them, take steps to repair those relationships and consciously practice recognizing the dignity in every person you encounter.

title: The Accusation That the Prophet Was Taught by the Christian Slave Jabr
summary: The Quraysh claimed that the Prophet's teachings came not from God but from a young Christian slave named Jabr, whom the Prophet would visit near al-Marwa. God refuted this accusation by revealing that the Quran is in clear Arabic while the man they pointed to spoke a foreign tongue.
story: He went on: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used often to sit at al-Marwa near the shop of a young Christian named Jabr, a slave of the Banū al-Hadrami. People would say, 'By God, it is merely Jabr who teaches Muhammad most of what he says!' "And so God Almighty revealed about their saying that, 'It is merely some mortal who teaches him. The language of him to whom they allude is foreign; yet this language is plain Arabic'" (Surat al-Nahl; XVI, v.105).
lessons: Those who seek to undermine truth will always look for mundane explanations for what is extraordinary; this has been the pattern in every age.
The clarity and eloquence of the Quran in its original Arabic is itself a testament to its divine origin, beyond what any foreign-tongued teacher could produce.
Slander and false accusations against those who call to truth are a form of persecution that tests their steadfastness and the sincerity of their followers.
practical_advice: When you or someone you respect is falsely accused, respond with clarity and evidence rather than anger, just as the Quran responded with a logical refutation.
Build a reputation for integrity so that false accusations against you are obviously inconsistent with your known character.
Do not let others' attempts to discredit your sources of guidance deter you from pursuing what you know to be true and beneficial.

title: Al-As ibn Wa'il Calls the Prophet 'Without Male Offspring' and the Revelation of Surat al-Kawthar
summary: After the death of the Prophet's son al-Qasim, al-As ibn Wa'il mocked the Prophet by calling him 'abtar' — one cut off from posterity — implying that without male heirs his memory would die. God revealed Surat al-Kawthar in response, declaring that it is the mocker himself who will be cut off from remembrance.
story: Then Ibn Ishaq recounts how Surat al-Kawthar (CVIII) was revealed concerning al-'As b. Wa'il when he said of the Messenger of God (SAAS), "He is without a male child, that is, he has no male offspring. If he dies, memory of him will be gone." And so God Almighty stated, "He who hates you shall be childless." That is, he would not be remembered after his death, even if he were to have thousands of offspring. For remembrance, reputation and credibility do not depend on multiplicity of children and offspring. We discussed this surat in our Tafsir (Exegesis). And to God be all praise. It has been related from Abū Ja'far al-Baqir that al-'As b. Wa'il said the above following the death of al-Qasim, the son of the Prophet (SAAS). He had reached an age when he could ride a mule and sit on a thoroughbred mount.
lessons: Lasting legacy is built on the quality of one's contribution to truth and goodness, not on biological descendants or worldly measures of success.
Grief over the loss of a child is among the deepest sorrows a person can bear, yet even in this vulnerability the Prophet was subjected to mockery — a reminder that hardship and cruelty can coincide.
Those who attempt to wound others through their losses often reveal the poverty of their own character; time vindicates those who are patient.
practical_advice: When grief makes you feel that your efforts or your life are without lasting meaning, reflect on the ways your character and actions ripple outward beyond what you can see.
Do not measure your worth or your legacy by what you have lost, but by the sincerity and goodness you continue to bring into the world.
If someone uses your pain or your losses to demean you, recognize that their cruelty speaks about them, not about your value, and seek comfort in God rather than in their approval.

title: The Quraysh Leaders Demand an Angel Be Sent Down and the Revelation of Their Warning
summary: Quraysh leaders including Ubayy ibn Khalaf and al-As ibn Wa'il demanded that God send down an angel to confirm the Prophet's message. God revealed that if an angel were sent, it would mean immediate destruction for those who still disbelieved.
story: Ibn Ishaq then related the revelation of the verse "... and they say, 'Now if only an angel had been sent down to him.' If We had sent down an angel, it would all have been finished!" (Surat al-An'am; VI, v.8). This referred to Ubayy b. Khalaf, Zam'a b. al-Aswad, al-'As b. Wa'il and al-Nadr b. al-Harith having said, "If only God had sent down for you an angel to tell the people about you."
lessons: Demanding signs on one's own terms rather than accepting the signs already given is a pattern of those who are not genuinely seeking truth but seeking to justify rejection.
God's mercy withholds certain signs because their manifestation would remove the possibility of repentance and faith — what feels like delay is often protection.
Every generation has those who raise objections not to find answers but to avoid the responsibility that comes with accepting the truth.
practical_advice: Examine your own objections and conditions honestly — ask yourself whether you are genuinely seeking clarity or constructing barriers to avoid a commitment you are not yet ready to make.
When waiting for a sign or answer from God feels frustrating, consider the signs and answers already present in your life that you may have overlooked.
Practice accepting guidance even when it does not arrive in the form you expected or demanded.

title: The Mockers of the Prophet and Gabriel's Protection
summary: Five leading men of Quraysh — al-Walid ibn al-Mughira, al-Aswad ibn Abd Yaghuth, al-Aswad ibn al-Muttalib, al-Harith ibn Aytil, and al-As ibn Wa'il — mocked and harassed the Prophet. When the Prophet complained to Gabriel, God struck each mocker with a different affliction that led to their destruction.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS), so we have been told, passed by al-Walid b. al-Mughira, Umayya b. Khalaf and Abū Jahl b. Hisham, and they insulted and mocked him. This angered him, and at that God Almighty revealed about them, 'Prophets before you were mocked, but it was their mocking that trapped them'" (Surat al-An'am; VI, v.10). I observe that God Almighty also stated, "Prophets before you were mocked. Yet they bore with fortitude the falsehoods and persecutions until Our help came to them. No one can change the words of God. And you have received information about the messengers sent" (Surat al-An'am; VI, v.34). And the Almighty also said, "We will protect you against those who mock" (Surat al-Hijr; XV, v.95). Sufyan stated, quoting Ja'far b. Iyyas, from Sa'id b. Jubayr, from Ibn 'Abbas, that the "mockers" consisted of al-Walid b. al-Mughira, al-Aswad b. 'Abd Yaghuth al-Zuhri, al-Aswad b. al-Muttalib Abū Zam'a, al-Harith b. 'Aytil, and al-'As b. Wa'il al-Sahmi and that: "Gabriel came to him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) complained to him about them, pointing out al-Walid. And so Gabriel gestured to his fingertips and said, 'So much for him!' "He then pointed out to him al-Aswad b. al-Muttalib and Gabriel gestured towards his neck, saying, 'So much for him!' "He then pointed out al-Aswad b. 'Abd Yaghuth and Gabriel gestured to his head, saying, 'So much for him!' "He then pointed to al-Harith b. Aytil and Gabriel gestured to his stomach, saying, 'So much for him!' "Then al-'As b. Wa'il passed by and Gabriel gestured towards the sole of his foot, saying, 'So much for him!' "As for Al-Walid, he later stopped by a man from Khuza'a who was mounting feathers on an arrow for him, but the arrow struck his fingertips and severed them.
lessons: God's promise to protect those who sincerely call to truth is not merely a consolation — it is a divine guarantee fulfilled in history, as the fate of each mocker demonstrates.
Bearing mockery and insult with patience, as the Prophets before Muhammad did, is a form of spiritual strength that God ultimately vindicates.
Complaining to God and to those He has appointed as protectors is not weakness but a legitimate and effective form of seeking help when one is wronged.
practical_advice: When you are mocked, ridiculed, or belittled for your values or your faith, take comfort in the knowledge that this is the experience of every sincere person who has stood for truth throughout history.
Rather than seeking revenge when wronged, bring your complaint to God in prayer and trust that justice, even if delayed, will come in ways beyond your planning.
Reflect on how the arrogance and cruelty of the mockers ultimately led to their own ruin — let this be a caution against using your tongue or position to demean others.

title: The Five Mockers and Gabriel's Divine Protection
summary: Five leading Qurayshi chiefs who mocked the Prophet were each divinely afflicted through Gabriel's intervention while circumambulating the Kaaba, each dying from the specific ailment Gabriel indicated.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The chief men who engaged in ridicule, as I was told by Yazid b. Rawmm, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, were five in number. They were all men of maturity and highly regarded among their people. The Messenger of God (SAAS) spoke a curse against al-Aswad h. al-Mutcalib Aha Zamk '0 God, blind his sight and make him bereft of his son!' "The others were al-Aswad b. CAbd Yaghath, al-Walid b. al-Mughira, al-'AS b. Wi?il, and al-Harith b. al-Talatil." He also recounted that Almighty God revealed about them, "Announce what you have been ordered, and turn aside from the polytheists. We will take care of the mockers for you, those who establish another god alongside God. They shall learn!" (swat al-Hajar; XV, v.944). He related, "Gabriel came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), while these men were circumambulating the kacba. Gabriel stood up, the Messenger of God (SAAS) at his side, while al-Aswad b. al-Muytalib went by. Gabriel threw a green leaf into his face and he became blind. "Al-Aswad b. 'Abd Yaghath next passed and Gabriel pointed at his stomach. It became swollen and so he died of dropsy. "Al-Walid h. al-Mughira went by and Gabriel pointed at the scar of a wound at the base of his ankle that he had suffered years before when he had passed by a man of Khuza'a re-feathering an arrow, the head of which had caught on his robe and slightly cut him. After this, however, it burst open and so he died. "Al-'AS b. WPil passed and Gabriel pointed to the underside of his foot. Al'AS later left on a donkey for ?%)if. The donkey threw him on to a thorn which pierced his foot and killed him. "Al-Harith b. al-Talzyil went by and Gabriel pointed at his head. It filled with pus and so killed him."
lessons: God promises to protect those who are mocked and persecuted for their faith — the believer does not need to retaliate, for divine justice is sufficient.
Worldly status, wealth, and tribal standing offer no protection against the consequences of arrogance and persecution of the righteous.
The Quran's promise 'We will take care of the mockers for you' is a source of deep comfort for anyone who feels helpless against those who ridicule their beliefs.
practical_advice: When you feel mocked or belittled for your values or faith, remind yourself that your role is to remain steadfast — not to seek revenge — and trust that accountability rests with God.
Recite or reflect on Surah al-Hijr (15:94-96) as a source of reassurance when facing ridicule, letting the Quranic promise replace the urge to retaliate.
Journal about a time you were mocked for doing what was right. Write down how the situation eventually resolved, and notice whether patience and reliance on God played a role.

title: The Last Wishes of al-Walid ibn al-Mughira and the Tribal Conflict That Followed
summary: As al-Walid ibn al-Mughira lay dying from his divinely inflicted wound, he gave his sons three final charges regarding unpaid debts and blood, setting off a chain of tribal tensions, killings, and near-war between Quraysh clans.
story: Ihn Ishaq then stated, "When al-Wdid h. al-Mughira felt the approach of death he made a request of his three sons Khiilid, Hisham, and al-Walid by name. He told them, 'Sons, I charge you with three tasks. My blood is on Khuza'a, and do not leave it unrevenged. I well know that they are innocent of it, hut I fear that you may he maligned by it later. Thaqif have debts owing me. Don't leave them alone until you get paid. And my dowry money is with Aba Uzayhir d-Dawsi; don't let him keep it from you.' Aha Uzayhir had married al-Walid to a daughter of his but later had kept her from him and had not let him in to her up to the time he died. And he had taken the money d-Walid had paid for her, which was her dowry. "When he died the Bana Makhzam hastened to Khuza'a seeking from them blood money for al-Walid. They said, 'It was an arrow of one of your men that killed him!' Khuza'a refused and they spoke angry poetry against one another, and the situation deteriorated greatly between them. But then Khuzzca did pay some of the blood-money, and they made peace and reconciled." Ihn Ishaq stated, "Then Hisham h. al-Walid attacked and killed Abii Uzayhir while he was at the D h a al-Majxz market. "He was a chief of his tribe and his daughter was Aba Sufyan's wife. This happened after the battle of Badr. Yazid h. Aha Sufyan went out and gathered his men against the Bana M a k h z m since his father was then absent. When Ahu Sufyan returned he was angered by what his son Yazid had done and berated and struck him for it. He then paid the hlood price for Aha Uzayhir and told his son, 'Did you plan to have Quraysh kill one another over one man from Daws?' "Hassan h. Thabit wrote an ode goading Aha Sufyiin over the spilling of Aha Uzayhir's blood. Regarding this poem, Ahti Sufyan commented, 'It's a really had idea of Hassiin that we should kill one another, now, after our chiefs died at the battle of Badr.'"
lessons: Unresolved grudges passed down through families and tribes multiply harm across generations — letting go of the desire for vengeance is an act of wisdom and mercy.
Anger and tribal pride can push people toward decisions that destroy the very community they seek to protect, as Abu Sufyan wisely recognized when he rebuked his son.
Conflict escalates most dangerously when grief and pride mix — creating space to grieve before acting prevents irreversible harm.
practical_advice: When you are grieving a loss and feel the urge to act out in anger, give yourself a deliberate pause of at least 24 hours before making any significant decision involving others.

If you have inherited a conflict or grudge from family, honestly ask yourself whether continuing it serves your wellbeing or simply perpetuates a cycle of harm — seek to be the generation that breaks the cycle.
Practice the habit of distinguishing between justice and revenge in your own life: justice restores balance, while revenge usually creates new imbalances.

title: Umm Ghaylan Protects the Quraysh Men in Daws Territory
summary: When a group of Quraysh men stayed in Daws territory after the killing of Abu Uzayhir, a freedwoman named Umm Ghaylan physically shielded them from the Daws who sought to kill them in retaliation.
story: Ihn Ishaq went on, "Some scholars informed me that the following verses were revealed about this, '0 you who believe, fear God and give up whatever interest payments are due, if you are indeed believers'" (siirat al-Bagara; 11; v.278). Ihn Ishaq stated, "And we know of no vengeance taken by Aha Uzayhir's people up to when Islam put an end to conflict between these men. Although Dirar b. al-Khaffah h. Mirdas al-Aslam~left with a group of Quraysh men into Daws territory. There they stayed with a woman named Umm Ghaylw, a freedwoman of Daws. She would comb the women's hair and prepare brides for marriage. The Daws wanted to kill the Quraysh for killing Aba Uzayhir, hut Umm Ghaylan and some women who were with her stood in their way and protected the Quraysh men." Al-Suhayli said, "It is said that she hid him inside her clothing." Ibn Hisham said, "In the days when Wmar b. d-KhaytZb ruled, Umm Ghaylan came to him, believing that Dir* was his brother. 'Umar told her, 'I am only his brother in Islam. I know of your favour to him. He gave her a gift as though she were a traveller.'" He went on, ''Dira b. al-Khatpb met up with Wmar b. al-Khay~bat the battle of Uhud, and began striking him with the flat of his spear, saying, 'Save yourself, Ibn d-Khattab, I shall not kill you! ' 'Umar thus acknowledged this act of his after Islam. God bless them both.
lessons: Courage in protecting the vulnerable is not limited to the powerful — a freedwoman with no tribal standing became the instrument of protection for an entire group of men.
Acts of kindness are remembered and rewarded, sometimes years later and by those we least expect, as Umar's gift to Umm Ghaylan demonstrates.
True brotherhood transcends blood and tribe — Umar's statement 'I am only his brother in Islam' shows that the bonds of shared values can be deeper than family.
practical_advice: When you witness someone being threatened or marginalized, ask yourself what small but courageous action you can take — protection does not always require power, only will.
Keep a record of kindnesses others have shown you. Revisiting these when you feel alone or unsupported reminds you that you are not without allies.
Reflect on at least one relationship in your life built on shared values rather than shared background, and invest in nurturing it this week.

title: The Prophet's Supplication Against Quraysh and the Seven Years of Famine
summary: After Quraysh persisted in rejecting and opposing the Prophet, he supplicated to God to afflict them with seven years of hardship like those of Prophet Joseph, leading to a devastating famine in Makkah that reduced people to eating carrion.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related at this point the imprecation of the Prophet (SAAS) against Quraysh when they withheld from him, resulting in seven years similar to the seven in Joseph's case. His account tallies with those in the jahih collections narrated through al-A'mash, from Muslim b. Subayh, from Masriiq, from Ibn Mus'iid. He (the Prophet (SAAS)) said, "Five things have come to pass; al-lzzdm, the punishment, the Romans, the 'smoke,' the conflict, and the moon." There is an account from Ibn Mus'iid saying, "When Quraysh rejected the Messenger of God (SAAS), and refrained from accepting Islam, he (the Prophet (SAAS)) said, '0 God, bring down upon Quraysb for me the seven years like those for Joseph!'" The account went on, "And they were aftlicted for a year until everything was completely ruined. They were reduced to eating carrion, and some, out of hunger, hallucinated so that they would imagine seeing smoke all the way to the heavens. Then he prayed, and God gave them relief. 'Abd Allah (b. Musciid) thereafter recited the following verse, 'We are withdrawing from you the punishment for a while; but you will revert' (swat al-Dukhan; XLIV, v.15). They did revert and disbelieved, and so were put off till Judgement Day. Or he said, 'and they were put off till the battle of Badr.' 'Abd Allah said, 'If the reference were really to Judgement Day, he would not have exposed them to the words, '... the day when we confront them with the great conflict. We will take revenge!' (swat al-Dukhdn; XLIV, v.16). He said, 'At the battle of Badr.' In (another) account from him, he said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw the people backing away, he said, '0God, (inflict) seven years (of calamity) like those of Joseph.' And so they suffered a year so bad that they ate carrion, leather and bones."
lessons: Even in the depths of persecution and rejection, the Prophet did not take personal revenge but turned to God — teaching us that the most powerful response to injustice is sincere supplication.
Hardship can be both a consequence of wrongdoing and an opportunity for people to return to God — suffering is not always punishment alone but sometimes an invitation to reflect.
The story of Prophet Joseph, referenced here as a template, teaches that years of hardship do not negate God's plan — relief comes in its own divinely appointed time.
practical_advice: When you feel powerless against a situation causing you harm, redirect your energy from seeking revenge toward sincere dua (supplication), trusting that God sees and responds.

In moments of personal 'famine' — emotional, financial, or relational scarcity — take inventory of what you still have and what you may need to let go of, rather than resorting to spiritually or ethically harmful shortcuts.

Study the story of Prophet Joseph (Surah Yusuf) during your next difficult period. Notice how the arc of his story — patience through layers of hardship leading to relief — can reframe your own suffering as part of a larger narrative.

title: Abu Sufyan Asks the Prophet to Pray for Rain After the Famine
summary: As the Makkan famine grew unbearable, Abu Sufyan and other Meccans — still non-believers — approached the Prophet and asked him to pray for relief, and the Prophet compassionately did so, resulting in abundant rain.
story: Aba Sufyan and other Meccans came to him and said, '0 Muhammad, you claim to have been given your mission of mercy. Here your people are perishing. Pray to God for them.' "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) did say a prayer and profuse rain descended on them. It poured down on them for seven days and people complained about the volume of rain. And so he said the words, '0 God, around us, hut not upon us! ' The clouds were then withdrawn from above his head, and the people around them were watered." He said, "The verse of siirat al-Dukhan was mentioned earlier and it referred to the starvation that had afflicted them. This refers to God's statement, 'We are withdrawing from you the punishment for a while; hut yon will revert.' And the verses of scrat al-Riim (XXX), and those referring to the 'grievous hold' (of siirat al-Dukhan; XLIV, v.16), and the 'splitting of the moon' (ofsiirat al-@mar; LIV, v.l), all came true at the battle of Badr."
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to pray for the relief of people who were actively persecuting him demonstrates that mercy is not conditional on receiving good treatment — it is a character trait, not a transaction.
Hardship has a way of humbling even the most arrogant — Abu Sufyan's approach to the Prophet he had been opposing shows that suffering opens doors that pride keeps shut.
God's mercy is wider than our expectations: relief can come even before a person has fully turned back to God, as an invitation rather than a reward.
practical_advice: Practice praying for people who have wronged or opposed you. This is not weakness — it is one of the highest forms of spiritual maturity and it protects your own heart from bitterness.
When you are in a period of sustained difficulty, consider whether pride or stubbornness is preventing you from reaching out to someone who could help. Humility, like Abu Sufyan's, is not defeat.

When you receive relief after hardship, pause to make a specific prayer of gratitude naming the exact difficulty that has passed. This anchors gratitude and prevents you from reverting to heedlessness.

title: The Prophecy of Byzantine Victory Over Persia and Abu Bakr's Wager
summary: When the Persians defeated the Byzantines, the Muslims hoped for a Byzantine recovery as fellow People of the Book. Abu Bakr, guided by the Prophet's prophecy, wagered with the polytheists that the Byzantines would prevail, and they did—reportedly on the day of the Battle of Badr.
story: Then al-Bayhaqi related the story of the Persians and the Byzantines and Almighty God's revelation of the verses: "Alif. Lam. Mim. The Byzantines have been defeated in the neighbouring territory, yet after their defeat they will overcome, after a few years. God makes the decision, both before and after. And on a certain day the believers will rejoice at God's assistance. God assists whomever He wishes, for He is the Almighty, the Merciful" (swat al-Riim; XXX, v.2). He then related through Sufyan al-Thawn, from Hahih b. Aba 'Amr, from Sacid h. Juhayr, from Ihn {Abbns, who said, "The Muslims would have liked the Byzantines to he victorious over the Persians because the former were the 'People of the Book'. The polytheists wanted the Persians to overcome the Byzantines because the former were idol worshippers. The Muslims mentioned this to Aba Bakr, who spoke about it with the Prophet (SAAS). The latter commented, 'They will prevail.' Ahn Bakr related this to the polytheists who said, 'Set a term for us and if they do prevail, then you get so-and-so while if our side wins we get so-and-so.' Aha Bakr then related this to the Prophet (SAAS), who said, 'So now I've made it a wager!' He went on, 'In less than ten (days).' And thereafter the Byzantines were victorious." We gave various lines of transmission for this hadith in our Tafsir (Exegesis) and recounted that the one who engaged in the wager with Aba Bakr was Umayya b. Khaiaf, that the bet was for five young ostriches and thar it had a time limit. T o this "the Trusting", Aba Bakr, made an increased bet on the orders of the Messenger of God (SAAS). The victory did go to the Byzantines over the Persians and it occurred on the day the battle of Badr was fought. Or it may have been the day of the Hudaybiyya truce. God knows best. I t is also related through al-Walid b. Muslim, who said, "Usayd al-Kilxbi related to us that he heard al-'Al%' b. al-Zubayr al-Kiabi relate from his father, as follows, 'I saw the victory of the Persians over the Byzantines and then that of the Byzantines over the Persians. I then saw the victory of the Muslims over both Rome and Persia, and their conquering of Syria and Iraq. And all of that over a period of 15 years!'"
lessons: Divine prophecy is a source of certainty for believers even when circumstances appear bleak—the Quran's prediction of Byzantine victory came at a moment of their defeat, teaching that God's promise transcends visible reality.
Standing firm in one's convictions when surrounded by mockery and opposition, as Abu Bakr did when wager against the polytheists, reflects the deep trust of a believer in God's word.

History's turning of empires and powers is a reminder of God's ultimate sovereignty; no worldly power endures forever, and believers should anchor their hopes in the divine rather than in political outcomes.
practical_advice: When facing situations that seem hopeless or are being mocked by others, recall that divine promises are not bound by present circumstances—reflect on a time God's guidance proved true in your own life.
If you are anxious about world events or political outcomes, redirect your energy toward what is within your control: your faith, your conduct, and your trust in God's plan.
Use moments of uncertainty as opportunities to deepen your study of the Quran and its prophecies, so that your faith is built on knowledge and conviction rather than emotion alone.

title: The Night Journey (Isra'): Scholarly Debate on Its Date and Significance
summary: Islamic scholars recorded differing opinions on when exactly the Prophet's Night Journey took place, with accounts placing it anywhere from 16 months to one year before the Hijra, in various months including Rajab and Rabi' al-Awwal.
story: Ibn CAs&ir related traditions about the night journey in materials telling of the early beginnings of the mission. Ibn Ishaq, however, gave them in this location, some ten years after the mission. Al-Bayhaqi related, through Miisa b. Wqba, from al-Zuhri whom he quoted as saying, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was taken on the night journey one year prior to his departure to Medina." He said, "And Ibn Lahi'a related it thus, from Aha al-Aswad, from Wrwa." Also, Al-H&im related, from al-Asamm, from A h a d b. 'Abd al-Jabbzr, from Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Asbar b. N q r , from Ismacil alSuddi, who said, "The five daily prayers were enjoined upon the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Jerusalem the night of his journey there, 16 months prior to his emigration." According to al-Suddi's statement, then, the night journey occurred in the month of Dha alaa'da, whereas the reports of al-Zuhri and Wrwa place it in Rabic al-Awwal. Aha Bakr b. Aba Shayba stated, "Wthmm related to us, from Sa'id b. Mins', that J%birand Ibn <Abb%s said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was born in the year of the elephant, Monday the 12th of Rabical-Awwal, and on that same day and month he was appointed Prophet, was taken up to heaven, emigrated, and died.'" The chain of transmission of the aforementioned hadith is flawed. The hZ.2 'Abd al-Ghani b. Surnr al-Maqdisi, however, included it in his biography, and he also quoted a tradition whose chain of authorities is unsound. We made mention of it in the material relating to the virtues of the month of Rajab. This tradition states that the night journey took place on the night of the 27th of Rajab. But God knows best. There are some who claim that the night journey took place on the first Friday night of Rajab, this having been the "night of the al-ragha'ib", the night of the great gifts, wherein the common system of prayer was first established. But there is no foundation for that. But God knows best. Some recite the following line concerning this: "It was a Friday night when the Prophet was made to ascend, the night of Friday, the first of Rajab." This poetry is somewhat weak, but we have quoted it merely as testimonial for those who maintain this.
lessons: Scholarly humility is a virtue—the repeated phrase 'God knows best' in discussions of disputed historical details models the importance of acknowledging the limits of human knowledge.
Uncertainty about secondary details does not diminish the core truth of an event; believers can hold firm to the essence of the Night Journey without needing to resolve every scholarly disagreement.

The convergence of key milestones—birth, prophethood, ascension, migration, death—on the same date in some narrations reflects a divine intentionality that invites deep reflection on the unity of God's plan for His Prophet.
practical_advice: When you encounter contradictory information on religious or historical matters, resist the urge to feel destabilized—acknowledge the uncertainty with humility and focus on the undisputed spiritual core of the matter.
Practice saying 'I don't know' or 'God knows best' in areas where certainty is beyond your reach; this frees you from the anxiety of needing to have all the answers.
Use moments of scholarly disagreement as an invitation to study the methodology of Islamic scholarship, which can deepen your appreciation for how carefully the tradition has preserved knowledge.

title: The Night Journey: The Prophet Rides Al-Buraq to Jerusalem and Leads the Prophets in Prayer
summary: The Prophet Muhammad was carried on the heavenly mount al-Buraq by the Angel Gabriel from the Sacred Mosque in Mecca to Jerusalem, where he found Abraham, Moses, and Jesus assembled, and led them all in prayer.
story: We will give here the gist of the words of Ibn Ishaq, God bless him. Having recounted the earlier sections, he went on, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) was taken by night from the mayid al-haram to the mayzd al-a@, the latter being the holy temple of Aelia. By then Islam had spread in Mecca into Quraysh and all the Arab tribes." He continued, "Concerning the night journey of the Messenger of God (SAAS), I learned what follows from a hadith that came down from Ibn Muscad, Aba Sa'id, 'A'isha, Mu'awiya, Umm Hani', daughter of Aba Talib, God bless them all, and al-Hasan b. Aba al-Hasan, Ibn Hisham al-Zuhri, Qatada and other scholars. Individually they gave information that combines in this account. "The night journey of the Messenger of God (SAAS) was filled with trials and tribulations and (instances) of God's power and authority; it provides a lesson for men of intelligence, a guidance, a mercy and an affirmation for those with faith and belief. It was certainly an act of God. "He carried him on this journey as and how He wished, to show him those of His signs He so desired. He thus surveyed some of the might of God, His will, power and authority by which He accomplishes His purposes. "'Abd Allah b. Masciid would say, as I have been informed, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS), was brought al-Burzq, that being the name of a mount on which previous prophets were carried; its stride was such that it could place its hooves as far as it could see. He was borne away on it. "His companion (Gabriel) then took him off to see the signs between heaven and earth. Eventually he reached Jerusalem, where he found Abraham, Moses and Jesus, in a company of prophets assembled for him. He led them in prayer. "Then he was brought three vessels, containing milk, wine and water. He said that he drank the vessel of milk and was told by Gabriel, 'You have been given guidance, and so has your nation.'" Ibn Ishaq related through al-Hasan al-Ba~ri, but with an incomplete line of transmission, that Gabriel woke him up then conducted him to the door of the masjzd al-haram, where he set him upon al-Bur~q,a white mount that was a cross between a mule and a donkey; on its flanks it had wings with which it set its feet in motion. It could place its hooves as far ahead as it could see. The account quotes the Messenger of God (SAAS) as saying, "He placed me upon it and then took me away, he not out-distancing me, nor I him." I may add that in the hadith, which came down from Qatada, as related by Ibn Ishnq, it says that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was about to mount al-Burzq, it became restive and so Gabriel placed his hand upon its neck where the mane grows and said, "Now Burnq, aren't you ashamed to do that? I swear, no servant of God more noble than Muhammad has ever ridden you." H e commented, "And it was so ashamed it burst out in sweat, then quietened down until I mounted it." Al-Hasan stated, in his Eadith, "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) departed, Gabriel accompanying him, until they reached Jerusalem. There he found Abraham, Moses and Jesus among a group of prophets. The Messenger of God (SAAS) acted as their Imam and led them in prayer." He then explained his choice of the vessel of milk over that of the wine, and how Gabriel told him, "You have been given guidance, as has your nation, and you have been forbidden wine."
lessons: The Prophet's selection of milk over wine symbolizes the choosing of natural purity and guidance over intoxication and heedlessness—a reminder that the choices we make in small moments reflect our deeper orientation toward God.

Leading the greatest prophets in prayer was a divine affirmation of the Prophet Muhammad's station; for believers in periods of humiliation or hardship, this event is a reminder that God elevates whom He wills.

The story of al-Buraq's initial restlessness and then submission upon hearing of the Prophet's nobility teaches that recognition of true greatness produces reverence and calm—a model for how we should approach sacred people and things.
practical_advice: When you feel uncertain about your path or direction in life, reflect on the image of al-Buraq—a creature that could see as far as it could place its feet—as an image of moving forward with purposeful, God-guided vision one step at a time.
If you are feeling spiritually low or insignificant, meditate on the fact that the Prophet was honored above all creation in this journey; your own dignity as a believer is connected to that honor.

Practice making conscious, guided choices—like the Prophet's choice of milk—by pausing before decisions to ask which option aligns with clarity, health, and divine guidance rather than momentary desire.

title: The Prophet Tells the People of the Night Journey and Is Disbelieved
summary: When the Prophet returned from the Night Journey and told Quraysh what he had experienced, most people disbelieved him and some who had previously accepted Islam even renounced their faith, while Abu Bakr immediately affirmed his belief.
story: He went on, "And then the Messenger of God (SAAS) departed for Mecca and set about telling Quraysh of all that." He recounted that most of the people disbelieved him and that one group reneged after having previously accepted Islam. Abii Bakr, "the Trusting", responded by expressing his belief and said, "I give him credence in communication from heaven, early in the day or in the evening, so how should I not believe him regarding Jerusalem?" (In his account al-Hasan) recounts that Aha Bakr asked him to describe Jerusalem, and that the Messenger of God (SAAS) did so. And, he states, "From that day on Aha Bakr was known as al-Szddiq, 'the Trusting'." Al-Hasan said, "And on that subject God revealed, 'And we rendered the visions we showed you nothing but a way of testing people'" (szirat al-Isra"; XVII, v.60).
lessons: Faith is tested most sharply when truth seems impossible to the rational mind; Abu Bakr's immediate belief without hesitation earned him the title 'al-Siddiq'—the Truthful—showing that loyalty to the Prophet is itself a form of nearness to God.
The apostasy of some believers upon hearing the account of the Night Journey is a sobering reminder that faith must be continuously nurtured; it is not a fixed possession but a living commitment.

God explicitly states that the Night Journey was a test for people—meaning that challenges to our faith, including things that seem incomprehensible, are by divine design meant to distinguish the sincere from the insincere.
practical_advice: When you share something true and meaningful and are met with disbelief or ridicule, find strength in the Prophet's example—he declared it openly despite knowing he would be mocked, because truth is worth speaking.
Identify the 'Abu Bakr' in your life: people whose faith and loyalty are steadfast regardless of how strange or difficult the circumstances seem, and draw close to them in times of social rejection.

If your own faith feels shaken by something you cannot fully comprehend or explain, do not rush to abandon your conviction—sit with the discomfort, seek knowledge, and allow your understanding to grow before making a verdict.

title: Umm Hani's Account: The Night Journey Began From Her Home
summary: Umm Hani', the Prophet's cousin, recounted that the Night Journey began from her own home, where the Prophet had slept, and described her attempt to stop him from telling people out of fear for him, which he refused.
story: Ibn Ishaq related, quoting Umm Hani' as his source, that she said, "It was from nowhere but my home that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made his night journey. He slept that night after having made the final evening prayer. When it was just before dawn he woke us up and when it was morning we prayed together and he said, '0 Umm HXni', last evening I prayed with you in this valley, then I went to Jerusalem and prayed there. And now here I am having said the morning prayer with you, as you see.' "He then arose to leave, but I took hold of the hem of his cloak and said, '0 Messenger of God, do not make that statement to people; they will surely disbelieve you and do you harm.' "But he replied, 'By God, I will certainly tell it to them!' And he did tell them and they did disbelieve."
lessons: The Prophet's insistence on speaking the truth even when warned of social harm teaches that moral courage is not the absence of awareness of consequences but the choice to speak truth despite them.

Umm Hani's concern for the Prophet, though it came from love, reflects a human tendency to try to protect those we love from discomfort at the cost of truth—a reminder to examine whether our caution is wisdom or a limitation of faith.
The ordinariness of the setting—a family home, an evening prayer, a dawn awakening—is a reminder that the most extraordinary divine encounters can occur in the most ordinary human contexts.
practical_advice: When you feel tempted to hide a truth because you fear people's reaction, ask yourself whether your silence serves God's purpose or merely protects you from discomfort—then find the courage to speak with wisdom and clarity.

Reflect on the people in your life who, like Umm Hani, care deeply for you but sometimes caution you out of fear rather than faith; receive their concern with love while discerning when to move forward anyway.
Create space in your daily routine—morning prayer, quiet reflection before dawn—for the kind of spiritual attentiveness that allows you to recognize and absorb extraordinary moments when they occur.

title: The Prophet Provides Proof of the Night Journey: The Caravans
summary: When the Quraysh demanded proof of the Night Journey, the Prophet described passing specific caravans along the route to Jerusalem, including details about a stray camel and a covered water vessel, as verifiable evidence of the journey.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on (and the Prophet (SAAS) told the sceptical, tr.) "And the proof of this is that I passed by the caravan of such-and-such a tribe in suchand-such a valley, and the sound of the animal (al-Buraq) startled them and so one of their camels ran away. I led them to it, being then on my way to al-Sh%. Then I continued on until I reached DajanXn, where I passed a caravan of suchand-such a tribe. I found the people asleep. They had a vessel with water in it that they had covered over with something. I took off the cover and drank the water and replaced its cover. And the proof of this is that their caravan is now making its way down from the pass at al-Tan'im al-Baydz. Leading it is an ash-coloured camel on which are two sacks, one of which is..."
lessons: The Prophet's offering of verifiable, specific, testable evidence for the Night Journey demonstrates that faith in Islam is not blind—it engages the rational mind and welcomes scrutiny.
Even in the face of mass disbelief, the Prophet did not retreat into vagueness but gave precise details, modeling the confidence of one whose conscience is clear and whose source is divine.

The details of caravans and covered water vessels remind us that divine power operates through the real, physical world—God's miracles are not always spectacular abstractions but are woven into the texture of everyday reality.
practical_advice: When defending something true that others doubt, remain specific and grounded rather than becoming defensive or vague—specificity is the language of truth and it builds credibility over time.
If you are in a situation where your integrity is being questioned, reflect on what concrete, verifiable evidence you can offer rather than relying solely on the force of your assertion.

Use this story as a reminder that God's care for the Prophet extended even to leaving behind traces of evidence for skeptics—trust that God can and does provide what is needed to vindicate the truth at the right time.

title: The Mi'raj: The Ladder to Heaven
summary: After completing his journey to Jerusalem, the Prophet Muhammad was brought a magnificent ladder — the mi'raj — through which he ascended to the heavens, an experience he described as unlike anything he had ever seen.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "A source I do not doubt quoted Aha Sacid as having said, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, "When I had finished in Jerusalem, the mi%aj, the ladder, was brought to me. Never before did I see anything finer; it is that to which your dying turn their eyes at the point of death. "'My companion (Gabriel) led me up it until I reached one of the gates of heaven known as the bab al-hafi?a, 'the guards' gate'. It is overseen by an attendant who is an angel, and his name is Ismacil. He commands 12,000 angels, each of whom controls 12,000 more."' "He (Ah3 Sacid) stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) whenever he tells this hadith recites, "And no one but He knows the armies of your Lord"' (swat al-Muddaththir; LXXIV, v.34)."
lessons: The beauty and grandeur of what awaits beyond this life is beyond human imagination — the Prophet's awe reminds us that this world is only a small part of a much larger reality.
Guidance and companionship in difficult journeys is a mercy from God — Gabriel's role as a companion on the mi'raj reflects how God does not leave His servants to navigate the unknown alone.
The armies and structure of the heavens, beyond all human comprehension, remind us that God's dominion is vast and our worries, however heavy, are held within something infinitely greater.
practical_advice: When life feels overwhelming or claustrophobic, remind yourself that existence extends far beyond what your eyes can see — this perspective can loosen anxiety's grip.
Seek a trustworthy companion or mentor when navigating unfamiliar or frightening territory; isolation amplifies fear.
Recite or reflect on Quranic verses about the unseen when you feel small or lost — they are anchors to a larger reality.

title: The Ascent Through the Seven Heavens
summary: During the Night Journey, the Prophet Muhammad ascended through the seven heavens on a ladder, being welcomed at each level by its angels and meeting the great prophets — Adam, John, Jesus, Idris, Moses, and Abraham.
story: He rose one heaven to another on the ladder until he passed over the seventh heaven. Whenever he reached a heaven its favoured attendants and the most important angels and prophets therein would meet him. He made reference to major persons from among the mursalzn, the messengers of God, like Adam in the nearest heaven, John and Jesus in the second, Idris in the fourth, Moses in the sixth, and Abraham in the seventh, leaning his back against the bayt al-ma'mur, the "eternal abode", that is entered each day by 70,000 angels who worship therein in prayer and circumambulation, and they do not return there again until Judgement Day. He then ascended above their ranks and reached a level where the squeaking of pens could be heard.
lessons: Every stage of spiritual elevation brings new encounters and new forms of support — the Prophet was not alone at any level of the heavens, reminding us that growth is accompanied by community.
The bayt al-ma'mur, perpetually filled with worshipping angels, teaches that devotion is a constant, ceaseless act — not reserved for when we feel motivated.
The "squeaking of pens" recording destiny is a reminder that all things are in God's hands, which can be a source of profound relief rather than anxiety.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually stagnant, recall that elevation happens step by step — you do not leap from one level to the highest; trust the process of gradual growth.
Establish a consistent, daily act of worship even if it is small — consistency matters more than intensity, as the angels of the bayt al-ma'mur show us.
When anxious about the future, meditate on the idea that your destiny is already written by a Wise, Knowing God — this is not fatalism, but a reason for peace.

title: The Sidrat al-Muntaha and the Vision of Gabriel
summary: At the furthest boundary of the heavens, the Prophet Muhammad saw the Sidrat al-Muntaha — a magnificent tree shrouded in divine light — and beheld Gabriel in his true, original form of 600 wings.
story: There the sidrat al-muntahii, the "late tree at the boundaryn,8 stood high before the Messenger of God (SAAS); its leaves were like the ears of an elephant, its fruit like summits of Mt. Hidjr, and covered all about by great deeds and many splendid colours, with angels perching upon it as numerous as starlings on a tree. And there was a canopy of gold, bathed in the light of our Lord, All-Glorious is He. There also he saw Gabriel, peace be upon him. He had upon him 600 wings with the space between each two wings as great as that between heaven and earth. It is Gabriel of whom God Almighty spoke thus: "And he certainly saw him in another revelation, at the s~dratalmmtaha, at which is the garden of eternity. When the tree was shrouded in its covering, sight did not turn aside, nor did it exceed its bound" (siirat al-Nap; LIII, v.13-17). That is, it (i.e. sight, tr.) did not turn aside to the right or to the left, nor did it look up at that which was out of bounds. This was both tremendous fortitude (on the part of Muhammad) and a great show of respect (for God). This second vision (that he had) of Gabriel, peace he upon him, in the natural form in which God created him, is in accord with the statements transmitted by Ibn Mus'iid, Abn Hurayra, Abii Dharr and 'il'isha, God be pleased with them all.
lessons: The Prophet's gaze did not wander or exceed its bound even in the most overwhelmingly magnificent place in creation — self-restraint and humility are the marks of true spiritual strength.
Gabriel, a being of immense power spanning between heaven and earth, was entirely in the service of God and of the Prophet — greatness is not about dominance but about purpose-driven service.
Beauty beyond imagination exists in the next life; when this world feels dull, grey, or painful, remembering that God has prepared something far greater can sustain the heart.
practical_advice: Practice the discipline of keeping your focus where it belongs — in prayer, in work, in relationships — rather than letting your attention drift to what is forbidden or distracting.
When you feel small and overwhelmed by life's magnitude, remember that the most powerful beings in creation are in service to the one God who also cares for you.
On days when the world feels colourless or hopeless, spend time reflecting on descriptions of Jannah — not as escapism, but as genuine spiritual renewal.

title: The First Vision of Gabriel at al-Abtah
summary: The Prophet Muhammad's first vision of Gabriel in his true form occurred at al-Abtah near Mecca, where Gabriel stretched out across the entire span between earth and sky until only a bow's length or two separated them.
story: The first vision (that Muhammad had of Gabriel) was, in the words of the Almighty, when, "(an) all-powerful (angel) taught him, the one endowed with supreme power, who (appeared) in his natural form while in the highest point on the horizon. He then drew near and came close, until he was two bow lengths away, or closer. Then he made his revelation to His servant" (siirat al-Najm; LIII, v.6-10). That took place at al-Abt&.9 Gabriel stretched out over the Messenger of God (SAAS), the enormity of his being dominating all between earth and sky, until there was only the space of a bow's length or two between them both.
lessons: The first encounter with something far greater than ourselves can be terrifying — the Prophet's ability to remain present and receive revelation in such a moment shows that courage is not the absence of awe but the presence of trust.

God prepares His servants for great responsibilities through great experiences — the overwhelming nature of the first revelation was not meant to crush the Prophet but to establish the weight and importance of what was being given.
Drawing near to the Divine, even through its representatives, requires that we come with openness rather than resistance.
practical_advice: When you encounter something that overwhelms or frightens you, pause before reacting — breathe, ground yourself, and ask what God may be calling you toward through this experience.
If a responsibility feels too large for you, remember that those entrusted with the greatest missions were also given the greatest support — you are not expected to carry your burdens alone.
Reflect on Surah al-Najm (53:6-10) as a meditation on closeness to God and the nature of divine communication.

title: The Prescription of the Five Daily Prayers
summary: On the night of the Mi'raj, God prescribed fifty daily prayers upon the Prophet, but through Moses's counsel and repeated intercession, the number was reduced to five, which carry the reward of fifty.
story: It was on that night that God Almighty and Glorious enjoined the daily prayers upon His servant Muhammad (God's peace and blessings be upon him and upon his nation); these were to number fifty prayers each day and night. Thereafter he repeatedly went between Moses and his Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, until finally the Lord, All Resplendent is He, and to Him is all credit due, decreed that they he five. He stated, "They are five and (yet) they are fifty, credit being ten times the number." That evening speech came to him directly from the Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He. The I m a s of the orthodox faith are unanimous regarding this.
lessons: God's mercy is embedded in the very structure of worship — what He asks of us has already been made lighter than what His generosity would reward, showing that He wants ease not hardship for His servants.

Moses's practical wisdom — advising the Prophet to ask for reduction because he knew the difficulty of human capacity — teaches that drawing on the experience of those who came before us is a form of wisdom, not weakness.
The five prayers are not a burden but a gift of fifty rewards — reframing obligations as gifts transforms our relationship to duty.
practical_advice: If your spiritual practice feels overwhelming, examine whether you have added burdens upon yourself that God has not required — return to the essentials and let them be enough.
When you are struggling to maintain consistency in worship, recall that God reduced fifty to five out of mercy for human limitation — He knows your capacity and is not asking for the impossible.
Seek advice from those with more experience when a responsibility feels too heavy; Moses's guidance to the Prophet is a model of mentorship across generations.

title: The Scholarly Dispute Over the Prophet's Vision of God
summary: Islamic scholars differed over whether the Prophet Muhammad saw God during the Night Journey — some held he saw God with his heart, others with his eyes, and others denied any direct vision occurred.
story: The I m a s of the orthodox faith are unanimous regarding this. They differ regarding his seeing Him. Some consider that he saw God twice, in his heart. Ihn 'Abbas and his adherents maintain this. Ihn 'Abbas generalized (in discussion) about the vision, hut others have offered a more narrow interpretation. Those who spoke in general terms about the vision include Aha Hurayra and Ahmad b. Hanhal, God he pleased with them. Some authorities, however, state their conviction that the vision was by means of his eyes. Ibn Jarir preferred this and insisted upon it, being followed in this by others of the more recent authorities. Among those considering that the sight occurred through the naked eyes included Sheikh Abn al-Hasan al-Ash'ari, as reported by al-Suhayli and it is the preferred view of Sheikh Ahii ZakariyyZ' al-Nawawi in hisfitama,1° his legal decisions. One faction maintains that the vision never occurred, because of the hadith in the s h r h collection of Muslim, from Abn Dharr. It states, "I said, '0 Messenger of God, did you see your Lord?' He replied, 'A light! How could I have seen it!' In another account the words are, 'I saw a light'." These scholars say that the sight of the Everlasting could never he through eyes that are ephemeral. Therefore God Almighty said to Moses, as is reported in certain holy texts, "0 Moses, no mortal being can see me until he dies, nor any dry thing until it moves." The dispute over this question perplexed the early scholars just as it does those who succeeded them. God alone knows best.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement on profound questions is a sign of intellectual honesty, not weakness — the greatest minds in Islamic history held different views on this question and remained united in their faith.
Some questions are not meant to be fully resolved in this life — accepting the limits of human knowledge is itself an act of submission and trust in God.
The phrase 'God alone knows best' is not an intellectual retreat but a profound act of humility that keeps the heart open rather than closed.
practical_advice: When you encounter religious questions you cannot resolve, resist the pressure to force a definitive answer — sit with the question and trust that uncertainty does not undermine your faith.
Explore multiple scholarly perspectives on matters of confusion rather than accepting only one view — the diversity of Islamic scholarship is a resource, not a problem.
Practice saying 'I don't know, and that is okay' — intellectual humility is a strength that protects you from both arrogance and unnecessary anxiety.

title: The Prophets Descending with Muhammad from the Divine Presence
summary: After the Prophet Muhammad returned from the Divine Presence, the prophets of the heavens descended with him to Jerusalem as an act of respect and honour, as is the custom for those who have visited a great host.
story: Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) came hack down to Jerusalem. And it seems clear that the prophets descended with him to express their respect for him on his return from the glorious Divine Presence, as is the custom for those who visit; visitors do not assemble with others before meeting that person to whom they have been summoned. That is why when he passed by one of them Gabriel would say,
lessons: Honour is often expressed through presence — the prophets did not merely send well-wishes but accompanied the Prophet, showing that being with someone in their significant moments is itself a form of love and respect.
The Prophet Muhammad was held in honour not only by his community on earth but by the greatest figures in human history — this speaks to the weight and dignity of his mission.
Community and solidarity at moments of profound spiritual experience amplify and affirm what the individual has been through.
practical_advice: Show up for the people you love at their important moments — your presence is often more meaningful than any gift or words you could offer.
When you have been through something spiritually significant, seek to share it with your community — isolation after a transformative experience can cause it to fade rather than take root.

Reflect on the honour God bestowed on the Prophet through the gathering of all the prophets — when you feel unacknowledged or undervalued, remember that true recognition comes from God, not only from people around you.

title: The Night Journey: Return to Jerusalem and Leading the Prophets in Prayer
summary: After his ascent to the heavens, the Prophet Muhammad descended back to Jerusalem where he led all the gathered prophets in prayer as their Imam, then returned to Mecca on al-Buraq.
story: Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) came back down to Jerusalem. And it seems clear that the prophets descended with him to express their respect for him on his return from the glorious Divine Presence, as is the custom for those who visit; visitors do not assemble with others before meeting that person to whom they have been summoned. That is why when he passed by one of them Gabriel would say, as each approached to greet him, 'This is so-and-so; greet him.' If he had met with them before his ascent he would not have needed introduction to them a second time. Substantiation for this is given in his saying, 'And when it was time for the prayer, I acted as their Imam.' The only time for that, then, would have been the dawn prayer. He came forward as their Imam on the instruction of Gabriel, relaying what his Lord, Almighty and Glorious is He, told him. Some scholars deduce from this that the most eminent Imam takes precedence over the master of the house, since Jerusalem was their (the other prophets) place of residence. He then left there, riding on al-Buraq, and returned to Mecca.
lessons: True leadership is a trust given by God, not seized by ambition — the Prophet led the greatest assembly of prophets not by his own claiming but by divine instruction.
Humility accompanies the highest honors; even after the greatest spiritual experience in human history, the Prophet returned quietly and soberly to his people.
Every prophet is part of one unified tradition; the Night Journey revealed the deep brotherhood and continuity across all messengers of God.
practical_advice: When you are placed in a position of responsibility or leadership, remind yourself that it is a trust from God and approach it with humility rather than pride.
After profound or transformative experiences, take time to be still and reflective before rushing to share or act — the Prophet returned in calm sobriety.
Recognize that you are part of something larger than yourself; connecting to a greater tradition or community can ground you when you feel isolated or overwhelmed.

title: Abu Bakr's Unwavering Faith: 'If He Said It, He Spoke the Truth'
summary: When the Quraysh mocked the Prophet's account of the Night Journey and went to Abu Bakr to discredit him, Abu Bakr immediately affirmed his belief without hesitation, earning the title Al-Siddiq (the Truthful Affirmer).
story: Next morning he was back, in a state of complete security, peace and dignity. That night he saw such signs and things as to make anyone else who saw them, even in part, either utterly amazed or even insane. He, however, became merely sombre, quiet that is, and was fearful that if he started to tell his people what he had seen they would have promptly disbelieved him. And so at first he told them calmly that he had been to Jerusalem that night. That was when Abu Jahl, God damn him, saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), in the sacred mosque sitting in sombre silence and spoke to him, asking, 'Well, anything new?' He replied, 'Yes.' 'What is it?' Abu Jahl asked. 'I was taken this night to Jerusalem.' 'To Jerusalem?' 'Yes.' Abu Jahl asked, 'If I were to call your people over to you for you to tell them, would you say to them what you said to me?' 'Yes, I would,' he replied. Abu Jahl wanted to assemble Quraysh to hear that from him, and so too did the Messenger of God (SAAS) also want to gather them to tell them and so give them information. Abu Jahl then said, 'Come, O tribe of Quraysh!' They gathered there from their assemblies. He then said, 'Well, tell your people what you told me.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) related to them what he had seen, that he had gone to Jerusalem that night and prayed there. Amidst clapping and whistling of disbelief and derision at this report, the news spread quickly over Mecca. People then went to Abu Bakr, God bless him, and told him that Muhammad (SAAS) was saying such-and-such a thing. He responded, 'You are telling lies about him!' They replied, 'No, we swear it, he is saying that.' 'Well,' said Abu Bakr, 'if he said that, he spoke the truth.' He then went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) who was surrounded by the pagans of Mecca, and asked him about that. He told him of it all and Abu Bakr asked him to describe Jerusalem, so that the polytheists would hear him and recognize the veracity of what he had told them.
lessons: True friendship means standing by someone when the whole world turns against them, not only when it is comfortable or safe to do so.
Faith that is rooted in deep personal knowledge and trust can remain unshaken even when the evidence presented by others seems overwhelming.
The people who believe in you during your darkest and most ridiculed moments are among the most precious gifts God can give.
practical_advice: When someone you trust and love is being mocked or dismissed by others, investigate before you join the crowd — Abu Bakr's first instinct was to defend, not to doubt.
Reflect on the relationships in your life: who would stand by you unconditionally? Invest in and nurture those bonds deeply.
If you face public ridicule for something you know to be true, find strength in even one sincere ally — that solidarity can carry you through the storm.

title: The Prophet Describes Jerusalem to the Quraysh as Divine Proof
summary: When the Quraysh demanded the Prophet describe Jerusalem to test the truth of his Night Journey, he became momentarily confused until God made Jerusalem visible to him, allowing him to describe it perfectly.
story: In the sahih collection the account has it that it was the polytheists who asked the Messenger of God about that. He said, 'I then began telling them about His signs, and I became somewhat confused. And so God made Jerusalem clear to me until I could see it beyond 'Uqayl's house, and I described it to them.' He (Ibn Ishaq) went on, 'In his description he was correct.' Ibn Ishaq recounted the information we gave earlier about him telling them of his having passed by their caravan and of having drunk their water. And so God provided proof for them and illuminated the straight path for them. Some did believe because of their conviction from God, while others disbelieved despite the proof they had. As God Almighty said, 'And we only made the visions we have shown you as a test for the people' (surat al-Isra'; XVII, v.60). That is, they were a way of testing and trying them.
lessons: When you are put on the spot and feel your mind go blank, turning inward to God in that moment of confusion is itself an act of trust and worship.
Divine support does not always come before the moment of need — sometimes it arrives precisely at the moment of greatest pressure, teaching us to rely on God rather than only our own preparation.
Signs and miracles do not guarantee that hearts will open; belief ultimately rests on the condition of the heart, not the quantity of proof presented.
practical_advice: In moments of mental confusion or pressure — during a difficult conversation, test, or confrontation — pause, breathe, and ask God internally for clarity before continuing.
Do not be discouraged when you present truth clearly and others still reject it; your responsibility is to convey sincerely, not to control outcomes.
Recognize that trials and tests are woven into even the most extraordinary divine gifts; expect difficulty alongside blessing and prepare your heart accordingly.

title: Scholarly Debate: Was the Night Journey Physical, Spiritual, or Both?
summary: Islamic scholars across generations have debated whether the Prophet's Night Journey was a physical journey with his body, a purely spiritual experience, or a combination of both, with the majority concluding it was both body and spirit while awake.
story: Ibn 'Abbas said, 'These were visions perceived by the eye that were shown to the Messenger of God (SAAS).' This view, that of the majority of scholars both ancient and more recent, holds that the night journey was both a physical and a spiritual experience for the Messenger of God (SAAS). This is shown in the clear accounts of his making a journey and of his ascending on the ladder, and such-like. God therefore stated, 'Glory be to Him who took his servant by night from the sacrosanct mosque to the further mosque whose precincts we have blessed, to show him our signs' (surat al-Isra'; XVII, v.1). Such evocation of glory would only occur for truly great and extraordinary signs. And this proves that it was by both body and spirit, and the word 'servant' gives expression to both of these together. Also, if it had been a dream, the Quraysh polytheists would not have promptly expressed their disbelief and outrage. For that would not have been so important a matter. And so it shows that he did tell them that he had been taken on his night journey while awake, not asleep. And then there is the statement of Shurayk, from Anas: 'Then I awoke and found myself inside the hijr, the sacred enclosure.' This is either to be considered an error of Shurayk, or it must be concluded that the movement from the one state to the other is to be noted as 'wakefulness'... Ibn Ishaq stated, 'God knows best which of that it was. He did go there and did see there what he saw of God Almighty, whether he was asleep or awake. All of that is, however, factual and true.' My own comment is that Ibn Ishaq stopped at that point, having combined both possibilities in one whole. But one thing beyond doubt or disagreement is that he was certainly awake, for all the reasons given above. It is not necessary to interpret the words of 'A'isha, God bless her, that it was not his body, but his spirit that made the journey, as implying that he was asleep, as Ibn Ishaq understood it. The journey might well have in fact occurred for his soul while he was awake and not asleep; he could have ridden al-Buraq, gone to Jerusalem, ascended into heaven and seen all he did while awake and not asleep.
lessons: Scholarly disagreement on matters where certainty is beyond human reach is a sign of intellectual honesty, not weakness — saying 'God knows best' is itself wisdom.
Not every question of faith needs a definitive human answer; sitting with uncertainty while affirming core truth is a mark of spiritual maturity.
The miraculous nature of divine events is not diminished by human inability to fully classify or explain them — wonder and reverence are appropriate responses.
practical_advice: When you encounter complex theological or philosophical questions that create confusion, focus on what is certain and agreed upon rather than being paralyzed by peripheral disputes.
Practice saying 'God knows best' genuinely — it is not an evasion but an honest acknowledgment of human limits before infinite divine knowledge.
Allow yourself to hold open questions without anxiety; not all uncertainty is a crisis, and faith can coexist with unanswered questions.

title: The Night Journey (Isra'): Prophet Muhammad Describes Jerusalem to Quraysh
summary: After Quraysh disbelieved his account of the Night Journey, the Prophet described Jerusalem's distinctive features from the Hijr, while God made the city visible to him so he could recount it accurately.
story: On the morning following the night of his journey, Gabriel came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at about noon. He then explained to him the manner of prayer and its times. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told his followers to assemble and Gabriel led him in prayer all that day until the next, with the Prophet (SAAS) acting as the Imam of the Muslims, and following the example of Gabriel. Al-Bukhari's text reads: 'It was related to us by Yahya b. Bukayr quoting al-Layth, from 'Uqayl, from Ibn Shihab who said that Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman related to him as follows, "I heard Jabir b. 'Abd Allah say that he heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'When Quraysh expressed their disbelief in me I was in the hijr. And God made clear Jerusalem to me and so I set about telling them its distinctive features while I viewed it.'"' Muslim, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related this from a hadith of al-Zuhri, from Abu Salama from Jabir. Muslim, al-Nasa'i and al-Tirmidhi also related it from a hadith of 'Abd Allah b. al-Fadl, from Abu Salama, from Abu Hurayra, from the Prophet (SAAS) in similar terms.
lessons: When facing disbelief and ridicule, God can provide miraculous support to affirm the truth of His messenger's experience.
Steadfast honesty in the face of mockery is a mark of true character; the Prophet did not waver in his account despite Quraysh's rejection.
Divine assistance often comes in unexpected forms — clarity, vision, and certainty granted at the moment of greatest need.
practical_advice: When others doubt or dismiss your experiences, remain calm and grounded in what you know to be true rather than seeking their validation.
In moments of public rejection, recall that God's affirmation is more valuable than social acceptance.
Share your truth with clarity and confidence, trusting that the evidence you need will be provided at the right time.

title: The Ascent Through the Heavens (Mi'raj): The Prophet Meets the Prophets
