summary: During the Night Journey and Ascent, the Prophet Muhammad was taken through the seven heavens on al-Buraq, meeting Adam, Jesus, John, Joseph, Idris, Aaron, Moses, and Abraham, each welcoming him as he ascended.
story: 'It was related to us by Hudha b. Khalid, from Hammam and Qatada, from Anas b. Malik, from Malik b. Sa'sa'a, that the Prophet (SAAS) related to them as follows about the night he was taken on a journey, "While I was lying down there in the hatim" — or he may well have said the hijr — "an apparition came to me and began cutting." I (Malik b. Sa'sa'a, tr.) then heard him say, "And he split open from this to this." I asked al-Jarad who was by my side, "What does he mean by that?" And he replied, "From the hollow of his neck down to his pubic hair." And I heard him say, from "his breastbone down to his pubic hair." The Prophet (SAAS) went on, "Then he withdrew my heart and a basin of gold filled with faith was brought to me. He then washed my heart and it was stuffed and replaced. After that I was brought a white mount that was smaller than a mule but larger than a donkey." Al-Jarad asked, "Was that al-Buraq, Abu Hamza?" Anas responded, "Yes." The Prophet (SAAS) continued, "It could span with its stride as far as it could see. I was mounted upon it and Gabriel led me away until he reached the lowest heaven. He asked for me to go in and a reply came, 'Who is this?' 'Gabriel,' he replied. 'And who is with you?' He replied, 'Muhammad.' 'And has he been given a mission?' 'Yes,' Gabriel answered. 'Then welcome to him! His arrival is a pleasure!' And so he opened up. When I went in I found Adam therein. Gabriel said, 'This is your father Adam; greet him!' I did so and he saluted me in return, saying, 'Welcome to my pure son, to the pure Prophet!' I was then taken up to the second heaven and when he asked to go inside, he was asked who it was. The same responses and questions as before followed and when I went in there were John and Jesus, who were cousins on the maternal side. Gabriel again introduced me and we exchanged the same greeting as with Adam. Then I was taken up to the third heaven where Gabriel again asked entry and was met with the same questions and responses. When I went in, there was Joseph, whom I greeted, and I received welcome from him as before. Taken to the fourth heaven, there were the same questions and responses and when I went in I found Idris there, exchanged greetings, and he welcomed me as before. Brought up to the fifth heaven, the same questions and responses were made, and when I entered there was Aaron, with whom I exchanged greetings and from whom I received welcome. Then at the sixth heaven the same questions and responses were made and when I entered, there was Moses, with whom I exchanged greetings and who made me welcome. And when I went further on, he wept. When he was asked why, Moses replied, 'I weep because a young man who has received his mission after me will have a greater number of his nation enter paradise than will of mine.' I was then taken to the seventh heaven where the same questions and responses were made. When I went in there was Abraham, with whom I exchanged greetings and who welcomed me."'
lessons: Spiritual elevation is a process — the Prophet ascended step by step, meeting each prophet in turn, showing that growth and closeness to God comes in stages.
The brotherhood of all prophets is affirmed; they welcomed one another with love and respect, demonstrating unity of purpose across generations.
Moses's tears at the success of another nation reflect a profound humility and sincerity — he celebrated righteousness even when it exceeded his own legacy.
practical_advice: Approach your own spiritual journey as a gradual ascent — do not be discouraged if closeness to God comes in stages rather than all at once.
Honor the teachers, mentors, and spiritual guides who came before you; their wisdom is a gift passed down through generations.
When you witness others succeeding beyond your own achievements, train your heart to respond with joy and encouragement rather than envy.

title: The Sidrat al-Muntaha and the Four Rivers of Paradise
summary: At the farthest boundary of heaven, the Prophet witnessed the Sidrat al-Muntaha, four rivers — two hidden rivers of paradise and the visible Nile and Euphrates — and the Bayt al-Ma'mur where 70,000 angels enter daily.
story: "Then I was raised up to the sidrat al-muntaha; there were four rivers, two open to see, two hidden. I asked Gabriel, 'What is this, Gabriel?' He replied, 'The two hidden rivers are those of paradise, while the two visible ones are the Nile and the Euphrates.' Then the bayt al-ma'mur was raised for me to see, wherein each day 70,000 angels enter. I was then brought a vessel of wine, another of milk, and a third of honey. I took the milk. Gabriel said, 'That is al-fitra that you and your nation follow."'
lessons: The fitra — the natural human disposition — is affirmed as the blessed path; choosing it, as the Prophet did instinctively, is always the right choice.
The unseen world is vast and filled with worship; 70,000 angels entering the Bayt al-Ma'mur daily reminds us that we are never alone in our devotion.
God's blessings flow into this world from their heavenly origins; the Nile and Euphrates connecting to the rivers of paradise shows the divine generosity embedded in creation.
practical_advice: Trust your natural, wholesome instincts when making choices — the fitra is a built-in moral compass given by God.
When you feel alone in your worship or struggle, remember that creation is filled with beings in constant devotion; you are part of something far greater.
Reflect on the blessings of nature around you — rivers, rain, sustenance — as expressions of divine mercy flowing from their source.

title: The Negotiation of the Five Daily Prayers: Moses's Counsel to the Prophet
summary: During the Mi'raj, God initially prescribed fifty daily prayers; through repeated intercession advised by Moses, the Prophet returned to God multiple times until the prayers were reduced to five, carrying the reward of fifty.
story: "Prayer was then enjoined on me, 50 prayers each day. Then I went back and when I passed by Moses, he asked, 'What orders were you given?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'I was ordered to pray 50 times each day.' Moses commented, 'Your nation can't manage 50 prayers a day. I swear, I put people to the test before your time and I made some very severe requirements of the people of Israel. Return to your Lord and ask him for some relief for your nation.' And so I did return and ten prayers were lifted from me. I went back to Moses, he said as he had before, I returned and ten more were removed. Again back to Moses, I returned once more and ten more were removed. Once more to Moses, he said as before, and I returned and was ordered to make ten prayers each day. Moses then spoke as before, and again I returned and was ordered to make five prayers each day. When I went back to Moses, he asked, 'What were your orders?' I replied, 'To make five prayers each day.' He commented, 'Your people can't manage five prayers each day. I tried my people before your time and made severe requirements of the people of Israel. Return again and ask your Lord for some alleviation for your nation.' I replied, 'I have already made such requests of my Lord as to make me ashamed; therefore I'd much rather accept and be satisfied.' As I passed on, a voice called out to me, 'I have completed my decree. I have alleviated the burden upon my servants.'"
lessons: God's mercy is boundless — He reduced the burden of prayer from fifty to five while preserving the full spiritual reward, showing His deep care for human capacity.
Seeking counsel from those with experience, even when you have direct access to guidance, is a mark of wisdom; Moses's practical knowledge of human nature proved invaluable.
There is a noble moment when one must stop asking for more concessions and accept God's decree with gratitude — the Prophet's sense of haya' (dignified restraint) models this beautifully.
practical_advice: When a religious or personal obligation feels overwhelming, seek wise counsel from those who have experience managing such burdens — they may see solutions you cannot.
Recognize that God does not burden a soul beyond its capacity; if you are struggling with spiritual practice, start with what is manageable and build gradually.
Know when to stop negotiating with life's difficulties and instead accept what has been decreed with trust and contentment — this itself is a form of spiritual strength.

title: Gabriel Teaches the Prophet the Times and Manner of the Five Daily Prayers
summary: On the morning after the Night Journey, Gabriel came to the Prophet at noon and demonstrated the five daily prayers by leading him in prayer at their prescribed times across two days, establishing the practice for the Muslim community.
story: On the morning following the night of his journey, Gabriel came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) at about noon. He then explained to him the manner of prayer and its times. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told his followers to assemble and Gabriel led him in prayer all that day until the next, with the Prophet (SAAS) acting as the Imam of the Muslims, and following the example of Gabriel. This is told in the hadith from Ibn 'Abbas and Jabir, "Gabriel led me in prayer twice at the house (of God)." He thus explained to him the two times, the beginning and the end, that is, of the five daily prayers. The expanse of time between them is called the al-waqt al-muwassa' the 'extended period', but he made no mention of such an extension of time for the prayer at sunset.
lessons: The greatest gifts from God come with practical instruction — after the miraculous experience of the Mi'raj, Gabriel immediately taught the Prophet how to implement its most important decree.
Learning through example is a powerful form of transmission; Gabriel's leading the Prophet in prayer established a living demonstration that passed through generations.
The prayer times are not arbitrary but are tied to the rhythm of the day — dawn, noon, afternoon, sunset, and night — connecting human worship to the natural order of creation.
practical_advice: When you receive a new commitment or responsibility, seek out a mentor or guide who can show you how to fulfill it properly through their own example.
Establish the five daily prayers as anchors in your day; rather than fitting prayer into your schedule, build your schedule around your prayers.
If the prayers feel burdensome, remember they were a gift negotiated with care and mercy — approach each one as a personal meeting with God rather than an obligation to check off.

title: The Prescription of Prayer and Its Rak'at
summary: An account of how the daily prayers were first prescribed as two rak'at, later expanded for residents while kept at two for travelers, with scholarly discussion on the matter.
story: of which are in the compendium of Muslim. Discussion of this occurs in our book al-Ahkam (The Regulutions). And to God is all praise. Also affumed in the hadith of the compendium of al-Bukhsri is the statement brought down from Macmar, from al-Znhri, from Wrwa, from 'A'isha, who said, "When prayer was first prescribed it was just two rak'at,16 prostrations; this was subsequently established only for the prayer on travel, with more rak'at being added for prayer when in residence." Al-Awza'i related it thus, from al-Zuhri, and al-Sha'bi gave it from Masrnq back to 'A)isha. This is problematic in view of the fact that 'A'isha used to perform the prayer in its entirety while travelling, as also did Wthmm b. 'Affan. We discussed this matter with regard to God's words, "And if you journey in the world there is no blame on you if yon shorten the prayer if you fear incitement from those who disbelieve" (siirat al-Nzs2; IV, v.101). Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Al-Hasan al-Basri held the view that prayer while resident when first prescribed was made to be four rakCZt. He similarly referred to it, through a hadith with an incomplete chain of authorities, regarding the prayer made by the Prophet (SAAS) on the morning after the night journey, namely to the effect that there should be four rak'lit at midday, four in the late afternoon, three at sunset, with the prayers of the first two being recited aloud, four at night with just two being recited aloud, and two in the morning, both recited aloud." My own view is that 'A'isha may have meant that before the night journey the prayer consisted of two rak'lit, recited twice, separately, and then, when the five were made compulsory they were kept the same while in residence, and that permission was given that two rak'zt be prayed while travelling, as had been the case previously. Based on this explanation, there would be no controversy at all. But God knows best.
lessons: The forms of worship in Islam were revealed gradually, showing God's mercy and wisdom in guiding the community step by step.
Scholarly disagreement on religious details is a sign of intellectual engagement and sincerity, not weakness in the faith.
Prayer is a constant connection to God whether one is at home or traveling, and flexibility in its form reflects divine compassion for human circumstances.
practical_advice: When you feel confused by differing religious opinions, seek out trustworthy scholars and approach the question with patience rather than anxiety.
Maintain your prayer routine even during travel or difficulty, as the concession to shorten prayer shows that God never wants worship to be a burden.
Use moments of religious study and reflection to deepen your gratitude for the structure and mercy built into your faith practice.

title: The Splitting of the Moon as a Sign for the Quraysh
summary: The people of Mecca demanded a miracle from the Prophet, and God caused the moon to visibly split into two halves over the mountains of Mecca; yet the disbelievers dismissed it as sorcery and turned away.
story: Section: Concerning the splitting of the moon in the time of the Prophet (&US). God did provide the Messenger of God (SAAS) with a sign to prove the veracity of the guidance and religion of truth he brought, and that was at the time of his pointing (to the moon). God Almighty spoke the following in His noble book, "The hour has drawn near and the moon bas split asunder. And if they see a sign they turn away, saying, 'Just more trickery!' They call it deceit and follow their fancies, while all things are pre-determined" (surat al-Qamar; LIV, v.1-3). Muslims are agreed that this did occur in the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS); traditions with complete lines of transmission, through numerous paths, provide decisive proof for those who examine it and comprehend it. We will provide some of those easily available traditions, if God wills it and in Him is all faith and reliance. We detailed this in our Tafsir (Exegesrs), and there gave the various chains of authorities and differences in phraseology verbatim. Here we will refer to some aspects of these and will attribute them to well-known texts, with the power and strength from God. That is related from Anas h. Mxlik, Jubayr b. Mut'im, Hudhayfa, 'Abd All& b. 'Ahbas, 'Ahd Allah b. Wmar, and 'Abd Allah b. Mascad, God be pleased with them all. Regarding the hadith of Anas, Imxm A b a d stated, "'Abd al-Razzxq related to us that Ma'mar related to him, from Qatxda, from Anas b. Msiik, who said, 'The people of Mecca asked the Prophet (SAAS) for a sign. And so the moon over Mecca was split twice. And he spoke the words, "The hour has drawn near and the moon has split asunder"'" (surat al-Qamar; LIV, v.1). And Muslim related this, from Muhammad h. &fi' from 'Abd al-Razzxq. Both al-Bukhari and Muslim related this hadith on a path through Shayban. In his line of transmission al-Bukhai added Sacidh. Aba Wraha. Muslim added Shu'ba to his. AU three (sic) of them drew from Qatda, from Anas. The tradition states that the people of Mecca asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) to show them a sign. He showed them the moon in two parts, so that they could see Mt. Hira' hetween them both. This is the phraseology of al-Bukhw.
lessons: Even the most extraordinary divine signs cannot force belief into a heart that has chosen to remain closed; guidance ultimately comes from God, not from miracles alone.
The Prophet's patient continuation of his mission despite the rejection of undeniable signs teaches us to remain steadfast when our sincerity is questioned or dismissed.
What the world calls 'trickery' or 'impossible' may be God's direct response to our deepest needs; trust in His power even when others mock.
practical_advice: When your honest efforts are rejected or belittled by others, remind yourself that the Prophet himself faced the same response to divine miracles and continued with patience.
Do not tie your faith or sense of purpose to whether others validate your experience; seek confirmation from God and from sincere reflection.
In moments of doubt, revisit the multiple, well-attested accounts of God's signs throughout history to strengthen your certainty and reduce anxiety.

title: The Testimony of Jubayr ibn Mut'im Regarding the Moon's Splitting
summary: Jubayr ibn Mut'im narrated that the moon split during the time of the Prophet into two visible parts over separate mountains, and while some dismissed it as a trick, others acknowledged that no one could deceive the entire world.
story: Regarding the traditions quoted by Jubayr b. Mut'im, the Im%n Ahmad stated, "Muhammad h. Kathir related to us, that Sulayman b. Kathir related to him, from Husayn b. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mutcim, from his father, who said, 'The moon split in the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and became two parts; one was over this mountain, the other over that mountain. People said, "Muhammad has performed a trick on us." Then they said, "He may have tricked us, but he can't trick everyone."'" A b a d alone gives this hadith. Ibn J m r reported it similarly from a hadith of Mdpmmad b. Fudayl and others, from an account of Hu~ayn. Al-Bayhaqi related it through Ihrahim b. Tahman and Hushaym, both of them from Husayn b. 'Abd al-Rahmzn, from Jubayr b. Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mutrim, from his father and grandfather.
lessons: Even those who initially dismissed the Prophet's signs sometimes found themselves forced to acknowledge the limits of their own skepticism.
Truth has a way of persisting in the conscience even when it is verbally denied; the acknowledgment that 'he can't trick everyone' shows the seed of honest reflection.
Community witness and multiple testimonies are a mercy from God, making the truth accessible to those who seek it sincerely.
practical_advice: When you feel that your truth or sincerity is being dismissed by those around you, take comfort in knowing that God's reality cannot be hidden from all people forever.
If you find yourself skeptical of something, ask yourself honestly: could this really be a collective deception, or is there something here worth investigating further?
Share your positive experiences and spiritual moments with trusted others so that they too can serve as witnesses and mutual supports in faith.

title: Hudhayfa's Address on the Moon's Splitting and the Race to Paradise
summary: Hudhayfa ibn al-Yaman gave two Friday addresses confirming the splitting of the moon and urging his listeners to race toward paradise, explaining that the winner is the one who reaches it first.
story: As for Hudhayfa b. al-Yaman's tradition, Aba Nu'aym related in the DalZ)il (The Signs), on a line through 'Ap) b. al-Sa'ib from Aba 'Abd al-&an al-Sulami, as follows: "Hudhayfa b. al-Yam= made an address to us at al-MadPin. He gave thanks and praise to God and then said, 'The hour has drawn near and the moon has split asunder. Yes indeed, the time is near. And yes, the moon did split. The world really is close to partition. Today we have the race-track, and the race is tomorrow!' "The next Friday I went with my father to the mosque. Again he praised God and repeated what he had said, adding, 'And yes, indeed, the winner is he who comes first on Fridays.' "On our way back, I asked my father what he had meant when he said, 'the race is tomorrow!' He replied, 'For who first reaches paradise.'"
lessons: Life is a race whose finish line is paradise; urgency and purposeful action in worship and good deeds are the characteristics of those who win.
The reminder of signs already witnessed — like the splitting of the moon — should awaken us to the seriousness of the time we live in.
Fathers and teachers who explain spiritual matters to their children and students create ripples of understanding that outlast any single sermon.
practical_advice: Treat each week's Friday prayer as a fresh opportunity to reset your priorities and recommit to the race toward goodness and God's pleasure.
When you feel lost about the purpose of daily life, remind yourself that every sincere act of worship is a step forward in the most meaningful race there is.
Ask elders and scholars to explain the deeper meanings behind religious expressions and traditions; their wisdom can transform confusion into clarity.

title: Ibn Abbas on the Splitting of the Moon Before the Hijra
summary: Ibn Abbas narrated that the moon visibly split before the hijra as a sign for the people of Mecca, and God revealed verses of Surah Al-Qamar in response to those who called it trickery.
story: Regarding the tradition of Ibn 'Ahhas, al-Bukhari stated, "Yahya h. Bukayr related to us that Bakr related to him, from Jacfar, from 'Irak h. Mdik, from Wbayd Allah b.'Abd Allah h. Wtha, from Ibn CAbbas, who said, 'The moon did split during the time of the Prophet (SAAS).'" Al-Bukhari also, along with Muslim, related this from a hadith of Bakr, who , Jacfar. Regarding ". . . the time is near and the moon has split was Ibn N a ~ rfrom asunder. And if they see a sign they turn away, saying 'Just more trickery!'" He said, "That time is past. That was before the hijra. The moon did split, and people could see both parts of it."
lessons: Divine signs are given as mercy and guidance; their rejection by some does not diminish their reality or their power for those who reflect.
The Quran itself testifies to historical miracles, making scripture a living record of God's intervention in human affairs.
The period before the migration was one of the most difficult for the early Muslim community, yet God's signs sustained their faith and conviction.
practical_advice: When you feel that your faith lacks proof or grounding, study the well-attested historical signs and miracles that accompanied the Prophet's mission.
Do not be discouraged when people around you dismiss spiritual realities you have personally witnessed or experienced; remain firm and seek God's affirmation.
Reflect on the Quranic verses that address historical events, as they provide both historical evidence and timeless emotional comfort.

title: The Polytheist Leaders Demand the Moon Be Split
summary: The leading polytheists of Mecca, including Abu Jahl and al-Walid ibn al-Mughira, challenged the Prophet to split the moon as proof of his prophethood, promising belief; the Prophet asked God, and on a night of full moon the miracle occurred, witnessed by the Companions.
story: Al-'Awfi related it thus, from Ibn 'Ahbas, God bless him, this being one of his r n u r ~ a l traditions. The ha& Aba Nu'aym stated, "Sulayman b. Ahmad related to us, quoting Bakr b. Suhayl, quoting 'Ahd al-Ghani b. Sa'id, quoting Mas% b. 'Abd al-Rahmm, from Ibn Jurayj, from 'At%', from Ibn 'Abbas - also, it came from Muqatil, from d-Dahha, from Ibn 'Abbas - with reference to God's words, 'The hour has drawn near and the moon has been split asunder' as follows, 'The polytheists gathered around the Messenger of God (SAAS); they included al-Walid b. al-Mughira, Abu Jahl b. H i s h a , al-'AS b. Wa'il, al-'AS b. H i s h a , al-Aswad b. 'Abd Yaghath, al-Aswad b. al-Mutcalib b. Asad b. 'Abd alLcUzzz, Zam'a h. al-Aswad and al-Nadr b. al-Hzrith, along with many other such men. "'They said to the Prophet (SAAS), "If you are telling the truth, then split the moon for us into two parts, one half over Mt. Abn Quhays, the other over Mt. Qu'ayqu%'." The Prophet (SAAS) asked them, "Tf I did so, would you believe?" "Yes, we would," they replied. It was a night of full moon, and so he asked God the Almighty and Glorious to grant him what they requested. That evening the moon did have one half removed and placed over Mt. Aha Quhays, the other being above Mt. Qu%yquCan, while the ~ e s s e n ~ofe rGod (SAAS) called out, "0 Aba Sdama b. 'Abd al-Asad, and al-Arqam b. al-Arqam, be a witness (to this event)."'"
lessons: The Prophet did not shy away from the challenge of powerful opponents but turned to God with complete trust, and God answered his prayer in a dramatic and public way.
Those who set conditions for belief and then abandon those conditions when they are met reveal that their resistance was never truly about evidence.
God honors the sincerity of His Prophet by responding to his supplications even in the most extraordinary circumstances.
practical_advice: When you face pressure from those who challenge your beliefs or demand proof you cannot provide, turn to God in prayer and trust that He will support you in His own perfect way.
If someone in your life keeps moving the goalposts for accepting the truth, recognize that this may be a matter of the heart rather than the mind, and respond with patience rather than frustration.
Call trustworthy witnesses to important moments in your life, as the Prophet called his Companions to witness the miracle, so that truth may be confirmed and preserved.

title: Gabriel's Announcement and the Night of the Moon's Splitting
summary: Gabriel came to the Prophet and announced that the people of Mecca would witness a sign that night; on the 14th of the month the moon split into two halves visible over the mountains, yet the crowd dismissed it as the trick of a monk.
story: Moreover, Abu Nu'aym stated, "Sulayman b. Ahmad related to us, quoting d-Hasan b. al-'Abbas al-Razi, from al-Haytham b. al-'Urnan, as did Ismacil b. Ziyad, from Ibn Jurayj, from 'A@, from Ibn CAbbas, who said, 'The people of Mecca went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "Is there some sign by which we can know that you are the Messenger of God?" "'Gabriel then came down and said, "0 Muhammad, tell the people of Mecca that they will rejoice this night when they will see a sign and benefit thereby." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) informed them what Gabriel had said and they all went outside on the night of the splitting, that of the 14th, and the moon split into two halves, one above Mt. al-Safz, the other over Mt. al-Marwa. They looked, turned away and wiped their eyes, then looked once more, saying, "0 Muhammad, this is nothing more than the trickery of a monk!" "'Then God revealed His words, "The hour has drawn near and the moon has split asunder"'" (sarat al-Qamar; LIV, v.1-3).
lessons: God's response to sincere longing for a sign comes not through our own power but through divine initiative, as Gabriel came with a promise before the sign even appeared.
The habit of rationalizing away what the eyes clearly see — wiping their eyes and looking again, only to claim trickery — is a warning about the danger of a hardened heart.
God documents and answers every challenge made against His Prophet, and His Quran preserves these moments as eternal guidance for future generations.
practical_advice: When you earnestly seek a sign of God's presence in your life, approach that seeking with openness; sometimes the sign comes in a way you did not expect.
Be aware of the human tendency to explain away spiritual experiences that challenge your existing worldview; practice sitting with wonder before rushing to skepticism.
Memorize or reflect regularly on short Quranic chapters like Surah Al-Qamar, which connect cosmic signs with personal accountability and spiritual urgency.

title: Jewish Rabbis Request a Sign and Witness the Moon's Splitting
summary: Jewish rabbis came to the Prophet requesting a sign to confirm his prophethood; God showed them the moon split into two parts visible over the mountains of Mecca for a sustained period, yet they dismissed it as magic.
story: Al-Dahhak related from Ibn 'Abbas as follows, "Rabbis came from the Jews to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, 'Show us a sign so we may believe in you.' "He asked his Lord, and He showed them the moon split into two parts, one over Mt. d-Safa, the other above Mt. al-Marwa. It remained so between early evening until night, with them looking on, and then it disappeared. They commented, 'This is just magic, a trick.'"
lessons: Sincere inquiry is different from demanding proof while already having decided to disbelieve; the rabbis' response shows that seeing is not always believing when the heart is closed.
God's signs are not given to compel belief but to illuminate the path for those who genuinely seek guidance.
Patience in the face of mockery and dismissal is a prophetic quality; the Prophet did not retaliate or despair when his miracle was called magic.
practical_advice: Examine your own moments of doubt honestly: are you genuinely seeking truth, or have you already decided what you want the answer to be?
When others dismiss your faith or its evidences as irrational or magical thinking, respond with calm confidence rather than defensiveness.
Reflect on the history of how truth-seekers from all backgrounds have responded differently to the same signs; let this motivate you to keep your own heart soft and open.

title: The Moon's Splitting Witnessed Globally and Recorded in India
summary: Beyond Arabia, the splitting of the moon was reportedly witnessed in other parts of the world, including regions of India where the event was said to have been used as a historical date marker and commemorated in a building constructed that night.
story: This has a fine chain of authorities; it states that the moon was eclipsed that night; perhaps its splitting occurred on the night of its eclipse and this is why what happened to it was hidden from most of the earth's inhabitants. However, this was in fact witnessed in many parts of the world. It is said that in some parts of India that became a point for dating, and that a building was constructed that night which was dated by the night when the moon split.
lessons: Divine miracles transcend borders and cultures; when God acts, its effects can reach far beyond the immediate audience, affirming the universal nature of the Prophet's message.
The recording of sacred events in architecture and historical chronicles reflects how profoundly spiritual moments shape entire civilizations.
What seems confined to one people or one place may in fact carry global significance; the Prophet was sent as a mercy to all of humanity, not just the Arabs.
practical_advice: Broaden your study of Islamic history beyond the Arabian Peninsula to discover how the Prophet's message and even its miraculous signs touched civilizations across the world.
When you feel that your faith tradition is small or unrecognized, remember that its signs and truths have resonated across cultures and continents throughout history.
Keep a personal record or journal of moments when you have felt God's presence or witnessed what seemed like divine guidance; these become anchors for your faith in harder times.

title: The Death of Khadija and Abu Talib: The Year of Grief
summary: The Prophet Muhammad lost both his beloved wife Khadija and his protective uncle Abu Talib in the same year, leaving him exposed to unprecedented persecution from the Quraysh.
story: Section: The death ofAba Tulib, uncle of the Messenger of God ( W S ) ,and thereafrer that ofKhadiju, daughter ofKhuwaylid, and the wife, may God be pleased with her, o f the Messenger of God (&!AS). Some say that she preceded him in death, but the reverse is commonly known. These were both sympathizers, he publicly, she privately. He remained a disbeliever while she was trusting and a believer; may God be pleased with her and please her. Ihn Ishaq stated, "Thereafter Khadija and Aba Talib both died, in one year. "After the death of Khadija troubles fell upon the Messenger of God (SAAS) in quick succession. She had been his trusted adviser in all his troubles and he would seek reassurance from her. The death of his uncle Aba T d i b meant he had lost a source of support and protection against his people. "This occurred three years before his emigration to Medina. "When Abii Tdih died, Quraysh harmed the Messenger of God (SAAS) to an extent they would never have dared during Abii Tdib's life; one fool of Quraysh even went so far as to throw dirt on his head. "Hisham h. 'Urwa related to me, from his father, as follows, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went into his house with the dirt still on his head. One of his daughters went to h i , washing him off and weeping, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) told her, "Don't weep, child. God is your father's protector," while he also commented, "Quraysh never treated me so badly before Abii Tdib died."'" Ihn Ishaq previously reported that they might toss something harmful into his cooking pot when it was set up for him. When they did that, he stated, quoting Wmar b. 'Abd Allah from Wrwa, he would lift out on a stick whatever they had put there and take it to his door, saying, "0 Banii 'Abd Manaf, what kind of protection is this?" Then he would throw it out into the street.
lessons: The Prophet Muhammad experienced profound personal loss — the death of his closest companion and his protector — yet continued his mission, showing that grief and purpose can coexist.

Khadija's role as a trusted adviser and source of reassurance reveals that healthy, supportive relationships are among the greatest gifts God grants us and that losing them is a legitimate source of grief.

Even when stripped of human protection and subjected to humiliation, the Prophet maintained dignity and reminded his daughter that God remains the ultimate protector — a lesson in surrendering to divine care when human support is gone.
practical_advice: When you lose people who were your emotional anchor, allow yourself to grieve fully — the Prophet's companions named this the Year of Grief precisely because grief is real and must be acknowledged.

If you are experiencing loss of multiple supports at once, focus on the one constant that cannot be taken: your relationship with God, which the Prophet modeled by continuing to call on His protection.

When others take advantage of your vulnerability during a time of grief, respond with dignity rather than retaliation — protect your peace by naming the injustice calmly, as the Prophet did at his door.

title: The Quraysh Leaders Visit the Dying Abu Talib to Negotiate with the Prophet
summary: As Abu Talib lay dying, the Quraysh leaders attempted to negotiate a religious compromise with the Prophet through him, but the Prophet's call to monotheism caused the leaders to leave in rejection.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When Abn Talib became sick and Quraysh learned of his serious state, they noted how Hamza and Wmar had accepted Islam and how the prestige of Muhammad had spread through the Quraysh tribes, so they decided to go to Abn T a b to get him to reconcile his nephew to them. Otherwise, they concluded, he might subvert their authority completely. "Al-'Ahbas b. 'Abd Allah h. Ma'bad related to me, from a member of his family, from Ibn 'Abbzs, who said, 'When they went to Abii Talib and spoke to him, the Quraysh leaders, including Wtba b. Rabi'a, Shayba h. Rabi'a, Abii Jahl b. Hish-, Umayya b. Khalaf and Abii Sufyau h. Harb, along with others, said, "0 Abn Tdib, you know what standing you have with us, and that your serious condition, of which yon are aware, greatly concerns us. Yon also know of the dispute between us and your nephew. Would you now call him and reach a compromise by which he will do us no harm nor we him; let him tolerate our religion and we will his." "'Abii Talib sent for him and when he came, he told him, "0Nephew, these leaders of your people have gathered to reach a compromise with you and yon with them." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "You all just need to say one word, uncle, and through it you can rule the Arabs and have the non-Arabs subject to you." "' "Oh yes, I swear by your life," answered Abii Tdib, "along with ten words more!" "'He told him, "Say, 'There is no god but God.' And repudiate whatever gods you worship besides Him!" "'Those present clapped their hands and commented, "'0 Muhammad, do you want to make all the gods into one? How strange." "'Then they said to one another, "This man is not going to give us any part of what we want. We should leave and continue in the religion of our fathers until God decides between us and him." They then dispersed. "'At that Abn Talib said, "I swear, nephew, I don't think you asked anything excessive." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) had high hopes of him now and said to him, "0 Uncle, if you were to say it I would intercede for you on Judgement Day." "'Recognizing the eagerness of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Abii T a i b replied, "0 Nephew, I swear that were it not for fear of the curses there would be on you and your relatives after me, and the fact that Quraysh would think that I had only said it out of fear of death, I would say it. I will not say it only to please you thereby." "'When death approached Abii THlib, al-'Ahb% saw that he was moving his lips and so he put his ear close to hear. Then he said, "0 Nephew, I swear my brother did say the word you"
lessons: The Prophet did not compromise the core truth of his message even when faced with immense pressure from powerful leaders at the most vulnerable moment — the deathbed of his protector.
Abu Talib's refusal to say the testimony of faith out of pride and concern for social reputation is a timeless reminder that the heart can recognize truth while the ego blocks submission to it.
The Prophet's persistent hope that his uncle would accept faith, even at the last moment, reflects deep compassion and the Islamic teaching that guidance is never fully abandoned until death arrives.
practical_advice: When you are pressured to compromise your values by powerful people, remember that clarity and calmness — not aggression — is the most effective response, as the Prophet simply stated the truth.

If someone you love is resistant to something you know would benefit them deeply, continue offering with kindness without forcing, and surrender the outcome to God rather than carrying the burden of their choice.
When negotiations fail and others walk away, do not interpret rejection as a sign that you were wrong — sometimes the message is sound and the audience is simply not ready to receive it.

title: Abu Talib's Deathbed: The Prophet's Final Plea for His Uncle's Faith
summary: As Abu Talib lay dying, the Prophet repeatedly urged him to say the declaration of faith, but Abu Jahl and others pressured him to remain on the religion of his ancestors. Abu Talib's last words were that he followed the faith of Abd al-Muttalib, and God revealed that the Prophet cannot guide those he loves.
story: of God (SAAS), Abii T a i b replied, "0 Nephew, I swear that were it not for fear of the curses there would be on you and your relatives after me, and the fact that Quraysh would think that I had only said it out of fear of death, I would say it. I will not say it only to please you thereby." When death approached Abii THlib, al-'Ahb% saw that he was moving his lips and so he put his ear close to hear. Then he said, "0 Nephew, I swear my brother did say the word you asked of him!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "I did not hear." And God Almighty revealed about that group of men, "Sad. By the Qur'an of fine repute! Those who disbelieve act in pride and arrogance" (sarat Sad; XXXVIII, v. I). Imam Ahmad, al-Nasa'i and Ibn Jarir gave much the same chain, through Abii Usama from d-A'mash. Al-Tirmidhi related it and stated it to be a good tradition. The wording of the tradition on the line given by al-Bayhaqi through al-Thawri, from al-A'mash, from Yahya b. 'Amara, from Sacid b. Jubayr, quotes Ibn 'Abb% as saying, "Abii T d i b fell sick and men of Quraysh and the Prophet (SAAS) arrived at his home and there was a seat for one person at the head of Abii Talib's bed. Abii Jahl arose to prevent him sitting there, all the while protesting to Abii Talib about Muhammad. He asked, 'Nephew, what is it you want from your people?' He replied, 'Uncle, all I want from them is a statement through which the Arabs will submit to them and which will bring them tribute from the non-Arabs. Just one statement.' 'And what is that?' 'There is no god but God,' he replied. They commented, 'What, make all the gods into one? That's ridiculous!' He went on, 'And God revealed about them, "Sad. By the Qur'an of fine repute! ..." and so on to the words, "... nothing hut a forgery!"' (snrat Sad; XXXVIII, v.7)." Moreover Ibn IshZq's account is contradicted by one more authoritative, namely that of al-Bukhari, who said, "Mahmiid related to us, that 'Abd al-RazzBq related to them that Ma'mar informed them, from al-Zuhri, from Ibn al-Musayyab, from his father, God bless him, that Abn Talib was close to death when the Prophet (SAAS), along vith Abii Jahl, went in to see him. He (the Prophet) said, 'Uncle, say, "There is no god but God!" One statement; (and) I will plead with God thereby on your behalf.' Abti Jahl and 'Abd Allah b. Abii Umayya then said, '0 Ahii Talih, are you leaving the faith of 'Abd al-Muttalib?' And they both continued talking to him until he spoke his last words to them, namely, '... in the faith of 'Abd al-Munalib.' The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'I will seek forgiveness for you for as long as I am not restrained from doing so.' And so there was revealed, 'It is not appropriate for the Prophet and for those who believe to seek forgiveness for the polytheists, even though near relatives, after it has become plain to them that they would inhabit hell' (siirat al-Tawba (or al-Barz'a); IX, v.113). And there was also revealed, 'You cannot give guidance to those you love'" (swat al-Qa~a?;XXVIII, v.56). Muslim related this account from Ishaq h. Ibrahim and 'Ahd Allah, from 'Abd al-Razaq. They also drew it from a hadith of al-Zuhri, from Sa'id h. al-Musayyab, from his father in similar words. In it he states, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) continued to suggest this to him while the other two repeated their words until he spoke his final words, '... in the faith of 'Ahd al-Munalib.' And he refused to utter the words 'There is no god but God.' The Prophet (SAAS) said, 'I will seek forgiveness for you.' And so God sent down, thereafter that is, the words, 'It is not up to the Prophet and those who believe to seek forgiveness for the polytheists, even though near relatives.'" Concerning Abn Tdib, it was revealed, "You cannot give guidance to those you love." Imim Ahmad, Muslim, al-Timidhi and al-Nas9i related this from a hadith of Yazid h. Kaysa, from Aha H a i m , from Aha Hurayra, who said, "When death was near for Abii Tdih, the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to him and said, 'Uncle, say "There is no god hut God" and I will testify for you about it on Judgement Day.' He replied, 'If it were not for Quraysh insulting me and saying that it was only fear of death that made me say it, I would do so, and please you. I would not, however, say it only to please you.' And so God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed, 'You cannot give guidance to those you love.'" 'Abd Allah b. CAbbas, Ibn 'Umar, Muj&id, alSha'bi, and Qatada all stated that the above verse was revealed about Abii Tdib when the Messenger of God (SAAS) suggested to him that he say, "There is no god but God" and he refused to do so. Instead, he said that he subscribed to the faith of the elders. The last thing he said was that, "He is following the religion of 'Abd al-Mut@lib."
lessons: Even the Prophet Muhammad, the most beloved of God, could not control the hearts of those he loved most — guidance is ultimately from God alone, and we must accept what is beyond our power.
Social pressure and fear of others' opinions can prevent a person from making life-changing decisions; this is a warning to always prioritize truth over the judgments of others.
Grief over the spiritual state of loved ones is a deeply human experience shared even by prophets; it is not a weakness to feel pain over those we cannot help.
practical_advice: When you have done everything in your power for someone you love and they still do not change, remind yourself that your responsibility is only to convey truth with sincerity — the outcome belongs to God.

If you feel paralyzed by what others will think of you when making an important decision, write down what you truly believe is right and ask yourself: will these people's opinions matter on the day that truly counts?
When grieving a loved one's choices, allow yourself to feel the sadness without spiraling into guilt — even the Prophet felt this grief, and God responded by reassuring him, not blaming him.

title: Abu Talib's Fate in the Afterlife: The Prophet's Intercession and Divine Justice
summary: The Prophet Muhammad was told that his uncle Abu Talib, despite dying as a polytheist, would receive the lightest punishment in hell due to the Prophet's intercession — placed in a shallow stream of fire that would cause his brain to boil, rather than in its deepest depths.
story: All this is affirmed by al-Bukhzri's narration from Musaddad, who related from Yahya, from Sufym, from 'Ahd al-Malik b. Wmayr, from 'Abd All* b. al-Hzrith who said, "AlL'AbbXs b. 'Ahd al-Muttalib related to us that he said, 'I asked the Messenger of God (SAAS), "What benefit were you to your uncle? He used to protect and defend you." He replied, "He is in a shallow stream of hell-fire; if it were not for me, he would be in its lowest depths."'" Muslim related this same account in his compendium by various paths, from 'Ahd al-Malik h. Wmayr. Both these scholars narrated it in both their works from a Eadith of d-Layth, as follows, "Ibn al-Hadd related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Khabbzb, from Aha Sacid, that he heard the Prophet (SAAS) say when someone mentioned his uncle, 'Perhaps my intercession may help him on Judgement Day, and he will he placed in a shallow stream of hell-fire that will reach up to his ankles and cause his brain to boil.'" This is the phraseology of al-Bukhari. In one account the words used are, "... cause umm dimaghihi", the centre of his brain, instead of "dimaghuhn", his brain, to boil. Muslim related, from Aha Bakr h. Aha Shayba, from 'Affzn, from Hammad b. Salama, from Thi%hit, from Aba Wthmzn, from Ihn 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "Abti Talib receives more lenient punishment than any other inhabitant of hell; he wears two shoes of fire that cause his brain to boil." In the Maghzzi, the history of the conquests, by Ymus b. Bukayr, the statement given is "... and from them (his shoes) his brain will boil so that it streams down over his feet." Al-Suhayli makes mention of this. The ha& Abn Bakr al-BazzW stated in his compendium, "'Amr - he being the son of Isma'il h. Mujalid - related to us, saying that his father related to them, from Mujdid, from al-Sha'bi, from Jabir, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was asked, "Have you been of benefit to Aha Talib?" He replied, "I drew him forth from hell-fire into a shallow stream of it."'" Al-Bazzsr alone gives this tradition.
lessons: Divine justice is absolute and does not exempt anyone from accountability, yet within that justice there is room for mercy and degrees — no good deed, including defending truth, goes unrewarded.
The Prophet's love for his uncle did not cease even after death; he continued to seek ways to intercede, teaching us that love and loyalty are beautiful qualities even when we cannot change outcomes.

This account reminds us that proximity to righteousness and service to a noble cause carries weight, even if a person does not fully embrace faith — every act of goodness has some value in God's sight.
practical_advice: When you feel that someone you cared for deeply did not 'deserve' what happened to them, trust that God's judgment is the most precise and most merciful — He accounts for every circumstance no human eye can fully see.

If you feel guilt about a loved one who passed without the faith you wished for them, take comfort that your prayers and good relationship with God can have spiritual weight — keep making du'a and leave the ultimate judgment to God.

Reflect on how protecting and supporting people of goodness is itself an act of tremendous value — use this as motivation to surround yourself with and actively support those who are working toward good in the world.

title: Ali Informs the Prophet of Abu Talib's Death and Is Commanded to Bury Him
summary: When Abu Talib died as a polytheist, Ali went to the Prophet to inform him and was told to go and bury his father despite him having died outside of Islam, and upon returning the Prophet made prayers for Ali that gave him immense comfort.
story: Aba D 2 a d at-Tayzlisi stated, "Shu'ba related to us, that Aha Ishaq said, 'I heard Najiyya b. Ka'b say, "I heard 'Ali say, 'When my father died, I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him, "Your uncle has died." He replied, "Go and bury him." I said, "He died a polytheist." So he replied, "Go and bury him and do not cause (a scene), then return to me." I did so, returned to him and he told me to wash.'"'" Al-Nasa)i related this from Muhammad h. al-Muthanna, from Ghundar, from Shu'ba. Aha Da'ad and al-Nasz'i related it from a hadith of Sufyan, from Ahii Ishaq, from Najiyya, from 'Ali who said, "When Aba Talib died, I said, '0 Messenger of God, that misguided old man your uncle has died. Who will bury him?' He replied, 'Go and bury your father. Do nothing to cause (a scene), then return to me.' When I returned to him he told me to wash, and I did so. He then said some prayers for me that gave me more pleasure than would have anything else on earth." The hzjx al-Bayhaqi said, "Aha Sacd al-Malini informed us, Ahn Ahmad h. 'Adi related to us, quoting Muhammad b. Haran b. Humayd, quoting Muhammad h. 'Abd al-'Aziz h. Abii Razma, quoting d-Fadl, from Ibrahim h. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Ibn Jurayj, from 'At%', from Ibn 'Abbs, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) returned after attending the funeral for Ahn Talib and said, "May your family ties be secure, and you be well rewarded, uncle."'" He (al-Bayhaqi) went on to say that it was related from the Prophet (SAAS), though with an incomplete chain, with the addition "... and he did not stand at his grave."
lessons: Even in grief and religious uncertainty, the Prophet gave clear, compassionate guidance — fulfilling family duties like burial is an obligation of mercy regardless of theological differences.
The Prophet's prayers for Ali after a difficult task show that spiritual support and recognition from someone we respect can bring more comfort than worldly consolations.
Handling the death of a loved one who did not share your faith requires both personal grief and practical responsibility — Islam provides a humane framework for navigating this pain.
practical_advice: When you are unsure how to handle a painful duty involving a family member whose beliefs differ from yours, focus on what you can control — fulfilling your obligations with dignity and without drama, and then returning to your source of spiritual strength.
After completing a difficult emotional task, seek spiritual renewal — whether through prayer, ablution, time with a mentor, or quiet reflection — as Ali did when he returned to the Prophet.
If you are grieving someone whose faith or choices troubled you, allow yourself to honor the relationship and the loss without needing to resolve every theological question in that moment of grief.

title: Abu Talib's Death and the Prophet's Final Words to His Uncle
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) attended the funeral of his uncle Abu Talib, who had protected and supported him throughout his mission, and offered words of farewell despite Abu Talib's dying without embracing Islam.
story: Muhammad b. Haran b. Humayd, quoting Muhammad h. 'Abd al-'Aziz h. Abii Razma, quoting d-Fadl, from Ibrahim h. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Ibn Jurayj, from 'At%', from Ibn 'Abbs, who said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) returned after attending the funeral for Ahn Talib and said, "May your family ties be secure, and you be well rewarded, uncle."' He (al-Bayhaqi) went on to say that it was related from the Prophet (SAAS), though with an incomplete chain, with the addition '... and he did not stand at his grave.'
lessons: Deep gratitude and love for those who support us does not require agreement on all matters of belief or conviction.
The Prophet's gracious farewell to Abu Talib reminds us that we honor the good in people even when we grieve what they missed.
There is a profound difference between knowing truth intellectually and allowing it to transform the heart — a reminder to examine our own sincerity.
practical_advice: When you lose someone who supported you but never shared your faith or values fully, allow yourself to grieve the complexity of that relationship without guilt.
Express gratitude to those who protect and support you while they are still alive, and honor their memory with kind words after they are gone.
Reflect regularly on whether your own beliefs have moved from intellectual acknowledgment to sincere conviction, and ask God to strengthen your heart.

title: Abu Talib's Knowledge Without Faith — A Theological Reflection
summary: Islamic scholars reflect on the painful reality that Abu Talib knew the Prophet was truthful but his heart did not submit to faith, illustrating the distinction between intellectual knowledge and heartfelt belief.
story: We have already given accounts of the support and protection given by Abii Titlib to the Messenger of God (SAAS), how he defended him and his followers. We also quoted from the verses he spoke in their praise and of the love and sympathy for them these verses express, as well as the criticism and blame they contain for those who opposed and ridiculed them. These verses exemplify the eloquence, skill and fluency of the Hashim and Mutfalibi clans, an ability to use the language that could not be matched, rivalled or excelled by any Arab. In all of this he knew that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was truthful, innocent and right-guided, but his heart, nevertheless, did not believe. There is a difference between the heart knowing and believing. We have affirmed this fact in our exegesis of the Book of Faith in the rahih of al-Bukhzri. Evidence for this is given in the words of the Almighty, "Those to whom we have brought the Book know it as they do their sons; (however) a group of them conceal the truth, while knowing it" (surat al-Baqara; 11, v.146). Regarding Pbaroah's people, the Almighty said, ". .. and they denied them, though their souls were convinced ..." (surat al-Naml; XXVII, v.14). And Moses said to the Pharoah, "You know that it is only the Lord of the heavens and the earth who sent clear proofs, and I believe you, Pharoah, to be damned!" (siirat al-Isr~'; XVII, v.102). Some of the salafi or early authorities consider the Almighty's words ". .. they defend him yet reject him" (mrat al-Ancam; VI, v.26) to relate to Aba Talib, since he used to defend the Messenger of God (SAAS) from harm even though he rejected the guidance and true religion he brought. (This opinion) is related as from Ibn 'Ahbas, al-@sim b. Mukhaymira, Habib h. Abii Thabit, 'A@' h. Dinzr, Muhammad b. Ka'b and others and it is open to question. But God alone knows best. Preferable, though God knows best, is the other account from Ibn 'Abbas to the effect that the verse refers to those who prevented people from believing in Muhammad (SAAS). Mujahid, Qatada, al-Dahhak and others held this view. It is also the choice of Ibn Jarir; though God knows best. Its implication is that this verse emphasizes the blame for the polytheists for preventing others from following him while themselves not benefitting from him either. The Almighty therefore stated, "There are from among them those who listen to you over whose hearts we have placed covers that prevent them understanding, and holes in their ears; even if they see all signs they fail to believe in them. Even when they come to you to argue with you, those who disbelieve say, 'These are just legends of the ancients.' And they forbid others from (believing in it), and themselves keep away from it. It is only themselves they destroy, yet they do not sense this" (siirat al-Ancam; VI, v.25). The phrase here: ". .. and they . .." indicates reference to a group. As for "they" as referred to in the subsequent comment, ". .. it is only themselves they destroy, yet they do not sense this", this gives evidence of the totality of the blame. Such could not apply to Abii Talib. On the contrary, be restrained people from harming the Messmg~rorGod (SAAS), and his followers, using all action, speech, moral support and money available to him. Nevertheless, God did not decree faith for him, in all His mighty wisdom, nor did He furnish him with the clear, irrefutable and decisive proofs whereby (one) is compelled to believe and submit. And were it not for God's having forbidden us seeking forgiveness for disbelievers, we would certainly seek such forgiveness and mercy for him.
lessons: Knowing the truth and submitting to it in the heart are two different things — true faith requires surrender, not just intellectual recognition.
Even someone who does enormous good and protects the righteous may still miss the most essential step of inward belief, reminding us never to be complacent about our own relationship with God.
God's wisdom is beyond our understanding; we cannot always know why some hearts open to guidance and others do not, and this calls us to humility and prayer.
practical_advice: Examine your own beliefs honestly — ask yourself whether what you claim to know has truly settled in your heart and changed your actions.
Pray sincerely for God to open your heart and keep it firm in faith, recognizing that guidance is ultimately a gift from God.
Show compassion toward those who have not yet found faith, while continuing to live and model your own convictions clearly.

title: The Death of Khadijah and Her Virtues
summary: Khadijah, the beloved first wife of the Prophet and first believer in Islam, passed away in Makkah before the migration, and her death was a profound loss for the Prophet who continued to honor her memory throughout his life.
story: DIVISION On the death of Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid and reference to some of her qualities and virtues, may God bless and please her and make the gardens of paradise her place of dwelling and repose. And He has indeed done that in a reference made by him who is truthful and believed, in which he announced her being in a home in paradise made of pearl shell where there is neither discord nor distress. Ya'qah h. Sufyan said, "Aba Salih related to us, from al-Layth, who said that Wqayl related to him from Ibn Shihzh, as follows, 'Urwa h. al-Zuhayr said, "Khadija had died before the prayer was made obligatory."'" This was related also in another path from al-Zuhri which had him say, "Khadija died at Mecca before the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS) for Medina, and before the prayer was made obligatory." Muhammad h. Ishaq stated, "Khadija and Aba Talih died in the same year." Al-Bayhaqi stated, "I was informed that Khadija died three days after the death of Aba Tzlib. It was 'Abd Allah b. Mandah who mentioned this in his hook al-Ma'rifa (Knowledge), as did our sheikh Aba 'Abd All&, the ha&. Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Al-Wzqidi claimed that Khadija and Aba Talib both died in the same year they came out of the enclave, that is, three years before the emigration, and also that Khadija died 35 days before the death of Aba Talih." My comment is that they mean before the five daily prayers were made ohligatory, on the 'night journey'. It would have been more appropriate for us to make reference to the deaths of Aha Tdih and Khadija before the account of the 'night journey', as al-Bayhaqi and others did. We have postponed it till after the 'night journey' for a reason that you will see shortly. Discussion of this will effectively harmonize and order this section, as you will find, if God so wills it.
lessons: The loss of those closest to us who supported our journey of faith is among the most painful trials, and even the Prophet experienced such profound grief.
Khadijah's death so close in time to Abu Talib's illustrates that hardships can cluster together, yet God never abandons those who trust Him.
Honoring the memory of those we have lost is a form of gratitude and love that keeps their legacy alive in our hearts and communities.
practical_advice: When grief comes in waves or multiple losses occur together, allow yourself to mourn without rushing the process — even the Prophet experienced what is called the Year of Sorrow.
Remember those who supported you during difficult seasons of your life, and make du'a for them as an act of love and gratitude.
Take comfort in the Islamic understanding that righteous souls are honored by God even after death, and that your prayers and good deeds can be a gift to those who have passed.

title: Gabriel Announces Khadijah's House in Paradise
summary: The Angel Gabriel came to the Prophet with a personal message of divine honor for Khadijah — that God and Gabriel themselves send her greetings and that she has been promised a home of pearl shell in paradise with no discord or distress.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "Qutayha related to us, quoting Muhammad h. Fudayl h. Ghazwan, from Wmzra, from Aba Znr'a, from Aba Hurayra, who said, 'Gabriel came to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and said, "0 Messenger of God, this is Khadija who has brought a vessel of shortening - or food and drink. When she comes to you greet her from her Lord and from me and give her news of a house in paradise made of pearl shell and where there is neither discord nor distress."'" Muslim related this from a hadith transmitted from Muhammad h. Fudayl. Al-Bukhwi stated, "Musaddad related to us, from Yahya, from Ismacil, who said, 'I asked 'Ahd All& b. Aba Awfa, "Did the Messenger of God (SAAS) give glad tidings to Khadija?" He replied, 'LYes,of a house of pearl shell devoid of discord or distress."'" Al-Bukhxi also related it, as did Muslim, in lines from Isma'il b. Abn Khdid. Al-Suhayli stated, "He only announced good news of her having a house made of qasab, pearl shell, in paradise, because she had won the qasab, the first prize, (in the race) to faith. There was to be 'no discord or distress' there because she never raised her voice to the Prophet (SAAS), never once caused h i trouble or was discordant with him, and never harmed him."
lessons: A life of sincere service, gentleness, and support for those on the path of God carries a reward beyond what we can imagine — as Khadijah's prize of paradise illustrates.
Being the first to believe in and support a cause when it is most difficult carries special honor in God's sight.
Peace in one's home and relationships, cultivated through patience and kindness, is itself a reflection of the peace God grants in the hereafter.
practical_advice: Strive to be a source of peace and comfort to those around you rather than discord — this gentleness is both a spiritual virtue and a healing practice.
When you feel that your contributions go unnoticed, remember that God sees every act of support, sacrifice, and love, and none of it is wasted.
Reflect on the people in your life who have been like Khadijah to you — steadfast, believing in you before others did — and express appreciation for them today.

title: Aisha's Jealousy of Khadijah and the Prophet's Loyalty to Her Memory
summary: Aisha describes her intense jealousy of Khadijah, whom the Prophet constantly praised and honored even after her death, revealing the depth of his love and loyalty to his first wife.
story: Both scholars gave it in the ~ a b i hcollections, from a hadith of Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father that 'A'isha, God bless her, said, "The only one of the wives of the Prophet (SAAS) of whom I was jealous was Khadija - who died before he married me - and this was because of how I would hear him mention her. "And God ordered him to announce to her the tidings of a house of pearl shell for her in paradise; and if he slaughtered a sheep he would present to her friends as much of it as they wanted." These are the words of al-Bukhzri. In another version from 'A'isha, she said, "I was never so jealous of any woman as I was of Khadija; this was due to the frequency with which the Messenger of God (SAAS) would mention her. He married me three years after (her death). And his Lord, or Gabriel, ordered him to announce the tidings of her having a house of pearl shell in paradise." In another of his variant wordings, she said, "I was never so jealous of any of the wives of the Prophet (SAAS) as I was of Khadija. I did not see her, but he talked often of her. He sometimes slaughtered a sheep then would cut off its limbs for her and send them off to Khadija's women friends. I would say, 'Why, it's as if Khadija were the only woman in the world!' and he would reply, 'She was what she certainly was, and I had a son by her.'"
lessons: Jealousy is a natural human emotion that even the most beloved companions of the Prophet experienced — feeling it does not make us bad people, but how we handle it matters.
True loyalty to those who supported us in difficult times is a noble quality, and the Prophet's consistent honoring of Khadijah models faithfulness and gratitude.
Acknowledging past relationships and their significance does not diminish present ones — it reflects integrity and depth of character.
practical_advice: When feelings of jealousy arise, try to identify what underlying need or fear is driving them, and address that root cause with honesty and self-compassion.
If you find yourself comparing your value to someone else's legacy, remind yourself that your worth is not diminished by another person's excellence.
Practice gratitude for the people who are present in your life now, rather than measuring yourself against an idealized version of those who came before.

title: Hala's Visit and the Prophet's Tender Memory of Khadijah
summary: When Khadijah's sister Hala knocked at the door, the Prophet was so moved that he mistook her voice for Khadijah's, causing Aisha a pang of jealousy as she witnessed his enduring love for his deceased wife.
story: Then al-Bukhiri stated, "Isma'il b. Khalil related to us, 'Ali b. Mushir informed us, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, that 'A'isha said, 'Hala, daughter of Khuwaylid, Khadija's sister, asked to come in to see the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he thought of it as Khadija's asking for entry; he was confused, then said, "0 God, it's Hala! "'" 'A'isha went on, "And SO I was jealous and commented, 'Why think back on some red-mouthed old woman of Quraysh who died long ago and whom God has replaced for you with someone better!'"
lessons: Grief does not follow a timetable — deep love for someone we have lost can be triggered unexpectedly by a familiar voice, a scent, or a resemblance.
The Prophet's momentary confusion at Hala's voice reveals that even the greatest of people carry the weight of loss and hold their loved ones in their hearts.
Aisha's jealous remark, though humanly understandable, shows us how grief in one person can sometimes cause unintentional pain to another — a reminder to be gentle in moments of vulnerability.
practical_advice: If grief catches you off guard — a song, a voice, a smell bringing someone lost back to mind — allow yourself that moment without judgment rather than pushing it away.
When someone you love seems emotionally connected to a person from their past, try to respond with curiosity rather than competition, recognizing that love is not a finite resource.
Recognize that human emotions like jealousy are normal, but practicing pause before reacting can prevent words spoken in a painful moment from causing harm.

title: The Prophet's Reaction to Aisha's Critical Words About Khadijah
summary: When Aisha spoke disparagingly of Khadijah in a moment of jealousy, the Prophet's face changed in a way that Aisha associated only with the arrival of revelation or a serious divine matter, revealing how deeply he felt the insult to his late wife.
story: However, Imam Ahmad said, "Mu'ammil Abn 'Abd al-Ra?lman related to us, quoting Hammid b. Salama, from 'Abd al-Malik - he being Ibn Wmayr - from Miisa b. Talha, from 'A'isha, who said, 'One day the Messenger of God (SAAS) referred to Khadija and began speaking excessive praise for her. I was overcome by that jealousy that affects women and I said, "0 Messenger of God, God has awarded you with better than a red-mouthed old Quraysh woman!"'" His account proceeds, "The face of the Messenger of God (SAAS) underwent such a change as I only ever saw when revelation happened or when he was imagining something and not knowing whether the outcome would be mercy or punishment." He related it similarly on a path from Bahz b. Asad and Wthmm b. Muslim, both of these giving as authorities for it Hammad b. Salama, back to 'Abd al-Malik b. Wmayr. After the words, "red-mouthed" this last account added the phrase "who died ages ago". And then she said, "His face then flushed in such a way as I only ever saw when revelation came or he was imagining something that might mean either mercy or punishment." Ahmad alone gave this version. Its chain of authorities is excellent.
lessons: Words spoken in jealousy or pain can wound even those with the greatest forbearance — the Prophet's visible distress at Aisha's words is a reminder that careless speech carries real weight.
Honoring the memory of those who sacrificed for us is a matter of integrity, and defending that honor is not weakness but loyalty.
Aisha's own account of what happened reflects admirable honesty and self-awareness — acknowledging our moments of failure is itself a form of moral courage.
practical_advice: Before speaking from a place of jealousy or frustration, pause and ask yourself whether your words honor the dignity of everyone involved, including those who are no longer present.
If you have said something hurtful to someone you love, do not let pride prevent you from acknowledging it and making it right.
Reflect on the people who sacrificed for you in the past and actively speak well of them — this practice of gratitude cultivates contentment and reduces the grip of comparison.

title: The Prophet's Praise of Khadija and Aisha's Jealousy
summary: Aisha expressed jealousy over the Prophet's frequent praise of Khadija, and the Prophet responded by describing Khadija's unique virtues and loyalty during the early days of his mission.
story: Abad also stated, and quoted Ibn Ishaq as having said, 'Mujdid related to us, from al-Sha'bi, from Masruq, that Aisha said, "When the Prophet (SAAS) mentioned Khadija he would speak of her with the finest of praise. One day I got jealous and commented, "How often you refer to that old, red-mouthed woman; God has given you better than her in exchange!" "He replied, "God has not given me better than her in exchange. She believed in me when the people disbelieved, and trusted me when they distrusted. She shared her wealth with me when others denied me. And God endowed a son to me from her, yet He withheld from me having sons with other women.""'
lessons: Loyalty and support during times of hardship carries a profound and lasting weight that cannot be replaced by later comforts.
Gratitude for those who stood by us in our darkest moments is a mark of noble character and deep integrity.
Jealousy, though a natural human emotion, can cloud our recognition of the sacrifices others have made on our behalf.
practical_advice: When you feel underappreciated or overshadowed by someone from the past, reflect on what that person actually sacrificed rather than comparing circumstances superficially.
Practice expressing gratitude for those who believed in you before you had anything to offer, as remembering their faith can renew your own strength.
If jealousy arises in a relationship, use it as an opportunity for honest conversation and to deepen your understanding of shared history rather than letting it fester.

title: Scholarly Debate on the Virtues of Khadija and Aisha
summary: Islamic scholars across generations debated the relative virtues of Khadija and Aisha, with each side presenting compelling evidence from hadith and the Prophet's own words.
story: This is an issue subject to much debate among scholars old and new. On one side of it there are those versions to which members of the Shi'a and others restrict themselves, considering no woman equal to Khadija because of the greetings made to her by the Lord and because the only sons of the Prophet (SAAS), except for Ibrahim, were born to her. Also they refer to the fact that he married no other woman until she died, and out of his respect for her acceptance of Islam, and for her having been of those who trusted. She has the status of having believed at the beginning of the mission and of devoting herself and her wealth to the Messenger of God (SAAS). There are also ramnt, orthodox, scholars who take extreme positions and attribute to each of them special virtues, as is well-known. However, the strength of their orthodoxy leads them to prefer Aisha for having been the daughter of al-Siddiq, Abu Bakr, "the Trusting", and to her having been more knowledgeable than Khadija. For in no nation was there ever any woman the like of Aisha in her ability to memorize, in her knowledge, eloquence or intelligence. Moreover, the Messenger of God (SAAS) did not love any of his wives as much as he did her. And affirmation of her innocence came down from above the seven heavens. And she related after he had died a great deal of excellent and highly valued knowledge of him, to such an extent that many people make reference to the well-known hadith "Take one half of your religion from al-Humayra'." The truth is that both of them have virtues so evident as to amaze and dazzle anyone who might have witnessed them. It is therefore best to defer this matter to God almighty. One who possesses irrefutable proof, or is inclined to one new in this regard, ought to speak on the basis of such knowledge. Anyone who is hesitant about any such matter will find that the best and the straightest path is for him to say, "God knows best."
lessons: When faced with complex questions that scholars have debated for centuries, humility and saying 'God knows best' is itself a form of wisdom.
Recognizing virtue in multiple people simultaneously is a sign of spiritual maturity rather than weakness or indecision.
Different people contribute different gifts at different times, and all of those gifts can be honored without diminishing one another.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in an unresolvable debate or comparison, practice sitting with uncertainty rather than forcing a conclusion that the evidence does not support.
Study the lives of those who came before you not to rank them but to draw lessons from each of their unique contributions and sacrifices.
When others around you argue passionately about matters of preference or priority, model the practice of acknowledging multiple truths and deferring judgment gracefully.

title: The Hadith on the Greatest Women in History
summary: The Prophet Muhammad described the greatest women in history, naming Mary daughter of Imran, Asiyya wife of Pharaoh, and Khadija daughter of Khuwaylid, while also describing Aisha's virtue as surpassing other women like al-tharid surpasses other foods.
story: Imam Ahmad, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related, through Hisham b. Urwa, from his father, from Abd Allah b. Jafar, from Ali b. Abi Talib, God bless him, who stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The best of their women was Mary, daughter of Imran, and the best of their women was Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid.'" By this he meant the best women of their eras. Shu'ba related, from Muawiya b. Qurra, that his father Qurra b. Iyyas, God be pleased with him, stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'There have been many perfect men, but only three perfect women. These were Mary, daughter of Imran, Asiyya, the wife of Pharaoh, and Khadija, daughter of Khuwaylid. The greater virtue of Aisha over other women is like that of al-tharid over other foods.'" Scholars agree that the quality shared by these three women, Asiyya, Mary and Khadija is that each of them vouched for a prophet sent by God, gave them the best of companionship, and believed when they were given their mission. Mary gave the most complete and full support and credence to her son when he received his mission. Khadija wished to have the Messenger of God (SAAS) marry her and gave her wealth for that, as we have stated previously. She also had full faith in him when revelation came down to him from God, Almighty and Glorious is He.
lessons: True greatness is often defined not by power or status but by the courage to believe in and support God's messengers during their most vulnerable moments.
Faith that is given freely when it is most needed, rather than when it is most convenient, is the highest form of loyalty.
Excellence of character and contribution transcends time and continues to be recognized and honored long after a person has passed.
practical_advice: Reflect on the women in your own life who have shown quiet, steadfast faith and support, and find ways to honor and acknowledge their contributions.
When you are in a position to support someone who is doing meaningful work, give that support generously and early, as Khadija did, rather than waiting until success is assured.
Use the examples of great historical figures as personal benchmarks for your own character development, asking yourself what quality of theirs you can cultivate this week.

title: The Prophet's Dream Vision of Aisha Before Marriage
summary: The Prophet Muhammad received a vision of Aisha twice in his sleep, presented by an angel, which he interpreted as a divine sign indicating she would be his wife.
story: Al-Bukhari stated in his chapter on his marriage to Aisha as follows, "Mu'alla b. Asad related to us, quoting Wuhayb, from Hisham b. Urwa, from his father, from Aisha, that the Prophet (SAAS) told her, 'You have twice been shown to me in my sleep dressed in a silk cloth. And he (Gabriel) says, "This is your wife." And when I uncover her, it is you! And so I say that if this be God's will, then may He bring it about!'" The text of al-Bukhari reads, "Chapter on Marriage to Virgins. Ibn Abi Mulayka stated, 'Ibn Abbas said to Aisha, "The Prophet (SAAS) married no virgin other than yourself."'" Ubayd b. Ismail related to us, that it was related to him by Abu Usama, from Hisham b. Urwa, from his father, that Aisha said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, "You were shown to me in my sleep being brought in a cloth of silk by an angel who said to me, 'This is your wife.'
