I withdrew the cloth from your face and it was you! So I said, 'If this be God's will, then may He bring it about!'"' In one account the wording is, "You were shown to me three nights in my sleep." According to al-Tirmidhi Gabriel brought him her image in a piece of green silk and said, "This is your wife in this world and the next."
lessons: Divine guidance often comes in subtle and unexpected forms, and recognizing it requires a heart that is attentive and open.
Submitting important life decisions to God's will, rather than forcing outcomes through personal desire, is a path to peace and right action.
The spiritual dimension of relationships, including their beginning in divine arrangement, gives them a depth and sanctity beyond mere human preference.
practical_advice: When facing a significant life decision such as marriage or a major commitment, take time in prayer and reflection to seek clarity rather than acting solely on emotion or social pressure.
Practice the habit of saying 'if this be God's will' when hoping for something, as a way of aligning your desires with trust in a larger plan.
If you receive a meaningful dream or intuition about your path, write it down and sit with it patiently rather than dismissing it or acting impulsively upon it.

title: The Prophet Seeks Aisha's Hand and Abu Bakr's Response
summary: The Prophet asked Abu Bakr for his daughter Aisha's hand in marriage, and when Abu Bakr expressed hesitation by calling himself the Prophet's brother, the Prophet clarified the permissibility of the union.
story: Al-Bukhari's text reads, "Chapter on Marriage between the Young and the Old. Abd Allah b. Yusuf related to us, as did al-Layth, from Yazid, from Ira, from Urwa, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked Abu Bakr for Aisha's hand in marriage. Abu Bakr replied, 'But I'm your brother!' (the Prophet (SAAS)) responded, 'You are my brother in God's religion and His Book, and she is permitted to me.'" The text of this tradition clearly suggests it to be mursal, incomplete, even though al-Bukhari and the authorities consider it uninterrupted. This is because it is a hadith of Urwa from Aisha, God bless her. This is a tradition narrated by al-Bukhari alone, God bless him. Yunus b. Bukayr stated, from Hisham b. Urwa, from his father who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) married Aisha three years after (the death of) Khadija. At that time (of the contract) Aisha had been a girl of six. When he married her she was nine. The Messenger of God (SAAS) died when Aisha was a girl of eighteen."
lessons: Brotherhood and close bonds of faith are deeply meaningful, yet they do not override all other legitimate relationships and obligations.
Important decisions involving family and marriage deserve open, honest conversation rather than assumptions or silence.
Clarity in communication, especially around relationships and boundaries, prevents misunderstanding and builds trust.
practical_advice: When a close friendship or spiritual bond creates ambiguity about appropriate boundaries, address the question openly and directly rather than letting confusion linger.
Before making or accepting a major life commitment, ensure all relevant parties have had the opportunity to voice concerns and receive clear answers.
Recognize that deep bonds of brotherhood or sisterhood in faith are a gift, and that honoring them means communicating with both respect and honesty.

title: The Prophet's Marriage Contract with 'A'isha
summary: After Khadija's death, Khawla bint Hakim suggested 'A'isha, daughter of Abu Bakr, to the Prophet as a potential wife. Abu Bakr initially hesitated due to considering himself the Prophet's brother, but after clarification, agreed to the marriage contract when 'A'isha was six years old.
story: When Khadija died, Khawla, daughter of Hakim, the wife of 'Uthman b. Ma'un, came and said, "O Messenger of God, wouldn't you like to get married?" He replied, "To whom?" "To either a virgin or to someone previously married, as you wish." "And who would the virgin be?" He asked. She replied, "That creation of God you enjoy above all others, 'A'isha, daughter of Abu Bakr!" "And who would the previously married woman be?" he asked. "Sawda, daughter of Zam'a," she answered. "She has expressed belief in you and has become your follower." "You may go," he told her, "and make mention of me to them." She entered Abu Bakr's house and said to his wife, "Umm Roman, what goodness and blessings God brings you!" "How do you mean?" she enquired. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has sent me to ask to become engaged to 'A'isha!" "See Abu Bakr when he comes in," she replied. Abu Bakr did come and Khawla said, "O Abu Bakr, what goodness and blessings God brings you!" "How so?" he asked. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has sent me to ask to become engaged to 'A'isha!" "And would she be proper for him? After all, she is his brother's daughter," he responded. So I returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him that and he replied, "Go back and tell him 'I am your brother, and you are mine, in Islam. Your daughter would be proper for me.'" So I went back and told him this, and he replied, "Wait" and left. Umm Roman told me, "Mut'im b. 'Adi has asked for her in marriage to his son; and, I swear, Abu Bakr never before broke an agreement he had made." Abu Bakr went in to see Mut'im b. 'Adi who had his wife, Umm al-Sabi, with him. She commented, "Well, son of Abu Quhafa, are you perhaps having our friend change his religion and join yours if he gets married into your family?" Abu Bakr asked Mut'im b. 'Adi, "Is this how you respond?" He replied, "It's she who said that." And so Abu Bakr left, God having relieved him of the promise he had made to Mut'im. He returned home and told Khawla, "Call for the Messenger of God (SAAS) to come to me." She did so and he agreed to her marriage to him, 'A'isha at that time being six years old.
lessons: Brotherhood in faith creates bonds that transcend blood ties, reminding us that spiritual community is a profound form of family.
God's plans often relieve us of obligations that stood in the way of greater blessings, as He did for Abu Bakr with the prior promise to Mut'im.
Approaching life's major decisions with consultation and clarity, as exemplified by Khawla's role as intermediary, reflects wisdom and care for all involved.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult commitment or obligation that conflicts with a new opportunity, seek honest communication with all parties rather than acting unilaterally.
If you feel torn between loyalty to a promise and a new path forward, trust that clarity and honesty will open the way, just as Abu Bakr's honest engagement resolved his prior commitment.
When considering major life decisions, involving trusted advisors or community members, as Khawla served in this account, can provide wisdom and ease anxiety.

title: The Marriage of the Prophet to Sawda bint Zam'a
summary: After Khadija's death, the Prophet also married Sawda bint Zam'a, a widowed believer, through the mediation of Khawla bint Hakim. Sawda's elderly father arranged the marriage, and her brother returned from pilgrimage to find it done.
story: Khawla then left and went in to see Sawda, daughter of Zam'a, saying, "What goodness and blessings God brings you!" "How do you mean?" she asked. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has sent me to ask you to marry him!" "I would like that," she replied. "Go in and tell Abu Bakr of that." He was an elderly man, too old to have gone on the pilgrimage, so she went in to him and gave him the salutation used before the coming of Islam. "Who is that?" he asked. "The Messenger of God (SAAS)," she told him, "has sent me to arrange his marriage with Sawda." "A fine match! What does your lady say?" he responded. "She would like that," she replied. "Well, call her in to me," he told her. She did so and Abu Bakr said to Sawda, "My child, this woman claims that Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, has sent asking to become engaged to you. It's a fine match. Would you have me marry you to him?" "Yes, I would," she replied. "Then call for him," he said. And when the Messenger of God (SAAS) came, he did marry him to her. Her brother 'Abd b. Zam'a then returned from the pilgrimage, his head covered with dirt. After greetings, he commented, "By your life, what a fool I am! The same day I cover my head with dirt the Messenger of God (SAAS) marries Sawda, daughter of Zam'a!"
lessons: A believing woman who had suffered loss found renewed purpose and honor through her faith and willingness to serve the Prophet's household, reminding us that grief need not be the end of one's story.
Sawda's eagerness and her father's gracious response show that when the heart is aligned with faith, major life transitions can be embraced with peace rather than fear.
Even those who feel they missed a moment — like Sawda's brother — can reflect with humor and acceptance on God's decrees unfolding without them.
practical_advice: If you are navigating widowhood, loss, or loneliness, look for ways your community and faith can reconnect you to purpose and belonging rather than remaining in isolation.
When others around you make significant life decisions without your involvement, practice releasing the need for control and trust that God's plan unfolds even in your absence.
Allow trusted elders or community figures to support your major decisions, especially during vulnerable seasons of life, as Sawda's father did for her.

title: 'A'isha Describes Her Wedding Day in Medina
summary: 'A'isha recounts in detail the day her mother prepared her for her marriage consummation with the Prophet in Medina, describing being taken from a swing, dressed by the women of the Ansar, and seated in the Prophet's lap at age nine.
story: 'A'isha stated, "We moved to Medina and took up residence among the Banu al-Harith b. Khazraj in al-Sunuh. The Messenger of God (SAAS) came into our house where he had a meeting with some of the men and women of the ansar, 'the Helpers'. My mother came to me while I was on a swing, going to and fro between two date-palms. She took me down from the swing; my hair was short like a boy's and she parted it, washed my face with some water and then led me over to the door. I was out of breath and (stopped) there until I became calmer. Then she took me in and there was the Messenger of God (SAAS) seated on a bed inside our house, in the company of a group of the ansar, both men and women. She sat me down in his lap and said, 'These are your family; may God bless you by them and they by you.' The men and women then jumped up and left. The Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated our marriage there in our house; no camels or sheep were slaughtered for me. Eventually Sa'd b. 'Ubada sent us a bowl of food such as he would provide whenever the Messenger of God (SAAS) was visiting his wives. At that time, I was nine."
lessons: Community presence during life's major transitions — represented by the Ansar who gathered and then respectfully withdrew — reflects the balance between celebration and privacy.
Sa'd b. 'Ubada's gesture of sending food demonstrates that small, practical acts of generosity toward newlyweds and neighbors are a living expression of faith.
Being cared for by a mother who attended to every physical detail before a momentous occasion teaches us that love is often expressed through patient, attentive preparation.
practical_advice: During anxious transitions in life, allow yourself a moment to breathe and settle, as 'A'isha paused at the door until she became calmer, before stepping forward.
Accept the generosity of your community with gratitude; it is often through others' kindness that God's blessings are made tangible in difficult moments.
When supporting someone through a major life change, consider how practical gestures — preparing them, accompanying them, providing a meal — can be more powerful than words.

title: Sawda's Gift of Her Day to 'A'isha in Old Age
summary: When Sawda grew old, she gifted her designated day with the Prophet to 'A'isha out of love, and 'A'isha noted that Sawda was the first woman the Prophet married after herself in terms of the marriage contract.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, "Aswad related to us, that it was related to him from Shurayk, from Hisham, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When Sawda grew old and she gave to me the day assigned to her, the Messenger of God (SAAS) shared the day given to me (by her) with his other wives. She was the first woman with whom he contracted marriage after myself.'"
lessons: Sawda's selfless act of giving away her day reflects a profound generosity of spirit — prioritizing the happiness of others over one's own entitlement, a model of love without ego.
Aging gracefully in faith means recognizing how we can still give and contribute, even when our capacity changes, rather than clinging to what we once had.
The order and equity the Prophet maintained among his wives, and the way his household adapted with compassion, teaches us that fairness and love can coexist in complex relationships.
practical_advice: When you feel your role or relevance diminishing in a relationship or community, look for ways to contribute through generosity rather than retreating into resentment.
Practice releasing entitlements — time, attention, resources — you legitimately hold when you sense someone else may need them more.
Reflect on how those around you, particularly in family life, might be quietly making sacrifices for your benefit, and acknowledge their generosity openly.

title: The Prophet's Betrothal to Sawda the Widow with Many Children
summary: The Prophet approached Sawda, a widow with many children whose husband had died, and asked if anything held her back from him. Her response reflects both her faith and her circumstances.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, "Abu al-Nadr related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Hamid, Shahr and 'Abd allah b. 'Abbas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) became engaged to a woman from his people whose name was Sawda; she was a woman with many children, having some five or six from her husband who had died. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked her, 'Is there something holding you back from me?' She replied, 'O Prophet of God, nothing'"
lessons: Sawda's openness to remarriage despite her circumstances of widowhood and many children demonstrates that faith empowers us to embrace new chapters without shame or hesitation.
The Prophet's gentle and direct question — asking if anything held her back — models how to approach sensitive conversations with care and respect for the other person's autonomy.
Being a person of many responsibilities need not disqualify one from love, companionship, or a new beginning; faith opens doors that circumstances seem to close.
practical_advice: If you are a widow, widower, or single parent carrying heavy responsibilities, resist the inner voice that says your season for companionship or joy has passed.
When approaching someone about a significant matter, ask directly and gently whether they have reservations, giving them the space to be honest without pressure.
Reflect on what invisible barriers — assumptions, shame, or fear — may be holding you back from a blessing that is actually available to you.

title: The Prophet's Marriage Engagements: 'A'isha and Sawda
summary: Scholarly accounts debate the order of the Prophet's marriage contracts with 'A'isha and Sawda bint Zam'a, with Sawda expressing her devotion while humbly noting her concern about her many children.
story: given to me (by her) with his other wives. She was the first woman with whom he contracted marriage after myself.' Im w A b a d stated, 'Abn al-Nadr related to us, quoting 'Abd al-Hamid, Shahr and 'Abd all^ b. 'Abbs, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) became engaged to a woman from his people whose name was Sawda; she was a woman with many children, having some five or six from her husband who had died. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked her, 'Is there something holding you back from me?' She replied, 'O Prophet of God, nothing prevents you from being the most beloved of mankind to me. But I would do you honour by preventing these boys from being at your head morning and night!' 'Does anything else keep you from me?' he asked. 'No, I swear,' she replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then told her, 'God have mercy on you! The best of women ride on old camels; isn't that appropriate? The women of Quraysh are kind to a boy when young and they also take care of their husband however they can!' I observe that her husband before him was al-Sakm b. 'Amr, brother of Suhayl b. 'Amr. He had accepted Islam and gone into exile to Abyssinia, as we reported before. He had then returned to Mecca and died there before the emigration, God be pleased with him! All these accounts indicate that the marriage contract with 'A'isha was prior to that with Sawda, this being the statement of 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Wqayl. Yunus also related this from al-Zuhri. Ibn 'Abd al-Barr preferred the contract with Sawda to have preceded that with 'A'isha; he related this from Qatada and Abu 'Ubayda. He stated, 'Wqayl related it from al-Zuhri.'
lessons: Sincerity and love for the Prophet (peace be upon him) can coexist with honest, practical concerns — Sawda's candour shows that true devotion includes thoughtful consideration of others.
Scholarly differences on historical details are a normal part of Islamic tradition and teach us intellectual humility rather than rigid certainty.
A widow with children can find honourable companionship and security; new beginnings are possible after loss and hardship.
practical_advice: When entering any new relationship or commitment, communicate your genuine concerns openly rather than suppressing them out of politeness.
If you are navigating grief or widowhood, remember that life can open new, meaningful chapters — seek support from your community rather than isolating yourself.
When you encounter conflicting accounts or opinions, practise intellectual humility by acknowledging multiple perspectives before drawing conclusions.

title: Quraysh Embolden After the Death of Abu Talib
summary: After the death of his uncle and protector Abu Talib, the Prophet faced intensified harassment and humiliation from Quraysh, including having dirt thrown at him, yet he remained steadfast and trusted in God's protection.
story: Reference has previously been given to the death of Abu Talib, the uncle of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and to his having been a supporter and defender of his, with all the moral, financial, verbal and practical help he could give. When he died, foolish men of Quraysh were emboldened and engaged in such actions against him that they had not previously been able to undertake. As al-Bayhaqi reported from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm: 'Muhammad b. Ishaq al-San'ani related to us, from Yusuf b. Bahlul, from 'Abd Allah b. Idris, from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from someone else who told him, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, from 'Abd Allah b. Ja'far, who said, 'When Abu Talib died, a foolish fellow of Quraysh stood in the way of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and threw dirt at him. He, the Messenger of God (SAAS), then returned home and one of his daughters came and wiped the dirt from his face, weeping. He told her, "Don't cry, daughter; God will protect your father."'' He is supposed to have said during this conversation that Quraysh did not engage in any reprehensible actions against him until the death of Abu Talib, at which point they began (to harass him). Ziyad al-Baka'i related this from Muhammad b. Ishaq, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, with an incomplete chain of authorities. But God knows best. Al-Bayhaqi also related, from al-Hakim and others, from al-Asamm, from Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, from Yunus b. Bukayr, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Quraysh remained cowards until Abu Talib died.' He then related this from al-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from 'Abbas al-Duri, from Yahya b. Ma'in, who said, 'We were related by 'Uqba al-Majdar, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "Quraysh remained cowards until Abu Talib died."'
lessons: The loss of a protector or loved one can expose us to new hardships, but God's protection does not depend on any single human supporter.

The Prophet's comforting words to his weeping daughter — 'Don't cry; God will protect your father' — demonstrate that even in moments of humiliation, trust in God is the most powerful source of resilience.
Adversity often increases after losing a pillar of support, but this does not mean one is abandoned; it is often a test that deepens reliance on God.
practical_advice: When you lose a key source of support in your life — a mentor, parent, or protector — acknowledge the grief, but consciously re-anchor your trust in God rather than in human means alone.
If you are publicly humiliated or demeaned, resist the urge to retaliate in the moment; respond with dignity as the Prophet did, and seek solace in private prayer.

Comfort those around you who are distressed by your difficulties — the Prophet's first instinct was to console his daughter, which models prioritising the wellbeing of your loved ones even when you yourself are suffering.

title: Abu Lahab's Brief Protection and Its End
summary: After Abu Talib's death, Abu Lahab initially offered the Prophet temporary protection out of tribal loyalty, but was manipulated by Abu Jahl and 'Uqba into withdrawing it and becoming a bitter enemy.
story: The hafiz Abu al-Faraj b. al-Jawzi related with a chain of authorities from Tha'laba b. Suqayr and Hakim b. Hizam who both said, 'When Abu Talib and Khadija died, five days apart, he suffered two tragedies together. He kept to his house and rarely went out. Quraysh treated him worse than they would have ever intended before. 'News of this reached Abu Lahab, who came to him and said, "O Muhammad, pass on freely as you want. Do whatever you did when Abu Talib was alive. No, by al-Lat, I swear no harm will befall you before I die." 'Ibn al-Ghaymla insulted the Messenger of God (SAAS), and so Abu Lahab went to him and punished him and he went off yelling, "O tribe of Quraysh, Abu 'Utba has become a Sabian!" Quraysh men then came and stood before Abu Lahab. He told them, "I have not left the faith of 'Abd al-Muttalib. But I will prevent my nephew from being harmed and ensure his own freedom of action." 'They commented, "You have done well and right, and maintained your family ties." 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) thereafter spent a period of some days coming and going without interference from Quraysh who respected Abu Lahab. But eventually 'Uqba b. Abu Mu'ayt and Abu Jahl went to Abu Lahab and asked him, "Has your nephew told you where your father has been sent?" 'Abu Lahab then asked him, "Muhammad, where has 'Abd al-Muttalib been sent?" 'He replied, "With his people." So Abu Lahab went out to 'Uqba and Abu Jahl and told them, "I did ask him. And he replied, 'With his people.'" 'The two men commented, "Well, he's claiming that he is in hell-fire then!" 'Abu Lahab then asked him, "Muhammad, is 'Abd al-Muttalib in the fire?" 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Whoever dies in the state 'Abd al-Muttalib was in does go into hell-fire." 'Abu Lahab — God curse him — then announced, "By God, I shall always be your enemy for your claiming that 'Abd al-Muttalib is in the fire!" 'At that Abu Lahab and the rest of Quraysh intensified their attacks upon him.'
lessons: Human protection is fragile and conditional; those who offer it for worldly or tribal reasons can withdraw it just as quickly when pressured or offended.
The Prophet's honesty — refusing to give a comforting but false answer about his ancestor's fate — shows that integrity must not be sacrificed even to secure one's own safety.
Enemies of truth often use emotionally charged questions to manipulate others into withdrawing support from those who speak it.
practical_advice: When someone withdraws their support from you after you spoke an uncomfortable truth, resist the temptation to compromise your honesty to win them back — your integrity is worth more than temporary protection.
Be aware that those who oppose your values may try to use your relationships against you; stay alert to manipulation attempts that exploit the people around you.
In times of compounded loss and isolation, reduce unnecessary public exposure while you regroup spiritually and emotionally, as the Prophet did by staying home during that difficult period.

title: Neighbours Who Harassed the Prophet in His Home
summary: A group of the Prophet's neighbours, including Abu Lahab and 'Uqba b. Abu Mu'ayt, regularly threw filth into his home and cooking pot, yet the Prophet responded with dignified reproach rather than retaliation.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The group of men who used to harm the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his house were Abu Lahab, al-Hakam b. Abu al-'As b. Umayya, 'Uqba b. Abu Mu'ayy, 'Adi b. al-Hamra', and Ibn al-Asda' al-Hudhali. 'These were neighbours of his; the only one of them to ever accept Islam was al-Hakam b. Abu al-'As. 'It is said that one of them would throw a sheep's uterus over him while he was praying, or toss it into his cooking pot if set up for him. Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) would take a position near a wall when praying and when they threw some such thing at him he would carry it outside on a stick, stand at his door and say, "O Banu 'Abd Manaf, what kind of neighbourliness is this?" Then he would toss it out into the street.' My own view is that most of the matter being related here occurred after the death of Abu Talib. Though God knows best. It is certainly very appropriate to mention them here. These incidents included the throwing of a camel's placenta over his shoulders while he prayed, as Ibn Mas'ud related, and of Fatima's having come and removed it from him and having gone off and reviled them; when he had finished praying, he said prayers against seven of them. A similar incident was the account of 'Abd Allah b. 'Amr b. al-'As of their having severely throttled him until Abu Bakr intervened, saying, 'Would you murder a man merely for saying God is my Lord?' And there is the case of the intent of Abu Jahl, God damn him, to tread on his neck while he prayed, and of this being prevented.
lessons: Even when subjected to repeated degradation in one's own home, responding with principled, verbal reproach — as the Prophet did by appealing to the norms of neighbourliness — is more powerful than retaliation.
Continued worship in the face of deliberate disruption demonstrates that no external harassment can sever one's connection with God.
Community members like Abu Bakr and Fatima who intervene to protect the vulnerable play a vital role; standing up for the oppressed is a core Islamic value.
practical_advice: When people in your immediate environment are hostile or disrespectful, name the behaviour clearly and appeal to shared standards of decency rather than escalating the conflict.
Maintain your spiritual practices — prayer, remembrance of God — even when your environment makes them difficult; consistency in worship is itself an act of resilience.
If you witness someone being harassed or harmed, intervene as Abu Bakr did — even a verbal objection can halt injustice and demonstrates moral courage.

title: The Prophet's Journey to Ta'if and His Rejection by Thaqif
summary: After Abu Talib's death left him without protection in Mecca, the Prophet travelled alone to Ta'if to seek help from the tribe of Thaqif, only to be mocked, rejected, and driven out of the city by slaves and fools set upon him.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'When Abu Talib died, Quraysh treated the Messenger of God (SAAS) worse than had ever been the case during the life of his uncle Abu Talib. 'And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) left for Ta'if seeking help from Thaqif and their protection from his people. He hoped they would accept the message he brought them from God Almighty. 'He went to them alone. 'Yazid b. Abu Ziyad related to me, from Muhammad b. Ka'b al-Kurazi, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Ta'if, he made for a group of Thaqif, their leaders and nobles, who were three brothers. They were 'Abd Yalil, Mas'ud and Habib, all sons of 'Amr b. 'Umayr b. 'Awf b. 'Uqda b. Ghiyara b. 'Awf b. Thaqif. One of these was married to a Quraysh woman of the Banu Jumah. '"He sat with them and invited them to God, asked them to help Islam and himself against those of his people who were opposing him. One of them replied, 'He would tear off the covering of the Ka'ba if it were God who had sent you!' Another said, 'Did not God have anyone but you to send?' The third commented, 'By God, I will never speak to you. If you were a messenger from God, as you claim, you are far too important for me to argue with, and if you are lying against God, then it would certainly not be appropriate that I talk to you.' '"And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) arose and left them, despairing of any good from Thaqif. I have been told that he had asked them, 'Since you have so behaved, at least keep it secret for me.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not want his people to hear about this, since it would have encouraged them to oppose him. '"However, they did not agree, and incited their fools and slaves to revile him and to shout out after him. A crowd gathered against him and forced him to seek refuge in a garden belonging to 'Utba b. Rabi'a and Shayba b. Rabi'a, they both being there at the time. The Thaqif fools who had been following him then withdrew. '"He made for the shade of a grape vine, where he sat down, while the two sons of Rabi'a watched him, having seen his ill-treatment by the Thaqif fools. '"As I have been told, the Messenger of God (SAAS) had met the woman from the Banu Jumah and had told her, 'Well, how is it we've been so treated by your husband's family?'"
lessons: Seeking help from those who ultimately reject you is not a failure — the Prophet's journey to Ta'if shows that exhausting legitimate means of seeking support is itself an act of sincere striving.
Rejection by those in power does not diminish the truth of one's message; the worth of what you carry is not determined by the response of others.
Even enemies can unknowingly provide refuge: the sons of Rabi'a, who had not accepted Islam, offered the Prophet rest and compassion, reminding us that human decency can appear in unexpected places.
practical_advice: When you have been rejected after genuinely reaching out for help, allow yourself to grieve the disappointment rather than suppressing it — the Prophet himself left Ta'if 'despairing of any good from Thaqif.'
After a painful rejection, find a quiet place to rest and collect yourself before moving forward, just as the Prophet sought shade under the grape vine before turning to God in prayer.

Do not let the fear of others hearing about your failure or rejection prevent you from seeking help — while the Prophet wished for discretion, his mission ultimately did not depend on secrecy but on divine support.

title: The Prophet's Prayer in the Garden of Ta'if
summary: Alone and wounded in spirit after being driven from Ta'if, the Prophet offered one of the most moving supplications in Islamic history, expressing his weakness to God and seeking only His pleasure and protection.
story: 'When he felt secure, as I have been told, he spoke the prayer, "O God, I complain to You about my weakness and inadequacy before the people. O You, most Merciful of all, You who are Lord of the oppressed, You who are my Lord, to whom would You entrust me, to those far away who greet me with displeasure, or to some enemy to whom You entrust me? As long as You are not angry with me, I will not care, but I would prefer your favour. '"I take refuge with the light of Your face that brightens the shadows, repairs the troubles of this world and the hereafter, ensuring that Your anger or discontent not alight upon me. May You be content and be pleased; all power and strength stem from You."'
lessons: In the depths of helplessness and rejection, turning directly to God with complete vulnerability — acknowledging weakness rather than projecting strength — is the highest form of trust and the beginning of relief.

The Prophet's prayer shows that the greatest fear of a believer is not human rejection or physical harm, but the displeasure of God — when we align our deepest concern with God's approval, worldly hardships become bearable.
Expressing pain to God honestly, as the Prophet did ('I complain to You about my weakness'), is not a sign of weak faith but of profound intimacy with the Divine.
practical_advice: Memorise or reflect upon this prayer of Ta'if during your own moments of humiliation, rejection, or helplessness — use it as a template for bringing your rawest emotions before God.
When you feel abandoned by people, reframe the experience: ask yourself not 'why have people failed me?' but 'how is God still with me?' — this shift in focus is what sustained the Prophet.

Make it a habit to end moments of deep distress with an acknowledgement that 'all power and strength stem from You' — this declaration of divine sovereignty actively counters feelings of hopelessness and overwhelm.

title: 'Addas the Christian Servant Offers Grapes to the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet's rejection at Ta'if, 'Utba and Shayba b. Rabi'a, moved by compassion, sent their Christian servant 'Addas with grapes to the Prophet, leading to a tender encounter of kindness in his lowest moment.
story: 'When Rabi'a's two sons 'Utba and Shayba saw what had happened to him, they felt compassion for him. They summoned a Christian servant they had called 'Addas and told him, "Take a cluster of grapes, place it on a plate, then go over to that man and tell him to eat it." '"'Addas did this; he placed it before the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him to eat. '"As the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed his hand upon it, he spoke the words, 'In the name of God,' then he ate. 'Addas looked into his face and said,"
lessons: Compassion and human kindness can cross all boundaries of religion, tribe, and status — even those who had not accepted Islam were moved to care for the Prophet in his most vulnerable moment.
Never underestimate the impact of a simple act of kindness — a plate of grapes offered at the right moment is an act of profound humanity that echoes across centuries.

The Prophet's immediate utterance of 'In the name of God' before eating, even in a moment of pain and exhaustion, shows that maintaining spiritual mindfulness in small daily acts is a source of inner stability.
practical_advice: When someone around you is visibly suffering — even a stranger — follow the example of 'Utba and Shayba: offer something simple, practical, and nourishing without requiring them to explain their pain.

In your own moments of lowest energy and greatest hurt, try to maintain your small spiritual practices — saying 'Bismillah' before eating, offering a brief prayer — these anchors keep you connected to meaning even in darkness.
If you are in a position of relative comfort while others suffer nearby, act on your compassion promptly rather than waiting until you fully understand their situation.

title: The Encounter with 'Addas the Christian Servant
summary: After being wounded and rejected at Ta'if, the Prophet received grapes from a Christian servant named 'Addas, who upon hearing the Prophet mention Jonah recognized his prophethood and kissed his head, hands, and feet.
story: saw what had happened to him, they felt compassion for him. They summoned a Christian servant they had called 'Addas and told him, "Take a cluster of grapes, place it on a plate, then go over to that man and tell him to eat it." 'Addas did this; he placed it before the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him to eat. As the Messenger of God (SAAS) placed his hand upon it, he spoke the words, "In the name of God" then he ate. 'Addas looked into his face and said, "By God, people of this land do not say these words." The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him, "Where are you from, 'Addas, and what is your religion?" He replied, "I am a Christian, and I come from Nineveh." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "You mean from the village of that pious man Jonah, son of Amittai?" 'Addas asked, "How do you know of Jonah, son of Amittai?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "That man was my brother; he was a prophet and I am a prophet." 'Addas bent low over the Messenger of God (SAAS), kissing his head, hands and feet. The two sons of Rabi'a said to one another, "He's already corrupted our servant!" When 'Addas came back to them, they commented, "What was wrong with you, 'Addas? Why did you kiss the head, hands and feet of that man?" "On all earth there is none finer than this man; he has told me of something that no one but a prophet could know," he told them. "Be careful", they warned him, "you don't let him turn you from your faith. Your religion is better than his."
lessons: Even in moments of greatest suffering and rejection, God sends unexpected sources of comfort and recognition — kindness can arrive from the most unlikely places.

Truth has its own illuminating power; when 'Addas heard one piece of prophetic knowledge, his heart recognized the Prophet's authenticity immediately, showing that sincere hearts respond to truth regardless of their background.
Maintaining one's spiritual practice — saying 'Bismillah' even while wounded and exhausted — is itself an act of resilience that can open doors to connection and dawah.
practical_advice: When you are going through rejection or hardship, remain consistent in your remembrance of God in small acts like saying 'Bismillah' before eating — these habits anchor you spiritually and may become bridges to others.

Do not underestimate the impact of one honest, kind exchange; just as the Prophet's brief conversation with 'Addas left a profound mark, your authentic interactions can touch hearts even when larger efforts seem to fail.
If you feel that your efforts are going unrecognized by those around you, remember that recognition may come from unexpected quarters — stay open and keep presenting your best self.

title: The People of Ta'if Stone the Prophet on His Path
summary: As the Prophet left Ta'if after being rejected by its leaders, its people lined the road in two rows and pelted his feet with stones until his feet bled, forcing him to take shelter under a palm tree completely overcome.
story: people of Ta'if positioned themselves in two lines along his path and as he passed by every time he raised and put down a foot they threw stones at it until his feet began to bleed. His feet streaming with blood, he withdrew and made his way beneath the shade of a palm tree, completely overcome. In that garden were 'Utba and Shayba, the two sons of Rabi'a. He disliked being at their place because of their enmity towards God and His Messenger.
lessons: Physical and emotional pain can reach an extreme, yet the Prophet sought shade and rest rather than retaliation — pausing and withdrawing from harm is not weakness but wisdom.
Even the people who were enemies of the Prophet's mission were ultimately instruments in a larger divine plan; the hardest moments do not define the final outcome.
Being 'completely overcome' is a human experience even for the greatest of people — acknowledging your limits and resting is part of endurance, not a failure of faith.
practical_advice: When you feel utterly overwhelmed, permit yourself to stop, find shade — a quiet room, a trusted space — and rest before taking your next step, just as the Prophet sought the shade of the palm tree.
If you are being harmed or mistreated in a situation, physically removing yourself from that environment is a valid and wise response, not a sign of defeat.
Reflect on whether the pain you are experiencing is pushing you toward a necessary turning point; sometimes the lowest moments precede the most significant changes in direction.

title: The Prophet's Dua After Ta'if — Turning to God in Despair
summary: After suffering rejection and injury at Ta'if, the Prophet wandered dazed until he reached Qarn al-Tha'alib, where he raised his head and found Gabriel and the angel of the mountain offering to destroy his enemies — but the Prophet responded with hope for their descendants instead.
story: He replied, "The worst I suffered from your people was the day of al-'aqaba, when I presented myself to Ibn 'Abdu Yail b. 'Abd Kalal, and he refused my request to him. I wandered off, dazed and depressed, and only came to myself at Qarn al-Tha'alib. I raised my head and there above me was a cloud. Looking up, I saw Gabriel in it, and he called out to me, saying, 'God has heard what your people said to you, and how they rejected you. He has sent to you the angel of the mountain for you to order him to do with them whatever you like.' Then the angel of the mountain called out to me in greeting and said, 'O Muhammad, God has sent me. God has heard what your people said to you. I am the angel of the mountain; your Lord has sent me to you to order me to do whatever you wish. If you wish, you can bring down the two mountains the Akhshabayn upon them.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'I hope that God will bring forth from their loins those who will worship God and associate no other god or person with Him.'"
lessons: The Prophet's response to having total power over his oppressors — choosing mercy and hope over revenge — is one of the most profound demonstrations of prophetic character and long-term spiritual vision.
God's acknowledgment that He 'heard' what was said and done to the Prophet is a reminder that no suffering goes unseen; divine awareness of our pain is itself a source of comfort.
Hope for future good, even from the same people or circumstances that harmed you, is a spiritually mature and transformative posture that breaks cycles of bitterness.
practical_advice: When you have been deeply wronged and feel 'dazed and depressed,' pour out your true feelings to God honestly in dua — the Prophet did not suppress his pain but brought it before his Lord.
Practice the discipline of not seeking revenge even when you have legitimate grounds for it; ask yourself what long-term good might still come from the people or situation that hurt you.

If you are struggling with despair after a major rejection, recall this moment in the Seerah: God noticed, God responded, and the outcome of that painful journey became one of the most beloved stories in Islamic history.

title: The Jinn Listen to the Quran at Nakhla
summary: On the Prophet's return journey from Ta'if, a group of seven jinn overheard him reciting the Quran at Nakhla during the morning prayer, believed in his message, and this event was commemorated in Quranic revelation.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq told how the jinn, the spirits, overheard the recitation made by the Messenger of God (SAAS) upon his return from Ta'if when he spent the night at Nakhla and said the morning prayer with his Companions. The jinn who had been dispatched to him heard his reciting there. Ibn Ishaq stated, "They were seven in number. Concerning them God Almighty revealed His words, 'And when We dispatched towards you a group of jinn'" (surat al-Ahqaf; XLVI, v.29).
lessons: The Prophet's consistency in worship — praying Fajr with his companions even while wounded, rejected, and traveling — meant that his dawah reached beyond the human realm entirely.
When human communities close their doors, God opens other avenues; the acceptance of the jinn came precisely at the moment of the Prophet's greatest human rejection.
Consistency in worship during hardship, even when no human audience seems to respond, carries a spiritual weight and reach that we cannot fully perceive.
practical_advice: Maintain your daily acts of worship — prayer, dhikr, Quran — even and especially during your lowest moments; their impact extends far beyond what you can see.
When your efforts seem to go unnoticed by the people around you, remember that you do not know the full reach of your sincerity — stay consistent.
Use the story of the jinn at Nakhla as a reminder that a moment of worship in the dark, in difficulty, on a road of pain, can become eternally significant.

title: The Prophet Seeks Protection to Re-enter Mecca and Al-Mut'im b. 'Adi Grants It
summary: Upon returning from Ta'if, the Prophet needed tribal protection to re-enter Mecca and was turned down by two leaders before Al-Mut'im b. 'Adi agreed; Mut'im then escorted him with his armed sons into the mosque to complete his circumambulations.
story: Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Mecca on his return from Ta'if and came under the protection of al-Mut'im b. 'Adi. His people increased their anger and antagonism towards him and became ever bolder in their opposition. God is the one from whom to seek help, and reliance must be upon Him. In his work on the maghazi, the early military engagements, al-Umawi related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent Urayqit to al-Akhnas b. Sharif and asked him to give him protection in Mecca. But he replied, 'An ally of Quraysh cannot give protection for one of their own.' He then sent to Suhayl b. 'Amr to give him protection, and he responded, 'The Banu 'Amir b. Lu'ayy cannot give protection against those of the Banu Ka'b b. Lu'ayy.' He then sent him to al-Mut'im b. 'Adi for his protection, who agreed, saying, 'Yes; tell him to come.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) did go to him and spent that night with him. When morning came, he and his six or seven sons, all wearing swords, went out with the Messenger of God (SAAS). They entered the mosque and told the Messenger of God (SAAS), 'Make your circumambulations.' And they sat, with their legs drawn up over their belted swords in the mataf, the space around the Ka'ba for the circumambulations. Abu Sufyan came over to Mut'im and asked him, 'Are you giving protection or are you a follower?' He replied, 'No, I'm just protecting.' 'Then you'll not be watched,' Abu Sufyan told him. He then sat with him until the Messenger of God (SAAS) had finished his circuits, and when he left al-Mut'im and his men went with him. Then Abu Sufyan went off to his seat.
lessons: Practical necessity sometimes requires seeking help from those who do not share your faith or fully endorse your mission; there is wisdom and humility in asking for what you need without pride.

Al-Mut'im's act of protection — a non-Muslim defending the Prophet out of honor — was so noble that the Prophet remembered and honored it years later at Badr, showing that goodness is never forgotten by God or by righteous people.
Persistence in seeking help, even after multiple rejections, is part of taking the right means while trusting in God — the Prophet asked three people before finding one who said yes.
practical_advice: When facing a closed door, try another — do not let one or two rejections stop you from continuing to seek the help or opportunity you legitimately need.
Express gratitude for those who help you even partially or without fully agreeing with your cause; acknowledge their goodness as the Prophet honored Mut'im's memory.
When you re-enter a difficult environment after hardship, go with a plan and with support if possible — the Prophet did not return to Mecca alone but under structured protection.

title: Hassan b. Thabit's Elegy for Al-Mut'im b. 'Adi
summary: After Al-Mut'im b. 'Adi died, the poet Hassan b. Thabit composed verses honoring his nobility and protection of the Prophet, and the Prophet himself paid tribute to his memory at the time of the Badr prisoners.
story: Shortly after the Messenger of God (SAAS) had left Mecca for Medina, al-Mut'im b. 'Adi died. Hassan b. Thabit said, 'By God, I shall certainly eulogize him!' And part of what he spoke were the following verses: 'If honour could today render any one person everlasting, his honour would today select Mut'im. You protected the Messenger of God from them and they became your slaves for as long as pilgrims don the ihram and shout "labbayka". If all Ma'add, Qahtан or all the rest of Jurhum were asked about him, They would say, "He fulfils defence of his neighbour and protects if he takes on a difficult duty," The shining sun above them does not look down on his like among them, one greater or more noble. Resolute if refusing, but kind by nature, sleeping well even if the night is dark and he protecting another.' I observe that this is why the Prophet (SAAS) said, on the day the prisoners were taken at Badr, "If al-Mut'im b. 'Adi were alive now and asked me about these stinking people, I would have given them over to him."
lessons: Honoring those who did good, even after their death and even when they were not of your faith, reflects the highest moral character and keeps gratitude alive in community memory.
A single act of genuine protection and courage — as Mut'im performed — can outweigh years of opposition and be remembered and rewarded in extraordinary ways.
Poetry, remembrance, and public tribute are legitimate and meaningful ways to process grief and honor the contributions of those who have passed.
practical_advice: Make it a practice to express gratitude to those who helped you, both while they are alive and by honoring their memory after they are gone.
Reflect on the single most important act of support someone gave you during your hardest time — have you truly acknowledged it? Find a way to do so.
When someone helps you without being obligated to, recognize that this kind of unconditional assistance is rare and precious — reciprocate with loyalty and remembrance.

title: The Prophet Presents Himself to Arab Tribes at the Pilgrimage Seasons
summary: After returning from Ta'if to an even more hostile Mecca, the Prophet began systematically presenting himself to Arab tribes at the seasonal fairs, calling them to Islam and asking for their protection, but none agreed.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned to Mecca, where his people were even more antagonistic to him and his religion than before, except for a few persons of no power who did believe. The Messenger of God (SAAS) would present himself at the fairs when they were held, addressing the Arab tribes and inviting them to God, Almighty and Glorious is He. He would tell them that he was a prophet who had been given a mission, and would ask for their belief and their protection so that he could explain what God had entrusted to him."
lessons: Continuing your effort systematically and methodically after major setbacks — rather than retreating entirely — is a mark of true determination and trust in God's timing.
The Prophet's willingness to ask openly for help and protection, without shame, shows that seeking support for a righteous cause is not weakness but strategic wisdom.
The fact that none of the tribes agreed yet the mission ultimately succeeded is a reminder that human rejection does not determine divine outcomes.
practical_advice: After a significant failure or rejection, resist the urge to stop entirely — instead, identify a new audience or avenue for your efforts and approach it with fresh intention.

If you are working toward a meaningful goal, consider whether you are presenting it in the right settings and to the right people; the Prophet adapted his outreach to the seasons and gatherings of his time.
When no one seems to respond to something you believe in deeply, hold on to the knowledge that God's plan has a timing that does not always match our expectations — continue and trust.

title: Abu Lahab Follows the Prophet at Fairs to Contradict Him
summary: As the Prophet called Arab tribes to Islam at the market of Mina, his uncle Abu Lahab followed directly behind him at each gathering, telling the tribes not to listen and accusing the Prophet of wrongful innovation.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "One of our fellow scholars, a man in whom I trust, related the following to me, from Zayd b. Aslam, from Rabi'a b. 'Ibad al-Du'ali, and another person from whom Abu Zinad had narrated. I was also related this by Husayn b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Ubayd Allah b. Abbas, who said, 'I heard Rabi'a b. 'Ibad being addressed by my father who said, "I was once, when a young lad, with my father at Mina when the Messenger of God (SAAS) would stop at the camps of the Arab tribes and say to them, 'I am the Messenger of God to you. I tell you to worship God and to associate no other with Him and to abandon those others you revere; and you should have faith and belief in me and protect me so that I make evident that with which God sent me.' Behind him stood a squint-eyed, neatly dressed man, his hair in two braids and wearing a cloak from Aden. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) had finished his prayer and address, that man would say to the tribe, 'This man is only trying to get you to strip al-Lat and al-'Uzza off your necks, along with your allies the jinn of the Banu Malik b. Uqaysh in favour of the wrongful innovation he himself brings. Do not obey him and do not listen to him.' I asked my father, 'Who is that man who follows behind him and contradicts what he says?' He replied, 'That is his uncle, 'Abd al-'Uzza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, Abu Lahab.'"'
lessons: Opposition from within one's own family is among the most painful forms of rejection; the Prophet endured this publicly and repeatedly without abandoning his mission.

Active sabotage of your efforts by someone close to you is a test of whether your motivation comes from seeking human approval or from a deeper conviction — the Prophet's source of strength was clearly the latter.

Those who witness injustice and public persecution, like the young boy in this account, often carry these memories and their moral weight across generations — your conduct under pressure is being observed and remembered.
practical_advice: If someone close to you actively undermines your work or reputation, focus on the quality and consistency of your own message rather than on refuting every attack; let your conduct speak.
When you feel publicly humiliated or contradicted, especially by family, ground yourself in why you are doing what you are doing — return to your core intention and purpose.
Consider who is watching you navigate difficulty with grace; your resilience in hard moments may be shaping the character of others around you, especially those younger than you.

title: Abu Lahab Follows the Prophet at Dhu al-Majaz Market, Calling Him a Liar
summary: At the market of Dhu al-Majaz, the Prophet called people to say 'There is no god but God.' His uncle Abu Lahab followed him, calling him a liar and a Sabian, urging people not to follow him.
story: recounted this hadith from Ihrzhim b. Aba al-'Abbss saying, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Abo Zinad related to us, from his father, who said, 'A man named Rabi'a b. 'Ibid of the Bana al-Dil informed me - he having been a polytheist before Islam who converted - as follows, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the market of Dha al-Majaz in the period before Islam was accepted; he was saying, 'O people, say, "There is no god but God" and you will prosper.' As people gathered around him there was one man with a handsome face, squint-eyed and wearing two plaits who would say, 'He's a Sabian; a liar.' And he would follow behind him wherever he went. I asked about him and was told he was his uncle Abu Lahab."" Al-Bayhaqi related this, through Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah al-Ansari, from Muhammad b. 'Amr, from Muhammad b. al-Munkadir, from Rabi'a al-Dili, as follows, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the market of Dhu al-Majaz following people into their homes and inviting them to God. Behind him came a squint-eyed man with flaming-red cheeks who was saying, 'Do not let this man lead you astray from your religion and that of your forefathers.' I asked who he was, and I was told that this was Abu Lahab." Abu Nu'aym related this similarly in the Dala'il (The Signs), through Ibn Abi Dhi'b and Sa'id b. Salama b. Abi al-Huqayq, both of them relating a similar account from Muhammad b. al-Munkadir. Al-Bayhaqi also related it through Shu'ba, from al-Ash'ath b. Salim, from a man of Kinana, who said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) at the market of Dhu al-Majaz and he was saying, 'O people, say, "There is no god but God" and you will prosper.' And there was a man behind him tossing dirt at him. This was Abu Jahl and he was saying, 'O people, do not let this man lead you astray from your religion. He only wants you to give up worship of al-Lat and al-'Uzza.'" According to this account, these were words of Abu Jahl. This supposition might be illusory or it might sometimes have been Abu Jahl and sometimes the other, Abu Lahab. They might have taken turns in doing such harm to the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Steadfast dedication to one's mission in the face of public humiliation is a mark of true conviction.
Opposition from one's own family can be among the most painful trials, yet the Prophet continued without faltering.
Slander and mockery directed at truth-tellers have existed throughout history; their presence does not diminish the truth itself.
practical_advice: When you face ridicule or dismissal from those closest to you, remind yourself that opposition does not equal wrongness — stay focused on your purpose.
If someone publicly undermines your efforts, resist the urge to retaliate; redirect your energy toward those who are genuinely listening.
Reflect on moments when you persisted despite discouragement, and use those memories as anchors when new trials arise.

title: The Prophet Visits the Tribe of Kinda and Is Refused
summary: The Prophet visited the tribe of Kinda at their homes during the fair season, invited them to God, and offered himself to them, but they refused him.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Ibn Shihab al-Zuhri related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the tribe of Kinda, visiting them in their homes, including one of their leaders named Mulayh. He invited them to God, Almighty and Glorious is He, and offered himself to them, but they refused him."
lessons: Not every door you knock on will open — rejection is part of any sincere effort to do good.
The Prophet's willingness to personally visit tribe after tribe demonstrates that persistence in da'wah requires humility and effort.
Every refusal the Prophet received was part of a larger divine plan that would eventually lead to triumph.
practical_advice: When your sincere outreach is rejected, do not interpret it as a reflection of your worth — move on with dignity as the Prophet did.
Keep a long-term perspective: what seems like a closed door today may be preparation for a greater opening tomorrow.
Practice offering goodness to others without attaching your sense of self-worth to their acceptance or refusal.

title: The Prophet Visits the Banu Kalb and Is Refused
summary: The Prophet went to the Banu Kalb, called them to God, and honored their tribal name, but they were unable to accept what he proposed.
story: Ibn Ishaq said, "Muhammad b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Abd Allah b. Husayn related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the Banu Kalb in their homes in a valley of theirs where they were called the Banu 'Abd Allah. He called them to the path of God and offered himself to them, saying, 'O Banu 'Abd Allah, God named well indeed your forefather 'Abd Allah' (i.e. 'the slave or servant of God'). But they could not accept from him what he proposed to them."
lessons: Even a thoughtful, personal appeal — honoring people's heritage and identity — does not guarantee acceptance; the heart's opening is ultimately in God's hands.
The Prophet's gentleness in addressing the Banu 'Abd Allah by honoring their name shows that sincere outreach combines wisdom with warmth.
Rejection by many does not mean the message is wrong; it may simply mean the right people have not yet been reached.
practical_advice: When trying to connect with someone, acknowledge what is meaningful and honorable about them — it reflects sincerity even if they do not immediately respond.
Accept that you cannot control outcomes, only your own sincerity and effort; release the need for a particular response.
After repeated rejections, take time to rest and replenish your sense of purpose rather than letting discouragement accumulate.

title: The Prophet Visits the Banu Hanifa and Receives the Rudest Rejection
summary: Among all the Arab tribes the Prophet approached during the fair seasons, the Banu Hanifa gave him the rudest and most severe rejection.
story: "Some of our friends related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik that the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the Banu Hanifa in their homes, called them to the path of God and offered himself to them. None of the Arabs gave him so rude a rejection as they did."
lessons: The most severe rejection can come from those who seem outwardly strong; their harshness often reflects their own distance from truth.
The Prophet endured the rudest of rejections without abandoning his mission, showing that resilience is not the absence of pain but the refusal to quit.
History often reverses itself — many who rejected the message fiercely later became among its strongest carriers.
practical_advice: When you encounter unusually harsh rejection, resist the temptation to respond in kind; the harshness usually says more about the other person than about you.
Do not measure the value of your efforts by the worst reception you receive — one meaningful connection can outweigh dozens of refusals.
After a particularly painful rejection, give yourself compassionate acknowledgment of the difficulty before pushing forward again.

title: The Prophet Visits the Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a — The Power Question and the Wise Elder
summary: The Prophet approached the Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a, but their leader Bayhara refused after demanding political power. An elderly wise man of the tribe later lamented that his people had missed a historic opportunity.
story: "Al-Zuhri related to me that he went to the Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a and called them to the path of God, offering himself to them. One of their men, named Bayhara b. Firas, replied to him, 'I swear, if I were to have this brave man of Quraysh, I could eat up the Arabs with him.' He then said to him, 'If we were to follow your orders and then God gave you victory against those opposing you, would we have power after you were gone?' "He replied, 'God controls power and places it where He wishes.' "Bayhara commented in reply, 'Are we to present our throats to the Arabs in your defence and then, if God gave you victory, see power go elsewhere than to us? We'll have nothing to do with you!' And so they refused him. "When the people there dispersed, the Banu 'Amir returned to a sheikh of theirs who, being elderly, was unable to attend the fairs with them. When they returned home they would tell him what had occurred at the fair. That year on their return he asked them who had been at the fair. They told him, 'A man of Quraysh, of the family of 'Abd al-Muttalib, came to us claiming to be a prophet and he asked us to defend him and aid him and take him back to our territory.' "The old man put his hand to his head and said, 'Could your mistake be put right? Can its consequences be reversed? I swear no descendant of Ishmael ever made such a claim falsely. It has to be true. Where did your good judgement go?'"
lessons: Placing worldly ambition and political gain above truth is one of the most common reasons people miss transformative opportunities.
Wisdom often resides with those who have lived long enough to see beyond immediate self-interest; their counsel deserves respect.
Regret over a missed spiritual opportunity can be profound — the wise elder's grief reminds us to not let ego block our recognition of truth.
practical_advice: When evaluating an important decision, ask yourself honestly whether your hesitation is rooted in truth-seeking or in personal ambition and fear of losing control.
Seek the counsel of wise, experienced elders in your community — they often see what pride and urgency cause others to miss.
If you recognize that you have missed a meaningful spiritual or ethical opportunity, do not sink into despair; seek God's forgiveness and remain alert to the next one.

title: The Prophet's Approach to Seeking Protection from Arab Tribes — His Humility and God's Plan
summary: Musa b. 'Uqba narrates how the Prophet humbly sought only protection from tribes — not forcing anyone — and how every rejection was part of God's plan to reserve honor for the Ansar of Medina.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba stated, quoting al-Zuhri, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) would for the period of those years, present himself to the Arab tribes at each fair, speaking with each tribal leader but asking them only for their protection and support. He would say, 'I don't wish to force any one of you to anything. Any of you who agree to what I ask may do so, but I would not compel anyone not so wishing. All I want is to guard myself against those wanting to kill me, so that I may fulfil my Lord's mission and carry out whatever decree He wishes regarding myself and those who support me.' But not one of them accepted him. Every one of those tribes reached the following conclusion: 'The man's own tribe knew him best; how could we accept as suitable for us someone who has subverted his tribe and whom they have expelled.' "This rejection too was due to the honour God had in store for the Ansar (the 'Helpers') of Medina."
lessons: God's plan works through apparent failures — every door that closed on the Prophet was divinely preserving the honor for those truly ready to receive it.
True integrity means never coercing others into belief or support, even when you desperately need help.
What looks like universal rejection from a human perspective can be the precise unfolding of a merciful divine design.
practical_advice: When every avenue seems closed, hold onto the belief that God may be redirecting you toward something far better than what you sought.
In your own efforts to win support or allies, never compromise your integrity by pressuring or manipulating others — sincerity attracts the right people in the right time.
Reflect on past experiences where what seemed like failure turned out to be redirection; use those memories to sustain trust in God's timing.

title: The Prophet Asks His Uncle Abbas for Help Approaching Tribes at the Fair
summary: The Prophet told his uncle Abbas he did not feel protected by him or his brother, and asked Abbas to accompany him to meet Arab tribes at the market gathering.
story: The hafiz Abu Nu'aym related through 'Abd Allah b. al-Ajlah and Yahya b. Sa'id al-Umawi, who both took their information from Muhammad b. al-Sa'ib al-Kalbi, from Abu Salih, from Ibn 'Abbas, from 'Abbas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told me, 'I don't consider myself receiving protection from you or from your brother. Would you take me to the market tomorrow for us to stay in the homes of some of the tribespeople?' It was the time for the gathering of the Arab tribes. "He (the Prophet (SAAS)) went on, 'These are Kinda and those who mix with them. They are the best of those from Yemen who make the pilgrimage. Those are the homes of Bakr b. Wa'il; those belong to the Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a. Choose for yourself.'"
lessons: Acknowledging vulnerability and asking for help is not weakness — even the Prophet openly expressed his need for support.
Trusting a family member to accompany you through difficulty reflects the human need for companionship in trials.
The Prophet's strategic awareness — knowing which tribes to approach — shows that relying on God does not mean abandoning wisdom and planning.
practical_advice: Do not suffer in silence when you need help; reach out to someone you trust and be honest about your situation, as the Prophet did with Abbas.
When facing a daunting task, break it into manageable steps and involve a trusted companion — shared effort lightens the burden.
Combine sincere trust in God with thoughtful preparation; tawakkul (reliance on God) is not passivity but active effort paired with faith.

title: The Prophet Invites the Tribe of Kinda at the Fair — Refused Again Over the Question of Power
summary: Accompanied by Abbas, the Prophet approached the tribe of Kinda at the fair, invited them to faith and prayer, but they refused after demanding political power be promised to them.
story: 'Abbas continued, "He began with Kinda, asking them, 'From whom are you?' 'From Yemen,' they replied. 'From which tribe?' he asked. 'From Kinda.' 'From which sub-tribe?' he next enquired. 'From the Banu 'Amr b. Musawiya,' they told him. "'Would you like to achieve good?' "'How would that be?' they asked. "'You would bear witness that there is no god but God, and would engage in prayer and believe in God's message.'" 'Abd Allah b. al-Ajlah quoted his father as having said, on the authority of the elders of his people, that Kinda replied to him (the Prophet (SAAS)), "If you are successful, will you grant us power after yourself?" The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Power rests with God; He places it where He wishes." They responded, "We don't need what you bring." Al-Kalbi went on to state, "And they (Kinda) said, 'have you come to us to keep us from our gods and have us go to war with the Arabs? Remain with your people. We have no need of you!'"
lessons: Those who approach faith with conditions rooted in worldly gain will consistently find reasons to refuse — the heart must seek truth for its own sake.
The Prophet's consistent answer — that power belongs to God alone — reflects a profound integrity that refused to barter divine authority for human support.
Simple, direct invitations to goodness ('Would you like to achieve good?') are often more powerful than elaborate arguments.
practical_advice: Examine your own spiritual motivations: are you drawn to faith and goodness for their intrinsic value, or are you attaching conditions of personal benefit?
When someone refuses what is genuinely good for them, accept their decision with grace and move on — your responsibility is to offer, not to compel.
Practice framing invitations to goodness simply and warmly, as the Prophet did, rather than with pressure or lengthy argument.

title: The Prophet Approaches the Banu Qays b. Tha'laba of Bakr b. Wa'il — They Cite Dependence on Persia
summary: The Prophet approached a subtribe of Bakr b. Wa'il and invited them, but they declined, citing their proximity to Persia and their inability to offer him protection.
story: "He then left them and went to Bakr b. Wa'il. He asked, 'Who are these people?' 'They are part of Bakr b. Wa'il.' 'From which sub-tribe?' he asked. He was told, 'From the Banu Qays b. Tha'laba.' "'How many are they?' he asked. 'Very numerous,' he was told. "'How would their protection be?' he asked. "They replied, 'We border on Persia; we have no protection from them or for them.' "He told them, 'Grant God custody over yourselves and He will keep you safe until you descend upon their homes, marry their women and enslave their children. Then give praise to God three and thirty times, then give thanks to Him three and thirty times, and say He is most great four and thirty times.' "'Who are you?' they asked. "'I am the Messenger of God,' he replied. Then he left."
lessons: Perceived dependence on worldly powers can prevent people from placing their trust in God — yet the Prophet's promise showed that divine protection surpasses all earthly alliances.
The Prophet's teaching of dhikr (remembrance of God) even in the context of a refusal shows his constant care for people's spiritual wellbeing regardless of their response.
Fear of powerful neighbors or external forces is a real human experience, but faith invites us to reframe where our ultimate security lies.
practical_advice: Identify any 'Persia' in your own life — an external power, person, or circumstance that you fear and that prevents you from fully trusting God — and consciously practice surrendering that fear.
Incorporate the dhikr the Prophet prescribed (33 times subhanallah, 33 times alhamdulillah, 34 times Allahu akbar) into your daily routine as a grounding practice.
When someone cannot help you due to their own constraints and fears, accept their limitation with understanding and move forward without bitterness.

title: Abu Lahab Follows the Prophet Among the Tribes and Discredits Him, Calling Him Crazy
summary: As the Prophet moved between tribes at the fair, Abu Lahab followed behind him telling people not to accept him and calling him crazy, even after the Prophet had left a group.
story: "When he had left," al-Kalbi said, "his uncle Abu Lahab had been following him and telling people, 'Do not accept what he says.' When he passed by them, they asked, 'Do you know this man?' He replied, 'Yes, he's from our elite. What about him makes you ask?' They told him what he had offered them, saying, 'He claims to be the Messenger of God.' Abu Lahab commented, 'Take no account of whatever he says. He's crazy, and talks off the top of his head.' "'We saw that,' they commented, 'when he spoke as he did about Persia!'"
lessons: Those who undermine truth-bearers often use the label of madness or irrationality — this pattern has repeated throughout prophetic history.
Abu Lahab's persistence in following and discrediting the Prophet shows how deep-seated enmity can become an obsession that ultimately harms the one who harbors it.
The response of the tribespeople — finding confirmation of Abu Lahab's mockery in the Prophet's words about Persia — shows how bias filters perception of truth.
practical_advice: When others label your sincere convictions as foolishness or irrationality, take strength from the fact that this was the same accusation made against every prophet.
Do not allow the persistent negativity of a detractor to define how you see yourself or your mission; focus on those who listen with open hearts.
Notice if you yourself ever dismiss someone's ideas by labeling them as 'crazy' — this can be a defense mechanism that prevents genuine engagement with truth.

title: The Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a Offer the Prophet Protection Without Belief — Bayhara's Intervention
summary: A subtribe of Banu 'Amir initially agreed to protect the Prophet without believing in him, but Bayhara b. Firas intervened, warned them they would alienate all Arabs, and told the Prophet to leave.
story: Al-Kalbi stated, "'Abd al-Rahman al-'Amiri told me that elders of his tribe said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to us when we were at the 'Ukaz fair. He asked, "From whom are these people?" We replied, "From the Banu 'Amir b. Sa'sa'a." "From which sub-tribe?" he asked. "We responded, "From the Banu Ka'b b. Rabi'a." "He asked, "How would protection be with you?" "We replied, "The best imaginable; we are invincible." "He stated to them, "I am the Messenger of God, and I am coming to you to protect me until I fulfil the mission of my Lord. I would not force any of you to anything." "They asked him, "From which part of Quraysh are you?" ""From the family of 'Abd al-Muttalib," he replied. ""What are your relations with the 'Abd Manaf?" they asked. ""They were the first to deny and to drive me away," he told them. ""Well, we will neither drive you away nor believe in you; but we will protect you until you fulfil your Lord's mission." "So he took residence with them while they were attending the fair. And then Bayhara b. Firas al-Qushayri came to them and said, "Who is this man I now see with you; I don't know him?" ""He is Muhammad, son of 'Abd Allah, of Quraysh," they replied. ""And what do you have to do with him?" he asked. ""He claims to be the Messenger of God and he has asked us to protect him until he fulfils his Lord's mission," they replied. ""And how did you respond?" he enquired. ""We made him very welcome," they told him. "We said we would take him to our own territory and protect him as we do ourselves." "Bayhara commented, "I know of no one at this fair going back home with anything worse than you are. You have begun a policy that will alienate people; the Arabs will attack you in unison. His people know him very well; if they had perceived good in him, they would have been most delighted with him. Will you support a man cast out and denied by his own people and give him shelter and aid? Your policy is dreadful." "He then approached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and told him, "Get up and join your people. I swear if you weren't here among my kin, I would...""
lessons: Fear of social and political consequences — 'the Arabs will attack you in unison' — is one of the most powerful forces that prevents people from supporting truth.
The argument 'his own people rejected him' has been used throughout history to discredit truth-bearers, yet it is a logical fallacy: proximity does not confer wisdom.
Even when support is conditional or incomplete, the Prophet accepted what was offered without complaint — showing gracious receipt of partial goodwill.
practical_advice: When you hesitate to stand by someone or something true because of fear of social consequences, ask yourself whether you are letting the crowd's reaction override your own conscience.
Be cautious of those who argue 'if it were truly good, others would have accepted it' — this reasoning is used to suppress truth in every era; evaluate claims on their own merit.
When others offer you partial support or limited help, receive it with gratitude rather than frustration at what they cannot give.

title: Abu Bakr Questions the Rabi'a Tribe and Is Challenged by a Young Bedouin
summary: As the Prophet sought support from Arab tribes at Mina, Abu Bakr attempted to engage a gathering of the Rabi'a tribe in conversation about lineage, but a sharp-tongued young bedouin named Daghfal turned the questioning back on him and nearly reduced him to the lowest rank of Quraysh before the Prophet arrived smiling.
story: "Abu Bakr, God bless him, went forward and made his greetings. He was in the very vanguard of good, and an expert in genealogy. He asked, 'From whom do you people come?' 'From Rabi'a,' came their reply. "'From which Rabi'a are you, from its mainstream or from a branch?' "'From its greatest mainstream.' "Abu Bakr asked them, 'Is 'Awf of you, of whom it was said, "There is no harm, no heat, in the 'Awf valley?"' "'No,' they told him. "'Do Bishr b. Qays Ahu al-Liwa' and Muntaha al-'Atiya belong to your tribe?' "'No,' they replied. "'Is al-Hawfazan b. Shurayk, the killer of kings and robber of their souls, a kinsman of yours?' "'No,' they replied. "'Is Jassas b. Murra b. Dhuhl, the protector of honour and defender of the neighbour, from you?' "'No,' they said. "'Is al-Muzdalif, he of the unique turban, from you?' "'No,' they replied. "'Are you related to the kings of Kinda?' he asked. "'No,' they replied. "'Are you related to the kings of Lakhm?' he asked. "'No,' they replied. "Abu Bakr, God bless him, then commented, 'So you're not from its mainstream, but from a branch.' "At that a youth named Daghfal b. Hanzala al-Dhuhli, his beard beginning to sprout, jumped up and grabbed the bridle of Abu Bakr's camel, reciting, 'Those who ask of us will be asked of; as for the burden (of proof) we neither know it nor bear it (as a responsibility).' "He also commented, 'Hey, you, you asked and we replied, hiding nothing from you. We want to ask you something; who are you?' "He replied, 'A man of Quraysh.' "The youth commented, 'Well said!
