The disapproval I have seen in you has somewhat disturbed me." "'So off we went, asking the whereabouts of the Messenger of God (SAAS). We had not seen him prior to that. We met a Meccan and asked after him, and he replied, "Do you know him?" "No," we replied. "Then do you know alLCAbhasb. al-Muccalih, his uncle?" "Yes," we told him, for we did know al-'Abb~. He often came to us on business trips. The Meccan told us, "If you go to the mosque, he will he sitting next to al-'Ahh~s." "'So we went in the mosque and there was al-'Ahbas sitting with the Messenger of God (SAAS) by his side. We made greetings then sat down facing him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked al-'Abb~, "Do you know these two men, Aha al-Fadl?" He replied, "Yes; this is al-Bar2 h. Macmnr, his people's leader, and this is Ka'b b. Mdik."'" The account proceeds, "'I swear, I'll never forget what the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said: "You mean the poet?" "Yes," he replied. "'Then al-Bar2 b. Ma'mnr addressed him, "0 prophet of God, I have come on this journey having been given guidance to Islam by God Almighty. I had the idea of not facing my hack to this building, so I prayed towards it. My friends opposed me in this, causing me concern. What do you think?" "'"You already had a qibla, a direction for prayer, and should have kept to it," he replied. "'And so al-Bars' reverted to the prayer direction adopted by the Messenger of God (SAAS), and so prayed along with us towards Syria.'" The account concludes, "But his family claim that he continued his prayers towards the ka'ba until he died. But it was not as they said; we know better than they about it."
lessons: Sincere intention does not always equal correct action — al-Bara's love for the Kaaba was genuine and honourable, yet the Prophet gently redirected him, showing that good intentions must be paired with proper knowledge and guidance.

When we act on our personal understanding and later feel uncertain, the courageous response is to bring that uncertainty directly to a trusted source of guidance rather than double down or stay silent; al-Bara's willingness to be corrected is itself an act of spiritual courage.

The companions around us serve as a check on our individual reasoning — al-Bara's companions' quiet disagreement was not hostility but sincere care, and eventually it prompted the very conversation that brought clarity.
practical_advice: When you feel strongly pulled toward a personal religious or ethical instinct that differs from established guidance, take time to consult a knowledgeable and trustworthy scholar or mentor before acting — personal feeling and correct practice both have their place.

If you have been doing something you are uncertain about and the unease is growing, do what al-Bara did: name the concern openly to the appropriate person rather than letting it quietly disturb you. Bringing a worry into the open is often the fastest way to resolve it.

Practice holding your personal convictions firmly while remaining genuinely open to correction. Before any important decision, ask yourself: 'Am I willing to be told I am wrong about this, and to change course if I am?'

title: The Ansar Invite Abd Allah ibn Amr to Islam Before the Second Pledge
summary: On the night of the second pledge at al-Aqaba, the Medinan Muslims approached their own leader Abd Allah ibn Amr abu Jabir, warned him plainly about the consequences of remaining on his old ways, invited him to Islam, and he accepted, later becoming a leader of the new community.
story: Ka'b h. Mdik stated, "We then left on the pilgrimage, having made an agreement to meet the Messenger of God (SAAS) at d-'Aqaba in the middle of the tashriq period. When we had finished the pilgrimage the night arrived for which we had an appointment with the Messenger of God (SAAS). We had kept our purpose unknown to those of our people who were polytheists, hut there with us was 'Ahd Allah b. 'Amr b. Haran Abii Jabir, one of our leaders. T o him we spoke, telling him, 'Aha Jzhir, you are one of our noble leaders, and we would l i e you to give up your practices; otherwise one day you will end up as fuel for hell-fire.' We then invited him into Islam, and told him of our rendezvous with the Messenger of God (SAAS) at alLCAqaba.He did accept Islam, was present at d-'Aqaba and became a naqib, a leader."
lessons: True concern for those we love requires honest speech even when it is uncomfortable — the Ansar did not soften the warning to Abu Jabir out of politeness, yet they delivered it with care because they valued him and wanted him to flourish.

Inviting someone to goodness is itself an act of leadership; the Ansar did not wait for Abu Jabir to find his own way but actively brought the invitation to him, modelling that those who have received guidance carry a responsibility to share it.

Accepting a sincere invitation from trusted companions, even on a momentous night, can change the entire trajectory of a life — Abu Jabir went from polytheist leader to naqib of the new community because he listened and said yes.
practical_advice: If someone you care about is on a path you genuinely believe will harm them, find a moment to speak honestly and with warmth — not to judge, but because their wellbeing matters to you. Prepare what you want to say, then say it.

When a trusted friend or community member brings you a serious concern about your choices, resist the impulse to be defensive. Ask yourself whether their concern comes from care, and if it does, give it real weight.

Look around your immediate circle: is there someone who has not yet been included in something good that you are part of — a community, a support group, a healthy habit? Make a specific plan to extend the invitation this week.

title: The Prophet's Ten Years of Seeking Refuge and the Second Pledge of Al-'Aqaba
summary: After ten years of being rejected while seeking protection and support for his mission, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) finally found refuge when 70-73 people from Yathrib came during the pilgrimage season and pledged to defend him as they would their own families.
story: The Im%n Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Razzaq related to us, quoting Macmar, from Ihn Khuthaym, from Aba al-Zuhayr, that Jabir said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained for ten years in Mecca following people to their homes, going to 'Ukaz and Majanna during the festivals asking, "Who will give me refuge? Whoever will help me until I can fulfil the mission of my Lord will attain paradise." But he found no one to shelter or aid him. So much so that someone from Yemen or the Mudar would appear and respond favourably to this plea, only to have his relatives and friends tell him, "Watch out that that man of Quraysh doesn't corrupt you!" And if the Messenger of God (SAAS) were to pass through their encampments, they would point their fingers at him. "'This went on until God sent us from Yathrib (Medina) to him and we gave him refuge and believed in him. Our people would go off, believe in him, have the Qur'an recited to them and then return to their own folk who would then accept Islam as they had. Eventually no ansar home was devoid of Muslims displaying their faith in Islam. "'At last we all consulted together and asked ourselves how long we were going to leave the Messenger of God (SAAS) doing his rounds and being harassed in the Meccan mountains in a state of fear. "'So 70 of us men went to see him during the pilgrimage season. We made a rendezvous with him at the defile at al-'Aqaba, going there in ones and twos until all were present. Then we asked him, "O Messenger of God what do you wish us to pledge to you?" "'He replied, "You must pledge to hear and obey at times of both action and inaction, to give whether times are hard or easy, and to advocate goodness and prohibit evil. You must speak out for God and not fear any blame for supporting God. You must help and defend me if I come to you in the same ways you help and defend yourselves, your wives and your children. You will then attain paradise." And so we stood up before him and pledged allegiance. As'ad b. Zurara took him by the hand, he being one of the youngest there.'" In the account of al-Bayhaqi, the text reads, "He was the youngest of the 70 except for myself. He (As'ad) said, 'Slowly now, people of Yathrib! We only hurried here because we know that he is the Messenger of God. Bringing him out now, however, would be a provocation to all the Arabs that would cause you to lose your elite and would box you in with swords raised against you. If you are able to withstand that, then adopt him and it will be up to God to reward you. But if you are a people who have great fear for yourselves, then leave him and make that fact plain; that course would be more forgivable in God's sight.' "'Keep away from us, As'ad,' (they replied). 'We won't renege on this pledge. We will never deny it.'" He continued, "So we all stood and pledged to him. He made us make promises and assured us paradise in return."
lessons: Perseverance in the face of prolonged rejection is itself a form of worship; the Prophet's ten years of seeking shelter without success did not cause him to abandon his mission, teaching us that divine help has its appointed time.

True community is built on mutual commitment and shared sacrifice — the Ansar's pledge to defend the Prophet as they would their own families models the depth of loyalty that transforms individuals into an ummah.

Courage in commitment means not letting fear of consequences dissolve a righteous pledge once made; when As'ad urged caution, the people of Yathrib responded with resolve, showing that informed commitment is stronger than impulsive enthusiasm.
practical_advice: When you feel persistently rejected or unsupported in a worthy endeavor, remind yourself that the right help often comes from unexpected directions and at an appointed time — continue showing up and making your need known.

When making a serious commitment to support someone or a cause, think through the costs clearly beforehand as As'ad did, so that your pledge is made with eyes open and is therefore durable under pressure.

In moments of communal discouragement, take initiative to ask the honest question — 'How long will we leave things as they are?' — as the Ansar did, because named problems are the first step toward collective action.

title: Al-'Abbas Speaks at Al-'Aqaba and the Prophet's Covenant of Mutual Protection
summary: At the secret night meeting at al-'Aqaba, al-'Abbas, though not yet Muslim, spoke first to ensure the Ansar fully understood their responsibility, and the Prophet then delivered his terms of the pledge including the promise of mutual solidarity in blood and peace.
story: Ka'b b. Malik stated, "When we met at the defile we waited and the Messenger of God (SAAS) did come, accompanied by al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib. At that time al-'Abbas was still following his people's religion, although he was keeping track of his nephew's affairs and watching over him. "When they sat down, the first to speak was al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, who said, 'O Khazraj' — the Arabs used to know the ansar as Khazraj, whether they were Khazraj or Aws — 'Muhammad holds with us a position of which you are aware. We protect him from our people who think about him as we do. He is respected among his people and safe in his own town. But he is determined to join up with you. If you think you will keep trust with him in the invitation you have given him and will protect him from his opponents, then it's up to you to accept your responsibilities. But if you think you might deliver him over and abandon him after he has joined you, then leave him right now. He does have respect and protection among his own people and in his town.' "We replied, 'We hear what you say. Speak to us, O Messenger of God, and take for yourself and for your Lord whatever you want.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then spoke, recited the Qur'an, invited people to God and acclaimed Islam. He said, 'I ask you to pledge that you will defend me as you do your women and children.' "Al-Bara' b. Ma'mur then took him by the hand and said, 'Yes indeed; we will, I swear by Him who sent you with the truth, protect you as we do our women from whatever threatens them. We pledge ourselves to you, O Messenger of God, and we are, I swear it, warriors from father to son over many generations.' "While al-Bara' was speaking to the Messenger of God (SAAS) he was interrupted by Abu al-Haytham b. al-Tayyihan, who said, 'O Messenger of God, we have certain ties to others' — meaning the Jews — 'and if we break these, we are concerned that if God gives you victory, you might return to your own people and abandon us.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) smiled at this and said, 'If your blood be sought, our blood shall be sought, and your destruction is mine as well. I am of you and you are of me. I will battle those you battle and make peace with those with whom you make peace.'" Ka'b b. Malik went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Bring forth twelve from among you to be leaders to take charge of their people's affairs.' They selected twelve men, nine from al-Khazraj, three from Aws."
lessons: Genuine concern for someone's welfare sometimes means honestly naming the risks of helping them, as al-'Abbas did — real care protects people from uninformed commitments they cannot sustain.

Voicing a legitimate fear openly, as Abu al-Haytham did about the Jews, is not a sign of weakness but of sincerity; the Prophet's warm smile and reassurance showed that honest anxiety deserves a compassionate answer, not dismissal.
The words 'I am of you and you are of me' encapsulate the foundation of all healthy relationships — reciprocal belonging, where the fate of one is genuinely tied to the fate of the other.
practical_advice: Before entering a significant commitment, name your fears clearly to the other party rather than suppressing them; a relationship strong enough to address your anxieties openly is one strong enough to last.

When someone expresses vulnerability or doubt about whether you will abandon them, respond with specific, concrete words of solidarity rather than vague reassurances — the Prophet named blood, battle, and peace to make the bond tangible.

If you are in a leadership role, establish trusted representatives within each group under your care as the Prophet appointed twelve naqibs, so that every community has someone accountable to its particular needs.

title: The Second Pledge of Al-Aqaba: The Twelve Leaders
summary: Seventy men from Madinah met the Prophet at Al-Aqaba and pledged allegiance, with twelve leaders appointed among them. Gabriel guided the Prophet in selecting these leaders, who committed to supporting and protecting him as the disciples supported Jesus.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Aba Bakr related to me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to these leaders, 'You shall be those entrusted for your people just as the apostles were for Jesus, son of Mary. I shall be entrusted with my own people.' They replied, 'So be it.'"
Ibn 'Umar b. Qatada related to us that when this group met to pledge allegiance to the Messenger of God (SAAS), al-'Abbas b. 'Ubada b. Nadla al-Ansari, of the Banu Salim b. 'Awf, said, 'O Khazraj, do you know what it is you are pledging this man?' 'Yes,' they replied.
"He continued, 'You are pledging to go to war against all kinds of people. If you think that if you suffered great losses to your wealth and had your leaders killed, you would give him up, then do it now or suffer the punishment of this life and the next. If you think you will keep faith with him in what he has called upon you to do, despite loss of wealth and your leaders being killed, then do accept him. For he is, by God, the best in this world and the next.'
"They replied, 'We will take him regardless of loss of wealth or the death of our leaders. But what will we receive in return for this, O Messenger of God, if we keep faith with you?'
"'Paradise,' he replied.
"'Then hold out your hand,' they asked.
"He did so and they pledged allegiance to him."
lessons: True commitment requires honest acknowledgment of the sacrifices involved, as al-Abbas demonstrated by laying out the full cost before the pledge was made.
Trusting in divine promise — paradise — can empower people to endure tremendous worldly loss and hardship.
Leadership and responsibility are sacred trusts; the Prophet compared his leaders to the disciples of Jesus, emphasizing the weight of the role.
practical_advice: When making a major life commitment, honestly assess the challenges ahead rather than minimizing them, so your resolve is built on reality.
In moments of fear or doubt about what you may lose, reconnect with the higher purpose or reward you are working toward.
If you are in a position of leadership, take your responsibilities seriously as a trust given to you by those who depend on you.

title: Al-Abbas b. Ubada's Warning Before the Pledge
summary: Before the pledge of Al-Aqaba was completed, al-Abbas b. Ubada addressed the Ansar and challenged them to fully understand what they were committing to, warning them of war against all peoples.
story:
Ibn 'Umar b. Qatada related to us that when this group met to pledge allegiance to the Messenger of God (SAAS), al-'Abbas b. 'Ubada b. Nadla al-Ansari, of the Banu Salim b. 'Awf, said, 'O Khazraj, do you know what it is you are pledging this man?' 'Yes,' they replied.
"He continued, 'You are pledging to go to war against all kinds of people. If you think that if you suffered great losses to your wealth and had your leaders killed, you would give him up, then do it now or suffer the punishment of this life and the next. If you think you will keep faith with him in what he has called upon you to do, despite loss of wealth and your leaders being killed, then do accept him. For he is, by God, the best in this world and the next.'
"They replied, 'We will take him regardless of loss of wealth or the death of our leaders. But what will we receive in return for this, O Messenger of God, if we keep faith with you?'
"'Paradise,' he replied.
"'Then hold out your hand,' they asked.
"He did so and they pledged allegiance to him."
"Al-'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada commented, 'Al-'Abbas b. 'Ubada only said this to emphasize the pact they were making.'
"'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr claimed that al-'Abbas had only said that to delay the pledge of allegiance that night in the hope that 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul, leader of the Khazraj would attend so that the action of the group would be even stronger. God knows best which of these is true."
lessons: Informed consent and full awareness of consequences make any commitment more genuine and enduring.
True courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to proceed despite knowing the full weight of the cost.
Community decisions become stronger when leaders speak honestly rather than concealing difficulties.
practical_advice: Before making an important decision or commitment, seek out the full picture including potential hardships, so your choice is grounded and lasting.
When you feel pressure to commit to something significant, give yourself space to pause and reflect on whether you are truly ready.
Encourage honesty in group settings by welcoming those who raise difficult truths rather than silencing them.

title: The Terms of the Pledge of Al-Aqaba
summary: The Prophet outlined the terms of the second pledge of Al-Aqaba, asking the Ansar to worship God alone, give him refuge, and help protect him, in exchange for which he promised them paradise.
story:
Al-Bayhaqi stated, "Abu Tahir Muhammad b. Muhammad b. Muhammad b. Muhmish narrated to us from Muhammad b. Ibrahim b. al-Fadl al-Fahhan from Muhammad b. Yahya al-Dhuhli from 'Amr b. 'Uthman al-Raqqi, as follows: 'Zuhayr related to us, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Uthman b. Khuthaym, from Isma'il b. 'Ubayd Allah b. Rifa'a, from his father, who said, "Skins of wine were brought out, but 'Ubada b. al-Samit went up to them and pierced them open, saying, 'We have pledged to the Messenger of God (SAAS) that we will listen and obey, in action and at rest, expend our wealth in good times and bad, enjoin good and forbid evil, express our faith in God without fear of reproach, and assist the Messenger of God (SAAS) if he comes to Yathrib with the same protection we give ourselves and our children in body and soul. And we shall have paradise."'
"'This was the pledge we made to the Messenger of God (SAAS).'"'
Yunus stated, from Ibn Ishaq, "'Ubada b. al-Walid b. 'Ubada b. al-Samit related to me from his father, from his grandfather 'Ubada b. al-Samit, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) had us pledge to him that we would fight, that we would listen and obey in good and bad times alike, regardless of the incentives or disincentives or pressure upon us, that we would not dispute among ourselves, that we would speak the truth wherever we were and hear no reproach in our worship of God.'"
lessons: Commitment to God includes both personal worship and communal responsibility — the pledge covered worship, obedience, generosity, and moral courage.
Speaking truth without fear of reproach is a hallmark of true faith and inner strength.
Unity and the avoidance of internal dispute are foundational pillars of a strong community.
practical_advice: Reflect on your own commitments to your faith community — are you contributing in good times only, or also in hardship?
Practice speaking truthfully even in situations where it may be socially uncomfortable or costly.
When tensions arise in your community, remember the pledge to avoid dispute and seek reconciliation actively.

title: Satan's Cry at the Pledge of Al-Aqaba
summary: At the moment the pledge of Al-Aqaba was made, Satan cried out from the top of the mountain pass in a piercing voice, attempting to frighten and expose the gathering, but the Prophet rebuked him firmly.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated in his account from Ma'bad b. Ka'b, from his brother 'Abd Allah b. Ka'b b. Malik, who said, "When we pledged ourselves to the Messenger of God (SAAS) Satan called out from the top of the defile in the most piercing voice I have ever heard. He said, 'O people of the jabajib' — the word meaning 'houses' — are you going to support this reprehensible man and the fools with him who have tried to make war upon you?'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'That was Azabb of al-'Aqaba. He's the son of Azyab.' Ibn Hisham suggested that the name given was 'son of Uzayb'.
"'Do you hear, O enemy of God,' he went on, 'I swear I will destroy you!'
"Then he told them, 'Return to your caravans.'"
lessons: Evil forces seek to disrupt righteous commitments by sowing fear and casting doubt at the most critical moments.
The Prophet's immediate and fearless response to Satan's cry demonstrates that reliance on God removes fear of the enemy's tactics.
Recognizing the source of discouragement — whether internal whispers or external opposition — is the first step to overcoming it.
practical_advice: When you feel sudden doubt or fear at the moment of making a good decision, recognize it as a potential whisper of the ego or Satan and reaffirm your intention.
Seek the company of a spiritually grounded person or mentor when making important commitments, as the Prophet's presence steadied the Ansar.
After completing a significant pledge or decision, return to your normal responsibilities calmly, as the Prophet instructed the Ansar to return to their caravans.

title: Al-Abbas b. Ubada's Offer to Attack Mina with Swords
summary: After the pledge at Al-Aqaba, al-Abbas b. Ubada offered to attack the Quraysh at Mina with swords the next morning, but the Prophet declined, saying they had not yet been commanded to fight.
story:
The account continues, "Al-'Abbas b. 'Ubada b. Nadla said, 'O Messenger of God by Him who sent you with the truth, if you wish tomorrow we will fall on those at Mina with our swords!'
"The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'We have not been ordered to do that; however, do go back to your caravans.'
"And so we returned to our beds and slept in them until morning."
lessons: Righteous anger must be governed by divine guidance and proper timing, not by emotion alone.
The Prophet's discipline in restraining his companions despite provocation shows that strategic patience is a form of strength.
Acting outside of one's sanctioned boundaries, even with good intentions, can lead to harm; obedience to divine command is paramount.
practical_advice: When you feel a strong urge to react aggressively to injustice, pause and ask whether your action is guided or merely emotional.
Trust in the wisdom of timing — sometimes the right response to oppression is patient endurance while building strength.
Channel feelings of anger at injustice into constructive preparation rather than impulsive action.

title: Quraysh Confronts the Ansar the Morning After the Pledge
summary: The morning after the secret pledge at Al-Aqaba, leaders of the Quraysh came to the Ansar's camp and accused them of plotting with the Prophet. The polytheist members of the Khazraj denied knowledge of any pledge, which was truthful, while the Muslim Ansar exchanged anxious glances.
story:
"Next morning leaders of Quraysh came to us in our camps and said, 'O Khazraj, we have learned that you have come to this man of ours asking him to leave us and pledging to fight against us. We swear that there is no Arab group we would like less to do battle with than you.'
"Those of our people who were polytheists promptly swore that they had no knowledge of any such thing.
"And they spoke the truth; they knew nothing. We, meanwhile, were exchanging glances.
"Then Quraysh arose; they included al-Harith b. Hisham b. al-Mughira al-Makhzumi who was wearing new sandals. I spoke to him as if I wanted to associate the entire group with what the polytheists had said. Then I said (changing the subject) 'Abu Jabir, seeing that you are one of our chiefs, can't you get sandals like those of that young Quraysh fellow?'
"Al-Harith heard this, took off his sandals and hurled them at me, saying, 'By God, you can put them on!'
"Abu Jabir replied, 'Now easy there; you've annoyed the lad; give him back his sandals.'
"I replied, 'I swear, I'll not return them. They're a good omen; and if it proves true, I'll keep the sandals!'"
lessons: In moments of danger, composure and quick thinking can protect both yourself and your community from exposure.
Not every confrontation needs to be met head-on; wisdom sometimes means deflecting attention with calm and even humor.
The anxiety of carrying a secret righteous commitment is part of the test of faith — inner steadiness in such moments is a spiritual achievement.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a tense situation where revealing the truth could cause harm, look for ways to redirect the conversation calmly and honestly.
Practice composure under pressure by reflecting on moments when patience and calm thought led to better outcomes than reactive speech.
Find small ways to bring lightness or normalcy into high-stakes situations — it can ease your own anxiety and protect those around you.

title: Sa'd ibn 'Ubada Captured and Rescued in Mecca
summary: After the second pledge of al-'Aqaba, Quraysh pursued the Medinan pilgrims and captured Sa'd ibn 'Ubada, beating him and leading him through Mecca by his hair until two men he had previously protected intervened and secured his release.
story: The pilgrims then left Minx and some of them investigated the report further, finding it to have happened. They set about pursuing our people and caught up with Sa'd b. Wbnda at Adhkhir, along with al-Mundhi b. 'Amr, a brother from the Bana Sxcida b. Ka'b b. al-Khazraj; both of these were 'leaders' (i.e. appointed at al-'Aqaba, tr.). Al-Mundhir evaded them but they captured Sa'd h. Wbnda and tied his hands behind his neck with the cords holding his saddle and led him into Mecca, beating him and pulling him along by his hair which was very full. Sa'd reported, 'So there I was in their hands when up came a group of Quraysh among whom was one tall, handsome, fair-skinned man. I told myself, "If there is any good in any of their men, it has to be him." But when he drew near he raised his hand and struck me hard. I told myself, "Well, I swear, after this there's no good in them at all!" Well, there I was in their hands, being pulled along, when one of their men came up to me and said, "Too bad! Don't you have any pact or protection agreement with any Quraysh man?" I replied, "Yes, indeed I do. I used to give protection to Jubayr b. Mut'im and his merchants on business trips and prevent those of my people who wanted to harm them. And also I protected al-Harith h. Harb h. Umayya h. 'Ahd Shams." He commented, "Cry out the names of the two men and tell of your connection to them." So I did this and that man went off to look for those two merchants. He found them in the ka'ba and told them, "There's a man of Khazraj now out there in the valley being beaten and calling out for both of you." They asked, "Who is he?" "He is Sa'd b. Wbada," he told them. "Well," they said, "he has spoken the truth. He did protect our merchants from some in his country who wanted to harm them." The two men then went and released Sa'd from his attackers, and he fled. The man who struck Sa'd was Suhayl h. 'Amr. Ibn Hishxm stated, "The man who gave him refuge was Aba al-Bakhtari b. Hisham."
lessons: Acts of kindness and fair dealing, even toward those who may later become opponents, can return to protect you in moments of crisis.
Appearances can be deeply deceiving — outward beauty or status is no indicator of moral character or justice.
God arranges provisions for His servants through unexpected means; Sa'd's past generosity became his pathway to rescue.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult situation, reflect on the good you have done for others — those connections may become your support network in times of need.
Do not judge others' character by their appearance or social standing; look instead at their actions and treatment of the vulnerable.
Practice consistent goodness and fair dealing in your community now, knowing that such investments of character often protect you in future adversity.

title: The Mysterious Voice on Mount Abu Qubays Foretelling Safety
summary: After the pledge of al-'Aqaba, Quraysh heard mysterious voices in the night on Mount Abu Qubays foretelling the safety of the two Sa'ds and the security of the Prophet in Mecca, which Abu Sufyan eventually identified as referring to Sa'd ibn Mu'adh and Sa'd ibn 'Ubada.
story: Al-Bayhaqi related with a chain of authorities from Tsa h. Aba b. Juhayr, who said, 'Quraysh heard someone speak out in the night on the mountain of Abii Qubays, "If Sacdm (i.e. "two Sags", tr.) be safe, then Muhammad should not fear any opposition in Mecca." Next morning, Ahii Sufym asked, "Who is al-Sa'dm? As'ad b. Bakr or Sa'd b. Hudhaym?" The second night they heard someone reciting, "O Sa'd, Sa'd of al-Aws, may you be victorious; and you, O Sa'd, Sa'd of Khazraj, a hero. Answer to him who calls for guidance and beseech God for paradise as those who know. For God's reward to Him who seeks guidance is gardens of paradise where birds fly." Next morning Abii Sufyan said, "I swear, it has to be Sa'd b. Muqdh and Sa'd b. Wbada!"'
lessons: Divine support for the truth operates through means beyond ordinary human understanding, reassuring believers that they are not alone.
The cause of truth finds its champions even when those champions are not yet fully aware of the role they will play.
Those who oppose the truth may be confused and unsettled by signs they cannot explain, while believers can find comfort in such signs.
practical_advice: When you feel uncertain about whether you are on the right path, look for signs of divine support in your circumstances and reflect on them with gratitude.
Trust that your commitment to truth and guidance connects you to something greater than yourself, even when you cannot see the full picture.
In moments of fear or opposition, remind yourself that history is full of examples where divine help arrived in unexpected forms.

title: The Young Muslims Secretly Disposing of 'Amr ibn al-Jamah's Idol
summary: Young Muslim men of the Banu Salama repeatedly removed and humiliated their elder 'Amr ibn al-Jamah's wooden idol Manaf by throwing it into cesspools, until 'Amr finally reflected and accepted Islam upon finding it upside down tied to a dead dog.
story: Among their people there were still a few elders persisting in their polytheistic beliefs. One of these was 'Amr b. al-Jamah b. Zayd b. H a r m b. Ka'b b. Ghanm b. Ka'b b. Salama. His son Musdh b. 'Am was one of those who were present at al-'Aqaba. 'Amr b. al-Jamah was a leader of the Banii Salama. He had a wooden idol, named Manst, in his home; this was a practice common among those chieftains. They would treat these idols as gods and would venerate them and show them off. When the young men of the Banii Salama, Musdh, and Musdh h. Jabal accepted Islam, they would sneak in at night to that idol of 'Amr, carry it out and toss it head first into pits used to contain the excrement of the Banii Salama. Next morning 'Amr would ask, 'Who could have attacked our god tonight?' He would then look around for it and when he found it he washed it, purified it and scented it. Then he would say, 'I swear, if I knew who did that to you I would put him to shame!' When 'Amr slept at night they would again seize it and do as before. 'Amr again found it harmed as before and he would wash, purify and scent it. That night they returned and did the same, as he did. But this time he brought his sword and attached it to the idol saying, 'I swear I do not know who is doing this to you, but if there is any good in you, then defend yourself! You have this sword now.' That night while 'Amr slept they returned to the idol, took the sword off its neck and then replaced it with a dead dog they attached to it. Then they threw it into one of the cesspools of the Banu Salama. Next day 'Amr b. al-Jamah did not see it in its place, so he went out to find it and did so in the cesspool, upside down and with the dead dog attached. When he saw it in this state he reflected upon his state and one of his people who had accepted Islam went and spoke to him, and, by God's mercy, he accepted Islam himself and remained a good Muslim. When he accepted Islam and knew what he now did of God, he reflected on his own state and what he had seen happen to the idol. He thanked God who had saved him from his former blindness and error and spoke the following verses, 'By God, if you had been a god, you would not have been hurled into a well tied to a dog! Yekh! for treating you as a god; now we have assessed you and (turned from) evil ways, Praise be to God the Almighty, the Benevolent, the Giver, the Provider, the establisher of the religions, He it was saved me before I was encased in the darkness of a grave.'
lessons: Sometimes it takes a dramatic confrontation with the absurdity of false beliefs to open a person's heart to the truth.
Guidance is ultimately from God alone — human effort plants seeds, but the transformation of the heart is a divine gift.
A person's sincere reflection on the nature of what they worship or trust can be the turning point toward a life of true faith and gratitude.
practical_advice: If you find yourself clinging to habits, beliefs, or coping mechanisms that have repeatedly failed you, take time to honestly reflect on whether they are truly helping you.
When someone you care about seems resistant to change, continue showing them care and gently sharing truth — transformation often comes gradually through repeated gentle exposure.
Express gratitude when you recognize you have been saved from a harmful path, as this gratitude deepens your commitment to the better path you have chosen.

title: The Martyrdom of Habib, Son of Umm Umara, at the Hands of Musaylima
summary: Habib, son of the brave companion Umm Umara, was captured by the false prophet Musaylima and executed limb by limb after refusing to testify to Musaylima's prophethood, dying firm in his faith.
story: This son of hers, Habib, was killed by Musaylima, "the liar", the latter asked him, 'Do you bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of God?' 'Yes,' he replied. He asked, 'Do you bear witness that I am the Messenger of God?' He replied, 'I do not hear!' At that he began cutting off one member after the other from him until he died right there before him; he got nothing more from him. Umm Umara was one of those who left for al-Yamba with the Muslims when Musaylima was killed. When she came back she had 12 wounds caused by hits and cuts. God bless her.
lessons: True faith cannot be extracted by force or torture; the soul that is rooted in conviction remains unshaken even at the cost of life.
Bearing witness to the truth in the face of mortal danger is one of the highest expressions of iman, and such steadfastness is eternally honoured by God.
A mother's courage and sacrifice in the path of God is itself a form of martyrdom; Umm Umara's wounds were badges of honour, not defeat.
practical_advice: When you feel pressured to compromise your values or identity under social, professional, or family pressure, recall that integrity maintained under duress is the mark of genuine character.

If you are facing a situation where saying the wrong thing feels easier than holding your ground, pause and ask yourself what you would be giving up by conceding — and what you preserve by staying true.
Honour the sacrifices of those who came before you by refusing to take your own faith, values, and freedoms for granted; gratitude for their example is itself a spiritual practice.

title: The Prophet Receives the Divine Vision of His Migration Destination
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) received a vision from God showing him the place of his future migration — a salty, swampy plain with palm-groves between two tracts of rocks — which turned out to be the city of Yathrib (Medina).
story: Al-Zuhri stated, from Urwa, that 'A'isha said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS), he being at Mecca at the time, addressed the Muslims as follows, 'I have been shown the place of your migration; I have been shown a salty, swampy plain with palm-groves between two tracts of rocks.' "Some people migrated towards Medina when the Messenger of God (SAAS) said that, while other Muslims who had gone to Abyssinia returned and went there instead." Abu Musa stated that the Prophet (SAAS) said, "In my sleep I saw that I would be migrating from Mecca to a place where there were palm-groves. My imagination told me that it was to be in al-Yamama or Hajar, but it turned out to be the town of Yathrib."
lessons: Divine guidance often comes in unexpected and subtle forms; remaining attentive and open-hearted allows one to perceive direction even in uncertainty.
The Prophet's example shows that periods of waiting and not-yet-knowing are not abandonment — they are preparation; God's timing carries wisdom that our impatience cannot yet see.
Acting on trust when the full picture is unclear is itself an act of faith; the companions who migrated on the strength of a vision demonstrated that conviction precedes certainty.
practical_advice: When you are in a period of transition and do not yet know where you are headed, resist the urge to force clarity; instead, stay present, keep your obligations, and trust that the direction will emerge.

If you have received inner signs — through dreams, intuition, or recurring circumstances — pointing toward a particular path, do not dismiss them; reflect on them and seek counsel from those you trust.
Document or journal your inner experiences during uncertain times; looking back, you may find a pattern of guidance that was always present but only visible in retrospect.

title: The Prophet Commands the Muslims to Migrate to Medina After the Pledge of Aqaba
summary: After God granted permission for warfare and the Ansar pledged their support, the Prophet ordered his followers in Mecca to emigrate to Medina in groups, while he himself waited for divine permission to depart.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When God Almighty gave permission for warfare with His words, 'Permission (to fight) is being given to those against whom war is being wrongfully waged. God has power to give them victory, those who have been expelled unjustly from their homes merely for having said, "Our Lord is God"' (surat al-Hajj; XXII, v.39-40). "When God gave permission to do battle and that group of ansar had followed him into accepting Islam and had agreed to give him and his Muslim followers aid and refuge, the Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered his supporters, both those who had previously emigrated and those who had stayed with him in Mecca, to leave in migration to Medina to join their Muslim brethren there. He told them, 'God has provided brothers and a home where you may be secure.' "And so they left for Medina in groups. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed in Mecca waiting for his Lord to give him permission to emigrate from Mecca to Medina."
lessons: Leadership sometimes means sending others ahead while bearing the burden of waiting yourself; the Prophet remained in Mecca out of obedience to God, not out of reluctance, modelling that true leaders do not abandon their posts prematurely.
Brotherhood in faith is a form of divine provision; when God said He had prepared brothers and a home, He was affirming that human community built on shared values is itself a mercy.
Permission and timing in matters of great consequence belong to God; moving before the right moment, even with good intentions, can be as harmful as not moving at all.
practical_advice: If you are in a difficult environment — a toxic workplace, a harmful relationship, an unsupportive community — and are planning to leave, do so in a measured and intentional way rather than impulsively, seeking proper timing and preparation.
Cultivate a network of genuine brotherhood and sisterhood before you need it; the companions had a community waiting for them in Medina because relationships had been built in advance.

When you feel stuck in a hard place while others seem to be moving forward, remind yourself that your waiting may be purposeful; use the time to prepare, strengthen your faith, and trust that your moment of movement will come.

title: Abu Salama: The First Emigrant from Quraysh to Medina
summary: Abu Salama became the first of the Qurayshi Muhajirun to emigrate to Medina, having been harshly treated by Quraysh after returning from Abyssinia, and choosing Medina when he learned of the Muslim community there.
story: "The first of his supporters to emigrate to Medina of the Quraysh and of the Banu Makhzum was Abu Salama 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Asad b. Hilal b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Makhzum. His migration took place one year before the pledge made at al-'Aqaba. This was because, following his return from Abyssinia, he had been badly treated by Quraysh and had decided to return there, but, when he learned of fellow Muslims in Medina, he went there instead."
lessons: Persecution and hardship, though painful, can become the catalyst that propels a person toward a better life and a stronger community; what seems like a blow can be a redirection.

Being the first to take a courageous step — even when the path is uncharted — is itself an act of worship and legacy; Abu Salama's name is remembered precisely because he moved when others had not yet gathered the courage.
Knowing where your people are, and moving toward them, is not weakness but wisdom; community is a lifeline that the believer is entitled to seek.
practical_advice: If your current environment is actively hostile to your wellbeing, your values, or your growth, begin seriously planning your exit rather than enduring indefinitely in the hope that things will change on their own.
When you hear of a community, a place, or an opportunity where you might truly belong and thrive, do not dismiss it out of inertia or fear of the unknown; investigate it and take it seriously.
Honour the courage it takes to be the first — in your family, your community, or your circle — to make a difficult but necessary change; trailblazing carries a cost, but also a unique reward.

title: Umm Salama Separated from Husband and Son During the Hijra
summary: When Abu Salama attempted to emigrate to Medina with his wife Umm Salama and their infant son Salama, his wife was seized by her tribe and his son by his own tribe, leaving the family separated for nearly a year.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "My father related to me, from Salama b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Umar b. Abi Salama, from his grandmother Umm Salama, who said, 'When Abu Salama decided to depart for Medina, he saddled his camel for me, mounted me on it and put my son Salama in my lap. He then led us away. "'When some men of the Banu al-Mughira saw him they approached and said, "We can accept what you yourself do, but why should we allow this woman of ours to be taken off by you somewhere else?" She went on, "And they snatched the camel's bridle out of his hand and took me off it." "'This angered the Banu 'Abd al-Asad, Abu Salama's people, and they said, "By God, we'll not leave a son of ours with her now you've taken her away from our man." So they tugged at my son Salama and dislocated his arm. The Banu 'Abd al-Asad then took him away, while the Banu al-Mughira kept me among themselves. My husband, Abu Salama, then left for Medina. So I was separated from both my son and my husband. "'Thereafter I would go out every morning and sit in the valley and weep till evening; I kept this up for about a year.'"
lessons: Grief expressed openly — as Umm Salama wept daily for a year — is not weakness but a natural and honest response to genuine loss; suppressing such pain is not strength.

The bonds of family, when broken by circumstances beyond one's control, do not break the soul of the believer; Umm Salama endured one of the most painful separations imaginable while remaining steadfast in her faith.

Injustice and cruelty exercised under the banner of tribal honour or social convention do not make them right; the suffering of Umm Salama was caused by people who believed they had justification, and yet it was deeply wrong.
practical_advice: If you are experiencing a painful separation — from a loved one, a home, or a community — allow yourself to grieve fully rather than forcing yourself to 'move on'; grief acknowledged heals faster than grief suppressed.

When you feel trapped in a situation not of your making, focus on what small act of faithfulness you can perform each day, as Umm Salama continued going out each morning; consistency in small acts sustains the soul through long winters.

If someone in your life is visibly grieving and appears to be in sustained pain, do not merely offer advice — ask what they need, and if possible, advocate for them, as the relative who saw Umm Salama's suffering and spoke up on her behalf.

title: Umm Salama's Reunion and the Honourable Escort of Uthman ibn Talha
summary: After a year of separation, Umm Salama was permitted to rejoin her husband in Medina. Travelling alone with her infant son, she was met at al-Tan'im by Uthman ibn Talha, who accompanied and protected her with extraordinary honour until she reached Medina.
story: "'Then one day a man from my uncle's family, one of the Banu al-Mughira, passed by, saw the state I was in and took pity on me. He told the Banu al-Mughira, "Can't you let this poor woman go? You've separated her from both her son and her husband!" "'They then told me I could join my husband if I liked. "'So then the Banu 'Abd al-Asad returned my son to me; I saddled a camel, took my son in my lap and headed out to my husband in Medina. "'I was entirely alone. But eventually, at al-Tan'im, I met Uthman b. Talha b. Abi Talha, a relative of the Banu 'Abd al-Dar, and he asked, "Where are you going, daughter of Abi Umayya?" I replied, "I'm heading for my husband in Medina." He said, "And there's no one with you?" "There is no one with me except God and this son of mine," I replied. "'He commented, "You shouldn't be left like that." "'He then took the camel's halter and began accompanying me. And, I swear, I was never in the company of any Arab more honourable than him. When we made a stop, he would make my camel kneel and then he would move away until I had dismounted. When I made a halt he would take my mount away, unload it, and tie it to a tree. He would then move off and make his bed beneath a tree. When it was time to leave, he would get my camel, lead it up, saddle it and then stand away from me. He would then say, "Do mount!" And when I mounted and was secure, he would come and take its halter and lead me away until we next stopped. "'He continued behaving in this way until he brought me to Medina. When he saw the village of the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf at Quba', he said, "Your husband is in this village. Abu Salama is living here. Go on in, with God's blessings." He then left, returning to Mecca.' "She used to say, 'I know of no other family in all Islam who suffered like that of Abu Salama. And I swear I never had a more honourable companion than Uthman b. Talha.'"
lessons: True nobility of character is revealed not in grand declarations but in consistent, patient, and dignified conduct toward those who are vulnerable — as Uthman ibn Talha demonstrated at every stopping point of the journey.

Help extended to someone in need, even by someone outside one's own community or faith, is a moral act that God does not overlook; Uthman was not yet Muslim when he performed this service, yet his honour was immortalised.
After a long period of suffering, relief can come unexpectedly through a stranger's simple act of compassion; do not give up on the possibility of kindness arriving from an unexpected quarter.
practical_advice: When you encounter someone who is clearly alone, struggling, or in a vulnerable situation, ask yourself what the most honourable version of yourself would do — and then do that, regardless of whether it costs you time or convenience.

If you are currently walking through a painful stretch of life alone, remind yourself that Umm Salama said she had 'God and this son of mine' with her; sometimes the most important companionship is not human, and acknowledging the divine presence can sustain you.

Model the behaviour of Uthman ibn Talha in your own conduct: when you accompany someone through a difficult passage — a medical appointment, a hard conversation, a transition — be present with full dignity and respect for their space and needs.

title: The Emigration of Banu Jahsh and Their Abandoned Home
summary: The family of Banu Jahsh emigrated to Medina, leaving their home empty. Abu Jahl and Utba b. Rabi'a passed by the abandoned house, with Utba sighing at the sight and Abu Jahl showing no remorse.
story:
"The home of Banii Jahsh was shut up when the migration occurred. Wtba b. Rabi'a, al-'Abbas b. 'Abd al-Mugalib and Abii Jahl b. Hishm passed by it on their way up to the heights of Mecca. 'Utba looked at its doors blowing open, unoccupied, and he sighed deeply and said, 'Every house, no matter how long safe, will one day be beset by disaster and outrage.'"

Ibn Hishm, however, mentioned that this verse was a line from a poem by Aba Daiid al-Iyyadi. Al-Suhayli said that Aba Daiid was Hanzala b. Sharqi; also his name was given as Hawitha.

(Ibn Ishaq continued) "Utba then said, 'The home of Banii Jahsh has become devoid of its people.' Abu Jahl commented, 'No one at all will weep over that!' He then said, to al-'Abbas that is, 'All this is the work of your nephew! He has split us up and completely divided our community.'"
lessons: Leaving behind worldly comforts and homes for the sake of faith is one of the highest acts of sacrifice, and its impact resonates even in the hearts of opponents.
The indifference of those who oppose truth does not diminish the righteousness of one's sacrifice; even the sight of an empty home bore witness to the believers' courage.
Not everyone who witnesses the signs of truth will be moved to accept it — some, like Abu Jahl, harden their hearts further, while others like Utba feel a moment of quiet acknowledgment.
practical_advice: When you leave behind something precious for a higher purpose, do not measure the worth of your sacrifice by how others receive it — its value lies in your sincerity.
If you are going through a painful transition or loss, remember that what appears as emptiness or abandonment to others may in fact be a testimony to your courage and conviction.
Do not let the coldness or mockery of others in your darkest moments shake your resolve; their lack of empathy says more about them than it does about your path.

title: The Emigration of Banu Ghanm b. Dudan to Medina
summary: The entire clan of Banu Ghanm b. Dudan, men and women alike, embraced Islam and emigrated collectively to Medina, leaving behind their homeland in Mecca.
story: "Banti Ghanm b. Diidm had accepted Islam and moved as Emigrants to Medina, men and women both. They consisted of 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh and his brother Aba Ahmad, Wkasha b. Mihsan, Shuja' and Wqba, both sons of Wahb, Arbad b. Jumayra, Munqidh b. Nubata, Sa'id b. Ruqaysh, Muhriz b. Nadla, Zayd b. Ruqaysh, Qays b. Jabir, 'Amr b. Muhsin, Malik b. 'Amr, Safwan b. 'Amr, Thaqf b. 'Amr, Rabi'a b. Aktham, al-Zubayr b. 'Ubayda, Tamam b. 'Ubayda, Sakhbara b. 'Ubayda and Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh. Their women included Zaynab, Humna and Umm Habiba, daughters of Jahsh, Judama, daughter of Jandal, Umm Qays, daughter of Muhsin, Umm Habib, daughter of Thumama, Amina, daughter of Ruqaysh, and Sakhbara, daughter of Tamim."
lessons: When an entire community acts together upon conviction and faith, their collective sacrifice becomes a source of strength and solidarity that no individual could achieve alone.
The willingness to uproot one's entire life — home, land, and social ties — for the sake of faith demonstrates a depth of conviction that transforms communities.
The participation of women alongside men in the emigration reflects the equality of spiritual courage; faith calls all people, regardless of gender, to act with equal resolve.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult transition in life, seek the support of a community or trusted companions who share your values — collective resolve makes the journey far more bearable.
If you feel overwhelmed by what you must leave behind to pursue what is right, take courage from those who have walked that same road before you and found renewal on the other side.
Honor the sacrifices of those who came before you by living with intention and gratitude, recognizing that the lives you now enjoy were built on the foundations of others' hardship.

title: Abu Ahmad b. Jahsh's Poem on the Emigration to Medina
summary: Abu Ahmad b. Jahsh, a blind poet among the emigrants, composed verses expressing his family's grief at leaving Mecca, his trust in God, and his conviction that their emigration was an aspiration of faith rather than an act of revenge.
story: "Aba Ahmad b. Jahsh spoke the following verses concerning their emigration to Medina: 'When Umm Ahmad saw me leaving under the protection of One supernatural whom I fear and revere, She said, "If this is what you have to do, then take us some place else, far from Yathrib." I said to her, "Yathrib is not just a possible location; man does whatever the All-Merciful wishes. I head towards God and to the Messenger; and whoever directs himself to God one day will not be disappointed. What a lot of true, real friends we have left behind, and a woman, too, who weeps tears and laments. You think it is vengeance that distances us from our land; we think that it is our aspiration that we seek. I invited the Banii Ghanm to spare their bloodshed; for there is a course open to people when the path to truth is clear." They responded, praise the Lord, when someone invited them to the truth and to success, and they came united. We, and our fellows who had left the true path and had helped those using weapons against us, had become, Like two battalions; one of these was successful in achieving truth, rightly guided, while the other would be punished. Acting unjustly, they thought up lies; Satan made them err away from the truth. They failed and caused to fail. We delighted in the words of the Prophet Muhammad and those of us who supported truth acted well and were well treated. We are very closely related to them, though there is no close relationship if there is no closeness. What nephew would, after us, give you their husq what in-law after us could be respected? One day you will learn which of us is closer to the truth, when they have passed on and the people's dispute is concluded."'
lessons: True emigration — whether physical or spiritual — is not driven by bitterness or revenge, but by sincere aspiration toward something greater and more meaningful.
Even in the most painful separations, the believer finds comfort not in what is left behind, but in the direction they are moving — toward God and truth.
Poetry and expression of grief are not signs of weakness; they are a testament to the depth of love and sacrifice involved in living according to one's convictions.
practical_advice: When you must leave behind people or places you love for the sake of your values, allow yourself to grieve that loss honestly — acknowledging pain does not contradict trust in God.
Reframe difficult life transitions not as what you are losing, but as the aspiration you are moving toward; this shift in perspective can sustain you through the hardest moments.
When others misread your sacrifices as acts of bitterness or escape, take comfort in knowing that your own heart understands your true intention — and God knows it too.

title: Umar b. al-Khattab's Emigration and His Warning to Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a
summary: Umar b. al-Khattab arranged to emigrate to Medina with Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a and Hisham b. al-'As. Hisham was detained and later apostatized, while Umar warned Ayyash against returning to Mecca when approached by Abu Jahl and al-Harith b. Hisham with a scheme to lure him back.
story:
"Then Wmar b. al-Khattab left, along with 'Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a, and went to Medina.

"Nafi' related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Wmar, from his father, saying, 'Having made up my mind to emigrate to Medina, I arranged a rendezvous with 'Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a and Hisham b. al-'As at al-Tanaub of Idst of the Bana Ghifa, above Saraf. We agreed, "Any one of us who doesn't arrive there in the morning must have been detained; in such a case the others must go ahead."

"'Next morning 'Ayyash and I were there at al-Tanaub. Hisham was detained. He was enticed and apostatized.'

"'When we reached Medina we went to live among the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf in Quba'. Aba Jahl b. Hisham and al-Harith b. Hisham came over to see 'Ayyash who was their cousin and their brother through their mother. They came to Medina at a time when the Messenger of God (SAAS) was in Mecca. They talked to 'Ayyash and told him, "Your mother has warned that she will not allow a comb to touch her hair until she sees you, nor will she shelter from the sun." 'Ayyash was worried about her, but I told him, "These people, I swear, only want to entice you away from your faith; so beware of them. You can be sure that if your mother gets lice she'll be sure to comb her hair, and if she finds Mecca's heat severe, she'll surely seek shade!"

"'But 'Ayyash said, "I will remove my mother's oath, and I have money there I can get." I told him, "You already know I'm one of the wealthiest men of Mecca, and if you don't go, I'll give you half what I have!"

"'He refused my offer, insisting on leaving with them. When that was all he would do, I said, "Well, if you have to do it, then take this camel of mine. It is a splendid mount and docile. Keep on her back and if you get suspicious about them, then escape on her."

"'He then rode off on her with them. Some way along Aba Jahl asked him, "Say, brother, I find my mount difficult to ride. Would you mind if I rode behind on yours?" "Sure," he responded. Then he made his camel kneel as the other two men dismounted to change mounts. But when they were all on the ground the others ran up at 'Ayyash and tied him up. Then they took him off to Mecca, subverted him and he apostatized.'

"'And,' the narrator Wmar said, 'we used to say, "God will not accept repentance from those who apostatize." And those who apostatized made similar statements about themselves.'"
lessons: The manipulation of family sentiment is one of the oldest tools used to weaken a person's resolve — sincere friends who see through such schemes are among life's greatest blessings.
Umar's offer to give half his wealth to protect Ayyash's faith illustrates that true brotherhood means sacrificing personal resources, not just offering words of comfort.
Even the wisest warnings cannot override another person's free will; we can advise and protect those we love, but ultimately each person must choose their own path.
practical_advice: When you feel emotionally pulled back toward something harmful — whether by family pressure, nostalgia, or manipulation — pause and ask yourself whether the pull is rooted in genuine good or in a scheme designed to exploit your love.

If a trusted friend warns you strongly against a decision, take their concern seriously even if your heart feels otherwise; sometimes those on the outside can see the trap that our emotions hide from us.
Be the kind of friend who not only warns but also offers practical, material support — true loyalty shows itself in action, not only in words.

title: Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a's Capture and Apostasy, and the Revelation of Divine Mercy
summary: Ayyash b. Abi Rabi'a was lured back to Mecca by Abu Jahl, captured, and forced to apostatize. The Muslims believed apostates could not be forgiven, until God revealed verses of boundless mercy that reached even Hisham b. al-'As, who then returned to the faith.
story:
"'He then rode off on her with them. Some way along Aba Jahl asked him, "Say, brother, I find my mount difficult to ride. Would you mind if I rode behind on yours?" "Sure," he responded. Then he made his camel kneel as the other two men dismounted to change mounts. But when they were all on the ground the others ran up at 'Ayyash and tied him up. Then they took him off to Mecca, subverted him and he apostatized.'

"'And,' the narrator Wmar said, 'we used to say, "God will not accept repentance from those who apostatize." And those who apostatized made similar statements about themselves.'

"He went on, 'This was so until the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina and God revealed, "Say: '0my servants who have harmed themselves, do not despair of God's mercy. God will forgive all sins. He is the All-Forgiving, the All-Merciful. Turn back to your Lord and submit to Him before punishment reaches you; then you shall not be helped. Follow the best that has been revealed to you from your Lord before punishment comes suddenly to you, and you are caught unawares.'"' (siirat al-Zumar; XXXIX, v.53-5).

"'Umar went on, "I wrote this down and sent it on to Hisham b. al-'As."

"'Hisham said, "When it reached me I began reading it at Dha Tuwa, holding it this way and that, but I didn't understand it. Eventually I said, 'O God, make me understand this!' And God made me realize that it had been sent down specifically about us and what we used to tell ourselves and what was said about us."

"'So I went to my camel, mounted it and joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina.'"
lessons: No sin is too great for God's mercy — the verse revealed about the apostates stands as a timeless reminder that despair over one's own failures is itself a spiritual error to be corrected.
The act of Umar writing down the verse and sending it to Hisham illustrates how sharing knowledge and divine guidance can literally save a person's spiritual life.
Sometimes understanding comes not through our own intellect but through sincere prayer for comprehension — asking God to open one's heart is itself an act of faith.
practical_advice: If you are carrying guilt over past mistakes and feel that you are beyond forgiveness, return to the verse: 'Do not despair of God's mercy. God will forgive all sins.' Let this be a source of genuine healing, not just empty words.
When you see someone struggling spiritually or emotionally, a simple act — like sending them a meaningful passage or message — can be the thing that turns their life around.
When a text, teaching, or idea confuses you, do not abandon it; instead, sit with it, pray over it, and ask sincerely for understanding. Clarity often comes to those who seek it with humility.

title: The Joyful Arrival of the Emigrants in Medina and the Prophet's Welcome
summary: The emigrants arrived in Medina in successive groups, with Mus'ab b. Umayr and Ibn Umm Maktum arriving first. The Prophet's own arrival was met with extraordinary joy, with the slave girls of Medina singing out to announce his coming.
story:
Al-Bukhari stated, "Aba al-Walid related to us, quoting Shu'ba, quoting Aba Ishaq who heard al-Bara' say, 'The first to come to us were Mus'ab b. Wmayr and Ibn Umm Maktum, and they were followed by 'Ammar and Bilal.'

"Muhammad b. Bashshar related to me, quoting Ghundar quoting Shu'ba, from Aba Ishaq, who said, 'I heard al-Bara' b. 'Azib say, "The first to join us were Mus'ab b. Wmayr and Ibn Umm Maktum. They would read to the people. Then came Bilal, Sa'd and 'Ammar b. Yasir, followed by Wmar b. al-Khattab along with 20 of the supporters of the Prophet (SAAS)."

"'Then came the Prophet (SAAS); and I never saw the people of Medina so happy as they were to see him. So much so that the slave girls sang out, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) has come!" And he did not arrive until after I had read the verse in the Qur'an: "Glorify the name of your Lord most high!" (surat al-A'la; LXXXVII), along with other verses from al-Mufassalat.'"
lessons: The Prophet's arrival was not greeted with ceremony or power, but with the spontaneous, uncontainable joy of people whose hearts recognized the truth — a reminder that genuine love needs no formality.
The gradual arrival of emigrants in waves shows that migration and change rarely happen all at once; patience through a process is itself a form of trust in God's timing.
Even the most marginalized members of society — the slave girls of Medina — participated in welcoming the Prophet, illustrating that the joy of faith belongs to every soul equally.
practical_advice: When you are going through a period of waiting — waiting for relief, waiting for change, waiting for someone — remember that arrivals happen in God's timing, and the joy when they come can be greater than anything you anticipated.
Celebrate goodness openly and without restraint when it arrives; the people of Medina did not hold back their joy, and that unguarded happiness was itself a form of gratitude.
Cultivate within yourself the kind of sincere longing for what is good and true that makes its arrival feel like the greatest gift — because that longing is itself a sign of a living, seeking heart.

title: Umar b. al-Khattab's Emigration with His Family and Companions to Quba
summary: Umar b. al-Khattab emigrated to Medina with a large group including his brother Zayd, relatives, and allies, settling among the Banu Amr b. Awf at Quba.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When 'Umar b. al-Khattab and members of his family came to Medina, they included his brother Zayd b. al-Khattab, 'Amr and 'Abd Allah, the two sons of Suraqa b. al-Muctamir, Khumays b. Hudhafa al-Sahmi, husband of his daughter Hafsa, his cousin Sa'id b. Zayd b. 'Amr b. Nufayl, Waqid b. 'Abd Allah al-Tamimi, an ally of theirs, Khawla b. Aba Khawla and Malik b. Abi Khawla, two allies of theirs from the Bana Ijl, (and from) the Banii al-Bakayr, Ilyas, and Khalid, and 'Aqil and 'Amir, and their allies from the Bana Sa'd b. Layth. They took up residence with Rifa'a 'Abd al-Mundhir b. Zunayr among the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf at Quba'."
lessons: Leadership is not an individual act — Umar emigrated with his entire community, demonstrating that a true leader carries his people with him through hardship.
The bonds of kinship and alliance were honored and maintained even in the most difficult of migrations, showing that community ties are a source of resilience, not distraction.
Settling in a new land under the protection of welcoming hosts reflects the Islamic value of hospitality and brotherhood — no one should have to face upheaval alone.
practical_advice: When facing a major life transition, do not try to go through it entirely alone; bring your community with you, or at least lean on those who are walking a similar path.
If you are in a position to host or support someone going through difficulty — whether a refugee, a friend in crisis, or a newcomer — do so with the generosity that the Ansar of Medina modeled.
Take stock of the people in your life who walk alongside you through the hardest seasons — these companions are among your greatest blessings and deserve to be honored.

title: Suhayb al-Rumi's Sacrifice: Giving Up His Wealth to Emigrate
summary: When Suhayb ibn Sinan wished to emigrate to Medina, the polytheists of Quraysh blocked him, refusing to let him leave with his wealth. He offered them all his gold in exchange for his freedom, and the Prophet praised him saying 'Suhayb made a profit!'
story:
Ibn Hisham stated, 'It was told to me that Abu 'Uthman al-Nahdi said, "I learned that when Suhayb wished to emigrate the polytheists of Quraysh said to him, 'When you came to us you were a poverty-stricken beggar. With us you grew wealthy and acquired status and now you want to take yourself and your money away! By God, that won't happen!' "Suhayb told them, 'If I were to make my money over to you, would you let me leave?' "'Yes,' they agreed. "That information reached the Messenger of God (SAAS), and he commented, 'Suhayb made a profit! Suhayb made a profit!'"

Al-Bayhaqi stated, 'The hafiz Abu 'Abd Allah related to us, by dictation, that Abu al-'Abbas Isma'il b. 'Abd Allah b. Muhammad b. Mikal informed him, quoting 'Abdun al-Ahwazi, quoting Zayd b. al-Juraysh, quoting Ya'qub b. Muhammad al-Zuhri, quoting Husayn b. Hudhayfa b. Sayfi b. Suhayb, quoting his father and uncles, from Sa'id b. al-Musayyab, from Suhayb who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'I was shown the place of your emigration: a salt-plain between two uplands and two lava-rock plains. That would either be Hajar or Yathrib.'"

"He went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) left for Medina accompanied by Abu Bakr. I had wanted to leave with him, but some young Quraysh men blocked my way. I remained up that night, not lying down, and they commented, "God has diverted him from you through his stomach!" But I made no complaint, and they went to sleep. So I left, but some of them caught up with me after I had gone some way and wanted to take me back. I told them, "If I were to give you some ounces of gold, would you let me go and be fair to me?" They did so and I followed them back to Mecca. I told them, "Dig beneath the threshold of the door; that's where the ounces of gold are. And if you go off to a certain woman, you can take two sets of vestments."

"'So I left and joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) at Quba' before he moved out from there. When he saw me he said, "Well, Abu Yahya, you made a good deal!" I replied, "O Messenger of God, no one got here to you before me, so it could only be Gabriel, peace be upon him, who informed you!"'"

'
lessons: Suhayb's willingness to sacrifice all his worldly wealth for the sake of his faith demonstrates that true freedom and spiritual gain far outweigh material possessions.
The Prophet's words 'Suhayb made a profit' teach us that when we give up something for God's sake, we never truly lose — the real profit is in earning God's pleasure.
Suhayb's patient endurance through the night without complaint, even while being watched and mocked, shows that steadfastness in difficult moments is itself a form of worship.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult sacrifice, ask yourself what you are truly gaining in the long run — sometimes letting go of material security is the price of a greater spiritual freedom.
If you are in a situation where you feel trapped or blocked, look for creative and honourable solutions rather than giving up on your goal entirely, as Suhayb negotiated his way to freedom.

When others mock or belittle your efforts during hardship, resist the urge to complain or retaliate; quiet perseverance, as Suhayb demonstrated, often leads to a greater outcome than anger or argument.

title: The Emigrants Settle in Quba and Medina: A Community Takes Root
summary: Following the Prophet's permission, the Muslim emigrants from Mecca arrived one by one in Medina, each being hosted by an Ansari family, forming the first bonds of the new Muslim community in their adopted home.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Then the emigrants, God bless them, followed on after them. Talha b. 'Ubayd Allah and Suhayb b. Sinan, took up residence with Khubayb b. Isaf, brother of the Bana al-Harith b. al-Khazraj at al-Sunh. It is also said that Talha stayed with As'ad b. Zurara.'

Ibn Ishaq stated, 'Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib, Zayd b. Haritha, Abu Marthad Kannaz b. al-Husayn and his son Marthad, both of the Ghanawi tribe, allies of Hamza; Anasa and Abu Kabsha, freed-men of the Messenger of God (SAAS); all these stayed with Kulthum b. al-Hadm, brother of the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, at Quba'. However, it is also said that they stayed with Sa'd b. Khaythama. It is stated, moreover, that Hamza stayed with As'ad b. Zurara. But God knows best.'

He went on, ''Ubayda b. al-Harith and his two brothers al-Tufayl and Husayn, Mistaḥ b. Uthatha, Suwaybit b. Sa'd b. Huraymila, brother of the 'Abd al-Dar, Tulayb b. 'Umayr, brother of the Bana 'Abd b. Qusayy, Khabbab, freedman of 'Utba b. Ghazwan stayed with 'Abd Allah b. Salama, brother of Bal'ajlan at Quba'.

"'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf and some other emigrants stayed with Sa'd b. al-Rabi'. Al-Zubayr b. 'Awwam and Abu Sabra b. Abi Ruhm stayed with Mundhir b. Muhammad b. 'Uqba b. Ubayha b. al-Jul&a at al-'Usba, the home of the Banu Jabja b. Mus'ab b. 'Umayr stayed with Sa'd b. Mu'adh. Abu Hudhayfa b. 'Utba and Salim, his freed-man, stayed with Salama.'

Ibn Ishaq stated, ''Utba b. Ghazwan stayed with 'Abbad b. Bishr b. Waqqash among the Banu 'Abd al-Ashhal. 'Uthman b. 'Affan stayed with Aws b. Thabit b. al-Mundhir, brother of Hassan b. Thabit in the home of the Bana al-Najjar.'

His account continues, 'The unmarried emigrant men stayed with Sa'd b. Khaythama since he was unmarried. But God knows best about this.'

Ya'qub b. Sufyan stated, 'Ahmad b. Abi Bakr b. al-Harith b. Zurara b. Mus'ab b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf related to us, quoting 'Abd al-'Aziz b. Muhammad b. 'Ubayd Allah, from Nafi', from Ibn 'Umar, who said, "We arrived from Mecca and took up residence at al-'Usba, including 'Umar b. al-Khattab, Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah, and Salim, freed-man of Abu Hudhayfa. It was Salim who acted as their Imam because he had greater knowledge of the Qur'an than the others."'
lessons: The Ansar's generous hosting of the emigrants demonstrates that a true community is built on selfless giving and welcoming those in need, regardless of personal cost.
The emigrants' willingness to leave everything behind and rely on strangers shows that trust in God provides the courage to begin again in unfamiliar circumstances.

Leadership within the early community was based on knowledge and merit — Salim led the prayer because of his greater knowledge of the Quran, a reminder that spiritual excellence transcends social status.
practical_advice: If you are going through a period of displacement, loss, or major life transition, seek out a community of people who share your values — belonging to a supportive group is essential to emotional recovery.
Practise being a generous host or helper to those who are new or struggling in your community; small acts of hospitality can have a profound and lasting impact on someone's sense of security.
When feeling lost or out of place in a new environment, focus on contributing what you know and what you can offer, as Salim did by leading in what he knew best.

title: The Quraysh Council at Dar al-Nadwa: Plotting Against the Prophet
summary: As the Muslim emigrants established themselves in Medina, the Quraysh leadership convened an emergency council at Dar al-Nadwa, where Satan appeared in the guise of an old sheikh from Najd, to decide what to do about the Prophet before he could join his supporters.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed on in Mecca after his supporters had emigrated, waiting for permission to be given to himself to leave.

'There remained there with him only those (Muslims) who had been detained or who had apostatized, except for 'Ali, son of Abu Talib, and Abu Bakr b. Abu Quhafa, God bless them both.

'Abu Bakr often asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) for permission to leave, but he would reply, "Don't be in a hurry. God may well give you a companion." And Abu Bakr hoped it would be him.

'When Quraysh saw that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had assembled a party and had supporters from others than their own people and from a town other than theirs, and saw his Companions moving out to join these others, they realized that the Muslims had found a new home with them and had acquired their protection.

'Quraysh were concerned that the Messenger of God (SAAS) would leave and join them, since they knew that he had decided to do battle with them.

'They therefore gathered in the Dar al-Nadwa, the house of assembly, the home of Qusayy b. Kilab, where all their decisions were made. They discussed there what they should do about the Messenger of God (SAAS), since they now feared him.'

Ibn Ishaq continued, 'A colleague whose views I do not doubt, as did others I consider reliable, related to me, from 'Abd Allah b. Abu Najih, from Mujahid b. Jabr, that 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas said, "The day arrived when they had agreed to meet to enter their assembly to discuss what to do about the Messenger of God (SAAS). That day is known as the yawm al-zahma, 'the day of the gathering', and upon it Satan, God curse him, came before them in the form of a venerable sheikh dressed in a heavy cloak. He stood at the door of the house and, when they saw him they asked who he was. He replied, 'I am come from Nejd, from the highlands; I have heard why you agreed to meet, and I've come to listen to what you say and perhaps offer some comment or advice.' They invited him in.

"'He entered the meeting where the Quraysh nobles had assembled. They consisted of 'Utba, Shayba, Abu Sufyan, Ta'ima b. 'Adi, Jubayr b. Mut'im b. 'Adi, al-Harith b. 'Amir b. Nawfal, al-Nadr b. al-Harith, Abu al-Bakhtari b. Hisham, Zam'a b. al-Aswad, Hakim b. Hizam, Abu Jahl b. Hisham, Nabih and Munabbih, sons of al-Hajjaj, and Umayya b. Khalaf, along with various others of their supporters from Quraysh.

"'They reminded one another what they had experienced from the behaviour of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and agreed that they felt insecure from the possibility that he and his supporters might make an attack upon them.
