"'Having discussed the issue, one of them, said to have been Abu al-Bakhtari b. Hisham, suggested, "Put him in irons and gaol him; then wait for him to have the same fate that befell poets like him before, such as Zuhayr and al-Nabigha. He would die as they had."

"'But the Najdi sheikh objected, saying "No; that's not a good plan. If you do shut him up, news of him will get right past the door you have locked on him and reach his supporters. They will promptly attack you and release him and then increase in numbers until they overcome you. That's not a good idea."

"'They consulted further and one suggested, "Let's exile him from our territory. Once he has gone, we shouldn't care where he ends up or what happens to him. So long as he is gone, we'll be rid of him and we'll be able to restore our affairs as they were before."

"'But the Najdi sheikh observed, "No, that's not a good idea for you. You know how sweet his talk is, and his reasoning, and how he convinces others of his message. If you do that you'd have no security against him going to live with some Arab tribe and using his speech and discourse to get them to follow him. Then he would lead them against you and perhaps defeat (or) dispossess you and do what they like with you. No, think of something else."

"'Abu Jahl b. Hisham then said, "I have an idea that hasn't occurred to you yet."

"'"What is it, Abu al-Hakam?" they asked him.

"'He replied, "I think we should select one young man from each tribe, and someone who is strong, of excellent lineage and reputation as a leader. We should give each one a sharp sword and they would go to him and use the swords to strike him in unison. They would kill him and we would then be rid of him. If they do this, his blood will be spread over all the tribes. And
lessons: The fact that the Prophet calmly waited for divine permission to migrate, even as enemies plotted against him, teaches that true reliance on God means not acting out of panic but trusting in His perfect timing.
The Quraysh's desperation and the fact that they needed Satan's counsel to formulate their plan reveals that opposition to truth, no matter how organized, is ultimately built on a fragile foundation.
Abu Bakr's patient waiting at the Prophet's instruction, despite his eagerness to leave, demonstrates that loyalty and obedience to righteous leadership is itself a form of spiritual discipline.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by hostility or opposition, resist the urge to react impulsively; take time to seek guidance and wait for the right moment to act, as the Prophet did before his migration.
Recognize that those who scheme against goodness often do so out of fear, not strength — understanding this can help you respond with composure rather than anxiety.
If you are in a position of waiting and uncertainty, use that time as the Prophet and Abu Bakr did: staying present, staying connected to those who matter, and trusting that clarity will come.

title: The Quraysh Plot to Assassinate the Prophet
summary: The Quraysh leaders gathered and devised a plan to assassinate the Prophet by having young men from each tribe strike him simultaneously with swords, so that his blood would be spread across all tribes and the Banu Abd Manaf could not retaliate. Gabriel warned the Prophet of the plot.
story: have an idea that hasn't occurred to you yet." "'What is it, Aha al-Hakarn?" they asked him. "'He replied, "I think we should select one young man from each tribe, and someone who is strong, of excellent lineage and reputation as a leader. We should give each one a sharp sword and they would go to him and use the swords to strike him in unison. They would kill him and we would then be rid of him. If they do this, his blood will be spread over all the tribes. And the Banu 'Abd Manaf will not be able to do battle against them all. So they will accept blood money which we can pay them." "'The Najdi sheikh commented, 'What he says is right. This is the right idea, and no other.' "'Having agreed upon this, the assembly broke up. "'Gabriel then came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him, "Do not sleep tonight on the bed you usually use."
lessons: Even the most powerful and coordinated opposition cannot overcome divine protection when one places sincere trust in God.
Enemies may plan extensively against the righteous, but God is the best of planners — their schemes often unravel completely.
Receiving warning or guidance in a time of danger is a reminder to act wisely and trust the process, not to succumb to panic.
practical_advice: When facing threats or opposition that feel overwhelming, remind yourself that you cannot control every external force, but you can trust in God's protection and take the wise precautions available to you.
If you feel surrounded by hostility or people who wish you harm, channel your energy into planning your next right step rather than fixating on your enemies' plans.
Recite prayers of protection before sleeping or in moments of fear, knowing that seeking divine refuge is both spiritually powerful and psychologically grounding.

title: Ali Sleeps in the Prophet's Bed as the Prophet Escapes
summary: The Prophet instructed Ali ibn Abi Talib to sleep in his bed wrapped in his green cloak while he slipped past the armed Quraysh gathered at his door, miraculously passing through them unseen while sprinkling dirt on their heads.
story: When it was fully dark that night the Quraysh men gathered outside his door, waiting for him to sleep so they could attack him. Having seen where they were, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'Ali b. Abu Talib, 'Sleep on my bed, wrapping yourself in this green cloak of mine from Hadramaut; sleep in it. You'll not come to any harm from them.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) was in the habit of sleeping in that cloak of his. Yazid b. Aba Yaad related to me, from Muhammad b. Ka'b al-Qurazi who said, 'The men having met there at his door, Abu Jahl, who was among them, said, "Muhammad claims that if you follow him you'll become kings of the Arabs and non-Arabs alike. Then you'll be given life after death and be provided with gardens like those of the Jordan. If you don't do that, then, he says, you will be slaughtered, given life after death but be put into hell-fire where you will be burned."' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then picked up a handful of dirt, saying, 'Yes, I do say that. And you are one of them!' Then God took away their sight from them and they could not see him. He began sprinkling the dirt on to their heads while he recited the following verses: 'Yasin. By the Qur'an the wise. You are among those sent forth upon a straight path' up to the verse 'We have placed a barrier before them and a barrier behind them, and have covered them over so that they cannot see' (surat Ya-Sin XXXVI, v.3-9). Every single man among them had dirt thrown on his head. He then left, and went where he wished. Someone who had not been there with these men then arrived and asked, 'What are you waiting for here?' They replied, 'For Muhammad.' He commented, 'God damn you! Muhammad just came out to you and he did not leave any single one of you without throwing dirt on your head. He then left and went elsewhere. Can't you see what has happened to you?' Each man then placed a hand on his head and found dirt there. Looking closely, they saw 'Ali on the bed covered in the cloak of the Messenger of God (SAAS). They stayed there like that until morning; when 'Ali arose from the bed, they said, 'By God, what he told us must have been true!'
lessons: True courage is demonstrated by those like Ali who willingly sacrifice their own safety for a cause greater than themselves.
Divine assistance can manifest in ways beyond human comprehension — what seems impossible to escape from may already have been resolved by God.
The willingness to trust God completely, as both the Prophet and Ali did, brings a calm and certainty that no human threat can disturb.
practical_advice: When you feel trapped in a situation with no visible way out, take the steps within your ability and then trust that God can open paths you cannot yet see.
Reflect on times when you or someone you love has been protected from harm unexpectedly — use these memories to strengthen your faith during current difficulties.
If you are asked to make a sacrifice for someone or something you believe in, consider that such acts of loyalty and trust are among the most spiritually elevating experiences a person can have.

title: God Reveals Verses About the Quraysh's Plot
summary: Following the failed assassination plot against the Prophet, God revealed Quranic verses affirming that the disbelievers' plan to confine, kill, or exile the Prophet was foiled because God is the best of planners.
story: Concerning what they had agreed upon that day, God revealed, among others, the following verses, 'And when the disbelievers plotted to confine, kill or exile you they made plans; but God makes plans and He is the best planner of all' (surat al-Anfal; VIII, v.30). And there was the verse, 'Or do they say he is a poet; we await for him incidents of fate.' Say: 'Do await! I with you am one who awaits!' (surat al-Tur, LII, v.30-1). And so God gave His permission to His Prophet (SAAS) to emigrate.
lessons: Divine scripture can serve as a source of deep reassurance when we have faced danger or injustice — knowing that God sees and responds to oppression gives the heart peace.
The Quran being revealed in direct response to real events shows that faith is not abstract but deeply connected to lived human experience.
God's permission to emigrate came only after the community had endured great hardship — divine relief often follows a period of patience and perseverance.
practical_advice: When you feel that people are plotting against you or undermining your efforts, return to scripture or prayer to ground yourself in the knowledge that ultimate authority belongs to God alone.
Memorize or reflect upon Quran 8:30 ('God is the best of planners') as a practical anchor for moments when human opposition feels insurmountable.
Understand that hardship often precedes a breakthrough — when you feel at your lowest, consider that this may be the threshold before God's permission for a new chapter arrives.

title: The Prophet's Departure from Mecca — His Prayer at the Threshold of Emigration
summary: On the day God granted him permission to emigrate, the Prophet left Mecca and offered a deeply moving prayer seeking divine protection, companionship on the journey, and refuge from all hardship.
story: As he was about to leave Mecca as an emigrant to Medina for God, he said, 'Praise be to God who created me when I had been nothing. O God, protect me from earthly terrors, misfortunes and mishaps in the nights and days to come. O God, accompany me on my journey and keep my family safe. Bless me in what You have granted me and humble me before Yourself. Raise me to the finest qualities in my character. Endear me to You, O Lord. And do not entrust (my fate) to people. O Lord of the frail, you are my Lord. I take refuge in Your noble visage before which the heavens and the earth rejoice, the dark shadows dissipate and the troubles of those who are first and those who are last are made right. (I pray) that You spare me your anger and discontent. I appeal to You not to cease your favours and to spare me your sudden wrath, removal of your favour and all your anger. I will repay as best I can all your favours to me. And there is no power nor strength except in You.'
lessons: Beginning any significant journey or life transition with prayer and gratitude to God orients the heart toward reliance on Him rather than on human means alone.
The Prophet's prayer acknowledges vulnerability and weakness ('Lord of the frail') — admitting our limitations before God is a sign of spiritual maturity, not weakness.
Asking God to 'endear me to You' shows that the deepest human longing is for a connection with the Divine, which sustains us through every worldly trial.
practical_advice: Whenever you face a major transition — a move, a job change, a loss — take a quiet moment to pray and surrender the outcome to God, as the Prophet did at the threshold of emigration.
Adapt the spirit of this prayer for your own life: ask God to protect you from unseen harms, keep your loved ones safe, and elevate your character through the journey ahead.
Reciting 'La hawla wa la quwwata illa billah' (there is no power nor strength except in God) is a practical daily reminder of the same truth the Prophet expressed in this prayer.

title: Abu Bakr Weeps for Joy at Being Chosen as the Prophet's Companion in Migration
summary: When the Prophet arrived at Abu Bakr's home at an unusual hour and announced God's permission to emigrate, asking Abu Bakr to accompany him, Abu Bakr wept with overwhelming joy — the first time Aisha had ever seen someone weep from happiness.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) would go without fail to Abu Bakr's house either in the morning or the afternoon. Eventually that day arrived when God gave the Messenger of God (SAAS) permission to emigrate, to leave Mecca and his people there. He came to us that day at midday, a time unusual for him. When Abu Bakr saw him, he said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) can only have come at this hour because something has happened.' When he came in, Abu Bakr gave up his couch for him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat down. There was no one else in the house except myself and my sister Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Send away anyone else who is with you.' Abu Bakr replied, 'There are only my two daughters; what can be wrong, I pray you?' 'God has given me permission to leave, to emigrate,' he replied. 'As companions then, O Messenger of God?' Abu Bakr asked. 'Yes, as companions,' he answered. A'isha continued, 'And, I swear, before that day I never knew anyone who wept for joy until I saw Abu Bakr do so that day. He then said, "O Prophet of God, these are two camels I have readied for this."'
lessons: True friendship is demonstrated by preparation and readiness — Abu Bakr had already purchased camels in anticipation of being needed, showing that genuine love is expressed through action, not just feeling.
Tears of joy are among the most profound human experiences; they arise when something we have hoped for deeply is finally granted, reminding us that hope is always worth holding onto.
Being chosen to serve a noble cause or to stand beside someone we love and admire is itself a profound gift from God, worthy of deep gratitude.
practical_advice: Reflect on the relationships in your life where someone has shown readiness and preparation on your behalf — express gratitude for those people and let them know what their loyalty means to you.

If you are going through a period of loneliness or feeling unsupported, consider that deep companionship like that of the Prophet and Abu Bakr is built over years of consistent presence — invest in your relationships daily.
Allow yourself to feel and express joy openly when blessings arrive. Abu Bakr's tears of joy are a reminder that it is beautiful and human to be moved by gratitude.

title: The Prophet and Abu Bakr Hide in the Cave of Thawr
summary: The Prophet and Abu Bakr fled Mecca and hid in a cave on Mount Thawr while Abu Bakr's son gathered intelligence on Quraysh's movements, his freed-man erased their tracks with his flock, and his daughter Asma brought them food each evening.
story: They then made their way to a cave on Mt. Thawr, south of Mecca, which they entered. Abu Bakr ordered his son 'Abd Allah to spend the day listening to what people were saying about them and then to bring them whatever news there might be. And he told 'Amir b. Fuhayra, his freed-man, to tend his flock during the day and then to bring them to the cave in the evening. 'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr would spend the day among Quraysh listening to their planning and what they were saying regarding the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr. He would then visit them in the evening and report to them. 'Amir b. Fuhayra would tend his flock in the pasturage of the people of Mecca and in the evening he would bring them to Abu Bakr who would milk and slaughter them. When next morning 'Abd Allah, Abu Bakr's son, would leave them to return to Mecca, 'Amir would follow him down with the flock in order to efface his footprints. Each evening Asma', daughter of Abu Bakr, may God be pleased with her, would bring them food to restore them.
lessons: In times of crisis, an entire community of trusted people working together — each in their own capacity — can protect and sustain those who are vulnerable.
Every role, however small it may seem, matters: erasing footprints, bringing food, listening for news — all were equally essential to the mission's success.
Strategic wisdom and careful planning, when combined with trust in God, are marks of mature faith — Islam does not ask us to abandon practical wisdom in favour of passivity.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult situation, identify the people around you who can each play a small but meaningful role in supporting you, rather than carrying every burden alone.

Take inspiration from Asma's quiet consistency — bringing food every evening — as a model for showing up for others with steady, practical acts of care even when the situation is dangerous or difficult.
If you are helping someone in need, ask yourself what specific, concrete action you can take repeatedly and reliably, as that kind of steady support is often more valuable than grand gestures.

title: Asma Bint Abi Bakr is Struck by Abu Jahl While Protecting the Secret of the Migration
summary: When a group of Quraysh including Abu Jahl came to Abu Bakr's door seeking information about the Prophet and Abu Bakr's whereabouts, Asma bint Abi Bakr courageously refused to reveal anything, and was violently struck by Abu Jahl as a result.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr had left, a group of Quraysh, including Abu Jahl b. Hisham, came and stood at Abu Bakr's door. I went out to them and they asked, 'Where is your father, O Daughter of Abu Bakr?' I replied that I did not know, whereupon Abu Jahl, an evil and uncouth man, raised his hand and slapped me so hard on the cheek that my earring fell off. They then left.
lessons: Asma's courage in the face of intimidation and physical violence shows that protecting what is right sometimes demands personal sacrifice, and that such sacrifice is honoured by God.
Oppressors often target those they perceive as weaker or less able to fight back — but history remembers Asma's dignity, not Abu Jahl's violence.
Standing firm under pressure, refusing to betray trust even when threatened with harm, is one of the highest expressions of moral integrity.
practical_advice: If you have experienced or are experiencing intimidation or abuse, know that your dignity is not diminished by what others do to you — your integrity in how you respond is what defines you.

Asma did not give in despite the pain. If you are being pressured to betray someone's trust or to act against your values, remind yourself that the discomfort of holding firm is temporary, but betraying your integrity has lasting consequences.
If you witness or experience injustice, document it and seek support — you do not have to face it alone, just as Asma was surrounded by a community that honoured her courage.

title: Asma bint Abi Bakr Slapped by Abu Jahl
summary: After the Prophet and Abu Bakr secretly departed Mecca, Abu Jahl came to Abu Bakr's house demanding to know their whereabouts. When Asma bint Abi Bakr said she did not know, Abu Jahl struck her so hard on the cheek that her earring fell off.
story: the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Aba Bakr had left, a group of Quraysh, including Abn Jahl b. Hishm, came and stood at Aba Bakr's door. I went out to them and they asked, "Where is your father, 0 Daughter of Aba Bakr?" I replied that I did not know, whereupon Abii Jahl, an evil and uncouth man, raised his hand and slapped me so hard on the cheek that my earring fell off. They then left.
lessons: Standing firm in truth even under physical threat is one of the highest forms of courage.
Protecting those we love sometimes means enduring harm ourselves without compromising their safety.
Oppressors who resort to violence against the innocent reveal their own weakness and moral bankruptcy.
practical_advice: When pressured to betray someone you love, remind yourself that your loyalty in that moment is itself an act of worship and integrity.
If you experience unjust treatment for doing what is right, document your experience and seek support from trusted people rather than suffering alone.
Reflect on the stories of those who endured far greater pain for truth; let their example anchor you when you face injustice.

title: Asma Deceives Abu Quhafa to Protect His Heart
summary: After Abu Bakr took all his money for the migration, his blind father Abu Quhafa arrived worried that the family had been left destitute. Asma placed stones under a cloth to make him think money had been left behind, reassuring the elderly man without revealing the truth.
story: Yahya b. CAbbad h. 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr related to me that his father told him, that his grandmother Asma' said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left, along with Aba Bakr, the latter took all his cash with him, some 5,000 or 6,000 dirhams. My grandfather, Aba Quhafa, whose sight was gone, came in thereafter and commented, "I swear, he's put you all into some difficulty by taking his money with him." 'I replied, "No, father, he left us plenty." I then took some stones and placed them in a space in the house where my father used to put his money and placed a cloth over them. Then I took my grandfather's hand and told him, "Put your hand on this money." He did so and said, "Not bad at all. He did well by leaving you all this. This will be enough money for you." "'But in fact he had left us nothing; I just wanted to reassure the old man."'
lessons: Compassionate wisdom sometimes means shielding vulnerable loved ones from anxiety they cannot act upon.
True sacrifice means giving everything for what you believe in, even material security.
Family members left behind during times of upheaval carry their own quiet burdens of courage.
practical_advice: When a loved one is anxious about something you cannot change, focus on what you can offer them — presence, warmth, and reassurance — rather than forcing them to face a reality that will only cause them distress.
Recognize that those who sacrifice material wealth for a higher cause demonstrate that security comes from trust in God, not from possessions.
If you are the one left behind while others pursue a mission, find dignity in the role you play in protecting and supporting from where you are.

title: Abu Bakr Shields the Prophet on the Way to the Cave
summary: On the journey to the Cave of Thawr, Abu Bakr alternated walking in front of and behind the Prophet to protect him from ambush and pursuit, and when the Prophet's feet became sore, Abu Bakr carried him on his back.
story: Abn al-Qsim al-Baghawi stated, "Dz'od b. 'Amr al-Dabbi related to us, quoting Nzfi' b. Wmar al-Jumahi, from Ibn Abn Mulayka, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) left with Abii Bakr for Mt. Thawr, Abn Bakr would first position himself ahead and then at the rear. The Prophet (SAAS) asked him why this was and he replied, 'When I'm behind you I'm afraid you'll be attacked from the front, and when I'm ahead of you I fear you'll be attacked from behind.' "When they finally reached the cave on Mt. Thawr Abn Bakr said, 'Stay where you are until I put my hand inside and examine it; if there is some creature there it will attack me before you.' "NafiCsaid, 'I have heard that there was a crevice inside the cave in which Abn Bakr placed his foot, fearing that some creature might emerge and harm the Messenger of God (SAAS).'" Al-Bayhaqi related this comment from Wmar through a different chain. In that account the wording is "Abii Bakr sometimes walked ahead of the Messenger of God (SAAS) and at others behind him, sometimes to the left and at others to the right." That account also states that when the feet of the Messenger of God (SAAS) became sore, Abii Bakr would carry h i on his back.
lessons: True friendship means placing yourself between your companion and every conceivable harm, without being asked.
Devotion to a noble cause transforms ordinary acts — walking ahead, walking behind, carrying a friend — into acts of profound love.
Physical hardship borne for a sacred purpose becomes light when the heart is full of sincere intention.
practical_advice: When accompanying someone through a difficult time, think proactively about the threats they may not see — be the one who anticipates and shields.
Let Abu Bakr's example remind you that love is expressed most powerfully not in words but in positioning yourself to absorb what might hurt the one you care for.
When the journey becomes physically or emotionally exhausting, remember that those who walked before you also bled and ached — and kept moving.

title: Abu Bakr Enters the Cave First and Blocks the Crevices
summary: Before allowing the Prophet to enter the Cave of Thawr, Abu Bakr went in first to clear it of dangers, blocked all crevices with his clothing, and placed his heel over the last opening, where snakes bit him and his tears fell — upon which the Prophet comforted him saying, 'Don't be sad; God is with us.'
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The hafi* Abn 'Abd All& informed us, quoting Abii Bakr A b a d b. Ishaq, that Mnsa b. al-Hasan narrated that (Abbad related, quoting 'Affm b. Muslim, quoting al-Sari b. Y&a, quoting Muhammad b. Sirin, as follows, 'Some men were talking during the period of the rule of Wmar and apparently expressed their preference for Wmar over Abii Bakr. This reached 'Umar and he commented, "By God, a single night or a single day of Abii Bakr would be better than the whole clan of Wmar! On the night when the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the cave with Abii Bakr, the latter would walk in front for a whie, then walk behind. Eventually the Messenger of God (SAAS) realized this and asked, 'Abn Bakr, why do you walk behind for a while, then go and walk ahead?' He replied, '0 Messenger of God, I think of pursuit and walk behind you, but then I think of ambush and so walk ahead of you.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, 'You mean if something happened you'd rather it be to you than to me?' 'Yes indeed, by Him who sent you with the truth,' he replied. ""'When they reached the cave Abii Bakr said, 'Stay outside, 0 Messenger of God, until I make sure the cave is safe for you.' He went inside and made sure it was safe, but then remembered he had not checked out the crevice. So he said, 'Stay were you are, 0 Messenger of God, while I check again.' He then went back in, made sure the crevice was safe and said, 'Come on down, 0 Messenger of God.' And he did so." "'Wmar then commented, "By Him who holds my soul in His hand, that night was better than the whole clan of Wmar!"'" That account also states that when he went into the cave he blocked up all the crevices except one, and over that he placed his heel. Snakes then struck at him and his tears ran down. Seeing this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, "Don't be sad; God is with us!"
lessons: The words 'God is with us' spoken in a moment of pain and fear are among the most powerful sources of comfort a person can receive or offer.
Selfless service — absorbing harm so that another is spared — is the purest expression of love.
Umar's honest acknowledgment that Abu Bakr's single night surpassed his own entire lineage is a lesson in humility and recognizing virtue in others.
practical_advice: When you are afraid and in pain, recall the Prophet's words: 'Don't be sad; God is with us.' Let this be an anchor you return to whenever anxiety overwhelms you.
Practice recognizing and openly acknowledging the sacrifices of others; Umar's honest praise of Abu Bakr strengthened the community rather than diminishing himself.
When you feel compelled to protect someone you love, trust that impulse — even small, unseen acts of protection carry immense weight with God.

title: Abu Bakr's Verse When His Hand Was Struck in the Cave
summary: Inside the Cave of Thawr, Abu Bakr struck his hand on a rock and, rather than crying out in complaint, responded by reciting a verse dedicating his suffering to God's cause.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The informed us as follows, 'Abii al-'Abbas d-Asamm related to us, quoting 'Abbns al-Dciri, quoting Aswad h. 'Amir Shndhan quoting Isrs'il, from al-Aswad, from Jundub h. 'Abd Allah, who said, "Aba Bakr was with the Messenger of God (SAAS) in the cave when he hit his hand on a rock. He then spoke the following verse: 'You are nothing but a finger that bleeds; what happened to you was in God's cause!'""'
lessons: Reframing physical pain as an offering to God transforms suffering into dignity.
Poetry and words of remembrance can serve as immediate spiritual medicine in moments of acute pain.
A person of faith does not suppress their pain — they redirect it toward a higher meaning.
practical_advice: When you are hurting, try putting your experience into words that connect it to something larger than yourself — this is a time-honored way of finding meaning in suffering.
Keep a phrase or verse that centers you spiritually; Abu Bakr had his verse ready, and it steadied him in a moment of shock and pain.
Do not feel ashamed of physical or emotional pain; acknowledge it honestly, as Abu Bakr did, and then lift it toward God.

title: Quraysh's Plot to Imprison, Kill, or Exile the Prophet — and Ali Sleeps in His Bed
summary: Quraysh gathered and debated three options for dealing with the Prophet — imprisonment, killing, or exile. God informed the Prophet of their plan, so Ali ibn Abi Talib slept in the Prophet's bed while the Prophet slipped away, and when Quraysh attacked at dawn they found Ali instead.
story: Imw Ahmad stated, "'Ahd al-Razznq related to us, quoting M a h a r , quoting 'Uthmw al-Jazari, that Miqsam, the freed-man of Ibn 'Abbns, told him that Ibn cAhb% made the following comment about the verse in the Qur'w. 'And when those who disbelieve were scheming to imprison you' (siirat 41-Anfzl; VIII, v.30). He said, 'Quraysh were engaged in discussion one night in MecLa and some of them suggested, "In the morning imprison him in shackles." They were referring to the Prophet (SAAS). Others of them said, "No; kill him!" Yet others said, "No, exile him!" God made his Prophet (SAAS), aware of that and so 'Ali spent the night on his bed, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the cave. The polytheists spent the night watching 'Ali, thinking him to be the Prophet (SAAS). When morning came they attacked him and when they saw 'Ali, God turned their trickery against them. They said, "Where is that master of yours?" He replied, "I don't know." "'They followed his tracks, but these became too confused for them in the mountains. They climbed on up the mountain and passed by the cave but saw a spider's web over its opening. They said, "If anyone had gone inside here, the spider would not have put a web over its opening." And so he stayed there three nights.'"
lessons: God's protection operates through both the extraordinary and the ordinary — a confused trail and a spider's web are as mighty as any army.
Ali's willingness to sleep in the Prophet's bed knowing men had come to kill demonstrates that courage is not the absence of fear but the decision to act despite it.
When enemies plot with all their cunning, the believer's trust is not in outwitting them but in God who knows and overturns every scheme.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by threats you cannot control, remind yourself that God's protection does not require your full understanding — it only requires your trust.
Consider the role Ali played: he did not seek recognition for his sacrifice, he simply stepped forward when it was needed. Look for opportunities to quietly serve others who are vulnerable.
When the plans of those who wish you harm fall apart despite their efforts, pause and reflect — this is an invitation to gratitude, not merely relief.

title: The Spider's Web and the Doves Protect the Cave
summary: As Quraysh warriors approached the Cave of Thawr, a spider had woven a web over its entrance and two wild doves had nested there, convincing the trackers that no one had entered, and so the Prophet and Abu Bakr remained safe inside.
story: The hzj? Aba Bakr Ahmad b. 'All b. Sa'id al-di stated in his collection of traditions relating to Aba Bakr as follows, 'Bashsh%r al-Khaff~frelated to us, quoting Ja'far, quoting Sulaymm, quoting Abii 'Imran al-Jawni, quoting al-Mu'alla b. Ziyad, that d-Hasan al-Basri said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) hurried away with Abii Bakr to the cave. Quraysh came looking for him and when they saw the spider's web over the mouth of the cave, they said, "No one went in here." "'The Prophet (SAAS) was at the time standing there praying while Aha Bakr watched. Aba Bakr then said to the Prophet (SAAS), "Those are your people searching for you. It's not for myself I am sad, but because I might see something terrible happen to you." "'The Prophet (SAAS) replied, "Abn Bakr, don't be afraid. God is with us.""' Also in this hadith is the information that the tracker who guided the warriors was Suraqa. The hadith dealing with this was related by the hiif;? Ibn 'As&ir, through Yahyn b. Muhammad b. Sa'id, quoting ' A m b. 'Ali, who said that 'Awn b. 'Amr Ahii 'Amr al-Qaysi, nicknamed Wwayn, related to us that Aba Mus'ab al-Makki said, "I am aware of Zayd b. Arqam, al-Mughira b. Shu'ba and Auas b. M~likas relating that on the night the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the cave, God gave an order to a tree that emerged right in front of him and hid him. God also sent the spider that made a web between them, so hiding the face of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then commanded two wild doves which came fluttering down and alighted between the spider and the tree. Now the young warriors of Quraysh approached, one from each of the tribes there, carrying sticks, bows and staves. When they got to withii 200 yards of the Messenger of God (SAAS) the guide, who was S w q a b. Malik b. Ju'shum al-Mudlaji, said, 'That's the rock; but I don't know where he placed his foot.' The young warriors commented, 'You've not made a mistake since tonight began.' When morning came the guide told them to look in the cave. He went ahead of the rest until they were some 50 yards away from the Prophet (SAAS), and then there were the two doves. The guide came back and they asked hi,'What prevented you from looking in the cave?' LI saw two wild doves at its entrance', he said, 'so I knew there was no one inside.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) heard h i and knew that God had used the doves to save him and Abn Bakr. He then invoked a blessing upon them, and God brought them down to the holy shrine where, as you know, they proliferated."
lessons: God protects His servants through means that the powerful and well-armed never think to anticipate — a spider's thread can be stronger than iron when God wills it.
The Prophet's immediate response upon being saved was gratitude and blessing, not triumph over his enemies; this is the mark of a heart connected to God.
Abu Bakr's grief in the cave was not for himself but for the Prophet — this selfless orientation of concern is itself a spiritual station.
practical_advice: When you feel that your means of protection are too small for the threat you face, remember the spider and the doves. God's help does not always arrive in the form you expect.
After a moment of relief or rescue, follow the Prophet's example: pause, give thanks, and extend blessing to whatever or whoever was part of your protection.
If you are hiding your pain or fear to protect someone else, as Abu Bakr did, know that this quiet courage is seen and honoured — you do not need to announce your sacrifice for it to matter.

title: Umar Praises Abu Bakr's Night Above His Own Legacy
summary: Upon hearing that some companions preferred him over Abu Bakr, Umar ibn al-Khattab declared that a single night of Abu Bakr — the night of the cave — was worth more than Umar's entire lineage, honouring the depth of Abu Bakr's sacrifice.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The hafi* Abn 'Abd All& informed us, quoting Abii Bakr A b a d b. Ishaq, that Mnsa b. al-Hasan narrated that (Abbad related, quoting 'Affm b. Muslim, quoting al-Sari b. Y&a, quoting Muhammad b. Sirin, as follows, 'Some men were talking during the period of the rule of Wmar and apparently expressed their preference for Wmar over Abii Bakr. This reached 'Umar and he commented, "By God, a single night or a single day of Abii Bakr would be better than the whole clan of Wmar!... "'Wmar then commented, "By Him who holds my soul in His hand, that night was better than the whole clan of Wmar!"'"
lessons: True greatness of character includes the ability to recognize and publicly honour virtue in others, even when it surpasses your own.
A single sincere act done at the right moment with the right heart can outweigh a lifetime of ordinary deeds.
Humility before obvious truth is not weakness — it is the quality that made Umar himself great.
practical_advice: Practice naming the contributions and sacrifices of people around you openly and specifically — this builds community and reflects spiritual maturity.
Reflect on whether there is someone in your life whose quiet sacrifice you have overlooked; find a way to acknowledge them today.
When you feel overshadowed by another's virtue, resist the urge to compete; instead, let their example raise your own standard.

title: Abu Bakr Advises His Son to Use the Cave for Refuge
summary: Abu Bakr told his son that if trouble ever came to the people, he should go to the cave of Thawr where he and the Prophet had sheltered, promising that sustenance would come to him there morning and night.
story: This same source, I mean Abo Bakr Ahmad b. 'Ali al-@di, related from 'Amr al-Naqid, from Khalaf b. Tamim, from Miisi b. Mupr, from his father, from AbO Hurayra, that Aba Bakr said to his son, "Son, if something should happen among the people, then go to the cave where I and the Messenger of God (SAAS) took refuge. Stay there and you will find sustenance come to you both morning and night."
lessons: A parent's greatest inheritance to a child is not wealth but the transmission of sacred memory and the knowledge of where refuge lies.
The believer trusts that a place sanctified by the presence of the righteous retains a spiritual protection worth returning to.
Preparing those we love for times of trial is an act of love that extends beyond our own lifetimes.
practical_advice: Think about what 'cave' — what place of refuge, practice, or community — you would direct a loved one to in a time of crisis. Have that conversation before the crisis arrives.
Sacred spaces and memories can be genuine sources of comfort; do not dismiss the value of returning in heart or body to places where you have experienced God's care.
Pass on stories of resilience and divine protection to the young people in your life — they are a form of provision for the trials ahead.

title: God's Protection in the Cave: Abu Bakr's Fear and the Prophet's Reassurance
summary: While hiding in the Cave of Thawr, Abu Bakr expressed fear that the searching polytheists would discover them, but the Prophet reassured him that God was their third companion, and they were divinely protected.
story: God Almighty said, "Even if you do not aid him, God did so when those who disbelieved expelled him, he being the second of two, in the cave. (Therein) he said to his companion, 'Do not be sad; God is with us.' And so God sent down tranquillity upon him and aided him with troops you do not see, putting down very low the words of those who disbelieved; the words of God are the highest, for God is powerful, wise" (surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.40). Reprimanding those who refrained from engaging in battle on the side of the Messenger of God (SAAS) the Almighty stated, "Even if you do not aid him." God will assist him, be his helper and ally. As He did help him, "when those who disbelieved expelled him" from his people in Mecca, in flight and accompanied by no one but his friend and Companion Abu Bakr. Therefore He said, "being the second of two, when they were in the cave" where they stayed for three days until the search for them died down. This was because when the polytheists lost track of the two men, as recounted above, they went off in all directions searching for them. They offered 100 camels to anyone who might turn in both or even one of them. They followed their tracks until they became confused. The tracker working for Quraysh was Suraqa b. Malik b. Ju'shum, as mentioned above. They climbed the mountain where the two men were and went past the mouth of the cave. Their feet would move straight across the mouth of the cave without seeing the two men, God protecting them this way. As Imam Ahmad stated, quoting 'Affan, quoting Hammam, quoting Thabit, from Anas b. Malik, that Abu Bakr told him, "I said to the Prophet (SAAS), while we were inside the cave, 'If any one of them were to glance down at his feet he would see us below them!' "He replied, 'Abu Bakr, how would you regard the safety of two people who had God as their third companion?'" Al-Bukhari and Muslim gave this hadith in both their sahih collections, from Hammam.
lessons: True trust in God transforms fear into tranquillity — the Prophet's certainty that God was with them was not naive optimism but a deeply rooted conviction that replaced anxiety with peace.
Divine protection often works through unseen means; the searchers stood at the very mouth of the cave yet saw nothing, reminding us that God's assistance can operate beyond human comprehension.
Companionship in hardship is a mercy — Abu Bakr's presence and the Prophet's reassurance of him models how believers should support one another during times of danger and distress.
practical_advice: When fear overwhelms you in a difficult situation, pause and consciously remind yourself of God's nearness with the phrase 'God is with us' — let it anchor you as it anchored Abu Bakr.
Seek out a trusted companion during times of crisis; sharing your fear with someone of strong faith can help transform panic into calm, just as the Prophet's words transformed Abu Bakr's anxiety.
Reflect on past moments when you were protected or guided through difficulty without fully understanding how — use those memories as evidence of unseen divine support when facing new fears.

title: The Quraysh Plot to Kill, Imprison, or Exile the Prophet
summary: The polytheists of Quraysh conspired together to kill, imprison, or exile the Prophet, prompting God to reveal the plot to him and instruct him to leave Mecca with Abu Bakr while Ali slept in his bed.
story: Ibn Lahi'a related, from Abu al-Aswad, from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained in Mecca after the pilgrimage in which the ansar, 'the Helpers', pledged themselves to him, for the remainder of Dhu al-Hijja, al-Muharram and Safar. "Then the polytheists of Quraysh joined together and plotted to kill the Messenger of God (SAAS) or to imprison or exile him. And so God gave him foresight of this and revealed the words, 'And when those who disbelieved were scheming to imprison you' (surat al-Anfal; VIII, v.30). He then gave orders to 'Ali to sleep on his bed while he and Abu Bakr left. Next morning Quraysh went off searching for them everywhere."
lessons: When enemies plot against the righteous, God's knowledge encompasses their schemes and He prepares a way of escape for those who trust Him.

Courage in the face of mortal danger is demonstrated not by recklessness but by careful, God-guided action — the Prophet's calm departure in the night reflects spiritual steadiness under extreme pressure.
Sacrifice for a noble cause is a mark of true loyalty — Ali's willingness to lie in the Prophet's bed, risking his own life, shows that genuine love requires readiness to bear personal cost.
practical_advice: When you feel surrounded by opposition or hostility, resist the urge to panic and instead seek spiritual guidance through prayer and reflection, trusting that clarity will come.

In situations where you feel threatened, focus on what wise, measured action is available to you rather than fixating on the threat itself — act thoughtfully, as the Prophet did in organizing his departure.
Draw strength from the example of those who have sacrificed for others; ask yourself where you can show loyalty and courage in your own relationships and responsibilities today.

title: Abu Bakr's Attempted Migration to Abyssinia and Return to Mecca
summary: Abu Bakr attempted to emigrate to Abyssinia due to the harassment of Muslims in Mecca, but was intercepted by Ibn al-Daghina who offered him protection and brought him back, which Abu Bakr eventually relinquished in favor of God's protection alone.
story: 'A'isha stated, 'I have no awareness of my parents not practising the religion (Islam); and not a day passed for us without the Messenger of God (SAAS) visiting us at both ends of the day, in the morning and the evening. When the Muslims suffered harassment Abu Bakr left as an emigrant towards Abyssinia. He went as far as Bark al-Ghimad, where he met Ibn al-Daghina, the chieftain of the area.' She then recounted how he brought Abu Bakr back to Mecca and gave him protection. Her words are similar to our previous account of the migration to Abyssinia up to and including Abu Bakr's statement, "I therefore give back to you your protection and resign myself to the protection of God."
lessons: There are moments when worldly protection and alliances, however well-intentioned, are not the ultimate refuge — Abu Bakr's return to God's protection alone models the highest form of reliance on the divine.
Persecution and forced displacement are among the most painful trials a believer can face, yet history shows that those who endure them with faith are remembered with honor.
Knowing when to accept help and when to release it in favor of a deeper trust in God is itself a spiritual maturity that develops through lived experience.
practical_advice: When you feel forced out of a place or community you love, allow yourself to grieve that loss genuinely rather than suppressing it — grief is a natural part of any significant departure.
Reflect on whether the sources of security you rely on are truly sustainable; sometimes the most empowering step is to release a lesser protection and place your full trust in God.

If you find yourself in a situation where your dignity or faith is compromised by the conditions of someone else's protection, consider whether independence — even if harder — better preserves your integrity.

title: The Prophet's Vision of Medina and Preparation for Migration
summary: The Prophet told the Muslims he had been shown the place of their emigration — a land with palm groves between two rocky tracts — and asked Abu Bakr to wait so they could travel together, while Abu Bakr spent months preparing two camels for the journey.
story: She ('A'isha) stated, "At that time the Prophet (SAAS) was at Mecca. He told the Muslims, 'I have been shown the place of your emigration; it has palm-groves between two rocky tracts.' These are the Harratan. "And so some people did leave for Medina, and some who had left for Abyssinia came back and went to Medina. Abu Bakr himself made preparations to leave for Medina. The Messenger of God (SAAS) told him, 'Take it slowly; I am hoping I will be permitted to leave.' Abu Bakr asked, 'You really do have hopes of that?' 'Yes,' he replied. "Thereafter Abu Bakr restrained himself for the Messenger of God (SAAS), so that he could accompany him. He stabled two camels at his home, feeding them on mimosa leaves, crushed and mixed leaves, that is, for four months. Some, however, say he gave them fodder for six months."
lessons: Hope grounded in divine guidance is not wishful thinking but a confident expectation that shapes one's practical preparations — Abu Bakr's months of careful provision reflect faith made visible through action.
Patience in waiting for the right moment, even when departure feels urgent, is itself a form of wisdom and trust in God's timing.
God's guidance often comes in the form of vision or inner knowing that precedes outward confirmation — remaining attentive to such guidance while acting prudently is a mark of spiritual discernment.
practical_advice: When you are preparing for a major life transition, balance your eagerness with patience — rushing before the right moment can undermine what careful preparation would have secured.
Use periods of waiting productively, as Abu Bakr did, by making tangible preparations that align with the direction you believe you are heading.
Reflect on times when waiting turned out to be the right decision; let those memories strengthen your trust in divine timing when you are tempted to act prematurely out of anxiety.

title: The Departure from Mecca: The Prophet Visits Abu Bakr at Noon
summary: The Prophet arrived unexpectedly at Abu Bakr's home at noon, heavily veiled, to announce he had received permission to emigrate; Abu Bakr offered his camels and his daughter Asma' tied the provisions bag with her own girdle, earning her a lasting honorific.
story: Ibn Shihab stated, "'Urwa reported that 'A'isha said, 'One day, in the heat of noon, we were sitting in Abu Bakr's house when someone told him, "Here comes the Messenger of God (SAAS), heavily veiled, at a time he doesn't usually come!" Abu Bakr commented, "I swear, he can only have come at this time for one thing!"' "'A'isha went on, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived and asked to come in and was invited to do so. When he entered he said, "Ask everyone to leave." Abu Bakr responded, "But they are all like your own family, O Messenger of God." "'He explained, "I have been given permission to leave." "As companions, then!" said Abu Bakr. "Yes," he replied. "'Abu Bakr then said, "Do take one of these two mounts of mine." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "I'll pay you for it."' "'A'isha went on, 'So we quickly prepared some equipment for them and put some provisions in a leather bag. Asma', Abu Bakr's daughter, cut off a piece of her girdle to tie up the mouth of the bag; she was thereafter known as "she of the two girdles".'"
lessons: Sacred moments often arrive unexpectedly and at inconvenient times — Abu Bakr's immediate recognition of the significance of the Prophet's unusual visit shows the value of spiritual attentiveness.

Generosity in times of crisis is most meaningful when it is immediate and unconditional — Abu Bakr's offer of his camel without hesitation, and the Prophet's insistence on paying, both reflect the nobility of their characters.
Even small acts of sacrifice, like Asma' tearing her own girdle to secure the provisions bag, are remembered and honored; no act of service in a worthy cause is too insignificant.
practical_advice: Practice being present and attentive enough to recognize important moments when they arrive, rather than being so absorbed in routine that significant opportunities pass unnoticed.
When someone you care about is in urgent need, offer your resources freely and immediately — the moment of crisis is not the time for hesitation or elaborate negotiation.
Remember that you do not need to perform grand gestures to make a meaningful contribution; sometimes a small, practical act done wholeheartedly is what makes all the difference.

title: Three Nights in the Cave: The Network of Loyal Companions
summary: During three nights hiding in the Cave of Thawr, Abu Bakr's son gathered intelligence from Mecca, his freed-man Amir ibn Fuhayra brought sheep for milk and food, and a trusted guide was hired to lead the Prophet and Abu Bakr safely to Medina.
story: 'Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) went with Abu Bakr to a cave on Mt. Thawr, where they stayed for three nights. Also with them during the night was 'Abd Allah, Abu Bakr's son. He was a bright and intelligent lad and would leave them at dawn and be down among Quraysh by morning, as if he had spent the night there. He went back to them as soon as night fell with all the information he had gleaned of plans against them. 'Amir b. Fuhayra, Abu Bakr's freed-man, cared for a flock of sheep, which he would bring them after night fell; they would eat foods made with the milk they provided. Before dawn 'Amir b. Fuhayra would call the flock together and leave; he did this each one of those three nights. 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr hired a man of the Banu al-Dil, from the Banu 'Abd b. 'Adi, a skilled guide, a khirrit. This man, though a follower of the faith of the Quraysh polytheists, had sworn an oath to the family of al-'As b. Wa'il al-Sahmi. They had thus placed their trust in him and handed over to him their two riding camels and made an arrangement with him to meet at the cave on Mt. Thawr three nights thereafter; he was to bring their mounts on the following morning. This guide and 'Amir b. Fuhayra did set off with the two men, conducting them along the coastal route.'
lessons: In times of great trial, God provides help through a network of faithful people each playing their unique role — recognizing and honoring these contributions builds community resilience.

Trustworthiness transcends religious affiliation; the Prophet entrusted their lives to a skilled guide who did not share their faith, demonstrating that competence and integrity can be found in unexpected places.
Collective sacrifice — each person contributing what they uniquely could, whether intelligence, food, or guidance — is often what carries a noble cause through its most dangerous passage.
practical_advice: When facing a major challenge, take stock of the different people around you and how their unique skills or access might help — solutions often emerge from a network of small contributions rather than one heroic act.

Do not overlook the humble, consistent acts of service in your community; those who quietly show up every day, like Amir ibn Fuhayra bringing his sheep each night, are often the ones who sustain the mission.
When you must rely on someone outside your immediate circle of shared values, assess their character and track record carefully — trustworthiness demonstrated through action is a reliable guide.

title: Suraqa ibn Malik Pursues the Prophet and Abu Bakr
summary: Suraqa ibn Malik, lured by the Quraysh reward, secretly followed the Prophet and Abu Bakr along the coast after concealing his knowledge of their whereabouts from his tribe, and rode out in pursuit with his spear.
story: Ibn Shihab stated, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Malik al-Mudliji, he being the son of Suraqa's nephew, related that his father told him that he heard Suraqa b. Malik b. Ju'sham say, 'Messengers from the Quraysh polytheists came to us putting a price on the head of either the Messenger of God (SAAS) or Abu Bakr for anyone who killed or captured either. "'While I was sitting at a meeting being held by my people, the Banu Mudlij, one of their men arrived and stood there, while we sat, and said, "Suraqa, I've just seen some human forms in the distance towards the coast, and I think them to be Muhammad and his companions." "'Suraqa said, "I knew they would be them, but I told him, 'They are not them. You saw so-and-so and so-and-so whom we saw leave with our own eyes.'" "'I remained for a while there in that meeting, then got up and went inside. I told my servant girl to bring over my mare which was behind a hillock and to keep it there for me. Then I took my spear and went out the rear of the house. I made marks with its head on the ground and kept its shaft down low until I reached my horse. I rode off on it and it took me ahead until I drew close to them. Suddenly my horse stumbled and I came off it. I got up and stretched my hand out to my quiver and took out my'"
lessons: Greed and worldly reward can motivate people to pursue harmful actions against the innocent — recognizing this dynamic helps us understand the moral pressures people face and the importance of integrity.
The vulnerability of those who are fleeing injustice is real and dangerous; their protection ultimately rests in God's hands, as the subsequent miraculous events in Suraqa's story demonstrate.
Deception driven by self-interest, as Suraqa showed when he misled his own kinsmen to gain a head start in the pursuit, ultimately fails when confronted with divine protection.
practical_advice: Reflect on whether any of your current motivations are being driven primarily by material reward in a way that compromises your integrity or causes harm to others.
When you feel fear on behalf of someone vulnerable who is being pursued or harmed unjustly, channel that concern into prayer and whatever practical support you are able to offer.
Consider moments in your own life when pursuit of a worldly gain led you down a path that ultimately did not serve your deeper values — use that reflection to make more aligned choices going forward.

title: Suraqa's Pursuit: Horse Sinking and the Prophet's Protection
summary: During the Hijra, Suraqa ibn Malik pursued the Prophet and Abu Bakr seeking the Quraysh reward, but his horse repeatedly sank into the ground and he was thrown off multiple times, convincing him the Prophet was divinely protected. He ultimately sought a safe-conduct document from the Prophet and later embraced Islam.
story: got up and went inside. I told my servant girl to bring over my mare which was behind a hillock and to keep it there for me. Then I took my spear and went out the rear of the house. I made marks with its head on the ground and kept its shaft down low until I reached my horse. I rode off on it and it took me ahead until I drew close to them. Suddenly my horse stumbled and I came off it. I got up and stretched my hand out to my quiver and took out my divining arrows. I sought guidance from them whether or not I should harm them. It came out as I did not want. But I got hack on my horse, disobeying the arrows. "'My horse drew me ever nearer to them until I could hear the Mcsscngcr of God (SAAS) reciting, without looking around, while Aha Bakr looked around constantly. Then two of my horse's legs sank deep into the ground, right up to the knees, and I was again thrown. I scolded her and she stood up, though scarcely able to pull her legs out. When standing upright there was dust coming up like smoke into the sky from the impression left by her legs. Again I sought advice from the divining arrows and it again came out against my wish. "'I called out to them, reassuring them, and they stopped. I rode my horse up to them, it having dawned on me that my being kept from them was a sign that the Messenger of God (SAAS) was going to prevail. I told h i , "Your people have put a price on your head!" and I related to them what people wanted to do with them. Then I offered them provisions, but they would not accept anything from me and told me to leave them alone. I asked him if he would write down a safe conduct for me, and he told 'Amir b. Fuhayra who did so on a piece of leather. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then left.'" Muhammad b. Ishaq related this same story, from al-Zuhri, from 'Abd al-Rahm~nb. Mzhk b. Ju'sham, from his father, from his uncle Suraqa. However, his account has him seeking advice from the divining arrows when he first left the house, with the arrow telling him what he did not like, that is, that he should not harm the Messenger of God (SAAS). It went on to mention his horse stumbling with him on four different occasions. All that related to his seeking advice from the divining arrows and being told what he did not likr- that he should not harm him. It has him reassuring them and asking for a document that would be a token from the Messenger of God (SAAS). The account states, "He wrote a document for me on a bone, or a scrap of leather, or a scrap of cloth." He also tells how he went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) when the latter was at al-Jicr%na on his way back from Ta'if and that he said, "Today is one of keeping trust and of goodness; let him come near." The account ends, "So I approached him and accepted Islam." Ibn H i s h m stated, "The man relating this was 'Abd al-R&man b. al-Harith b. Malik b. Ju'sham. "And what he said was correct. "After Suri%qareturned, whenever he met one of the pursuers, he would turn them hack, saying, 'You've finished in this direction.' "When it was known that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had arrived in Medina, Suraqa began telling people what he knew and had witnessed relating to him, and about what had happened to his horse. He gained some notoriety for this, and the leaders of Quraysh were afraid he would bring them discredit, and that that would he the cause of many people accepting Islam. Suraqa was the leader of the Bana Mudlij. Abo Jahl, God damn him, composed the following verses to them, "0 Banii Mudlij, I fear your fool Sursqa misleading about Muhammad's victory. Take care that he not divide you so you become split, after former glory and leadership." Suraqa spoke the following in answer to Aba Jahl's poetry: "0 Aba at-Hakam, had you but been witness to what happened to my horse when its legs sank, You would have been amazed, and not doubted that Mutpmmad is a prophet and a proof; so who can oppose him? Take action, have your people leave him alone, for I think that some day his qualities will he clear to us. In a matter you would wish to win, for all the people, altogether will make peace with him."
lessons: Divine protection is real and operates through means we may not anticipate — obstacles in Suraqa's path were not coincidences but signs, reminding us that when God wills something, no human effort can obstruct it.

Honest reflection on lived experience can open the heart to truth: Suraqa's repeated encounters with the miraculous broke through his greed and worldly ambition and led him eventually to Islam, showing that sincerity about what one has witnessed is a pathway to guidance.

Integrity after a change of heart matters: once Suraqa recognized the Prophet's truth, he turned back fellow pursuers and publicly defended his testimony, demonstrating that genuine conviction requires courage to act on and speak about.
practical_advice: When you feel blocked or persistently thwarted in a pursuit, pause and honestly ask whether the obstacle might be redirecting you — reflect before pressing forward out of stubbornness alone.

If you witness something that challenges your current worldview or assumptions, give yourself permission to sit with it and examine it seriously rather than dismissing it to protect your comfort zone.
When you change your mind or position on something important, have the courage to say so openly — as Suraqa did publicly — even when those around you are still on the other side.

title: Abu Jahl's Verses Against Suraqa and Suraqa's Poetic Reply
summary: After Suraqa publicly shared his miraculous experience during the Prophet's migration and declared his belief in the Prophet's coming victory, Abu Jahl composed poetry mocking and warning his tribe, and Suraqa responded with verses affirming the Prophet's prophethood and urging peace.
story: Suraqa was the leader of the Bana Mudlij. Abo Jahl, God damn him, composed the following verses to them, "0 Banii Mudlij, I fear your fool Sursqa misleading about Muhammad's victory. Take care that he not divide you so you become split, after former glory and leadership." Suraqa spoke the following in answer to Aba Jahl's poetry: "0 Aba at-Hakam, had you but been witness to what happened to my horse when its legs sank, You would have been amazed, and not doubted that Mutpmmad is a prophet and a proof; so who can oppose him? Take action, have your people leave him alone, for I think that some day his qualities will he clear to us. In a matter you would wish to win, for all the people, altogether will make peace with him." Al-Umawi gave this poetry in his work on the military campaigns with a chain of authorities from Aba Ishsq. Abo Na'aym related it with a chain through Ziysd, from Ihn Ishsq. His account gives more poetry from Aba Jahl with verses that include overt disbelief.
lessons: Standing by truth when social pressure and ridicule target you is itself an act of courage — Suraqa's willingness to face Abu Jahl's mockery and respond clearly shows that conviction must sometimes be defended publicly.

Opposition from powerful people does not invalidate what is true; Abu Jahl's mockery of Suraqa did not change the reality of what Suraqa had witnessed, reminding us not to measure truth by the popularity or power of those who reject it.

Sharing personal testimony about transformative experiences can plant seeds in others' hearts even when the immediate response is hostility — Suraqa's public account worried Quraysh precisely because they knew it could guide others toward truth.
practical_advice: When others mock or belittle a change you have made or a position you have taken, respond with calm evidence and personal testimony rather than matching their hostility — reasoned dignity is more convincing than anger.
Do not allow powerful or influential voices to silence your honest testimony about what you have personally witnessed or experienced; your story has value and can help others.
If someone you respect faces public ridicule for speaking the truth, consider offering your support — even quietly — as social courage often requires knowing one is not entirely alone.

title: Al-Zubayr Clothes the Prophet and Abu Bakr on the Road to Madinah
summary: During the migration, the Prophet and Abu Bakr encountered al-Zubayr leading a caravan of Muslim merchants returning from Syria, who clothed them both in white garments.
story: Al-Bukhiiri stated, with a chain of authorities hack to Ibn Shihiih, who is quoted as saying, "Wrwa h. al-Zuhayr informed me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) met al-Zuhayr in a caravan of Muslims who were merchants coming in from Syria, and that al-Znhayr clothed the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Aha Bakr in white garments."
lessons: Small acts of practical generosity toward those in need — even if those people are great — carry immense weight and are remembered; al-Zubayr's gift of clothing was simple yet significant enough to be preserved in the historical record.

Community members supporting one another during hardship is a cornerstone of resilience; the Muslim merchants going about their trade while also looking after their Prophet models integration of worldly responsibility and communal care.
Generosity requires readiness — al-Zubayr had what was needed and gave it without hesitation, reminding us that preparedness to help is itself a form of virtue.
practical_advice: Look for practical, concrete ways to support people going through hardship rather than only offering words — sometimes what someone needs most is something tangible like food, clothing, or shelter.
When you encounter someone in need while going about your own business, do not let busyness be an excuse to pass by — the brief pause to help can mean everything to the recipient.
Keep in mind that acts of generosity you offer today, however small they seem to you, may be among the most meaningful moments of another person's journey.

title: The Muslims of Madinah Await the Prophet's Arrival
summary: The Muslims of Madinah eagerly went out each morning to the rocky lava plain waiting for the Prophet's arrival, returning home when the heat became unbearable, until a Jewish man spotted the Prophet and his Companions from atop a fort and announced their coming.
story: "The Muslims in Medina heard of the departure of the Messenger of God (SAAS) from Mecca and would go each morning out to the rocky lava plain to wait for him, until the heat of noon would force them back. "One day after they had returned to their homes, having waited a long time, one of the Jews went up on top of one of their forts for some reason, and he saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions dressed in white emerging through the haze. The Jew felt compelled to shout out, '0Arabs, here's your great man you've been awaiting!' "The Muslims leapt for their weapons and met up with the Messenger of God (SAAS) beside the rocky lava plain. He turned off, along with them, to the right to where the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf were; this was Monday in the month of Rahi' al-Awwal."
lessons: Sustained hope expressed through consistent action — the Muslims going out every single morning — is a powerful model of how to wait for relief without falling into passivity or despair.

Divine timing often delivers what we need through unexpected channels; it was not a Muslim but a Jewish observer who first announced the Prophet's arrival, reminding us that good news can come from surprising sources.
The community's collective anticipation and shared longing created a bond that would shape the warmth of the Prophet's reception, showing that shared hope strengthens communal identity and love.
practical_advice: When waiting for something important and uncertain, build a daily practice or ritual around your hope — the act of showing up consistently keeps your intention alive and your spirit engaged.
Remain open to receiving good news or help from unexpected sources; do not limit your expectation of support to only those you would predict.
Share your anticipation and hope with your community — when people wait and hope together, the eventual fulfillment becomes a shared joy that deepens bonds.

title: The Prophet's Arrival at Quba and Abu Bakr Shielding Him from the Sun
summary: When the Prophet arrived at Quba among the Banu Amr ibn Awf, Abu Bakr stood before the crowd while the Prophet sat quietly, causing many Ansar who had not seen the Prophet before to mistakenly greet Abu Bakr as the Prophet, until the sun struck the Prophet and Abu Bakr shielded him with his cloak, revealing which man was the Messenger of God.
story: "Aha Bakr stood up before the group, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) sat silently. The an@r who had come and who had not before seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) began greeting Ahii Bakr. But then the sun began striking the Messenger of God (SAAS), and AbQ Bakr went over and shielded him from it with his cloak. And so everyone then knew which one was the Messenger of God (SAAS). "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there with the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf for some ten nights and founded the mosque Lwhose foundations were based on piety' and the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed there."
lessons: True leaders do not always seek the spotlight — the Prophet sat quietly while Abu Bakr stood before the crowd, and his identity was revealed not by self-promotion but by a simple act of care, reminding us that genuine greatness is often understated.
Loyalty expressed through small, instinctive gestures — like stepping forward to shade someone from the sun — is one of the most authentic forms of love and friendship.

Misunderstandings about important matters often resolve themselves through patient observation; the Ansar's confusion about which man was the Prophet was gently corrected not by argument but by watching what unfolded naturally.
practical_advice: Resist the urge to constantly position yourself or explain who you are; let your character and the care others show you speak for themselves over time.
Look for small, consistent ways to protect or ease the burden of those you care about — these quiet gestures often communicate love and loyalty more powerfully than grand statements.
When you are uncertain or confused about something important, give yourself time to observe before drawing conclusions — clarity often comes through continued watching rather than immediate judgment.

title: Founding of the Mosque at Quba
summary: During his ten-night stay with the Banu Amr ibn Awf at Quba, the Prophet founded the first mosque of Islam, whose foundations were described in the Quran as based on piety.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained there with the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf for some ten nights and founded the mosque Lwhose foundations were based on piety' and the Messenger of God (SAAS) prayed there." A reference to the Qur'sn, Swat a/-Tamba, IX, v.108, where the piety of its founders is mentioned.
lessons: Even amid exhaustion from a dangerous journey, the Prophet's first priority was establishing a place of worship, teaching us that spiritual grounding is not a luxury to be postponed until conditions are comfortable — it is the foundation from which everything else grows.
Piety — sincerity and God-consciousness — is the most important foundation of any institution or community endeavor; structures built on this foundation are remembered and honored across generations.

Taking even small steps to establish order, beauty, and worship in a new environment helps anchor a community and give it identity and purpose; the mosque at Quba became a cornerstone of early Muslim life.
practical_advice: When starting over in a new place or situation, prioritize establishing your spiritual and moral anchor early — whether through prayer, reflection, or community — rather than waiting until everything else is settled.
Whatever project or community you build, be intentional about the values and intentions at its foundation, since the character of what you build will reflect what you built it upon.
Make time for worship and reflection even during your busiest or most stressful transitions — these moments of connection are what give the rest of your efforts meaning and sustainability.

title: The Prophet's Camel Kneels and the Site of the Prophet's Mosque is Chosen
summary: As the Prophet rode his camel through Madinah with people walking alongside him, the camel knelt at a date-drying shed belonging to two orphan youths, and the Prophet declared it the site of his mosque, insisting on purchasing it rather than accepting it as a gift.
story: "He then rode upon his camel, the people walking beside him, until it knelt down at the mosque in Medina of the Messenger of God (SAAS) where that day some Muslim men were praying. It was a drying shed for dates and belonged to Suhayl and Sahl, two orphan youths in the care of As%d b. Zuraa. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, when his camel knelt, 'This, if God wills it, will be the house.' "He then asked to see the two youths and negotiated with them over the drying shed so that it could become a mosque. They replied, 'No, we will give it to you, Messenger of God!' But the Messenger of God (SAAS) refused to accept it from them as a gift and did buy it. He then built a mosque."
lessons: The Prophet's insistence on paying for the land even when it was freely offered models scrupulous fairness and protection of the vulnerable — he would not take advantage even of generosity when accepting it could disadvantage orphans.

Divine guidance sometimes operates through seemingly ordinary events — a camel kneeling at an unremarkable shed — reminding us to pay attention to where things naturally come to rest in our lives as potential signs of where we are meant to invest.

Justice and integrity in transactions, even small ones, establish the moral character of a community from its very beginning; the Prophet's purchase set a precedent of fairness that would define the community he was building.
practical_advice: When someone in a vulnerable position offers you something generously, be especially careful not to take more than is fair — their generosity does not reduce your responsibility to protect their interests.
Pay attention to unexpected pauses or natural stopping points in your life — sometimes what feels like an interruption is actually an arrival, a signal that you have reached where you need to be.

Build your most important commitments — relationships, projects, communities — on a foundation of clear, fair agreement rather than ambiguity, even when the other party is willing to be generous without conditions.

title: Building the Prophet's Mosque: The Prophet Carries Bricks Alongside the Muslims
summary: The Prophet participated personally in building his mosque in Madinah, carrying bricks alongside the other Muslims and reciting verses of poetry expressing that the true reward for this labor lies in the hereafter and calling for God's mercy on the Helpers and Emigrants.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then set about bringing up the bricks for building it, along with the rest of the Muslims, saying as he did so the verse, 'This is not a load from Khaybar; this, 0 Lord, is more righteous more pure.' "And also: 'No matter; the wages for this are those of the other world; be merciful, 0 God, on the Helpers and the Emigrants.' "He quoted the verse of one of the Muslim men whose name is unknown to me. Ibn Shihzb said, 'We are not informed in the hadith literature that the Messenger of God (SAAS) quoted any full line of poe&y other than these.'"
lessons: Leadership means working alongside those you lead, not above them — the Prophet carried bricks with his own hands, demonstrating that genuine leaders do not exempt themselves from the hard work they ask of others.

Reframing labor as an act of worship and investment in the hereafter transforms the experience of difficult work; the Prophet's words while carrying bricks turned physical toil into a spiritual act, teaching us that intention elevates every action.
Honoring both those who help you and those who sacrifice alongside you — as the Prophet prayed for both Helpers and Emigrants — is essential to building a healthy, grateful, and cohesive community.
practical_advice: When you are in a position of leadership or authority, look for opportunities to work alongside your team in hands-on ways — this builds trust, respect, and shared ownership more powerfully than any directive.

When facing difficult or tedious work, try reframing it by connecting it to a larger purpose or long-term good — asking yourself 'what is this effort building?' can make the present burden feel meaningful rather than merely exhausting.

Consciously express gratitude to and pray for those who have sacrificed or helped you — make this a habit, not just an occasional gesture, as sustained appreciation is what keeps communities and relationships strong.

title: Abu Bakr Recounts the Hijra to al-Bara's Father: Midday Shelter and the Herdsman's Milk
summary: Abu Bakr recounted to al-Bara ibn Azib's father how during the Hijra he and the Prophet traveled intensely and at midday sheltered in the shade of a rock, where Abu Bakr found a herdsman and obtained fresh milk for the Prophet to drink before they continued their journey.
story: Aba Bakr stated, "We set off and travelled fast, by day and night until midday approached and was upon us. I strained my eyes to see if I could see some shade where we could shelter. I saw a rock and hurried down to it, and some shadow remained. I smoothed out a place for the Messenger of God (SAAS), and laid down a piece of leather for hi, saying, 'Do lie down, 0 Messenger of God.' And he did so. "I then left to see if I could locate any of the pursuers. I came upon a herdsman and asked him, 'Who employs you, lad?' He mentioned the name of a Quraysh man that I recognized.
