I asked him, 'Do you have any milk in your flock?' '0yes,' he replied. 'Will you give me some?' I requested. He agreed and I told him to get some. He tethered a ewe and at my request he wiped the dirt off its udders and his hands. I had with me a container with a cloth over its mouth. He poured a little milk for me and I poured it out into a cup until it was all cool. I then went over to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and gave it to him; he was awake. I asked him to drink, and he did so until I was content. I then asked, 'Should we continue with the travel?' And we left again, our pursuers still after us."
lessons: Caring for the physical needs of those we are responsible for — rest, food, water — is itself a sacred act; Abu Bakr's careful attention to the Prophet's comfort during their desperate flight shows that practical care and spiritual devotion are not separate.

Resourcefulness under pressure is a form of trust in God — Abu Bakr did not despair when faced with the need to rest and find food in the open desert; he looked around, found what was available, and made use of it.

Protecting those in your care sometimes means taking on uncomfortable tasks yourself — scouting for danger, negotiating with strangers, preparing food — rather than waiting for ideal conditions to materialize.
practical_advice: When you are under pressure or in survival mode, do not neglect your basic physical needs or those of the people depending on you — rest and nourishment are not weaknesses but the foundation of continued capacity.
Practice resourcefulness by training yourself to look for what is available and workable in your current circumstances rather than fixating on what is absent or ideal.

Take on the unglamorous, practical tasks of care without waiting to be asked — checking on someone's comfort, preparing food, arranging rest — these acts of service are often what people remember and are grateful for most.

title: Suraqa Catches Up with the Prophet: Abu Bakr's Fear and the Prophet's Calm
summary: As Suraqa ibn Malik gained on them during the Hijra, Abu Bakr wept out of fear for the Prophet rather than for himself, and the Prophet calmly reassured him with the words 'Do not be worried, God is with us,' before praying for Suraqa whose horse then sank into the ground.
story: "But the only one of them to catch up with us was Smsqa b. Mdik b. Ju'sham, riding a horse. I asked, '0Messenger of God, are our pursuers catching up?' 'Do not be worried, God is with us,' he replied. "He caught up with us until there was only a spear's throw or two - or the words may have been two or three spear-throws - and I said, '0 Messenger of God, this pursuer is gaining on us!' And I wept. He asked, 'Why are you crying?' I replied, 'It's not for me I'm crying, but for you!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said a prayer about him, saying, '0 Lord, protect us from him, as You wish.' The legs of his horse then sank down to its belly in firm ground and he fell off, saying, '0 Muhammad, I know this is your work; say a prayer to God for me to save me from my plight! I swear I will mislead the pursuers behind me. This is my quiver; take an arrow from it, for you will pass by my'"
lessons: The Prophet's calm in the face of immediate danger — 'Do not be worried, God is with us' — is one of the most profound models of tawakkul, trust in God, showing that certainty of divine support is not naive but a deeply grounded spiritual state.
Abu Bakr's tears were not for himself but for the Prophet, illustrating a form of love that transcends self-concern — when we truly love someone, their safety matters more to us than our own.
Prayer in the moment of crisis is not a last resort but a first response; the Prophet turned immediately to God when danger was closest, and the result was immediate and decisive protection.
practical_advice: When fear overwhelms you in a crisis, try to anchor yourself in the present moment with a simple act of trust — a short prayer, a breath, a reminder of past times you were protected — rather than catastrophizing about what might happen.

Examine whose wellbeing you are most anxious about in difficult times; if you find your worry is primarily other-directed rather than self-focused, recognize that as a sign of deep love and let it motivate action rather than paralyze you.

Make prayer your first instinct in moments of danger or distress, not your last resort after all human options are exhausted — the practice of turning to God immediately reorients your mind and heart even before the external situation changes.

title: Arrival in Medina: The Prophet's Welcome
summary: After the long and perilous journey from Mecca, the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) and Abu Bakr arrive in Medina to a joyous welcome from the people who come out in crowds to greet him.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) and I went further on and eventually reached Medina where people came out to meet him. They appeared on the roads and roofs and servants and young people thronged the streets, saying, 'God is Great! The Messenger of God (SAAS) has come! Muhammad is here!' "The people vied for the honour of having him stay with them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) therefore said, 'Tonight I will stay with the Banii al-Najjsr who are related by blood to 'Abd al-Mugalih, to honour them.' Next morning he was where he had been ordered to be.'
lessons: Even after great hardship and persecution, relief and joy can arrive unexpectedly — patience through trials leads to moments of celebration.
The Prophet's humility was evident even in his moment of triumph; he deferred the question of lodging to divine guidance rather than personal preference.
Community and belonging are healing forces — the outpouring of love from the people of Medina shows the power of a welcoming community.
practical_advice: When you are going through a painful transition, remind yourself that arrival and belonging are ahead — hardship does not last forever.
Practice humility in moments of success; let gratitude, not pride, define how you receive good fortune.
Invest in building a welcoming community around you, so that others who are struggling feel received with warmth.

title: The First Emigrants to Medina Before the Prophet
summary: Al-Bara' recounts that Mus'ab ibn Umayr, then Ibn Umm Maktum, and then Umar ibn al-Khattab arrived in Medina before the Prophet, and the community eagerly awaited the Prophet's arrival.
story: Al-Bars' said, "The first of the Emigrants to come to us was Mus'ab b. Umayr, a brother of the 'Abd al-Dar. Then came Ibn Umm Maktum, the blind, one of the Banii Fihr, followed by Umar b. al-Khattab, along with 20 riders. We asked them what had happened to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and they replied that he was coming later. And eventually he and Abu Bakr did arrive." Al-Bara' went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not arrive until after I had recited chapters from al-mufaasal."
lessons: Those who pave the way for others — like Mus'ab ibn Umayr — perform a noble service even if they receive less recognition than those who follow.
Anticipation of good things requires patience; the community of Medina waited eagerly and their waiting was rewarded.
Even the blind and the physically challenged, like Ibn Umm Maktum, were among the courageous early migrants, showing that courage is not defined by physical ability.
practical_advice: When you feel like a forerunner doing unrecognized work, take heart — your effort prepares the ground for something greater.
Practice patient anticipation: when waiting for something important, fill the time with worship or productive activity as al-Bara' filled his time reciting Quran.
Never underestimate those who seem limited by circumstance; look for courage and contribution in every person around you.

title: Quraysh Offer a Reward of 100 Camels for the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet and Abu Bakr hid in the cave on Mount Thawr for three nights, Quraysh offered a reward of 100 camels to anyone who could bring him back, while the hue and cry gradually diminished.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed for three nights in the cave along with Abu Bakr. Having lost knowledge of his whereabouts Quraysh offered a price of 100 camels to anyone who would return him. After the three nights, when the hue and cry had diminished, the man they had hired brought them their camels along with one for himself."
lessons: The greatest reward offered by Quraysh could not overpower divine protection — worldly incentives are powerless against God's plan.
Patience in hiding and stillness during a crisis is itself an act of trust in God; the Prophet did not flee recklessly but waited with composure.
Danger that seems overwhelming at its peak does diminish — the 'hue and cry' faded, and the path forward opened.
practical_advice: When you feel pursued or threatened, do not panic into hasty action; sometimes the wisest response is stillness and trust.
Remember that the plans of those who wish you harm often fail on their own — focus on your own integrity rather than obsessing over threats.
In times of crisis, ensure you have a trustworthy companion who can help practically, as Abu Bakr arranged for camels and provisions.

title: Asma bint Abi Bakr and Her Two Girdles
summary: Asma bint Abi Bakr brought provisions to the Prophet and her father in the cave but forgot to tie the food-cloth; having no cord, she tore her own girdle in two to secure it, earning the title 'she of the two girdles.'
story: "Abu Bakr's daughter Asma' also arrived with provisions in a leather table-cloth for them. But she forgot to tie it up and when they were about to depart she came out to tie it up but had no cord. So she untied her girdle to make a cord of it which she used to attach it. She used then to be called, 'she of the two girdles.'"
lessons: Resourcefulness in service of a noble cause is itself a form of worship; Asma' improvised rather than giving up when she faced an obstacle.
Small acts of sacrifice — even tearing one's own belt — are remembered and honoured; no act of sincere service is too small.
Women played a courageous and essential role in the early Muslim community, serving the Prophet at personal risk and cost.
practical_advice: When you face an unexpected obstacle in helping someone, look for creative solutions rather than abandoning the effort.
Honour the small, unseen acts of service that people around you perform — they are often the things that make great missions possible.
Be willing to give from what you have, even if it means personal inconvenience, when the cause is worthy.

title: The Prophet Refuses to Ride a Camel He Does Not Own
summary: Abu Bakr offered the Prophet his best camel as a gift, but the Prophet insisted on paying the price for it, refusing to ride something he did not own, demonstrating his scrupulous honesty.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Abu Bakr brought up the two mounts and offered the best of them to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him to mount the camel. But the latter responded, 'I couldn't ride a camel that I don't own.' Abu Bakr told him, 'But please consider it yours'. 'No,' he replied, 'but what price did you pay for it?' He told him and the Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'Well, I'll take it for that.' 'It's yours,' Abu Bakr told him." Al-Waqidi related, with full chains of authorities, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) took the camel called al-Qaswa'. He also said, "Abu Bakr had bought both camels for 800 dirhams."
lessons: Integrity in financial dealings is a mark of true character; the Prophet refused even a generous gift if it compromised the principle of fair exchange.
Scrupulous honesty in small matters builds a foundation of trust that sustains great missions.
Accepting generosity does not require abandoning principles; one can honour a friend's kindness while still insisting on fairness.
practical_advice: In your financial dealings, be scrupulous about what belongs to you and what does not — even in small matters with those closest to you.
When someone offers you something out of love, you can accept it graciously while still ensuring the transaction is fair and transparent.
Model integrity for those around you; your consistency in honest dealings will earn lasting trust and respect.

title: Abu Jahl Strikes Asma bint Abi Bakr
summary: After the Prophet and Abu Bakr departed, Quraysh men including Abu Jahl came to Abu Bakr's home and Abu Jahl struck Asma so hard on the cheek that he knocked off her earring.
story: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) departed with Abu Bakr, a number of Quraysh men came to us, including Abu Jahl." Ibn Ishaq then related how Abu Jahl struck her so hard on the cheek that he knocked off an earring, as told above.
lessons: The oppressors of every age resort to violence against the vulnerable when they cannot control those they truly fear — Asma's suffering was the cost of her family's courage.
Enduring harm with dignity in defense of what is right is itself a form of spiritual strength.
Those who harm the innocent in pursuit of power only deepen their own moral failure; Asma's honour was not diminished by the blow she received.
practical_advice: When you or someone you love is harmed because of standing for truth, acknowledge the pain while also recognising the honour in that position.
Do not measure your worth by the cruelty others direct at you; the cruelty reflects their character, not yours.
Seek community and support when you have experienced injustice — do not carry that pain alone.

title: The Jinn Poet Announces the Prophet's Direction to Medina
summary: While Abu Bakr's family had no knowledge of the Prophet's whereabouts, a voice — attributed to a man of the jinn — was heard singing verses revealing that the Prophet was heading to Medina, bringing relief to those who were worried.
story: "We remained for three nights without knowledge of the whereabouts of the Messenger of God (SAAS) until a man of the jinn, a spirit-man, arrived from the lower parts of Mecca, singing verses of Arabic songs. People followed him, listening to his voice without seeing him. Eventually he came out to the upper parts of Mecca, reciting, 'God, Lord of all men, awarded His very best reward to two Companions who stayed at the tents of Umm Ma'bad. They stayed there in good will, and then left; and lucky is he who becomes the Companion of Muhammad. Their girl's position brings credit to the Banu Ka'b, and her sitting there watching for the believers.' "Asma' said, 'When we heard what he said we knew where the Messenger of God (SAAS) was headed, towards Medina.'"
lessons: God's ways of providing reassurance are beyond what we expect; comfort and clarity can arrive through unexpected means when we are patient.
Anxiety about loved ones who are out of contact is a deeply human experience; even the family of the Prophet endured this uncertainty.
Good news, when it arrives, is sweeter for the worry that preceded it — trust that clarity will come.
practical_advice: When you have no news of someone you love and are filled with worry, focus on what is in your control and trust that God is aware of what you cannot see.
Look for signs of reassurance in unexpected places — sometimes a poem, a word from a stranger, or a small coincidence is enough to restore hope.
Do not let prolonged uncertainty collapse into despair; uncertainty is not the same as bad news.

title: The Route of the Hijra: The Long Journey to Medina
summary: The Prophet's guide 'Abdullah ibn Urayqit led the migration party along a long and difficult coastal route through many valleys and waypoints to avoid pursuit, arriving at Quba on a Monday, the 12th of Rabi al-Awwal.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When their guide, 'Abd Allah b. Arqat, led them off, he took them down to the lower part of Mecca and from there along the coast, crossing over the route below 'Usfan. He then took them along the lower parts of Amaj, then crossed the route after passing Qudayd, then from there across al-Kharrar and Thaniyyat al-Marra to Liqf. He guided them past the Liqf well, then down to the Mijaj well and so to Marjah Mijaj, then down again to Marjah Dhu al-'Adwayn and to the Dhu Kashr valley and so to al-Jadajid and al-Ajrad. Then he took them along Dhu Salam of the 'Arj valley, to the well at Ti'hin, then past al-'Ababid, across al-Qah, down to al-'Arj. One of their mounts was falling behind, so a man of Aslam called Aws b. Hajr mounted the Messenger of God (SAAS) on a camel called Ibn al-Rada' and led him into Medina; he sent with him a youth named Mas'ud b. Hunayda. Then their guide from al-'Arj took them to Thaniyya al-'A'ir on the right side of Rakuba; according to Ibn Hisham this place is known as Thaniyya al-Gha'ir. Then he took them down the Ri'm valley and so to Quba', to the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. It was now Monday, the 12th of Rabi' al-Awwal and the heat was extreme, the sun almost having reached its zenith."
lessons: The path to a better life is rarely straight or easy; the Prophet took the longer, harder road precisely because it was the safer one, teaching us that wisdom sometimes means choosing the difficult route.
Trusting a knowledgeable guide — even one who is not yet a believer, as the guide was still a polytheist — is a sign of practical wisdom, not weakness.
Every step of the journey mattered; no valley or waypoint was incidental — God's protection accompanied every mile.
practical_advice: When facing a major life transition, do not be discouraged by the length or difficulty of the path; focus on the next step rather than the whole journey.
Seek knowledgeable guides and experts when navigating unfamiliar territory, regardless of whether they share all your beliefs.
Keep track of the milestones in your own difficult journeys — they become a testimony to your resilience and God's care.

title: The Pun on Names: Aslam and Mas'ud
summary: During the migration, when the Prophet asked the name of a man from the tribe of Aslam whose camel helped them, the exchange of names produced an auspicious wordplay that Abu Bakr interpreted as a good omen.
story: Abu Nu'aym stated that Abu Hazim b. Jabala related to him that Muhammad b. Ishaq quoted from al-Sarraj, who quoted from Muhammad b. 'Ubada b. Musa al-Ijli from his brother Musa b. 'Ubada, from 'Abd Allah b. Sayyar, from Iyas b. Malik b. al-Aws al-Aslami, who quoted his father as saying, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) emigrated along with Abu Bakr, they passed by a camel of ours at al-Juhfa. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Who owns this camel?' 'A man from Aslam,' he was told. The Prophet (SAAS) turned to Abu Bakr, who commented, 'May you be safe, God willing!' The Prophet (SAAS) asked the man, 'What's your name?' 'Mas'ud,' he replied. The Prophet (SAAS) again turned to Abu Bakr, who commented, 'May you be happy, God willing!'" The account goes on, "My father then went to him and carried him on his way on a camel called Ibn al-Rada'."
lessons: Believers look for good omens and positive signs in their circumstances — this is a form of hope and trust in God, not superstition.
Gratitude and optimism in the middle of hardship are disciplines of the heart that strengthen the spirit for the journey ahead.
Small human encounters along the way — a name, a camel, a kind gesture — can become sources of divine blessing.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of looking for hopeful signs around you, especially during difficult journeys or transitions.
Speak words of blessing and good wishes to those you encounter — your encouragement may mean more to them than you know.
Do not overlook the small helpers in your life's journey; often it is an unexpected person who provides exactly what you need at the right moment.

title: The Story of Umm Ma'bad and the Miracle of the Dry Ewe
summary: When the Prophet and his companions stopped at the tent of Umm Ma'bad al-Khuza'iyya and she had no food or milk to offer, the Prophet wiped the teat of a dry ewe, prayed over it, and it produced abundant milk — a miracle witnessed by Umm Ma'bad and all the travellers.
story: Yunus stated, from Ibn Ishaq, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed at the tent of Umm Ma'bad, whose name was 'Atika, daughter of Khalaf b. Ma'bad b. Rabi'a b. Asram. The travellers wanted a meal served but she said, 'We have no food, no milch-camel and the only ewes we have are in heat.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked to be brought one of her sheep. He wiped its teat with his hand, said a prayer and it gave milk into a tumbler, foaming. He said, 'Drink this, Umm Ma'bad!' She replied, 'No, you drink it; you have more right to it than me.' But he gave it back to her and she did drink it. He then called for another ewe in heat and did the same as before and this time he drank it. He then called for another ewe, did the same and gave it to his guide to drink. Again he did the same, giving it now to 'Amir. And then they left. "The Quraysh men, searching for the Messenger of God (SAAS), eventually came to Umm Ma'bad and asked her about him, saying, 'Have you seen Muhammad who looks so-and-so?' And they described him to her. "She replied, 'I don't know what you are saying, though a young man did come and get milk from ewes in heat.' "'That's the one we want,' the Quraysh man told her."
lessons: Divine provision arrives in the most barren of circumstances; when there seems to be nothing, God can cause blessing to flow from the most unlikely source.
The Prophet's generosity was instinctive — even with the miraculous milk he had produced, he first offered it to his hostess before himself.
Hospitality offered even from a place of scarcity is honoured by God; Umm Ma'bad's honest acknowledgement of her poverty did not prevent a miracle.
practical_advice: When you feel you have nothing left to give or offer, do not despair — offer what little you have with sincerity and trust God to multiply it.
Practice the habit of serving others before yourself, even when you are the one who has worked hardest for something.
When you encounter someone in need, welcome them even if your resources are limited; the blessing of hospitality is not measured in what you provide but in the spirit with which you offer it.

title: Abu Bakr Blocks the Cave Crevice with His Heel
summary: While sheltering with the Prophet in the cave on Mount Thawr, Abu Bakr blocked a crevice in the cave wall with his heel through the night out of fear that something harmful might emerge and reach the Prophet.
story: The hafiz Abu Bakr al-Bazzar stated that Muhammad b. Ma'mar related to him from Ya'qub b. Muhammad, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Uqba b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Jabir b. 'Abd Allah, who quoted his father as having related that Jabir stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abu Bakr left as Emigrants and entered the cave, there was a crevice that Abu Bakr blocked off with his heel until morning, for fear that something might come out at the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: True companionship means accepting personal discomfort and pain so that the one you love is protected; Abu Bakr's loyalty was expressed not in words but in silent, painful service.
The greatest acts of devotion are often unseen — no one may have known what Abu Bakr did that night except God and the Prophet.
Protective love is an act of worship; guarding those in our care, even through bodily sacrifice, reflects the depth of our commitment.
practical_advice: Ask yourself: who in your life deserves the kind of loyal, self-sacrificing support that Abu Bakr gave? How can you offer that today?
Do not wait to be recognised for your acts of service and protection; do them quietly, for God's sake, and trust that they are seen and valued.
Small acts of courage — holding a post, standing guard, staying awake — are the fabric of great loyalty. Do not underestimate what your quiet faithfulness means to those you love.

title: The Miracle of Umm Ma'bad's Goat – The Blessed Traveler
summary: During the Prophet's migration to Madinah, he and Abu Bakr stopped at the tent of Umm Ma'bad, a woman suffering drought and scarcity. The Prophet miraculously milked her dry goat, producing an abundance of milk, and her husband later described the Prophet's noble character upon hearing the account.
story: Umm Ma'bad was a good, fearless, strong woman who would sit with her legs drawn up, wrapped in her garment, at the entrance to the tent and give out food and drink. They asked her whether she had any meat or milk they could buy from her. But they obtained none from her and she told them, 'If we had anything, you would not lack for hospitality, but our people are all out of provisions and we've been suffering drought.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) noticed a goat at the side of her tent and said, 'What about that goat, Umm Ma'bad?' She replied, 'She's a goat left over from the goats after the drought.' 'Does she give milk?' he asked. 'No, she's too dried up for that,' she replied. 'Would you permit me to milk her?' he asked. 'If she has any milk you can,' she replied. The Messenger of God (SAAS) called to the goat and stroked it, speaking God's name, wiped her teat and again invoked God's name. Then he called for a vessel large enough to satisfy them, and the goat opened its legs and milk poured out in a copious flow until it was full. He gave (the vessel to) her to drink, and then his Companions and thereafter they all had a second drink. When they were all quenched, he drank too, saying, 'The one who pours drinks last!' He put milk in it again, left it with her, and then they departed.
lessons: Generosity flows from character, not from material abundance — Umm Ma'bad offered what little she could even in drought, reflecting that the spirit of giving is not contingent on having plenty.
Divine blessings often arrive in moments of scarcity and hardship; what appears empty or dry can be transformed when approached with faith and trust in God.
The Prophet's example of drinking last — 'The one who pours drinks last' — teaches that true leadership means ensuring others are cared for before oneself.
practical_advice: When you feel you have nothing to offer in a difficult season of life, offer your presence and willingness anyway — that openness is itself a form of generosity that invites unexpected blessings.
In moments of scarcity or anxiety, invoke God's name consciously before undertaking any task, as the Prophet did before milking the goat, grounding your action in trust rather than fear.
Practice putting others first in small daily acts — serving food, letting someone go ahead, finishing last — as a way of building the patience and humility that sustain you through hardship.

title: Umm Ma'bad's Description of the Prophet
summary: After the Prophet's departure, Umm Ma'bad's husband returned home, found the miraculous milk, and asked her to describe the stranger. Her description of the Prophet's character and appearance became one of the most celebrated portraits of him in Islamic tradition.
story: Soon her husband, Abu Ma'bad, came home, leading emaciated goats, staggering they were so weak, and their brains scarcely functioning. When he saw the milk, he was amazed and said, 'Where did this milk come from, Umm Ma'bad? We don't have a milch-camel and the goat has not been with a male.' 'Well, a man who was blessed came past us and seemed from his speech to be such-and-such,' she replied. 'Describe him to me; I think he is that man the Quraysh are looking for,' he told her. She replied, 'I saw him to be a man of evident cleanliness, fine in character, his face handsome, slim in form, his head not too small, elegant and good looking, his eyes large and black, his eyebrows long, his voice deep, very intelligent, his eyelids brown, his brows high and arched, his hair in plaits, his neck long and his beard thick. He gave an impression of dignity when silent and of high intelligence when he talked. His logic was impressive, he was decisive, not trivial, not trite, his ideas like pearls moving on their string. He seemed the most splendid and fine-looking man from a distance and the very best of all from close-by, medium in height, the eye not finding him too tall nor too short. A tree-branch, as it were, between two others, but he was the finest-looking of the three, the best proportioned. He was the centre of his companions' attention. When he spoke, they listened well, and if he ordered, they hurried to obey, a man well helped, well served, never sullen, never refuted.' He — her husband — commented, 'That, I swear, has to be that man Quraysh are seeking. If I had chanced on him, I would have done my very best to follow him.'
lessons: True nobility of character is recognized even by strangers — the Prophet's inner qualities shone so clearly that a woman who did not know him could describe his essence with poetic precision.
When we cannot see a blessing directly, the traces it leaves behind — transformed circumstances, changed hearts, abundant provision — are themselves signs worth reflecting on.
Abu Ma'bad's immediate declaration that he would have followed the Prophet had he met him shows that the human heart naturally inclines toward genuine goodness when it encounters it.
practical_advice: When you feel lost or uncertain about your path, look for the traces of goodness and blessing left by those who have walked before you — their example can guide your heart even from a distance.
Reflect on the qualities you most admire in others and ask yourself honestly which of those qualities you are cultivating in yourself day by day.
In times of confusion, take a moment to describe to yourself — or write down — what a person of good character looks like in your context, then use that description as a personal compass.

title: Abu Ma'bad and Umm Ma'bad Accept Islam and Emigrate
summary: After the Prophet's migration, both Abu Ma'bad and Umm Ma'bad eventually accepted Islam and emigrated to join the Prophet in Madinah, completing the transformation that began with a single miraculous encounter.
story: He stated — 'Abd al-Malik b. Wahb, that is — 'I received information that Abu Ma'bad accepted Islam and emigrated to the Prophet (SAAS).' The hafiz Abu Nu'aym related this through 'Abd al-Malik b. Wahb al-Madhhiji, in the same terms. At the end of his account he added, ''Abd al-Malik said, 'I received information that Umm Ma'bad emigrated, accepted Islam, and joined the Messenger of God (SAAS).'
lessons: A single moment of genuine encounter with truth can set a person on a path that changes the entire direction of their life, even if the full transformation takes time.
Emigration — leaving behind the familiar for the sake of faith and truth — is a profound act of courage that demonstrates how deeply a new conviction has taken root.
Both husband and wife found their way to faith independently and together, showing that spiritual transformation can strengthen and unite families rather than divide them.
practical_advice: If you feel spiritually lost or disconnected, trust that a single sincere encounter with truth — a conversation, a book, a moment of reflection — can be the beginning of a whole new chapter.
Do not underestimate small acts of hospitality or kindness; Umm Ma'bad's simple openness led to a life-changing encounter she could not have predicted.
When someone you love begins a journey of growth and faith, support and accompany them rather than waiting — transformation is often a shared road.

title: The Heavenly Voice Over Mecca Praising the Two Companions at Umm Ma'bad's Tent
summary: After the Prophet's departure from Mecca, a voice was heard high above the city reciting verses praising the two companions who had stayed at Umm Ma'bad's tent, while the Meccans searched for the Prophet in vain.
story: A voice was heard in Mecca, high up, somewhere between heaven and earth, that people could hear though no one see, reciting the verses, 'God, Lord of all men, awarded His very best reward to two Companions who stayed at the two tents of Umm Ma'bad. They stayed there in good will, and then left; and lucky is he who becomes the Companion of Mubarak. O Qusayy, God did not withhold from you actions that will not be rewarded, nor leadership? Ask your sister about her goat and bowl; if you were to ask the goat, she would testify. He asked her for a goat without young, and it gave milk for him in plenty, its teat giving foam, So he pledged (with the goat) (milk) for the milker, such that it remained abundant at its commencement and at its completion.' Next morning the people, those of Mecca that is, having missed their prophet went to the tents of Umm Ma'bad, trying to catch up with the Messenger of God (SAAS).
lessons: Even when human eyes cannot see what is happening, the signs of divine approval and blessing are being recorded and announced — nothing righteous goes unwitnessed.
Those who opposed the Prophet in Mecca searched for him the morning after, a reminder that the pursuit of truth often begins only after the moment of opportunity has passed.
God's reward for sincere companionship and generous hospitality is not merely material but is announced and honored in ways beyond ordinary reckoning.
practical_advice: When you feel that your sacrifice or good deed has gone unnoticed by others, remind yourself that what matters is the awareness of God, not the applause of people.
Reflect on moments where you may have 'missed your prophet' — missed the opportunity to act with kindness, generosity, or courage — and use that reflection to respond differently the next time.
Cultivate sincere companionship in your life; being a true, loyal companion to someone on a meaningful journey is itself an act of worship worthy of the highest reward.

title: Hassan ibn Thabit's Poem in Honor of the Migration and Abu Bakr's Companionship
summary: The poet Hassan ibn Thabit composed verses mourning the departure of the Prophet from Mecca while celebrating the joy of those who received him, and praising Abu Bakr's loyal companionship.
story: Hassan b. Thabit responded with the verses, 'A people disappointed, having lost their prophet, while those to whom he hurried were pleased, He left a people and their spirits left them, and he took up residence with a people with a light renewed. Are those who mislead people out of blind stupidity equal to those rightly guided by a guide? A prophet, seeing about him what others do not see, reciting the Book of God at every scene. If one day he should speak the words of the unseen, it is corroborated the same day or, at most, the morning of the next. Abu Bakr must be pleased by the pleasure his grandfather enjoys at his accompanying him; whoever pleases God will be happy. Let the place of their woman please the Bano Ka'b, and her sitting watching for the Muslims.'
lessons: The departure of a blessing from a community is itself a form of loss and grief — recognizing what we have neglected or rejected is often the beginning of wisdom.
Poetry, art, and remembrance are legitimate and powerful ways to process collective joy, grief, and spiritual meaning — Hassan channeled the emotion of an entire community into verse.
Abu Bakr's loyalty to the Prophet in the migration was so profound that it brought honor to his lineage and was celebrated by believers across generations.
practical_advice: When you experience a loss — of a person, an opportunity, or a phase of life — allow yourself to grieve it genuinely and seek meaningful ways to express and process that grief rather than suppressing it.
Consider what 'lights' have entered your community or life recently; celebrate and nurture those arrivals with the gratitude they deserve before they pass.
Loyal, faithful companionship in difficult times is one of the most lasting gifts one person can give another — invest in being that kind of companion to someone who needs it.

title: The Miracle of Umm Ma'bad's Barren Goat
summary: During the emigration to Madinah, the Prophet passed by Umm Ma'bad al-Khuza'iyya whose goats were barren due to drought. He prayed over the goat and milk flowed miraculously, providing drink for the travelers.
story: 'Abd al-Munalib, quoting Muhammad b. Sulayman b. Salit al-Ansari, quoting his father, from his father Sali! al-Badri, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) left on the emigration accompanied by Aba Bakr, 'Amir b. Fuhayra with Ibn Wrayqit guiding them on their path, they passed by Umm Ma'bad al-Khuza'iyya, she not knowing who he was. He asked her, "Umm Ma'bad, do you have any milk?" She replied, 'LNo,I swear, our goats have not been with males." "What about that one?" he asked. "She is the last of the goats left after the drought."'
lessons: Divine provision can come in unexpected ways during times of hardship and scarcity.
The Prophet's reliance on God while still taking practical steps — asking for what was available — models balanced trust and effort.
Miracles often emerge precisely at the point of greatest need, reminding us that apparent impossibilities are never beyond God's power.
practical_advice: When resources feel depleted, make a sincere du'a and then take every small practical step available to you — don't wait for ease before acting.
In difficult journeys or transitions, look for unexpected sources of support; God often provides through seemingly unlikely means.
Document or reflect on moments in your own life when provision came unexpectedly, to build a personal record of God's care that you can return to during future anxieties.

title: The Shepherd Who Bore Witness: A Slave's Encounter with the Prophet
summary: A slave shepherd tending goats encountered the Prophet and Abu Bakr during the emigration. After the Prophet miraculously produced milk from a barren young goat, the shepherd bore witness to the Prophet's truthfulness and declared his faith.
story: The ha&. Abn 'Abd Allah related to us, by dictation, that Aba Bakr Ahmad b. Ishaq b. A y F b related to them, quoting Muhammad h. Ghdib, quoting Aha al-Walid, quoting 'Abd All& b. Iyyad b. Laqif, quoting Iyyad b. Laqi!, from Qays b. al-Nucma, who said, 'When the Prophet and Aha Bakr left in secret they passed by a slave watching some goats. They asked him for milk. He replied, "I don't have one giving milk. I just have this one young female goat here who gave birth at the beginning of the winter, hut her foetus was malformed and she has no milk left." "Call her over to me," he asked. He did so and the Messenger of God (SAAS) tethered her and stroked her teat and prayed until it began to flow. Aba Bakr then brought a vessel and he milked the goat, gave to Aba Bakr to drink, milked again, and gave to the shepherd, then milked again and drank.' "The shepherd then asked, 'For heaven's sake, who are you? I never saw the like of you!' "'Do you think you could keep it secret if I told you?' he asked. 'Oh yes,' he replied. "'I am Muhammad, the Messenger of God,' he told him. "'You mean you're the one Quraysh say claims to be a Sabian?' he asked. 'Yes, they do say that,' he told him. "'Well,' the shepherd said, 'I bear witness that you bring the truth, and that only a prophet could do as you have. I am your follower now.' "'You can't he that right now,' he told him. 'Come and join us when you hear I have declared myself openly.'"
lessons: Truth has a self-evident quality — even those who have heard only slander about a person can recognise genuine prophethood and goodness when they witness it directly.
The Prophet's care to share the miraculous milk with the shepherd before himself demonstrates that leadership means giving to others before oneself.
Accepting faith is the beginning of a journey; the Prophet wisely advised the shepherd to wait for the right moment, teaching us that timing and wisdom are part of following truth.
practical_advice: When others have formed negative opinions of you based on what they have heard, let your direct actions and character speak for themselves rather than arguing defensively.
Practice generosity even when your own resources are limited — share what you have with those serving you or working around you.
If you feel drawn to a positive change but the conditions are not yet right, hold that conviction close and act when the door genuinely opens, as the shepherd was counselled to do.

title: Ibn Mas'ud the Young Shepherd: An Early Miracle of the Prophet
summary: A teenage 'Abd Allah ibn Mas'ud was tending goats for 'Uqba ibn Abi Mu'ayt when the Prophet and Abu Bakr passed by and asked for milk. The Prophet prayed over an unbred ewe and milk flowed; Ibn Mas'ud later sought the Prophet's teaching and memorised seventy surahs directly from him.
story: Ahii Nucaym at this point related the story of 'Abd AllZh b. Masrfid stating, "'Ahd Allah b. Jacfar related to us, quoting Yanus b. Hahih, Aba D m d and Hammad b. Salama, from 'A~im,from Zirr, from 'Abd Allnh b. Mascad who said, 'I was a teenage boy working as a shepherd for goats belonging to Wqha b. Aba Mu'ayy at Mecca when the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abii Bakr came by, having fled from the polytheists. They asked me whether I had any milk to give them to drink. I replied, "I'm held responsible; I'm not here to provide drink for you." They asked, "Do you have a young sheep that hasn't yet been with a ram?" "Yes, I do," I replied. "'So I brought it to them and Abn Bakr tethered it, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) took hold of her teat and said a prayer. The teat flowed copiously and Abu Bakr brought a hollowed-out rock and milked into it. He and Ahii Bakr drank and they served me. Then he told the teat to close, and it did so. "'Later I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him, L'Teachme some of these fine words," meaning the Qur'm. The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Consider yourself a well-taught lad!" I took from his mouth 70 sfira~ and no one can dispute me about them.'"
lessons: An honest, cautious response to an unusual request — as Ibn Mas'ud gave — does not close the door to a life-changing encounter; integrity opens the right doors.
The Prophet's willingness to serve Ibn Mas'ud with milk before himself shows that true teachers lift up those under their care.
Witnessing something extraordinary should prompt us to seek knowledge and understanding, just as Ibn Mas'ud followed up his encounter by asking to learn the Qur'an.
practical_advice: When you witness something that moves or inspires you, do not let the moment pass — take a step toward learning more, as Ibn Mas'ud did by approaching the Prophet for teaching.
If you feel you are in a humble position in life — like a young shepherd — recognise that some of the most transformative encounters happen in ordinary circumstances.
Seek out those whose character you admire and ask them directly to teach you; a sincere request for knowledge is rarely refused by those who truly have something to offer.

title: The Two 'Disgraced' Men of Aslam Become the Two 'Honoured' Men
summary: During the emigration, the Prophet and Abu Bakr encountered two men of Aslam known disparagingly as 'the two disgraced men.' After the Prophet explained Islam to them and they accepted it, he renamed them 'the two honoured men' and they guided the party toward Madinah.
story: ImZm Ahmad stated that 'Ahd All& h. Mus'ah h. 'Abd Allith, he being al-Zuhayr, related to hi,that his father had quoted Falid, the freed-man of 'Abadil, as having said, "I left town in the company of Ibrxhim b. 'Ahd a l - w m ~ nh. Saw, when we reached d-'Arj Ibn Sacd came. Now Sa'd (his father) had been the man who had guided the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the road to Raktiba. Ibrnhim asked him, 'What anecdotes did your father relate to you?' Ihn Sacd replied, 'My father told me that the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to them in the company of Aha Bakr. Aha Bakr had a daughter with us who was suckling at that time and the Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted to find a short route to Medina. Sa'd told him, "This is the waste-land of Rakaba; out there are two thieves of Aslam who are known as the 'two disgraced men'. If you like we could get directions from them." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "Yes, let's do that." '"Sa'd went on, 'So we went off and came into view of them, whereupon one of them said to the other, 'That is the man from Yemen.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) called over to them and explained Islam to them, and they accepted it. He then asked them their names and they replied, 'We're the "two disgraced men".' He commented, 'No, you're the "two honoured men"!' He then told them to lead him into Medina. So we left and reached the outskirts of Qub~',and there he was met by the Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Where is Abii U m m a As'ad b. Zurua?' Sa'd b. Khaythama replied, 'So he scored (with you) before me, 0 Messenger of God; shouldn't I tell h i that?'"
lessons: How others have labelled us does not define who we are; the Prophet's renaming of these men illustrates that identity can be transformed by faith and by the recognition of those who see our true worth.
Willingness to be practically useful — guiding the travellers — was the very first expression of the two men's new faith, showing that sincere belief immediately manifests in service.
God can use even those society has written off as guides and helpers for the most important of missions.
practical_advice: Identify any labels or nicknames — from childhood, family, or community — that carry shame, and consciously replace them with a self-description rooted in your values and God's view of you.
Offer the specific skill or knowledge you have, even if you feel marginalised; your contribution may be exactly what a critical situation requires.
When you encounter someone others have dismissed, look past the social label and engage with the person directly — you may discover a loyal and capable ally.

title: The Prophet Arrives at Quba' and Sees the Date-Palms and Well
summary: As the Prophet approached the outskirts of Madinah he caught sight of date-palms and a full well at Quba', and told Abu Bakr that this was where he would stay, having been shown in a vision that he would settle near a stream resembling that of the Banu Mudlij.
story: "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) then passed on until he could see the datepalms and the well there was full. He turned to Abii Bakr and said, '0 Abn Bakr, this is where I shall stay; I saw myself staying at a stream like that of the Banii Mudlij.'"" A b a d alone gives this account.
lessons: Dreams and spiritual visions, when from God, find their fulfilment in their proper time — the Prophet's patient trust in what he had been shown was vindicated at Quba'.
Recognising the place of rest that God has prepared for you — after a long and dangerous journey — is itself a moment of profound gratitude.
Every journey of faith has a destination; enduring the difficulty of the road is made easier by holding onto the promise of arrival.
practical_advice: When facing a long trial, write down or hold clearly in your mind any genuine hope, dream, or conviction that sustains you — it can serve as your 'Quba'' to aim for.
Upon reaching a goal after great hardship, pause and explicitly acknowledge God's guidance before moving on to the next task.
Share your relief and joy with those who accompanied you through the difficulty, as the Prophet turned to Abu Bakr in that moment of recognition.

title: The Establishment of the Mosque at Quba'
summary: The Prophet stayed with the Banu 'Amr ibn 'Awf at Quba' for more than twenty nights, established the mosque of Quba' — the first mosque in Islam — and then rode on toward Madinah accompanied by his Companions.
story: Al-Bukhui recounted, from al-Zuhri, from Wrwa, that he stayed with Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf at Quha', for a score or more nights and established the mosque at Quba' during that period. He then mounted up, the people accompanying him, and went on until his camel knelt with him at the site of his mosque. That place was a drying shed for dates owned by two orphaned youths named Sahl and Suhayl. He bought it from them and used it as a mosque. And that was among the Bana al-Najju, may God be pleased with them.
lessons: The Prophet's first priority upon reaching safety was to establish a place of worship, reminding us that spiritual infrastructure is the foundation of any healthy community.
Buying the land fairly from two orphans rather than simply taking it demonstrates that Islamic community-building is grounded in justice and respect for the rights of the vulnerable.
Periods of rest after hardship — like the Prophet's weeks at Quba' — are not wasted time but necessary preparation for the next stage of one's mission.
practical_advice: When you settle into a new phase of life — a new home, job, or community — prioritise creating a dedicated space for prayer, reflection, and spiritual practice before focusing on other comforts.
When acquiring something for a communal or religious purpose, ensure the transaction is transparent and fair, especially when vulnerable parties are involved.
Use transitional periods between major life events to consolidate, rest, and lay foundations rather than rushing straight into the next challenge.

title: The Ansar Wait Daily for the Prophet's Arrival
summary: The Muslims of Madinah, having heard the Prophet was emigrating, went out every morning to the edge of the lava plain to await him, returning home only when the midday heat became unbearable — and on the very day they gave up waiting, the Prophet arrived.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "Muhammad b. Ja'far b. d-Zubayr related to me, from Wrwa b. d-Zubayr, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Uwaym b. Sa'ida, who said, 'Some of my tribesmen who were Companions of the Prophet (SAAS) said, "When we learned of the emigration of the Prophet (SAAS) from Mecca, we anticipated his arrival. We would go out, after saying the morning prayer, to the edge of the rock lava plain and await him. We would stay there until the sun would force us to seek shade, failing which, we would go indoors. It was the hot season at that time. "'"Eventually the day arrived when the Messenger of God (SAAS) did come. We had sat as usual until there was no shade left and then we had entered our homes. But when we had done so, the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived. The first to see him was a Jew, and he shouted at the top of his voice, 'Bann Qayla, your great man has come!' "'"So we all went outside to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and found him in the shade of a date-palm, accompanied by Abn Bakr, a man of similar age. Most of us had not seen the Messenger of God (SAAS) before that, and people crowded around him, not knowing how to distinguish him from Abil Bakr. But when the shade moved away from the Messenger of God (SAAS) Abii Bakr stood up and shaded him with his cloak. And at that point we recognized him.'""
lessons: Consistent, daily effort in anticipation of a blessing — even when it doesn't arrive on our schedule — is itself an act of devotion that God honours.
God's timing is perfect: the Prophet arrived on the very day the Ansar felt they had to give up waiting, teaching us that relief often comes at the moment of greatest fatigue.
Abu Bakr's instinct to shield the Prophet with his cloak the moment the shade moved is a model of attentive, self-giving love for those we are committed to protecting.
practical_advice: When waiting for something important — a job, a recovery, a reconciliation — build a daily ritual of hopeful preparation rather than passive anxiety, as the Ansar went out each morning.
If something you have been waiting for arrives at an unexpected or inconvenient moment, don't let weariness cause you to miss it; stay ready until the last.
Look for small, practical ways to protect or ease the burden of someone you care about, as Abu Bakr shaded the Prophet; sometimes a cloak held up is more eloquent than any words.

title: The People of Madinah Greet the Prophet: 'We Never Saw Such a Sight Before'
summary: When the Prophet entered Madinah, the entire city — men, women, children, and elderly — poured into the streets and climbed onto rooftops shouting 'God is Most Great!' Anas ibn Malik, who witnessed both the day of arrival and the day of the Prophet's death, said he never saw two such days.
story: ImZm Ahmad stated, "Hshim related to us, quoting Sulaymm, from Thabit, from Anas b. Miilik, who said, 'I moved along through young men shouting, "Muhammad has come!" And I'd move ahead but still see nothing. "'But then the Messenger of God (SAAS) did come, accompanied by Aba Bakr. They hid in a ruin in Medina, then sent out a bedouin asking for the an@r, the Helpers, to let them enter town. Thereupon some 500 of the aqzr went out to greet them, saying, "Do come on; you are safe and will be obeyed." '"The Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companion then came on in among them and the townspeople, even the old people as well came out to greet them, climbing on top of the houses and shouting, "Which one is he?" We never saw such a sight before.'" "Anas said, 'I witnessed the day he entered among us and the day he died, and I never saw two such days as those.'" Al-Bayhaqi related this from d-Hakim, from al-Asamm, from Muhammad b. Ishiiq d-SanCani, from Abii al-Nadr H%him b. d-Qsim, from Sulayman b. al-Mughira, from Thiibit, from Anas, whose account was very similar. In both ~ahihcollections, through Isra'il, from Aba Ishaq, from d-Bar%', we find that Abn Bakr says, in his account of the emigration, 'LWhenwe arrived in Medina, the people came out filling the streets and the roof tops, with the young and the servants all shouting and repeating, 'God is most Great! The Messenger of God has arrived. God is most Great! Muhammad has come! God is most Great!'
lessons: Communal joy expressed openly — in the streets, on the rooftops, with voices raised — is a spiritually legitimate and powerful response to a great blessing.

Anas's comparison of the day of the Prophet's arrival and the day of his death captures the profound truth that our greatest joys and deepest sorrows are often bound to the same beloved person or cause.
The 500 Ansar who went out to escort the Prophet demonstrate that welcoming someone who has suffered and travelled far is an act of communal honour and protection.
practical_advice: Allow yourself to experience and express genuine joy without restraint when a long-awaited blessing finally arrives; suppressing it robs the moment and those around you.

When someone arrives in your community after hardship — a refugee, a returning traveller, someone recovering from illness — organise a visible, warm welcome; it heals wounds that private kindness alone cannot reach.

Reflect on the people and moments in your life that represent both your highest joy and your deepest grief — understanding that the depth of one often reveals the depth of the other can bring peace to your experience of loss.

title: The Prophet's Arrival in Medina: The People's Joyful Reception
summary: When the Prophet Muhammad arrived in Medina, the people of the city flooded the streets and rooftops, with men, women, children, and servants all joyfully proclaiming his arrival with cries of 'God is most Great!'
story: When we arrived in Medina, the people came out filling the streets and the roof tops, with the young and the servants all shouting and repeating, 'God is most Great! The Messenger of God has arrived. God is most Great! Muhammad has come! God is most Great! Muhammad has come! God is most Great! The Messenger of God has come!' The next morning he moved on to where he had been ordered to go.
lessons: Moments of arrival and new beginnings can be met with overwhelming communal joy, reminding us that our presence matters to others.
Gratitude expressed openly and communally strengthens bonds and uplifts the spirit of both the giver and receiver.
Even the greatest of people benefit from the warmth and welcome of a community; belonging is a profound human and spiritual need.
practical_advice: When someone dear to you arrives after a long absence or hardship, express your joy openly — a warm welcome can heal wounds that words alone cannot.
Cultivate the habit of communal gratitude; gather with others to celebrate blessings rather than always rejoicing alone.
If you are going through a transition or migration in your own life, remind yourself that a community of welcome may be waiting for you on the other side.

title: The Chant of the Full Moon: Women and Children Welcome the Prophet
summary: Upon the Prophet's arrival in Medina, women and boys came out chanting the famous poem, 'The full moon has risen over us, from the passes of al-Wada',' expressing their joy and gratitude.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina the women and boys began chanting, 'The full moon has risen over us, from the passes of al-Wada'; We must give thanks, for what a supplicant has prayed to God.'
lessons: Poetry, song, and artistic expression have always been legitimate and beautiful ways to express spiritual joy and gratitude.
Even children and those considered ordinary members of society have a meaningful role in welcoming and honoring the righteous.
Celebrating the arrival of goodness and guidance in one's life is itself an act of worship and gratitude.
practical_advice: Find your own way to mark meaningful arrivals and transitions in your life — whether through words, art, or gathering with loved ones.
Do not underestimate the spiritual power of a song, a poem, or a chant; use creative expression to process and celebrate your emotions.
Teach children to recognize and celebrate moments of blessing; their joy is pure and contagious.

title: Ali ibn Abi Talib Returns Deposits and Joins the Prophet in Medina
summary: After the Prophet's migration, Ali ibn Abi Talib stayed behind in Mecca for three days to faithfully return all the deposits entrusted to the Prophet, then emigrated to join him in Quba.
story: Ibn Ishaq also stated that 'Ali b. Abii Talih remained in Mecca for three days and nights until he had handed back the deposits made with the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) and stayed with Kulthiim b. al-Hadm; apparently 'Ali b. Abii Talib stayed in Quba' only a night or two.
lessons: Fulfilling one's trusts and obligations, even at personal risk and inconvenience, is one of the highest expressions of integrity.
Courage is not only shown on the battlefield; staying behind alone in a hostile city to discharge duties requires immense moral fortitude.
Delaying one's own comfort or safety in order to fulfill an obligation to others is a mark of the highest character.
practical_advice: When you face a difficult transition or migration in your life, take the time to properly close your obligations before moving forward — unfinished duties create lasting anxiety.
Identify the trusts — emotional, financial, or practical — that others have placed in you, and make their return or fulfillment a priority even when it is personally costly.
Reflect on the courage of those who have gone before you in doing what was right under pressure; let their example inspire you when integrity feels costly.

title: Sahl ibn Hanif Secretly Destroys Idols at Night
summary: In Quba, Ali ibn Abi Talib noticed a man visiting a Muslim woman at night suspiciously, only to learn it was Sahl ibn Hanif secretly bringing her the destroyed remains of idols for firewood, an act of quiet, dedicated faith.
story: 'He would say, "In Quba' there was a Muslim woman who had no husband. I saw a man go to her in the depths of the night; he would knock on her door and she came out. He then gave her something he had with him which she took. I found her conduct very suspicious, so I asked her, "Who is it that knocks at your door every night; you go out to him and he gives you something I can't see properly. How can you do this, you being a Muslim woman without a husband?"" "She replied, "The man is Sahl b. Hanif. He knows I am a woman with no one and after dark he damages and destroys his people's idols then brings their remains to me. He tells me to use them as firewood."" "'Ali, God be pleased with him, used to eulogize Sahl b. Hanif in this way when he was killed in his company in Iraq."'
lessons: Righteous deeds done in secret, without seeking recognition, are among the most sincere expressions of faith and character.
Do not rush to judge the actions of others based on appearances; what looks suspicious may be an act of profound goodness.
Even in the earliest and most dangerous days of faith, ordinary people performed extraordinary acts of devotion quietly and consistently.
practical_advice: Practice doing at least one act of goodness or charity each week that no one else knows about; secret generosity purifies the intention.
Before making a judgment about someone's character or behavior, seek to understand the full context — reserve judgment until you have more information.
Find small, consistent ways to quietly support vulnerable people in your community, as Sahl did for a woman who had no one.

title: The Prophet Founds the Mosque of Quba
summary: During his stay with the Banu Amr ibn Awf at Quba, the Prophet Muhammad founded the first mosque of Islam before departing for Medina on Friday.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained with Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf in Quba' from the Monday through Thursday and founded a mosque there. Then God took him forth from among them on the Friday, though the Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf claim that he stayed there longer among them.
lessons: The very first act of community-building by the Prophet in his new home was the establishment of a place of worship, demonstrating that spiritual foundation must precede all else.
Even brief stays in a new place can be used productively to plant seeds of lasting benefit for the community.
Sacred spaces of prayer and reflection are among the most enduring gifts one generation can leave to another.
practical_advice: When starting a new chapter in your life — a new home, a new job, a new community — prioritize establishing your spiritual routines first before anything else.
Consider how you can leave a lasting positive mark on every place you live or work, no matter how briefly.
Create or dedicate a physical space in your home or life specifically for prayer, reflection, and spiritual renewal.

title: The Prophet's Camel Guided by God: Tribes Compete to Host the Prophet
summary: As the Prophet rode his camel through Medina, tribe after tribe eagerly invited him to stay with them, but he let the camel move freely, saying it was divinely guided, until it finally knelt at the site of what would become his mosque.
story: She proceeded and got as far as the homes of the Banu Bayada, where he was met by Ziyad b. Labid and Farwa b. 'Amr, along with other men of the Banu Bayada. They asked him, 'O Messenger of God, come and join us and share our protection and our wealth.' He replied, 'Make way before her, she has received a command.' And they cleared a path for her. She went on again and passed by the homes of the Banu Sa'ida where she was blocked by Sa'd b. 'Ubada and al-Mundhir b. 'Amr along with some men of the Banu Sa'ida. They said, 'O Messenger of God, come to us and have our protection.' 'Make way before her,' he replied, 'she has received a command.' And they did so. On she went until she was opposite the home of Banu al-Harith b. al-Khazraj. There her way was blocked by Sa'd b. al-Rabi', Kharija b. Zayd and 'Abd Allah b. Rawaha, along with men of Banu al-Harith b. al-Khazraj. They called out, 'Messenger of God, come to us; we have many to protect you.' But he replied as before. She proceeded further, as far as the homes of the Banu 'Adi b. al-Najjar, to whom he was related. The closest of these was the mother of 'Abd al-Muttalib, Salma, daughter of 'Amr, being one of their women. There were Salit b. Qays and Abu Salit, Usayra b. Abi Kharija and some men of the Banu 'Adi b. al-Najjar. They told him, 'O Messenger of God, join your own relatives and enjoy our wealth and protection.' 'Make way before her,' he replied, 'she has received a command.' And they did so. She went further, up to the homes of the Banu Malik b. al-Najjar, where she knelt down before what is today his mosque. At that time it was a drying shed for dates owned by two orphan youths of the Banu Malik b. al-Najjar, Sahl and Suhayl by name, sons of 'Amr. They were under the protection of Mu'adh b. 'Afra'.
lessons: When we surrender our choices to divine guidance rather than human preference or social pressure, we often end up exactly where we are meant to be.
The eagerness of so many tribes to host the Prophet reflects how a community's love and honor for righteous leadership transforms social fabric.
Important decisions about where to settle, whom to align with, and how to proceed are sometimes best left to a higher wisdom rather than forced by personal desire or social obligation.
practical_advice: When you face a major decision and multiple good options are before you, try releasing your grip on the outcome — pray for guidance and watch where the path leads naturally.
Do not let social pressure from well-meaning people rush you into a decision that does not feel right; politely and graciously decline until you feel truly settled.
Reflect on a current situation in your life where you are trying to control the outcome; practice trusting that divine wisdom may be guiding you somewhere better than you planned.

title: Abdullah ibn Ubayy Refuses to Welcome the Prophet
summary: As the Prophet passed by the house of Abdullah ibn Ubayy, the chief of the Khazraj, he stopped expecting to be invited in, but Ibn Ubayy coldly told him to stay with those who had invited him, revealing early signs of his hypocrisy.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba related that the Messenger of God (SAAS) passed by 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul while he was there in his house. The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to a stop, expecting to be invited into the house since he was at that time the chief of the Khazraj. But 'Abd Allah told him, 'Look to those who invited you. Stay with them!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) related this to a number of the ansar and Sa'd b. 'Ubada commented, apologizing for him, 'Now God has granted you to us, O Messenger of God; whereas we had wanted to place a crown on his head and make him king over us.'
lessons: Rejection from those we expect to welcome us, especially those in positions of leadership or respect, can sting deeply, but it does not diminish our worth or mission.
The envy and resentment of those who feel displaced by our arrival can manifest as cold indifference; recognizing this dynamic protects us from internalizing their hostility.
Community members who apologize and advocate for us in the face of another's rudeness are precious allies whose loyalty should be recognized and valued.
practical_advice: If you experience a cold or dismissive reception from someone you expected to welcome you, do not take it as a reflection of your value — consider what insecurities or resentments may be driving their behavior.
Identify the 'Sa'd ibn Ubada' figures in your life — the people who stand up for you and offer context when others are unfair — and make sure they know how much you appreciate them.
When you witness someone being treated coldly or rejected, consider whether you can step in to offer warmth and an explanation, as Sa'd did, so the person does not feel alone in the moment.

title: The Ansar Compete to Hold the Prophet's Camel Reins
summary: Before the Prophet left the Banu Amr ibn Awf, the Ansar gathered around his camel competing with one another to hold its reins as an expression of their love and honor for him.
story: Musa b. 'Uqba stated, 'The ansar had held a meeting before the Messenger of God (SAAS) left the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. They had gathered around his camel arguing over which of them should hold its reins, vying to honour and show their respect for the Messenger of God (SAAS). Whenever he passed by the home of one of the ansar they would invite him inside, but he would say, "Leave her alone; she has received a command. I will only stay where God decides."'
lessons: Genuine love for a spiritual leader or mentor expresses itself in the desire to serve and honor them, not merely to receive from them.
A community united in love and reverence for goodness and guidance is a community with extraordinary spiritual strength.
Trusting God's decision about where to belong — even when many good options present themselves — reflects the deepest form of reliance on divine wisdom.
practical_advice: Reflect on who in your life you deeply love and respect; find a concrete, humble way to honor them this week without expecting anything in return.
When you feel torn between multiple good communities or paths, pause before choosing based on social pressure; ask yourself where God seems to be guiding you.
Practice being the kind of person that others compete to serve and honor — not through self-promotion, but through consistent integrity, kindness, and genuine care.

title: The Prophet Stays with Abu Ayyub al-Ansari
summary: The Prophet's camel finally knelt at the door of Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, who joyfully took in the Prophet's belongings, and the Prophet stayed in his home until the mosque and its quarters were built.
story: When she arrived at the house of Abu Ayyub she knelt before the door and he dismounted. He entered that house and stayed there until his mosque and its apartments were built. Ibn Ishaq stated, 'When the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS) first knelt he did not dismount and she got up and went a little further, while the Messenger of God left its rein free without his control. She then turned around and came back to where she had knelt and did so there again. She then shook herself, and sank to the ground stretching out fully. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then dismounted. Abu Ayyub b. Khalid b. Zayd then picked up his bags and took them into the house. The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed there with him. He asked who owned the drying shed and Mu'adh b. 'Afra' told him, "It is owned by Sahl and Suhayl, two sons of 'Amr. They are orphans under my care." "Then I will buy it from them and have it as a mosque," said the Messenger of God (SAAS), and later he had it built.'
lessons: Divine selection often falls not on the most powerful or prominent, but on those whose hearts are most open and ready to receive.
The Prophet's insistence on purchasing the orphans' land rather than simply taking it demonstrates that justice and fair dealing must accompany even the greatest spiritual projects.
A home opened in genuine hospitality to a person in need of refuge becomes one of the most blessed places on earth.
practical_advice: Open your home generously to those who are new to your community, travelling, or in need of temporary shelter; hospitality is among the most powerful acts of spiritual service.
When building something for the community — a project, an initiative, a space — make sure that you deal fairly with everyone involved, especially those with less power.
If you feel 'chosen' for a task or blessing that you did not seek, receive it with humility and gratitude, as Abu Ayyub did, and give your very best in service.

title: Girls of Banu al-Najjar Welcome the Prophet with Drums and Chanting
summary: Servant girls of the Banu al-Najjar came out striking drums and chanting poetry welcoming the Prophet as their neighbor, and when he asked if they loved him, they affirmed it and he responded with equal and open love for them.
story: She knelt at the door of Abu Ayyub and servant girls of the Banu al-Najjar came out striking drums and chanting, 'We are girls of the Banu al-Najjar; how wonderful if Muhammad were our neighbour.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then came out to them and asked, 'Do you love me?' They replied, 'Oh yes, O Messenger of God!' 'And I,' he said, 'by God, love you all! By God, I love you all!'
lessons: The Prophet's open, unashamed declaration of love for his community models that expressing love and affection for others is a spiritual virtue, not a weakness.
Joy expressed through music, rhythm, and communal celebration has its place in spiritual and community life.
Mutual love between a leader and their community creates a bond of trust, safety, and belonging that no external force can easily break.
practical_advice: Tell the people you love that you love them — openly, clearly, and without embarrassment; the Prophet's example shows that this is an act of faith, not sentimentality.
Celebrate the arrivals and milestones of people in your community with whatever joyful expression is natural to your culture and context.
If you are in a position of leadership or influence, make it a practice to affirm your love and care for those you serve; it transforms the relationship from transactional to transformational.

title: The Prophet's Arrival in Medina: Welcome by the Ansar and the Banu al-Najjar Girls
summary: As the Prophet Muhammad arrived in Medina, servant girls of the Banu al-Najjar tribe sang and struck drums welcoming him, and he expressed his deep love for the people of Medina three times.
story: quarter where the Banii al-Najjw lived. And there were servant girls striking drums and chanting, "We are girls of the Banii al-Najjar; how wonderful if Muhammad were our neighhour." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "God knows that my heart loves you all!"'" This was related by Ihn Mzja, from H i s h m h. 'Ammw, from <isa b. Yanus. In the jahih of al-Bukhi%ri it is stated from Macmar, from 'Abd al-Waith, from 'Ahd al-'Aziz, from Anas, who said, "The Prophet (SAAS) saw the women and boys coming - I believe he said that it was a wedding procession. So he stood up in full view and said, '0 God, these are the people I love most of all.' He repeated this three times."
lessons: Expressing genuine love and appreciation for one's community is a prophetic practice that strengthens bonds.
True leaders acknowledge the warmth of those who support them with humility and heartfelt gratitude.
Communal joy and celebration of righteousness are encouraged and spiritually meaningful.
practical_advice: Express appreciation to those around you who show you kindness, as this nurtures lasting bonds.
When you feel welcomed by a community, reciprocate with warmth and sincerity rather than indifference.
Allow moments of communal joy to remind you of God's blessings and express gratitude openly.

title: Abu Bakr's Wise Deflection on the Journey to Medina
summary: During the migration to Medina, when travelers asked Abu Bakr about the young man with him, he wisely said 'This man shows me the path,' meaning the path of good, while protecting the Prophet's identity.
story: "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) set out for Medina with Aba Bakr riding behind h i on the same mount. Aha Bakr was an elderly man who was well known, whereas the Messenger of God (SAAS), was youthful and not known. "'If a person met Ahii Bakr and said, "Aha Bakr, who is that man in front of you?" H e would reply, "This man is showing me the path." "'He would think he was referring to him showing him the road, hut what he really meant was "the path of good"."
lessons: Wisdom sometimes lies in choosing words that protect others without resorting to outright falsehood.
Loyalty to those we love and believe in may require creative courage and presence of mind.
God provides means of protection through the intelligence and quick thinking of sincere companions.
practical_advice: In high-pressure situations, pause before speaking and choose words that are both truthful and wise.
Cultivate presence of mind by regularly reflecting on your values so you can act on them instinctively.
When supporting someone vulnerable, think proactively about how your words and actions either shield or expose them.

title: The Pursuer Who Became a Guard
summary: A horseman who set out to capture the Prophet during his migration was miraculously unhorsed and, humbled, ended up serving as the Prophet's armed guard for the rest of the journey.
story: "'Ahii Bakr looked hack and saw a horseman had caught up with them. So he said, "0Prophet of God, a rider has caught up with us!" The Messenger of God (SAAS) turned around and said, "0 God, bring him down!" The man's horse then fell, tossed him and stood neighing. The man then said, "0 Prophet of God, give me your orders!" He replied, "Stay where you are and don't let anyone catch up with me!" "'So the man had started off the morning trying to catch the Messenger of God (SAAS) and by the end of it he was acting as his armed guard!"
lessons: What begins as a threat can, by God's will and the softening of hearts, transform into protection and support.
Trust in God's power to turn adversaries into allies when one's cause is just.
Humility before truth, once recognized, can radically change the trajectory of a person's life.
practical_advice: When you face hostility, resist despair — circumstances can reverse in ways beyond your expectation.
Respond to those who change their stance toward you with directness and a clear task, as the Prophet did, rather than with resentment.
Reflect on moments in your own life when an apparent obstacle became a source of help, and let this strengthen your trust in God.

title: The Prophet Arrives in Medina and Stays with Abu Ayyub al-Ansari
summary: Upon arriving in Medina, the Prophet's camel stopped near Abu Ayyub al-Ansari's home, and the people of Medina joyfully celebrated his arrival while he settled in with Abu Ayyub's household.
story: "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) alighted at the edge of the rock lava plain and sent a message off to the an@r. When they came they greeted the two men and told them, "Mount up; you will be safe and you will be obeyed." "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) and Abii Bakr then rode on, the an@r all around them fully armed. "'In Medina people were saying, "The Prophet of God, has come!" And they raised their glances to him, repeating that the Prophet had come. '"He proceeded on and dismounted beside the house of Abii Ayyab."' Al-Wiqidi recounted that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed at the home of Abii Ayyiib, Ascad b. Zurzra took hold of the nose-rein of the camel of the Messenger of God and it thus remained with hi. "'The Messenger of God (SAAS), alighted from his mount and went to the shade. Ahii Ayytib then approached him and said, '0 Messenger of God, my house is the one closest to you; shall I carry your baggage to my place?' 'Yes,' he said. So he took his baggage into the house."
lessons: Divine guidance can work through subtle signs, such as where an animal naturally stops, to direct important decisions.
A welcoming community is one of the greatest blessings God can grant a migrant or traveler in need.
Eagerness to serve a guest or person of righteousness reflects genuine faith and noble character.
practical_advice: When facing a major life transition, look for signs of where you are most welcomed and settle there with gratitude.
If you are in a position to host or assist someone in need, act promptly as Abu Ayyub did, rather than hesitating.
Celebrate the arrival of goodness in your community; collective joy at righteousness strengthens social bonds.

title: Abdullah ibn Salam Embraces Islam Upon the Prophet's Arrival
summary: Abdullah ibn Salam, the leading Jewish scholar of Medina, immediately recognized the Prophet upon his arrival and publicly declared his faith in Islam, then challenged the Prophet to test the Jews' knowledge of his identity.
story: "'While he was talking to members of its household 'Abd Allah b. S a l b heard of him while working in a palm-grove belonging to his family.
