He quickly stopped what he was doing, she accompanying him, heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) then went back to his own people. "'The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, "Which of our people's houses is closest?" Ahii Ayyiib replied, "Mine, 0 Messenger of God; this is my house, this my door." He asked, "Then go and prepare a place for us to rest." He went and did so, then returned and said, "0 Messenger of God, I have prepared a place for your siesta. Do come, with God's blessings." They did so. "'Now that the Messenger of God (SAAS) had arrived, 'Abd Allah b. S a l b came to h i and said, "I hear witness that you are really the Prophet of God, that you have brought the truth. The Jews know me to be their leader and the son of their former leader, their most learned man and son of their former most learned man. Call them and ask them." "'When they came the Messenger of God (SAAS) addressed them, "0Jews, woe upon you! Fear God! By the God other than whom there is none, you well know that I am truly the Messenger of God, and that I bring you the truth. Accept Islam!" "'But three times they replied, "We do not know this."'"
lessons: Sincere knowledge of scripture and honest reflection can lead a person to recognize truth even before others do.
Acknowledging truth publicly despite social pressure is an act of profound moral courage.
Those who reject clear truth out of pride or communal loyalty carry a heavy spiritual burden.
practical_advice: When you sense truth in something, do not delay acting on it out of fear of social consequences.
Seek out those who are recognized for wisdom and learning when you are searching for guidance.
Reflect on moments where group pressure has caused you to deny what you privately knew to be right, and resolve to be more courageous.

title: Abu Ayyub's Reverence: Refusing to Live Above the Prophet
summary: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari was deeply uncomfortable living on the floor above the Prophet and persistently asked him to take the upper floor, eventually leading to the Prophet moving upstairs out of accommodation.
story: "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) took up residence in my house, he lived on the lower floor while I and Umm Ayyiib lived upstairs. I told h i , '"0 Messenger of God, I really hate being above you, with you beneath me. Come along and live above, while we come down to the lower floor." "'He replied, "Aha Ayyah, it's better for us and for our guests for me to live downstairs." "'And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained down while we stayed ahove in the house. It occurred to Aha Ayyiib that he was walking on top of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and so he was careful to keep to the sides, where they also slept. He then spoke to the Messenger of God (SAAS) about that, commenting, 'The lower floor is more appropriate for us; I can't he ahove a ceiling when you are beneath it.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) moved to the upper floor, while Aha Ayyah moved to the lower."
lessons: Deep reverence for righteousness moves a person to sacrifice their own comfort for the sake of those they honor.
The Prophet's humility in preferring the lower floor for the convenience of guests models selfless consideration.
Insisting on expressing respect through action, as Abu Ayyub did, reflects the sincerity of one's faith.
practical_advice: Honor those who deserve respect in practical, tangible ways rather than limiting your reverence to words.
When hosting a guest, consider their needs and comfort thoughtfully rather than offering only the minimum.
Reflect on who in your life deserves more visible, deliberate respect, and find a meaningful way to show it.

title: The Broken Water Jug and Abu Ayyub's Anxiety
summary: When a water jug broke in Abu Ayyub's home, he and his wife desperately used their only velvet garment to mop up the water, terrified that a single drop might disturb the Prophet below.
story: "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) remained down while we stayed ahove in the house. It happened that a large jug of water of ours broke and my wife and I had to mop it up as best we could with a velvet garment, since we had no other cloth. We used it to dry up the water, fearing to have any drop on the Messenger of God (SAAS) and so harm him."
lessons: Genuine love for a person of righteousness inspires extraordinary care and self-sacrifice in small daily moments.
Anxiety born of love and reverence, rather than fear of punishment, reflects a beautiful quality of the heart.
Caring for one's guests with such attentiveness is a high expression of generosity and faith.
practical_advice: Channel anxiety productively by directing it toward careful, considerate action rather than paralysis.
When you love and respect someone, let that feeling translate into attentiveness to their wellbeing in small, everyday ways.
Do not underestimate the spiritual value of small sacrifices made for others out of sincerity.

title: Seeking the Prophet's Blessed Touch in Leftover Food
summary: Abu Ayyub and his wife would seek the parts of returned food that the Prophet had touched with his fingers, eating only those portions in hopes of receiving blessing from his contact.
story: "We used to prepare his evening meal for him and send it in to him. If when he returned it there were any morsels left we would try to see where his fingers had touched it and eat that part, hoping to be blessed by that. One night we sent him his supper, having included onions or garlic and he returned it without us detecting any marks of his hands on it. So I was shocked and went to him and asked, '0Messenger of God, you have sent hack the food without me seeing any sign you have touched it.' He explained, 'I sensed the smell of that plant in the food; I'm someone who whispers to others (and such an odour may he offensive to them), so why don't you eat it.' "'We did so and never served him those vegetables again.'"
lessons: Love and devotion to the Prophet expressed itself in the intimate details of daily life, not only in grand acts.
The Prophet's concern for others, even in the small matter of food odor during private conversations with angels, shows his constant mindfulness.
True hosting means learning and honoring the preferences of your guest, even when they do not complain.
practical_advice: Pay close attention to the unspoken preferences and needs of those you care for — attentiveness is a form of love.
When someone you respect declines something, ask gently and without offense, as Abu Ayyub did, so you can serve them better.
Seek blessing in your daily life through sincere intention and attentiveness rather than only through grand religious gestures.

title: The Prophet Declines Garlic Out of Consideration for His Companions
summary: The Prophet returned food containing garlic uneaten, explaining to Abu Ayyub that because he privately converses with people — understood to include angels — he was mindful of offensive odors, so Abu Ayyub resolved never to serve him those vegetables again.
story: "'He used to prepare food for the Messenger of God (SAAS), and if it was returned he would ask about where he had placed his fingers and keep track of this. Once he made food for h i that contained garlic and when the food came back he asked where the Messenger of God (SAAS) had placed his fingers. He was told, 'He didn't eat any.' Abn Ayyiib was shocked and went up to him and asked, 'Is it a forbidden food?' T h e Prophet (SAAS) replied, 'No, but I do dislike it.' Aha Ayyab commented, 'I dislike whatever you dislike'; or he may have used the words, 'whatever you have disliked.' He also said, 'The Prophet (SAAS) would be visited by angels.' It is established in both sahib collections, that Anas b. Malik said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) was brought a large platter' - in one account it was a bowl - 'containing green vegetables. He asked and was told what these were and when he looked at them, he did not want to eat them. He commented, "You eat them; I have private conversations with people with whom you do not!"'"
lessons: The Prophet's avoidance of garlic was not religious prohibition but personal consideration for those he met — a model of social mindfulness.
Aligning one's personal choices with those of a righteous person out of love, as Abu Ayyub did, is a praiseworthy expression of devotion.
Being mindful of how our habits affect those around us is a mark of refined character.
practical_advice: Consider how your habits — including dietary choices — may affect those around you, especially in close social settings.
Distinguish between what is forbidden and what is simply disliked or inconsiderate; wisdom lies in understanding the difference.
When you discover a preference of someone you love, honor it without being asked a second time.

title: The Community of Medina Sending Nightly Food to the Prophet
summary: Every evening during the Prophet's stay at Abu Ayyub's home, people of Medina would come one after another bearing food as gifts, such that no night passed without abundant provisions arriving at his door.
story: It is related from Zayd b. Thabit that he said, "The first gift made to the Messenger of God (SAAS), after he took up residence in the home of Abii Ayyash, was brought to h i by myself. It was a big wooden bowl filled with bread, crumbled up with yoghurt and butter. I told him that my mother had sent the bowl. He commented, 'God bless her!' Then he called over his Companions and they ate. Then a wooden bowl came from Sa'd b. Wbada, a mixture of bread and meat gravy. "Not an evening went by without there being at the door of the Messenger of God (SAAS) three or four people who would come one after the other carrying food. He remained there in the home of Abii Ayytib for seven months."
lessons: A community that rushes to serve its righteous leader with generosity reflects the highest values of brotherhood and faith.
Sharing food is a powerful act of community-building and a practical expression of love.
Even small gestures of generosity, like a child bringing a bowl from his mother, carry weight when done with sincerity.
practical_advice: Look for practical, tangible ways to support those who carry heavy responsibilities in your community.
Do not wait for a grand occasion to show generosity — consistent, daily acts of giving have profound impact.
When someone in your circle is going through a difficult transition, show up with something concrete and nourishing.

title: The Prophet Sends for His Family in Mecca After Settling in Medina
summary: After settling in Medina at Abu Ayyub's home, the Prophet sent his freed-man Zayd ibn Haritha with 500 dirhams to bring his daughters, wife Sawda, and others from Mecca to join him, reuniting his family.
story: "While resident in the home of Abii Ayyiib, the Messenger of God (SAAS) along with two camels and sent his freed-man Zayd b. Hzritha and Abii carrying 500 dirhams, to bring back Fiicima and Umm Kulthm, daughters of the Messenger of God (SAAS), his wife Sawda, daughter of Zam'a and U s k a b. Zayd. Ruqayya had already emigrated with her husband 'Uthmw, while Zaynab remained with her husband, Abii al-'As b. al-Rabic in Mecca. Umm Ayman, the wife of Zayd b. Hiwitha, came with these, and so did 'Abd All& b. Abii Bakr with the other children of Abii Bakr who included CA'isha, 'mother of the believers'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had not yet consummated his marriage with her."
lessons: Even in the midst of establishing a new community, the Prophet prioritized the unity and care of his family.
Separation from loved ones during migration is a real and painful sacrifice that deserves acknowledgment and remedy.
Providing materially for the safe passage of family members is a prophetic responsibility that must not be neglected.
practical_advice: If circumstances have separated you from loved ones, make deliberate and resourced plans to restore that closeness when possible.
Recognize that migration and major life transitions carry a cost of loneliness that should be actively addressed, not merely endured.
Care for those in your household by ensuring their safety and provision is arranged, not left to chance.

title: The Prophet's Camel Divinely Guided to the Site of the Mosque and Abu Ayyub's Home
summary: When the Prophet arrived in Medina, his camel was left to move freely and knelt at a date-drying shed, which became the site of the mosque, with the Prophet declaring she was divinely guided.
story: "Al-Bayhaqi stated, '"Ah b. A b a d b. 'Abdxn informed us, quoting Ahmad b. Wbayd al-Safftr, quoting Khalaf b. CAmr al-'Akbari, quoting Sa'id b. Mansiir, quoting 'Inf b. Khdid, quoting Sadiq b. Mnsii, from 'Abd All& b. al-Zubayr, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina his mount knelt down between the homes of Jacfar b. Muhammad b. 'Ali and al-Hasan b. Zayd. People came to him then saying, '0Messenger of God, my house!' But his camel got up with him again and he said, 'Let her proceed; she is being divinely guided.' "She then moved away with him and came to the place for the rnmbar, the pulpit, where it knelt down and shook itself. A shed was there where they were storing and drying dates. The Messenger of God (SAAS), alighted from his mount and went to the shade. Ahii Ayytib then approached him and said, '0 Messenger of God, my house is the one closest to you; shall I carry your baggage to my place?' 'Yes,' he said. So he took his baggage into the house. Another man then came to him and said, '0 Messenger of God, where are you staying?' He replied, 'A man stays'"
lessons: Trusting in divine guidance means releasing control over outcomes and allowing God's will to determine the destination.
When multiple people compete for the honor of serving a righteous leader, God's guidance — not human preference — determines the outcome.
Sacred places and institutions are often established in ways that transcend human planning, affirming their divine origin.
practical_advice: When facing important decisions about where to settle or invest your efforts, seek divine guidance through prayer and trust the signs you are given.
Resist the urge to force outcomes driven by your desire for honor or recognition; let sincerity and readiness speak louder.
Reflect on how the most meaningful things in your life came about through paths you did not fully plan, and let this build your trust in God.

title: The Prophet's Camel Kneels at the Site of the Mosque
summary: Upon arriving in Madinah, the Prophet's camel knelt at a specific spot, which the Prophet recognized as divinely chosen, and he stayed in a nearby shed for 12 nights until the mosque was built.
story: came to the place for the mrnbar, the pulpit, where it knelt down and shook itself. A shed was there where they were storing and drying dates. The Messenger of God (SAAS), alighted from his mount and went to the shade.
lessons: Divine guidance can manifest in unexpected and subtle ways, even through the behavior of animals.
Patience in waiting for the right moment — the Prophet stayed 12 nights humbly in a shed — is a mark of true leadership.
Sacred spaces are not determined by grandeur but by divine selection and blessing.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult decision, trust that God may guide you through circumstances beyond your control rather than forcing an outcome.
Be willing to live simply and patiently during transitional periods rather than rushing to establish comfort.
Recognize moments of divine guidance in your life by reflecting on how circumstances aligned without your planning.

title: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari Hosts the Prophet
summary: Abu Ayyub al-Ansari honored the Prophet by taking his baggage and hosting him in his home, an act that brought him great distinction and was later reciprocated by Ibn Abbas toward Abu Ayyub himself.
story: Abii Ayytib then approached him and said, '0 Messenger of God, my house is the one closest to you; shall I carry your baggage to my place?' 'Yes,' he said. So he took his baggage into the house. Another man then came to him and said, '0 Messenger of God, where are you staying?' He replied, 'A man stays wherever his baggage is.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed there in the shed for 12 nights, until the mosque was built. This was a great honour for Abii Ayyiib Khalid b. Zayd, God bless him, to have the Messenger of God (SAAS) stay with him. It was related to us through Yazid h. Abii Habib from Muhammad b. 'Ali b. 'Ahd All& b. 'Ahbas, may God be pleased with him, that Abii Ayyiib came to Basra over which Ibn 'Abbas had been appointed governor by 'Ali b. Abii Tdib, may God be pleased with him. Ibn 'AhbS came out of his house and invited Abii Ayyiib to stay there, just as the latter had provided accommodation for the Messenger of God (SAAS) in his house. He also presented him with everything within the house; and when he came to leave, Ibn 'Abbas gave him 20,000 dirhams and 40 slaves. Abii Ayyiih's house later passed into the hands of his freed-man Aflah; al-Mughira h. 'Abd al-Rahman b. aI-Harith b. H i s h m bought it from him for 1,000 dinars, repaired it and donated it for the use of the poor of Medina who belonged to the household of the Prophet (SAAS).
lessons: Acts of sincere generosity toward those close to God are never forgotten and are often repaid manifold in this life and the next.
Hospitality is a living tradition — Ibn Abbas honoring Abu Ayyub the way Abu Ayyub had honored the Prophet shows how good deeds inspire and perpetuate goodness across generations.
The homes and spaces associated with righteous acts carry a lasting blessing that continues to benefit communities long after the original event.
practical_advice: Look for opportunities to offer hospitality and care to guests or those in need without waiting to be asked.
When someone has shown kindness to you or to those you love, find a meaningful way to honor them in return.
Give generously and without calculation — true generosity, like that of Abu Ayyub, becomes part of your lasting legacy.

title: God Chooses the Home of Banu al-Najjar for the Prophet
summary: Among the nine tribal complexes of Madinah, God chose the home of Banu Malik b. al-Najjar for the Prophet, a distinction that became a source of honor for that clan and a teaching about divine selection over human preference.
story: Similarly his staying in the home of the Banii al-Najjsr and God's choosing that for him, was also a great honour. In Medina there were many such complexes, nine in number. Each of these was separate and self-sufficient, with its own date-palms, gardens and families. Each one of their tribes were gathered in their own quarters, making them like adjoining villages. God chose for the Messenger of God (SAAS) the home of the Banii Malik h. al-Najjar. In both fahih collections it is established from a hadith of Shu'ba as follows, 'I heard Qatada quote Anas b. Mdik as stating, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'The best of the amdr homes is that of the Banii al-Najjsr, then comes that of the Banii al-Ashhal, then the Banii al-H&th b. al-Khazraj, then the Banii Sa'ida; and in all the homes of the anfar there is good.'" Sa'd h. W a d a stated, "I never saw the Messenger of God (SAAS), fail to express preference for others over us." But he was told instead, "He gave preference to you over many."'
lessons: Divine selection often surpasses human expectation — what God chooses carries a honor beyond what any person could assign.
Even when we feel passed over or ranked lower than we hoped, we may still hold a high station we are unaware of.
Gratitude for one's position, rather than competition for rank, is the mark of spiritual maturity.
practical_advice: When you feel overlooked or undervalued, remind yourself that God's assessment of your worth is not always visible to others or even to yourself.
Resist the urge to compare your standing with others; focus instead on the good you have been given.
Practice contentment by reflecting on the blessings in your situation rather than what you perceive others to have received.

title: The Virtues of the Ansar and the Prophet's Deep Love for Them
summary: The Prophet expressed profound love and loyalty to the Ansar, the Helpers of Madinah, declaring them his household and warning that love for them is a sign of faith while hatred for them is a sign of hypocrisy.
story: Great honour and credit were firmly established for the people of Medina, the anfar, both in this world and the next. God Almighty stated, "As for those who are first and foremost, of the muhajrrin, the Emigrants and the aqar, 'the Helpers', and those who followed them in goodness, God is pleased with them, and they are with Him. He has prepared for them gardens beneath which rivers flow; they will live in them forever. That is the mighty success" (sarat al-Tawba or al-Bar2a; IX, v.100). And God also stated, "And (give also) to those who lived in this area and had faith before them, loving those who emig~atedto them, and they do not find in themselves any grudge because of what others were given, rather, prefemng others over themselves, though they may themselves be in need. Those spared from the meanness of their selves shall be those who succeed" (%rat al-Hashra; LIX, v.9). The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "Had I not been compelled to perform the emigration personally, I would have been an an$ri, a Helper; and if people had gone to any valley or detle, I would have gone to the valley or defile of the Helpers. The Helpers are as an undergarment, the people at large as an overgarment." He also said, "The Helpers are my household and my own family." And he said, "I am peace to those who make peace with them and war to those who make war upon them." Al-Bukhzri stated that Hajjaj b. Minhd related to him, quoting Shu'ba, quoting 'Adi h. Thzbit, as follows, "I heard al-Bar2 b. 'Azib say, 'I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say' - or he said - 'the Messenger of God (SAAS), said, "The a q d r are loved only by those who believe and hated only by those who are hypocritical. Whoever loves them is loved by God, and whoever hates them is hated by God.""' Al-Bukhzri also stated, "Muslim b. Ibrzhim related to us, quoting Shu'ba, from 'Abd al-RabZn b. CAhd Allah b. Jubayr, from Anas b. Mdik, from the Prophet (SAAS) who said, 'A sign of faith is love for the Helpers; a sign of hypocrisy is hatred for the Helpers.'"
lessons: True brotherhood and solidarity — as modeled by the Ansar who welcomed the Emigrants selflessly — is one of the highest expressions of faith.
Love and loyalty to the righteous are spiritually significant virtues, not merely social preferences.
The Prophet's declaration that he would have been an Ansari had he not been commanded to emigrate shows that honor lies in service and sacrifice, not in status or lineage.
practical_advice: Cultivate genuine love and appreciation for those who support and uplift others selflessly in your community.
Examine whether your relationships are built on mutual support and preference of others over yourself, or on competition and comparison.
Reflect on those in your life who have been your 'helpers' in difficult times and express sincere gratitude to them.

title: Abu Qays Sirma's Poem on the Ansar Welcoming the Prophet
summary: The Ansari poet Abu Qays Sirma b. Abu Anas composed verses celebrating how the Prophet spent a decade preaching in Makkah without support, and how the Ansar of Madinah finally gave him refuge, friendship, and divine help.
story: How fine are the verses spoken by Aha Qays Sirma b. Aha Anas, mentioned above, a poet from the Helpers, concerning the amval of the Messenger of God (SAAS) among them, and of the help and comfort they gave to him and to his Companions, God bless them all. Ibn I s b q stated, "Abii Qays Sirma b. Ahn Anas also spoke the following, making reference to how God had honoured them by bringing them Islam and His Messenger (SAAS), 'A decade or so he stayed among Quraysh, preaching in hopes of finding agreeable friends, Offering himself to those attending the fairs, but he found no one to protect or invite him. When he came to us his mind was put at ease and he became pleased and contented, He found friends and his mind became at ease, and he received evident help from God. He would tell us what Noah informed his people, and what Moses said when he answered the call, And so he came to fear no one, whether near or far away. We gave of our best wealth and of ourselves amidst the uproar and the shared sorrows, Opposing all those people that he opposed, even those who had been dear and close. We know that there is nothing besides God, and that the book of God has become our guide. I would say, when praying at any temple, "Have pity, do not show enmity towards us!" I would say, when crossing any dangerous land, "Blessed are You, the One relied upon." Even if you step to one side the ways of death are many, and it is not you who can preserve yourself. And, by God, a man does not know how his course will be, if he does not have God watch over him. The thirsty date-palm will do its owner no good if it becomes well-watered yet he becomes buried!"'
lessons: Years of rejection and isolation before a breakthrough are not signs of failure but part of a divinely ordered journey.
A community that opens its heart to those who are marginalized or displaced becomes a source of divine help and blessing.
Reliance upon God in both prayer and in crossing dangerous paths is the foundation of true courage and peace.
practical_advice: When you are going through a prolonged period of loneliness or rejection, hold onto the knowledge that your breakthrough may come from an unexpected place, just as the Prophet's relief came from Madinah.
In your own community, actively look for those who are isolated or without support and extend genuine welcome and friendship to them.
Adopt the habit of beginning difficult tasks or journeys with a sincere invocation of God, anchoring your heart in trust rather than anxiety.

title: The Prophet's Prayer That Madinah Become Dear to Him
summary: Upon arriving in Madinah, the Prophet prayed to God, acknowledging that Makkah was the dearest place to his heart, and asking God to make Madinah dear to him instead — a prayer that reflects both his human longing and his submission to God's will.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The hdfi? Abii <AbdAllah quoted Abii al-Walid and Aba Bakr b. 'Abd All&, both of whom said, 'Al-Hasan b. Sufym related to us, quoting Aba Miisa al-Anszri, quoting Sacid b. Sacid, quoting his brother, from Aba Hurayra, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "0God, you have brought me out of the town that is dearest to me; let me now dwell in the town dearest to You." And God sent h i to dwell in Medina.'"
lessons: The Prophet's prayer reveals that faith does not erase human emotion — he genuinely grieved leaving Makkah — but it redirects longing toward God's will.
When we are separated from what we love, turning that pain into prayer is a powerful act of trust and surrender.
God's chosen place for us may not be the place we would have chosen for ourselves, yet it holds blessings we cannot yet see.
practical_advice: When forced to leave something or someone you love, allow yourself to grieve honestly while simultaneously asking God to make your new reality a source of goodness.
Practice reframing loss not as abandonment but as redirection, trusting that God's choices for your life are better than your own.
Use the Prophet's prayer as a template in your own life: acknowledge what you love, acknowledge what you've lost, and ask God to fill the new space with His blessing.

title: The Prophet's Declaration of Makkah's Greatness Before Leaving
summary: Standing at al-Hazwara in the market of Makkah before his forced migration, the Prophet declared Makkah to be the best and dearest land on earth to him, expressing that he would never have left it had he not been compelled.
story: The most famous proof for them of this is in what Imam A h a d stated, namely, "Abii d-Yaman related to us, quoting Shukyb, quoting al-Zuhri and Abii Salama h. <Abdal-Rahan that 'Abd All* b.cAdi b. al-Hamra' told h i that he heard the Prophet (SAAS), while standing at al-Hazwara in the market of Mecca, say, 'By God, I swear that of all God's earth you are the best, and the dearest to me; and had I not been forced out of you I would never have left.'" Mmad related the same, from Yacqoh b. Ibrahim, from his father, from Sdih b. Kaysn, from al-Zuhri. Al-Tirmidhi related it thus, as did al-Nasa1i and Ibn Maja, from a hadith of al-Layth, from 'Uqayl, from al-Zuhri. Al-Tirmidhi stated it to be "hasan, $ahi!z'", "good and authentic".
lessons: Even the Prophet experienced the deep pain of forced exile from his homeland, showing us that displacement and grief over one's roots is a profoundly human experience.
Being compelled to leave what we love for a higher purpose does not mean we stop loving it — faith and longing can coexist.
The Prophet's honesty about his love for Makkah teaches us that acknowledging grief is not weakness but authenticity.
practical_advice: If you have been forced to leave your home, community, or a cherished part of your life, allow yourself to grieve that loss without guilt.
Recognize that being compelled by circumstances beyond your control to change your path is not a failure — it can be the beginning of a greater purpose.
Honor your roots and the places and people that shaped you, even when circumstances take you far from them.

title: Madinah as a Spiritual Fortress: Faith Retreats to It Like a Snake to Its Hole
summary: The Prophet described Madinah as a city where faith retreats and is purified, comparing it to a bellows that removes dross from iron, and a snake that retreats to the safety of its hole.
story: It is established in both ~ahihcollections through Habib b. Yasiif, from Ja'far b. 'Avim, from Aba Hurayra who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Faith fits into Medina l i e a snake into its hole.'" Muslim also related this, from Shababa, from Muhammad b. hfic, from 'A~irnb. Muhammad b. Zayd b. 'Abd Allah b. Wmar, from his father, from Ibn Wmar, from the Prophet (SAAS), in much the same words. Also in the sahih collections is the hadith of Mdik, quoting Yahya h. Sa'id as saying that he heard Abii al-Hubab Sacid b. Yasar report, "I heard Abii Hurayra say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "I was commanded to go to a town that eats up other towns; they call it 'Yathrib', but it is 'al-Madina'. It purifies men just as a bellows purifies the dross from iron."'"
lessons: Sacred spaces and righteous communities have a real purifying effect on the soul — the environment we choose to inhabit shapes our faith.
Faith, like a snake returning to safety, seeks out places of security and nourishment; we must be intentional about creating and finding such environments.
The transformation of 'Yathrib' to 'al-Madina' (the city) reflects how divine purpose can redefine the identity of a place and its people.
practical_advice: Be intentional about the communities, spaces, and relationships you surround yourself with, as they have a genuine impact on the health of your faith and character.
When your faith feels depleted, seek out environments of worship, community, and remembrance of God that can renew and purify your spiritual state.
Reflect on how your own character and community can be like a 'purifying bellows' — refining rather than corrupting those who enter it.

title: The Prophet's Farewell Words to Mecca
summary: As the Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) departed Mecca, he stood at al-Hazwara and expressed his deep love for the city, declaring it the dearest land to him and lamenting that he would not have left had his people not expelled him.
story: al-Zuhri. Al-Tirmidhi related it thus, as did al-Nasa1i and Ibn Maja, from a hadith of al-Layth, from 'Uqayl, from al-Zuhri. Al-Tirmidhi stated it to be "hasan, $ahi!z'", "good and authentic". Yonus related this from al-Zuhri. Muhammad b. 'Amr related it from Abii Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahmsn, from Abo Hurayra. In my view the hadith of al-Zuhri is more reliable. I m a Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Razzaq related to us, quoting Macmar, from al-Zuhri, from Aba Salama h. 'Abd al-Rahman, from Abii Hurayra, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stood at al-Hazwara and said, "I know you to be of all God's earth the best, and the dearest to me; and if your people had not expelled me I would not have forsaken you."'" Al-Nasa'i related this thus from an account of Macmar. The hdfiq al-Bayhaqi stated, "This is a mistake on Macmar's part." Some authorities relate this from Muhammad b. 'Atnr, from Abii Salama, from Abii Hurayra; it too is a misconception, the authentic version being that of the community of scholars. Ahmad also stated, "Ibrzhim b. Khalid related to us, quoting Rab*, from Macmar, from Muhammad b. Muslim b. Shiiab al-Zuhri, from Abii Salama, from some other scholars, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, while he was in the market place of al-Hazwara, 'By God, I swear that of all God's earth you are the best, and the dearest to me; and had I not been forced out of you, I would never have left.'" Al-Tabrxni related it from &mad b. Khulayd al-Halabi, from al-Humaydi, from Ibn Akh al-Zuhri, from Muhammad b. Jubayr b. Mutcim, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Adi b. al-ham^".
lessons: Loving one's homeland is a natural human emotion; the Prophet's grief at leaving Mecca shows that attachment to one's roots is not a weakness but a sign of a deep and feeling heart.
Enduring forced separation from what one loves most — whether a home, a community, or a way of life — can be a divine test that opens the door to a greater mission and purpose.
When we are compelled to leave behind what we cherish, we can take comfort in knowing that God's plan encompasses our losses and that what lies ahead may be greater than what was left behind.
practical_advice: When facing an unwanted change or loss, allow yourself to acknowledge the grief honestly rather than suppressing it, as the Prophet openly expressed his love for Mecca even as he departed.
Remind yourself that being forced out of a comfort zone, as painful as it feels, may be the very catalyst for the growth and contribution you were meant to make.
Write down or verbally express your feelings about a place, relationship, or chapter of life you have had to leave; this act of acknowledgment can begin the process of healing and acceptance.

title: The Establishment of the Islamic Hijri Calendar
summary: During the Caliphate of 'Umar ibn al-Khattab, the Companions unanimously agreed to begin the Islamic calendar from the year of the Prophet's emigration to Medina, choosing al-Muharram as the first month of the new era.
story: The events of the first year of the hegira: An Account of the major events and happenings in the f i s t year following the emigration of the Messenger of God ($'AAY). The ~ahaba, the "Companions", God be pleased with them, agreed in the year 16 AH - or some say 17 or 18 AH - during the Caliphate of Wmar, on having the Islamic era begin with the year of the emigration. This came about because a contractual document or agreement was referred to Wmar by one man complaining against another. In it was stated that it came into effect in the month of Sha'bim. Wmar asked, "Which Sha'ban? That of this, last or next year?" He then assembled the Companions and discussed the need for a calendar whereby contracts concerning debts and other matters should come into effect. One speaker suggested, "Date as the Persians do." But this met no favour. For the Persians used to date by the reigns of their kings, one following the other. Another man suggested, "Use the same dating system as the Byzantines." They used to date by the reign of Alexander, son of Phillip, the Macedonian. But this met no favour. Another man suggested, "Date from the birth of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Another suggested, "No, date from his receiving the Mission." Another suggested, "No, from his emigration." Others suggested by the date of his death. 'Umar, God be pleased with him, preferred the date of his emigration, because it was so definite and well known, and the others agreed with him. In his ~ahih collection al-Bukhari stated, under the heading: The dating system and when it was established. '"Ahd Allzh b. Muslim related to us, quoting 'Abd alLCAziz, from his father, from Sahl b. Sa'd, who said, "They did not have the dating system start after the beginning of the mission of the Prophet (SAAS), nor after his death, but after his arrival in Medina."' Al-Waqidi stated, "Ibn Aha al-Zinad related to us, from his father, who said, 'Wmar sought advice on a dating system and they agreed upon the emigration.'" Aha Da'iid al-Taydisi stated, from Qurra b. Khalid al-Sadasi, from Muhammad h. Sirin, who said, "A man went up to Wmar and said, 'Set a dating system!' He asked, 'What's that?' The man explained, 'It's something other peoples do; they record things in the form: in such-and-such a month of such-and-such a year.' 'Fine,' agreed Wmar, 'just set it up.' They then discussed the matter of the date from which it should begin. Some suggested from the beginning of the Mission of the Messenger of God (SAAS), others suggested from his death. Finally they agreed it should be from his emigration. Then they discussed at which month it should commence. Ramada was suggested, and then al-M~&arram, it being the time for the return of people from their pilgrimage and it was also a sacred month. They agreed on al-Muharram." Ibn Jarir stated, "Qutayba related to us, quoting Niih b. Qays al-Ta'i, from 'Uthmanh. Muh~in, that Ibn CAbbas used to say in regard to the words of the Almighty, 'By the dawn and ten nights!' (shrat al-Fair; LXXXIX, v.l), that the reference was to al-Muharram, the dawn of the year." It is related that 'Ubayd h. Wmayr stated, "Muharram is God's month; it is the beginning of the year and in it the kacba receives its new cover. It is the month from which people establish the date and in which coins are minted." Ahmad stated, "Rawh b. Whada related to us, quoting Zakariyya' b. Ishaq, from 'Amr h. Din%, who said, 'The first man to put a date on a document was Ya'ln h. Umayya in the Yemen. The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina in Rabi' al-Awwal, and people marked that as the first of the year.'" Mulpmmad b. Ishnq related from al-Zuhri and Muhammad b. Salih related from al-Sha'bi, both saying, "The B a a Isma'il dated from the fire of Abraham and then from the building by Abraham and Isma'il of the temple, then from the death of Ka'b b. Lu'ayy, then from the [year of the] elephant. After that 'Umar b. al-Kha~ab began dating from the emigration. That was in the year 17 or 18 AH." The conclusion is that they did make the beginning of Islamic history from the year of the emigration. They set its beginning from al-Muharram, this being well known. This is the consensus of the Imams. Al-Suhayli and others quoted I m m MZlik as having said, "The Islamic year began in RahiC al-Awwal, because that was the month when the Messenger of God (SAAS) emigrated." He also made such an assertion elsewhere by referring to the Almighty's words, "certainly a mosque founded in piety from the first day" (siirat al-Tamba or al-Barz'a; IX, v.108). That is, from the first day of the arrival of the Prophet (SAAS) in Medina. This was the first day of the era, just as the Companions have agreed to its first year having been that of the emigration. There is no doubt that this, as stated by Imzm Mdik, God bless hi, is appropriate. However, the actual practice is contrary to this. The first month of the Arabs is indeed al-Muharram. They thus made the first year of the calendar that of the emigration, and had it begin with al-Muharram (instead of Rahic al-Awwal) so that there would he no confusion in the system.
lessons: Community consultation and collective decision-making, as modeled by 'Umar in assembling the Companions, leads to outcomes that stand the test of time and earn broad acceptance.
Marking time from a moment of sacrifice and migration rather than conquest or worldly glory reflects an Islamic value system that honors perseverance and devotion over power.
Even practical administrative matters, such as setting a calendar, can be rooted in spiritual meaning, reminding us that Islam integrates the sacred and the worldly.
practical_advice: When facing an important decision with multiple valid options, follow 'Umar's example by consulting trusted people around you rather than deciding in isolation.
Reflect on which moments in your own life represent true turning points of faith and commitment; these are the landmarks worth remembering and building from.
Use the beginning of a new Islamic year as an intentional moment to reset your goals, just as 'Umar used the Hijra as a fixed reference point for order and clarity.

title: The Prophet's Arrival in Medina and Scholarly Debate on His Years in Mecca
summary: The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) entered Medina on a Monday at approximately noon, concluding his emigration from Mecca, where scholars debate whether he had spent 10 or 13 years after receiving revelation before departing.
story: Our comment is - and we rely on God's help - that the year of the blessed emigration begins while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was still resident in Mecca. He had received the pledge of the an$&, "the Helpers", at the second 'Aqaba meeting, as we have explained, in the middle days of the iashriq period, that is on the 12th of Dhii al-Hijja, preceding the year of the emigration. The a q a r then went back and the Messenger of God (SAAS) permitted the Muslims to emigrate to Medina. One by one his Companions left for Medina to the point where no one was left in Mecca who could emigrate except for the Messenger of God (SAAS). Abii Bakr had kept himself hack so as to be able to accompany the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the journey, as we have explained earlier. They then emigrated together, as is detailed above. 'Ali b. Abn Tdib was to leave later, at his command, to take care of the deposits entrusted to the Messenger of God (SAAS); he joined the other two men at Quba'. The Messenger of God (SAAS) entered Medina on a Monday at approximately noon, when the heat was extreme. Al-Waqidi and others stated, "That occurred on the 2nd of the month of Rabic al-Awwal." Ibn Ishaq also related this, except that he did not stop there but preferred a date of the 12th of that month. This is the popular view held by most scholars. The Messenger of God (SAAS) according to the best accounts, remained in Mecca following his receiving the Mission for 13 years. This is what we learn in the account of Hammad b. Salama, from Aba Harnza al-Dabbi, from Ibn 'Abhas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) received the Mission at the age of 40 and remained living in Mecca for 13 years." Ibn Jarir related it similarly from Muhammad b. Mu'ammar, from Rawh b. Wbada, from Zakariyyz' b. Ishaq, from 'Amr b. Din= that he (Ihn 'Ahbas) used the words, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed in Mecca 13." It has been earlier stated that Ibn {Ahhas wrote down verses of Sirma h. Abii Anas b. Qays, including, "Some decades he stayed among Quraysh, preaching in hopes of finding agreeable friends." Al-Waqidi stated, from I b r a i m b. Ismacil, from Da'iid b. al-Husayn, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas, that he cited that same verse as testimony (to the residence having been for 13 years). Ibn Jarir related the same, from a l - H ~ t h, from M u b a d b. Sa'd, from al-Wqidi, but with reference to "15 pilgrimages", a very strange statement indeed. Even stranger than that is what Ibn Jarir stated, namely, "It was related to me from Rawh b. Wbada, who said, 'Sa'id related to us, from Qatada, who said, "The Qur'an was revealed to the Messenger of God (SAAS) over a period of 8 years in Mecca and 10 in Medina."'" Al-Hasan used to say "over 10 years in Mecca and 10 in Medina." This last statement to which al-Hasan al-Basri subscribed, that he remained in Mecca 10 years, was one with which Anas b. Mdik, '.&'isha, Sacidb. al-Musayyab and 'Amr b. Din% all agreed, according to what Ibn Jarir related of them. And there is the account from Ibn CAbbas, related by Ahmad b. Hanbal from Yahya b. SaSd, from Hishzm, from CIkrima. It has Ibn 'Abbas as saying, "The Prophet (SAAS) received revelation at the age of 43 and he remained in Mecca for 10 years." We recounted above that al-Sha'bi stated, "Ismfil was linked with the Messenger of God (SAAS) for 3 years during which he communicated to him the Word and the Message. In one account, he would hear his voice but not see his person; and thereafter it was Gabriel." Al-Wzqidi related that one of his sheikhs rejected that statement of al-Sha'bi. Ibn Jarir tried to combine the statements of those who maintained that the Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed for 10 years in Mecca with those who considered it to have been 13 years by reference to what al-Sha'bi had said. When the party of the Prophet (SAAS) came to Media he first stayed, as stated above, at Qubz' in the home of the Banii 'Amr.
lessons: The Prophet's 13 years of patient preaching in Mecca before emigrating illustrates that true change and mission require sustained effort over long periods of time, not overnight results.
Abu Bakr's devoted self-restraint — holding back from emigrating solely to accompany the Prophet — is a model of the kind of loyal friendship that places another's needs before one's own.

'Ali's willingness to stay behind in Mecca at personal risk to return the trusts of others demonstrates that integrity and fulfilling one's obligations must be upheld even in the most difficult circumstances.
practical_advice: When you feel your efforts are yielding no visible results, reflect on the Prophet's years of perseverance in Mecca; meaningful missions often require a long season of patient, unseen work before the breakthrough comes.
Cultivate at least one friendship in your life characterized by the kind of selfless loyalty Abu Bakr showed — someone who holds back for your sake and journeys with you through difficulty.
Before moving on from any role or responsibility, follow 'Ali's example by ensuring that all obligations and trusts entrusted to you have been properly discharged.

title: The Mosque at Quba': The First Mosque Built in Medina
summary: Upon arriving in Medina, the Prophet Muhammad established the mosque at Quba', the first mosque built in Islam for the general Muslim community, which was praised by God in the Quran for being founded in piety.
story: When the party of the Prophet (SAAS) came to Medina he first stayed, as stated above, at Quba' in the home of the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. He remained there at the most for 22 nights, though some say it was 18, others 10 or so, and according to Musa b. Uqba, 3 nights. What is widely accepted is what Ibn Ishaq and others stated, namely that he was there at Quba' from a Monday to a Friday. He did establish during this period of disputed length, as we have indicated, the mosque at Quba'. Al-Suhayli claimed that the Messenger of God (SAAS) founded it on the first day he arrived at Quba', citing to support this view the Almighty's words, "certainly, a mosque founded in piety from the first day" (Surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.108). And he refuted those who would have the wording read "from the foundation of the first day". It is a fine, much honoured mosque. God Almighty revealed about it, "certainly a mosque founded in piety from the first day is fully deserving of you standing (for prayer) inside it; therein are men who love that they be purified, and God loves those who purify themselves" (Surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.108). It is also stated in a hadith that it was Gabriel, peace be upon him, who pointed out to the Prophet (SAAS) the place for the qibla, the prayer niche, for the mosque at Quba'. This mosque was the first built in Medina; indeed it is the first that was made for people in general in this community (of believers). The Prophet (SAAS) used to visit it in later times and would pray there. He would go to Quba' each Saturday, sometimes mounted and sometimes on foot. It is a hadith from him that, "Prayer in the mosque at Quba' is like an 'Umra."
lessons: A new beginning, no matter how humble, can carry immense spiritual significance — the first mosque was built not in grandeur but in simplicity and piety.
God recognizes and praises those who strive for spiritual and physical purification, showing that sincere effort in worship is seen and valued.
Sacred spaces created with pure intention become sources of blessing for entire communities for generations to come.
practical_advice: When starting something new in your life — a new chapter, a new routine, a new relationship — begin it with sincere intention and piety, as this foundation shapes everything that follows.
Invest in your acts of purification and worship, even when they seem small or routine; God rewards consistent spiritual effort just as He praised the people of Quba'.
Create or seek out a dedicated space in your life for prayer and reflection, even if modest, as having a consistent place of worship strengthens your spiritual anchor.

title: The People of Quba' and the Verse of Purification
summary: The Prophet Muhammad asked the people of Quba' about their purification practices after God praised them in the Quran, and they explained they had learned to wash thoroughly after following the practice of their Jewish neighbours.
story: We also made mention of the hadith related by the Imam Ahmad as follows: "Hasan b. Muhammad related to us, quoting Abu Idris, quoting Shurahbil quoting Uwaym b. Sa'ida who said that it was related to him that the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to them in the mosque in Quba' and said, 'God has given you much good praise for purifying yourselves, in the reference to your mosque; what is it you do to so purify yourselves?' They replied, 'We swear by God, O Messenger of God, that the only thing we know is that we had some Jewish neighbours who would wash their behinds after defecating, and so we washed like them.'" Ibn Khuzayma included this hadith in his Sahih collection and there are other testimonies to it. It is also related from Khuzayma b. Thabit and Muhammad b. 'Abd Allah b. Salam and Ibn 'Abbas. Abu Da'ud, al-Tirmidhi and Ibn Maja related from a hadith of Yunus b. al-Harith, from Ibrahim b. Abi Maymuna from Abu Hurayra, from the Prophet (SAAS), who said, "The following verse was revealed about the people of Quba': 'In it there are men who love to be purified, and God loves those who purify themselves.'" He then went on, "They would wash after excretion and this verse was revealed about them."
lessons: Goodness and virtue can be learned from any source; the people of Quba' adopted a better practice of cleanliness from their neighbours and were praised by God for it.
Small, consistent acts of physical and spiritual purification are noticed by God and can elevate a person's standing in ways they may not even realize.
Humility in acknowledging where you learned a good habit is a sign of sincerity and genuine piety.
practical_advice: Be open to learning beneficial habits and practices from those around you, regardless of their background — good character and hygiene are universal values worth adopting.
Do not underestimate the spiritual value of your daily routines of cleanliness and self-care; these are acts of self-respect that align with what God loves.
Reflect regularly on whether your daily habits — physical and spiritual — reflect someone who loves to be purified and to grow, and make small adjustments where needed.

title: Salman the Persian Brings a Gift to the Prophet at Quba'
summary: Salman the Persian, upon hearing of the Prophet's arrival in Medina, came to him at Quba' twice — first offering charity, which the Prophet declined to eat, and then a gift, which he accepted — thereby confirming one of the signs of prophethood he had been seeking.
story: The matters relating to the acceptance of Islam by Salman have been referred to above in the chapter on the bisharat, the early signs. They tell how when Salman the Persian heard of the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to Medina, he came and brought him something that he laid before him while he was at Quba', saying, "This is a donation." But the Messenger of God (SAAS) refrained from eating it and told his Companions to do so and they did eat it. Salman then came a second time with something he presented saying, "This is a present." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then ate some of it himself and told his Companions to do the same, which they did.
lessons: The Prophet's discernment between charity and a gift — eating from one but not the other — demonstrated that true prophethood is confirmed through knowledge, consistency, and integrity, not mere claims.
A sincere seeker of truth, like Salman, persists and tests carefully rather than accepting or rejecting rashly; patience in the search for truth is rewarded.
Small interactions and gestures can carry enormous spiritual significance when they occur at the right moment in a person's journey toward faith.
practical_advice: When you are searching for truth or meaning in your life, be patient and deliberate — test what you encounter with reason and sincerity rather than rushing to conclusions.
Honor those who have been long searching for something meaningful by engaging with them with care and clarity, as the Prophet did for Salman.
Remember that the fulfillment of a long spiritual journey, like Salman's years of searching, can arrive suddenly and quietly — stay ready and open to recognizing it when it comes.

title: Abdullah ibn Salam Embraces Islam Upon the Prophet's Arrival in Medina
summary: Abdullah ibn Salam, a leading Jewish scholar of Medina, recognized the Prophet Muhammad as truthful upon his arrival and embraced Islam, devising a plan to expose the dishonesty of the Jewish leaders before they could slander him.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina, the people ran away scared, as I did myself. But as I learned more about him I realized that he was no liar. The first thing I heard him say was, 'Spread peace, feed others, pray at night while others sleep, and you will achieve paradise in peace.' This tradition requires that 'Abd Allah b. Salam had heard of the Prophet (SAAS) and saw him directly following his arrival at Quba', when he dismounted there among the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. In the account of al-Bukhari, through 'Abd al-'Aziz from Anas, the last-mentioned stated, "When the Prophet (SAAS) arrived, 'Abd Allah b. Salam came to him and said, 'I hear witness that you are the Messenger of God and that you bring the truth. The Jews recognize me as their leader and the son of their leader, their most learned man and the son of their most learned man. Summon them and ask them about me before they know that I have embraced Islam, because if they know I have done so they will say of me things that are untrue.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then sent for the Jews and when they came he asked them, 'O Jews, woe upon you! Fear God! For, by God other than whom there is none, you certainly know that I am the Messenger of God, in truth, and that I bring you the truth. Therefore accept Islam!' They replied, 'We do not know it.' Three times this was repeated. Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Which of your men is 'Abd Allah b. Salam?' "They replied, 'That is our leader, and the son of our leader, our most learned man and the son of our most learned man.' He then asked, 'What would you think if he accepted Islam?' They replied, 'God forbid! He would never accept Islam!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then called out, 'Ibn Salam, come on out to them!' "When he did so he addressed them as follows, 'O Jews, fear God! By God other than whom there is none, you do certainly know that he is the Messenger of God, and that he brings you the truth.' They replied, 'No, you are lying!' Thereupon the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent them away." Those are the words used in the above account. In one version the text states, "when he came out before them and gave testimony to the truth they said, 'You are the worst of us, and the son of the worst of us!' And they spoke ill of him. He then said, 'O Messenger of God, this is what I was fearing.'"
lessons: Embracing truth can cost you your social standing, reputation, and community ties — yet the courage to follow conviction is itself a form of spiritual victory.
The Prophet's wisdom in gathering testimony before revealing Ibn Salam's conversion shows that strategic wisdom and foresight are part of good leadership and sincere care for others.
When people who once praised you suddenly slander you upon learning of your changed convictions, it reveals that their praise was never about your character but about your conformity.
practical_advice: If you are considering a significant change in your beliefs or values, think wisely about timing and context, as Ibn Salam did — not out of cowardice, but out of prudence.
Prepare yourself emotionally for the reality that those who praised you most may become your harshest critics if you change in ways that challenge their worldview; this is a test of your sincerity.

When you face rejection and slander after making a courageous decision, remember that the loss of approval from others does not diminish the truth of what you have chosen — hold firm and seek reassurance in God.

title: Abdullah ibn Salam Tests the Prophet with Questions of Prophethood
summary: Abdullah ibn Salam posed three questions that only a prophet could answer — about the signs of the Day of Judgement, the finest food of paradise, and the resemblance of a child to its parents — and the Prophet answered all three through knowledge given by Gabriel, leading Ibn Salam to declare his faith.
story: Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The hafiz Abu 'Abd Allah told us, quoting al-Asamm, quoting Muhammad b. Ishaq al-Sanaani, quoting 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr, quoting Humayd, from Anas, who said, "'Abd Allah b. Salam heard of the arrival of the Prophet (SAAS) while he was out in some land he owned. He went to the Prophet (SAAS) and said, 'I shall ask you three things for which only a prophet would know the answers. They are: What are the signs of the Day of Judgement? What is the finest food that the people of paradise would eat? And what causes a child to resemble his father or his mother?' "'He replied, "Gabriel told me of these previously." "What, Gabriel?" he asked. "Yes," he replied. "But," commented 'Abd Allah b. Salam, "he is the angel who is the enemy of the Jews." The Messenger of God (SAAS) then recited, "Whoever is the enemy of Gabriel, who surely revealed it to your heart by God's permission" (Surat al-Baqara; II, v.97). "'He then said, "Regarding the signs of the Day of Judgement, the first will be a fire that comes at people from the East and leads them to the West. As for the prime food of paradise eaten by those that are there, that is a plentitude of whale's liver. And if the male's liquid precedes that of the female, he will resemble the child, while if the female's liquid precedes that of the male, she will resemble the child." ""'Abd Allah b. Salam exclaimed, "I testify that there is no god but God and that you are the Messenger of God; O Messenger of God, the Jews are a people of liars. If they learn of my accepting Islam before you ask them about me, they will lie to you." "'The Jews arrived and the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them, "What kind of man is the (one of you named) 'Abd Allah?" They replied, "He is the best of us and the son of the best of us; he is our leader and the son of our leader." "What," he then asked, "would you say if he were to accept Islam?" They replied, "May God spare him from that!" ""'Abd Allah then came forth and said, "I give testimony that there is no god but God, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God." They said, "You are the worst of us, and the son of the worst of us!" And they spoke ill of him. "'He then commented, "This, O Messenger of God, is what I feared."""
lessons: Sincere intellectual inquiry, approached with an open heart, can lead to the most profound spiritual transformation — Ibn Salam's three questions were not tricks but a genuine search for confirmation.
The Quran addresses doubts and objections in real time; the Prophet's recitation of the verse about Gabriel showed that scripture itself speaks to the heart of a sincere seeker's concern.

The transformation from being called 'the best of us' to 'the worst of us' by the same people in the same moment reveals how quickly tribal and social loyalty overrides honesty when truth becomes inconvenient.
practical_advice: When you have doubts or questions about your faith or convictions, bring them forward honestly and seek answers with sincerity — genuine questions deserve genuine engagement, not suppression.
Be aware that your worth in the eyes of God does not shift based on the approval or disapproval of those around you; Ibn Salam went from leader to outcast in moments, yet his truth did not change.

If you find yourself suddenly rejected or spoken ill of after a courageous decision, recognize this as confirmation of your sincerity rather than evidence of your failure — those who change their praise to slander reveal their own dishonesty, not yours.

title: Abdullah ibn Salam's Conversion to Islam
summary: Abdullah ibn Salam, a learned Jewish rabbi, secretly recognized the Prophet Muhammad as the awaited messenger and strategically arranged to have his standing confirmed among the Jews before publicly declaring his Islam, knowing they would reject him once they learned of his conversion.
story:
"'Abd Allah then came forth and said, 'I give testimony that there is no god but God, and that Muhammad is the Messenger of God.' They said, 'You are the worst of us, and the son of the worst of us!' And they spoke ill of him.
'He then commented, 'This, O Messenger of God, is what I feared.'"

Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to me, from Yahya b. 'Abd Allah, from a man of the family of 'Abd Allah b. Salam, who said, "Included in the statement reported as having been made by 'Abd Allah b. Salam, a learned rabbi, at his conversion to Islam, is the following, 'At Quba' I heard of the Messenger of God (SAAS), and learned of his name, qualities and appearance, and that his coming was at a time when we were expecting him. I none the less remained secretive about this and silent until he came to Medina.
'When he arrived in Quba', he stayed among the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf. A man came and told me of his arrival; at that time I was at work at the top of a datepalm and my aunt Khalida, daughter of al-Harith, was sitting below me.
'When I heard the news of his arrival, I loudly praised God, and my aunt exclaimed, upon hearing that, "Why, you'd not have been more pleased at hearing of the coming of Moses, son of Aaron!"
'I told her, "Yes indeed, Aunt. He is, I swear, the very brother of Moses, son of Aaron, and recognizing the same faith. He has been given the same mission assigned to Moses."
'She responded, "O Nephew, is he the one whom we were told would be sent with news of Judgement Day?" "Yes," I told her, and she said, "Then so it is he."
'Abd Allah continued, 'So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and embraced Islam, returned to my household and ordered them to do the same. I kept my acceptance of Islam from my people, and told the Messenger of God (SAAS), "The Jews are a people of liars; I would like you to let me inside a house of yours, hide me from them, then ask them about me what status I have among them before you tell them of my conversion to Islam. If they knew that they would call me unreliable and would speak ill of me."
The account then continues approximately as above. 'Abd Allah continued, 'I then openly declared the acceptance of Islam by myself and by my household; my aunt Khalida, daughter of al-Harith, also accepted the faith.'"
lessons: Standing firm in truth often comes at a great social cost, as Abdullah ibn Salam lost the esteem of his entire community the moment he declared his faith, yet he proceeded anyway.
Recognizing truth requires intellectual honesty and the courage to follow evidence even when it contradicts communal loyalty or self-interest.
Wisdom in how one shares difficult truths — as shown by Abdullah's careful strategy to have his credibility confirmed before his conversion was announced — does not contradict sincerity of faith.
practical_advice: If you fear rejection from your community for a truthful or righteous stance, prepare thoughtfully before making your position known, just as Abdullah secured his credibility first.
When others speak ill of you for doing what is right, remind yourself that their rejection is a reflection of their own biases, not a measure of your worth before God.
Share your spiritual journey with family, as Abdullah did when he returned and guided his household, because your growth can be a light for those closest to you.

title: Safiyya's Account of Her Father and Uncle's Reaction to the Prophet's Arrival
summary: Safiyya bint Huyayy recalls how her father Huyayy ibn Akhtab and uncle Abu Yasir spent an entire day with the Prophet upon his arrival at Quba, returned exhausted and shattered, and despite recognizing him as the awaited prophet, her father vowed eternal enmity against him.
story: Yunus b. Bukayr quoted Muhammad b. Ishaq as follows, "'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to me, quoting someone, from Safiyya, daughter of Huyayy, who said, 'None of the children of my father or my uncle were more popular with them both than I was. Whatever child of theirs wanted their attention it was always me they put first. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Quba', the village of the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, my father and my uncle, Abu Yasir b. Akhtab, went to him early in the morning and only returned at sunset, so tired out that they could scarcely walk, staggering from exhaustion. I raced over to greet them as was my custom, but, I swear, neither so much as looked at me. I heard my uncle Abu Yasir ask my father, "Is that he then?" "Yes, I swear by God," he replied. "You recognize him from his character and description then?" "I certainly do, I swear it!" he responded. "So how will you relate to him?" he asked. "With enmity, I swear by God, for as long as I live!"'"
lessons: Knowing the truth and choosing to reject it out of pride or communal allegiance is one of the gravest spiritual failures, as demonstrated by Huyayy ibn Akhtab.
A child can sense when something is profoundly wrong in those they love, even when no words are spoken, which teaches us the weight our choices carry for those around us.
The contrast between Abu Yasir's initial openness and Huyayy's chosen enmity shows that even in the same family, hearts can take vastly different paths when confronted with the same truth.
practical_advice: If you find yourself resisting a truth you already know to be valid, ask yourself honestly whether pride, tribalism, or fear of change is what is really holding you back.

When loved ones seem emotionally distant or cold, as Safiyya's father was on his return, give them space while remaining present — sometimes people are processing something too heavy to share immediately.
Examine whether any ongoing conflicts or enmities in your life are driven by principle or by ego, as Huyayy's vow of enmity was driven by the latter with devastating consequences.

title: Abu Yasir's Openness Versus Huyayy ibn Akhtab's Chosen Enmity
summary: After both Abu Yasir and his brother Huyayy ibn Akhtab met with the Prophet and recognized him, Abu Yasir urged his people to follow the Prophet while Huyayy vowed perpetual opposition, and despite his brother's pleading, Huyayy refused to obey and led his people into enmity until his execution.
story:
Musa b. 'Uqba recounted, from al-Zuhri, that Abu Yasir b. Akhtab, following the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina, went to him, heard him speak, spoke with him and then returned to his people. He told them, "People, obey me; God has sent you the one you have been awaiting. Follow him and do not oppose him."
"His brother Huyayy b. Akhtab, at that time the leader of the Jews, they both being of the Banu al-Nadir, then went and sat and listened to the Messenger of God (SAAS), and returned to his people who were obedient to him. He told them, 'I come to you from being with a man whom, I swear it, I will for ever oppose.' His brother, Abu Yasir, answered him, 'O cousin, obey me in this matter and disobey however you like thereafter without penalty.'
"Huyayy insisted, 'No, by God, I will never obey you!' And so Satan took possession of him and his people followed him in his opinion."
As for Abu Yasir, whose name was Huyayy b. Akhtab, his fate is unknown to me. Regarding Huyayy b. Akhtab, the father of Safiyya, he expressed enmity for the Prophet (SAAS) and his Companions, and continued to do so, God damn him, until he was executed, bound, before the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the day the warriors of Banu Qurayza were killed.
lessons: Leadership carries a heavy moral responsibility — when Huyayy as the leader chose enmity, his entire community followed him into the same destruction.
Those who sincerely advise others toward good, as Abu Yasir did, have fulfilled their obligation even if the advice is rejected; the outcome rests with the one who refuses.
Stubbornness in the face of clear guidance, when dressed in the language of honor or loyalty, is one of the most dangerous spiritual traps a person can fall into.
practical_advice: When someone in authority over you is leading in a clearly wrong direction, look for other voices of reason within your own circle, as Abu Yasir's example shows that such voices exist even in resistant communities.
If you have repeatedly advised someone toward what is right and they keep refusing, recognize that your responsibility has been fulfilled and that you cannot force another person's heart to open.
Reflect regularly on whether the communities and leaders you follow are pulling you toward truth or away from it, because collective choices have collective consequences.

title: The First Friday Prayer in Medina
summary: As the Prophet departed Quba on his camel al-Qaswa on a Friday, he stopped at noon in the valley of Ranuna among the Banu Salim and led the first Friday congregational prayer with a sermon, marking a historic milestone in the establishment of Islamic worship in Medina.
story: When, on a Friday, the Messenger of God (SAAS) left Quba' mounted on his camel al-Qaswa', it was noon by the time he was in the quarter of the Banu Salim b. 'Awf. He performed the Friday prayers there with the Muslims in a valley called Ranuna'. This was the first Friday prayer which the Messenger of God (SAAS) performed with the Muslims in Medina, or perhaps the very first time of all. This is because - though God knows best - it had not been possible for him and his Companions in Mecca to have any Friday prayers with a sermon and joint open prayer. The strong opposition to him from the polytheists and the harm they were doing to him had precluded any such possibility.
lessons: The establishment of communal worship was itself an act of liberation — in Mecca it had been suppressed by persecution, making the first Friday prayer in Medina a symbol of what freedom of faith makes possible.
Every milestone of goodness, no matter how long delayed by oppression, eventually arrives for those who remain patient and steadfast.
The Prophet's first act upon entering the new community was to establish collective worship, reminding us that community and remembrance of God are foundational to a healthy society.
practical_advice: If circumstances have long prevented you from practicing or expressing your faith openly, use any new opportunity for freedom and safety to re-establish those practices with gratitude.
Mark meaningful spiritual milestones in your life with reflection and communal celebration, as the first Friday prayer was a watershed moment worthy of remembrance.
When you finally emerge from a period of suppression, hardship, or restriction, resist the urge to rush past it — pause, gather with your community, and acknowledge what God has made possible.

title: The Prophet's First Friday Sermon in Medina
summary: The Prophet delivered his first Friday sermon in Medina among the Banu Salim, calling the community to fear God, cultivate their relationship with Him sincerely, and prepare for the afterlife, establishing the spiritual and moral foundations of the new Muslim community.
story:
Ibn Jarir stated, "Yunus b. 'Abd al-A'la related to me, quoting Ibn Wahb, that Sa'id b. 'Abd al-Rahman al-Jumahi reported that he was informed that the following was the content of the sermon of the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the first Friday he prayed in Medina, among the Banu Salim b. 'Amr b. 'Awf, God bless them: 'Praise be to God; I give Him praise, and I ask His help. I ask for His forgiveness and for His guidance. I believe in Him, will not disbelieve in Him, and will do battle with those who do. I bear witness that there is no god but God, that He has no associate, that Muhammad is His Servant and Messenger whom He has sent with guidance and the religion of truth and light, at a time without messengers, when there is little knowledge, the people are misguided, when the time is out of joint, and the day of retribution and fate is at hand.
'Whoever obeys God and His Messenger is right-guided; whoever disobeys them is misled, and has erred and gone far astray.
'I charge you to fear God; this is the best advice a Muslim can give a Muslim, to urge him on to the afterlife, to order him to fear God. Beware of what God has, of Himself, cautioned you. There is no better advice nor charge than this. It is an act of piety for those who accomplish this with apprehension and fear and a true means of assistance towards your aspirations from the afterlife.
'Whoever cultivates, both openly and in secret, his relationship with God, seeking only God's favour thereby, shall receive recognition in the short term and reward after death, when a man will wish he had done differently than he had before, and would want to put great distance between himself and that behaviour. God bids you beware of Him, though He is merciful towards His worshippers.
'Whoever believes His words and fulfils His promise will find nothing taken back therefrom, for God Almighty states, "Whatever I say shall not be changed, (Qaf, L, v.29). and I am not unjust towards my worshippers."
'Fear God both in matters that are at hand and those that follow, in secret and openly, for "whoever fears God, God will remove his evil ways from him and give him great reward" (surat al-Talaq; LXV, v.5). And "Whoever fears God and His Messenger will have won a mighty success." Fearing God will forestall His disapproval, His punishment, His anger. Fearing God will bring satisfaction, please the Lord and elevate one's status.
'Take advantage of your good fortune and do not be lax in matters pertaining to God. God has instructed you in His book, laid out for you His path, so that He may know those who speak the truth and those who lie. Do good, just as God does good to you. Oppose His enemies and strive energetically for God. It is He who chose you and named you "Muslims", so that those who perish do so for evident cause, while those who live do so for evident cause. There is no power but with God. Make frequent mention of God. Strive for what comes after death. For he who makes good his relationship with God will find his relationship with people satisfactory. That is because God passes judgement upon people, while they do not pass judgement upon Him. It is He who controls people, while they do not control Him. God is truly great! There is no power but with God, the Almighty and Glorious.'"
lessons: The Prophet's first public address prioritized God-consciousness and preparation for the afterlife, teaching us that any community's health begins with its individual members' relationship with God.
Cultivating one's relationship with God both privately and publicly, without seeking human approval, is described in this sermon as the surest path to recognition in this life and reward in the next.
The reminder that God is merciful to His worshippers, even as He cautions them, balances fear with hope and prevents despair from taking root in the believer's heart.
practical_advice: Begin each new chapter of your life — a new job, a new city, a new community — by grounding yourself in your relationship with God before anything else, as the Prophet did upon arriving in Medina.

When feeling overwhelmed by worldly concerns, return to the sermon's core message: make frequent mention of God, do good, and trust that improving your relationship with God will improve all your other relationships.

Take the sermon's advice to 'take advantage of your good fortune and do not be lax' as a practical reminder to use periods of stability and ease to invest in your spiritual and moral growth before difficulty arrives.

title: The Prophet's First Address in Medina — Warning About the Day of Judgement
summary: In his first address in Medina as recorded by al-Bayhaqi, the Prophet warned his community to send forward good deeds for themselves before death, vividly describing the moment a person stands alone before God with nothing having been prepared, and urged them to protect themselves from hellfire even with the smallest of good deeds.
story: Al-Bayhaqi's text reads, 'Chapter on the first address given by the Messenger of God (SAAS) after his arrival in Medina. Abu 'Abd Allah informed us, quoting Abu al-'Abbas al-Asamm quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar, quoting Yunus b. Bukayr, from Ibn Ishaq, who stated, "Al-Mughira b. 'Uthman b. Muhammad b. 'Uthman related to me, along with al-Akhnas b. Shariq, from Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. 'Awf, who said, 'The first address given by the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina began with him standing before them and expressing appropriate praise and adoration for God, and then saying, "O people, send forward for yourselves! You well know that any one of you may be struck down and so his flock be left with no shepherd. His Lord will then say to him - and there will be no interpreter or attendant to come between yourself and Him - 'Did not My Messenger come to you and inform you? Did I not give you wealth and benefit? What is it you have sent forward for yourself?' And He will look to right and left but see nothing. Then the man will look in front of him and see only hell-fire. So, whoever is able to protect his face from the fire, even if only with a slice of dates, let him do so. If he finds nothing, then let him say one good word for thereby one good deed will be rewarded by 10 times its like, or even by 700 times. May peace..."'"'
lessons: The image of standing alone before God with no intermediary is one of the most powerful motivators for consistent good deeds — no status, wealth, or community affiliation will accompany a person at that moment.

Even the smallest act of goodness — symbolized by a slice of dates — has immense value before God, which means no one is ever too poor, too weak, or too insignificant to make a meaningful contribution.

The Prophet's emphasis on preparing for death while alive is not meant to induce paralysis or morbid fear, but to inspire purposeful action and prevent complacency while one still has the opportunity.
