practical_advice: When you feel your good deeds are too small to matter, remember this sermon's reminder that a single good word carries a reward multiplied up to 700 times — begin with what you have.

Use the visualization of standing alone before God as a regular spiritual exercise to recalibrate your priorities, especially when worldly pressures make you feel too busy for acts of worship or kindness.

Do not postpone acts of charity, reconciliation, or service to others under the assumption you will have more time later — the sermon's urgent tone reflects how suddenly the opportunity to prepare can end.

title: The Prophet Stays with Abu Ayyub al-Ansari Upon Arriving in Madinah
summary: After arriving in Madinah, the Prophet Muhammad stayed in the home of Abu Ayyub al-Ansari while his mosque and quarters were being built, with the chiefs of Banu al-Najjar escorting him.
story: Al-Bukhwi stated that Ishaq b. Mansiir related to him, from 'Abd al-Samad, who quotes a hadith from his father, as follows, 'Aha al-Tayyah Yazid b. Yumayd al-Dabbi related to us, quoting Anas b. Malik, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina, he stayed in the upper part of the town in the quarter named for the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf. He stayed among them for 14 nights, then sent a message to the chiefs of the Bana al-Najjiir who came wearing their swords. It's as if I can still see the Messenger of God (SAAS) riding his camel with Abii Bakr mounted behind him and the chiefs of the Bana al-Najjar all around him until they reached the courtyard of Abn Ayyilb's house. He used to pray wherever he happened to be when time for prayer came; he would even pray in sheep pens."'
lessons: The Prophet's willingness to accept humble hospitality — staying in an ordinary home and praying in sheep pens — teaches that dignity is never diminished by simple circumstances.
True community is shown in the way the people of Madinah rallied around the Prophet with honor and protection, modeling what it means to welcome a stranger with wholehearted care.
Great works often begin with small, unglamorous steps; the Prophet's arrival was modest, yet it was the foundation of a civilization.
practical_advice: If you are going through a period of transition or displacement, take comfort in the Prophet's example: even the greatest of people relied on the kindness and shelter of others without shame.
Practice gratitude for the people in your life who offer you a place of safety and belonging — express that gratitude to them directly.
Do not wait for ideal conditions to worship or do good; the Prophet prayed in sheep pens. Use whatever space and moment you have available.

title: The Banu al-Najjar Refuse Payment for the Mosque Land, Seeking Only God's Reward
summary: When the Prophet asked the Banu al-Najjar tribe about the price of their land for the mosque, they refused any worldly payment, declaring they would seek their reward only from God.
story: He summoned the chiefs of the Bana al-Najjar, and when they came he asked them, "0 Bann al-Najjar, what price for this wall of yours?" They replied, "Oh no, by God, we'll seek its price from God, the Almighty and Glorious, alone!"
lessons: True generosity is rooted in sincerity — the Banu al-Najjar's refusal of payment is a model of giving purely for God's sake, expecting nothing from people.
Material wealth is not always the measure of a transaction's value; some exchanges carry a spiritual currency that far outweighs any price.
When we invest in communal acts of worship and goodness, we are making a transaction directly with God — the best of all possible exchanges.
practical_advice: Identify one place in your life where you can give time, effort, or resources without expecting recognition or compensation, purely as an act of worship.
Reflect on what it would feel like to approach your daily good deeds as investments with God rather than as obligations — let that perspective renew your motivation.
Practice letting go of the need for acknowledgment when you do good; the Banu al-Najjar asked for nothing from people yet earned something eternal.

title: The Companions and Prophet Chant Together While Building the Mosque
summary: As the Prophet and his Companions worked together to build the mosque, they chanted verses expressing their longing for the hereafter and calling on God to bless both the Emigrants and the Helpers.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed with Aba Ayyab until his mosque and apartments were built. The Messenger of God (SAAS) himself worked in it to inspire the Muslims with a desire to work there. Both Emigrants and Helpers co-operated busily in this. One Muslim worker spoke the verse, 'For us to sit while the Prophet worked would indeed be misdirected action.' As they worked the Muslims chanted, 'There's no life but that of the hereafter; 0 God, have mercy on both Helpers and Emigrants.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) himself spoke the words, 'There's no life but that of the hereafter; 0 God, have mercy on both Emigrants and Helpers.'"
lessons: Work becomes worship when accompanied by the remembrance of God; the Prophet transformed the physical labor of construction into a spiritual act through dhikr and supplication.
Unity between different groups — here the Emigrants and Helpers — is a divine gift that must be actively nurtured, and the Prophet modeled this by naming both groups in his supplication.
A leader who works beside his people, rather than above them, inspires effort not through command but through example.
practical_advice: Introduce a small spiritual practice — a prayer, a phrase of remembrance, or a moment of intention — at the beginning of your daily work to transform routine effort into purposeful worship.
When you are part of a team or community effort, find ways to honor every member's contribution, as the Prophet honored both the Helpers and the Emigrants equally.

On days when work feels meaningless or draining, reconnect with the idea that effort done for a noble purpose outlasts the physical task itself — the mosque built with those chants still stands in spirit today.

title: Ammar ibn Yasir Overloaded with Bricks and the Prophecy of His Martyrdom
summary: While building the mosque, Ammar ibn Yasir was overloaded with bricks by his fellow workers, and the Prophet wiped the dust from his hair and foretold that he would be killed not by them but by an evil faction.
story: 'Ammzr b. Yasir came in, overloaded with bricks, and said, "0 Messenger of God, they're killing me by loading me with what they wouldn't carry!" Umm Salama said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) wipe his hand through his long, thick hair — which was curly — and say, 'Alas, Ibn Sumayya, it's not them killing you but the evil gang who will!'"
lessons: The Prophet's tenderness toward Ammar — wiping the dust from his hair with his own hand — shows that great leaders notice the suffering of individuals even amid large communal efforts.
Ammar bore an unjust burden without abandoning his commitment to the work; endurance under unfair treatment, while continuing to serve a higher cause, is itself a form of nobility.
The Prophet's words, though a prophecy of hardship, were delivered with compassion — even hard truths can and should be shared with gentleness and care.
practical_advice: When you feel that others are not sharing the load fairly and you are carrying more than your share, speak up as Ammar did — honest expression of burden is not weakness.
If someone you care about is overwhelmed and struggling, take a moment to acknowledge their effort personally and tenderly, as the Prophet did with Ammar.
When you receive difficult news or a sobering truth, notice the manner in which it is delivered — and strive to deliver hard truths to others with the same compassion the Prophet showed.

title: The Prophet's Prophecy About 'Ammar ibn Yasir's Martyrdom
summary: While building the mosque in Madinah, 'Ammar ibn Yasir carried double loads of bricks despite illness. The Prophet Muhammad (SAAS) repeatedly prophesied that 'Ammar would be killed by an evil rebellious gang, a prophecy fulfilled at the Battle of Siffin.
story: 'Ammzr b. Yasir came in, overloaded with bricks, and said, "0 Messenger of God, they're killing me by loading me with what they wouldn't carry!" Umm Salama said, "I saw the Messenger of God (SAAS) wipe his hand through his long, thick hair - which was curly - and say, 'Alas, Ibn Sumayya, it's not them killing you but the evil gang who will!'" This tradition is devoid of a link from this line; indeed it is problematic between Muhammad b. Ishaq and Umm Salama. Muslim, in his Sahih collection, joined it back to a hadith of Shu'ba, from Khalid al-Hadhdha', from Sa'id and al-Hasan, that is from the two sons of Abu al-Hasan of Basra, from their mother Khayra, the freed-woman of Umm Salama, from Umm Salama, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'The evil gang will kill 'Ammar.'" He also related it from a hadith of Ibn 'Aliyya, from Ibn 'Awn, from al-Hasan, from his mother, from Umm Salama, which reports that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said to 'Ammar as he was transporting rocks, "Alas for you, O Ibn Sumayya! The evil gang will kill you!" 'Abd al-Razzaq stated, "Ma'mar related to us, from al-Hasan, from his mother, from Umm Salama, who said, 'While the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions were building the mosque, his men would carry the bricks one at a time, while 'Ammar would carry two, one for himself and one for the Prophet (SAAS). So the latter patted him on the back and said, "Ibn Sumayya, the others will get a reward, but you will receive two. And the last food for you will be a drink of milk and the evil gang will kill you!"'" This chain of authorities is in accordance with the criteria established in both Sahih collections. Al-Bayhaqi and others tell us, through a group, from Khalid al-Hadhdha', from 'Ikrima, from Abu Sa'id al-Khudari, who said, "While building the mosque we would be carrying bricks one by one, while 'Ammar would bear them two at a time. The Prophet (SAAS) saw him and, while wiping the dust off him said, 'Alas, poor 'Ammar! The evil gang will kill him. He will invite them to paradise, while they will invite him to the fire!' 'Ammar commented, 'I seek refuge with God from strife!'" This hadith comes from the Dala'il al-Nubuwwah (Signs of the Prophethood) wherein the Messenger of God (SAAS) tells 'Ammar that the evil gang will kill him. In fact he was killed by the Syrians at the battle of Siffin, 'Ammar having fought with 'Ali and the Iraqis. 'Ali had greater right to rule than did Mu'awiya. But their being called bughat, unjust, does not imply that the supporters of Mu'awiya have become unbelievers, as some ignorant persons do who belong to the Shi'a, along with others as well. For even if they were essentially rebellious, they had in fact erred in their interpretation of the circumstances surrounding the conflict. And not everyone who undertakes interpretation is right; those who are right receive double reward, while those in error receive only one. Regarding the words, "He invites them to paradise, while they invite him to the fire", they refer to 'Ammar and his friends inviting the Syrians to friendship and to unity, while the Syrians wanted to give preference to some over those whose right was greater, which would result in the people being divided, each one having its own leader. This would have led to dissention and division within the nation and resulted inevitably from their philosophy and policies, even though this was not what they intended.
lessons: Those who serve others with extra effort and sacrifice, like 'Ammar carrying double loads for the Prophet, are recognized and honored by God with greater reward.
The Prophet's compassion for his companions was profound — he noticed 'Ammar's exhaustion, wiped the dust from his head with his own hand, and comforted him with reassurance of his elevated status.
Standing on the side of truth and justice, even when it leads to hardship or death, is the mark of a true believer — 'Ammar's entire life exemplified this principle.
practical_advice: When you are carrying a heavier burden than those around you and feel unappreciated, remember that God sees every extra effort you make, and your reward is multiplied accordingly.
If you feel overwhelmed by workload or unfair treatment, turn to God in prayer as 'Ammar did, seeking refuge from strife and chaos rather than responding with anger or resentment.

When facing a situation where you must choose between what is right and what is easy or politically convenient, look to the example of 'Ammar who remained steadfast on the side of truth until his last breath.

title: The Placement of Stones and the Prophecy of the Three Caliphs
summary: While building the Prophet's mosque in Madinah, Abu Bakr, Umar, and Uthman each placed stones beside the Prophet's stone, upon which the Prophet declared they would be the Caliphs to succeed him.
story: The hafis al-Bayhaqi stated in Dala'il (The Signs) as follows, "The (Abd Allah) related to us by dictation, Abu Bakr b. Ishaq related to us, 'Ubayd b. Shurayk informed us, Nu'aym b. Hammad related to us, quoting 'Abd Allah b. al-Mubarak, Hashraj b. Nubata informed us, from Sa'id b. Jumhan, from Safina, freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS), who said, 'Abu Bakr brought a stone and placed it down, then Umar did the same, then Uthman did the same. The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "These will succeed to power after myself."'" He (al-Bayhaqi) then related this from a hadith of Yahya b. 'Abd al-Hamid al-Himmani, from Hasbraj, from Sa'id, from Safina, who said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) built a mosque he put down a stone, saying, 'Let Abu Bakr place a stone next to mine; let Umar place his next to that of Abu Bakr, and let Uthman place his next to that of Umar.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, 'These shall be the Caliphs after me.'" This hadith through this line of transmission is gharib, "unique". What is well known is what Imam Ahmad related, from Abu al-Nadr, from Hashraj b. Nabata al-'Absi, and from Bahz and Zayd b. al-Habbab, and 'Abd al-Samad, and Hammad b. Salama, both authorities quoting Sa'id b. Jamhan from Safina, who said, "I heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say, 'The Caliphate period will last for 30 years. Then thereafter will come the kingdom period.'" Safina then commented, "Count! The Caliphate of Abu Bakr lasted 2 years, that of Umar 10 years, that of Uthman 12 years, and that of 'Ali 6 years." This wording is that of Ahmad. Abu Dawud, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related it through various lines from Sa'id b. Jamhan. Al-Tirmidhi stated, "This hadith is hasan, 'good'. We know of it only in this form and with the alternate wording, 'The Caliphate after me will be for 30 years, and then will come a mordacious kingdom era.'"
lessons: The Prophet's act of designating companions to place their stones beside his was a gentle, symbolic way of preparing his community for leadership transition, showing that wise leaders think ahead about continuity.
Divine knowledge and prophecy are manifest in the smallest acts — even placing stones during construction carried meaning about the future of the entire Muslim community.
The fulfillment of the Prophet's prophecy about the thirty-year Caliphate is a sign of his truthfulness and a reminder that God's plan unfolds precisely as revealed.
practical_advice: When you are part of a team or community project, consider how your contribution — no matter how small it seems — may carry significance and meaning beyond what you can immediately see.
Trust in God's plan for leadership and transition in your own life; just as the community was guided through successive leaders, you too will be given what you need for each new season.
Reflect on fulfilled prophecies and historical evidence of divine guidance as a means of strengthening your faith during times of doubt or uncertainty.

title: The Moaning of the Palm Tree Trunk at the Prophet's Mosque
summary: When the Prophet moved from leaning against a palm tree trunk to deliver his sermons to using a newly built minbar, the palm trunk moaned loudly like a longing camel. The Prophet returned to it, embraced it until it quieted like a soothed baby.
story: I would comment that when first built the mosque of the Prophet (SAAS) had no minbar from which to address the congregation. He would speak while leaning against a palm tree trunk in the wall next to the qibla near where he prayed. Eventually he began to use a minbar, as we will explain in its proper place. As he moved over towards it to make his address from it and passed by that tree trunk, it moaned like a love-lorne camel because it had always heard his speeches delivered near itself. And so the Prophet (SAAS) returned to it and hugged it until it settled down, just like a baby, and became quiet. Details of this will be given hereafter through various lines, from Sahl b. Sa'd al-Sa'idi, Jabir, 'Abd Allah b. Umar, 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbas, Anas b. Malik and Umm Salama, God be pleased with them. What more appropriate than the comment made by al-Hasan al-Basri after relating this story, from Anas b. Malik, "O Muslims! A piece of wood so pining for the Messenger of God (SAAS)! Do not men hoping to meet him have even more right to yearn for him?"
lessons: Even inanimate creation longed for the presence of the Prophet, reminding us of the extraordinary spiritual reality of his being and the love that all of existence had for him.

The Prophet's response to the moaning trunk — returning to it and embracing it with tenderness — illustrates that he never neglected what depended on him, no matter how small or seemingly insignificant.

Al-Hasan al-Basri's reflection invites every believer to examine their own longing for the Prophet: if wood could grieve, how much more should the human heart that hopes for his intercession yearn to follow his example.
practical_advice: When you feel a deep sense of longing — for a lost loved one, a better time, or spiritual connection — know that longing itself is not a weakness but a sign of a heart that loves deeply, and channel it toward drawing closer to God.

If you have ever felt overlooked or moved aside for something newer or more impressive, take comfort in knowing that the Prophet (SAAS) himself turned back and comforted what was left behind — God does not forget those who served faithfully.

Use the story of the moaning trunk as a meditation: sit quietly and ask yourself how much of your daily life reflects a genuine longing for nearness to God and love for the Prophet, then take one small step today to nurture that connection.

title: The Mosque 'Founded in Piety': Resolving the Dispute Between Two Companions
summary: Two companions disagreed over whether the Qur'anic reference to the 'mosque founded in piety' meant the Prophet's mosque in Madinah or the mosque at Quba. They brought their dispute to the Prophet, who confirmed it referred to his own mosque while affirming the great good in Quba.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, "Yahya b. Anis b. Abi Yahya related to us, quoting his father, as follows, 'I heard Abu Sa'id al-Khudari say, "Two men, one from the Banu Khudra, the other from the Banu 'Amr b. 'Awf, disagreed over which mosque was referred to by the words in the Qur'an, 'the mosque founded in piety' (surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.108). The Khudari said, 'It is the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' The 'Amri insisted, 'No, it's the mosque at Quba.' So they went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him about it. He replied, 'It is this mosque,' referring to that of the Messenger of God (SAAS). 'In that one,' he went on, meaning the mosque at Quba, 'there is much good.'""'" Al-Tirmidhi related this from Qutayba, from Hatim b. Isma'il, from Anis b. Abi Yahya al-Aslami. He characterized it as hasan, sahih, "good", "authentic". Imam Ahmad, al-Tirmidhi and al-Nasa'i related, from Ishaq b. 'Isa, from al-Layth b. Sa'd, from Qutayba, from al-Layth from 'Imran b. Abi Anas, from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Sa'id, from his father, who said: "Two men argued over which was the mosque 'founded in piety'. Thereafter this account proceeded as above. In the Sahih collection of Muslim, there is the hadith of Humayd al-Kharraj, from Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Rahman, who is quoted as saying that he asked 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Sa'id, "What was it you heard your father say about the 'mosque founded in piety'?" He replied, "My father said, 'I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him about the "mosque founded in piety". He picked up a handful of pebbles and dashed them to the ground, saying, "It is this, here, your mosque."'" Imam Ahmad stated, "Waki' related to us, as did Rabi'a b. Uthman al-Tamimi, from 'Imran b. Abi Anas, from Sahl b. Sa'd, who said, 'Two men alive at the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS) differed over which mosque was "founded in piety". One of them said, "It is the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." The other said, "No, it's the mosque at Quba." So they went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him. He replied, "It is this mosque of mine."'"
lessons: When companions disagreed, they did not let the dispute fester or divide them — they brought it directly to the Prophet for resolution, modeling how disputes should be taken to a trusted, knowledgeable authority.
The Prophet's humble and direct answer — picking up a handful of pebbles and gesturing to the ground beneath them — shows how great truths are often communicated with simplicity and without fanfare.

Acknowledging that both places carried goodness and virtue, the Prophet honored both mosques without diminishing either, teaching us to find the good in multiple things rather than insisting on exclusive rivalry.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a disagreement about religious or spiritual matters, resist the urge to argue indefinitely — instead seek out a scholar, teacher, or trusted source of Islamic knowledge to help clarify the matter.

Do not let disagreements over details of religion damage your brotherhood or sisterhood with fellow believers; the companions disagreed yet walked together to seek truth, and remained united in their respect for the Prophet's answer.

Take a moment to appreciate the sacred spaces in your own life — your local masjid, your home prayer space, the places where you have felt closest to God — and renew your intention to fill them with sincerity and devotion.

title: Debate Over the Mosque 'Founded in Piety'
summary: Two companions disagreed about which mosque was 'founded in piety' as mentioned in the Quran, so they asked the Prophet, who clarified it was his mosque in Medina. Scholars later discussed whether this contradicted traditions about the mosque at Quba.
story: I m w A b a d stated, "Waki' related to us, as did Rabi'a b. Wthmm d-Tamimi, from % r a n b. Abii Anas, from Sahl b. Sa'd, who said, 'Two men alive at the time of the Messenger of God (SAAS) differed over which mosque was "founded in piety". One of them said, "It is the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." The other said, "No, it's the mosque at Qubnl." So they went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and asked him. He replied, "It is this mosque of mine." "' I m w Ahmad stated, "Abii Nu'aym related to us, quoting 'Abd Allah b. 'Amir al-Aslami, from 'Imr?in b. Abii Anas, from Sahl b. Sa'd, from Ubayy b. Ka'b, that the Prophet (SAAS) said, 'The mosque "founded in piety" was this mosque of mine.'" These numerous sources come close to giving conclusive evidence that the mosque in question is that of the Messenger of God (SAAS). Wmar, his son 'Abd Allah, Zayd b. Thbit, SaFd b. al-Musayyab and Ibn Jarir all agreed with this. Others say that there is simply no contradiction between the revelation of this verse being applied to the mosque at Quba' and these traditions. This is so, they say, because this mosque (i.e. the Medina mosque) is simply more worthy of such than is the other since it is one of the three mosques referred to as those worth striving to attend. This is established in both ~ a h i hcollections from a hadith of Aha Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Only three mosques should be the object of navel: this mosque of mine, the harZm mosque (in Mecca) and the Bayt al-Maqdrs mosque (in Jerusalem).'" In the sahib collection of Muslim there is a tradition from Abii Sa'id, from the Prophet (SAAS), who said, "Only three mosques should be the object of travel" and he named these. It is established in both ~ a h i hcollections that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "A prayer in this mosque of mine is better than a thousand prayers in any other, with the exception of the haram mosque." In the hadith collection of A h a d , with a good cham of authorities, there is mention of an additional, bounty namely the words, L'And that one is better." In both collections, there is a hadirh of Y&y% al-Qattm, from Habib, from H a f ~b. 'him, from Abii Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, LBetweenmy house and my pulpit there is one of the gardens of paradise, and my pulpit stands by my pool.'" There are numerous traditions referring to the virtues of this holy mosque.
lessons: When genuine disagreement arises among believers, the proper response is to seek authoritative guidance rather than insist on one's own view.
Sacred spaces carry profound spiritual value, and understanding their significance deepens one's connection to worship.
Scholarly differences of opinion can coexist respectfully; not every theological debate needs a winner, as both positions may reflect different dimensions of truth.
practical_advice: When you feel confused or conflicted about a religious matter, seek knowledge from reliable sources rather than remaining in uncertainty.
Visit or pray in spaces of spiritual significance when possible, allowing their history and sanctity to inspire deeper reflection and gratitude.
When you disagree with someone over a matter of principle, model the companions' approach: bring the dispute to a trustworthy mediator rather than letting it fester.

title: The Virtues of the Three Sacred Mosques
summary: The Prophet Muhammad taught that only three mosques are worthy of special travel for spiritual reward: the Masjid al-Haram in Mecca, the Prophet's Mosque in Medina, and Masjid al-Aqsa in Jerusalem, and that prayer in his mosque surpasses a thousand prayers elsewhere.
story: This is established in both ~ a h i hcollections from a hadith of Aha Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, 'Only three mosques should be the object of navel: this mosque of mine, the harZm mosque (in Mecca) and the Bayt al-Maqdrs mosque (in Jerusalem).'" In the sahib collection of Muslim there is a tradition from Abii Sa'id, from the Prophet (SAAS), who said, "Only three mosques should be the object of travel" and he named these. It is established in both ~ a h i hcollections that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, "A prayer in this mosque of mine is better than a thousand prayers in any other, with the exception of the haram mosque." In the hadith collection of A h a d , with a good cham of authorities, there is mention of an additional, bounty namely the words, L'And that one is better." In both collections, there is a hadirh of Y&y% al-Qattm, from Habib, from H a f ~b. 'him, from Abii Hurayra, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, LBetweenmy house and my pulpit there is one of the gardens of paradise, and my pulpit stands by my pool.'" Imam Malik and his associates support the view that the Medina mosque is superior to the haram mosque because the latter was built by Abraham, and the former by Muhammad (SAAS); it being obvious that Muhammad (SAAS) was superior to Abraham, God bless him. However, the majority opinion opposes this view and maintains that the haram mosque is superior. This is because it is in a land made sacred by God at the time when He created the heavens and the earth. And it was made sacred by Abraham al-Khalil, True Friend", peace be upon him, and Mulpmmad, "the Seal of the Prophets". The (mosque in Mecca) is thus endowed with qualitiff not present in any other.
lessons: Some places on earth carry a special divine sanctity, and recognizing this elevates one's appreciation for the gift of worship.
The legacy of the prophets is woven into the physical world; when we visit sacred places, we walk in the footsteps of those who surrendered entirely to God.
Even scholarly disagreement among the pious can be a source of learning rather than division, showing that love of truth unites sincere seekers.
practical_advice: When feeling spiritually low, consider how even a single prayer performed with full presence and sincerity in a blessed moment carries immeasurable reward — quality of worship matters deeply.
Cultivate a personal sense of gratitude for access to mosques and sacred spaces in your life, however near or far from the great holy sites you may be.

If you aspire to visit one of the three sacred mosques, set it as a life intention and take small, consistent steps — saving, planning, and spiritually preparing — so the goal remains alive in your heart.

title: The Prophet's Humble Apartments Built Around the Mosque
summary: After building his mosque in Medina, the Prophet constructed small, simple apartments for himself and his family around it, made of palm stalks, mud, and stone, with ceilings so low a youth could reach them by hand.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) built apartments around his mosque which would be dwellings for himself and his family. These were small dwellings with narrow courtyards. Al-Hasan b. Abii al-Hasan al-Basri stated, he having been a youth in the company of his mother Khayra, the freed-woman of Umm Salama, "I used to be able to reach up with my hand to the highest ceiling in the apartment of the Messenger of God (SAAS)." I would observe, however, that al-Hasan al-Basri was large in size and tall. God bless him. Al-Suhayli stated in al-Rawd (The Gardens), "The apartments of the Messenger of God (SAAS) were built of the stalks of palm leaves covered with mud and partly of stones stacked upon one another. The ceilings of all were made of palm fronds." This account was related from al-Hasan al-Bqri. His rooms were made of hair-cloth held together with juniper-tree wood. In al-Bukhai's work of history we learn that finger nails could knock on the door of the Messenger of God (SAAS); this shows that there were no cloth coverings over his doors. He stated, "And after the deaths of the wives of the Messenger of God (SAAS) all these apartments were added to the mosque."
lessons: The Prophet's contentment with extreme simplicity in his personal life while dedicating his energy to building a community teaches that greatness is not measured by material comfort.
True leadership is demonstrated not through the luxury of one's home but through the richness of one's service to others.
Detachment from worldly possessions creates inner freedom and allows one to focus entirely on what truly matters.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the pressure to acquire more possessions or live a more impressive lifestyle, reflect on the Prophet's example of dignified simplicity and ask yourself what you truly need.
Practice gratitude by occasionally taking inventory of what you already have, recognizing that sufficiency — not abundance — is the foundation of contentment.
Consider simplifying one area of your living space or daily routine as a practical exercise in non-attachment, observing how it affects your sense of peace.

title: Zayd and Abu Rafi' Bring the Prophet's Family from Mecca to Medina
summary: After settling in Medina, the Prophet dispatched Zayd ibn Haritha and Abu Rafi' with provisions to bring his family and the family of Abu Bakr from Mecca, reuniting loved ones who had been separated during the migration.
story: Al-Waqidi, Ibn Jarir and others said, "When 'Ahd Allah h. Urayqic al-Dili returned to Mecca, the Messenger of God (SAAS) and Ahii Bakr dispatched Zayd b. Haritha and Abii R3ic, both freed-men of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to bring back their families from Mecca. They sent with them two loads of goods and 500 dirhams with which to purchase a camel of Qudayd. They left and soon returned with the two daughters of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Fahma and Umm Kulthiim, his two wives, Sawda and 'A'isha, and the latter's mother, Umm Riimw, along with other members of his and Abn Bakr's family, including the latter's son, 'Ahd Allah. On the road hack, the mounts of 'A'isha and her mother Umm Riiman strayed apart and Umm Riimzn began calling out, 'Bride! My daughter!' 'A'isha later said, 'I heard a voice say, "Let loose its bridle!' So I did this and it stopped, at God's will; we give praise to God the Almighty and Glorious.' They stayed at alSunh on the way. Eight months later, in Shawwa, as we will relate, the Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated his marriage with 'A'isha. "Asma', daughter of Ahii Bakr and wife of al-Zubayr h. a l - ' A w ~ m arrived also; she was pregnant and near term with 'Ahd Allah h. al-Zubayr, as we will relate in its proper place in material relating to the end of that year."
lessons: Even in the midst of building a new community, the Prophet prioritized family bonds, showing that personal relationships are not secondary to larger responsibilities.
When something goes wrong on a journey or in a plan — as when the mounts strayed — placing trust in God and following guidance can bring matters to a peaceful resolution.
Reunion after separation is a profound blessing; the sacrifice of the migration made the sweetness of reunion all the more meaningful.
practical_advice: If you are separated from loved ones due to circumstances beyond your control, take practical steps toward reunion while trusting that God can smooth the path in ways you cannot foresee.
When anxiety strikes during travel or transitions, recall that the companions faced far greater uncertainties and found steadiness through trust in God and in one another.
Use moments of family reunion or reconnection as opportunities to express gratitude openly — say what you feel, as those moments are gifts not guaranteed to come again.

title: The Companions Fall Ill with Medina Fever After Arrival
summary: After arriving in Medina, many of the Prophet's companions, including Abu Bakr, Bilal, and Amir ibn Fuhayra, fell gravely ill with fever. Aisha visited them and found them delirious, reciting sorrowful poetry expressing longing for Mecca and nearness to death.
story: Al-Bukhari stated, "'Abd Allah h. Wahh b. Yiisuf related to us, quoting Malik h. Hisham h. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'Abii Bakr and Biial fell sick around the time that the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina. I went in to see them and asked, "How are you, father? Bild, how are you?"' She said: "Whenever Aha Bakr developed a fever he would recite, 'Every man might arise among his family, yet his death be closer than his sandal thongs.' "When the fever was raised from Bilal he would raise his voice and say, 'O how happy I would be to spend a night in a valley covered with fresh grass and verdure, Will I ever again descend to Majinna's waters, or see Shma and Tufayl?'" 'A'isha went on, "So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him." In al-Bukhari's account of it from Abn UsZma, from Hishim b. Wrwa, from his father, from CA'isha, there is an addition after the verses of BilZl. In this he says, "O God, curse Wtba b. Rabica, Shayba b. Rabi'a and Umayya b. Khalaf alike for them having forced us out into the land of pestilence!" ZiyZd quoted Muhammad b. Ishaq as follows, "Hishim b. Wrwa and Wmar b. 'Abd Alhh b. Wrwa b. al-Zubayr related to me that 'A'isha stated, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina, it was the most pestilential place on God's earth. His Companions fell sick from fever and illness, but God kept that away from His Prophet. Abn Bakr, 'Amir h. Fuhayra and Bild, the two freed-men of Abn Bakr, were in one house, struck down by fever. I went in to them to visit them, that being before the the veil, was prescribed for us. Only God knows how very sick they were. I approached Abn Bakr and said, "How are you, father?" He replied, in verse: "Every man might arise among his family, yet his death be closer than his sandal thongs!" "'I exclaimed, "I swear, my father doesn't know what he is saying!" Then I went over to 'Amir b. Fuhayra and asked, "How are you, 'Amir?" H e replied, in verse, "I have found death before tasting it; a coward has his demise right above hi. Every man fights hard with all his power, as a bull protects its skin with its horns." "'I exclaimed, "By God, he doesn't know what he is saying!" Bila was in the habit of resting in the courtyard of the house whenever the fever struck him, then he would raise his voice and say, "O how happy I would be to spend a night in Fakh, while all around me was fresh grass and verdure. Will I ever again descend to Majima's waters, or see S h w and Tufayl?"' "'A'isha went on, 'I told the Messenger of God (SAAS) what I had heard from them, saying, "They are raving, irrational from the intensity of the fever."'"
lessons: Physical suffering can bring deep emotional vulnerability to the surface, including grief, longing, and fear of death — these are natural human responses and not signs of weak faith.
The longing that Bilal and Abu Bakr expressed for their homeland in the midst of illness reflects how sacrifice for a higher cause does not eliminate the ache of loss.
Aisha's compassionate act of visiting the sick, even before the veil was prescribed, models how caring for others in their suffering is a profound spiritual duty.
practical_advice: When illness or hardship makes you feel homesick, lonely, or fearful, allow yourself to acknowledge those feelings honestly rather than suppressing them — the companions did the same.
Check on those around you who may be suffering in silence; a visit, a kind word, or simply your presence can be more healing than any remedy.

If you find yourself expressing sadness or longing in poetry, song, or other creative forms during difficulty, know that this is a deeply human and honourable response — the companions themselves did it.

title: The Prophet's Prayer to Make Medina Beloved and Lift Its Fever
summary: Distressed by the illness afflicting his companions and their longing for Mecca, the Prophet turned to God in supplication, asking Him to make Medina beloved, bless it with health, and redirect its fever to al-Juhfa.
story: "So I went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him and he said, 'O God, make Medina beloved by us as Mecca is, and more so; make it healthy and bless us in its foods. Turn aside its fever and send it off to al-J~hfa!'" Muslim related this from Abii Bakr b. Ahii Shayba from Hisham in abbreviated form. In al-Bukhari's account of it from Abn UsZma, from Hishim b. Wrwa, from his father, from CA'isha, there is an addition after the verses of BilZl. In this he says, "O God, curse Wtba b. Rabica, Shayba b. Rabi'a and Umayya b. Khalaf alike for them having forced us out into the land of pestilence!" "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'O God, make Medina beloved by us, as Mecca is, and more so; make it healthy and bless us in its prosperity. Turn aside its fever and send it off to al-Juhfa!' "She said, 'When we arrived in Medina, we found it the most pestilential place on God's earth. It was just two basinshaped valleys wet with najl, that is brackish water.'" 'A'isha went on, 'I told the Messenger of God (SAAS) what I had heard from them, saying, "They are raving, irrational from the intensity of the fever." He replied, "O God, make Medina beloved by us as Mecca is, and more so; make it healthy and bless us in its prosperity. Turn aside its fever to Mahya'a!" Mahya'a is a reference to al-Juhfa.' Im%mA h a d stated, "Yiinus related to us, quoting Layth, from Yazid b. Abti Habib, from Abii Bakr b. Ishzq b. Yasar, from 'Abd All& b.Wrwa, from 'Unva, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina, Abii Bakr and 'Amir b. Fuhayra, Abii Bakr's freed-man, and Bilal, fell sick.' 'A'isha asked permission from the Messenger of God (SAAS) to visit them, and he agreed. She then asked Abii Bakr, 'How are you?' "He replied, 'Any man might arise among his family, yet his death be closer than his sandal thongs!' "She then asked 'Amir and he replied, 'I have found death before tasting it; a coward has his demise right above him.' "Then she asked Bilal and he said, 'O how happy I would be to spend a night in Fakh, while all around me was fresh grass and verdure.' "So she went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him. He then looked up to heaven and said" [the supplication].
lessons: When those we love are suffering and we feel helpless, turning to God in sincere supplication is not a last resort but a first and powerful act of care.

The Prophet's dua shows that it is not only permissible but beautiful to ask God to transform a difficult environment into one of blessing and love — our circumstances can be changed by sincere prayer.
God's Prophet was spared the fever not because suffering was beneath him, but so he could remain strong enough to care for his community — sometimes God protects us so we can serve others.
practical_advice: When you feel hopeless in the face of circumstances you cannot control — illness, a new and difficult environment, loss — make dua with specificity and sincerity, naming exactly what you need from God.
If you are struggling to feel at home in a new place or situation, ask God to place love for it in your heart, as the Prophet asked for Medina; sometimes belonging is a gift that must be requested.
When you witness others in distress, do not wait until you have a practical solution — bring their pain to God in prayer immediately, as the Prophet did when Aisha reported the companions' suffering.

title: The Companions' Illness in Medina and the Prophet's Prayer for Healing
summary: When the Prophet and his Companions arrived in Medina, they were struck by a severe pestilential fever. The Prophet prayed to God to make Medina beloved and healthy, and to transfer its fever elsewhere.
story: visit them, and he agreed. She then asked Abii Bakr, 'How are you?' "He replied, 'Any man might arise among his family, yet his death be closer than his sandal thongs!' "She then asked 'Amir and he replied, 'I have found death before tasting it; a coward has his demise right above him.' "Then she asked Bilal and he said, '0how happy I would be to spend a night in Fakh, while all around me was fresh grass and verdure.' "So she went to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and told him. He then looked up to heaven and said, '0 God, make Medina beloved by us as Mecca is, and more so; make it healthy and bless us in its foods. Tum aside its fever to Mahya'a.' This is a reference to al-Juhfa, so they claim." Al-Nasa'i related this similarly from Qutayba, from al-Layth. The Imim Ahmad related it through 'Abd al-Ra!nnZn b. al-HBritis from her as above. Al-Bayhaqi stated, "The ha&. Abii (Abd Allzh related to us, quoting Abii Sa'id b. Abii 'Amr, both saying, 'Abii al-'Abbas al-Asamm related to us, quoting Ahmad b. 'Abd al-Jabbar and Yiinus b. Bukayr, from Hishim b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina and it was the most pestilential place on God's earth. It was just two basinshaped valleys wet with najl, brackish water.""'" Hishim stated, "Its pestilential nature was well known before Islam. When the valley was disease-ridden and a man were to arrive there, he would be told to neigh like a donkey; if he did this the valley's pestilence would not harm him. A poet once said, coming to Medina, 'By my life, if I, fearing death, were to emit a donkey's neighing, I would be expressing my anxiety.' Al-Bukhitri related from a hadith of Moss b.cUqba, from Sdim, from his father, that the Prophet (SAAS) said, 'I saw what seemed like a black woman, her head in disarray, emerge from Medina and travel to Mahya'a, which is al-Juhfa. I interpreted from this that Medina's pestilence had been transformed to Mahya'a, that is, to al-Juhfa.' This wording is from al-Bukhiiri. Muslim did not give this tradition. AlTirmidhl did relate it and pronounced it authentic, while al-Nass'i and Ihn Mzja gave it from a hadith of Miisn b. Wqba. Hammzd b. Zayd related, from Hishim b. Wrwa, from 'A'isha, who said, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived at Medina the pestilence was active.' She related the tradition in full as above but gave the final words of the Messenger of God as 'remove its fever to al-Juhfa!' Hishim stated, 'A child born in al-Juhfa never reached adolescence without being struck down by the fever.' Al-Bayhaqi related this in the Dalsil al-Nubuwwah (Signs of the Prophethood). Yonus stated, from Ibn Ishnq, 'When the Messenger of God (SAAS) arrived in Medina, the pestilence was active. All his Companions became seriously stricken with that. But God kept it away from his Prophet (SAAS).'"
lessons: Hardship and illness can befall even the most righteous people; suffering does not indicate abandonment by God.
Turning to God in supplication during times of physical and communal distress is the prophetic response to affliction.
God's protection of the Prophet while his Companions suffered reminds us that divine wisdom operates in ways beyond our immediate understanding.
practical_advice: When facing illness or hardship in a new environment, make sincere du'a asking God to bring ease, health, and love for your circumstances.
Draw comfort from the accounts of the Companions who endured physical suffering with patience and longing for God's mercy rather than despair.

If you feel homesick or unwell after a major life transition, recognize this as a normal human experience even the closest Companions of the Prophet went through, and ask God to make your new situation beloved to you.

title: The Companions Pray Seated Due to Illness, and the Prophet Encourages Them to Stand
summary: The Companions in Medina were so weakened by fever that they prayed seated. When the Prophet informed them that seated prayer carries half the reward of standing prayer, they exerted themselves to stand despite their weakness.
story: Ziy?id stated, quoting Ibn Isbq, "Ibn Shihzb al-Zuhri related from 'Abd All& b. 'Amr b. al-'&, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions arrived in Medina they became seriously ill from the Medina fever, to the extent that they had to say their prayers seated. However, God kept this affliction from affecting His Prophet (SAAS)." The account states, "And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) went out before them while they were praying in this way and told them, 'You should know that the prayer of someone seated is only half as valuable as that of someone standing.' At this the Muslims, despite their weakness and sickness, exerted themselves to stand, in order to gain grace."
lessons: Even in states of weakness and suffering, the believers sought to maximize their spiritual effort and not settle for less than their best.
The Prophet's guidance during hardship was practical and motivating rather than dismissive of the Companions' genuine struggle.
Spiritual reward and physical effort are connected; when the body can give more, the soul benefits more, and this awareness can be a source of inner strength.
practical_advice: When illness or exhaustion tempts you to reduce your worship, remember that even a small increase in effort during hardship carries great spiritual weight.
Use the example of the Companions who stood despite illness as motivation to push through spiritual and personal commitments even when conditions are difficult.
Rather than abandoning good habits during hard times, scale them appropriately and give what you genuinely can, knowing God values sincere effort over perfect performance.

title: The Prophet Establishes the Charter of Brotherhood Between Emigrants and Helpers
summary: Upon arriving in Medina, the Prophet drew up a formal document establishing brotherhood, mutual obligations, and rights between the Emigrants from Mecca and the Helpers of Medina, forming them into one unified community.
story: Section: On the establishment of friendship between the Emigrants and the Helpers through the document he had drawn up between them. On the nature of that brotherly relationship he made between them, and on the friendly relations he established with the Jews who were in Medina. In the city's Jewish quarters at that time there were the Qaynuqac, Nadir and Qurayza tribes. According to al-Tabari they had settled in the Hijzz before the anpir, the Helpers, in the days of Nebuchadnezar, when he had conquered Jerusalem. When the Iram flood occurred and people had scattered in all directions, the Aws and the Khazraj tribes had settled in Medina with the Jews. They had established pacts of alliance with these Jews and imitated their ways because of the virtue they saw in them that they had acquired through the prophets. However, God had bestowed guidance and Islam upon those who had been polytheists, while they, in their envy, pride and wrongheadedness, had failed to follow the truth. Imam Ahmad stated, "CAffxn related to us, quoting Hammad b. Salama and LAvim al-Ahwal, from Anas b. Malik, as follows, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) established a pact between the Emigrants and the Helpers in the house of Anas b. Mdik.'" This was also related by Imam Ahmad, al-Bukh~ri, Muslim and Aba DZ'ad, through various lines of transmission from CAsim b. Sulaymxn al-Ahwal, from Anas b. MHlik, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) established a pact between Quraysh and the Helpers in my house." Imam Ahmad stated, "Nasr b. Bzb related to us, from Hajjzj, he being Ibn Artat, who said, 'It was related to us by Surayj from 'Abbad, from Hajjaj, from 'Amr b. Shucayb, from his father and grandfather, to the effect that the Prophet (SAAS) wrote up a contract between the Emigrants saying that they would respect one another's strongholds, that they would treat with kindness those in distress and bring peace between the Muslims.'"
lessons: Building structured frameworks of mutual support and accountability transforms a group of individuals into a resilient community.
The Prophet's immediate priority upon arriving in a new city was social cohesion, reminding us that community is a foundation of wellbeing.
Loneliness and displacement can be addressed not just by emotional comfort but by formal commitments of belonging and responsibility to one another.
practical_advice: When joining a new community, actively seek to formalize bonds of support and mutual obligation rather than remaining on the periphery.
If you are experiencing loneliness or displacement, look for ways to contribute to and invest in those around you, as giving to a community deepens your sense of belonging.

Reflect on the relationships in your life and consider whether you have made clear, mutual commitments of support with those you care about, as the Prophet established these formally for his community.

title: The Covenant of Medina: The Prophet's Constitutional Document for a Multi-Faith Community
summary: The Prophet drafted a comprehensive constitutional document between the Muslim Emigrants, the Helpers, and the Jewish tribes of Medina, defining their rights, obligations, mutual defense, and recognition of God's authority in disputes.
story: Muhammad b. Ishzq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) wrote a contract between the Emigrants and the Helpers in which he expressed conciliation towards the Jews and made a pact with them, confirming them in their religion and their properties. He stipulated their rights and obligations as follows, 'In the name of God, the Merciful, the Beneficent, this is a document from Muhammad, the umms (unlettered) Prophet, between the believers and Muslims of Quraysh and Yathrib and their followers, allies and supporters, establishing that they are one nation apart from all others. The Emigrants from Quraysh will maintain their current practice and will honour blood-money contracts between themselves and will treat their weaker members with kindness and justice. The Banti 'Awf shall maintain their current practice, and honour their former blood-money contracts, each party treating their weaker members with kindness and justice, among all believers.' "He then made reference to each clan of the Helpers and to the families of each home, mentioning the Bana Sxcida, the Bana Jusham, the Ban0 al-Najjar, the Bana 'Amr b. 'Awf and the Bann al-Nabit. "He went on to say, 'The believers shall not leave anyone among them burdened by debts without giving to him in kindness and in paying for him any ransom money or blood-money he owes. A believer will not ally with any freed-man of a second believer against that man. Pious believers act against any of their own who practise evil or seek to bring harm, sin, aggression or corruption among believers. Believers will unite against anyone doing this, even if he be one of their own sons. No believer shall kill any believer for any non-believer. Nor shall he help any nonbeliever against a believer. God's protection is all one; the least of them (believers) can offer protection to others. Believers are allies to one another, excluding others. "'Whatever Jews follow us shall receive help and equality. They shall not be maltreated nor will help be afforded others against them. Peace for all believers is one; no peace shall be accepted for any one believer to the exclusion of others when the battle is for God's cause; among them equality and justice must prevail. In every expedition we engage in riders shall take turns on their mounts. "'Believers shall avenge one another for blood shed in God's cause. Believers who are pious are well-guided on the straightest of paths. No polytheist shall be allowed to offer protection for the property or persons of Quraysh nor try to interfere with any believer. Anyone who has clearly aggressed against and caused the death of any believer shall be subject to retaliation for him unless the dead man's executor can be satisfied. Believers as a whole shall take action against him and no excuse for their not doing so will be acceptable. "'It shall not be permissible for any believer who has accepted what is in this document and who believes in God and Judgement Day to give help or shelter to any wrong-doer. Any believer who does this shall be the object of God's curses and anger on Judgement Day and neither compensation nor excuse will be acceptable from him. Any matter in which you disagree must be referred to God, Almighty and Glorious is He, and to Muhammad (SAAS). "'The Jews shall pay expenses along with the Muslims so long as they are allied in warfare. The Jews of the BanB 'Awf are one nation with the believers; the Jews shall have their religion, the Muslims their own. This applies to their freed-men and to themselves, except for those who commit sins and are unjust. Such persons only harm themselves and their families. "'What applies to Bano 'Awf shall also apply to the Jews of Banii al-NajjZr, Bana al-Hzrith, Bana Sa'ida, Bana Jusham, Bana al-Aws, Bann Thaclaba and Jufna, and Bano alShutayba. The close associates of the Jews shall be viewed as themselves. None of them shall go to war without Muhammad's permission. However, he is not (thereby) constrained from taking revenge for injury. Whoever attacks another may do so (to protect) himself and his family, but (act) not unjustly, for God condemns such acts. The Jews shall bear their own expenses, the Muslims theirs. Each must help one another against any who fight those who adopt this document. They must give one another advice and consultation; and do good and avoid evil. A man is not held blameworthy on behalf of'"
lessons: True leadership addresses not only the spiritual needs of a community but its social, legal, and civic structures as well.
Justice and coexistence require explicit, agreed-upon frameworks; the Prophet's document shows that peace is built deliberately and not merely assumed.
Referring disputes to God and His Messenger is a principle of humility and trust that prevents communities and individuals from being destroyed by internal conflict.
practical_advice: When navigating complex relationships or group dynamics, consider establishing clear and mutually agreed expectations in writing to prevent misunderstanding and conflict.
Reflect on whether you are treating those who are weaker or more vulnerable in your community with the kindness and justice this document demanded of all believers.
When facing disagreement or conflict, commit to referring the matter to a higher principle or trusted authority rather than letting ego or pride escalate the situation.

title: The Charter of Medina: Terms of the Agreement
summary: The Charter of Medina outlined the rights and responsibilities of Muslims, Jews, and allied tribes in Yathrib, establishing a framework for governance, mutual defense, and dispute resolution under the Prophet Muhammad's authority.
story: The close associates of the Jews shall be viewed as themselves. None of them shall go to war without Muhammad's permission. However, he is not (thereby) constrained from taking revenge for injury. Whoever attacks another may do so (to protect) himself and his family, but (act) not unjustly, for God condemns such acts. The Jews shall bear their own expenses, the Muslims theirs. Each must help one another against any who fight those who adopt this document. They must give one another advice and consultation; and do good and avoid evil. A man is not held blameworthy on behalf of his ally. Help must be given to those wronged. The centre of Yathrib shall he a sanctuary for those who agree to this document. One's neighbour shall he ueated as oneself, without being harmed or sinned against. No property shall be encroached upon without the permission of its owner. "'In the case of any dispute or incident occurring between those agreeing to this document, and involving consequences likely to be harmful, the matter must be referred to God and to M+ammad, the Messenger of God. God acknowledges what is very good and very pious in this document. Quraysh and those who help them shall receive no protection. (Those accepting this document) must help one another against anyone attacking Yathrib. If they are called upon to make peace and do so and keep to it, they will he making peace; if they make a similar demand, then it is up to the Muslims to agree except if the warfare is over the Faith. Each participant shall receive whatever portion is due to him from his own side's winnings. "'This document will not provide protection for anyone sinful or unjust. Whoever goes to war will he safe; whoever stays in the town will be safe - except for those who have sinned or been unjust. God provides protection for those who are good and pious."' Ibn Ishiiq gives it in approximately this form.
lessons: Establishing clear agreements and boundaries is an act of wisdom and prevents conflict before it begins.
Justice must be universal — no community is above accountability, and protection is earned through righteousness, not tribal loyalty.
Referring disputes to a trusted, God-conscious authority reflects humility and preserves communal harmony.
practical_advice: When entering a new environment or relationship, establish clear expectations and mutual responsibilities early to prevent misunderstandings.
When conflict arises in your community or workplace, seek a neutral, principled mediator rather than letting grievances fester.
Reflect on whether your actions would hold up to a standard of justice — ask yourself if what you are doing is something you could defend openly and fairly.

title: Quranic Praise for the Helpers Who Welcomed the Emigrants
summary: Allah praised the Ansar (Helpers) in the Quran for their generosity and selflessness in welcoming the Muhajirun (Emigrants), preferring others over themselves despite their own poverty.
story: As the Almighty stated, "And those who made their abode in the town and in the faith before them do love those who emigrated to them. They do not find in their hearts a need for what they are given, and prefer (them) even before themselves, despite their own poverty. Those who are saved from the meanness of themselves shall be successful" (ssrat al-Hashr; LM, v.9). And He also said, "as for those with whom you made agreements, give them their due; God is wihless to all things" (siirat al-Nk2; IV, v.33).
lessons: True generosity means giving even when you yourself are in need — it is a mark of the highest character.
Allah notices and honors selflessness; those who overcome inner miserliness achieve spiritual success.
Welcoming others wholeheartedly, without resentment or expectation of return, is a form of worship.
practical_advice: Practice generosity in small, daily ways — sharing your time, resources, or space with someone who needs it more than you, even when it is inconvenient.
When you feel reluctant to give or help, pause and examine whether it is genuine scarcity or inner selfishness holding you back, and challenge yourself to act generously anyway.
Remind yourself that God witnesses every act of kindness, even those no one else sees, and let that awareness motivate your giving.

title: The Brotherhood Pact Between the Emigrants and the Helpers
summary: The Prophet Muhammad established a formal pact of brotherhood between the Emigrants (Muhajirun) and the Helpers (Ansar) in Medina, pairing each Emigrant with a Helper to create bonds of loyalty, support, and mutual care.
story: Muhammad b. Ishnq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) established brotherhood between his fellow Emigrants and the Helpers, and said, from what I have heard - and God forbid our atmbuting to him what he did not say - he said, 'Become brothers in God's cause, each taking a brother for himself.' "He then took the hand of 'Ali b. Abn Talib and said, 'This is my brother.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS), lord of the prophets, the leader of the pious, the Messenger of the Lord of the worlds, and the most important and peerless of men, and 'Ali b. Abn T a i b became brothers. "Hamza b. 'Abd al-MutMib, lion of God and lion of His Messenger as well as his uncle, and Zayd b. Haitha, the freed-man of the Messenger of God (SAAS), became brothers. It was Zayd that Hamza appointed his heir at the battle of Uhud. Dbn al-Jan?&ayn and M u a h b. Jabal became brothers." Ja'far b. Aba Ibn Hishm pointed out that at that time Jacfar was absent in Abyssinia. Ibn I s b q went on: "Aba Bakr and Kh%ija b. Zayd al-Khazraji, Wmar b. a l - K h ~ p band ltbiin b. Mdik, Aba Wbayda and Sa'd b. Mu%&, 'Abd al-Ra!mi%n b. 'Awf and Sa'd b. al-Rabic, al-Zubayr b. al-cAww?un and Salama b. Salama b. Waqsh, all became brothers. It is also said that al-Zubayr and 'Abd AU& b. Masctid, W t h m b. 'Affan and Aws b. Thabit b. al-Mundhir al-Najjzri, Talha b. Wbayd Allah and Ka'b b. Md&, Sa'id b. Zayd and Ubayy b. Ka'b, Mus%bb. Wmayr and Aba Ayyiib, Aba Hudhayfa b.Wtba and 'Abbad b. Bishr, ' A m and Hudhayfa h. al-Yamzn al-'Absi, the ally of 'Abd al-Ashhal, all became brothers. It is also said, however, that 'Amma and Thabit b. Qays b. Shammas became brothers." I observe that this chain of authorities comes through two lines. He went on, "Abii Dharr Barir b. Junzda and al-Mundhir b 'Arnr, known as 'he who hurried on to death', Hatib b. Aba Balta'a and Wwaym b. Sacida, Salman and Abii al-Darda', Bilnl and Abn Ruwayha 'Abd All& b. 'Abd a l - R a b w al-Khath'ami, one of the Fazac- all these became brothers." He continued, "These are the men who were named to us from among his Companions between whom the Messenger of God (SAAS) established pacts of brotherhood. May God be pleased with them all."
lessons: The Prophet understood that belonging and brotherhood are essential human needs, especially for those who have lost their homes and families.
True brotherhood transcends blood ties — it is built on shared values, faith, and a commitment to support one another through difficulty.
Leadership means actively creating conditions for community — the Prophet did not leave the Emigrants to find their own way but personally initiated bonds of belonging for them.
practical_advice: If you are going through a major life transition — a move, a loss, a new beginning — actively seek out a person or community who can serve as an anchor of support rather than waiting for connection to happen on its own.
Consider whether there is someone in your life who is new, displaced, or isolated, and take a concrete step to include them — invite them, introduce them, or simply check in consistently.
Reflect on your existing friendships: are they built on mutual support and shared values, or only on convenience? Invest more deeply in relationships that bring out your best self.

title: Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf Finds His Footing in Medina
summary: After migrating to Medina with nothing, Abd al-Rahman ibn Awf declined his Helper brother Sa'd ibn al-Rabi's offer to share his wealth and wives, instead asking only to be shown the marketplace, where he quickly prospered through trade and soon married.
story: Al-Bukh%iri's text reads as follows, "Chapter on h o the ~ Prophet ($US), established brotherhood between his Companrons. 'Ahd a l - m m a b. 'Awf stated, 'The Prophet (SAAS), established a bond of brotherhood between myself and Saw b. al-Rabi' when we came to Medina.'" Aba Juhayfa stated, "The Prophet (SAAS) established brotherhood between SalmZn the Persian and Aba al-Darda'. God be pleased with him." Muhammad b. Ynsuf related to us, quoting Sufym, from Humayd, from Anas, who said, "'Abd al-Ra!nnm b. 'Awf amved and the Prophet (SAAS) established brotherhood between him and Saw b. al-Rabic, the Helper. The latter suggested that he share his family and his property with him. 'Abd al-Rahm replied, 'God bless you in both your family and your wealth. Show me the way to the market.' There he made some profit from cottage cheese and fat. The Prophet (SAAS) saw him a few days later and he looked stained with yellow upon hi. The Prophet (SAAS) asked him, 'What's the matter with you, 'Abd al-Rhiin?' He replied, 'I married a woman from among the Helpers.' 'What did you give her as dowry?' he asked. 'The weight of 5 dirhams worth of gold,' he replied. The Prophet (SAAS) responded, 'Well, have a feast, if only with a sheep!'" Imam Ahmad stated, "'ilffiin related to us, quoting Hammiid, Thiihit and Hamid, from Anas, that 'Abd al-Rahmm h. 'Awf came to Medina and the Messenger of God (SAAS) established a pact of brotherhood between him and Sa'd b. al-Rabic, the Helper. Sacd said to him, 'Now brother, I'm the wealthiest man in Medina. Take a share that appeals to you. I have two wives. Look and see which of them you prefer and I'll divorce her!' "'Ahd al-Rahmm replied, 'May God bless yon in your family and in your wealth. Show me the way to the market.' They did so and he engaged in buying and selling and made a profit. He ended up by bringing back some cottage cheese and cooking fat. Some time thereafter the Messenger of God (SAAS) (saw him) with traces of saffron upon him and asked hi,'What's the matter with you?' He replied, '0 Messenger of God, I married a woman.' 'What did you give her in dowry?' he asked. 'The weight of 5 dirhams worth of gold,' he replied. 'Well,' he commented, 'have a feast, if only with a sheep!' "'Ahd al-Rahman observed, 'I was in such a state that if I had lifted a single rock I would have hoped to find gold and silver.'"
lessons: Self-reliance and a willingness to work are dignified responses to hardship — Abd al-Rahman did not want charity but the opportunity to use his own abilities.
Barakah (divine blessing) follows honest effort and gratitude — when you begin with sincerity and trust in God, small beginnings can grow into great abundance.
Celebrating life's milestones, even modestly, is a Prophetic encouragement — joy and gratitude should be expressed, not suppressed, even in times of rebuilding.
practical_advice: When facing a new start after a major loss, focus on what skills and abilities you still have rather than what you have lost — ask 'what can I do from here?' rather than dwelling on what was taken.
If someone offers you more than you need or more than feels right to accept, it is noble to graciously decline excess while accepting genuine goodwill — receiving help with dignity is itself a skill.
Mark your small victories and milestones with gratitude and simple celebration — do not wait until you have fully 'recovered' to acknowledge progress along the way.

title: The Emigrants' Gratitude and the Helpers' Generosity: Sharing Rewards
summary: The Emigrants expressed concern to the Prophet that the Helpers' generosity was so great they feared the Helpers alone would receive divine reward, and the Prophet reassured them that their prayers and praise for the Helpers also counted as reward.
story: Iman Ahmad stated, "Yazid related to us, that Humayd informed him, from Anas, as follows, 'The Emigrants said, "O Messenger of God, we've never been to people who are better prepared to give help in hopes of so little or more willing to give from so much. They have spared us such difficulty and shared such comfort with us we're afraid they're the only ones getting rewards." He replied, "No, there's all the great praise you've given them, and you have also prayed to God for them."'" The line of transmission of this hadith is two-thirds in accord with the criteria for both Sahih collections. None of the authors of the six canonical books of traditions give this particular line. It is established in the Sahih collections along a different path. Al-Bukhari stated, "Al-Hakam b. Nafi' informed us, quoting Shu'ayb, quoting Abu al-Zinad, from al-A'raj, from Abu Hurayra, who said, 'The Helpers said, "Divide up the palm trees between us and our brothers." "No," he replied. They said, "You work with us in hard work, and we will have you share recompense in the fruits." "We hear and obey," they responded.'" He is alone in giving this hadith. 'Abd al-Rahman b. Zayd b. Aslam said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) told the Helpers, 'Your brothers have left behind their wealth and their children and have come forth to you.' They commented, 'Our property is to be divided up between us then.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked, 'Well, is there some alternative?' 'What could there be, O Messenger of God?' they asked. He replied, 'These are people who don't mind hard work; provide some for them and share the fruits.' 'We will,' they agreed."
lessons: Gratitude and appreciation expressed in words and prayers are themselves a form of worship and reward from God.
Generosity given selflessly for God's sake is never diminished, as God rewards all forms of sincere contribution.
Community bonds are strengthened when each member contributes according to their capacity, whether through wealth, labor, or prayer.
practical_advice: When you feel you have little to offer compared to others, remember that sincere prayer and heartfelt appreciation for those around you are themselves valuable acts.
Practice expressing genuine gratitude to those who support you — this is spiritually meaningful and emotionally healing for both parties.
In times of hardship, focus on what you can give rather than what you lack; even encouragement and kind words carry great weight.

title: The Death of Abu Umama As'ad b. Zurara
summary: Abu Umama As'ad b. Zurara, one of the first leaders of the Ansar and a key figure of the early Madinah community, died while the Prophet's mosque was still being built, and the Prophet addressed concerns about what enemies would say about this loss.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Abu Umama As'ad b. Zurara died in these months, while the mosque was being built. He died from diptheria or from coughing." Ibn Jarir stated in his history, "Muhammad b. 'Abd al-A'la informed us, Yazid b. Zuray' related to us, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, from Anas, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) cauterized As'ad b. Zurara in the tongue." The men in this chain of authorities are considered reliable. Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abu Bakr b. Muhammad b. 'Amr b. Hazm related to me, from Yahya b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd al-Rahman b. As'ad b. Zurara, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, "The death of Abu Umama is a great pity with respect to the Jews and the Arab hypocrites. They will say, 'If he were a prophet, his companion would not have died.' But I have no power from God over this for myself or for my friends."'" This means that he has to have been the first man to die after the arrival in Medina of the Prophet (SAAS). Abu al-Hasan b. al-Athir has claimed in al-Ghaba (The Jungle) that he died in the month of Shawwal, seven months following the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina. But God knows best. Muhammad Ibn Ishaq related, from 'Asim b. Umar b. Qatada, that the Banu al-Najjar asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) to appoint a leader for them to succeed Abu Umama As'ad b. Zurara. He replied, "You are my uncles and I am here among you; I will be your leader." He disliked the idea of singling out any one of them above the others. It was a credit to the Banu al-Najjar that they took pride among their peers in having the Messenger of God (SAAS) as their leader.
lessons: Death is a reality that befalls even the most beloved and righteous companions, reminding us that life belongs entirely to God.
The Prophet's acknowledgment of his own powerlessness over death is a model of humility and complete trust in God's decree.
Community leadership and care can be expressed through closeness and personal presence, as the Prophet himself became the leader of the Banu al-Najjar.
practical_advice: When you lose someone dear and struggle to understand why, remember that even the Prophet acknowledged he had no power over the timing of death — this is a comfort, not a failure of faith.
Resist the temptation to interpret loss as a sign of divine abandonment; the Prophet's companions died and this did not diminish the truth of his mission.
In times of communal grief, lean on collective support and shared leadership rather than isolating yourself in your sorrow.

title: The First Births Among the Emigrants and Helpers in Madinah
summary: The birth of 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr was the first birth among the Emigrants in the Islamic era in Madinah, bringing great joy to the Muslim community who feared Jewish magic had rendered them unable to have children after emigration.
story: The first birth to the Emigrants in the Islamic era was 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr. Similarly, al-Nu'man b. Bashir was the first child born to the Helpers after the emigration. God be pleased with them both. Some claim that Ibn al-Zubayr was born 20 months after the emigration. Abu al-Aswad said this. Al-Waqidi related this from Muhammad b. Yahya b. Sahl b. Abu Hathma, from his father, from his grandfather. They claim that al-Nu'man was born 6 months before al-Zubayr, some 14 months after the emigration. The truth is as we have said above. Al-Bukhari stated, "Zakariyya' b. Yahya related to us, quoting Abu Usama, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from Asma', who said, regarding her pregnancy with 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr, 'When I emigrated, I was at term. When I came to Medina I stayed in Quba' and gave birth to him there. Then I took him to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and placed him in his lap. He then called for a date, which he masticated then spat into his mouth. And so the first thing that entered the baby's stomach was the saliva of the Messenger of God (SAAS). He then gave him a date to chew on, prayed for him and blessed him. He was the first baby born in Islam.'" This account was followed by one of Khalid b. Makhlad, from 'Ali b. Mushir, from Hisham, from his father, from Asma', to the effect that she was pregnant when she emigrated to the Prophet (SAAS). Qutayba related to us, from Abu Usama, from Hisham b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, "The first child born in Islam was 'Abd Allah b. al-Zubayr. They brought him to the Prophet (SAAS) and he took a date which he chewed, then placed in the baby's mouth. And so the first food that entered his stomach was the saliva of the Prophet (SAAS)." This refutes al-Waqidi and others, because he reported that the Prophet (SAAS) sent Zayd b. Haritha and Abu Rafi' back to Mecca with 'Abd Allah b. Urayqit to bring his and Abu Bakr's children. They brought them back right after the emigration of the Prophet (SAAS). Asma' was pregnant and near term, that is almost ready to deliver her child. When she did so the Muslims let out great shouts of "God is Most Great" in their joy at the birth because it had come to their attention that the Jews had used magic against them so that they would not have children following their emigration. And so God had refuted what the Jews were claiming.
lessons: New life in times of hardship and displacement is a sign of God's mercy and a refutation of despair and hopelessness.
The Prophet's blessing of the newborn with his saliva and prayer shows the importance of invoking divine blessings at the beginning of new beginnings.
Joy shared by a community in difficult times strengthens collective resilience and faith in God's promise.
practical_advice: When facing fears about the future — whether about family, security, or loss — remember that hope and new beginnings can arrive even in the most uncertain circumstances.
Celebrate the small blessings and new beginnings in your community; collective joy is a powerful antidote to collective grief.
Begin important new chapters in your life with prayer and seeking God's blessing, following the example of the Prophet who blessed the first newborn in Islam.

title: The Prophet's Marriage with 'A'isha Consummated in Shawwal
summary: The Prophet Muhammad consummated his marriage with 'A'isha in the month of Shawwal, and 'A'isha later used this as evidence to refute the superstition that marriage between the two Eid feasts brings separation.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) consummated his marriage with 'A'isha in the month of Shawwal that year. Imam Ahmad stated, "Waki' related to me, quoting Sufyan, from Isma'il b. Umayya, from 'Abd Allah b. 'Urwa, from his father, from 'A'isha, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) made a marriage contract with me in (the month of) Shawwal and consummated the marriage in Shawwal; and which of his wives did he favour more than myself?'" 'A'isha used to prefer to have her women marry in Shawwal. Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa'i, and Ibn Maja related this on various paths from al-Thawri. Al-Tirmidhi considered it hasan sahih, 'good and authentic'; we know of it only from Sufyan al-Thawri. According to this the marriage of the Messenger of God (SAAS) to 'A'isha took place in the seventh or eighth month after the emigration. Both statements were related by Ibn Jarir, who commented earlier, in his treatment of his marriage with Sawda, on how he had married 'A'isha after they had arrived in Medina, and that his marriage was at al-Surah and during the daytime. This is in contrast to the normal practice of people today. In his marrying her in Shawwal there is a refutation of those who express disfavour for marriage occurring between the two feasts, out of fear of the couple's later separation.
