There is nothing to this, as is shown by what 'A'isha said in reply to those people who imagined this for that time, that is, she said, "He made the marriage contract with me in Shawwal and consummated the marriage in Shawwal; and which of his wives did he favour more than myself?" This shows that she understood from him that she was the wife he favoured most. And this belief of hers is shown to have been correct by various clear proofs, if only by that definite statement given in the Tahib collection of al-Bukhari from 'Amr b. al-'As which states, "I said, O Messenger of God, which person do you most favour?" He replied, "'A'isha." I then asked, "And which man?" "Her father," he replied.
lessons: Superstitions and unfounded cultural fears about timing should not override trust in God's blessings and the Sunnah of the Prophet.
Love and favour in marriage are built on genuine closeness, mutual respect, and sincere companionship, as exemplified by the Prophet's relationship with 'A'isha.
The Prophet's example challenges cultural taboos that have no basis in revelation, encouraging Muslims to follow guidance over inherited superstition.
practical_advice: Do not let cultural superstitions or unfounded fears dictate major life decisions such as marriage; trust in God's timing and seek guidance from authentic religious sources.
Reflect on the relationships in your life and invest in building genuine closeness and trust, which are the true foundations of lasting bonds.
When others challenge your choices based on superstition, respond with knowledge and personal testimony as 'A'isha did — confidently and gently.

title: The Institution of Prayer in Medina and the Obligatory Rakaat
summary: Upon the Prophet's arrival in Medina, Islam became firmly established and the obligatory prayers were instituted, with scholarly discussion on whether the prayer at home was originally two or four rakaat before being increased.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "That same year, the first year of the hegira, the length of the saltit al-ha4ar, the prayer while at home, was increased by two rakcat," prayer cycles, or so it is said. Previously both the prayer at home and while travelling had been two rakeat. This came about after the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS) on the 12th of Rabical-kkhir." He said, "Al-Wzqidi maintained that there was no dispute among the people of Hijaz on this subject." I observe that previously I have given the hadith related by al-Bukhm through Makar, from al-Zuhri, from Wrwa, from 'k'isha, who said, "When first made obligatory, prayer was prescribed as needing two rakcat. This was affirmed for prayer while travelling, but it was increased for prayer while at home." This was also related through al-Sha'bi back to Masriiq. Al-Bayhaqi related, from al-Hasan al-Ba~ri, that when the prayer at home was first made obligatory, it was established as four rakcat. But God knows best. We discussed this in the exegesis of swat al-Nis3, with regards to the words of the Almighty, "And when you travel over the earth, there is no blame on you if you shorten the prayer" (sarat al-Nis2; IV, v.101).
lessons: The establishment of religious practices often involves scholarly discussion and refinement, teaching us that seeking knowledge and clarity in worship is itself an act of devotion.
The shortening of prayer during travel is a mercy from God, reminding us that religion is meant to ease our burdens, not increase them.
The gradual institution of religious obligations reflects divine wisdom in not overwhelming believers, a principle we can apply when building new habits in our own lives.
practical_advice: When feeling overwhelmed by responsibilities, remember that God's religion is built on ease and mercy — seek the accommodations and concessions available to you rather than suffering in silence.
Establish a consistent prayer or reflection routine, even a brief one, as a daily anchor for emotional stability and connection to the divine.

When facing uncertainty or conflicting guidance in life, follow the example of scholars who humbly say 'God knows best' and make decisions with the best available understanding rather than waiting for perfect certainty.

title: The Consolidation of Islam in Medina
summary: After the Prophet's arrival in Medina, the Muslim community united, Islam took firm hold, and the foundational pillars of prayer, zakat, fasting, and Islamic law were established.
story: Ibn Ishzq stated, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) was secure in Medina, his emigrant brothers gathered around him and the Helpers were united, Islam became firmly established. Prayer was instituted and the zakat, the alms tax, and fasting were made obligatory. Punishments were prescribed, and those matters that were made permissible or forbidden were determined. Islam took firm hold there among them. It was this community of Helpers to whom God made reference in the words, 'And those who made their abode in the town and in the faith' (sgrat al-Hashr; LIX, v.9).
lessons: True community is built when people unite around shared values and responsibilities, not merely shared interests or proximity.
The establishment of a just social order requires both spiritual foundations and practical laws — inner faith and outward structure reinforce one another.
Security and belonging enable people to thrive; the Ansar's welcoming of the Emigrants demonstrates how hospitality and solidarity can transform lives.
practical_advice: When going through a major life transition, actively seek out a community of people who share your values — belonging to a supportive group is essential for emotional resilience.
Establish clear daily routines and obligations for yourself, as structure and consistency provide stability during uncertain times.
Look for ways to be an 'Ansar' — a helper — to someone in your life who is going through a difficult transition, as acts of generosity strengthen both the giver and receiver.

title: The Vision of 'Abd Allah ibn Zayd and the Institution of the Adhan
summary: The call to prayer (adhan) was established in Medina after the companion 'Abd Allah ibn Zayd saw a vision of a man in green garments teaching him the words of the adhan, which Bilal was then appointed to proclaim due to his powerful voice.
story: When the Messenger of God (SAAS) first came to Medina, the people would gather with him for prayer at the appointed times without any summons to them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) then wanted to have a horn blown like that the Jews use to call their people to prayer. But he grew to dislike this and ordered a hand bell be used. One was made to be struck to bring the Muslims to the prayer. While this was the practice 'Abd All& b. Zayd b. Tha'laba h. 'Abd Rabbihi, the brother of Ibn al-n?trith b. al-Khazraj, saw a vision and came to the Messenger of God (SAAS) and said, "0 Messenger of God, last night I had a vision. I saw a man pass me by who was wearing two green garments and he carried a bell in his hand. I asked him, 'Will you sell me that bell?' He responded, 'What will you do with it?' I replied, 'We'll use it to summon people to prayer.' He asked, 'Shall I tell you a better way?' 'What would that be?' I asked. He told me, 'You should call out, "AlldIu Akbar! AlldIu Akbar! All&u Akbar! (God is Most Great) I hear witness that there is no god but God. I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of God! I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of God! Come to prayer! Come to prayer! Come to salvation! Come to salvation! Allahu Akbar! All&u Akbar! There is no god but God!"' "Having been told this, the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'These are - if God wills it - visions of truth. Stand with Bilal and deliver them to him so that he can use them to announce the call to prayer; he has a stronger voice than you.' "When Bild made the call to prayer using these words Wmar b. al-Khatrzb heard him while in his house and came out to the Messenger of God (SAAS) dragging his cloak behind him and saying, '0 Prophet of God, I swear by Him who sent you with the truth that I saw the same vision!' "'All praise be to God!' exclaimed the Messenger of God (SAAS)."
lessons: Divine guidance can come through unexpected channels — a dream, a vision, or an ordinary companion — reminding us to remain humble and open to wisdom from all sources.
When multiple people independently receive the same guidance, it is a powerful sign of divine confirmation, teaching us to trust in the convergence of sincere hearts.
The Prophet's willingness to consult his companions and act on their visions demonstrates that leadership rooted in humility and consultation produces the best outcomes.
practical_advice: When you feel spiritually disconnected or uncertain about the right path, pay attention to recurring thoughts, dreams, or signs — they may be guiding you toward something important.
Like Bilal, recognize and use the unique gifts God has given you in service of something greater than yourself, even when others might have been the ones to receive the original inspiration.
When multiple trusted sources in your life point you toward the same decision or course of action, take that convergence seriously as a sign worth acting upon.

title: Umar ibn al-Khattab's Vision of the Adhan
summary: Umar ibn al-Khattab independently saw the same vision of the adhan as 'Abd Allah ibn Zayd and rushed to tell the Prophet, only to find that divine revelation had already confirmed it before him.
story: Ibn Hishzb stated that Ibn Jurayj quoted 'At&' as having said, "I heard Wbayd b. Wmayr say, 'The Prophet (SAAS) had discussed with his Companions the use of a bell to summon people to prayer. While Wmar b. al-Khacph was planning to buy two pieces of wood for the bell, he had a vision while he slept and was told, "Do not make a bell; instead call people to prayer." 'Wmar went to the Prophet (SAAS) to tell him what he had seen. The Prophet had himself received revelation of that. Wmar was consequently very surprised to hear Bild make the call to prayer. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said to him, when he (Wmar) told him of his experience, "Revelation of this preceded you!"'" This shows that the revelation had come in confirmation of what 'Abd Allah b. Zayd b. 'Ahd Rabhihi had seen in the vision or so some authorities state. But God Almighty knows best.
lessons: Even the most sincere and eager person may find that God's plan is already unfolding ahead of them — humility means accepting this with joy rather than disappointment.
Divine wisdom often confirms truth through multiple witnesses, reinforcing that important guidance is never arbitrary or accidental.
The eagerness of Umar to act immediately on his vision teaches us the value of responding swiftly to spiritual inspiration.
practical_advice: When you feel you have missed your chance to contribute or that someone else has acted before you, remember that sincerity of intention is never wasted before God even if the action is carried out by another.
Practice acting promptly on righteous intentions rather than delaying, as hesitation can cause us to miss meaningful opportunities for contribution.

When you feel surprised or caught off guard by how quickly events unfold, take a moment to reflect that God's plans are always moving and that your role is to align yourself with them rather than direct them.

title: Bilal's Pre-Dawn Supplication on the Rooftop
summary: A woman of the Banu al-Najjar recalled how Bilal would climb to the roof of her house every morning before dawn, wait for daybreak, and make a personal supplication for Quraysh to accept Islam before making the call to prayer.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Muhammad h. Ja'far b. al-Zuhayr related to me, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, that a woman of the Bann al-Najjzr told him, 'My house was one of the tallest around the mosque. Bild would make the call to prayer on top of it early each morning. He would come just before dawn and sit above the house waiting for daybreak. When he saw it he would stretch out and say, "0 God, I praise You and seek Your help for Quraysh to adopt Your religion."' She concluded, 'And then he would make the call to prayer. I never knew him to miss one night.'" That is, to miss saying those words.
lessons: True devotion is expressed not only in public acts of worship but in the private, consistent prayers that no one sees — consistency in private is the mark of sincere faith.

Bilal, once a slave tortured for his belief in God, now stood atop rooftops calling the world to prayer and praying for his former oppressors — a profound example of rising above resentment through faith.
Praying for those who have wronged or rejected you, as Bilal prayed for Quraysh, is one of the highest expressions of spiritual maturity and emotional liberation.
practical_advice: Establish a personal pre-dawn or early morning ritual — even a few minutes of quiet supplication or reflection — and protect it as a daily non-negotiable anchor for your emotional and spiritual wellbeing.
If you carry resentment toward people who have wronged you, try praying for their guidance rather than their punishment; this act can begin to free you from the weight of that resentment.
Let Bilal's example inspire you: no matter how low life has brought you, consistency in worship and service can elevate you to heights you cannot currently imagine.

title: The Prophet's Affection for Aisha and Her Father Abu Bakr
summary: When asked who he loved most, the Prophet named Aisha among people and her father Abu Bakr among men, a testament to the depth of his personal bonds and the honor of those closest to him.
story: That she understood from him that she was the wife he favoured most. And this belief of hers is shown to have been correct by various clear proofs, if only by that definite statement given in the Tahi!, collection of alBukhm from 'Amr b. albCAswhich states, "I said, 0 Messenger of God, which person do you most favour?" He replied, "ck'isha." I then asked, "And which man?" "Her father," he replied.
lessons: Expressing love and preference openly to those we care about strengthens bonds and gives people a sense of security and belonging.
The Prophet's love for Abu Bakr was inseparable from his love for Aisha, reminding us that our deepest relationships are often woven together in ways that multiply their blessings.
Being known as someone who is beloved is itself a form of honour — the Prophet's words elevated both Aisha and Abu Bakr in the eyes of all who heard them.
practical_advice: Do not assume the people you love know how much you value them — express your appreciation and affection clearly and specifically, as the Prophet did.
Reflect on the relationships in your life that are most nourishing and ask yourself whether you are investing in them with the same depth of care that they deserve.

When feeling unappreciated or taken for granted, remember Aisha's quiet confidence in the Prophet's love — trust built through consistent, loving behaviour over time is more reliable than any single grand gesture.

title: The Expedition of Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib
summary: The Prophet sent his uncle Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib with thirty Emigrants to intercept a Quraysh caravan, resulting in a standoff with Abu Jahl's force of three hundred men that was defused without battle by a mediator.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "Al-Waqidi claimed that in the month of Ramadan of that year the Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Harnza b. 'Abd al-Muc@lib over 30 of the Emigrants, having presented him with a white standard, to interfere with Quraysh caravans. Al-W~qidi states that Hamza confronted Aba Jahl at the head of 300 Quraysh men, hut that Majd~h. 'Amr intervened between them, and so they did not engage in battle. "He also said that it was Aba Marthad al-Ghanawi who carried the flag for Hamza."
lessons: Standing firm in the face of overwhelming odds, as thirty men faced three hundred, demonstrates that courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to act despite it.
A single peacemaker intervening at the right moment can prevent great harm — the role of the reconciler is among the most valuable in any conflict.
The white standard given to Hamza symbolised legitimacy and purpose — having a clear mandate and sense of mission fortifies people to face great challenges.
practical_advice: When facing a situation where you are vastly outnumbered or outmatched, focus on your purpose and the legitimacy of your cause rather than the odds against you.

If you find yourself in the middle of a conflict between others, consider whether you might play the role of the mediator — sometimes the bravest act is stepping between two forces and calling for calm.
Prepare thoroughly before any confrontation — Hamza's thirty men were chosen, equipped, and given clear leadership, showing that preparation is itself a form of courage.

title: The Expedition of Ubayda ibn al-Harith
summary: The Prophet entrusted a white banner to Ubayda ibn al-Harith and dispatched him into the Rabigh valley at the beginning of the eighth month of the first year of Hijra.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "Al-Waqidi also claimed that the Prophet (SAAS) in ShawwS, at the beginning of the eighth month of that year, entrusted a white banner to Whayda b. al-Hsith and ordered him to proceed into the Rzbigh valley.
lessons: Delegating important missions to trusted lieutenants reflects the Prophet's confidence in his companions' capabilities and teaches leaders the importance of developing and empowering others.
Early proactive measures, even before a direct threat materialises, reflect strategic wisdom and the importance of preparing rather than merely reacting.
Each companion was given a role suited to their strengths — recognising and deploying people according to their abilities multiplies the effectiveness of any community.
practical_advice: When facing a prolonged challenge, take proactive steps to address potential threats before they become crises, rather than waiting until you are forced to react.
Identify trusted people in your life to whom you can delegate important tasks — trying to carry every burden alone leads to exhaustion and poor decisions.
When given a responsibility or mission, carry it with the same dignity and commitment as Ubayda carried his white banner — your task, however modest it seems, matters.

title: Early Military Expeditions: Hamza, Ubayda, and Sa'd's Missions
summary: The Prophet dispatched several early military expeditions including those led by Hamza, Ubayda ibn al-Harith, and Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas, all of which involved encounters with Quraysh forces but resulted in no major battles.
story: at the head of 300 Quraysh men, hut that Majd~h. 'Amr intervened between them, and so they did not engage in battle. "He also said that it was Aha Marthad al-Ghanawi who carried the flag for Hamza." Chapter: On the expedition of'ubayda b. al-Hzrith b. 'Abd al-Mug~alib. Ibn Jarir stated, "Al-Waqidi also claimed that the Prophet (SAAS) in ShawwS, at the beginning of the eighth month of that year, entrusted a white banner to Whayda b. al-Hsith and ordered him to proceed into the Rzbigh valley. "His flag was carried by Mistah b. Uthztha. He reached Thaniyyst al-Murra, not far from al-Juhfa, in the company of 60 Emigrants, there being no Helpers present. They met up with the polytheists at a well called AhyZ'. They shot arrows at one another, but did not engage in sword play." Al-Wzqidi stated, "The polytheists were 200 in number and were led by Abii Sufyan Sakhr b. Harb. This is what we believed to he well established, though it is also said that they were led by Mikraz b. Hafs. Al-W~qidistated, "In that same first year, in Dhii al-Qa'da, the Messenger of God (SAAS) presented a white banner to Sa'd b. Abii Waqqa~and sent him to al-Kharrzr; the flag was carried by al-Miqd~db. al-Aswad. "Abo Bakr b. Ismncil related to me, from his father, from 'Amir b. Sa'd, from his father, who said, 'I left with 20 men, by foot' -or he said 'with 21 men'. 'We would hide by day and travel by night. We got to al-Kharra on the morning of the fifth. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had instructed me to go no further than al-Kharra. The caravan had preceded me there by a day.'" Al-Wxqidi stated, "The caravan consisted of 60; all those with Sa'd were Emigrants."
lessons: Obedience to leadership and staying within defined boundaries, as Sa'd did by not exceeding al-Kharra, reflects trust and discipline.
Courage does not always require combat; restraint and avoiding unnecessary bloodshed is also a form of strength.
Strategic patience — hiding by day and traveling by night — shows that wisdom and planning are essential companions to bravery.
practical_advice: When facing a challenging task, follow the guidance given to you carefully and resist the urge to overstep, trusting that the boundaries set are for a reason.
In high-stress situations, focus on what is within your control and execute your part faithfully, even when outcomes are uncertain.
Practice patience and strategic thinking before reacting impulsively when confronted with adversity.

title: The First Child Born to the Emigrants After the Hijra: 'Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr
summary: Abd Allah ibn al-Zubayr was born as the first child among the Emigrants after the migration to Madinah, an event celebrated as a sign of blessing and new beginnings for the Muslim community.
story: Those born in that blessed year, the first of the hegira, included 'Abd Alkh b. al-Zubayr. He was the first to be born in Islam following the emigration, as al-Bukhai related from his mother AsmP and his aunt 'A'isha, mother of the believers, both daughters of Abn Bakr, "the Trusting"; may God be pleased with hoth women. There are those who say that al-Nucm2n b. Bashir was born six months prior to him. According to that, Ibn al-Zubayr would have been the first child horn to Emigrants following the emigration. Some people suggest that they were both horn in the second year of the hegira. The former is obviously correct, as we have shown above. And all credit be to God.
lessons: New beginnings, even amid hardship and displacement, carry within them the seeds of hope and blessing.
The birth of children in a community is a sign of continuity, resilience, and the promise of a flourishing future.
Historical memory matters — recording and preserving moments of joy strengthens communal identity and gratitude.
practical_advice: When going through major life transitions or difficulties, look for signs of new life and growth around you as reminders of hope.
Celebrate milestones within your community, no matter how small, as they reinforce bonds and shared purpose.
Cultivate gratitude for the blessings that emerge even in the hardest seasons of life.

title: Deaths of Early Companions and Polytheist Leaders in the First Year of Hijra
summary: Several notable figures, both Muslim Companions and polytheist leaders of Makkah, passed away in the first year of the Hijra, marking the close of an era.
story: Among the Companions who died that first year were Kulthiim b. al-Hadm al-Awsi, in whose home the Messenger of God (SAAS) stayed in QuhS' prior to his departure to the home of the Bana al-Najja, as told above. Later that same year Abii U m m a As'ad h. ZurBra, the leader of the Banii al-Najjar, died, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was still building the mosque. This has been related above. May God be pleased with both these deceased men. Ibn Jarir stated, "That year, the first of the hegira, Abii Whayha died on his property in Tz'if. Also al-Walid h. al-Mughira died, as did al-'A$ b. Wa'il al-Sahmi in Mecca." I observe that these men died while still polytheists. They did not accept God, the Almighty and Glorious.
lessons: Death comes to all people regardless of their status, wealth, or standing — a reminder to prioritize what truly matters.
The deaths of sincere believers who served the Prophet are remembered with honor, while those who rejected the truth passed away having squandered their opportunity.
Every era ends and transitions; loss, even of those who supported us, is part of the natural order and calls us to reflection.
practical_advice: Reflect regularly on your own legacy — how do you wish to be remembered, and are your actions aligned with that vision?
When grieving the loss of those who supported and sheltered you, honor their memory by continuing the good work they enabled.
Use awareness of mortality as motivation to live with intention and not delay acts of goodness or faith.

title: The Battle of Badr: The Great Distinction Between Truth and Falsehood
summary: The battle of Badr in the second year of Hijra was the most significant military event of early Islam, described as God's means of making a clear distinction between truth and falsehood.
story: An Account of what happened in the secondyear of the hegira. In this year many military exeditions took place, the most important and glorious of these being the great battle of Badr, which occurred in Ramadan. It was the battle that God used to make a clear distinction between truth and falsehood, right guidance and error.
lessons: Truth and falsehood inevitably come into clear confrontation; remaining steadfast on the side of truth requires courage and conviction.
Divine assistance comes to those who strive sincerely in righteous causes, even when the odds appear overwhelming.
Pivotal moments in history are often the result of long periods of patient endurance finally reaching their turning point.
practical_advice: When facing a decisive moment in your life, draw strength from the knowledge that standing for what is right, even when difficult, brings divine support.
Prepare thoroughly for challenges while placing your ultimate trust in God, knowing that the outcome rests with Him.
Reflect on moments when clarity came to you after confusion — use those as anchors when navigating new uncertainties.

title: Jewish Rabbis and Their Enmity Toward Islam
summary: Ibn Ishaq lists the Jewish rabbis and leaders of Madinah who displayed open hostility toward the Prophet and sought to undermine Islam through questions, doubts, and antagonism.
story: Imam Muhammad b. Ishaq b. Yasar stated the following in his work of biography, after giving information about the Jewish rabbis and their declaration of enmity against Islam and the verses making mention of them. Of these men was Huyayy b. Akhcab and his two brothers, Aba Yasir and Judayy. Also there were Sallam b. Mishkam, Kinana h. al-Rahic b. Abn al-Huqayq, Sallam b. Abii al-uuqayq, otherwise known as Aba W' al-Acwar. The latter was a trader of the H i j a and one who was killed by the Companions at Khaybar, as will be shown later. Also, there were 'Amr b. Jahhsh, Ka'b h. al-Ashraf, he being of Tali and of the Banii Nabha; his mother was of the Banii al-Nadir. He was killed by the Companions before Abii h f i c , as will be shown, along with his two allies al-Hajjaj b. Wmar and Kardam b. Qays. God curse them all! These men were from the Banii &Nadir. From the Bana Thalaba b. al-Fiyyawn there was 'Abd Allah b. Sciriya. There was no man in the Hijaz more learned in the Torah than he was. I would observe here that he is said to have accepted Islam. And there were Ibn Saliiba and Mukhayriq, the latter accepting Islam, as will be mentioned later, at the battle of Uhud; he had been his people's rabbi. From the Banii Qaynuqa'there was Zayd h. al-lasit, Sa'd b. Hanif, M + m d b. Sayha, Wzayz b. Abii Wzayz, 'Abd Allah b. Dayf, Suwayd b. al-Haith, Rifa'a b. Qays, Finhas, Ashya: N u W n b. Adz, B*ri b. 'Amr, Sha's b. 'Adi, Sba's b. Qays, Zayd b. al-Harith, NuCm?mb. 'Amr, Sukayn b. Abii Sukayn, 'Adi b. Zayd, Nucman b. Aba Awfa, Abii Anas, Mahmiid b. D&ya, Mdik b. Sayf, Ka'b b. h s h i d , 'Azir, Rgfi' b. Abii Rxfi', Khdid, and Azar b. Aba Az%. The lastmentioned should be named Azar b. Abii Azar according to Ibn Hishim. Also there were R%ficb. Haritha, Rxfi' b. Huraymala, &fi' b. Khaija, Malik b. 'Awf, Rif%'ab. Zayd b. al-Tabiit and 'Abd All& b. Salam. I observe that the last-mentioned had earlier accepted Islam, God he pleased with him. Ibn Ishaq stated, "He was their rabbi and most learned man. His name had been al-Husayn, and when he accepted Islam the Messenger of God (SAAS) named him 'Abd All&." Ibn Ishaq stated, "From the Bana Qurayza there were al-Zubayr b. Bat%b. Wahb, 'Azzd b. Shamwil, and Ka'b b. Asad. The last-mentioned was the one who had signed for his tribe the agreement they broke in the year of the ahzzb, the schisms. Also there were Shamwil b. Zayd, Jabal b. 'Amr b. Sukayna, al-N&hm b. Zayd, Kardam h. Ka'b, Wahb b. Zayd, Naficb. Abii Nafic, 'Adi b. Zayd, al-Harith b. 'Awf, Kardam b. Zayd, Umna b. Habib, hficb. Rnmayla, Jabal b. Abii Qushayr and Wahb b. Yahiidha. From the Banii Zurayq there was Labid b. A'sam, who had put a spell on the Messenger of God (SAAS). From the Jews of the Banii Haitha there was Kinana b. Siiriya. From the Jews of the Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf there was Qardam b. 'Amr. From the Jews of the Banii al-Najj% there was Silsila b. Barham." Ibn Ishaq stated, "All these men were rabbis and men of evil intent and full of antagonism towards the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his Companions, God be pleased with them. They were those who asked the Messenger of God (SAAS) many questions in their desire to confuse and to express their obstinacy and disbelief. They were men who made difficulties for Islam and who tried to extinguish it. Exceptions to this were 'Abd All& b. S h and Mukhayriq."
lessons: Knowledge and religious scholarship do not automatically lead to truth if the heart is closed; sincere seeking matters more than erudition alone.
Even within communities of opposition, there are always individuals who recognize the truth and have the courage to follow it.
The Prophet faced organized, sustained intellectual and social opposition and still persevered with patience and dignity.
practical_advice: When you face persistent opposition or attempts to undermine your convictions, remain grounded in your values rather than being destabilized by hostility.
Avoid judging an entire group by the actions of those most vocal in opposition; always remain open to encountering sincere individuals within any community.
Strengthen your own knowledge and faith so that cleverly framed doubts or provocative questions do not shake your sense of purpose.

title: Abd Allah ibn Salam: The Greatest Rabbi Embraces Islam
summary: Abd Allah ibn Salam, the most learned rabbi in the Hijaz, accepted Islam and was renamed by the Prophet, standing as a shining exception among those who opposed the message.
story: I observe that the last-mentioned had earlier accepted Islam, God he pleased with him. Ibn Ishaq stated, "He was their rabbi and most learned man. His name had been al-Husayn, and when he accepted Islam the Messenger of God (SAAS) named him 'Abd All&." Ibn Ishnq then related the acceptance of Islam by 'Abd Allah b. Salim, along with that of his aunt Khalida, as we mentioned above.
lessons: True knowledge, when pursued with sincerity, leads to recognizing the truth even when it requires leaving behind status and privilege.
Transformation is possible for anyone — the most entrenched scholar can become the most devoted believer when the heart opens.
The Prophet's practice of giving new names upon conversion symbolizes a new identity and a fresh beginning, affirming the person's new path.
practical_advice: Never let your current position, reputation, or community affiliation prevent you from following what your conscience tells you is true.
When you experience a profound change of heart or belief, embrace it fully and allow yourself to be transformed rather than clinging to your old identity.
Surround yourself with people who will support your growth and transformation, as Abd Allah found in the Prophet's community.

title: Mukhayriq the Rabbi: Sacrificing Everything for the Prophet at Uhud
summary: Mukhayriq, a Jewish rabbi and wealthy man, defied his people on the Sabbath to fight alongside the Prophet at the battle of Uhud, bequeathing all his wealth to Muhammad before dying in battle.
story: He also told of the conversion to Islam by Mukhayriq on the day of the battle of Uhud, details of which will come later. He told his people, it being a Sabbath, "0Jews, you well know that Muhammad has a right to expect your help." They replied, "But today is the Sabbath." He replied, "You shall have no Sabbath!" He then took his weapons and went off to battle. He made a charge upon his people who were staying behind, as follows, 'Tf I should be killed today, then my properties are to go to Muhammad to dispose of as God shows him." H e was a very wealthy man. "He then joined the Messenger of God (SAAS) and fought until he was killed. God be pleased with him." Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) used to say, as I have been told, 'Mukhayriq was the best Jew.'"
lessons: Moral courage sometimes means standing alone against your entire community in defense of what is right.
Generosity in the face of death — giving everything away before a final sacrifice — reflects the highest level of selflessness.
The Prophet's recognition of Mukhayriq as 'the best Jew' shows that sincere virtue is acknowledged regardless of background or origin.
practical_advice: When the moment comes to act on your deepest convictions, do not let social pressure, convention, or fear of community judgment stop you.
Consider what you would give, sacrifice, or stand up for if you knew it might be the last thing you do — let that clarity guide your priorities now.
Honor the sincere goodness in others regardless of their background, as the Prophet honored Mukhayriq's courage and generosity.

title: The Hypocrites of Aws and Khazraj: Julas ibn Suwayd's Slander and Repentance
summary: Julas ibn Suwayd, a hypocrite from Aws, spoke words of disbelief and was exposed by his own stepson, after which a Quranic verse was revealed about him, though he is said to have later repented sincerely.
story: Ibn Ishaq then Listed the names of those hypocrites from Aws and Khazraj, who joined the Jews antagonistic to Islam. From Aws there were: Zuwayy b. al-Harith, Julas b. Suwayd b. d-Samit, the Helper. It was about him that the verse was revealed, "They swear by God that they did not speak. But they did speak words of disbelief; they apostatized after accepting Islam" @rat al-Tamba or al-Barrt'a; IX, v.74). This was because he said, after having withdrawn from the expedition against Tabiik, "If this man speaks the truth, then we are worse than donkeys!" "The son of al-Julas's wife, a man named Wmayr b. Sa'd reported this to the Messenger of God (SAAS) but d-Julas denied it, swearing that he had never said that. The verse was therefore revealed concerning him." Ibn Ishnq went on, "It is claimed that he later nuly repented and that he was known for his piety."
lessons: Hypocrisy corrodes the soul from within; living a double life — outwardly professing faith while inwardly rejecting it — is spiritually destructive.
True repentance is always possible and can transform even one who was known for hypocrisy into one recognized for piety.
Divine revelation exposed what human testimony alone could not settle, a reminder that nothing is hidden from God.
practical_advice: Examine your own consistency — do your private thoughts and words align with your public professions of faith and values?
If you have acted hypocritically or said something harmful in a weak moment, do not let shame prevent you from genuinely repenting and changing.
When you witness wrongdoing or hypocrisy, consider the difficult but courageous act of speaking truth to those in authority, as Umayr ibn Sa'd did.

title: Al-Harith ibn Suwayd: The Hypocrite Who Killed Muslims at Uhud for Personal Revenge
summary: Al-Harith ibn Suwayd, outwardly fighting on the Muslim side at Uhud, betrayed the community by killing two Muslim soldiers to avenge his father's pre-Islamic death, then defected to the Quraysh.
story: "Also there was his brother al-Harith h. Suwayd; it was he who killed al-Mujadhdhar b. Dhiyzd d-Balawi and Q y s b. Zayd, one of the Banii Dubay'a at the battle of Uhud. Al-Harith had gone to war on the Muslims' side, but he was a hypocrite and when the battle was joined he fought and killed both these men and joined the Quraysh forces." Ibn Hisham stated, "Al-Mujadhdhar had killed his father Suwayd b. al-Smit in an engagement before the coming of Islam. Al-Hi%rith was taking revenge upon h i at the battle of Uhud."
lessons: Unresolved grudges and old wounds, if left to fester, can corrupt a person's judgment and lead them to devastating betrayal.
Outward participation in a righteous cause means nothing if the inner motivation is self-serving or driven by vengeance.
The danger of hypocrisy in a community is not just spiritual but existential — it can cause real harm from within.
practical_advice: Identify any long-held grievances or wounds from the past that may be shaping your decisions in the present and seek to genuinely resolve them.
Be honest with yourself about your motivations — are you engaging in good works out of sincere commitment, or are you using a righteous platform to serve a private agenda?
Cultivate genuine forgiveness for past wrongs not only as a moral ideal but as a practical protection against allowing bitterness to govern your actions.

title: Al-Harith's Apostasy and Quranic Revelation
summary: Al-Harith, a hypocrite who fought on the Muslims' side at Uhud, killed two Muslims and joined the Quraysh forces, prompting a Quranic verse about apostasy after belief.
story: Al-Harith had gone to war on the Muslims' side, but he was a hypocrite and when the battle was joined he fought and killed both these men and joined the Quraysh forces. Al-Mujadhdhar had killed his father Suwayd b. al-Samit in an engagement before the coming of Islam. Al-Harith was taking revenge upon him at the battle of Uhud. The Messenger of God (SAAS) had ordered 'Umar b. al-Khattab to kill him if he could catch him. Al-Harith, however, sent a message to his brother al-Juhs seeking forgiveness so that he could return to his people. And so it was, as I have been informed on the authority of Ibn 'Abbas, that God revealed, 'How could God lead a people aright who have apostatized after having believed and given testimony that the Messenger is the truth, and after they had received proofs? God does not guide a people who are unjust' (surat Al-'Imran; III, v.86).
lessons: Betrayal of one's community after sincere commitment carries profound spiritual consequences, as reflected in this Quranic revelation.
Personal grievances and desires for revenge can corrupt one's spiritual path if they override commitment to truth and faith.
Even after serious transgression, seeking forgiveness and reconciliation with one's community remains important, as Al-Harith's message to his brother shows a remnant of conscience.
practical_advice: When you feel pulled between personal loyalty and a broader commitment, pause to examine whether revenge or grudge is driving your choices rather than principle.
If you have acted against your values or community, take the step of seeking reconciliation rather than remaining in a state of separation.
Reflect on what beliefs and commitments you hold sincerely versus those you perform outwardly, and work to align your inner and outer life.

title: Nabtal ibn al-Harith: The Spy Among the Hypocrites
summary: The Prophet described Nabtal ibn al-Harith, a hypocrite who spied on the Prophet's words and reported them to enemies, and a Quranic verse was revealed about him.
story: Also there were Bijad b. 'Uthman b. 'Amir and Nabtal b. al-Harith. It was about the latter that the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Whoever would like to see Satan, let him look at this man!' He was a very tall, black man with a mass of hair, red eyes and flushed cheeks. He used to listen to what the Messenger of God (SAAS) said then report on it to the hypocrites. It was he who said, 'Muhammad is just ears; he believes what anyone says to him!' God revealed concerning him, 'And among them are those who harm the Prophet by saying that he is ears' (surat al-Tawba or al-Bara'a; IX, v.61).
lessons: Those who spread misinformation and slander about righteous leaders ultimately expose their own spiritual corruption.
Listening carefully to truth and conveying it faithfully is a virtue, not a weakness — the Prophet's openness to people was a mercy, not a flaw.
God's awareness of hidden actions and words provides assurance that injustice done in secret will be addressed.
practical_advice: When others mock your openness or trust as naivety, recognize that these are virtues — be discerning but do not harden your heart out of fear of being deceived.
If you suspect someone around you is acting in bad faith, seek clarity through observation over time rather than immediate confrontation.
Guard your tongue from mocking or belittling those who sincerely pursue goodness, as such mockery reflects poorly on one's own character.

title: Mu'attib ibn Qushayr's Complaints at Uhud and al-Ahzab
summary: The hypocrite Mu'attib ibn Qushayr expressed doubt and complaint at two pivotal battles, and Quranic verses were revealed rebuking his faithlessness.
story: It was Mu'attib who said at the battle of Uhud, 'If we had any say in it, we'd not be killed here!' A verse was then revealed about him. It was also he who said, at the battle of al-Ahzab, the 'schisms', 'Muhammad used to promise us we would enjoy the treasures of Chosroe and Caesar, but we're not safe to go to the toilet!' The verse revealed about him was 'and when the hypocrites, and those in whose breasts is disease, say, "All that God and His Messenger have promised us is mere fancy"' (surat al-Ahzab; XXXIII, v.12).
lessons: Doubt in God's promises often peaks precisely during the most difficult trials — this is when faith is most tested and most needed.
Complaining about hardship while abandoning one's commitment does not remove the hardship but deepens one's spiritual loss.
The promises of God are fulfilled on God's timeline, not on the timeline of human comfort and convenience.
practical_advice: When you face hardship and begin to doubt whether your sacrifices are worth it, write down the promises you believe in and why you chose your path — return to these during moments of doubt.
Notice when anxiety is causing you to catastrophize, as Mu'attib did, and practice grounding yourself in what you know to be true rather than what you fear.
Resist the urge to voice cynicism about your community's shared goals during moments of collective stress, as it spreads despair to others who are also struggling.

title: Tha'laba and Mu'attib's Broken Promise to God
summary: Tha'laba b. Hatib and Mu'attib b. Qushayr promised God that if given wealth they would believe and give charity, but broke their promise, and a Quranic verse was revealed about them.
story: And there was Abu Habiba b. al-Az'ar. He was one of those who built the mosque at al-Dira. Also there were Tha'laba b. Hatib and Mu'attib b. Qushayr. It was these two who promised God that if He gave them of His bounty they would believe. They, however, broke their promise upon which the above was revealed.
lessons: Promises made to God carry a spiritual weight that cannot be ignored — breaking them damages one's integrity and relationship with the divine.
Prosperity and ease can sometimes be a greater test of faith than hardship, as comfort may breed complacency and broken commitments.
Making sincere vows requires the genuine intention and preparation to fulfill them, not merely wishful thinking in a moment of need.
practical_advice: Before making a spiritual or personal commitment, honestly assess whether you have the will and means to follow through, rather than making promises in emotional moments.
If you have broken a commitment — to God, to others, or to yourself — acknowledge it openly and make a concrete plan to repair it rather than suppressing the guilt.
Use moments of abundance and ease as opportunities to deepen your commitments, not to relax them.

title: The Mosque of Dirar: A Place of Harm Built in Deception
summary: A group of hypocrites built a mosque called Masjid al-Dirar as a place of harm and division, using it to undermine the Muslim community, and it was eventually destroyed.
story: And there was Abu Habiba b. al-Az'ar. He was one of those who built the mosque at al-Dira. Also there was 'Abbad b. Hunayf, brother of Sahl b. Hunayf, and Bahaj, who participated in building the mosque, al-Dira. And there were 'Amr b. Khidham, 'Abd Allah b. Nabtal, Jaria b. 'Amir b. al-'Ataf and his two sons Yazid and Mujmi'; also involved in the construction of the Dirar mosque. Mujmi' was a young man who had collected most parts of the Qur'an and would pray with the others in prayer there. When the Dirar mosque was destroyed, after the Tabuk expedition, as will be explained hereafter, during the reign of 'Umar, the people of Quba' asked the Caliph whether Mujmi' could lead them in prayer. He refused, saying, 'By God, was he not the Imam of the hypocrites in the Dirar mosque?' But Mujmi' swore by God that he knew nothing about them; and they say that 'Umar let him go and that he did lead them in prayer. And there was Wadi'a b. Thabit, also one of those who built the Dirar mosque. It was he who said, 'But we're only being sociable and having fun.' And a reference was made in the Qur'an to that. And there was Khidan b. Khalid; it was he from whose house the Dirar mosque was made.
lessons: Outward acts of piety can mask destructive intentions — sincerity of purpose is what makes worship and community-building genuinely beneficial.
Leadership and religious authority carry responsibility that extends beyond ritual knowledge, requiring integrity of character and alignment of inner and outer conduct.
Even those associated with wrongdoing may be innocent — 'Umar's eventual acceptance of Mujmi''s oath reflects the importance of giving individuals the opportunity to clear their name.
practical_advice: When evaluating institutions or groups, examine not just their stated purposes but whether their actual effects are building trust and community or sowing division.
If you find yourself associated with people or projects that conflict with your values, take clear steps to distance yourself and clarify your own position honestly.
When someone's past associations cast doubt on their integrity, allow them the opportunity to explain themselves before rendering judgment.

title: Mirbac ibn Qayza: The Blind Hypocrite Who Confronted the Prophet
summary: The blind hypocrite Mirbac ibn Qayza refused to let the Prophet pass through his garden on the way to Uhud, threw dirt at him, and was rebuked by the Prophet as blind in heart as well as sight.
story: And there was Mirbac b. Qayza; he was blind. It was he who said to the Messenger of God (SAAS), who was crossing through his garden on the way to the battle of Uhud, 'I'll not allow you, even if you are a prophet, to pass through my garden!' And he picked up a handful of dirt and said, 'I swear, if I knew I would not strike anyone but yourself, I'd throw this at you!' The Muslims made for him to kill him, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Leave him alone. This blind man is blind in his heart as well as in his sight.' Sa'd b. Zayd al-Ashhal, however, had already injured him with his bow.
lessons: The Prophet's restraint in response to personal insult and provocation stands as a profound model of emotional intelligence and mercy.
Physical sight is secondary to spiritual insight — a person can perceive the world clearly yet be utterly blind to truth and goodness.
Hostility toward truth often intensifies precisely when truth is nearest, as Mirbac's aggression came when the Prophet was literally passing by.
practical_advice: When someone responds to your goodness or good intentions with hostility, practice the Prophet's response: do not retaliate, but calmly note that their aggression reflects their own inner state.
Reflect regularly on whether you have any 'blind spots' — areas of your life where you refuse to see truth despite it being right in front of you.
In moments of anger, ask yourself whether your reaction is proportionate to what actually happened, or whether deeper fears and biases are driving your response.

title: Yazid ibn Haqiq: A Righteous Son of a Hypocrite Father
summary: Yazid ibn Haqiq, a good Muslim severely wounded at Uhud, was mocked by his own hypocrite father as he lay dying while the Muslim community gathered to comfort him with glad tidings of paradise.
story: And there was Hatib b. Umayya b. Rafi'. He was a powerful sheikh who was long set in the ways of pre-Islamic ignorance. He had a son who was among the best of Muslims. His name was Yazid b. Haqiq, and he was so injured at the battle of Uhud that his wounds disabled him and he was borne to the home of the Banu Zafar. 'Asma' b. 'Umar b. Qatada related to me that the Muslims there, both men and women, gathered around him while he was dying and began telling him, 'Rejoice in the gardens of paradise, Ibn Haqiq!' His father's hypocrisy then emerged and he began saying, 'Right! Gardens of rue! You have, by God, deluded this poor wretch about himself!'
lessons: A person's spiritual character is their own — the corruption of a parent does not determine the destiny of a child who chooses righteousness.
The community's role in surrounding a dying believer with comfort and hope is a profound act of mercy that honors both the individual and the faith.
Cynicism about the afterlife, especially when directed at the dying, reveals a deep spiritual emptiness in the one who speaks it.
practical_advice: If you come from a difficult or dysfunctional family environment, recognize that your spiritual journey is your own — you are not bound by the choices or character of those who raised you.
When a friend or loved one is suffering, show up and offer genuine words of hope and comfort rather than remaining distant out of discomfort with grief.
When someone mocks your faith or hope, do not let their cynicism take root in you — trace their words back to their source and choose consciously whether that source deserves your belief.

title: Bashir ibn Ubayriq and the Stolen Breastplates
summary: The hypocrite Bashir ibn Ubayriq stole two breastplates, and a Quranic verse was revealed warning against arguing in defense of those who are treacherous to themselves.
story: And there was Bashir b. Ubayriq Abu Tu'ma, the man who stole the two breast-plates. It was of him that God spoke in the words, 'Do not argue for those who are treacherous to themselves' (surat al-Nisa'; IV, v.107).
lessons: Those who steal and then rely on others to cover for them bring harm to both themselves and to those who defend them.
Enabling dishonesty by arguing on behalf of known wrongdoers is itself a form of moral complicity.
Treachery against others ultimately begins as treachery against oneself, as the Quranic verse reveals.
practical_advice: When you are tempted to defend someone whose wrongdoing you know about, ask yourself whether you are serving justice or merely protecting your relationship with them.
If you have taken something that was not yours — whether material or otherwise — the path to inner peace runs through returning it and seeking forgiveness, not through denial.
Reflect on whether there are ways in which you have been 'treacherous to yourself' — compromising your own values in ways that erode your self-respect.

title: Quzman: The Fighter Who Denied Fighting for Islam
summary: Quzman killed seven men at Uhud but when wounded killed himself, declaring he had only fought for his people and not for Islam, revealing that outward valor means nothing without sincere intention.
story: And there was Quzman, the ally of the Banu Zufr, who killed seven men at Uhud and then when in pain from his own wounds, killed himself, saying, 'I swear, I only fought in defence of my people!' He then died, God damn him!
lessons: Outward heroism and even great sacrifice mean nothing spiritually if they are not rooted in sincere intention — action is ultimately judged by the heart behind it.
The end of a person's story can reveal truths that their impressive deeds had concealed — character is tested most severely at moments of suffering.
Tribal loyalty and communal pride are not substitutes for genuine faith and cannot provide the inner resources needed to endure the deepest trials.
practical_advice: Regularly examine the intentions behind your most effortful actions — are you doing them for an audience, out of habit, or out of genuine conviction?
When you face physical or emotional pain, notice what values and beliefs your mind returns to — these reveal what you truly live for.
Do not allow the admiration of others for your achievements to substitute for your own honest self-evaluation of why you do what you do.

title: Abdullah ibn Ubayy: The Chief of the Hypocrites
summary: Abdullah ibn Ubayy, who had been on the verge of becoming king of Medina before Islam, became the chief hypocrite and led many in secret opposition to the Prophet and the Muslim community.
story: And there was 'Abd Allah b. Ubayy b. Salul who was the chief hypocrite and leader of the Aws and Khazraj as well. They had previously agreed, before the coming of Islam, to appoint him their king, but when God led them to Islam before they did so, the accursed fellow was greatly offended and angered. It is he who said, 'If we get back to Medina the strong will definitely drive out of it the weak!' Very many verses were revealed about this man and also about Wadi', a man of the Banu 'Awf, as about Malik b. Abi Qawqal, Suwayd and Da'is who were all of his gang. About them the Almighty revealed, 'If they are driven out, they will not go forth with them' (surat al-Hashr; LIX, v.12). This was when they allied secretly with the Banu al-Nadir.
lessons: Wounded pride and thwarted ambition can fester into a lifelong resentment that poisons one's relationship with truth and community.
Those who have power over others through social status are especially susceptible to resisting any new authority that diminishes their influence.
Secret alliances formed out of bitterness and ambition ultimately weaken those who form them, as the Quranic verse about their abandonment reveals.
practical_advice: If you have lost a position, role, or status you deeply wanted, give yourself time to grieve it honestly rather than suppressing the loss — unprocessed disappointment often emerges as bitterness.
Examine whether any of your current relationships or associations are driven more by shared resentment than by shared values.
When you feel the impulse to undermine someone in a position of authority, ask yourself honestly whether your objection is principled or personal.

title: Zayd ibn al-Lasit Mocks the Prophet Over a Strayed Camel
summary: The hypocrite Zayd ibn al-Lasit mocked the Prophet for not knowing where his strayed camel was, but the Prophet was divinely guided to its exact location, vindicating his prophethood.
story: It was the latter who commented, when the camel of the Messenger of God (SAAS) strayed, 'Muhammad claims that information reaches him from heaven, yet he doesn't know the whereabouts of his camel!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'I only know what God tells me; and He has guided me towards it. My camel is in this defile, her bridle caught in a tree.' Some of the Muslims then went and found it just so.
lessons: Divine guidance operates on its own terms and timeline — the Prophet's knowledge came through revelation, not omniscience, and he was transparent about this distinction.
Those who mock faith using trivial, practical objections often reveal that their real resistance is not intellectual but emotional.
Small, verifiable moments of truth — like finding a camel exactly where the Prophet described — can powerfully affirm faith for those with open hearts.
practical_advice: When people challenge your beliefs with dismissive or mocking arguments, respond calmly with what you actually know and believe rather than becoming defensive.
Notice when your own doubts are driven by a desire to undermine something rather than a genuine search for understanding.
Practice intellectual humility by distinguishing clearly between what you know, what you believe, and what you are uncertain about.

title: Rifa'a ibn Zayd and the Wind at His Death
summary: When the great hypocrite Rifa'a ibn Zayd died, a strong wind arose as the Prophet returned from Tabuk, and the Prophet declared it was due to the death of a great unbeliever, which was confirmed on their arrival in Medina.
story: Also there was Rifa'a b. Zayd b. al-Tahit. On the day of his death a great wind arose just as the Messenger of God (SAAS) was returning from Tabuk and he said, 'It is blowing due to the death of a great unbeliever.' And when they reached Medina they found that Rifa'a had died that day.
lessons: The Prophet's awareness of spiritual realities beyond the visible world is demonstrated in small, precise observations like this one.
The death of those who spread corruption and division in a community can paradoxically mark the beginning of relief and healing.
Every life has a weight in the spiritual order — those who use their lives against truth leave a mark upon the world even at their departure.
practical_advice: Pay attention to the way events in your community shift when toxic influences are removed — this awareness can help you understand what was weighing on the collective spirit.
Do not underestimate the cumulative effect that dishonest or divisive people have on those around them — address such dynamics early.
Reflect on what kind of spiritual atmosphere you create around you through your daily choices and words.

title: Hypocrites Expelled from the Mosque
summary: The Prophet ordered hypocrites who were gathering in the mosque to mock and undermine the Muslims to be physically removed, and companions like Abu Ayyub carried this out forcibly.
story: These hypocrites would attend the mosque and listen to the Muslims' talk, ridiculing and making fun of their religion. Some of them gathered in the mosque one day and the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw them talking among themselves, sitting together and speaking in low voices. The Messenger of God (SAAS) ordered that they be removed from the mosque and they were, forcibly. Abu Ayyub went up to 'Amr b. Qays, one of the Banu al-Najjar, who had been the guardian of their gods in the pre-Islamic era, took him by the foot and pulled him until he had got him outside, while the other man said, 'God damn! Would you evict me, Abu Ayyub, from the date shed of the Banu Tha'laba?' Abu Ayyub then went up to Rafi' b. Wadi'a al-Najjar and grabbed him by the cloak, pulled at him hard, slapped his face and threw him out of the mosque, saying, 'Yekh! You dirty hypocrite!' 'Umar b. Hazm seized Zayd b. 'Amr, who had a long beard, and pulled him along by it until...
lessons: Sacred spaces must be protected from those who enter them not to seek truth but to undermine and mock — boundaries around communal worship are legitimate and necessary.
The companions' immediate and direct action in defense of their community's integrity reflects the courage that sincere faith cultivates.
Those who infiltrate communities they despise in order to gather information and sow doubt ultimately face exposure and removal.
practical_advice: If someone in your community, workplace, or family consistently undermines shared values while pretending to belong, address the behavior directly rather than tolerating it indefinitely out of politeness.
Protect the spaces — physical, emotional, and communal — where you and others seek peace and meaning; not everyone who enters them does so in good faith.
When you feel mocked or dismissed for your beliefs in a space that should be safe, advocate for yourself and your community with clarity and firmness.

title: Expulsion of Hypocrites from the Mosque
summary: The companions of the Prophet forcibly expelled the hypocrites from the mosque after revelations exposed their hypocrisy, with each companion confronting and removing specific individuals in a dramatic series of encounters.
story: gods in the pre-Islamic era, took him by the foot and pulled him until he had got him outside, while the other man said, 'God damn! Would you evict me, Abii Ayyiib, from the date shed of the Bann Thaclaha?' "Abii Ayyab then went up to &fiCb. Wadi'a a l - N a j j ~and grabbed him by the cloak, pulled at him hard, slapped his face and threw him out of the mosque, saying, 'Yekh! You dirty hypocrite!' "'Umaa b. Hazm seized Zayd h. CAmr, who had a long beard, and pulled h i along by it until he had evicted him from the mosque. 'Umara then clenched his fists and hit him with them hard in his chest, knocking him down. Zayd complained, "Umzra, you've grazed me!' At this W m a a replied, 'Clear off with you, hypocrite! What God has in store for you is far worse! Don't ever come near the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS) again!' "Abii Muhammad Mascod b. Aws b. Zayd b. Asram b. Zayd h. Tha'laba b. Ghanm b. Malik b. al-Najjar, who fought at Badr, went up to Qays b. 'Amr h. Sahl, who was just a lad, the only youth among the hypocrites. Abii Muhammad pushed Qays by the back of his neck till he had evicted him. "One of the Bana Khudra approached a man called al-Harith b. 'Amr who wore his hair thick, seized him by it and pulled h i violently across the ground until he had evicted him. At this the hypocrite yelled, 'You're being very rough, Aha al-Harith!' 'You deserve it,' he was told, 'you enemy of God you, for what has been revealed about you. Don't you come near the mosque of the Messenger of God (SAAS) again; you're filth!' "A man of the Banii 'Amr b. 'Awf went for the previous man's brother, Zuway b. al-Herith and forcibly evicted him, expressing his disgust for him and saying, 'Satan and his work have charge of you!'" Ibn Ishaq then referred to the passages in sdirat al-Baqara (Qur'an, 11) and in sarat al-Tmba (Qur'm, I X ) that refer to the hypocrites and offered full and effective explanations of these.
lessons: Hypocrisy that hides within sacred spaces is ultimately exposed — sincerity of heart cannot be indefinitely concealed from a community of faith.
Divine revelation provided the community with clarity and discernment, reminding believers that God's knowledge encompasses what people conceal.
The companions' decisive response shows that protecting the sanctity of communal worship spaces is a shared responsibility of the faithful.
practical_advice: When you sense insincerity or hidden negativity in your environment, address it directly but with clarity of purpose rather than personal vendetta.
Reflect on whether your own intentions in acts of worship and community are genuine — hypocrisy begins subtly and grows when left unexamined.
Surround yourself with communities that hold shared values accountable, as righteous peer accountability strengthens collective integrity.

title: Counting the Military Expeditions of the Prophet
summary: Various scholars and narrators recorded differing accounts of the total number of military expeditions and raids organized by the Prophet, ranging from 17 to over 100, reflecting the breadth and complexity of early Islamic military history.
story: An Account of thejrst military expedition, that of al-Abw2, also called Waddzn, and thejrst of the delegations sent. This involved the dispatch of Hamza b. 'Abd al-Mutcalib or Wbayda b. al-H~rith,as will be explained regarding the military expeditions. Al-Bukhari's text reads as follows: Book of Nie M~litay Exfeditions. Ibn Ishaq stated, "The first battle fought by the Messenger of God (SAAS) was that of al-Ahwa', followed by that of Buwq, followed by that of al-'Ushayra." He then related how Zayd b. Arqam was asked how many military expeditions the Messenger of God (SAAS) had sent. He replied, 'LTherewere 19, in 17 of which he participated; the &st of these was to al-Wsayra (or al-Wshayra).' Al-Bukhari stated, in his jahih collection from Burayda, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) set in motion 16 military expeditions." Muslim quoted Burayda as saying that he accompanied the Messenger of God (SAAS) on 16 expeditions. He also quotes him as saying that the Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 19 expeditions and himself fought in 8 of them. Al-Husayn b. Waqid stated, from Ibn Burayda, from his father, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 17 expeditions and fought in 8, those at Badr, Uhud, al-Ahzah, al-Muraysi: Qudayd, Khaybar, Mecca and Hunayn, and that he sent out 24 raids. Ya'qiib b. Sufyan stated, "Muhammad h. Wthmm al-Dimashqi al-Taniikhi related to us, quoting al-Haytham b. Humayd, quoting al-Nu'm from Makhal, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 18 expeditions and himself fought in 8. The first of these was at Badr, followed by m u d , al-AbSb, Qurayza, Bilr Ma'iina, that against the Banii al-Mugaliq of Khm'a, Khaybar, Mecca, Hunayn and al-Tx'if." His placing that of Bi'r Ma'iina after that of Qurayza is open to dispute. The truth is that it followed Uhud, as will be shown. Ya'qiib stated that Salama b. Shabih related to him, from 'Abd al-Razzaq, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, (who stated) "I heard Sa'id b. al-Musayyab say, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 18 expeditions.' But on another occasion I heard him say 24. I don't know whether this was mere fancy or something he heard later." Al-Tabrmi recounted from al-Dabari, from 'Abd al-Razz~q, from Ma'mar, from al-Zuhri, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 24 expeditions." 'Abd al-Rahman h. Humayd stated in his hadith collection, "Sa'id b. Sallam related to us, quoting Zakariyyal h. Ishaq and Aba al-Zubayr, from Jabir, who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) organized 21 expeditions.'" Al-H&im recounted, through Hishm, from Qatsda, that the expeditions and night raids organized by the Messenger of God (SAAS) totalled 43. Al-Hakim then commented, "Perhaps he meant raids apart from the military expeditions, for in the work al-Iklil (Three Bnght Stars) more than 100 raids of the Messenger of God (SAAS) are arranged in order." He went on, "A reliable scholar of Bukhaa told me he had read in the work of Abii 'Abd Allah Muhammad b. Nasr that the raids and delegations sent out totalled some 70, not counting the actual battles." This information related by al-H&im is very strange; and the statements he attributes in them to Q t a d a are controversial. Imam Ahmad recounted, from Azhar b. al-@sim al-msibi, from Hisham al-Dastuwa'i, from Qatada, that the military expeditions and raids of the Messenger of God (SAAS) totalled 43, consisting of 24 missions and 19 military expeditions. In 8 of these he himself participated, at Badr, Uhud, al-Ahzab, al-Muraysic, Khaybar, the conquest of Mecca, Hunayn, and al-Ta'if. Miisa b. 'Uqba quoted al-Zuhri as stating, "These were the military expeditions in which the Messenger of God (SAAS) fought: Badr in Ramada of 2 AH; Uhud in Shawwal, 3 AH; and al-khanduq, which was also known as al-Af?zaband the 'Banii Qurayza battle', in Shawwzl, 4 AH. He then fought the Banii al-Mus~aliqand the Banii Lihym in Sha'bm, 5 AH; at Khaybar in 6 AH; at the Yawm al-Fath (Mecca) in Ramadan, 8 AH. Thereafter he fought at al-Hunayn and besieged the people of al-Talif in Shaww~l, 8 AH. Then, in 9 AH Abii Bakr performed the pilgrimage, and in 10 AH the Messenger of God (SAAS) went on the so-called h y a t al-ma&, the 'farewell pilgrimage'. He organized 12 expeditions that did nor result in fighting. The first of his expeditions was that of al-Abwa'."
lessons: The variation among scholars in recording historical details demonstrates that honest scholarly inquiry embraces uncertainty rather than fabricating false precision.
The sheer number of expeditions the Prophet undertook reflects extraordinary dedication and perseverance in the face of persistent opposition — a model of endurance.
Islamic scholarship has always valued chains of transmission and critical analysis, reminding us that truth-seeking requires diligence, not shortcuts.
practical_advice: When you encounter conflicting information about an important topic, rather than becoming paralyzed by confusion, seek out reliable primary sources and scholars with sound methodology.
Recognize that great leaders and great causes require sustained, long-term effort — not a single decisive moment — and pace your own commitments accordingly.
If you feel overwhelmed by the complexity of a subject you are studying, break it into chronological or categorical segments, as scholars of Seerah did, to make it more manageable.

title: The Importance of Knowing the Prophet's Military Expeditions
summary: Early Muslim scholars emphasized that knowledge of the Prophet's military expeditions was taught like chapters of the Quran, as it contained wisdom about both this world and the hereafter.
story: This is an area of expertise requiring care and proper training; as Muhammad b. Wmar al-Waqidi related, from 'Abd Allah b. Wmar b. 'Ah, from his father, who said, "I heard CAli b. al-Husayn say, 'We used to teach the military expeditions of the Prophet (SAAS) just as we would chapters from the Qnr'an.'" Al-Waqidi stated, "I heard Muhammad b. CAbd Allah say, 'I heard my uncle al-Zuhri say, "In knowledge of the military expeditions there is knowledge of the hereafter as well as of this world."'"
lessons: The Prophet's life, including his struggles and military efforts, is a comprehensive source of guidance that encompasses both spiritual and worldly wisdom.
The early Muslims honored the Prophet's biography with the same reverence as scripture, reminding us that his example is foundational to Islamic practice and identity.
True knowledge is not merely academic — it carries within it lessons for living righteously and navigating the challenges of life.
practical_advice: Dedicate time regularly to studying the Seerah of the Prophet with the same intentionality you would bring to studying the Quran, approaching it as a living guide for your circumstances.
When you feel lost or directionless, return to the life of the Prophet as a source of practical and spiritual orientation — his responses to adversity mirror challenges we all face.
Share knowledge of the Prophet's life with others in your community, as the early Muslims did, treating it as a collective inheritance worth preserving and transmitting.

title: The Prophet's Arrival in Madinah and Preparation for Battle
summary: After settling in Madinah for approximately twelve months, the Prophet prepared to engage his enemies in battle as commanded by God, marking the transition from a period of establishment to one of active defense.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq gave further information concerning the military expeditions after having given that we have quoted above. In this he named the chief unbelievers from among the Jews and the hypocrites, God damn them all and gather them in the depths of hell. He stated, "Then the Messenger of God (SAAS) prepared to do battle against them, to wage war and fight against his enemies and their allies as God had ordered him. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) had arrived in Medina on a Monday when it was very hot, and the sun was near its zenith; it was the 12th of Rahical-Awwal. At that time he was 53 years old, and God had given him his mission 13 years before. He stayed there for the remainder of Rabi' al-Awwal and for Rabi' al-Akhir, for both Jumadas, Rajab, Sha'bm, Ramadm, Shawwd, Dhii al-Q'da, Dhn al-Hijja, the polytheists being in charge of the pilgrimage that month, and al-Muharram. He then, in Safar, at the start of the 12th month after his arrival there, went forth to do battle." Ibn Hishxm stated, "He left Sa'd h. Wbada in charge of Medina."
lessons: The Prophet spent a full year in Madinah building community, establishing trust, and consolidating resources before taking any military action — a lesson in the wisdom of patient preparation.
Even the greatest of leaders delegate responsibility to trusted companions when they must be absent, showing that building capable people around you is part of leadership.
Permission to fight came from God after years of suffering persecution — illustrating that there is a proper time and season for every action, and acting prematurely undermines the mission.
practical_advice: Before taking any major action in your life — whether a new project, a confrontation, or a significant change — invest adequate time in preparation, relationship-building, and grounding yourself in your values.
Identify trustworthy people in your life to whom you can delegate responsibilities when you are stretched, and actively develop their capacity rather than trying to do everything yourself.
When facing a difficult or long-delayed challenge, remember that timing matters — patience in waiting for the right moment is not weakness but strategic wisdom.

title: The Expedition to al-Abwa (Waddan) — The First Military Expedition
summary: The Prophet led the first military expedition to al-Abwa, also known as Waddan, targeting the Quraysh and the Banu Damra, who subsequently made a peace treaty with the Muslims without any battle taking place.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "He continued as far as Waddan; this was the expedition to al-Abw2." Ibn Jarir commented, "It is also known as the Waddm expedition." Ibn Ishaq continued, "He was heading for Quraysh and the Banii Damra b. Bakr b. 'Abd Manat b. Kinma. The Bann Damra made peace with him there; the one who actually did this was Makhshi b. 'Amr al-Damri, who was their leader at that time. "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then returned to Medina without having engaged in battle, remaining there for the rest of Safar and the early part of Rabic al-Awwal." Ibn H i s h b added, "This was the &st expedition made by the Messenger of God (SAAS)." Al-Waqidi commented, "His banner was entrusted to his uncle Hamza; it was white."
lessons: The first expedition of the Prophet ended in a peace treaty rather than battle, demonstrating that the goal was always security and justice, not conquest for its own sake.
Even in moments of potential conflict, open doors for peaceful resolution — the Banu Damra's willingness to negotiate and the Prophet's acceptance shows that dialogue can prevent bloodshed.
Entrusting honored companions like Hamza with the banner of leadership reflects the importance of recognizing and utilizing the strengths of those around you.
practical_advice: When approaching a conflict or confrontation, go prepared but remain genuinely open to a peaceful resolution — the willingness to accept an honorable agreement is a sign of strength, not weakness.
In your own leadership roles, visibly honor and empower trusted people around you, as the Prophet did with Hamza, so that your team feels recognized and invested in the mission.
Do not measure the success of an effort only by whether a dramatic outcome occurred — a peaceful resolution achieved through presence and readiness is often the best possible result.

title: The Expedition of Ubayda ibn al-Harith and the First Arrow Shot in Islam
summary: The Prophet dispatched Ubayda ibn al-Harith with sixty to eighty mounted Emigrants who encountered a large Qurayshi force near a well in Hijaz; though no full battle occurred, Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas shot the first arrow ever fired in the cause of Islam.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, "While resident then at Medina, the Messenger of God (SAAS) dispatched Wbayda b. al-Haith b. al-Mucmlib b. 'Abd Manaf b. Qu~ayyalong with 60 - or 80 -men, mounted and of the Emigrants; not one of them was a Helper. The party went as far as a well in Hijaz below Thaniyyat al-Murra, where he met a large contingent of Quraysh. There was no engagement with them, however, except that Sa'd b. Aha Waqqas did that day cast one arrow. That was the first arrow shot for God's cause after the coming of Islam. "The two forces separated thereafter, the Muslims positioning a rear-guard. Miqdad b. 'Amr al-Bahrmi, an ally of the Bann Zuhra, fled from the polytheists to the Muslims, along with Wtba b. Ghazwm b. Jahir al-Mzzini, an ally of the Banii Nawfal b. 'Ahd Manaf. These were really Muslims, but they had gone forth to have contact with the unbelievers." Ihn Ishaq continued, "'Ikrima b. Aha Jahl was leader of the polytheists on that occasion."
lessons: Even a single act of courage — like Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas's lone arrow — can mark a historic turning point; do not underestimate the significance of small but sincere acts of commitment.
The two men who defected from the polytheist side to the Muslims illustrate that people trapped in harmful environments will find their way to truth when given the opportunity.
Maintaining a rear-guard as the Muslims retreated shows that disciplined, thoughtful disengagement is as important as bold advance — wisdom knows when to hold and when to withdraw.
practical_advice: Recognize that your first step of courage in a difficult situation — even a small one — may be the most historically significant thing you do. Do not wait for a grand opportunity to act with integrity.
If you are currently in an environment or relationship that conflicts with your values, look for the moment to align yourself with what you truly believe, as Miqdad and Utba did.
Practice strategic withdrawal in conflicts — not every confrontation needs to be pressed to its conclusion. Sometimes a disciplined retreat preserves the mission better than a reckless advance.

title: The First Arrow Shot in Islam: Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas at the Expedition of Ubayda b. al-Harith
summary: Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas is credited with shooting the first arrow for God's cause after the coming of Islam, during the early expedition led by Ubayda b. al-Harith, an event commemorated in verses attributed to Sa'd.
story: arrow shot for God's cause after the coming of Islam. "The two forces separated thereafter, the Muslims positioning a rear-guard. Miqdad b. 'Amr al-Bahrani, an ally of the Bann Zuhra, fled from the polytheists to the Muslims, along with Wtba b. Ghazwm b. Jahir al-Mzzini, an ally of the Banii Nawfal b. 'Abd Manaf. These were really Muslims, but they had gone forth to have contact with the unbelievers." Ibn Ishaq continued, "'Ikrima b. Abi Jahl was leader of the polytheists on that occasion." Ibn Hisham related, from Ibn Abi 'Amr b. al-'Ala', that Abu 'Amr al-Madani said, "It was Mikraz b. Hafs who led them." I observe that al-Waqidi's account, heretofore, gives two statements, one that the leader was Mikraz, the second that it was Abu Sufyan Sakhr b. Harb; but it seemed to him more likely that it was Abu Sufyan. God knows best. Ibn Ishaq then gave the ode attributed to Abu Bakr "the Trusting", written about this night raid. It begins, "Is it for your vision of Salma in the flat desert plains you lay awake, and for something happening in the tribe? In Lu'ayy you see a faction that no preaching, no messenger's mission could keep from unbelief. A truth-telling messenger came to them but they denied him and told him he could not stay among them. When we called them to the truth, they turned their backs and fled like panting dogs retreating to their lair." He then gave the response of 'Abd Allah b. al-Ziba'ra: "Is it because of the traces of a home now desolate amidst the sand dunes that you weep, your tears soon gone, And because of time's surprises, and destiny is all surprises, of things gone by and new.
