A great army that came to us, led by Ubayda who is called Ibn Harith, in battle, So we would leave idols set up in Mecca, passed down by heir to noble heir." Ibn Ishaq goes on to give the complete ode. And nothing prevents us from doing the same except for the fact that Imam 'Abd al-Malik b. Hisham, God bless him, who was an Imam in language, reported that many scholars, expert in poetry, deny the authenticity of both these poems. Ibn Ishaq further stated, "Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas spoke the following verses concerning his having shot an arrow, or so they say, 'Has the Messenger of God heard that I protected my companions with my arrow? Through it I well protected their front ranks over land both rough and easy. And no one shooting an arrow at an enemy will be counted before myself, O Messenger of God! And that was because your religion is that of truth; it was with truth and virtue that you brought it. By it the believers are saved, by it the unbelievers are punished at a place of boiling pitch. Take care, for you have erred, and do not decry me; woe upon you, Ibn Jahl, misleader of your tribe!'" Ibn Hisham commented, "Most authorities on poetry deny that this poem is to be attributed to Sa'd."
lessons: Small acts of courage and sacrifice for a righteous cause carry immense value, even when they go unrecorded or disputed by history.
Standing firm for truth when surrounded by falsehood, as Miqdad and Utba did in returning to the Muslims, requires extraordinary courage and clarity of faith.
Sincere deeds done for God's sake are their own reward, regardless of whether they receive recognition or historical credit.
practical_advice: When you feel your contributions go unnoticed, remember that God witnesses every sincere effort, and that recognition from Him is infinitely more valuable than human praise.
If you find yourself in an environment that conflicts with your values, take courage from those who, like Miqdad and Utba, made the difficult choice to return to what was right.
Channel feelings of being overlooked into renewed commitment to your principles rather than bitterness, as the early Muslims transformed their hardships into poetry and remembrance of God.

title: The Dispute Over the First Banner in Islam: Hamza and Ubayda b. al-Harith
summary: Scholars disputed whether Hamza b. Abd al-Muttalib or Ubayda b. al-Harith received the first banner awarded by the Prophet in Islam, with various historians offering differing accounts and poems attributed to Hamza on the matter.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated further, "The banner given to Ubayda, so I have been told, was the first awarded by the Messenger of God (SAAS) to any Muslim." Al-Zuhri, Musa b. Uqba, and al-Waqidi disputed Ibn Ishaq in this and maintained that Hamza received a flag before Ubayd b. al-Harith. But God knows best. It will be related hereafter, from a hadith of Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas, that the first leader of these raids was 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh al-Asadi. Ibn Ishaq went on, "Some scholars maintain that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent him out when he arrived from the mission to al-Abwa' before he reached Medina. Musa b. Uqba related it so from al-Zuhri." Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS), while resident there, sent Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib b. Hashim to the sea coast near al-'Is along with 30 mounted men drawn from the Emigrants; there were no Helpers among them. Hamza confronted Abu Jahl b. Hisham and 300 mounted Meccans there on the shore. But Majdi b. 'Amr al-Juhani interposed himself between the opposing parties, for he was at peace with both. Both sides withdrew from one another and so no fighting occurred." Ibn Ishaq continued, "Some people say that Hamza's banner was the first awarded by the Messenger of God (SAAS) to any of the Muslims. This was because he had dispatched Hamza and Ubayda at the same time; this confused people." I comment that Musa b. Uqba quoted al-Zuhri as saying that he had dispatched Hamza before Ubayda b. al-Harith. He maintained that Hamza's mission came before the expedition to al-Abwa'. And that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) returned from al-Abwa' he dispatched Ubayda b. al-Harith along with 60 Emigrants. He then related much as above. It has been stated above that al-Waqidi said, "The raid made by Hamza in Ramadan took place in 1 AH; Ubayda's expedition came thereafter, in Shawwal in the same year." But God knows best. Ibn Ishaq quoted from Hamza, God be pleased with him, poetry indicating that his banner was the first awarded in Islam. However Ibn Ishaq stated, "If Hamza did speak this, then so it was. He only ever spoke the truth. But God knows best what happened. What we have heard from scholars was that Ubayda was first. The poem is as follows: 'O my people, contemplate wisdom and foolishness and failure in man's thought and intellect, About those who have wronged us while we have not violated their people or property. As though we had been hostile to them, but we have no hostility for them; all we told them is to be chaste and just. And we told them of Islam, but they do not accept it and treat it as if it were a joke. They kept this up till I was appointed to raid them where they reside, seeking security in virtue. At the order of the Messenger of God, being the first to have his banner fly above me, it not having appeared before. A flag bringing victory, from a God of honour and power, His deeds the best of all. They left in the evening, gathered together, the cooking pot of each of us boiling with the rage of his Companions. When we saw one another they made their mounts kneel and tied up their mounts, and we tied ours an arrow's reach away. We told them, "Our connection to God is our aid. But the only connection you have is to error." Abu Jahl arose to fight there, acting in evil, but he was disappointed, for God rejected Abu Jahl's tricks. We had only thirty mounted men, while they numbered two hundred, with one extra yet. O Lu'ayy, do not obey your evil men; come over to Islam, to the path that is plain. For I fear that you will have pain wrought upon you and you will cry out in regret and mourning.'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "And Abu Jahl b. Hisham, speaking verses in response, God damn him, spoke the following: 'I was amazed at the reasons for false worship and ignorance and at those who seek to do harm through division and idiocy, At those who abandon the practices of our fathers and forebears, those fine and noble leaders.'" He then quoted the rest of the poem. Ibn Hisham stated, "Most poetry scholars deny that the first poem can be attributed to Hamza, God be pleased with him, or the second to Abu Jahl, God damn him!"
lessons: Uncertainty about historical details does not diminish the sincerity and sacrifice of those who acted for God's cause; what matters is the purity of intention behind the deed.
When outnumbered and facing overwhelming odds, as Hamza's thirty men faced three hundred, reliance on God rather than numbers is the true source of strength.

Disputes and differences of scholarly opinion are a natural part of preserving knowledge; humility in acknowledging what is unknown, expressed repeatedly as 'God knows best,' is a mark of true scholarship and wisdom.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about not receiving proper recognition for your efforts, remind yourself that God's record is perfect and complete, even when human records are disputed or incomplete.
In moments of feeling overwhelmed by being vastly outnumbered or outmatched in any struggle of life, draw strength from the example of those who faced far greater odds and still chose the right path.
Practice intellectual humility by acknowledging the limits of your own knowledge, saying 'God knows best' in situations of uncertainty rather than forcing premature conclusions.

title: The Expedition to Buwat: The Prophet's First Major Caravan Interception
summary: The Prophet led 200 men toward Buwat near Radwa in 2 AH to intercept a Quraysh caravan led by Umayya b. Khalaf, but returned to Medina without engaging in battle.
story: THE EXPEDITION TO BUWAT, IN THE NEIGHBOURHOOD OF RADWA. Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sent an expedition against Quraysh in the month of Rabi' al-Awwal, that is in 2 AH." Ibn Hisham commented, "He left al-Sa'ib b. Uthman b. Maz'un in charge over Medina." Al-Waqidi stated, "He left Sa'd b. Mu'adh in charge of it. The Messenger of God (SAAS) was accompanied by 200 mounted men. His banner was entrusted to Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas. His purpose was to interfere with a Quraysh caravan which consisted of Umayya b. Khalaf with 100 men and 2,500 camels." Ibn Ishaq stated, "He proceeded until he reached Buwat, near Radwa. He then returned to Medina without engaging in battle. He remained there for the remainder of Rabi' al-Akhir and part of Jumada al-Ula."
lessons: Preparation and mobilization for a righteous cause has value even when no direct confrontation occurs; the effort itself demonstrates commitment and readiness.
A leader who returns without achieving the immediate objective, when circumstances require it, shows wisdom and restraint rather than weakness.
Trust in God's plan means accepting that sometimes our preparations lead to outcomes different from what we intended, and that this redirection is itself part of divine wisdom.
practical_advice: When you have prepared thoroughly for a goal that does not materialize as planned, resist the feeling of failure; the effort, discipline, and intention were not wasted.
Practice patience when your plans are interrupted or redirected; use the waiting period, as the Prophet used his return to Medina, to consolidate and prepare for what comes next.
If you feel anxious about unresolved conflicts or threats in your life, take the step of active preparation and readiness rather than passive worry, trusting that the outcome rests with God.

title: The Expedition to al-Ushayra: Peace with Banu Mudlij and Banu Damra
summary: The Prophet led an expedition to al-Ushayra in the Yanbu valley to intercept Quraysh caravans heading to Syria, but instead concluded peaceful agreements with the Banu Mudlij and their allies the Banu Damra, returning to Medina without battle.
story: THE EXPEDITION TO AL-'USHAYRA. He then made an expedition against Quraysh. This refers to the expedition known by the name "al-'Ushayra" or "al-'Usayra". Ibn Hisham commented that he left Abu Salama b. 'Abd al-Asad in charge of Medina. Al-Waqidi stated that his banner was carried by Hamza b. 'Abd al-Muttalib and stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth to interrupt the Quraysh caravans going to Syria." Ibn Ishaq stated, "He made his way through the territory of the Banu Dinar, then past Fayfa' al-Khabar, and made camp beneath a tree in the Ibn Azhar valley at a place called Dhat al-Saq. He said his prayers there, where his mosque is now. Food was prepared for him there which he ate, and the others ate there with him. The remains of the supports used for his cooking pot are still known there. He used the water from a place called al-Mushayrih. "He then moved on, leaving al-Khala'iq to his left and traversed the 'Abd Allah defile. From there he kept left, coming down the Yalyal trail till it meets that of al-Daha'a. He then crossed the Malal plain until he met the road at Sukhayrst al-Yamam and then went on along it till he reached al-Ushayra, in the Yanbu' valley. "There he remained for Jumada al-Ula and some nights of Jumada al-Akhira; there he made peace with the Banu Mudlij and their allies of the Banu Damra, then returned to Medina, without having engaged in battle." Al-Bukhari stated, "'Abd Allah related to us, quoting Wahb, quoting Shu'ba, from Abi Ishaq, who said, 'I was standing next to Zayd b. Arqam when he was asked, "In how many military expeditions did the Messenger of God (SAAS) take part?" He replied, "Nineteen." "And on how many did you accompany him?" "Seventeen," he replied. "Which of them came first?" I enquired. "Al-Ushayra - or al-Usayr," he replied. I told this to Qatada and he said it was "al-Ushayra"."' This hadith is clear in stating that the first of the military expeditions was at al-Ushayra, which can also be spelled "al-Usayra", and that in both of these spellings the final feminine ending, the "a" may be dropped or a lengthened "a" be substituted. However, what could be meant here is that al-Ushayra was the first expedition in which Zayd b. Arqam participated with the Messenger of God (SAAS), and there could have been earlier ones which he did not witness. This could reconcile between this hadith and that of Ibn Ishaq. But God knows best."
lessons: Choosing peaceful agreements over armed conflict when possible reflects a higher wisdom; strength is not always measured by battle but sometimes by the alliances and trust one builds.

The Prophet's detailed, purposeful journey to al-Ushayra — praying at each stop, sharing food with his companions — teaches that the journey itself, conducted with mindfulness and community, is as important as the destination.
Strategic patience, represented by spending weeks at al-Ushayra without forcing a confrontation, is often more powerful than impulsive action.
practical_advice: When facing a conflict or difficult situation, explore whether peaceful resolution or alliance-building might achieve better long-term results than direct confrontation.

Practice mindfulness during your journeys and transitions in life by observing your prayers, sharing meals with others, and marking meaningful moments along the way rather than focusing only on the final goal.

If you feel anxious about a threatening situation in your life, remember that sometimes the most powerful response is strategic positioning and building relationships of trust, rather than immediate aggressive action.

title: Ali and Ammar's Adventure Among the Banu Mudlij at al-Ushayra
summary: During the expedition to al-Ushayra, Ali b. Abi Talib and Ammar b. Yasir were companions together, and Ali invited Ammar to go watch the Banu Mudlij working at a well, leading to a memorable moment between them and the Prophet.
story: Muhammad b. Ishaq stated, "It was on that occasion that the Messenger of God (SAAS) made his comment to 'Ali. Yazid b. Muhammad b. Khaytham related to me, from Muhammad b. Ka'b al-Qurazi, quoting Abu Yazid Muhammad b. Khaytham, from 'Ammar b. Yasir, who said, 'Ali b. Abi Talib and I were companions on the expedition to al-'Ushayra in the Yanbu' valley. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) encamped there he remained for a month and made peace with the Banu Mudlij and their allies of the Banu Damra. 'Ali b. Abi Talib said, "Abu al-Yaqzan, how would you like for us to go and watch these men of the Bana Mudlij who are doing some work on a well of
lessons: Brotherhood and companionship in shared endeavors creates bonds of friendship that sustain the spirit during long and difficult missions.

Simple moments of curiosity and human connection, like Ali inviting Ammar to watch workers at a well, are reminders that life's meaning is found in small shared experiences as much as in grand events.
The company of righteous friends who invite you toward wholesome activity is one of the greatest gifts a person can have during times of hardship and displacement.
practical_advice: During stressful or prolonged difficult periods in your life, seek out small moments of connection and curiosity with trusted companions rather than isolating yourself.
Cultivate friendships with people who, like Ali and Ammar, share your values and invite you toward positive, grounding activities even in the midst of serious responsibilities.
When feeling lonely or overwhelmed during a long journey — whether literal or metaphorical — look for the small human moments of warmth and companionship that remind you that you are not alone.

title: Ali Ibn Abi Talib Nicknamed 'Abu Turab' at al-Ushayra
summary: During the expedition to al-Ushayra, Ali and Ammar fell asleep among date palms and were woken by the Prophet who affectionately called Ali 'Abu Turab' (father of dirt) due to the soil on him, and foretold his martyrdom.
story: Muhammad b. Khaytham related to me, from Muhammad h. Ka'b al-Qurki, quoting Abii Yazid Muhammad b. Khaytham, from 'Ammar h. Yasir, who said, "Ali b. Abii Titlib and I were companions on the expedition to d-'Ushayra in the Yanbu' valley. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) encamped there he remained for a month and made peace with the Banii Mudlij and their allies of the Banii Damra. 'Ali b. Abii Tdib said, 'Abii al-Yaqzan, how would you like for us to go and watch these men of the Bana Mudlij who are doing some work on a well of theirs?' So we went to them and watched for a while, then were overcome by sleep. We made our way over to a place where there were young date-palms growing and the ground was soft and there we slept. And, by God, we were eventually woken up by the Messenger of God (SAAS) moving us with his foot. We sat up, soiled with the earth there and it was then that he said to 'Ali, 'What have you been up to, Aba Turab?' (i.e. 'father of dirt') because of the soil that was upon him. We told him what had happened to us and he said, 'Shall I tell you who are the two most pitiable men?' We replied, 'Yes, do tell us.' He replied, 'They are Uhaymir of the tribe of Thamiid who slaughtered the camel and the man who will strike you on this' - and he placed his hand on 'Ali's head - 'so that this' - and he touched 'Ali's beard - 'will be become wet from it.'" This hadith is unique in this line given. From another line there is also testimony to reference being made to 'Ali as 'Abii Turab'. Similarly, in the fahih of al-Bukhari it states that 'Ali left his house angry at Fatima and went and slept in the mosque. When the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to his house to ask after him, Fatima told him he had left angry. So the Messenger of God (SAAS) went to the mosque and woke him up and, wiping the dirt from him, told him, "Up you get, Abii Turab! Up you get!"
lessons: Even the closest companions of the Prophet experienced moments of human weariness and rest, reminding us that physical and emotional rest is a natural and necessary part of life.

The Prophet's gentle and affectionate manner of waking Ali, giving him a loving nickname rather than reproaching him, teaches that compassion and warmth should characterize our relationships with those we love.
The Prophet's foretelling of Ali's martyrdom reflects that trials and ultimate sacrifice may await the righteous, and that facing one's destiny with faith is the highest form of courage.
practical_advice: When someone you care about makes a mistake or falls short, respond with warmth and humor rather than criticism, following the Prophet's example of affectionate correction.
Allow yourself necessary rest without guilt; the companions of the Prophet rested when their bodies demanded it, and so should you.
Reflect on the loving nicknames or terms of endearment used by those close to you as reminders of the bonds that sustain you through hardship.

title: The First Expedition to Badr: Pursuing Kurz ibn Jabir
summary: Shortly after returning from al-Ushayra, the Prophet led an expedition in pursuit of Kurz ibn Jabir al-Fihri who had raided Medina, travelling as far as the valley of Safwan near Badr, though Kurz escaped capture.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Upon his return from al-Ushayra, the Messenger of God (SAAS) had been in Medina only a few days, less than ten, when Kurz b. Jabir al-Fihri made a raid on the town. The Messenger of God (SAAS) went forth in search of him and travelled as far as a valley called Safwan, near Badr. This, then was the first expedition to Badr; Kurz evaded him and was not captured." Al-Waqidi stated, "His banner was entrusted to 'Ali b. Abi Talib." Both Ibn Hisham and al-Waqidi related that he had left Zayd b. Haritha in command of Medina.
lessons: Responding swiftly and decisively to threats against one's community is a mark of responsible leadership, even when the outcome does not bring immediate resolution.
Not every effort leads to a visible result; the Prophet pursued Kurz diligently yet he escaped, teaching us that our duty is to act rightly, not to guarantee outcomes.
Delegating responsibility wisely, as the Prophet left Zayd ibn Haritha in charge of Medina, reflects the importance of trust and shared leadership in protecting a community.
practical_advice: When faced with a threat or crisis, respond with calm, organized action rather than panic, trusting that doing your part is what is required of you.
Accept that some situations will not resolve as you hope; let go of the need to control outcomes and focus instead on the quality of your effort.
Identify trustworthy people in your life to whom you can delegate responsibilities during times of stress, so that important things are not neglected while you address a crisis.

title: Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas's Raid and the Division Among Companions
summary: The Prophet sent Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas with a small force on a raid, but the group became divided in their decisions; when some returned and reported the split to the Prophet, he expressed sharp displeasure, warning that division had destroyed those before them.
story: Imam Ahmad stated that 'Abd al-Mut'ib b. 'Abd al-Wahhab related to him, quoting Yahya b. Sa'id; and 'Abd Allah b. al-Imam Ahmad stated that 'Sa'id b. Yahya b. Sa'id al-Umawi related to him, quoting his father, from Mujahid, from Ziyad b. 'Alaqa, who quoted Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas as having said, "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina, he was visited by some of the Juhayna who told him, 'Now you have come to live among us, make a pact with us so we can join you.' He did so, and they accepted Islam. In Rajab the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent us out, a force of less than 100, with orders to make a raid on a quarter of the Banu Kinana, who were over next to the Juhayna. We did raid them, but there were many of them and we took refuge with the Juhayna who gave us protection. They asked us, 'Why are you fighting in the holy month?' We asked one another what to do and some of us thought we should go back to the Prophet of God (SAAS) and tell him. Others thought they should stay there. I, and some others decided we would do neither and would attack and cut off a Quraysh caravan. The arrangement over booty at that time was that you could keep anything you won. "So we set off against the caravan, while some of our Companions went back to the Prophet (SAAS). When they told him the situation, he became very angry and red-faced and said, 'You left me as one group and you've returned divided! Those before you who became divided were only ever destroyed! I will appoint over you someone who is no higher than you in rank, but who will give you fortitude against hunger and thirst.' "He then appointed over us 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh al-Asadi, who was the first commander in Islam."
lessons: Unity and obedience to leadership are foundational to the success of any collective endeavor; division, even when motivated by individual reasoning, can unravel what was built together.

The Prophet's visible anger at the division of his companions teaches that some mistakes are serious enough to merit strong correction, and that honest accountability is an act of love for a community.
Individual initiative, however well-intentioned, must be weighed against the harm it causes to group cohesion and trust in leadership.
practical_advice: When your group or team faces a difficult decision, prioritize consultation and consensus over unilateral action, even if you believe your individual choice is the better one.
If you have acted in a way that divided or undermined a community you belong to, take responsibility directly rather than justifying your actions, and seek to repair the rift.
Reflect on moments when anger from a mentor or leader stung you; consider whether that correction, however uncomfortable, pointed to a genuine blind spot in your behavior.

title: The Expedition of Abd Allah ibn Jahsh to Nakhla
summary: The Prophet sent Abd Allah ibn Jahsh with eight Emigrants on a secret mission to observe the Quraysh at Nakhla; they encountered a Quraysh caravan, killed one man, captured two others, and seized the caravan during the sacred month of Rajab, causing serious controversy.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "In Rajab, following his return from the first expedition to Badr, the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh b. Ri'ab al-Asadi along with eight Emigrants; their number included none of the Helpers. The names of these men were as follows: Abu Hudhayfa b. 'Utba, 'Ukkasha b. Mihsan b. Hurthan, an ally of the Banu Asad b. Khuzayma, 'Utba b. Ghazwan, an ally of the Banu Nawfal, Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas al-Zuhri, 'Amir b. Rabi'a al-'Anzi, an ally of the Banu 'Adi, Waqid b. 'Abd Allah b. 'Abd Manaf b. 'Arin b. Tha'laba b. Yarbu' al-Tamimi, also an ally of the Banu 'Adi, Khalid b. al-Bukayr, one of the Banu Sa'd b. Layth, also an ally of the Banu 'Adi, and Sahl b. Bayda' al-Fihri. These seven men were led by their commander 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh, God be pleased with him." Yunus quoted Ibn Ishaq as stating that there were eight men and their commander was their ninth. But God knows best. Ibn Ishaq stated, "He wrote a letter for 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh which he told him not to open until he had travelled for two days. After opening it he was to do as it ordered him, but not to force any of his companions to do so too. "Having travelled for two days, he opened the letter. It stated, 'Having read this letter, proceed to Nakhla, between Mecca and Ta'if. Stay there and observe Quraysh and report back to us news of them.' Having read the letter, he said, 'To hear is to obey!' And he told his men what was in the letter. He said, 'It forbids me from forcing any of you to comply with this. Those of you seeking martyrdom should go forward. Those reluctant to do this should return. As for myself, I shall proceed to fulfil the order of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' "He then proceeded forward accompanied by his men, none of whom stayed behind. He travelled through Hijaz until he reached a mine above al-Furu' known as Bahran. There Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas and 'Utba b. Ghazwan lost a camel they had that they had been riding, so they remained behind to look for it. 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh continued ahead with the rest of the party and made camp at Nakhla. "There a Quraysh caravan in which was 'Amr b. al-Hadrami - Ibn Hisham commented that his full name was 'Abd Allah b. 'Abbad, one of the Sadif - along with 'Uthman b. 'Abd Allah b. al-Mughira al-Makhzumi, and his brother Nawfal, along with al-Hakam b. Kaysan, the freed-man of Hisham b. al-Mughira. "When the caravan party saw them they were concerned because the Muslims had encamped near themselves. But 'Ukkasha b. Mihsan appeared before them, having cut his hair. When they saw him, they felt secure. 'Ammar told them, 'You don't need to worry about them.' "The Companions consulted about how to treat them. They realized that if they were to leave them unharmed, that night they would enter sacred territory and be safe, but if they were to kill them they would be doing so on the last day of the sacred month of Rajab. They were undecided and ill at ease about attacking them. "But then they encouraged one another to do so, deciding eventually to kill those they could and to seize their goods. And so Waqid b. 'Abd Allah al-Tamimi shot an arrow at 'Amr b. al-Hadrami and killed him, 'Uthman b. 'Abd Allah and al-Hakam b. Kaysan were taken prisoner, and Nawfal b. 'Abd Allah evaded them and escaped. 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh and his men then returned to the Messenger of God (SAAS) with the caravan and the two prisoners. "Some of 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh's family maintain that 'Abd Allah told his men, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) is due one-fifth of what we have taken' and that he set this portion aside and divided up the rest between them. This was before the revelation came down concerning that fifth." When this revelation came down it was as 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh had divided it, as Ibn Ishaq observed. "When they reached the Messenger of God (SAAS) he told them, 'I did not order you to kill anyone in the sacred month!' He suspended disposition of the caravan and the prisoners and refused to take any of it. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) said this, the raiders were very worried and thought they were ruined, and their fellow Muslims criticized them harshly for what they had done. Quraysh said, 'Muhammad and his men have made it lawful to use violence in the sacred month; they have shed blood and taken booty and seized prisoners during it.' Those Muslims in Mecca opposing Quraysh, however, maintained that the action had occurred in Sha'ban. Some Jews said, 'You must consider this an omen against the Messenger of God (SAAS). The killing...'
lessons: Acting in moral grey areas, even with sincere intention to serve a righteous cause, can bring unforeseen consequences and communal harm; careful consultation and restraint are always preferable to impulsive action.

The anguish the companions felt when the Prophet withheld approval of their actions teaches that the desire for validation from those we respect is a powerful human need, and that feeling 'ruined' after a mistake is a sign of a living conscience.

External criticism from enemies can intensify the guilt of those who have erred, but the response of faith is to return to God and one's leader for guidance rather than to collapse under the weight of public condemnation.
practical_advice: Before taking an action that sits in an ambiguous moral space, pause and ask yourself whether you would be comfortable fully disclosing it to the leader or mentor whose judgment you most respect.

When you have made a decision that caused harm, resist the urge to rationalize it to others; instead, acknowledge the mistake openly and accept the discomfort of accountability as part of the healing process.

If you are being criticized publicly for a mistake, separate the valid concerns in that criticism from the hostile intent behind it, and use the valid concerns to guide genuine correction without being crushed by the hostility.

title: The Nakhla Raid: Controversy Over Fighting in the Sacred Month
summary: Abdullah ibn Jahsh and his companions killed a man and took prisoners during what may have been the sacred month of Rajab, causing great controversy among Muslims, Quraysh, and Jews until a Quranic revelation provided clarity and relief.
story: the prisoners and refused to take any of it. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) said this, the raiders were very worried and thought they were ruined, and their fellow Muslims criticized them harshly for what they had done. Quraysh said, 'Muhammad and his men have made it lawful to use violence in the sacred month; they have shed blood and taken booty and seized prisoners during it.' Those Muslims in Mecca opposing Quraysh, however, maintained that the action had occurred in Sha'ban. Some Jews said, 'You must consider this an omen against the Messenger of God (SAAS). The killing of 'Amr b. al-Hadrami by Waqid b. 'Abd Allah means as follows, "Amr" stands for "amarat al-harb", (i.e. "war has spread"). "Al-Hadrami" stands for "hadarat al-harb", (i.e. "war has come") "Waqid b. 'Abd Allah" stands for "wuqidat al-harb", (i.e. "war has been kindled"). God, however, turned this around against them. "When people gossiped excessively about this, God Almighty sent down the following words to His Messenger (SAAS), 'They will ask you about the sacred month, and about fighting in it. Say: fighting therein is a grave matter; but blocking off God's path and disbelief in Him and (hindering men from) the sacred mosque, and expelling its people from it, (all this) is extremely grave in God's view; and causing unrest is worse than killing. And they will not stop fighting you until they turn you from your religion, if they can!' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.217). This means "Even if you have fought in the sacred month, they have blocked you from God's path by disbelieving in Him, and also by keeping you from the holy mosque and expelling you from it when you were of its people. This is more grave in God's view than your fighting those of them you did. For causing unrest is worse than killing. That is, they used to seduce Muslims from their religion, trying to return them to disbelief from faith. This was worse in God's view than the fighting. Their conduct was far worse and they were completely unrepentant. This is why God Almighty stated, 'They will not stop fighting you until they turn you from your religion, if they can!'" Ibn Ishaq went on, "When the Qur'an was revealed about this and God had alleviated the concerns of the Muslims, the Messenger of God (SAAS) took possession of the caravan and the two prisoners. Quraysh then sought to provide ransom for 'Uthman and al-Hakam b. Kaysan, but the Messenger of God (SAAS) responded, 'We will not release them to you until our two men come forth.' He was referring to Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas and 'Utba b. Ghazwan. 'We are concerned', he told them, 'about your treatment of them. If you kill them, we will kill your men.' "Sa'd and 'Utba then came forth, and the Messenger of God (SAAS) ransomed them. Al-Hakam b. Kaysan accepted Islam and became a Muslim, remaining with the Messenger of God (SAAS) until he was killed, a martyr, at the battle of Bi'r Ma'una. 'Uthman b. 'Abd Allah stayed in Mecca where he died an unbeliever." Ibn Ishaq stated, "When 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh and his companions had been relieved of their anxiety when the revelation came in the Qur'an, they sought reward. They asked, 'O Messenger of God, may we hope that this be considered an expedition for which we will be given reward as warriors for God's cause?' And so God revealed concerning them, 'Those who believe and those who have emigrated and those who have fought for God's cause, those persons may hope for the mercy of God, for God is merciful and forgiving' (surat al-Baqara II v.218). Through this God gave them very great hope."
lessons: Divine revelation can bring clarity and relief in moments of overwhelming guilt and uncertainty, reassuring believers that God understands context and intention.
The scale of oppression matters in moral judgment — those who expel, persecute, and obstruct others from their faith bear a greater burden than those who err in defending against such oppression.
Hope in God's mercy is never closed off to sincere believers, even after mistakes; the companions immediately sought to understand their standing with God and were answered with reassurance.
practical_advice: When you have made a mistake and are overwhelmed by the criticism of others, take time to seek deeper understanding rather than spiraling into despair — clarity often comes when you look beyond the immediate noise.

If you are carrying guilt over an action taken under ambiguous or difficult circumstances, remind yourself that God knows your intention and context in ways others cannot; seek His forgiveness and move forward.
When facing public criticism or social pressure, ground yourself in principle rather than popular opinion — ask what is truly just and weigh the full picture before accepting condemnation.

title: Al-Hakam ibn Kaysan Accepts Islam After Being a Prisoner
summary: Al-Hakam ibn Kaysan, one of the prisoners taken during the Nakhla raid, embraced Islam while in captivity and went on to remain with the Prophet until he was martyred at the battle of Bi'r Ma'una.
story: Al-Hakam b. Kaysan accepted Islam and became a Muslim, remaining with the Messenger of God (SAAS) until he was killed, a martyr, at the battle of Bi'r Ma'una. 'Uthman b. 'Abd Allah stayed in Mecca where he died an unbeliever.
lessons: A person's lowest moment — even captivity — can become the turning point that leads them to their highest purpose and greatest transformation.
The path each person takes is their own; two people can face the same circumstances and make entirely different choices, each bearing the consequences of their decision.
Choosing faith and commitment to truth, even when it demands sacrifice, can lead to a life of profound meaning that extends far beyond one's years.
practical_advice: If you find yourself in a difficult or constraining situation, consider whether it might be an unexpected opportunity for reflection and redirection rather than only a loss.
Do not measure the value of a life by its length but by the depth of its commitment — small acts of sincere faith can carry immense weight.
When you feel lost or uncertain about your path, look for the example of those who found clarity in the hardest of circumstances and let their courage inspire your own next step.

title: The Companions of the Nakhla Raid Seek Reward and Receive Hope
summary: After the Quranic revelation relieved the companions of their anxiety over the Nakhla incident, they asked the Prophet whether their expedition would count as a rewarded act of striving in God's cause, and God answered them with a verse of hope.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh and his companions had been relieved of their anxiety when the revelation came in the Qur'an, they sought reward. They asked, 'O Messenger of God, may we hope that this be considered an expedition for which we will be given reward as warriors for God's cause?' And so God revealed concerning them, 'Those who believe and those who have emigrated and those who have fought for God's cause, those persons may hope for the mercy of God, for God is merciful and forgiving' (surat al-Baqara II v.218). Through this God gave them very great hope."
lessons: It is a mark of spiritual health to seek not just forgiveness after a mistake but also to ask how one can still contribute positively — the two are not in conflict.
God's mercy encompasses those who believe, emigrate, and strive sincerely, even when their efforts are imperfect or disputed by others.
Asking honest questions about our spiritual standing, rather than assuming the worst or the best, invites divine guidance and reassurance.
practical_advice: After working through guilt or anxiety about a past action, give yourself permission to also ask what good can still come from the experience rather than remaining stuck in regret.
When you are uncertain whether your efforts are worthwhile or recognized, bring that question sincerely to God in prayer — the act of asking itself is an expression of faith.
Remember that hope in divine mercy is not complacency; it energizes action and encourages you to keep striving even after setbacks.

title: Abdullah ibn Jahsh: The First Appointed Commander in Islam
summary: Abdullah ibn Jahsh was noted by Sa'd ibn Abi Waqqas as the first amir (commander) appointed in Islam, a distinction tied to the mission to Nakhla where he led seven companions under sealed orders from the Prophet.
story: I comment that in his aforementioned report Imam Ahmad quoted Sa'd b. Abi Waqqas as having said, "'Abd Allah b. Jahsh was the first amir, commander, appointed in Islam." We have given in our Tafsir (Exegesis) various testimonies, with their chains of authorities, in support of what Ibn Ishaq recounted. One such is what was reported by al-Hafiz Abu Muhammad b. Abi Hatim, namely, "My father related to us, quoting Muhammad b. Abi Bakr al-Muqaddimi, quoting al-Mu'tamir b. Sulayman, quoting his father, quoting al-Hadrami, quoting from Abu al-Siwwar, from Jundab b. 'Abd Allah, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sent forth some men, having appointed Abu 'Ubayda b. al-Jarrah — or 'Ubayda b. al-Harith — as their commander. But when he came to leave, he wept in longing for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and sat down. And so he appointed 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh over them and wrote a letter to him, telling him not to read it until he reached a certain place. He told him, 'Do not force any of your men to proceed further with you.' "When he read the letter, he spoke the words, 'We are God's and to Him do we return,' and said, 'To hear is to obey, both God and His Messenger.' He then told them what the letter contained. Two of their number then went back, while the rest stayed. They met up with Ibn al-Hadrami and killed him, not knowing whether that day was in the month of Rajab or Jumada. The polytheists told the Muslims, 'You have killed a man in the sacred month!' God then revealed, 'They will ask you about the sacred month, and about fighting during it. Say: fighting then is a grave matter' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.217)."
lessons: Leadership is not always sought — sometimes it is thrust upon a person at a moment of another's vulnerability, and the true test is whether one rises to it with obedience and sincerity.
Saying 'we are God's and to Him do we return' in the face of a difficult duty is not resignation but a profound act of trust that enables courageous action.
The willingness to give companions a choice — not forcing anyone to proceed — reflects a leadership ethic rooted in respect and conscience rather than coercion.
practical_advice: When you are unexpectedly placed in a position of responsibility, resist the urge to deflect out of fear; accept it with sincerity and do your best with the information and resources you have.
Before taking on a difficult task, remind yourself of your ultimate return to God — this perspective can quiet anxiety and anchor your resolve.
If you are leading others through uncertainty, give them agency where possible; people perform and cope better when they feel their participation is a choice rather than a compulsion.

title: The Change in Prayer Direction (Qibla) from Jerusalem to Mecca
summary: In the second year of the Hijra, the direction of Muslim prayer was changed from Jerusalem to the Sacred Mosque in Mecca, an event scholars date to either Rajab or mid-Sha'ban of that year.
story: Section: Concerning the change in the prayer direction in 2 AH before the battle of Badr. Some authorities maintain that this occurred in Rajab of the second year of the hegira. Qatada and Zayd b. Aslam stated this, in a tradition related from Muhammad b. Ishaq. Ahmad related, from Ibn 'Abbas, evidence supporting this and that is what is clearly implied by the account of al-Bara' b. 'Azib, as will be shown. But God knows best. It is also said to date from Sha'ban in that year. Ibn Ishaq stated, after his account of the raid conducted by 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh, "It is said that the change in the direction of the prayer occurred in Sha'ban, at the beginning of the 18th month following the arrival of the Messenger of God (SAAS) in Medina." Ibn Jarir related this through al-Suddi with a chain of authorities from Ibn 'Abbas, Ibn Mas'ud and various Companions. The great majority of scholars maintain that it was changed in fact in the middle of Sha'ban, at the start of the 18th month following the hegira. It was also related from Muhammad b. Sa'd, on the authority of al-Waqidi to the effect that it was changed on a Tuesday in mid-Sha'ban, though there is some dispute about this. God knows best. We argued against this, with evidence, in the Tafsir (Exegesis) regarding the words of the Almighty, "We see you turning your face about in the sky and..."
lessons: Divine commands can shift, and the believer's role is not to cling to the familiar form but to remain oriented toward the essence — sincere worship of God wherever He directs.
When scholars disagree on details, the honest response is to acknowledge uncertainty rather than assert false confidence; intellectual humility is itself a form of worship.
A change in outward practice does not indicate inconsistency in God's guidance — it reflects a living relationship between the divine and the community of believers across time.
practical_advice: When a meaningful change is introduced into your life or practice, rather than resisting it out of attachment to the familiar, ask what the change is orienting you toward and embrace that deeper purpose.

In moments of confusion about religious or life decisions where scholars or advisors disagree, acknowledge the uncertainty honestly and choose the path most consistent with sincerity and sound reasoning.
Use transitions — even unexpected ones — as moments to recommit to your core values and intentions rather than as disruptions to be endured.

title: The Change of Prayer Direction (Qibla) from Jerusalem to Mecca
summary: After praying toward Jerusalem for 16-17 months in Medina, the Prophet Muhammad received divine revelation ordering the change of the prayer direction (qibla) to the Sacred Mosque in Mecca, prompting criticism from Jews and hypocrites but joyful compliance from the believers.
story: a chain of authorities from Ibn 'Abbits, Ibn Masciid and various Companions. The great majority of scholars maintain that it was changed in fact in the middle of Sha'bm, at the start of the 18th month following the hegira. It was also related from Muhammad b. Sag, on the authority of al-Witqidi to the effect that it was changed on a Tuesday in midSha'ban, though there is some dispute about this. God knows best. We argued against this, with evidence, in the Tafiir (Exegesrs) regarding the words of the Almighty, 'LWesee you turning your face about in the sky and we shall provide you with a qrbla that will please you. Turn your face towards the Sacred Mosque; wherever you may be, turn your face towards it. Those to whom the Book has been brought know that it is the truth from their Lord. And God is not unaware of what they do" (siirat al-Baqara; 11, v.144). 11 (My commentary also examines) the objections made both before and after the change by foolish Jews, hypocrites and low-down ignorant people, and their criticisms were because this was the first abrogation that occurred in Islam. Regarding this, God had previously stated in the course of the Qur'm the permissibility of making abrogation. He had stated, "Whatsoever verse we may cancel or cause to be forgotten we replace with one better or similar. Do you not know that God is capable of all things?" (siirat a&Baqara; 11, v.106). Al-Bukhari had stated, "Abn Nu'aym related to us, (that he had) heard Zuhayr (say) from Aba Ishnq, from al-Bara', who said that the Prophet (SAAS) prayed towards Jerusalem for 16 or 17 months, though it would have pleased him to have faced towards the kacba. He also said that the first time he worshipped towards it was during the al-'ay (late afternoon) prayer. Others were praying along with him at that time. Afterwards a man who had been with him there passed by some people in a mosque prostrated in prayer. He told them, 'I swear by God, I have prayed with the Prophet (SAAS) in the direction of Mecca.' They turned right then and there towards the kacba. We didn't know what to say about people who had died or been killed before the direction for prayer was changed. And so God sent down, 'God is not one to consider your prayer as valueless; God is merciful and kind towards people'" (siirat al-Baqara; 11, v. 143). Muslim related this from another path. Ibn Aba H%im stated, "Aha Zur'a related to us, quoting al-Hasan b. 'Auyya quoting Isra'il, from Aba Ishsq, from al-Bar%", who said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) had been praying towards Jerusalem for 16 or 17 months though he wanted to face towards the kacba. And so God sent down, "We see you turning your face about in the sky and we shall provide you with a qibla that will please you. Turn your face towards the Sacred Mosque" (sarat al-Baqara; 11, v.144). And so he did face towards the kacba. "The foolish people - the Jews - asked 'What could it be that turned them from the prayer direction they had previously?' And so God sent down the verse, 'Say: T o God belong both East and West; he guides whomever He wishes to the straight path'" (stirat al-Baqara; 11, v.142). In short, the Messenger of God (SAAS) used to pray in Mecca towards Jerusalem, while the kacba was directly in front of him, so Im%n A b a d related from Ibn CAbbas, God be pleased with hi.And when he emigrated to Medina it was not possible for him to combine both and so he prayed towards Jerusalem immediately after his arrival there, keeping his back directed to the kacba for 16 or 17 months. This would require it to have been until Rajah of 2 AH. But God knows best. The Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted to change his prayer direction towards the kacba, the qrbla of Abraham, and often prayed and entreated God, Almighty and Glorious is He, to allow this. In doing so, he would raise his hands and sight to the sky in supplication. And so God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed the words, "We see you turning your face about in the sky and we shall provide you with a qabla that will please you. Turn your face towards the Sacred Mosque." When the order came down to change the direction of prayer, the Messenger of God (SAAS) made an address to the Muslims and told them of this, as al-Nasali related, from Aba Sa'id b. al-Mu'alla, and this was at noon. S o m e scholars say that the change was revealed between two prayers. Mujahid and others stated this. This is substantiated by what is given in both sahih collections from al-Bar%]. Namely, that the first prayer he made in Medina towards the kacba was that of al-<asr. It is strange that the people of QubZ1 did not hear of this until the early morning prayer of the next day, as is established in the ~ahihcollections, from Ibn Wmar. He stated, "While the people in Quba' were at the morning prayer someone came to them and said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has received tonight a Qur'an revelation! He has been ordered to face the kacba in prayer, so you must too.' They had been facing towards Syria, but they now turned towards the kacba." In the sahih collection of Muslim, from Anas b. Mdik there is a similar account. What is shown here is that when the change in the direction of the prayer to the kacba was decreed, and God Almighty abrogated the direction of prayer towards Jerusalem, various fools and ignorant people made criticisms, asking "What was it that turned them away from the direction of prayer they had before?" They said this even though the unbelieving "People of the Book" well knew that the order came from God, since they recognized the Messenger of God (SAAS) from the description of h i given in their books, from the information there that Medina would be the place to which he would emigrate, and also that he would he ordered to face towards the ka'ba in his prayer. As God stated, "Those to whom the Book was brought know well that it is the truth from their Lord" (siirat al-Baqara; 11, v.144). Despite all this, God Almighty responded to their question and mentioned them specificallly when He stated, "The fools will ask, 'What was it that turned them away from the direction of prayer they had before?' 'Say: T o God belong both East and West; He guides whomever He wishes to the straight path'" (siirat al-Baqara, 11, v.142). That is, He is the Lord, the Judge and Ruler of the Universe, whose decree no one can revise, He who does as He wishes with His creation, He who decrees whatever He wishes in His law, He who leads whomever He wishes to the straight path, misleading whomever He wishes away from the right road; in all that, His is the wisdom to which all must agree and accept. Then the Almighty stated, "And thus We have made you an intermediate nation" (siirat al-Bagara; 11, v.143). That is, the best "so that you may be witnesses to the people and have the Messenger be witness for you" (swat alBagara; 11, v.143). That is, just as We have chosen for you the best of directions for your prayer, having led you to the gibla of your father Abraham, father of the prophets, to which thereafter Moses and those messengers before him also turned. Likewise We have made you the best of nations, the very essence of the world, the most noble of all groups, the most honourable of the ancient and the high-born, so that you may be witnesses on Judgement Day against those who have combined against you, and so that they may give testimony on that day to your great virtue. account in the sahih collection of This is so substantiated in a al-Bukhari, from Abn Sa'id, referring to Noah's testimony to this nation on Judgement Day. And if Noah should testify for them despite the fact of his own time having been so much before, then it is even more appropriate and proper a course for those after him. God then mentioned His wisdom in sending his retribution to those who doubted and denied this reality, and His pleasure to those who believed and accepted this actuality. He said, "And We decreed the &a you used to observe only to know who it is who follows the Messenger" (siirat al-Baqara; 11, v.143). Ibn 'Abbas commented, "That is, only to see those who follow the Messenger, and those who turn back upon their heels." And 'Leventhough this was a grave matter" (swat al-Baqara; 11, v.143). That is, even though this matter was difficult and of great impact and importance, except for those guided by God. For they had full faith and belief in it, and were without doubt or uncertainty, but rather were satisfied, had faith and took action, for they were obedient to the Great Ruler, the Mighty and AU-Powerful, the Patient and AU-Knowing, the Gracious and Omniscient. And God's words, "God is not one to consider your prayer as valueless" (swat al-Baqara; 11, v.143). That is, by His decision regarding facing towards Jerusalem and prayer towards it, (for) "God is merciful and kind towards people" (stirat al-Baqara; 11, v.143). The accounts and proofs of this are very numerous and would take long to ennumerate; this issue is examined in the exegesis and we will include additional comments thereupon in our work al-Ahkzm al-Kabir (The Major Statutes). I m m Ahmad recounted quoting CAli b. '.&vim, quoting Huvayn b. 'Abd al-R*mm, from 'Amr b. Qays, from Muhammad b. al-Ash'ath, from 'A'isha, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) stated, in regards to the 'People of the Book', that is, 'They envy us for nothing so much as for our Friday to which God led us, but they erred from it, the qibla that God guided us to while they strayed, and our saying "Amen" after the Imxm.'"
lessons: Trusting in divine wisdom means accepting changes and new commands even when they are difficult to understand at first, just as the believers accepted the change of qibla with full faith.

Previous acts of sincere worship are never wasted in God's sight — the prayer performed toward Jerusalem was honored and accepted, as God reassured the believers that He does not consider their prayers valueless.

When others criticize your religious practices or try to create doubt, it is a test of your conviction; remaining grounded in your faith and the guidance of your community is the response of the truly faithful.

Being part of a community of believers means that significant changes affect everyone together — the companions who were mid-prayer simply turned, demonstrating unity, flexibility, and collective obedience.
practical_advice: When you face a sudden change in life — a new direction, an unexpected transition — remind yourself that God's wisdom encompasses what you cannot yet see, and take the step of compliance even before full understanding arrives.

If people mock or question your faith practices, reflect on the Quranic response: God is the Lord of East and West, and His guidance is not subject to popular approval. Find confidence in the source of your direction rather than the opinions of critics.

If you have been consistent in a good practice and it changes or ends, do not grieve that your past efforts were wasted — trust that sincere intention and action done in obedience to God are always recorded and valued by Him.

When your community makes a collective shift — in values, direction, or practice — be among those who turn immediately, like the companions in the mosque who turned on the spot, rather than those who delay out of stubbornness or doubt.

title: The Prophet's Longing for the Kaba as the Qibla and His Supplications to God
summary: The Prophet Muhammad longed deeply for the qibla to be changed to the Kaba and repeatedly raised his hands and gaze to the sky in earnest supplication until God answered his prayer with revelation.
story: The Messenger of God (SAAS) wanted to change his prayer direction towards the kacba, the qrbla of Abraham, and often prayed and entreated God, Almighty and Glorious is He, to allow this. In doing so, he would raise his hands and sight to the sky in supplication. And so God, Almighty and Glorious is He, revealed the words, "We see you turning your face about in the sky and we shall provide you with a qabla that will please you. Turn your face towards the Sacred Mosque."
lessons: Persistent, heartfelt supplication is a powerful act — the Prophet's repeated turning of his face to the sky in longing was seen and answered by God, demonstrating that sincere du'a is never ignored.
It is not wrong to have a deep longing or preference in your heart, as long as you submit your desire to God's will and ask Him through prayer rather than acting unilaterally.

God's answer to sincere prayer sometimes comes in ways that also bring collective benefit — the change of qibla was not just personal fulfillment for the Prophet but a divine gift for the entire Muslim community.
practical_advice: When you are waiting and longing for something you believe is right and good, do not give up on supplication — make du'a consistently, with the same earnestness the Prophet showed when he would raise his gaze to the sky in hope.

Channel your desires and longings into prayer rather than frustration; surrender the outcome to God while continuing to ask sincerely, trusting that He sees your yearning even before you articulate it.

When God answers a long-awaited prayer, pause to recognize the moment with gratitude — just as the revelation brought the Prophet what pleased him, your answered prayers deserve conscious acknowledgment of God's mercy.

title: The People of Quba Learn of the Qibla Change During Morning Prayer
summary: The community of Quba did not learn of the change of qibla until the following morning's prayer, when a messenger arrived mid-prayer and they immediately turned toward Mecca, demonstrating swift obedience to divine command.
story: It is strange that the people of QubZ1 did not hear of this until the early morning prayer of the next day, as is established in the ~ahihcollections, from Ibn Wmar. He stated, "While the people in Quba' were at the morning prayer someone came to them and said, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) has received tonight a Qur'an revelation! He has been ordered to face the kacba in prayer, so you must too.' They had been facing towards Syria, but they now turned towards the kacba." In the sahih collection of Muslim, from Anas b. Mdik there is a similar account.
lessons: Immediate obedience upon receiving authentic guidance — even mid-action — is a hallmark of sincere faith, as shown by the people of Quba turning mid-prayer without hesitation.
The speed with which truth spreads through a community of believers who are genuinely listening is remarkable; one messenger was enough to redirect an entire congregation.
Information gaps and delays are part of human experience; what matters is what you do the moment you receive authentic guidance, not that you were the first to receive it.
practical_advice: When you receive clear and authentic guidance that requires a change in your direction — in prayer, in habit, in a life decision — practice responding immediately rather than delaying for comfort or convenience.
Do not feel guilty or lesser because you were among the last to hear important news; what defines your character is the quality of your response when the truth reaches you.
In community and team settings, recognize that one person willing to carry good news can redirect an entire group — be willing to be that person who shares beneficial guidance promptly with others.

title: The Institution of the Fast of Ashura and the Prophet's Claim to Moses
summary: Upon arriving in Medina and finding the Jews fasting on the day of Ashura, the Prophet inquired about the reason, and upon learning it commemorated God's rescue of Moses, he declared Muslims had a greater claim to Moses and instituted the fast.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "It was in that year that fasting for the month of Ramadzn was made obligatory. It is also said that it was declared obligatory in Sha'bm of that year. It is related, moreover, that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina, he found that the Jews were fasting for the day of cAshiiral. He asked them about it and they told him, 'This was the day when God rescued Moses.' He replied, 'But we have a greater claim to Moses than you do!' So he fasted it and told his people to do so too." This hadith is firmly substantiated in both ~ahihcollections from Ibn 'Abbns.
lessons: The Prophet's curiosity and willingness to inquire about others' practices rather than dismiss them reflects a model of engaged, thoughtful religious life that builds meaning rather than walls.

Gratitude for God's past acts of deliverance — even those experienced by earlier prophets — is a source of spiritual renewal; all of God's prophets and their victories belong to the heritage of believers.

Claiming a spiritual legacy is not about ethnic or tribal belonging but about faithfulness to the same God and the same essential message; the Prophet's declaration shows that true continuity lies in submission to God.
practical_advice: When you encounter a practice or tradition from another community that carries genuine spiritual meaning, approach it with curiosity and respectful inquiry rather than immediate rejection or uncritical adoption.

Reflect regularly on the great moments of divine rescue recorded in religious history — stories like Moses and the Red Sea — and let them strengthen your trust that God is capable of delivering you from your own difficulties.

Build a personal connection to the broader prophetic tradition; reminding yourself that you are part of a long line of those who surrendered to God can give you strength and identity when you feel lost or isolated.

title: The Institution of the Obligatory Fast of Ramadan
summary: In the second year after the Hijra, God made fasting the entire month of Ramadan obligatory upon Muslims, establishing it as a pillar of Islamic practice with provisions for the ill and travellers.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "It was in that year that fasting for the month of Ramadzn was made obligatory. It is also said that it was declared obligatory in Sha'bm of that year. God Almighty stated, "0believers, fasting has been prescribed for you as it was for those who preceded you, so that you may be properly pious. (It shall be) for a specific number of days. Those of you who are ill or travelling (shall fast) a different number of days. And those with the means to do so may redeem this by feeding a poor person. And whoever does more good than he is bound to do does good unto himself thereby; for you"
lessons: Obligatory worship, when entered with sincere intention, is not a burden but a path to personal transformation — the stated purpose of Ramadan's fasting is the development of taqwa, true God-consciousness and piety.
Divine commands come with mercy built in: provisions for the ill and the traveller show that God's law accounts for human vulnerability and never demands the impossible.
Voluntary extra acts of goodness — doing more than the minimum required — are described as self-benefiting; generosity and additional worship enrich the worshipper, not only the recipient.
practical_advice: When beginning a demanding spiritual or personal discipline, focus on its purpose — piety, growth, self-mastery — rather than only on its difficulty; this reorientation transforms endurance into meaning.

If you are going through illness, hardship, or a season of heavy demands, know that the divine framework makes allowances for your condition; do not add guilt to your burden when circumstances legitimately limit your practice.

Look for opportunities to do more than the bare minimum in your acts of service and worship — not to earn approval, but because the habit of generosity and extra effort shapes you into a more whole and spiritually alive person.

title: The Institution of Ramadan Fasting: Revelation and Progressive Legislation
summary: The Quranic verses prescribing Ramadan fasting were revealed in stages, progressively establishing the obligations and exemptions for believers in Madinah. The account traces the evolution from voluntary fasting to the firm prescription of the month of Ramadan.
story:
God Almighty stated, "0 believers, fasting has been prescribed for you as it was for those who preceded you, so that you may be properly pious. (It shall be) for a specific number of days. Those of you who are ill or travelling (shall fast) a different number of days. And those with the means to do so may redeem this by feeding a poor person. And whoever does more good than he is bound to do does good unto himself thereby; for you to fast is better for you, if only you knew it. It was the month of Ramadan in which the Qur'an was revealed, as a guidance to man, and as clear proof of guidance and of the differentiation (between good and evil). Whoever of you are present that month, let them fast it; whoever is sick or travelling should fast a (like) number of other days" (surat al-Baqara; II, v. 183-5).

Imam Ahmad has stated, "Abu al-Nadr related to us, quoting al-Mas'udi, quoting 'Amr b. Murra from 'Abd al-Rahman b. Abi Layla, from Mu'adh b. Jabal, who said, 'The prayer underwent three changes and fasting underwent three changes.' He referred to those respecting prayer, then went on, 'Regarding the changes in fasting, when the Messenger of God (SAAS) came to Medina, he began fasting three days each month, and he also fasted 'Ashora'. "'Then God made fasting incumbent upon him and sent down the words, "0 believers, fasting has been prescribed for you as it was for those who preceded you" (surat al-Baqara; II, v.183), and so on to the words, "And those with the means to do so may redeem this by feeding a poor person" (surat al-Baqara; II, v.184). As a result, those who wished to fast did so, while those who preferred to feed a poor person did so, and that was considered sufficient for them. Then God sent down another verse, "the month of Ramadan in which the Qur'an was revealed" up to the words "whoever of you are present that month, let them fast it" (surat al-Baqara; II, v.185). This firmly prescribed fasting for those who were at home and healthy, and exempted those who were sick or travelling. It also established that the elderly unable to fast should be fed. These, then, were two changes."'
lessons: Divine legislation is often introduced gradually, with wisdom and mercy, reflecting God's deep knowledge of human capacity and need.
Religious obligations carry layers of meaning; fasting is not merely abstention but a means of cultivating genuine piety and gratitude.
God's mercy is woven into every commandment — exemptions for the sick, elderly, and travellers demonstrate that Islam does not intend hardship.
practical_advice: When facing a new obligation or challenge that feels overwhelming, remind yourself that God does not burden a soul beyond what it can bear, and seek the exemptions and relief that have been provided.
Use periods of structured discipline, like fasting, as an opportunity to reset your habits and reconnect with gratitude for daily blessings you normally overlook.
If you struggle with a religious practice, study the wisdom and history behind its prescription; understanding the 'why' can transform compliance into genuine devotion.

title: The Story of Sirma: Exhaustion and the Revelation Easing the Fast
summary: A Helper named Sirma fasted through hard labor until evening, then fell asleep without eating and woke to fast again, leaving the Prophet distressed at his condition. His exhaustion, along with 'Umar's situation, prompted a divine revelation easing the restrictions on eating and intimacy during Ramadan nights.
story: Thereafter people would eat, drink and go to their women before retiring to bed; after retiring for sleep they would refrain from these. One Helper, however, a man named Sirma, once fasted while working until evening, went home to his family, performed the evening prayer and then retired without eating or drinking before the next morning, when he again began fasting. The Messenger of God (SAAS) saw him under great strain and so he asked him, 'How is it I see you under such great stress?' So he informed him. He went on, "'Umar was in the habit of having sexual relations even after he had slept a while and he came to the Messenger of God and told him this. And so God sent down, 'It is lawful for you to visit your wives on the night of the fast; they are as apparel for you' and so on, to the words, 'and then complete the fast till the night' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.187)."
lessons: God's commandments are responsive to the lived realities of people; when sincere believers struggle, divine mercy intervenes with relief.
The Prophet's attentiveness to the wellbeing of his companions — noticing Sirma's exhaustion and asking about it — is a model of compassionate leadership.
Honesty with one's spiritual guide or community about personal struggles can lead to shared benefit and the clarification of what is truly required.
practical_advice: If you find yourself pushing to the point of physical or emotional exhaustion in pursuit of religious or personal goals, recognize this as a sign to reassess and accept the relief God has provided.
Do not suffer in silence; sharing your genuine struggles with a trusted person, as both Sirma and 'Umar did, can open doors to solutions you could not find alone.
Remember that God desires ease for you, not hardship — when a path feels unsustainably difficult, seek the legitimate accommodations built into the framework of your faith.

title: The Prescription of Zakat al-Fitr and the First Eid Prayer
summary: In the same year that Ramadan fasting was prescribed, the Prophet commanded the giving of zakat al-fitr before Eid al-Fitr and led the first Eid prayer, establishing these as enduring practices of the Muslim community.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, "In that year people were commanded to give the zakat al-fitr. It is said that the Messenger of God (SAAS) addressed the people ordering this a day or two before the 'id al-fitr." He also stated, "In that same year the Messenger of God (SAAS) said the salat al-'id prayers for the first time and conducted the people to the musalla. They set out in front of him carrying the harba, a spearhead, that had belonged to Zuhayr to whom it had been presented by the Negus. It would be carried forth before the Messenger of God (SAAS) on feast days."
lessons: The establishment of communal celebrations like Eid reflects Islam's emphasis on collective joy, gratitude, and shared identity among believers.
Charity prescribed at the moment of celebration — zakat al-fitr — ensures that the joy of Eid is extended to those in need, linking personal gratitude to communal care.
The Prophet's personal leadership in inaugurating new practices gave them dignity and meaning that resonated across generations.
practical_advice: In moments of personal celebration, make it a habit to give something to those in need; this transforms private joy into a community-wide experience.
Participate in communal religious gatherings even when individual worship feels sufficient — the shared experience of prayer and celebration builds bonds that sustain faith over time.
Mark transitions and milestones in your life with both gratitude to God and generosity toward others, following the spirit of the first Eid.

title: The Prelude to Badr: Abu Sufyan's Caravan and the Mustering of Quraysh
summary: The Prophet learned that Abu Sufyan was returning from Syria with a great Quraysh caravan and mobilized his companions to intercept it. Abu Sufyan, sensing danger, sent a warning to Mecca, setting in motion the events that would lead to the Battle of Badr.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, after giving his account of the expedition led by 'Abd Allah b. Jahsh: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then heard that Abu Sufyan Sakhr b. Harb was arriving from Syria with a great Quraysh caravan consisting of money and goods, as well as 30 or 40 men, who included Makhrama b. Nawfal and 'Amr b. al-'As."

Musa b. 'Uqba stated, from al-Zuhri, that this was two months after the killing of Ibn al-Hadrami. He also said that the caravan consisting of 1,000 camels carried property belonging to all of Quraysh except for Huwaycib b. 'Abd al-'Uzza, and that this was why he was not present at Badr.

Ibn Ishaq went on, "Muhammad b. Muslim b. Shihab, 'Asim b. 'Umar b. Qatada, 'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr and Yazid b. Ruman related to me from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr and others of our scholars, from Ibn 'Abbas, each of these having related to me a part of this hadith which I have joined together in the following account about Badr. They said that when the Messenger of God (SAAS) heard that Abu Sufyan was arriving from Syria, he sent the Muslims out against them, saying, 'This caravan belongs to Quraysh, and will carry much wealth. Attack it; God may present it to you.'

The men got ready, some pleased, others apprehensive because they had not thought the Messenger of God (SAAS) would make war. When Abu Sufyan approached Hijaz he sought information from each mounted man he encountered since he was concerned what might develop. Eventually he received news from some travellers that Muhammad had readied a force of men against him and his caravan. This alarmed Abu Sufyan and he commissioned Damdam b. 'Amr al-Ghifari, sending him on to Mecca with orders to go to Quraysh and get them to prepare for battle to save their property, by telling them that Muhammad and his men were about to attack it. Damdam b. 'Amr raced into Mecca to do so."
lessons: Moments of great historical significance often begin with small decisions made under uncertainty; both sides in this account acted on incomplete information, reminding us that courage means acting despite not knowing outcomes.
The companions' mixed reactions — some eager, some apprehensive — show that doubt and hesitation in the face of danger are natural human responses, not signs of weak faith.
A leader's willingness to share the stakes openly, as the Prophet did by telling his companions what they were pursuing, builds the trust necessary for people to follow into difficult circumstances.
practical_advice: When you are anxious about a coming confrontation or challenge, focus on what you can prepare and control rather than the worst-case scenarios your mind generates.

If you find yourself reluctant to step into a difficult situation that your conscience tells you is right, reflect on what is holding you back — fear of the unknown is often more paralyzing than the reality ahead.
Prepare diligently for challenges, as Abu Sufyan did by gathering intelligence and sending for help, but ultimately surrender the outcome to God.

title: 'Atika's Dream: A Vision of Coming Calamity
summary: Three nights before the arrival of Damdam's warning in Mecca, 'Atika bint 'Abd al-Muttalib had a terrifying dream foretelling disaster for the Quraysh. Her brother al-'Abbas kept it secret but it spread through Mecca, drawing the mockery of Abu Jahl who challenged its truthfulness.
story:
Ibn Ishaq stated, "An unimpeachable source related to me, from 'Ikrima, from Ibn 'Abbas and Yazid b. Ruman related to me from 'Urwa b. al-Zubayr as follows: 'Atika, daughter of 'Abd al-Muttalib had, three nights prior to the arrival of Damdam, a dream that had terrified her. She sent for her brother al-'Abbas 'Abd al-Muttalib and told him, 'Last night, brother, I swear I had a vision that really scared me. I'm concerned that because of it some harm might come to you or your people, so please keep confidential for me what I'm about to tell you.'

"'What was it you saw?' he asked.

"She replied, 'I saw a man arrive mounted on a camel who came to a halt in the valley then shouted out at the top of his voice, "Come forth to your deaths in three days hence, you people of perfidy!" I saw people gather around him. He then entered the mosque, with the people following. While they were there around him, his camel took him to the top of the Ka'ba. Once there he called out as before: "Come forth to your deaths in three days, you people of perfidy!" His camel next took him up to the top of Mt. Abu Quhays where he shouted the same. Then he picked up a rock, flung it away and it came on down, bursting into fragments when it reached the bottom of the mountain. And no one house or building in Mecca escaped being pierced by one of its fragments.'

"Al-'Abbas told her, 'That was some vision! Keep quiet about it. Don't tell anyone!'

"Al-'Abbas then went off and met with al-Walid b. 'Utba, who was a friend of his, told him of it and asked him to keep it confidential. Thereafter al-Walid told it to his son 'Utba, and so soon the story was spread all about and all Quraysh were discussing it.

"Al-'Abbas said, 'I arose early to circumambulate the Ka'ba and there was Abu Jahl b. Hisham sitting with a group of Quraysh leaders discussing 'Atika's dream. When Abu Jahl saw me, he said, "Abu al-Fadl, why don't you come and join us when you finish your circumambulation?" When I had finished I went over to him and sat down among them. Abu Jahl then asked me, "How long has your family of 'Abd al-Muttalib had this prophetess?" "How do you mean?" I asked. "I'm talking about the visions 'Atika saw," he replied. "What were they?" I enquired. He responded, "Aren't you 'Abd al-Muttalib's satisfied with having your men become prophets? Has it got to be your women too? 'Atika claims that in her vision a man said, 'Come forth in three days!' We're going to watch you closely for these three days and if what you say is true, so be it. But if three days pass with nothing happening, then we'll judge you the biggest liars of all the temple-worshippers among the Arabs!"'

"Al-'Abbas commented, 'I swear, I've never had any problem with him before. All I did was repudiate what he said and deny that she had seen'"
lessons: Dreams and premonitions — especially those that cause genuine fear — are often messages that deserve thoughtful reflection rather than dismissal, even when others mock them.
The mockery of Abu Jahl toward 'Atika's dream reflects how arrogance causes people to ridicule what they cannot control or explain, a defense mechanism that history repeatedly proves hollow.
Confidences that are shared, even with trusted people, have a way of spreading; this is a reminder to be measured about what we reveal and to whom.
practical_advice: When you receive a warning — whether through intuition, a dream, or another person's concern — take time to reflect on it seriously rather than dismissing it out of pride or inconvenience.
If someone mocks you for your beliefs or your spiritual experiences, do not let their scorn shake your inner knowing; respond with calm dignity as al-'Abbas did, rather than anger.
Be mindful of the confidences others place in you; the spread of 'Atika's dream from al-'Abbas to al-Walid to all of Mecca is a vivid reminder that gossip can spiral far beyond our intentions.

title: Atika's Vision and Abu Jahl's Mockery
summary: Abu Jahl mocks the Banu Abd al-Muttalib clan after a woman named Atika has a prophetic vision warning of disaster in three days, calling them liars if nothing happens.
