story: 'Abd al-Muttalihs satisfied with having your men become prophets? Has it got to be your women too? 'Atika claims that in her vision a man said, 'Come forth in three days!' We're going to watch you closely for these three days and if what you say is true, so be it. But if three days pass with nothing happening, then we'll judge you the biggest liars of all the temple-worshippers among the Arabs!" "Al-cAbbas commented, 'I swear, I've never had any problem with h i before. Ail I did was repudiate what he said and deny that she had seen anything.' "He went on, 'We then parted company. That night every woman of the 'Abd al-Muttalib clan came to see me and said, "Have you agreed to let that dirty old reprobate attack first your men and then your womenfolk while you just listened? Didn't anything you heard h i say offend you?" I replied, '7 would have done something, hut I never had a problem with him before. I swear I will confront him and if he repeats it, I'll take care of him for you!"' "He went on, 'On the morning of the third day after 'Atika's vision, I was extremely angry, feeling I had let him get away with something I should have put a stop to. I went into the mosque and saw him. I swear I was making towards him to confront h i to get h i to retract what he had said - he was a slight man, with a face, voice and gaze that were all sharp - when he hurried off towards the door of the mosque. I asked myself what could he the matter with the fellow, and whether he was doing this because he feared I was about to confront hi. But he had heard something I had not - the voice of Damdam b. 'Amr al-Ghifwi who was out in the centre of the valley, standing by his camel. He had lopped its ears and turned its saddle back to front, rent his shin and was yelling, ''0 Quraysh! T h e baggage camels! The baggage camels! Your goods being brought by Aha Sufyw have been waylaid by Muhammad and his men! I don't think you can save them! Help! Help!"' "Ibn 'Abbas concluded, 'And so all this diverted us both from our personal conflict. "'The Meccans then made quick preparations commenting, "Do Muhammad and his men think it's going to turn out as it did with the raid on the caravan of Ibn al-Hadrami? No way! By God, they'll find out differently!""'
lessons: Divine signs and warnings often come through unexpected sources; dismissing them out of pride can lead to harm.
Anger that is restrained for the right moment can be redirected by greater events, reminding us that God's plan supersedes personal disputes.
Those who mock sincere believers and their spiritual experiences often find themselves proven wrong by unfolding events.
practical_advice: When someone dismisses or mocks your sincere beliefs, take a breath and remember that truth vindicates itself in time — you do not need to prove yourself immediately.
If you feel you failed to stand up for yourself or your family, channel that energy constructively rather than letting guilt fester.
When a personal conflict is interrupted by a larger event, consider whether God may be redirecting your attention to what truly matters.

title: Damdam ibn Amr's Cry and the Quraysh Mobilization for Badr
summary: The crier Damdam ibn Amr arrives in Mecca in a state of alarm, warning Quraysh that Abu Sufyan's caravan has been intercepted, prompting the entire tribe to mobilize urgently for war.
story: Masa b. Wqba related the vision of 'Atika in much the same terms as did Ibn Ishaq. He stated, "And when Damdam h. 'Amr spoke in this fashion the Meccans were very worried about 'Atika's vision and went forth to battle feeling insecure and fearing the worst." Ihn Ishaq stated, "The Meccans used to respond in two ways. They either went forth themselves or sent another in their place. Not one of their leaders stayed behind now, except for Abii Lahab b. 'Abd al-Muttalib; he sent al-'Asi b. al-Mughira in his place. He hired him for a debt of 4,000 dirhams al-'&i owed him that he could not pay."
lessons: A single voice of warning, delivered at the right moment, can move an entire community — the power of communication and urgency should never be underestimated.
Even those who try to avoid their responsibilities find ways to delegate them; accountability cannot be fully escaped.
Fear and anxiety, while uncomfortable, can be signs that one's conscience is active and aware of the consequences of one's choices.
practical_advice: When you feel anxious about an impending situation, examine whether your anxiety is pointing to a truth you have been avoiding.
Do not let fear of confrontation cause you to send others in your place when a responsibility is truly yours to face.
If you are overwhelmed by a rapidly developing crisis, pause to assess the facts before reacting, rather than being swept up in the crowd's emotion.

title: Uqba Shames Umayya ibn Khalaf into Joining the Army
summary: Uqba ibn Abi Muayt uses public humiliation, placing a censer before the reluctant Umayya and calling him a woman, to shame him into joining the Quraysh army marching to Badr.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Ibn Abn Najih related to me that Umayya b. Khalaf had made up his mind to stay behind; he was a dignified old sheikh, tall and portly. 'Uqba b. Abii Muby< came up to him, while he was sitting there in the mosque with some of his family, carrying a censer with lit scented embers which he placed before him, saying, 'Here, Abn 'Ali, enjoy the perfume! You're just a woman!' "Umayya responded, 'God damn you, and what you bring!' He then got himself ready and left with the others."
lessons: Peer pressure and public shaming are powerful tools of manipulation; recognizing them for what they are is the first step to resisting them.
Decisions made in anger or to salvage one's pride, rather than from sound judgment, often lead to harmful outcomes.
True dignity is not preserved by yielding to mockery, but by standing firm in one's reasoned convictions.
practical_advice: When someone tries to shame you into a decision, ask yourself whether you are acting out of genuine conviction or merely to protect your ego.
Resist the urge to prove yourself to those who mock you — their opinion of you does not determine your worth.
If you feel embarrassed or humiliated in public, take time alone before making any major decision so that emotion does not drive you to a choice you will regret.

title: Saad ibn Muadh Warns Umayya of His Fate
summary: During a visit to Mecca, Saad ibn Muadh warns his friend Umayya ibn Khalaf that the Prophet had foretold Umayya would be killed, leaving Umayya deeply frightened and reluctant to march to Badr.
story: Al-Bukh~ri told it quite differently. He stated, "Ahmad b. 'Uthman related to me, quoting Shurayh b. Maslama, quoting &%him b. Ynsuf, from his father, from Aba Ishaq quoting 'Amr h. Maymiin, that he heard 'Abd Allah b. Mascad relate the following from Sacd b. MuCadh. Sa'd recounted that he was a friend of Umayya b. Khalaf and that when Umayya visited Medina he would stay with him, just as Sa'd would stay with Umayya when he came to Mecca. "After the Messenger of God (SAAS) had moved to Medina, Sa'd went to stay with Umayya in Mecca while performing the Cumra, the 'lesser pilgrimage'. Sa'd asked Umayya, 'Would you look out for me some time when it's empty there and I can circumambulate the ka'ba?' Umayya accompanied him forth at close to midday, but they were met by Aba Jahl, who said, '(Abii) Safwm, who is this with you?' He replied, 'This is Sacd.' Aha Jahl said (to Sa'd) 'What's this? Do I really see you going in safety around Mecca when you've given shelter to the Sabians and proclaimed your help and protection for them? I swear, if you weren't in the company of Abii Safwan you'd not return home safely!' "Sag replied, raising his voice, 'If you were to block my way here I'd do worse to you by blocking yours to Medina!' "Umayya asked him, 'Sacd, don't raise your voice to Abij al-Hakam; he is the leader of our valley.' "Sacd responded, 'Let's talk no more of it; and, I swear, I've heard the Messenger of God (SAAS) say that they are going to kill you!' Umayya asked, 'In Mecca?' S a g replied, 'I don't know.' "Umayya was very much afraid at this. "When he returned home he asked his wife, 'Umm Safwm, do you know what S a g told me?' 'What did he say?' she asked. 'He claims that Muhammad told them they would kill me! I asked him whether in Mecca, and he said he didn't know. So, I swear, I'm not leaving Mecca,' Umayya told her. "When the day of the battle of Badr arrived, Abn Jahl assembled the Meccan force by asking them to save their caravan. Umayya did not want to go to battle, but Abn Jahl came to him and said, 'Aba Safwzn, when people see that you are staying behind, you, one of the valley leaders, they will remain with you.' "Abii Jahl kept on at him until Umayya responded, 'You win! I'll purchase the finest camel in Mecca!' "He then told his wife, 'Umm Safwnn, get my equipment ready!' 'But have you forgotten what your Yathrib friend told you?' 'No,' he replied, 'I only want to go a little way with them.' "After leaving, Umayya tied up his camel at every stop they made, right on up to Badr, where God took his life." Al-Bukh~ri also told this elsewhere, on the authority of Muhammad b. Ishnq, from Wbayd Allzh b. Mnsa, from Isrx'il, from Abo IshZq, that account being similar. Al-Bukh~ri alone gives this. Im%n Ahmad related it from Khalaf h. al-Walid, from Aha Sacid, both of them quoting Isr2il. In Isrn'il's account Umayya's wife commented, "I swear, Muhammad never lies."
lessons: Prophetic warnings are a mercy, giving people the opportunity to reflect and change course before it is too late.
Friendship that transcends religious and tribal boundaries, as seen between Saad and Umayya, reflects the universal human capacity for sincere connection.
Yielding to social pressure after having received clear guidance is a recurring human weakness; steadfastness in one's convictions can be lifesaving.
practical_advice: When you receive a sincere warning from someone who cares about you, do not dismiss it because of outside pressure — give it serious, private reflection.
If you feel torn between what you know is right and what others expect of you, write down the reasons for each path to make your thinking clearer before deciding.
Acknowledge when fear is giving you sound guidance rather than treating it only as a weakness to overcome.

title: Quraysh's Fear of Banu Bakr and Satan's Deception
summary: As Quraysh prepared to march to Badr, their fear of an old blood feud with Banu Bakr nearly stopped them, until Satan appeared in human form to give them false assurance and drive them toward their destruction.
story: Ibn Ishzq stated, "When they had gathered their equipment and were assembled to depart, Quraysh remembered the dispute they had with the Banii Bakr h. 'Abd Manat h. Kinma. They expressed fear that they might attack their rear. This quarrel related to a son of H a f ~b. al-Akhyaf of the Banii 'Amir b. Lu'ayy who had been killed by a man of the Bano Bakr, on the orders of 'Amir b. Yazid b. 'Amir b. al-Mulaww&. His brother Mikraz b. Hafs took revenge for him, killing 'Amir by plunging his sword into his stomach. "He then brought the sword by night and hung it up in the curtains of the kacba. Quraysh therefore feared the Banii Bakr for what had occurred between them." Ihn Ishzq went on, "Yazid b. Riimn related to me, from Wrwa b. al-Zubayr, who said, 'When Quraysh were ready to leave they remembered their problem with the Banii Bakr and that almost diverted them. But Iblis, Satan, appeared to them in the form of Surxqa b. Mgik b. Jucsham al-Mudliji, one of the nobles of the Banu Kinma. He told them, "I will give you protection against Kinma attacking you from your rear." They therefore hurried away to battle."' I observe that this illustrates the words of the Almighty, "Do not be like those who left their homes exulting and showing off before men, blocking people from the way of God, for God was aware of what they were doing. And when the devil made their works look good to them, he said, 'No one will overcome you today; I will be your protector.' But when the two factions met he turned on his heels, saying, 'I am quit of you; I see what you do not see. I fear God! God is fierce in punishing.'" (sarat al-Anfa& VIII, v.47-8). He, God curse him, deceived them, so that they left, with him following behind them, stage by stage, bringing his troops and his banners with hi, as several of them reported. And so he delivered them to their deaths. When he saw the strife, and the angels coming down to help and sighted Gabriel, he turned on his heels and said, "1 am quit of you; I see what you do not see. I fear God!" This is similar to the words of God Almighty, "like the devil, when he says to man, 'Disbelieve!' and when man disbelieves, he says, 'I am quit of you; I fear God, the Lord of the worlds'" (sarat al-Hash?; LIX, v.16). And God Ahnighty said, "And say: 'The truth has come; and falsehood has vanished; falsehood is ever bound to vanish'" (sarat Bang Isr2il or al-Isr8); XVII, v.81). And so when Satan, God damn him, on that day saw the angels descend to give victory, he fled. He was the first to flee, though he had been the chief encourager and helper of Quraysh, deceiving them and making them false promises. Satan's promises were only to mislead.
lessons: Those who abandon the truth in favor of arrogance and show are especially vulnerable to deception, because their pride leaves no room for honest self-reflection.
Evil always abandons its followers at the moment of true consequence — no worldly ally can protect against divine judgment.
Falsehood may appear strong and reassuring in the short term, but it is inherently unstable and will ultimately vanish.
practical_advice: When you feel falsely reassured about a wrong path you are considering, ask yourself honestly whether the reassurance is coming from a place of wisdom or from your own desires.
Be especially cautious of advice that encourages you to continue down a harmful road by promising protection from consequences.
In moments of confusion about which path to take, return to your core values and principles rather than relying on the opinions of those around you.

title: The Quraysh Army Departs for Badr
summary: The Quraysh force of 950 warriors departs Mecca for Badr with cavalry, slave girls playing tambourines, and daily slaughter of camels at each stop, displaying arrogance and confidence on their march to battle.
story: Yanus stated, quoting Ibn Ishaq, "Quraysh went forth with a sense of anxiety and weakness. They consisted of 950 warriors led by 200 cavalry. They had slave girls with them striking tambourines and chanting invective against the Muslims." He lists day by day those providing food for the Quraysh force. Al-Umawi stated, "The first person to slaughter for them following their departure from Mecca was Aha Jahl. He slaughtered ten camels. At 'Asfm Umayya b. Khalaf slaughtered nine, and Suhayl b. 'Amr ten more at @did. At Qadid they turned off to the wells in the direction of the sea coast and remained a day there. Shayba b. RabiCd slaughtered nine more for them. Next morning they reached al-Juhfa, where 'Utba b. Rabica slaughtered ten and the next morning, at al-Abwn), Nubih and Munbih, two sons of al-Hajjaj slaughtered ten. Next, alLCAbbash. 'Abd al-Mu~alibb slaughtered ten, and Aba al-Bakhtari ten more at the well at Badr. They then ate from their provisions." Al-Umawi stated, "My father related to us, quoting Aba Bakr al-Hudhali, as follows, 'The polytheists had with them 60 horses and 600 coats of mail, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) had with him 2 horses and 60 coats of mail.'"
lessons: Outward displays of strength and numbers can mask inner anxiety and weakness, as even the Quraysh marched with a sense of unease despite their superior force.
Material superiority does not determine the outcome of a conflict when divine will is aligned against it.
Arrogance and extravagance on the road to a wrong cause consume resources and energy that serve no ultimate benefit.
practical_advice: When you feel overwhelmed by the apparent strength of an adversary or obstacle, remember that outward appearances do not reveal the full reality of a situation.
Avoid letting the display of confidence by others convince you that they are in the right — assess situations by their moral foundation, not their apparent power.
Conserve your energy and resources for what truly matters rather than spending them on impressions and shows of strength.

title: The Prophet Departs Medina for Badr
summary: The Prophet sets out from Medina at the start of Ramadan with his companions, making careful appointments for leadership in his absence, entrusting the banner to Musab ibn Umayr, and marching toward Badr.
story: Regarding the activities of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Ibn Ishnq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved out with his men several days after the start of Ramadan. He appointed Ibn Umm Maktam to lead the people in prayer and he sent back Aba Lubnha from al-Rawhn) to administer Medina. "He entrusted the banner, which was white, to Mu~Labb. Wmayr, while he was preceded by two black flags one, called al-'Uq~b, the standard, carried by 'Ali b. Aba Tnlib,"
lessons: Responsible leadership includes thoughtful delegation — the Prophet ensured that those left behind were cared for and guided even as he marched to a critical battle.
Preparation and order, even in the face of uncertainty, reflect trust in God combined with taking all practical precautions.
Beginning a momentous undertaking in the blessed month of Ramadan reflects the importance of aligning action with spiritual readiness.
practical_advice: Before embarking on any major endeavor, ensure that your responsibilities to those depending on you at home are properly arranged.
Assign clear roles and responsibilities to your team before entering a period of high stress or crisis so that confusion does not compound the difficulty.
Use spiritually significant times, such as Ramadan or moments of personal renewal, to launch important positive changes in your life with intention and focus.

title: The Muslim Army's Departure for Badr: Organization and Equipment
summary: The Messenger of God organized and departed from Medina toward Badr with his companions, appointing leaders for various roles including banner-bearers, rear guard, and flanks, with limited horses and shared camels.
story: with him 2 horses and 60 coats of mail. This, then, was how they left Mecca and moved towards Badr. Regarding the activities of the Messenger of God (SAAS), Ibn Ishnq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) moved out with his men several days after the start of Ramadan. He appointed Ibn Umm Maktam to lead the people in prayer and he sent back Aha Lubnha from al-Rawhn) to administer Medina. "He entrusted the banner, which was white, to Mu~Labb. Wmayr, while he was preceded by two black flags one, called al-'Uq~b, the standard, carried by 'Ali b. Aba Tnlib, the other borne by one of the Helpers." Ibn Hisham stated that the Helpers' flag was carried by Sacd b. Mu5dh. AlUmawi said it was with al-Khabbab b, al-Mundhu. Ibn Ishaq went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) appointed Qays b. Aba SaCsaCa, a brother of the Bana M e i n b. al-Najjw, over the rear guard." Al-Umawi said that they had two horses, on one of which rode Mus'ab h. Wmayr, and on the other al-Zubayr b. al-'Awwm. Sa'd b. Khaythama had charge of the right flank, al-Miqdad b. al-Aswad that of the left.
lessons: Careful organization and delegation of responsibilities reflects wisdom in leadership, even when resources are scarce.
Sharing burdens equally, as the Prophet did by taking turns on the camel, demonstrates humility and builds unity.
True leadership means bearing the same hardships as those you lead, not exempting yourself from difficulty.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult challenge, organize your available resources carefully and assign clear responsibilities to trusted people around you.
Practice sharing burdens equally in your family or team rather than placing all hardship on others.
Reflect on moments when a leader or mentor shared your hardship — let that inspire you to do the same for others.

title: The Prophet Refuses to Be Excused from Sharing the Camel
summary: During the march to Badr, companions offered to walk so the Prophet could ride, but he refused, saying he was no stronger than them and equally in need of reward.
story: Im%n A h a d stated, "'Affm related to us, from Hammad h. Salama, 'Asim b. Bahdala related to us, from Zirr h. Huhaysh, from 'Abd All& h. Mascad, who said, 'At Badr we were three to a camel. Aha Luhaha and 'Ali were the Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS).' He also said, 'They were the ones taking turns riding with him, and they told him, "Let us walk." He replied, "You are no stronger than me; nor can I dispense with the reward more than you can."'"
lessons: True humility means refusing special treatment even when it is sincerely offered.
Every act of patience and sacrifice carries spiritual reward that no one should deprive themselves of.
Leaders who share in the struggle earn not just respect but deep love from those around them.
practical_advice: When others offer to take on your burden out of care for you, consider whether accepting might deprive you of growth or connection.
Remind yourself during hardship that enduring it with others rather than above them builds bonds that last a lifetime.
Reflect on the spiritual dimension of difficulty — sometimes the struggle itself is the blessing, not just what comes after it.

title: The Bedouin's Provocative Question at 'Irq al-Zabiyya
summary: A bedouin encountered during the march to Badr asked the Prophet an obscene question about his camel's pregnancy, and when a companion responded with crude language, the Prophet rebuked him.
story: "Arriving at 'Irq al-Zabiyya he met a bedouin. They asked him about other people, but received no news from him. The Muslims told him, 'Pay your respects to the Messenger of God! (SAAS).' The man asked, 'Do you have the Messenger of God (SAAS) among you?' 'Yes,' they replied. The man then did greet him, but asked, 'If you're the Messenger of God, then tell me what is inside the belly of this female camel of mine.' Salama b. Salaria b. Waqsh responded, 'Don't you put questions to the Messenger of God! (SAAS). Come on over to me, and I'll tell you that. You mounted her and so she has a little goat inside her belly from you!' The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, 'You've used obscene language to the man!' He then turned away from Salama."
lessons: Zeal in defending what is sacred must never lead one to respond with indecency or vulgarity.
The Prophet's correction of his companion shows that good intentions do not excuse harmful speech.
Maintaining dignified conduct even when provoked reflects a higher level of character.
practical_advice: When you feel the urge to defend someone you love or respect, pause and ensure your response reflects the values they stand for.
Practice responding to provocation with composure — your tone and words reveal your character more than your intentions do.
If you have responded poorly in a moment of zeal, acknowledge it honestly as the Prophet's rebuke modeled accountability without cruelty.

title: The Prophet Avoids Villages with Ill-Omened Names
summary: During the march to Badr, the Prophet inquired about the names of two mountains and the tribes living on them, and disliking their inauspicious meanings, changed his route to avoid them.
story: Ibn Ishsq, God bless him, went on, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) proceeded ahead and approached al-Saffl", a village set between two mountains. He asked what were the names of the two mountains and was told that one was called Muslih and the other Mukbri'. He then asked about the people living on them and they were named as the Banii al-N% and the Banii Huraq, two sub-mibes of Ghifw. The Messenger of God (SAAS) did not l i e these names, thinking them an ill omen, and so he left them and al-Saf@ to his left, making his way along a valley called Dhafirsn. Having gone through it, he made camp."
lessons: Being mindful of one's environment and the influences around a journey — physical or spiritual — is a form of practical wisdom.
Avoiding what troubles the heart, when a better path is available, is not superstition but sensible spiritual care.
Leadership sometimes means making small adjustments to preserve the morale and focus of those depending on you.
practical_advice: When you sense unease about a particular path or environment, give yourself permission to choose a different one without overthinking it.
Pay attention to what names, symbols, or environments negatively affect your mindset, and consciously orient yourself toward what uplifts you.
Small decisions to steer clear of negativity accumulate into a habit of protecting your inner peace.

title: The Prophet Consults His Companions Upon Learning Quraysh Has Mobilized
summary: When the Prophet received news that Quraysh had mobilized to protect their caravan, he consulted his companions; al-Miqdad gave a stirring pledge of loyalty, and Sa'd ibn Mu'adh spoke on behalf of the Helpers with total commitment.
story: Ibn Ishaq continued, "There he received the news that Quraysh had set out to protect their caravan. "He told his men about this, and asked their opinion. "Aba Bakr arose and spoke, giving good advice. '"Umar b. d-Khattah arose and spoke, giving good advice. "Al-Miqdad h. cAmr then got up and said, 'Carry on as ever God tells you, O Messenger of God. We are with you. We will certainly not say to you what the people of Israel said to Moses - that is, "Yon and your Lord go and fight together; we are staying here!" Rather, (we say) you and your Lord go and fight together, and we will fight along with you! By Him who sent you with the truth, , if you were to take us even to Bark al-Ghimnd we would fight with you all over the place until you reached it!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) expressed his appreciation and blessed him. He then said, 'Now you advise me, men!' He was really addressing the Helpers, because they were the majority and because, when they had given him allegiance at al-LcAqaba they had told him, 'We are free of responsibility for you until you reach our territory. When you join us, you will be under our protection, and we will then protect you as we do our own sons and women.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) was therefore apprehensive that they would feel obliged to help him only against those enemies attacking him in Medina, and that they had no duty to proceed against an enemy beyond their lands. "When he said this, SaCd h. Musdh responded, 'Is it us you are asking, 0 Messenger of God?' When he said that it was, Sa'd affirmed, 'We do believe in you and hear witness that you bring the truth. We have given you our oaths and agreement to listen to and to obey all your commands. Proceed as ever you decide, for we are with you. I swear, if you were to plunge into the sea, we would do so with you, not one of us would remain behind. We would have no reluctance to accompany you in confronting our enemy tomorrow. We are stoic in battle, trustworthy in the fray. Let us hope that God will give you pleasure in us. Go forward with God's blessings!' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) was delighted with Sa'd's words and they spurred him on. He then said, 'Go forward in good spirits! God has promised me I shall take one of the two parties. I swear by God I feel I can already see their destruction!'"
lessons: Sincere consultation before a major decision reflects humility and wisdom, even for those with authority and divine guidance.
True loyalty is expressed not through empty words but through unconditional commitment in the face of real danger.
When people around you rise to meet a challenge with courage and faith, it multiplies your own resolve — community is a source of strength.
practical_advice: Before a major and frightening decision, seek counsel from people whose wisdom and loyalty you trust, and truly listen to their perspectives.
When someone you care about faces a daunting challenge, let them know explicitly and completely that you stand with them — vague reassurance rarely provides the same strength as a clear pledge.
Reflect on a moment when someone's courage inspired your own, and consider how you can be that source of encouragement for others facing fear today.

title: Al-Miqdad's Pledge: We Will Not Say What Moses' People Said
summary: Al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad's declaration of loyalty before the battle of Badr — that the Muslims would fight alongside the Prophet rather than abandon him — moved the Prophet visibly and is recorded in multiple hadith collections.
story: One such is what d-Bukhzri related in his ~ahih collection. He stated that, "Aba Nucaym related to him, quoting Ism'il, from Mukhzriq, from T a i q h. Shihab, who quoted Ibn Masciid as having said, 'I witnessed a scene involving al-Miqdad b. al-Aswad such as to make me more his friend than I would have been from what was reported of him. He went to the Prophet (SAAS) while he was praying against the polytheists. Al-Miqdzd said, "We will not say as Miisa's people said to him, 'Yon go off with your Lord and fight along with Him; we're staying here!' Instead, we will fight on your right and your left, and in front of you and behind you!"' "(Ibn MasLiid went on) 'At that I saw the face of the Prophet (SAAS) light up in delight.'"
lessons: Standing firm beside those who carry a heavy burden — rather than stepping away when things become dangerous — is one of the highest expressions of loyalty.
A single act of sincere courage and solidarity can leave an impression on witnesses that lasts a lifetime.
Choosing to act with conviction when others might retreat transforms not only the moment but the character of everyone who witnesses it.
practical_advice: Think of someone in your life who is carrying a heavy burden alone and find a way today to tell them clearly: I am with you.
When you are tempted to step back from a commitment because the cost has grown higher than expected, recall that the most meaningful loyalties are tested precisely in such moments.
Let the image of the Prophet's face lighting up with delight remind you of the joy you can give someone simply by showing up for them when it counts most.

title: The Helpers' Pledge of Full Support Before Badr
summary: Before the Battle of Badr, the Prophet consulted his Companions, and the Helpers pledged unconditional support, declaring they would follow him even into the sea or to the ends of the earth.
story: Imam Ahmad stated, 'Wbayda related it to us, he being the son of Humayd, from Humayd al-Tawil, from Anas, who said, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) sought advice concerning going to battle at Badr. First Abii Bakr gave him advice and again he sought it from all. Then Wmar gave him advice and again he sought it. One of the Helpers observed, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) wants your advice, 0 Helpers.' "One of them said, 'Well, 0 Messenger of God, we'll not speak as the people of Israel said to Moses, that is, "Go off with your Lord and fight; we're staying here!" If you were to race all the way to Bark al-Ghimad, we'd follow you!"'
lessons: True solidarity means standing with your community even when the path is uncertain and the outcome unknown.
Courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to commit fully when duty calls.
Sincere support for righteous leadership is itself an act of worship and community resilience.
practical_advice: When someone you trust needs your support, offer it wholeheartedly rather than hedging with conditions.
Reflect on moments when others showed up for you unconditionally, and let that inspire you to do the same.
Practice making firm commitments to the causes and people that matter most to you, even when the road ahead is unclear.

title: Sa'd ibn Ubada's Oath of Total Devotion
summary: Sa'd ibn Ubada rose before the Battle of Badr and pledged that the Helpers would plunge into the sea itself if the Prophet commanded it, offering everything they possessed.
story: Ahmad also stated, "'Affan related to us, quoting Hammiid, from Thiibit, from Anas b. Mdik, that the Messenger of God (SAAS) sought advice when he learned of the approach of Abii Sufyan. Abii Bakr spoke, and he turned away from him. Then Wmar spoke, and he turned away from him. Sa'd b. Wbada then said, 'It's to us the Messenger of God (SAAS) is wanting to respond. By Him in whose hand is my soul, if you were to order us to plunge into the sea we would do so. And if you told us to race all the way to Bark al-Ghimad we would do so.' And so the Messenger of God (SAAS) urged his men forward."
lessons: Wholehearted commitment to a righteous cause brings clarity and removes the paralysis of indecision.
Leaders who hear sincere pledges of support are strengthened and emboldened to act.
Generosity of spirit — offering one's wealth, effort, and very life — is the highest expression of faith in community.
practical_advice: When your community or family needs you, speak up with clarity and commitment rather than waiting for someone else to volunteer.
Identify one cause or relationship in your life where you can deepen your level of commitment starting today.
Let the example of Sa'd inspire you to match your words of loyalty with concrete action and sacrifice.

title: Al-Miqdad's Declaration Before Battle
summary: Al-Miqdad ibn Amr stood and declared that unlike the people of Moses, the Muslims would not abandon the Prophet but would fight alongside him, prompting divine revelation.
story: "Then al-Miqdxd h. 'Amr arose and said, 'In such case we would not say to you what Moses' people told him — that is, "You and your Lord can go off and fight; we're staying here."' We wished that our people, the 'Helpers', had spoken as al-Miqdsd had done, preferring that to great wealth. And then God the Almighty and Glorious made revelation to His Messenger, with the words, 'Just as your Lord brought you forth from your house with the truth. A group of the believers are reluctant' (siirat al-Anfal; VIII, v.5)."
lessons: One person's bold and principled statement can shift the entire direction of a group.
Choosing courage over comfort, even when the easier path is available, earns divine recognition.
Our words have weight — speaking truth and commitment in difficult moments is a form of worship.
practical_advice: In group settings where courage is needed, be willing to be the first voice of resolve rather than waiting for consensus.
Reflect on a time when you took the harder, more principled road — acknowledge that as spiritual growth.
When feeling reluctant or afraid, recall that God sees and values the effort it takes to choose faithfulness over ease.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's Pledge at al-Rawha'
summary: At al-Rawha', Sa'd ibn Mu'adh gave a stirring pledge to the Prophet before Badr, offering complete obedience, all their wealth, and promising to follow him to the ends of the earth.
story: "Sa'd b. Mugdh then asked him, '0 Messenger of God, is it us you ask? By Him who honoured you and sent down the Book to you, I've never travelled this road before and so have no knowledge of it. But if you were to travel on to Bark al-Ghimad, in Yemen, we'd accompany you. We'll not be like those who told Moses, "Go off with your Lord and fight; we're staying here." Instead, you go off with your Lord and fight, and we will follow you. And if you proceeded for one purpose and God gave you another, then recognize what God has charged you with and proceed. Make or break with whomever you wish, make war or peace with whomever you wish, and take from our wealth whatever you wish.' There was a revelation in the Qur'an concerning Sa'd's words, 'Just as your Lord brought you forth from your home with the truth. A group of the believers are reluctant' (s~rat al-Anfal; VIII. v.5). Al-Umawi related this in his work on the military expeditions and he added, after his words, 'and take from our wealth whatever you wish' the phrase 'and give us what you wish. And what you take from us will be dearer to us than what you have left. Whatever orders you give us we will follow completely. And, I swear, if you were to go on until you reached Bark of Ghamdm, we would go with you.'"
lessons: Humble acknowledgment of one's own limitations combined with full commitment is the mark of true loyalty.
Placing God's will above one's own agenda — even when plans change — is the essence of trust in divine wisdom.
Generosity that costs you something real is more valuable than any pledge made from abundance.
practical_advice: Practice saying to those who lead or depend on you: 'I don't have all the answers, but I am fully with you.'
When circumstances change and your plans are disrupted, try reframing it as God redirecting you toward something better.
Find one practical way this week to give generously to a cause you believe in, beyond what feels comfortable.

title: The Prophet's Secret Reconnaissance and the Old Bedouin
summary: On the eve of Badr, the Prophet rode ahead with Abu Bakr to gather intelligence and encountered an old Bedouin who unknowingly reported the exact positions of both armies.
story: Ibn Ishiiq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) travelled on from Dhafan and traversed the passes called al-Asziir. From there he went down to a town called al-Diibba, leaving d-Hamiin on his right. This is a huge sandhill, like a mountain, and from there he went on down to near Badr. He and another man then rode on further." Ibn H i s h m stated that this man was Ahn Bakr. Ibn Ishiiq went on, "This was related to me by Muhammad b. Yahy b. Hahbiin. They went on and finally stopped near an old bedouin sheikh. He asked him about Quraysh and about Muhammad and his Companions and what news he had of them. The old man replied, 'I'll not tell you until you tell me to whom you belong.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'You tell us and we'll tell you.' The sheikh asked, 'This for that?' 'Yes,' he agreed. The old man then said, 'I have been told that Muhammad and his Companions left on such and such a day. And if the person who told me this spoke the truth, then today they should be at such and such a place.' This was the name of the very place where the Messenger of God (SAAS) actually was. 'And,' he went on, 'I was told that Quraysh left on day so-and-so. And if the person who told me this spoke the truth, then today they should he at such-and-such.' This was the place where Quraysh then were. When he had finished his report, he asked, 'And who are you?' The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, 'We are from m d, "water".' He then left. As he did so the old man was asking, 'What's that about m d? You mean you're from the water of Iraq?'"
lessons: Divine providence arranges circumstances in ways that confirm the righteous on their path, even through unexpected sources.
Wisdom sometimes requires strategic discretion — not every truth must be disclosed before its time.
Careful preparation and personal involvement in gathering knowledge is part of responsible leadership.
practical_advice: When facing a major decision, do your research thoroughly and personally rather than relying solely on others' reports.
Pay attention to seemingly coincidental confirmations that you are on the right path — these can be sources of reassurance.
Practice the art of listening carefully before disclosing your own position; understanding others first often yields more information.

title: The Scouts at Badr's Well and Intelligence on Quraysh
summary: The Prophet dispatched Ali, Zubayr, and Sa'd to the well at Badr to gather intelligence, and they captured Quraysh water-carriers who revealed the size and leadership of the enemy force.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then returned to his Companions. That evening he dispatched 'Ali b. Abii Tzlih, d-Zuhayr h. al-'Awwm and Sa'd b. AbO Waqqiis, along with a number of his men, to the well at Badr to seek news; it was Yazid h. RiimZn who told me this, from Wrwa h. al-Zubayr. They came across a watering-party of Quraysh among whom were Aslam, a youth of the Banti al-Hajjiij, along with 'Arid Abii Yass, a youth of the Banii al-'AS h. Sa'id. They brought them back and questioned them, while the Messenger of God (SAAS) was standing praying. The youths told them they were water carriers sent to bring them water. The Muslim force disliked what they said and, believing they were under the command of Aba Sufym, beat them. After they had been severely beaten, the youths said that they were from Aba Sufyan. The Muslims then released them. The Messenger of God (SAAS) bowed and prostrated in prayer twice and said, 'When they spoke the truth you beat them and when they lied you released them! They did speak the truth, by God. They are of Quraysh. Now, you two, tell me about Quraysh!' They responded, 'They are beyond that sandhill you can see over on the far side of the valley.' That sandhill was called al-'Aqanqal. The Messenger of God (SAAS) asked them, 'How many men do they have?' 'Very many,' they replied. 'How many in number?' he asked. 'We don't know,' they answered. 'How many camels do they slaughter each day?' he next asked. 'Some days nine, others ten,' they told him. The Messenger of God (SAAS) concluded, 'So their force must be between 900 and 1000 men.' He then asked them, 'What Quraysh nobles do they have among them?' They replied, 'Wtba b. Rabi'a, Shayba b. Rabi'a, Aba al-Bukhtari b. Hishm, Hakim b. H i z m , Nawfal b. Khuwaylid, al-Harith b. 'Amir b. Nawfal, TuCayma b. 'Adi b. Nawfal, al-Nadr b. a l - H ~ t h, Zam'a b. al-Aswad, Aba Jahl b. Hishm, Umayya b. Khalaf, Nabih and Munabbih, two sons of al-Hajjaj, Suhayl b. 'Amr and 'Amr b. 'Abd Wudd.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out to his men and told them, 'This Mecca has thrown at you slices of its very liver!'"
lessons: Injustice committed under pressure — even in service of a good cause — must be corrected immediately by those in authority.
A wise leader uses every available piece of information, however indirect, to understand the reality he faces.
Truthful witnesses deserve protection; suppressing truth out of inconvenience or fear is a grave error.
practical_advice: When you are anxious and under pressure, be especially careful not to react harshly to those who bring you unwelcome but honest news.
Train yourself to ask precise, methodical questions when gathering important information rather than acting on vague impressions.
If you have treated someone unjustly in a moment of stress, acknowledge it promptly and make it right as the Prophet did.

title: The Prophet Rebukes His Companions for Beating an Honest Captive
summary: When the Prophet saw his Companions beating a captive youth who was telling the truth about Quraysh's army, he interrupted his prayer to rebuke them, declaring they beat him for truth and released him for lies.
story: "They set off and reached Badr. People came to them with reports of Quraysh, and one of these was a black youth of the Banii al-Hajjiij whom they captured. The Companions of the Messenger of God (SAAS) asked him about Abii Sufyan and those men with him. He would respond, 'I have no knowledge of Abii Snfym. But it was Abii Jahl b. Hisham, 'Utba b. Rabica and Umayya b, Khalaf.' When he said this they beat him. When they did so, he would then say, 'Yes, I will tell you! It was Aba Sufyan.' When they let him go and asked him, he said, 'I have no knowledge of Abii Sufyan. But it was Abii Jahl, Wtba, Shayba and Umayya.' When he said this too, they would beat him. Meanwhile the Messenger of God (SAAS) was standing there praying. When he saw this scene, he left, saying, 'By Him who holds my soul in His hands, you beat him when he speaks the truth, and you leave him alone if he lies!'"
lessons: Truth must be honored even when it is inconvenient or contradicts what we wish to hear.
Anxiety and fear can cause good people to act unjustly; awareness of this tendency is the first step to preventing it.
A leader's role is to defend truth and correct injustice, even when it means interrupting other important obligations.
practical_advice: Notice when anxiety is driving you to dismiss or punish truthful feedback — pause and ask whether you are reacting to the truth or to your own discomfort.
Practice welcoming honest answers even when they are not the answers you were hoping for.
When you see injustice happening around you, find the courage to speak up promptly, as the Prophet did even while in prayer.

title: The Two Girls at the Well: Intelligence Overheard at Badr
summary: Muslim scouts Basbas and Adi, sent ahead to Badr's well, overheard two local girls discussing a debt to be repaid from the incoming Qurayshi caravan, unknowingly revealing its imminent arrival.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "Basbas b. 'Amr and 'Adi b. Aba al-Zaghba' had gone on ahead to Badr and halted at a hill near the well. They then took a water-skm they had and went to fetch water. Majdi b. 'Amr al-Juhani was there at the well and 'Adi and Basbas heard two girls from the village laying claims on the water. The one under obligation was saying to her companion, 'The caravan will arrive tomorrow or the day after. I will work for them, then pay you what I owe you.' Majdi answered, 'You are right.' He then made a settlement between them. 'Adi and Basbas overheard"
lessons: Critical information sometimes comes from the most ordinary and unexpected conversations; attentiveness is a virtue.
Patient, careful listening is as much a skill of leadership and wisdom as bold speech or decisive action.
Providence often arranges that those who are diligent and prepared receive the guidance they need at exactly the right moment.
practical_advice: Cultivate the habit of attentive listening in everyday settings — important insights often come from unexpected sources.
When preparing for something difficult, do the groundwork quietly and thoroughly rather than relying on dramatic gestures.
Reflect on moments in your life when an overheard word or a chance encounter turned out to carry exactly the guidance you needed.

title: Abu Sufyan's Escape and Abu Jahl's Defiance Before Badr
summary: Abu Sufyan cautiously navigates his caravan past Badr after detecting the Prophet's scouts, then sends word to Quraysh to turn back. Abu Jahl refuses and insists on proceeding to Badr for a show of force.
story: Thinking he had saved his caravan, Aba Sufyan sent word to Quraysh, 'You came out to do battle in order to protect your caravan, people and wealth; God has now saved these, so go back!' But Abn Jahl b. Hisham said, 'By God, we'll not turn around until we reach Badr!' Badr was a place where the Arabs would hold a fair and market each year. (Aba Jahl went on) 'We'll stay there three days, slaughter some camels, eat well, drink wine and the entertainers will play music for us. The bedouin will get to hear of our journey and of our gathering there, and they will always respect us for it. Let's proceed ahead.'
lessons: Pride and arrogance can lead a person to reject sound counsel and walk toward destruction.
When safety is offered, wisdom lies in accepting it rather than pressing ahead out of ego.
Those who prioritize reputation and worldly prestige over reason often suffer the consequences of their own stubbornness.
practical_advice: When you are given a way out of a difficult situation, pause and honestly ask whether continuing forward serves a genuine purpose or only your pride.
Reflect on decisions you are making: are they driven by clear thinking or by a desire to appear strong to others?
Practice recognizing the moments when letting go is the wiser and more courageous choice.

title: The Vision of Juhaym b. al-Salt on the Eve of Badr
summary: On the march to Badr, a man from Quraysh named Juhaym b. al-Salt sees a prophetic vision of the leaders of Quraysh being killed, which is dismissed arrogantly by Abu Jahl.
story: Quraysh advanced and at al-Juhfa Juhayrn b. al-Salt b. Makhrama b. al-Muccalib b. 'Abd Manaf had a vision. He said, 'I had a vision in my sleep, while half-way between waking and sleeping, of a man riding a horse and leading a camel, who came to a halt. He then said, 'Wtba b. Rabi'a, Shayba b. Rabica, Aba al-Hakam b. Hisham, Umayya b. Khalaf and so on, are all killed!' He went on to enumerate other chiefs of Quraysh as well who were killed at Badr. Juhaym went on, 'I then saw him strike the upper chest of his camel and send it on into the camp. And every single tent there was splattered with its blood.' When Juhaym's vision was reported to Aba Jahl, God damn him, he said, 'So he too is a prophet from the 'Abd al-Mucwlib family! He'll know tomorrow who will be killed, if we meet in battle!'
lessons: Warnings from God can come in many forms, yet arrogance blinds a person from heeding them.
Mocking signs of truth does not make those signs untrue; it only deepens one's own loss.
Even clear warnings cannot benefit someone whose heart has hardened through pride.
practical_advice: When you receive a warning — whether through intuition, a trusted person, or reflection — take it seriously rather than dismissing it defensively.
Notice when your reaction to counsel is sarcasm or mockery, as this often signals a fear of being wrong that is worth examining.
Cultivate the humility to consider that you may not have the full picture, especially in high-stakes moments.

title: Al-Akhnas b. Shariq Counsels Banu Zuhra to Return from Badr
summary: Al-Akhnas b. Shariq wisely counsels the Banu Zuhra tribe to withdraw from the march to Badr since their stated purpose — protecting the caravan — had already been achieved, and they obey him.
story: AI-Akhnas b. Shariq b. 'Amr b. Wahb al-Thaqafi, an ally of the Bana Zuhra, who lived in al-Juhfa, said, 'O Bana Zuhra, God has saved your wealth for you and has now rendered your companion Makhrama b. Nawfal safe; since you only came out to protect him and the wealth he bears, put the blame for cowardice on me and go back. There is no need for you to go to battle when you have suffered no loss. Don't do as he says.' And they did go back; not one member of the Zuhra tribe took part in the battle of Badr. They obeyed him because they recognized his authority. Every sub-tribe of Quraysh except the Bana 'Adi did participate. Not a single one of them took part. And so the Bana Zuhra went back with al-Akhnas; not a man from these two tribes engaged in the battle.
lessons: True leadership sometimes means giving people permission to step back from unnecessary harm.
Distinguishing between one's original purpose and the current situation is a mark of sound judgment.
A person willing to absorb social blame in order to protect others demonstrates genuine moral courage.
practical_advice: When a situation has escalated beyond its original purpose, ask yourself honestly: 'Why am I still doing this? Is the original reason still valid?'
Be willing to let others take credit or blame if it means protecting those in your care from unnecessary harm.
Look for the advisors in your life who speak plainly and put your wellbeing above group pressure — and listen to them.

title: Talib b. Abi Talib Returns to Mecca Before Badr
summary: Talib b. Abi Talib, present in the Quraysh force, is accused of sympathizing with the Prophet and returns to Mecca before the battle, expressing his conflicted loyalty in verse.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, 'And so the force proceeded. There was some debate between Talib b. Aba Talib, who was present there, and some of the Quraysh. They told him, "We well know, you men of the Bana Hashim, that even though you have come out with us, that your sympathy lies with Muhammad." And so Talib went back to Mecca with some other men. On that subject he spoke the following verses, "O God, if Tdib goes to battle with a force allied to fight in great troops like there, Then let him be the one despoiled, not the despoiler, the one defeated, not the one defeating."'
lessons: Being caught between two loyalties is deeply painful, and it is better to withdraw honestly than to act against one's conscience.
Even in the midst of tribal and social pressure, a person's inner conviction often surfaces and guides them.
Honest self-examination — even when expressed privately — is a form of integrity.
practical_advice: When you find yourself in a situation that conflicts with your deeper values, give yourself permission to step back rather than forcing yourself to act against your conscience.
If you feel loyalty pulling you in two directions, write down or speak honestly about where your heart truly lies — clarity often comes through expression.
Recognize that withdrawing from a harmful course of action, even when others pressure you to stay, is a sign of strength, not weakness.

title: The Rain Before Badr: Different Effects on Two Armies
summary: On the eve of Badr, God sent rain that steadied the ground for the Muslim forces and purified their hearts, while the same rain immobilized the Quraysh forces entirely.
story: Ibn Ishaq went on, 'And God dispatched the heavens (with rain). The valley was soft ground and the water from the sky merely dampened the earth for the Messenger of God (SAAS) and his force but did not impede their progress. But Quraysh had such rain fall upon them that they could not move ahead.' I comment that on this God spoke, 'And He sent down upon you water from the sky to purify you thereby, to remove from you the devil's filth, to strengthen your hearts and to steady your feet' (surat al-Anfal; VIII, v.11). Here God refers to cleansing them both inside and out, to steadying their feet and giving them courage, to thwarting the devil and his inspiring them with fear and doubts. This action was to give strength both within and without.
lessons: Divine support can manifest through the most ordinary natural occurrences in ways that only the faithful recognize.
The same circumstances can be a mercy for one group and a difficulty for another, depending on their orientation toward God.
Inner purification — removing doubt, fear, and the whispers of despair — is as important as physical preparation.
practical_advice: When facing a difficult trial, look for the small signs of support around you — these may be the ways God is strengthening you.
Before a major challenge, take time to cleanse your intentions: ask yourself whether your heart is free of fear, doubt, and impure motives.
Trust that when you are on the right path, even hardships can become sources of steadiness and resolve.

title: The Prophet's Night of Prayer Before the Battle of Badr
summary: On the night before the Battle of Badr, the Prophet Muhammad (peace be upon him) spent the entire night in prayer and weeping beneath a tree while his companions slept.
story: Ibn Jarir stated, 'Harun b. Ishaq related to me, quoting Mus'ab b. al-Miqdad, quoting Isra'il quoting Aba Ishaq, from Haritha, from 'Ali b. Aba Talib, who said, "That night — the one preceding the morning when the battle of Badr occurred — it drizzled and we took shelter from it beneath the tree and under leather shields. The Messenger of God (SAAS) remained up praying and he exhorted us for the battle ahead."' The Imam Ahmad stated, "'Abd al-Rahman b. Mahdi related to us, from Shu'ba, from Abi Ishaq, from Haritha b. Mudrab, from 'Ali, who said, 'The only horseman we had at the battle of Badr was al-Miqdad. From what I saw, all slept except for the Messenger of God (SAAS) who remained beneath a tree praying and weeping until morning came.'" I comment that the night before Badr was the eve of Friday, the 17th of Ramadan, 2 AH. The Messenger of God (SAAS) spent that night near the stump of a tree there, praying. He kept prostrating himself and repeating, 'O Everlasting One! O Self-Reliant One!' (surat al-Baqara; II, v.255).
lessons: In the face of overwhelming challenge, turning to God in sincere prayer is the most powerful form of preparation.
The Prophet's weeping in prayer shows that expressing vulnerability before God is not weakness but the deepest form of devotion.
True leadership carries a weight that others do not see; the leader who bears it in prayer rather than despair is a model for all.
practical_advice: When facing something that feels too large to handle, dedicate time the night before to quiet, sincere prayer or reflection — releasing your fears to God rather than carrying them alone into the morning.
Do not be ashamed of tears in your private moments of prayer; they are a sign that your heart is present and your trust is genuine.
Repeat names or attributes of God that speak directly to your need — if you feel alone, call on the Ever-Present; if you feel powerless, call on the All-Capable.

title: Al-Hubab b. Mundhir's Strategic Counsel at the Wells of Badr
summary: Al-Hubab b. Mundhir asks the Prophet whether their position at Badr is divinely fixed or a matter of military judgment, and upon learning it is the latter, proposes a superior tactical position which the Prophet accepts.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, 'The Messenger of God (SAAS) went out to urge his men on to the well, and when he reached the nearest source at Badr, he stopped there. It was related to me by some men of the Banti Salama, that they had been told that al-Hubab b. Mundhir b. al-Jamiih said, "O Messenger of God, is this the place about which God revealed to you that we should not advance beyond nor stop before? Or is this a question of opinion, warfare and tactics?" He replied, "It's one of opinion, warfare and tactics." Al-Hubab then said, "Well, O Messenger of God, this isn't a place to stop. We should go ahead till we get to the well nearest to their force and stop there. We should then stop up the wells behind it and build a cistern and fill it with water. Then we can fight them and have water to drink, while they'll have none." The Messenger of God (SAAS) commented, "You've had a good idea!"' Al-Umawi stated, 'My father related to us as follows: "Al-Kalbi claimed, quoting Abti Salih, from Ibn 'Abbas, who said, 'As the Messenger of God (SAAS) was gathering the men into groups with Gabriel on his right, an angel came to him and said, "O Muhammad, God greets you with peace." The Messenger of God (SAAS) replied, "He is peace; from Him comes peace, and to Him peace goes." The angel then said, "You are to do as al-Hubab b. Mundhir told you.""' The Messenger of God (SAAS) then asked, "O Gabriel, do you know this man?" He replied, "I don't know all the people of heaven, but he is truthful. He is no devil." The Messenger of God (SAAS) and those with him arose and proceeded on to the well nearest to the Quraysh force, where they halted. He then ordered that the other wells be blocked and built a cistern at the well where he had stopped and filled it with water. They then threw the water jugs into it.'
lessons: Consulting qualified people and genuinely listening to their counsel is a prophetic virtue, not a sign of weakness.
Distinguishing between divine instruction and human judgment opens space for wisdom and expertise to contribute meaningfully.
When a believer's sincere advice is confirmed even by the divine, it affirms that God works through human reason and consultation.
practical_advice: Before acting in a high-pressure situation, ask yourself honestly: 'Is this approach fixed, or is there room to reconsider based on better information?'
Create space in your decision-making for the counsel of people with relevant expertise, even if you are the one in charge.
When someone in your life offers sound, practical advice, acknowledge it plainly — doing so builds trust and strengthens the bonds of your team or community.

title: Sa'd b. Mu'adh Proposes a Shelter for the Prophet at Badr
summary: Sa'd b. Mu'adh suggests building a protective shelter for the Prophet at Badr, assuring him that the Ansar's love and commitment remain firm regardless of the battle's outcome.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "'Abd Allah b. Abi Bakr related to me that he was informed that Sa'd b. Mu'adh stated, 'O Prophet of God, should we not build you a cover where you could stay and near which we could tether your mounts, and then we can meet our enemy. If God gives us victory over our enemy, that will be fine by us; but if the opposite happens, you can mount up and join those of our people behind us; those who stayed do not love you any less than we do. If they had thought you would be going into battle, they would not have stayed. God will protect you through them and they will give you sincere counsel and will fight hard with you.' The Messenger of God (SAAS) thanked him and prayed for him. Then a shelter was constructed for the Messenger of God (SAAS) where he stayed."
lessons: Genuine love for a leader expresses itself not only in battle but in thoughtful, practical care for their safety and wellbeing.
Planning for difficult outcomes is not a lack of faith — it is wisdom and responsibility combined with trust in God.
A community that prepares contingency plans while remaining committed to their mission models mature, balanced reliance on God.
practical_advice: When supporting someone you care about through a difficult challenge, think practically about what they need — your presence, a plan, a safety net — not only emotional reassurance.
Allow yourself to plan for difficult outcomes without interpreting that planning as pessimism; preparing wisely and trusting God are not opposites.
Express your commitment to those who lead or care for you in concrete, actionable ways — it means more than words alone.

title: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh's Pledge to the Prophet Before Badr
summary: Sa'd ibn Mu'adh reassures the Prophet before the Battle of Badr, pledging the full support of the Ansar and offering him a shelter, after which the Prophet thanks him and prays for him.
story: will he h e by us; but if the opposite happens, you can mount up and join those of our people behind us; those who stayed do not love you any less than we do. If they had thought you would be going into battle, they would not have stayed. God will protect you through them and they will give you sincere counsel and will fight hard with you.' "The Messenger of God (SAAS) thanked him and prayed for him. Then a shelter was constructed for the Messenger of God (SAAS) where he stayed."
lessons: True loyalty is expressed not just in words but in readiness to sacrifice one's life for what is right.
A leader who is sincerely supported by his community can face overwhelming odds with confidence.
Gratitude and prayer for those who support you strengthens bonds of brotherhood and trust.
practical_advice: When you feel alone in a difficult situation, take stock of the people around you who genuinely care — their presence is a form of divine protection.
Express sincere thanks to those who stand by you in hard times; a heartfelt prayer or word of appreciation can be more powerful than any material reward.
In moments of crisis, build a 'shelter' — a quiet space, physically or mentally — where you can gather your thoughts and reconnect with your purpose.

title: The Prophet's Du'a as the Quraysh Army Advanced at Badr
summary: As the Quraysh army advanced into the valley of Badr, the Prophet supplicated earnestly to God, calling on the divine promise of victory and asking for the destruction of the enemy forces.
story: Ibn Ishaq stated, "When morning came, the Quraysh force advanced. "When the Messenger of God (SAAS) saw them coming forward into the valley from their position behind the sandhill at al-LAqanqal, he said, 'O God, these men advancing are Quraysh in all their vanity and pride; they are antagonistic to You and are calling Your Prophet a Liar. O God, give us Your victory You promised us! O God, destroy them this morning.'"
lessons: In moments of extreme fear and vulnerability, turning to God in prayer is not weakness but the most powerful act of a believer.
Acknowledging the arrogance and wrongdoing of one's opponents in supplication — rather than personal grievance — keeps the heart aligned with justice rather than ego.
God's promises to those who strive sincerely are a foundation for courage; recalling those promises in dark moments renews resolve.
practical_advice: When facing a situation that feels completely out of your control, pause and make a sincere du'a or prayer — articulating your need to God can bring immediate calm.
Identify whether your struggle is for a righteous cause; if it is, remind yourself that you are not alone and that help can come from unexpected sources.
Write down or memorize a few supplications for moments of acute stress so that you have words ready when your mind goes blank with anxiety.

title: The Prophet's Observation of 'Utba ibn Rabi'a on the Red Camel
summary: Before the Battle of Badr, the Prophet observed 'Utba ibn Rabi'a mounted on a red camel and remarked that whatever good existed among the Quraysh resided in that man, and that had they followed him they would have been rightly guided.
story: "The Messenger of God (SAAS) then said, having seen Wtba b. Rabi'a in the Quraysh force mounted on a red camel, 'Whatever good may be in them resides in the man mounted on the red camel. If they were to obey him, they would be well guided.'"
lessons: Goodness can exist even in the hearts of those who are on the wrong side of a conflict; recognising it is a mark of wisdom and fairness.
Leadership that seeks peace and avoids unnecessary bloodshed is a virtue, even when it goes unheeded by the majority.
The Prophet's ability to see the good in an opponent reflects the Islamic teaching that one should be just even toward those one is in conflict with.
practical_advice: In any dispute or conflict, make an effort to honestly acknowledge the good qualities or valid concerns of the other side — this opens the door to resolution.
If you are in a position of influence, use it to de-escalate tension rather than inflame it, even when others push for confrontation.
Reflect on whether your current course of action is being driven by wisdom or by the pressure of the crowd around you.

title: Khufsf's Offer to Quraysh and Their Response About Fighting God
summary: Khufsf ibn Ayma' offered military support to the Quraysh before Badr, but Quraysh returned his offer saying that if they were fighting God rather than men, no one could stand against Him — an inadvertent admission of their precarious position.
story: "Khufsf b. Aym$ b. w a d a, or his father Ayma' b. Rahada al-GhifZri, had sent to Quraysh a son of his with some slaughtered camels which he was donating to them, telling them, 'If you wish us to provide you with weapons and men we will do so.' Quraysh sent back with his son the following response, 'You have maintained family ties and fulfilled your duty. If we are only fighting men, we will not lack the power. But if we are fighting God, as Muhammad claims, no one could stand up to Him.'"
lessons: Even those who oppose the truth can inadvertently speak it; their own words can bear witness against them.
No amount of material preparation or alliance-building can substitute for being on the side of what is right and true.
When people acknowledge the truth in private but refuse to act on it publicly, they set themselves on a path of self-destruction.
practical_advice: Pay attention when your own reasoning leads you to a conclusion you are not willing to act on — that gap between knowing and doing is where growth is needed.
Do not let social pressure or tribal loyalty cause you to ignore the truth your conscience is telling you.
When in a difficult situation, ask yourself honestly: 'Am I fighting a person, or am I fighting against what is right?' The answer should guide your next step.

title: Hakim ibn Hizam Drinks from the Prophet's Cistern at Badr and Survives
summary: When some Quraysh men approached the Prophet's water cistern at Badr, the Prophet permitted them to drink; every man who drank was later killed in the battle except Hakim ibn Hizam, who survived, later accepted Islam, and used this event as a mark of his oath.
story: "When the Quraysh force made camp, some of their men came forward to the water cistern of the Messenger of God (SAAS); they included Hakim b. H*. The Messenger of God (SAAS) said, 'Let them come.' And every man who drank that day was killed, except for Hakim h. Hiz-, who survived. He later accepted Islam and became a good Muslim; whenever he later made a serious oath, he would say, 'By Him who saved me at the battle of Badr.'""
lessons: Divine protection can reach a person even when they are among those who have come to oppose the truth, as long as goodness remains in their heart.
The mercy of the Prophet extended even to his opponents; he did not turn away those who came to his camp, and this openness became a means of guidance for Hakim.
A person's life can be transformed by a single moment of divine grace; what matters is how they respond to that grace afterward.
practical_advice: If you have been spared from a calamity that befell others, treat that as a serious sign to reflect on your path and the choices ahead of you.
Do not close doors on people who have been adversaries — sometimes an act of openness toward them plants a seed of transformation.
Mark the moments in your life when you were unexpectedly saved or guided; let those moments become anchors of gratitude that keep you grounded.

title: The Number of Muslim Fighters at Badr Compared to the Companions of Talut
summary: Islamic scholars and hadith narrations record that the Muslims at Badr numbered 313, a figure compared with spiritual significance to the number of believers who crossed the river with Talut (Saul), all of whom were true believers.
story: I would comment that the troops of the Messenger of God (SAAS) consisted of 313 men, as will be shown in a chapter we will give following the account of the battle in which we will list their names in alphabetical order, if God wills it. In the sahih collection of al-Bukhari, from al-Barn', there is the hadith: "We used to relate that those Muslims present at Badr totalled 300 men and some tens more, the same number as the companions of T&t, who crossed the river along with him; and all those who did so were believers." Al-Bukhm- also quotes from the same source as follows, "I and Ibn Wmar were deemed too young to participate in the battle of Badr. The Emigrants there numbered some 60 men, while the Helpers totalled some 240." Imxm Abxd related from Nxqr b. Ri'xb, from Hajjxj, from al-Hakam, from Muqsim, from Ibn 'Abbxs, who said, "Those participating at Badr totalled 313, while the Emigrants numbered 76. The defeat occurred on Friday, 17th Ramadxn."
lessons: Small, sincere groups have changed the course of history when they were united in purpose and trust in God.
The comparison to the companions of Talut teaches that quality of faith and commitment matters far more than quantity.
Recording and preserving the names and numbers of those who sacrificed for a righteous cause is itself an act of honour and gratitude.
practical_advice: When you feel outnumbered or under-resourced in a righteous effort, remember that history is full of examples where small determined groups achieved what seemed impossible.
Do not measure your capacity to do good by the size of your team or resources — focus on sincerity and preparation.
Take time to honour and acknowledge those who stood with you in your hardest moments; their contribution deserves to be remembered.

title: God Showing the Enemy as Few in Number — The Vision Before Badr
summary: Before and during the Battle of Badr, God caused each side to perceive the other as fewer in number, first to give the Muslims courage to engage, and then to cast fear into the hearts of the disbelievers once battle was joined.
story: God Almighty stated, "And recall when God showed them to you in your sleep as few; and if He had showed them to you as many, you would have lost courage, and would have disputed about the matter. However, God granted salvation" (stirat al-AnjZl; VIII, v.43). That related to a dream he had that night. It is said that he slept in the shelter and that he told his men that they should not fight until they received permission. When the Quraysh force approached, Abn Bakr began to wake him up, saying, "O Messenger of God, they are getting close! Wake up!" God had showed them to him in his sleep as being few in number. Al-Umawi related this, and it is very strange. The Almighty stated, "And when He showed them to you, when you met, as few in number in your sight, as He diminished you in theirs, in order to bring about a matter already decreed" (stirat al-Anfzl; VIII, v.44). When the two sides were to engage in battle, God made each of them fewer in the view of the other so that they would have courage to face each other, there being very great wisdom in this. This is not contrary to the statement of God Almighty: "There was a sign for you in the two sides battling together, one fighting for God, the other unbelieving and seeing them by their own sight as twice their own like. And God aids with His victory those He wishes" (stirat Al-'Imrzn; III, v.12). The meaning according to the most authentic statements is that the unbelievers did indeed view the believers as twice their number. This was when the battle and swordsmanship had begun, God cast weakness and terror into the hearts of the unbelievers. He first led them on by showing them as few in the initial engagement and then aided the believers by making them seem twice as many as they were so that the enemy was weakened and overcome. For this reason God stated, "God aids with his help those He wishes. And there is a lesson in that for those who have vision" (szirat Al-'Imrzn; III, v.12). Isra'il stated, from Abn Ishnq, Abn Whayd and 'Abd All*, "They were made fewer in number in our sight on the day of the battle of Badr, to the point where I said to a man by my side, 'Do you think they are 70 strong?' He replied, 'I see them as 100!'"
lessons: Divine wisdom operates in ways that are beyond human calculation; what appears small or manageable may be by God's design to give courage to the righteous.
Fear of the unknown or the seemingly overwhelming can paralyse good action; trust in God recalibrates our perception of what is truly possible.
The Quranic principle that God aids those who strive in His cause is not merely a metaphor — it has been demonstrated in concrete historical events.
practical_advice: When facing a challenge that seems overwhelming, try to break it down into smaller parts — sometimes what feels like an enormous obstacle is far more manageable when approached step by step.
Reflect on past moments when a feared situation turned out to be far less catastrophic than you anticipated; use that memory to reduce anxiety in the present.
Practice tawakkul (reliance on God) by completing your preparation and then consciously releasing the outcome, trusting that divine wisdom is at work even when you cannot see it.

title: 'Umayr ibn Wahb Scouts the Muslim Camp at Badr
summary: 'Umayr ibn Wahb was sent by Quraysh to estimate the Muslim force at Badr; after scouting the valley, he returned to warn them that the Muslims, though few in number, were utterly committed to fighting to the last man.
story: Ibn IshZq stated that Abii Is&q b. Yasar and other scholars related to him that sheikhs of the Helpers had said, "When the Quraysh force felt secure they sent out Wmayr b. Wahb al-Jumahi with orders to estimate the size of the force of the Messenger of God (SAAS). "He circled the camp on his horse and returned, saying, 'They are approximately 300 men in number; but be patient while I check out whether they have any others hidden or in reserve.' "He rode far off into the valley but saw nothing. So he came back and told them, 'I saw nothing more; but what I did see, O Quraysh, was camels bearing death. The Yathrib mounts bear imminent death. They are people whose only aid and refuge is their swords. I swear I don't foresee any of them being killed before he kills one of you. And if they do kill of you a like number as their own, what good will it be to live after that? Consider carefully.'"
lessons: True courage is not the absence of fear but the willingness to face mortal danger for a cause one believes is just.
Even an enemy can be forced to acknowledge the moral superiority and determination of those who stand for the truth.
A sincere and honest assessment — even when it is unwelcome — is more valuable than false reassurance.
practical_advice: When preparing for a difficult challenge, do thorough reconnaissance — understand what you are truly facing rather than relying on assumptions.
If someone gives you an honest but difficult assessment of a situation, resist the urge to dismiss it; sit with it and let it inform your decision.
Reflect on what you are truly willing to sacrifice for your beliefs and values — clarity about this in advance gives you strength when the moment of testing arrives.

title: 'Utba ibn Rabi'a's Counsel to Quraysh to Return Home Before Badr
summary: 'Utba ibn Rabi'a urged the Quraysh army to turn back before the Battle of Badr, arguing that fighting Muhammad would bring lasting grief and shame regardless of the outcome, but Abu Jahl undermined him by accusing him of cowardice.
story: "When Hakim b. Hizm heard that he walked through the force to Wtha b. Rabi'a and said, 'Al-Walid, you are a leader and a commander of Quraysh; do you wish to be remembered with pride by your people until the end of time?' "'How do you mean, Hakim?' he asked. 'Take your force back and deal with the issue of your ally 'Amr b. al-Hadrami.' 'Consider it done,' he agreed, 'you have my guarantee of it. He was under my protection and so it is my duty to pay his bloodwit and provide compensation for what was taken from him. Go and talk to Ibn al-Hawaliyya - meaning Aba Jahl - he is the only one I fear might make trouble.' "Wtba then arose to speak, saying, 'O Quraysh, by fighting Muhammad and his Companions you will accomplish nothing. If you do attack him, each of you will always be looking at others who will dislike looking back at you; you will be known to have killed their cousin or a member of their tribe. Go home and leave Muhammad up to the other Arabs. If they kill him that will be accomplishing your aims and if otherwise, he will find you not to have been exposed to (the risk of) what you now plan.' "Hakim went on, 'So I went on to Aba Jahl whom I found to have put on a coat of mail and was getting it ready. I told him, "Aba al-Hakam, Wtha has sent me to you." And I told him what he had said. He replied, "I swear, his lungs filled with terror when he saw Muhammad and his men. By God, we will not return until God decides the issue between us and Muhammad. Wtha is not sincere in what he says. He sees Muhammad and his men as fodder for camels awaiting slaughter, and his own son is there among them. He's scaring you for his own sake."'"
lessons: Giving sincere counsel that prevents harm is a moral responsibility, even when that counsel is rejected by the majority.
Those driven by ego and pride — like Abu Jahl — will always find ways to dismiss wisdom that threatens their sense of power.
Being publicly accused of cowardice for advocating peace is a test of character; 'Utba's advice was sound even if his motives were mixed.
practical_advice: If you are in a position to prevent harm — to yourself or others — speak up, even if you expect resistance; the responsibility to try is yours regardless of the outcome.
